author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Amazing But would definitely recommend adding extra spices! Your choice of course

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Serve as is for your fussy "I don't like too much flavor" relatives, definitely add some zest with your favorite veggie and spices.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: We really need a downvote button to push irrelevant comments down the list.  A comment which impacts the recipe, improves technique, adds an ingredient that makes a crucial difference, or a brand that is particularly helpful in creating the recipe, great.  But opining, for instance, about why strawberry-rhubarb pie needs strawberries, why this version of the crust is not the same as your grandmother's tried and true version is not only not helpful, it's annoying clutter of more useful comments.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: Totally agree!  I'm so tired of wading through posts from people who clearly haven't made the recipe, change a ton of ingredients and then complain about the outcome, or sharing "that's not how my grandmother did it."

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Douglas Short I’ll be the contrarian here.  My late mother’s observations, along with tips from siblings and friends, could be and still are often irrelevant or annoying, but I wouldn’t ban any of this from my kitchen.  Craziness helps make cooking the joy it is, and do any of us really want more computer-curated conversation?  Let’s let folks be folks, and make our “on-line” kitchens welcoming to all input — and feel free to ignore what’s not useful!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: 100% agree. Like, if you don't want a "strawberry rhubarb" pie, why comment on the "strawberry-rhubarb pie" recipe? There is room for both in life. (and if you want a cherry rhubarb pie - that's a different recipe)

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: Been baking pies for the better part of 60 years.  I assemble the dough and roll it out and into the pie pan, then I chill it for several hours. For the last decade or so I have been cutting the rhubarb into larger pieces and then cooking them for not more than 2-3 minutes until they are just at the point of softening. Cool completely and add a pint of raspberries, grate an orange and squeeze the juice into the mix.  If I don't have an orange I add a splash of Cointreau then do the top crust.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody:
My mom used to roll the dough between two sheets of waxed paper.  I have found using wider sheets of parchment paper (with the lightest dusting of flour) works even better.  

I also keep my rolling pin in the freezer.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: Love this rolling pin tip! I'm going to have to try that! I sometimes roll between or on top of parchment too, but it's gotten so expensive that I just use my kitchen counter and clean it well before and after rolling.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody:
@Genevieve Ko
Your use of vegetable oil in the crust was disappointing. Melissa Clark can help you with making pie crust. Stu

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: @sw Have you actually tried this? I make a stellar pie crust, and I’ve been using a recipe similar to this for decades.  It is hands down the best crust I’ve ever made, and so easy to work with.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: I have never understood why anyone would spoil the perfection of a rhubarb pie with strawberries. Nice looking crust though.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Tom Agree 1000%!!!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Stephen Martin vice versa

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Tom they go SO well together that’s why! There a place for both!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody:
My dear departed Mom won dozens of ribbons for her pies over the years at our local fair in Connecticut.
Her secret? Use lard for the crust, not butter, not Crisco. It will give you the flakiness and flavor you are seeking.
Life is short. Enjoy yourself once in a while with an old-school pie.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: Lard in the crust is amazing! It's hard to find great natural rendered leaf lard, but if you can get your hands on some, by all means use it in place of the shortening. I also have had delicious success using rendered duck fat (from searing duck breasts) in place of the shortening. Just be sure to start with hard, cold lard or duck fat to ensure the crust doesn't end up greasy.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Susan Even better… rendered leaf lard which you can find

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Genevieve Ko I always use what I call farm lard, meaning it was rendered by the farmer or butcher. Usually pork venders at farmers market sell pure lard. Now that I live in the Camden, ME area I get lard from a true carriage trade butcher and luxury goods purveyor called  Bleecker & Greer in Rockport; the  butcher sells freshly rendered lard. Depending on the pie I’ll use all lard or mostly butter and a few ounces of lard. The pastry is perfectly flakey.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: I have been baking pies for 60 years too, and have tried every method ever invented for making crust. The absolute best way to go is King Arthur’s method for making pie dough in a stand mixer as explained here. I have done the same in a food processor with similar results. https://www.kingarthurbaking.com/blog/2016/10/16/make-pie-crust-in-your-stand-mixer

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: I'm excited to try that stand mixer method, though I have to confess that part of the reason I like making pie dough by hand is the very feel of it. It's such a tactile joy.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Cathy Thank you for the link! Step-by-step instructions with pictures are perfect for a baker who has always struggled with pie crusts. Can’t wait to try it!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: Way too much sugar!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: Can we do this recipe using frozen strawberries? We froze them last season after picking a whole lot more than we were able to use. Will they be too mushy??

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: I tried this a couple of weeks ago and the bottom crust got soggy; I'm going to try roasting fresh strawberries today.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Rick G what may help sogginess from happening is sprinkling sugar (turbinado is a good option) on pie bottom before adding filling. It will somewhat carmelize and provide barrier preventing juices from sinking into crust

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: I like to add a few sliced up hard nectarines to the rhubarb and strawberries.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: Same! I like throwing diced peaches or nectarines into the mix when they appear at the markets. They go so well with the rhubarb and berries.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: To improve the flavor immensely try these substitutes: use tart cherries for the strawberries, and 1/4 cup instant tapioca for the corn starch. You won't be sorry!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: We used a standard 9 Inch pie pan and carefully weighed the strawberries and rhubarb. This was nearly twice as much filling as could be accommodated by a standard 9 inch pan. We only used 3/4 to mound, but it  couldn’t sealthe top and it was a mess in the oven. Why  was such a large amount of filling suggested in the recipe?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: Why does everyone put "shortening" in their piecrust. Cold butter works beautifully. Cut up and frozen. Then use the food processor. I process it until the biggest bits are about 1/2 inch in diameter then add the very cold water. Voila! it's the flakiest and most delicious crust.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: I usually prefer an all-butter pie crust (my recipe is here: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1023573-all-butter-pie-crust), but for a soft, juicy filling like strawberry-rhubarb, I like the way shortening makes the crust shatter easily. A fork can very easily glide right through those finer flakes.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Genevieve Ko I truly appreciate your responses on here. I can’t wait to try this recipe, might do a butter lard mix.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: What about the rhubarb? It is mentioned in the ingredients but there is nothing in the instructions- is it roasted or stewed ? Is it simply thrown in with the strawberries?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: The rhubarb is tossed in raw in step 6. Because it's so fibrous, it breaks down easily and the time inside the pie dough in the oven will cook it through but still leave it somewhat intact. That way, you can enjoy its tartness and also it's tender bite.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: I would replace the shortening with farm lard available from vendors at farmers markets or butcher. Don’t use that supermarket lard, which is highly processed

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: Whenever I put rhubarb in a pie, I freeze it first and thaw to drain some of the water/juice off to avoid a wet filling as recommended by the team at Four and Twenty Blackbirds.  That tip has never let me down. You can discard the liquid or if you have time, reduce it down and add it back to the filling

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: I didn't have any clementine or mandarin, so I used a Meyer lemon instead. Made the error of brushing the top crust with buttermilk instead of water and had to tent it during the second half of baking to prevent from burning. Loved the roasted strawberry trick - didn't really add much work, just a little extra heat in the kitchen

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: Much better without the strawberries! You can't beat plain rhubarb pie. Strawberry-rhubarb sounds good, but it can't lay a candle to rhubarb pie all by itself.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: I’m an experienced home chef, but an inexperienced baker. So, making pie crusts usually makes me a bit nervous. Not to worry! This one came out perfectly. And the filling was scrumptious! Going against my better judgment, I did as the recipe prescribed and omitted the pan juices from the roasted strawberries. I must admit that the technique worked. So, I’m saving the juices for other uses (a strawberry daiquiri, perhaps?). I’m open to suggestions.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody:
@Paul in Atlanta
Strawberry Daiquiri sounds perfect!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Paul in Atlanta my grandson was baking with me and he took the roasted strawberry juice and added bubbly water and vanilla ice cream. Delicious !

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: When we bought our house it had a rhubarb patch in the backyard… the rhubarb is a hybrid variety that yields beautiful red stalks, not the green and pink ones we were used to. The flavour is the same but when cooked it has a rich dark pink colour, absolutely beautiful stewed or in pies.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody:
I love rhubarb and have moved away from strawberry - rhubarb pie because most strawberries are a bit bland unless locally picked. But I'll try this recipe to see if roasting them concentrates the flavors. 
    Apricots pair well with rhubarb, also best if very flavorful ones - locally bought if possible.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Sierra Sarah OMG apricot and rhubard sounds like perfection! Going to try this combo!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody:
I’m going to make it today but have 2 questions:
The recipe calls for 4 pounds of strawberries and rhubarb! Is that accurate? Seems like an awful lot for a 9” pie, even accounting for the filling reducing during baking.
Also, is the rhubarb cooked or out raw?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: Four pounds total is correct! It's intentionally a lot of fruit. I love a generous proportion of fruit to crust. The strawberries shrink significantly after roasting and the rhubarb is added raw. It's piled pretty high in a standard 9-inch pie dish, then bakes down into a nice even layer flush with the top of pan. Enjoy!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Genevieve Konut why brush the top with water? Cream or beaten egg much better

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: Why anyone would want to ruin a delicious strawberry pie with rhubarb is beyond me.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: I made this for my husband for Father’s Day! We both love this pie. It’s time consuming but I am totally into baking and cooking as a special event. That’s exactly how this felt. This made for a fun afternoon and was so Delia. I will make it again!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: Try this pie with a dollop of sour cream on top.  It sounds awful, but it's amazing.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: Fresh Rhubarb isn't always available here & is not as of today. Can a decent version of this pie be made with frozen rhubarb and, if yes, would you please give recommendations for using it for the best result?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Scott Yes! Ive used frozen (store bought and with some Ive frozen myself from the spring prior) works well. In any case, freezing rhubarb prior to making pie, allowing some of the water to drain from it when thawing before mixing filling helps to reduce sogginess of crust

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: Roasting the strawberries was easy and made the house smell wonderful. It also intensified the strawberry flavor, which can be a little bland and watery otherwise. But the orange rind and juice really elevated this pie. My family adored it.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: They dropped this recipe on June 5. Way too late in the season. As every good student of European folklore knows, you can't eat rhubarb after the summer solstice or it gives you diarrhea. The leaves only two weeks for rhubarb pie!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: @Dan good thing I only have diarrhea so I can enjoy rhubarb year round! Which begs the question: Chicken or the egg?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody:
I have a recipe cut out of the Times from maybe 30 years ago. It was “the pie lady’s pie recipe’ a woman had a pie shop that sold amazing pies.

It had you use the tips of your fingers to smash the butter… not squish… but smash as smashing caused your fingers to melt the butter… you do it to the cornmeal texture phase.

The recipe also called for no sugar in the dough. I make an apple pie with tapioca with sugar… but no apple spice. The result is a very adult, Very flakey and very delicious pie

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: An even easier way to hull a strawberry is with a melon baller. One swift rotation and you’re done.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: Tapioca is my preferred thickener for pie filling. I use 2 TBS for about 6 cups of fruit. It thickens the fruit without the chalky or gluey texture of cornstarch or flour.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: Made 6/6/26 as per recipe but substituted blood orange for clementine as that was what I had. Used 1c sugar as tiny farmer's market strawberries were quite sweet. Crust is quite forgiving, rolled out without cracking. Used kitchenmaid pastry cutter blade to cut in the butter and shortening, then brought together by hand. Froze for one hour before baking and let sit 4 hours before slicing--was still watery, would consider a lattice top with more openings next time.  Nicely tart, family devoured.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody:
We were off to a good start by making the very first step, put the water in the freezer. Being a forgetful person I have cheated many times by mixing ice and water and using the cocktail strainer to get just ice water, but I feel silly about it every time.  
Many things here one can noodge one way or the other, or just follow the recipe.
When, after many pies, I figured out about rolling the flattened pastry onto the pin to move it to the pan it decreased my life stress incredibly.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: Long ago I learned that substituting chilled vodka for some of the cold water yielded a fantastic, flaky crust. I haven't looked back! The vodka helps in shaping the crust, but evaporates on baking, so the crust is never "doughy" or wet. Try it!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: I opt for straight rhubarb (who needs strawberries in this delicious pie?) but I've always had trouble with the filling becoming too liquidy. This year I tried maserating the rhubarb with the sugar. It yielded a lot of juice, which I thickened then added back to the mixture before baking. The pie turned out perfect.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: @S. Marie Fanny Flags recipe uses a scoop of flour in filling which Ive adopted to help with sogginess. Also a sprinkle/rough coveringof turbinado sugar at bottom of crust before adding filling carmelizes and helps for a barrier btwn crust and filling

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody:
I nake flaky dough with 1 part unsalted butter and 2 parts flour. I mix everything by hand as noted in this recipe and then add water . The key is not to overwork the dough so it is easy to roll. Also I let the dough sit for a few days: as it dries, it makes the dough flakier as a result.
Also, I never add sugar in the dough even for fruit pies. The slight saltiness highlights the sweetness in a pleasant way.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody:
Most recipes for pie crust call for ice water, not merely cold water.  Toss an ice cube into your measuring cup, scoop it out before adding to dough mixture. I make my dough in a food processor, which requires just a bit more water -- the dough should roll neatly into a smooth ball in the carafe.

I make my mom's rhubarb cream pie, which has a custard filling of egg, sugar, and flour, with just a whisper of nutmeg.  Best ever!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: Did you miss the part where it says to put the water in the freezer?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: I'm another who has been making pie dough a very long time.  When I broke my wrist I discovered that pie dough was better made in a food processor.  Cold butter, flour, zap until smaller crumbles, add cold water, pulse until a bit of clumping.  Then either use immediately or rest in refrigerator.  I roll out on a stainless steel counter top, which is naturally cold, fold over and pop over pie, unfold to cover other half.  If I am making a fruit pie I put sugar in with the flour to roll the dough

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: I agree with other commenters, cut the sugar in half.  If you have good fruit you don't need as much -- and it hides the tartness of the rhubarb. Haven't done it yet but plan to exchange better and shortening with lard.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody:
I'm with Tom.  I love rhubarb.  I don't think strawberries add anything. 
But...to each their own.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: I’m in agreement with Tom (see 6 days ago)—why mess with the purity of fabulous rhubarb by including the strawberries?!?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: I used half of a blood orange instead of a clementine or mandarin and it was delicious. The crust was crispy and the roasted strawberries created a lovely flavor!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody:
This looks a whole lot like the pie dough recipe that came with the Cuisinart food processor. I think it asks for a quarter teaspoon of baking powder, which I’ve been doing.  In any event the processor works pretty well. Recently, I got a dough paddle for my KitchenAid mixer and tried that. it went a little fast and was hard to control.  Or just use two knives like I learned in cooking class in 1958 or so. 

Pie crust is simple. just roll it out by eye to the thickness you want, which will likely be quite a bit larger than the pan. And if you have parchment paper over it to keep it from sticking to the rolling pin, just roll it back on the rolling pin with the paper and use it to move it to the pan. 

And yes, I use ice water. I measure the vegetable shortening by putting water in a measuring cup, and then adding the shortening. Then I just throw a few ice cubes and some water in the measuring cup for later use.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: I add a handful of blueberries and use orange zest.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: I made this recipe but subbed 2 T. Tapioca because of an allergy to corn products. I also added 2 T. Matzah meal. The pie was 100% fabulous. No more having to use a straw to eat this pie! The juices firmed up perfectly. Roasting the strawberries was also a big improvement. But for my oven, 375 was hot enough. The berries may burn at higher temp.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: Best way to hull a strawberry is to use a drinking straw (Starbucks works well!) insert into the base of the strawberry and out of the top removing the hull and leaves cleanly and completely. Fast easy and effective! I do this as soon as I get my strawberries and they seem to last longer too.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody:
@Manda AZ
A strawberry huller is a pretty terrific tool. Very easy and effective. Takes up almost no kitchen space and is inexpensive.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody:
I use Minute Tapioca, which I grind to a powder in a spice grinder, as a thickener when I make strawberry rhubarb pie (and other fruit pies).  It works reasonably well, although there is guesswork as to how much to use.
I note no thickener is listed in this recipe.  Does baking the strawberries remove all of the extra juice or will I still need some thickener to prevent a runny pie?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: David - it does call for cornstarch which is a thickener.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: 1/4 cup cornstarch in the recipe

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: Spring Pansy & David are right, but I found the 1/4 cup cornstarch wasn't nearly enough. I have yummy crust & rhubarb sauce.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: I used to add leaf lard to the butter for crispier crusts. Over time, I began increasing the amount of butter until I cut out the leaf lard altogether. There is nothing better than a pure butter crust! They are flaky too, if handled gingerly. Also, instead of ice water I pour 1/3c of vodka over a small bowl of ice. The vodka evaporates from the crust as it bakes, so the crusts don’t get glutinous and pasty.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: I love roasted strawberries, they are very versatile in baking. However they are so tasty that I usually take a three pack of berries from the market, roast them up & eat them with yogurt for a mid afternoon treat and forget to bake with them.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: Passing on this as IMHO mixing rhubarb and strawberries in pies wrecks the purity and goodness of both a rhubarb pie and a strawberry pie.  Sigh

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: I want to know … if anyone who has made this has actually been able to fit 4 pounds of fruit into this pie!! I made a strawberry rhubarb pie yesterday and could barely fit 2 pounds of fruit in a 9 inch, deep dish pie!!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: I found the same. Made the filling as per the recipe and then had to divide it between 2 pies.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: Followed the recipe, weighed the fruit. Added some tapioca because I thought the filling looked to watery. Even with the extra tapioca, way too watery. Flavor, very nice.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: I hate rhubarb, would it ruin the pie to leave it out? That is to say, does the rhubarb thicken or thin or do something else to the filling? I want to try roasting the berries!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: This is insanely good. Followed recipe exactly but used all butter for the crust.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: My husband has a commercial greenhouse and grows strawberries. He also gets a lot of rhubarb from customers to make strawberry - rhubarb jam. I was excited to try this pie recipe because we have plenty of fruit. I will not make it again. I measured the fruit which barely fit in the pie plate. The roasted strawberries lost all their wonderful sweetness. The rhubarb did not cook during the bake and there was A LOT of juice in the pie plate after baking. I bake fruit pies often and have never experienced this. I probably did something wrong but wanted other bakers to offer their advice to bakers who are thinking of trying this recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: I can't eat rhubarb, due to the high level of oxalates (I'm prone to oxalate kidney stones, and rhubarb is one of the worst offenders), so I will try this with cranberries and roasted strawberries. I generally use Julia Child's recipe for piecrust, but I will try this one, although I will use the food processor, as Julia allows. I always keep butter and shortening in the freezer for piecrust.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: my berries disintegrated at 425!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: I made this as written and it was fabulous. One of the best crusts I've ever tried!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: I always wonder why people concentrate on  putting ice in water or water in the freezer when the rest of the ingredients are room temperature. If you’re trying to keep the overall temperature of the dough low, put the flour in the freezer before you start. And use frozen shortening (I strongly prefer butter). Those ingredients are,by far. the predominant mass of the pie crust and will keep temps low. I like grating the frozen shortening into the cold flour although consider 1/2 grating and 1/2 small pieces that I work by hand.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Rhubarb and Roasted Strawberry Pie Recipe
commentBody: My never-fail crust recipe is 2.5 cups flour, 1 cup crisco, 1/2 tsp. salt for sweet 1 tsp. for savory, ice water. Cut shortening into flour and salt, add enough ice water to get a cohesive ball. Cover bowl with a plate and let it rest on the counter for 3-4 hours (or overnight in the fridge) to relax the gluten. Then roll as desired, fill and bake. The less you handle the dough the better. I love my zippered plastic circle to roll out the crusts, wipe it off and reuse it for years.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I’m actually planning to make this soon too, so I went down a bit of a rabbit hole researching the method and why it’s done this way. You’re totally right that active dry yeast usually gets bloomed in warm water first to dissolve the outer coating — but in this case, the recipe warms the buttermilk and water to around 100°F, mixes it with the flour and yeast, and rests it for an hour. That works like a sponge or pre-ferment, and it gives the yeast everything it needs to activate — warmth, moisture, and time.

This isn’t just a food writer’s riff, either — the recipe comes straight from Chef Sam Yoo at Golden Diner in NYC. It was developed for his restaurant and published by Bon Appétit. Not sure why this stuck in my brain, but I ended up researching it anyway. Turns out the method actually holds up. Looking forward to trying

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Ok, I actually made them! The whole shebang. A few thoughts:
- Yes, you can make the preferment the night before. I used instant yeast, didn’t bother warming the buttermilk, whisked it all together and threw it in the fridge, then took it out when I got up in the morning to warm up a bit. Was bubbly and beautiful. 
- Flipping isn’t as hard as you fear. Run a silicon spatula around the edges, then lift, put a flat spatula underneath, and flip with confidence. You can flip the pan and position it above the pancake to help. Worked fine in my (well-seasoned) mini cast iron, but be wary of the heat. 
- Don’t make these if you aren’t committed to adding LOTS of the toppings. Alone, they’re tasty but pretty bready. Drenching them in the syrup and butter make them sing. 
- That being said, if you make the preferment the night before, the pancakes themselves are really fast to come together. If you don’t have time in the morning to make the butter/syrups (though you could also do those the night before) I imagine that drenching them in honey, maple syrup, butter, and salt would achieve 80% of the effect, since the toppings are just different permutations of those ingredients. Don’t skip the compote, though, or at least some berries on top.

commentBody: Made exactly as directed, and these were def the best pancakes I’ve ever made. There’s a place in Brooklyn that also makes a very thick, fluffy pancake with a nut syrup and these are definitely a contender with those for best pancakes ever. Made all of the components, and they are so indulgent. Certainly not the easiest pancakes to make but worth it if you are just really craving an amazing pancake. My biggest tip is to choose skillets that are smaller and easy to flip with the flip of wrist as opposed to a utensil. There were a lot of comments about flipping the pancakes and in the directions, it seems clear that you need to flip the pancakes to brown on both sides and then you finish in the oven. That’s what I did and it worked well. I started with a larger skillet and that was too difficult so we moved to a smaller skillet and just use the spatula to loosen the sides and then quickly flip. I think you could probably also flip it into a plate and then flip it back into the pan if it’s too hard to do with the flip of the wrist, Enjoy!

commentBody: I made waffles with this batter and they turned out great. So fluffy!!! I also used fresh peaches and rhubarb for the compote.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The griddle/bake technique is fabulous. The maple/butter/honey/soy umami condiments are a fun variation.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Should that be instant yeast, rather than active dry yeast? Usually, active dry yeast has to be activated in liquid, while instant yeast can be mixed directly into the flour.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: According to King Arthur, they can be used interchangeably, although instant yeast takes less time to rise. KA prefers using instant all the time. Here's a link to a more in-depth article: https://www.kingarthurbaking.com/blog/2022/08/15/active-dry-versus-instant-yeast

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: can these be made on a griddle or waffle iron?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: these look quite intriguing - never had pancakes that are made with yeast. But, my family absolutely loves pancakes and maple syrup with bacon. (It's the only time we ever eat bacon). I wonder if I could make these and just omit the berry compote. Any thoughts?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @JMR Bacon AND berry compote!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: There is a video on YouTube (not an NYT one) that features this specific pancake the chef making it. Worth a watch if you are curious about the technique! Search golden diner pancake.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I have had these with confectioners sugar piled on and lemon wedges squeezed to 'melt' that sugar. Really lifts the entire process.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Recipe looks delicious. I've made the Hellbender's Masa Harina pancakes and they
are also wonderful.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Honestly, I'm a pretty good cook and this were hugely disappointing.  As in I would never make these again and I wouldn't go out of my way to eat these anywhere.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was worth the time and effort! Others mentioned that the pancake itself was flavorless. I don’t think so - it had a yeasted flavor and light, fluffy interior, which the toppings made them extra yummy. The honey butter was seriously the best, and I only used kerrygold salted butter in this recipe, bc it’s superior to unsalted. I still even added the salt from the recipe when called for. I didn’t have buttermilk and made my own with yogurt, milk, and a little bit of vinegar. I only have 10” pans and didn’t fill them to made the pancakes so that might have affected the pancake rise in the pan but they were still very fluffy. Even my 5yo ate them and he never eats pancakes w/o chocolate chips.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I thought this would be a nice addition to a party I was attending.  I thought it would be fussy and I was not looking forward to making it.  I was shocked at easy and fast it went.  I was done before I knew it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: They are really good but having had them at the restaurant, I will stick to having them there. They are a lot of work and just didn't taste quite as good as the real thing. That being said, the maple-honey butter is SO good and something I will continue to make for biscuits, waffles, french toast, etc!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I wasn't too impressed with these pancakes on their own. I found them bland and bready. I did not make the compote or the syrup, but I did make the honey butter, which helped somewhat with the flavor. The cooking method worked as described, but I would not make this recipe again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Fun to make and turned out well, but not worth the effort, because nobody in my family enjoyed them much. My partner thought they were too salty, I didn’t enjoy the mixing of honey and maple- perhaps down to the flavour of the particular honey we bought. I did buy a tiny pan specifically to make these, and look forward to trying other puffy pancakes. The flavour of the yeast and puffiness of the cakes themselves was great. Compote was perfect- will have leftovers with that & maple syrup.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I just made this! I subbed gluten free flour (bobs). Instead of following the directions, I didn’t realize I wasn’t supposed to add all of the flour. Then needed extra buttermilk to make it into a thick paste. Refrigerated it overnight and pulled everything together quickly in the morning. Didn’t have berries so I didn’t make the compote.

This sub works great for GF cause it gives the flour time to properly hydrate. These came out perfect! Just like the picture and great texture and flavor. Only have stainless steel pans but it did not stick to a well warmed and preheated pan and flipped with ease with just a spritz of olive oil in the pan.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made exactly as written. Not nearly worth the bowls and wait.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Glad I tried it!
The actual pancake is very bread-y and heavy and without all the butter, buttery syrup, and sugary compote wouldn’t stand up on its own. That said, it’s a pretty meal and more like shortcake dessert. My barbaric teenage son loved it. 
Next time I’ll make the compote and return to a lighter and fluffier buttermilk pancake recipe, though.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I've made these twice. Ate them once fresh and then made them in advance on a Saturday evening to have for a Sunday brunch the next day. Everything stores well in the fridge and reheats very easily the next day. Warm the pancakes in the oven, then reheated the syrup and the compote on the stove and served everything warm. I feel like it was just as good.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I added the zest of the lemon and juice from the lemon to the compote.  Amazing pancakes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I watched a YouTube video about their pancakes, but in it, they said they don't flip, and instead just cook in the oven until done. So now I'm not sure which to believe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @When and to all those wondering whether you flip the pancakes before putting them in the oven, you do here! At the restaurant, they flip after they set the top under a salamander, but they also have a lot of pans and a professional salamander, which allows more temperature control than a home broiler, so I adapted the recipe for home cooks and kitchens with Sam Yoo’s tips and sign off. In this method, you use a spatula to turn the pancake over on the stovetop to brown both sides then transfer the pancake to the oven to cook through.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These were incredible and I can’t wait to make them again. I ate 2 pancakes! What an amazing combination of flavors.  I had the pan a bit too warm for the first pancake and also made it too big but got the hang of it quickly.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These have great texture and the honey soy sauce syrup was really nice. But I think the batter needed something else- maybe vanilla? I tasted the pancake on its own and it was pretty blah.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I loved the pancakes. Next time, I'll simplify the toppings as the honey butter curdled and the syrup (pure maple) separated out.  

But adored the fluffiness and found I was able to make a couple on the griddle since the batter was thicker than expected and held it's shape easily on a griddle.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Yeast?  Assuming that is proofed with the buttermilk but it’s not mentioned.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It's listed in step 1, add yeast and 1 cup of flour to a bowl, heat the buttermilk with 1/4 cup of water until warm 100° whisk with flour and yeast until smooth, cover with plastic wrap for 1 hour.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The yeast is added to the first cup of flour, then mixed with the buttermilk (and some water) to create a pre-ferment. See Step 1.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
The advice to switch steps 2-3-4 to 4-3-2 is good advice.  
I only had a few berries so I added frozen peach dice for the compote.
Too many bowls!  Mix the 2nd dry ingredients together then just add that to the pre-ferment bowl, then add the eggs and oil directly into the pre-ferm bowl and mix.
I pre-fermented and preped everything the afternoon be
A complicated recipe that doesn't  need to be complicated, hence the 4 rating.  The pancakes are delicious, but worth it, first thing in the morning?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I enjoy yeast waffles so I thought I would give this a try. Maple, honey butter was fantastic and the lemon zest was spot on.the batter was thicker than I thought it should be but it cooked in the time specified. Texture and flavor was there as well.  I just prefer a good old fashioned buttermilk pancake.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: oh gosh this is soooooo good.  We made this for a lazy vacation breakfast and it was next level.  the butter maple syrup was incredible and worth making.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: 2 hours for pancakes?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can a similar effect be achieved with sourdough starter instead of yeast?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I was dying to make these for a while, but when I did, I found they weren’t worth the extra effort. They taste less like pancakes and more like bready-cake. The syrup was nice, though.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Absolutely delicious! The pancake itself has so much flavor! Got 2 nice size pancakes using two 8” skillet pans. I halved the butter and syrup recipe and it was more than enough for the 2 pancakes. Could have even halved the compote but will use the leftovers anyway. Def time consuming but was so fun to make on a 13degree freezing NY Saturday morning!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
WOW! These are amazing. A hassle, so celebration food in our house, but definitely worth it. A quick word in portions:
> I doubled the pancake recipe, and it made six large pancakes (in the pan sizes recommended). I probably put in 1-1.25 cups of battery per pancake.
> made a single portion of the butter, more than enough
> made a single portion of the (delicious) syrup; it was just enough
> made 1.5 of the berry compote, it was just enough (could’ve made a double and people would’ve eaten it)

Big success overall!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Sbeesh.  Maple-honey / Honey-maple ????   Why on earth would anyone want to dilute 100% pure maple syrup?  Skip this step, avoid any American maple-ish product with corn, cane or any other cheap filler, and enjoy the awesome pancakes as prescribed.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Might these be made with gluten free flour?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Why use unsalted butter and then add salt?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Tom S. Most recipes call for unsalted butter. It allows you to better control the amount of salt in a dish. The saltiness of some butters can vary, so it’s a more precise way of doing it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Since both waffles and King Arthur have been mentioned, I note that the technique of preferment / poolish is shared with KA’s overnight sourdough waffles. Mutatis mutandis, an overnight ferment in the fridge or at cool room temperature would work fine, indeed producing a sharper tang.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can you use this batter for waffles?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I didn't test this batter in my waffle maker, but I imagine it'd work. I'll try it out soon and am excited to hear from anyone who has tried it!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Has anyone successfully made these with GF flour? If so, please share what kind and how much

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The Bon Appetite video shows how they make it in the restaurant. They use xanthum gum for the syrup and also use an ice bath for the compote. The NYtimes recipe is probably simplified for home cooks.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made these as written, although I used instant yeast. I'm going to second what several others have said in that these are a project and while the pancakes are thick and fluffy, they're pretty bland. The magic is in the toppings. Make them the night before, along with the preferment, and pile them on with wild abandon! For me, I'm planning to make our regular family pancake recipe which comes together quickly, and use every last drop of the syrup, butter, and compote on those.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: To avoid having to wait an extra hour for pancakes, what do we think about prepping the pre-ferment the night before and refrigerating?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It might over-proof and be flat instead of fluffy. But you should try it and come back to report your findings.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I made the pre-ferment the night before -- it doubled in size but then fell during the night, which is unusual for a pre-ferment. The pancakes definitely didn't rise as high as in the picture, though they seemed to me to be of average fluffiness. 

I'm going to stick with Fanny Farmer's recipe, which you can mix up in a few minutes: 1 c flour, 2 tsp baking powder, 2 tbsp sugar, 1/2 t salt, 3/4 c milk, 1 egg and 2 tbsp melted butter.  (For cornmeal pancakes, use 1/2 c flour and 1/2 c cornmeal.)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made the preferment the night before! Didn't warm anything up and it was super bubbly the next morning.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Didn’t have any yeast. Added two teaspoons of baking powder (which eliminated many of the steps). Only made the maple honey butter not the syrup. Made the compote from frozen berries in the microwave and turned out perfectly. It was divine even with the shortcuts.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: What benefit is there to making the butter into 'football' shapes?  It seems to add to the load of prep when things are coming to a point where butter,  no matter the shape, will soon be melted.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It's pretty.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Any approximation on how much batter (roughly, I know it depends on pan size!) is 1/3"? Half cup?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Great recipe, but I have lots of leftover batter. What’s the best option to store it. Should I cook the pancakes and refrigerate or just store the batter as is?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: There is lemon zest on the this. How do we add that flavor? Actually, this is the only reason I love these pancakes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If I'm understanding your question correctly, you should be able to simply grate some lemon zest and add it with the other toppings at the end.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My version on the app on my iPhone, definitely lists a lemon in the ingredients, and under instructions at the end, to zest the lemon on top and then serve.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It didn't initially. It was added later.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Really good.  Made in my 8” cast iron.  Flipped so didn’t need to finish in oven and was tasty.  Agree with other commenters - needs a little vanilla but the sauces/butter are the bomb.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: How do I make these without butter or dairy?? Maybe olive oil instead? Ughh so sad I can't have these.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: There are many fine vegan butter substitutes you could try, and it's fairly simple to make buttermilk from any milk substitute (such as soy or almond milk). :)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @C.A.M try it with a good margarine or vegan butter substitute and report back!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Has anyone tried something like these with a sourdough discard/starter?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I haven't tried this with sourdough starter, but here are two pancake recipes that use sourdough starter/discard: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1018025-sourdough-pancake-or-waffle-batter https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1018025-sourdough-pancake-or-waffle-batter Both would taste great with all the toppings here!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Tried these pancakes without the maple-honey butter or compote using a griddle set to 350 °F. With about 5 minutes of total cooking time, flipping after 3 minutes, and put them in the oven for a couple minutes. The pancakes had a nice texture - fluffy and sturdy and thick even without using a pan. Bland without the butter and other condiments. Didn't care for the soy sauce flavor. Might try again using butter instead of oil.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Gf flour possible?

commentBody: Has anyone tried this recipe with a waffle maker?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Several have commented on it, below, and said it works great!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Has anyone tried these with gluten free flour? Would they work?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Buttermilk is something I never buy. Can I use a substitute without spoiling this recipe? 
Usually I sub with milk that has been mixed with a bit of lemon juice, cider vinegar or plain yogurt and left to sit for a bit. 
Thoughts? 

This recipe looks pretty special and also time consuming. The ingredients other than the buttermilk are everyday items for me, though, so it could be made on a whim when I have the time. 

Thanks for your thoughts.

Co

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Another alternative to buttermilk, which I picked up from the King Arthur Flour site, is 1/3 plain yogurt to 2/3 milk. I use nonfat versions of both. Works fine in their buttermilk and sourdough pancake recipes, as well as in their yeasted waffle batter.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Vinegar and milk works well. Or vinegar and soy milk. It will make your pancakes extra fluffy, too!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It took some time, but well worth it. I used half the butter for the syrup and honey-maple butter, and it worked very well. The pancakes by themselves don't need lots of flavor because the butter, syrup, and compote are super rich. So, combined them all in each bite. They were a big hit at home, and they will be in our rotation. Next time, I will make the yeasted dough the night before and leave in the fridge. And the butter can be made ahead of time as well. We ate the leftover compote the next day, and it was good. So, many of the steps can be done ahead of time. Can't wait to make them again!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The lemon, and tip to zest it over all, is there now.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If it doesn't have vanilla in it, it can't be better than mine!  My not-so-secret ingredient in pancakes and waffles for years.  Lauded by my around-the-world visitors.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can I make the entire batter up to step 6 the night before or just up to the pre-fermented portion in step 1?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’ve been to GD many times and these are spot on - but cooked in a hot pan in the oven the entire way - they’re light and fluffy. I added the zest no compote bc that’s the OG way to have these and it’s delish. So glad I don’t have to keep recipe testing.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Five minutes over “medium low” heat to reach 100 degrees?  I’m stymied by the 2nd sentence of this recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The pancakes were absolutely delicious! I added the lemon zest to the syrup.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is absolutely fantastic! There is a bit of work to this, but the first bite quickly erases any of that. I love pancakes and have used my grandmother's recipe for decades. These are in a dead heat with those..maybe slightly ahead. I will leave out the salt in the syrup as there is a noticable taste from batter, butter, syrup combination.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It’s criminal how good these pancakes are! Legitimately the best pancakes I’ve ever had. And I even halved the recipe since cooking for one. Can’t wait to make them again!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Great recipe! I did leave the pre-ferment for longer than an hour and it still turned out deliciously 🤤

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’m about to make some pancakes. My recipe will seem sooo much easier now, compared with this one!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This sounds so fire

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I don’t even like thicc pancakes…but these are LITERALLY the best pancakes I’ve ever had. I even burnt some and they were still better than any ordinary pancake.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My only problem with these was not having enough pans to make them in so the first four that I made ended up being more like crackers from spending too much time in the oven. But the last few were still fluffy and the overall flavor was pretty good. They are definitely “eat as you make them” pancakes. A bit time consuming so I think I’ll just mark it as cooked and remember enjoying them for the most part.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Living in Spokane, WA I don’t anticipate being able to go by Golden Diner any time soon, so I am very thankful NYT Cooking published the recipe! I just made a 1/2 batch which resulted in 2 pancakes that did not fill the bottom of the 8” pan completely. I had good success with a small, well seasoned cast iron pan. I skipped the fruit compote as I don’t typically like fruit on my pancakes, and in a video I saw about Golden Diner other people order them without as well. However, the butter and syrup are so unique to these pancakes I feel it would be a mistake to skip them and opt for just plain butter and maple syrup. These are so big I could barely eat one, but I don’t have much of an appetite anyway. I somehow forgot the lemon zest at the end, which I will have to be sure to add when I eat that 2nd pancake. Yes, they are a little finicky, but other than the little extra planning for the preferment, but are not much more complicated to make than other pancakes (not counting the accompaniments).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Mine came out great, but at 1/3 inch thick i only had enough batter for 3 pancakes

And the ones in the photo look closer to 1 inch thick - next time I will double the recipe and make them thicker.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Wow! This is a true special occasion dish. Don’t skip a step. They all make a difference.

Next time I will make these in smaller skillets because several of us ended up cutting them in half to eat less. But they’re so pretty round. 

And I will make all the parts in advance, including the pancakes, I think and warming and assembling at the last minute.

I also think these would be amazing desserts, made smaller individual size.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Best pancake recipe ever tried!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These thick “pancakes” taste like cornbread! Not worth the time or effort, especially if you live in the South (surely I’m not the only one?).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Does anyone have good advice on a way to get an even golden smooth surface when putting batter into the pan? I can somewhat shape the non cooking surface and smooth it a bit but for the initial scoop onto the cast iron it’s proving to be difficult to get that even golden appearance?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Followed the recipe to a T. Had to add a little bit more liquid to the batter at the end. Sprinkle the top with Malden salt before serving. Over the top delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These were a project but delicious! It only made four but were very filling (neither me or my husband could finish our 2 stack!). To save time, I’d definitely try proofing overnight and making honey butter and compote ahead of time. Scooping the right amount and flipping took a little finesse, I did too much on the first one and the last one ended up too small.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Made these today for the first time! Very tasty and a good way to use up spare yeast.

My only tip would be to NOT preheat the pans super hot in advance. My pancakes burned this way (but were just as good with that bit cut off). The batter seemed to be forgiving and cooperative to cook a little slower and lower like regular pancakes. when I flipped my second pancake I put the heat down lower and obtained a normal textured, beautiful golden brown exterior.

I didn't realize they required multiple small diameter frying pans so I improvised last second with an 8" wide 2qt stock pot and le creuset 8" frying pan. Next time I think I'll use just one pan at a time and cook in batches, or test to see if it's really necessary to use a small pan as a form.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This involved a lot of bowls, utensils and steps. All the accoutrements were great. The pancakes were not. The batter was not loose enough to spread and round in the pan. The pan should not be as hot as the directions indicate unless you want a very dark pancake before you’re done trying to spread the batter in the pan. The pancakes were unfortunately stiff, dense and heavy which is not how I like my pancakes. The syrup, butter and compote improved them but we could each only eat one. It was disappointing after all the time and effort.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These were great but the instructions are not very orderly, making it much hard to understand what to do

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This batter has the consistency of bread dough. On the whole I don’t think this was worth the trouble

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These pancakes were luscious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: People who said the pancakes were too cakey or bready - maybe it was how the flour was measured? I weighed the ingredients and the texture was different from my usual pancakes; however 100% delicious. I even used GF flour.  My go to for GF is Chef Alina and she uses a standard 130 grams for 1 cup of all purpose flour - just like this recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Dawne Did you use her flour blend or a commercial blend? I keep King Arthur and Bob’s Red Mill (both 1 to 1) on hand, but should have Chef Alina’s recipe somewhere.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’ve made these twice and they’re worth the effort. While the pancakes are phenomenal, what I truly appreciated were the leftover butter and the syrup sitting in my fridge, waiting to elevate my every day buttermilk pancakes. If I never make these pancakes again, I plan the condiments on hand at all times.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This only works if you're having pancakes for dinner. I'm not getting up an hour early to wait for dough to rise and it doesn't take an hour to make maple honey syrup. What can they never say in breakfast recipes if you can start this the night before?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I fear I may have overcooked the actual pancake just a bit, but with all the glorious accoutrements this was still a 5 star pancake. I am excited to try again!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: As someone else has mentioned, these are definitely for dinner. I woke up early to make the pre ferment and went back to sleep, dreaming about the pancakes. Woke up without hitting snooze only to realize I had forgotten yeast right before I was ready to cook them. I doubled the soda and it turned out okay. Made a half recipe that made a single 8" round. Lacking a skillet, I used an 8"saucepan> center cooked in oven and top dried out> flipped to griddle with handle to brown the other side. Would recommend cooking both sides on the stove for the crisp, though the oven ensures it is cooked through.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can the preferment proof for more than an hour (like 2) or will that be too long?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: In the fridge, overnight.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Even better if you use whole grains

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: A lot of work, but fun results. The syrup is too salty though. I’d keep the soy for its color and depth of flavor and omit the extra salt in the syrup next time. I used unsalted butter for the honey maple butter but the butter could probably handle 1/3-1/2 the extra salt as well. I might try different flavorings next time as well. The yeasty pancakes were great though.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: First off, I don't own any non-stick pans anymore and failed in using my GreenPan for this recipe. Better luck using my larger Carbon Steel pans with a bit of butter too. I was the guinea pig this Saturday in making these without the compote or butter-slurring mixtures. This is great adding my RedStar Gold Yeast for pancakes and will use this more often. Plus tweaking as others have commented with lemon zest etc. My rating will increase as I become more proficient in execution. Cheers

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Very nice! My notes: Recipe makes 4 pancakes, so 1/4 the batter in the pan. On my range, burner to medium. When fully cooked, Internal temp. 160F.

commentBody: At the restaurant the berry compote is optional and an extra charge. So the pancakes should still be awesome without it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I loved these but came up with a simplified version:
-preheat the oven
-in a large bowl, microwave milk until lukewarm. Add 1T vinegar if you don’t have buttermilk. Add a splash of water, or 1 T yogurt for depth of flavor 
-add 1c flour, 2T sugar and 4t instant yeast to the milk. Whisk and let sit 10-60 mins
-in a small bowl, put 4T butter, 3T honey, 2 T maple syrup. Microwave 30 seconds then stir. 
-add in the remaining baking soda flour and salt. Cook as directed, serve with the honey butter.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Antonella you probably overstirred. The initial one should be well mixed, but when mixing the wet+dry+proof, you need to be very gentle, and slowly stir just enough so that there’s no more dry ingredients. If you stir too quickly or too much, you’ll puff the air out of your ferment, and overwork the gluten making them stronger ie chewier.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
There's no way this would have been a prep in the morning meal for me. So I prepared most of the ingredients the night before. That would be my strongest recommendation. The preferment I did that morning. I didn't find the flipping of the pancakes difficult. I'm not sure whether setting in the oven was necessary but I did it anyway. Overall I really liked how the pancakes turned out. They are definitely more dense than fluffy. But the special syrup, butter and compote do add nice layers of complexity. That being said, it is a lot of work just for pancakes. So while I could see myself making this exact recipe again it would be for a special occasion or if I just had a lot more time on my hands. 
Otherwise, I would just use elements of it--like just making the yeast pancakes with regular maple syrup. I could see someone making bigger batches of the syrup and butters for later use, however. I would think the butter, and probably the syrup, would freeze well. I probably would do that the next time I tried the full recipe.

Also forgot to add the honey to the batter but the glaze more than makes up for it. Muffins are plenty moist and soft without it. Overall delicious and forgiving recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The King Arthur Flour ingredient weight chart says 1/2 cup of wheat bran is 32 grams, therefore making 2 cups 128 grams. That's significantly less than the 160 grams this recipe says 2 cups is. Likewise, the 3/4 cup whole wheat flour according to the KA chart would weigh 85 grams, not the 100 grams indicated here. The buttermilk conversion seems closer (for 1/2 cup, KA says 113.5 grams and NYT says 116 grams.)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Lurlene I just bought some Bob's red mill wheat brand so I can make the muffins… But then I forgot to buy the oranges and the dates! Anyway, their packaging says a quarter cup is 15 g which means a cup would be 60 g and 2 cups would be 120 g. NYT recipes are usually much more accurate than this. i'm gonna go with the the weight on the package when I get the rest of my ingredients

author: Genevieve Ko
Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Somewhere in these online Cooking pages, I read that the NYT does not test the ingredient weights that they give in their recipes. Maybe someone can comment on this. I do notice that, from recipe developer to recipe developer, the weight of common ingredients, such as all-purpose flour, varies, sometimes considerably. I use the Bob's Red Mill ingredient weights.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I recently bought blackstrap molasses and understand why the recipe says not to use it - because blackstrap is triple reduced, higher in minerals, and lower in sugar, it can have a strong, bitter edge. It's tasty if you like a really pronounced, robust molasses flavor, but can easily overwhelm whatever it's added to. Look for regular, unsulphured (be aware there are also unsulphured blackstraps), or "sweet" "baking" molasses for a milder, sweeter flavor.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made six large muffins which kept them from potentially drying out. Each muffin was about 150 g or 5 ounces each. I also used a light colored muffin pan. I gave the bran-orange water mixture extra time to soften the bran. Also, after mixing, I placed the batter in the refrigerator for about 30 mins to get the leavening a head start. Finally, I baked the muffins at 400 degrees for five minutes then lowered the oven temperature to 375 for 20-22 minutes. Tip I learned from the author of The Scone Queen.
