commentBody: Had to order wheat bran online, but also useful for making granola and flat bread. This muffin has all the heartwarming flavors from my childhood but with the extra kiss of the syrup and citrus in the batter, these are stunning. Made them with my 4 yr old, and came together in a snap. Subbed in cutie clementines for the orange zest/juice and bc they were so tender, actual pieces made it into the mixture and syrup. No regrets!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
These were delicious.  I love the glaze.  I did not measure my ingredients by weight and I used wheat germ instead of bran.  I have found wheat germ and wheat bran interchangeable but not much easier to find one over the other.  

I used chopped apricots because it's what I had on hand. Another bran muffin recipe I follow, very similar to this one, uses dried fruits pureed after a soak in hot water.  You just add the puree to the batter.  Doesn't affect it one bit.  Very malleable recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are terrific. Made just as written using measuring cups, not weight and threw in a handful of craisins in the final mix. Yum! Having one more just to be sure my initial review is correct...yep, fantastic!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: if you can't find wheat bran, you can substitute all-bran cereal. let the cereal soak in the liquid for about 15 minutes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The flour in grams was a little off, but if you use measuring cups they come out delicious!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Used wheat germ and no glaze. Results were moist and delicious. Will make again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is one of those recipes that have a guise of healthfulness, but it still has 24 grams of sugar or about 2 tablespoons of sugar per serving.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I made these last night. I went by weight and yes the amount of bran was much more that 2 cups but I went with it anyway. I made a mistake with the honey and put the whole amount in the batter. It turned out better I think. They were awesome!!!
This morning there were only 4 left. Lol.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I followed the recipe as written, using the provided weights throughout and it made 12 regular sized muffins that are soft and moist.  They are a little orange-forward for my taste but it is lovely to have a bran muffin that really tastes like bran and is still almost fluffy. Next time I'm adding raisins. Need to use up that bag of Bob's Red Mill bran I ordered online. Plain bran is   nowhere to be found in my grocery store.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: in he article which contains this recipe it suggests soaking bran first.  How long?  3 coups water or what ratio?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Cbickster the soaking is described in step 1.  It’s not separate from the recipe, it’s part of the recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Any suggestions for substituting the canola oil? Trying to stay away from seed oils.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: why is that? I'm curious as I have used canola oil for 40 years and haven't heard of a recall.. now I'm worried.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I make bran muffins regularly - no pun intended - and get good results using olive oil.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Genevieve

Oops, I hit send by mistake: I think NYT had an article about whether seed oils really are bad to eat. It depends what your concerns are, environmental, nutritional, who you believe is a reliable source, etc. 

I don’t make my nutrition decisions based on the comments here bc I have people I know in my life who I trust more for that, and I’ve done my own reading over the years of sources I trust. 

This particular comment rankles me bc RFK is against seed oils and I can’t stand him. That’s my personal bias, so I suggest checking in with sources you know and trust, outside of the comments here.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @dhwsmith My go-to bran muffin recipe is from David Lebovitz, who in turn credits Nancy Silverton.   What makes it special is a) toasting the bran prior to a good soak and b) macerating the raisins and then whipping them into a puree that ensures a decadent moist muffin.  It's too much work sometimes but every once in a while when the fever hits....this recipe for me merely passable, but they work in a pinch.  I didn't use the syrup after reading the comments but perhaps that's necessary.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made a batch tonight and followed the recipe. Pretty easy to follow. Tastes good and they’re moist. The flavor is good but it’s missing something. Can’t quite figure out what…

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I have been using brown sugar, rather than honey (not a 1:1 sweetness swap).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Buy all- Bran Cereal, add a banana to the recipe on the box, enjoy. Genevieve Ko makes the best carrot cake in the world.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Try searching 6 weeks bran muffins. They use both bran and all bran. You keep the mixture refrigerated and bake as needed.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @lupi robinson my Aunt Pat (83) in Ohio makes bran muffins this way. She swears that they only taste good after the batter has ripened for a whole month at the back of her fridge. They are to die for. Talk about patience!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Couldn’t find wheat bran so used wheat germ. I doubled the recipe. The doubled recipe hasn’t been tested by NYT but it turned out fine. I was one muffin shy of two dozen. Very flavorful and moist but a lot of work for two pans of muffins. There was a very slight bitterness to the crumb and so I’ll toast the wheat germ next time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Super dry results.  Not worth the effort of using orange juice, molasses and honey to moisten the muffins.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I grind and sift whole wheat berries to get the bran and flour for the muffins.  The taste difference is marked.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made a mistake and put the orange juice in the muffins.  No problem, they were great. And they have kept very well in an airtight container. 20 seconds in a microwave and they are better than just out of the oven.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this exactly as written. Used Bob’s Red Mill bran. Not moist enough nor sweet enough for my taste

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Absolutely delicious.  I used a coarse bran and extra course whole-wheat flour (made in Ireland) as well as treacle and they turned out amazing.  Very moist and easy to make.  No need for glaze. One less step and they are sweet enough.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @ Genevieve, would be more helpful to see your post once you've made these. And no need to brace yourself for others' comments!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I tried making these muffins. They are disgusting. They are sticky and overly sweet. Bran muffins, which I make very often, should be hearty. Don't waste your time with this recipe. I ended up tossing them into the compost.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These muffins are really tasty...I substituted yogurt and milk for the buttermilk and raisins for the dates. The recipe makes more than 12 small size muffins so have another muffin tin ready.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are moist muffins, moister than my long-time Martha Stewart recipe, so a good improvement. We didn't find them too sweet, even though I typically reduce the sugar in Genevieve's sweet recipes by about 10%. However, there is a bitter taste, probably contributed to by the orange zest, so I would omit the zest. We also don't like cooked oranges much. Any comments on substituting (cow's) milk for the orange juice?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If you want moist bran muffins, these are an excellent. I have made these a few times and have modified them to suit my family's tastes. I used apple juice instead of orange juice, melted butter instead of oil, and 120 grams of wheat bran (2 cups as per the Bob's Red Mill bran package).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I loved this recipe. Bran muffins are getting so hard to find! This one was a near dupe to my favorite from the VIkor Benes bakeries inside Gelson's supermarkets in Southern California.  Unfortunately, my local store inconsistently stocks them. Made the recipe as written, weighing the bran but using a measuring cup for the whole wheat flour after reading other notes. The consistency was perfect. My oranges (from a neighbor's tree) were a little bitter, but that was my only complaint.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Like many others, I couldn't find wheat bran so substituted wheat germ which worked just fine. These are delicious and I'll definitely make them again. I think I may add some candied ginger next time, think that could work well here but not sure whether that would make them too sweet. Hmmm

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Just to be clear…I made these muffins and used what I had available in my pantry and not for any reason other than that.
I had the essentials, WW flour, wheat bran, honey. Used banana instead oranges, used dark corn syrup instead of molasses, few dates added raisins to make correct measurements. Was more generous with buttermilk than was asked. THEY TURNED OUT FABULOUS. 
Lol
Very forgiving recipe with delicious result. It is a keeper.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made a half batch and they just came out of the oven. I did not do the sauce on top as they are sweet enough already. I could not find wheat bran at either of the 3 supermarkets in my small town. I used Bob's oat bran instead. These are delicious and I will make them again soon.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made these using the reduced weights for the bran and flour as suggested in the comments and still found them to be slightly dry and delicate. I also felt like the dominant flavor was baking soda; if I were to make these again I would reduce the soda to 1t instead of 1.5t. That might help with the metallic flavor that some folks commented about, as well as the tops rising and spreading too much.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Is there a sub for molasses in a pinch?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Moist and delicious. I’ll be making these many more times. I found the wheat bran at wegmans in the cereal aisle.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can you freeze the muffins?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Kim
Yes. Using baking cups, fill the muffin pan with the batter. Put the pan on the freezer. When frozen solid, take the muffin cups and store them in a freezer bag. Good for a couple of months. When you want to bake, bake from frozen. Bake at the same temperature, but double the time more or less.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Tender, yes. But way too sweet. Discovered I much prefer an oat bran muffin.   The recipe in The Fannie Farmer Baking Book is reliable and tastes great.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I did not have molasses so subbed brown sugar. No whole wheat flour so used white.  Also used coconut oil and raisins.
 Did not bother with the syrup at the end.
They came out beautifully, and were nice in texture and feel. 
Not sure about the flavor - it was good but it felt like something was missing. Something that would have complimented the orange flavor better.
I would make them again, this time with molasses or maple syrup, and add in more raisins or dates than called for.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Alcyon something to compliment the flavor better…like omitting the coconut oil you added in, and using whole wheat flour and molasses that like the recipe states instead of substituting as you did?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: All of the measuring comments nearly scared me off! Result is  good using the King Arthur weights. Caution do not overfill! They will stick even with oiling.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wow, I've made these muffins twice and only noticed the second time around that I had used oat bran instead of wheat bran- both times!! The texture is softer and a bit denser, I think, than if wheat bran had been used -but delicious anyway. Next time I try it with wheat bran.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @gee wood - yup, I did it by mistake- used oat bran instead of wheat bran. The muffins were very good-just a bit softer and denser.  I used regular whole wheat flour so not sure how different the texture would be using  gluten free flour.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Suggest something other than orange juice please, as I am allergic. Thank you.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I followed the recipe as is. Delicious! Very moist and I loved the orange essence.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Very good bran muffins-- without the extra sweet stuff on top.  Though they were not quite cooked through even though they baked for 25+ minutes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I read all the comments before making this. Upon weighting the ingredients my results were the same as dhwsmith. I went with the cup measurement of wheat bran rather than the weight and added extra buttermilk. Also I added dates times two and used 1/3 cup maple syrup rather than a half cup. We liked the  esults were quite good.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I was fortunate to find a wheat bran that was the correct weight at 2 cups. I did add a little extra buttermilk and dates, walnuts too. Otherwise followed the recipe and was very pleased with results. Have shared with others and they are a hit. I used the extra orange sauce to flavor my plain yogurt- so so yummy.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Loved! made exactly as written (but halved the recipe) and very good flavor. I was thinking of skipping the glaze but it was delicious

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Did not add dates. Only had blackstrap molasses and they muffins were great.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I love these muffins. Has anyone tried to make it gluten free using oat bran and a GF flour? If so, any substitution in amount or other ingredients?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Three cups is a lot of dressing, particularly considering it can only last in the fridge for three days.  How many households do you know of that will go through the equivalent of one and a half standard bottles of salad dressing in three days?  I'd have to scale down to about 1/6 or less of what's here to make this usable for my home.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @NE I agree. On the same line as the “Yield,” and to the right, there is an ikon that enables halving or doubling the recipe. I would make even less than half but maintain the proportion of silken tofu to olive oil.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I would expect this would freeze well. Maybe in small portions, tightly and double wrapped; not in one big container...
I think the idea is that one may as well use the whole block of tofu, otherwise you are storing that opened in the fridge; same issue.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Judy. That only changes your preferred choice of measurement ( US or metric)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: From an experienced home cook who lived in Japan for 14 years: Neri goma's flavor is wildly different from that of tahini. Easy enough to make your own neri goma--just dry roast hulled white sesame seeds in a fry pan (it goes from done to burnt instantly--keep a close watch and stir frequently). Transfer to a mortar and pestle and crush till it turns into a paste (easiest to do this w/a Japanese style m & p). Can also use a small food processor.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Yes, thank you!  Neri goma is indeed a very different flavor profile and should be used in this recipe.   It is the same for gomadofu as well.   Too, I wish they would give the Japanese name for the Japanese ingredients in these recipes.   I have traveled to and/or lived in Japan for the past 30 plus years, and am a total fan of Elizabeth Andoh, who was teaching washoku practices and ingredients out of her Tokyo home before most of these chefs were born.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @June Weird because they do use neri goma at RVR. Maybe they figure it isn’t as available nationally as it is in major cities?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Why does it have to be consumed within three days?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Brian See the end of Step 1- assume that’s the safe length of time before bacterial growth ?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I was wondering the same thing!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Brian This contains raw garlic with little acid to counter the threat of botulism.  I don’t have experience with the other ingredients, but raw garlic in an anaerobic environment is potentially dangerous after a few days.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This makes 3 cups of dressing that keeps for only 3 days. Not a practical recipe for many folks.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Don’t miss the forest for the trees- I made this with reg old tahini and white miso, halved the recipe with a whole clove of garlic. I didn’t tell my fam what was in it and they all guessed blue cheese. It was delish and nutrish so we loaded it up on the greens with very little leftover. I highly recommend. Served with romaine, radicchio, parsley, Parmesan shavings and a drizzle of lemon juice. I will use this in a variety of ways.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Just made this…it’s…ok…i made it exactly as writ; did have barley miso (i cook w it often enough), but the more rustic kind from South River. The dressing is similar to a decent homemade mayonnaise or aioli. I frequently make a dinner or lunch from a lot of steamed vegetables and aioli / romesco / etc; this would be an ok substitute because a lot less oil than aioli, if one is working on trimming down a little. Maybe add a little citrus (yuzu if you’re keeping to the theme) and / or some dried hot pepper.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @robert melater agreed I kept adding miso tahini oil even mirin - blah

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: How does it hold up to freezing?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Yikes! Nearly every comment concerns the yield of this recipe or its shelf life. Almost no comments discuss the recipe itself. I cannot wait to make it exactly as Genevieve Ko has written it. 

P.S.: If this recipe yields too much for you to use within three days, use your third grade math division skills to make the amount that suits you.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: For people concerned about the quantity being too much, why not use it as a salad dressing one day and a dip for crudités etc the next, or add it liberally to some grain bowls or roasted veggies? It’s pretty protein- and nutrient-dense, so I’d eat it in more generous amounts. I plan on making it this weekend and foresee no issue with using it all within three days in our two-person household! If we like it as much as I think we will, I’ll happily plan the next few meals around it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Marmie Just another note to say that a box of Mori Nu silken tofu is about 10 ounces now thanks to shrinkflation. So that’s another way to make less! I scaled the miso, vinegar, salt and and olive oil back by about a third and left the rest the same, then ended up adding a little more sesame paste and salt at the end. It’s really nice.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Sounds delicious. I am still waving the pro-vowel flag proudly. Just stop calling River RVR.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @LA in BNA Yes to vowels! Or maybe we are simply not cool enough.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: to avoid wasting tofu if making a smaller portion, divide it and stash the extra in the freezer. That improves the texture, too.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
In the tradition of the great Nero Wolf, I make our salad dressing every day, right before serving.  Not sure where the recipe came from.
For one 2 TBL serving, in a 2-oz OXO measuring cup:
-1 TBL olive oil 
-1/4 TSP dry ground mustard, like Colman's
-1/4 TSP Trader Joe's Chili Lime Seasoning Blend
-1 TBL lemon or lime juice (scant, just enough to fill to the 2 TBL line in the OXO cup.

stir well with the measuring spoon and serve immediately

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Given that miso is quite salty I bet you could get away with considerably less salt if you want to reduce the sodium content.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Richard I reduced the salt to 1/2 tsp. That’s still plenty salty.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Leave out the garlic when you make the dressing. Add garlic to the serving of dressing when you use it. Or, in the morning, portion out the dressing for tonight into a small container and add garlic, which will let the garlic infuse.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is spectacular, fully flavored yet light.  It is more sauce-like than vinaigrette-like and that quality, coupled with the lightness, would make it not at all difficult to use up three cups in three days. Note that Ko comments she can eat it by the spoonful without salad.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Anchovy crumbs.  I tin King of Prussia in EVO.  Drain oil into skillet.  Add 2T EVO. Chop all anchovies fine and cook over low/medium heat until anchovies dissolve.  Add 1.5 C Panko and stir until they are light golden brown.  Cool on a plate and add black pepper and finely grated parm if you like.  They are delicious.  I freeze them in a glass jar.  They pretty much last forever.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Elkmtnman THANK YOU! I’ll try your technique with both panko and stale sourdough crumbs.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It has truly changed my life.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I only saw 2 comments from people who actually made this. One positive the other meh. I’d love to see actual reviews of this recipe before going out and buying all the ingredients.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Based on the negative comments, I will not be trying this recipe. Also, I don’t think of this as a place to say anything except how it turned out for you. Why do people have a need to say something. 
Make it, rate it. Suggest a change. Thank you.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Mary Rail I reduced the salt to 1/2 tsp, used 1/3 cup EVOO, and tahini instead of sesame paste. It is delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Mary Rail I was so excited for a creamy dressing and bought all the ingredients, assuming I would make it multiple times, but I can barely eat one salad with it. Meh.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Unlike most of the commenters here who are complaining rather than reviewing, I actually made this recipe last night and it was delicious. I served it over a salad of lentils, carrots and radicchio. I can imagine a ton of other uses for it including in my lunchtime grain bowl or as a dressing g for a sheet pan full of roasted veggies. Scale down if you don’t want so much, and note the author’s comment about why she suggests it only keeps for three days (a flavor issue, not a good safety issue).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Very good and versatile dressing. I used dried granulated garlic instead of fresh.
If you make with traditional barley miso the dressing will be brown, not white as in the photo. If you want a white dressing use milder white miso.
I tossed with cold brown rice ramen noodles, fried tofu, cucumbers, scallions and edamame. Also good on romaine with shredded carrots, radishes and crispy chickpeas and sourdough croutons. Toss it with the salad rather than pour it on like bottled dressing.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I frequently make salad dressing with silken tofu, but always blanch the tofu first because Isa Chandra Moskowitz recommends blanching silken tofu—simmering for 2–3 minutes followed by an ice bath—to remove any "beany" or "cardboard" taste before using it in recipes, particularly for sauces, dips, and dressings.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I have found that Mori Nu has little to no taste, particularly after other ingredients are added. More perceptive palates may disagree.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The many times I visited Japan I noticed that there was seldom if ever any garlic in the food .  And since I no longer can eat garlic Japanese food has replaced my many favorites here in the United States., I am surprised to see garlic in this recipe, and I am wondering whether this is an American edition or do they actually make it this way in Japan.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made half a recipe and it worked great. I didn’t have tahini so I skipped it, but it would have been good.  I added Ponzu, toasted sesame oil and lemon juice, cause I have them.  Easy to be creative.  Loved being able to use leftover tofu.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I didn't find the amount to be too much - I *smothered* the greens in the dressing the way you would for an old-fashioned caesar, and it was perfect. Worked out that used it up in 2 days with 2 heads of lettuce.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The dressing was good but the salad assembled as directed was excellent. I am vegan and this scratched the itch for good Caesar salad! I toasted panko in garlic and olive oil and used Rebel’s vegan Parmesan.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @livcooks thanks for this. Did you use a substitute for the honey? My go-to is always maple syrup.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The NYT headline said this was a salad dressing “that would change your life”. I have made it, and my life has not changed yet. How long does that take? I will say this though: this dressing is a wonderful replacement for mayonnaise. It’s higher protein and lower fat than mayo. I used it in a chicken salad (shedded rotisserie chicken, halved red grapes, celery, and apple pieces). I will use it for coleslaw and potato salad, also. It’s wonderful on top of roasted vegetables. 3 cups is a lot of dressing, but its uses are nearly infinite.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Erica Lee There are no icons on my page that give directions for making a smaller amount. I’m not complaining, I’m just sayin’.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Sue A
It is apparently only available on the app.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Used this as a sauce for a quick 'n dirty dinner of roasted frozen broccoli, butternut squash and  chickpeas. Very nice. Next time I'll be sure to use the anchovy crumbs, I could imagine how much even nicer with it. I will try freezing this, curious about the result.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: For all the complainers here about the amount of dressing this recipe makes, just select the 0.5x option and get on with your life.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Actually that option is only on the mobile app, not the website in Chrome.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
This is a delicious salad dressing, and I appreciate the protein boost from the tofu! We eat mostly plant-based so I'm always looking for ways to up our protein intake. This dressing turns a salad into a meal with protein.

It has a really great flavor. I served it over Napa cabbage and other Asian greens, with Tamari-Sesame roasted cauliflower on the side. 

Yes, the amount made in this recipe is a lot, but it's pretty easy to cut the recipe in half.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: just wow! We usually thicken our salad dressing by using avocado or emulsifying oil with water e.g. in lemon juice, but I can't believe I've never thought of using silken tofu! This is awesome!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This looks like it would be really good on roasted vegetables (cauliflower, cabbage, roots).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The second I saw this recipe I knew I wanted to make it to serve over roasted root vegetables. Now my diet revolves on what I can eat with this dressing. Which is still pretty much everything. So if you're worried about three cups being too much, don't. Or use powdered garlic. Don't let me tell you how to live your life.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made it, although with white miso and tahini. I find myself in the odd position of not know what it is *supposed* to taste like. It’s mild, looks remarkably like mayonnaise but looser, but it doesn’t scream salad dressing to me. Since my wife ordered this to be made, I’ll have to wait and see what she thinks.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Just made this as written. Meh. Lots of tweaks will be required I think.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
This sounds great. 

It does make more than I can use, so I'll make a half recipe. And have a couple of tofu sandwiches.

Easy-Peasy.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
One needs two tablespoons of salad dressing per salad. What is one to do with the equivalent of three bottles of salad dressing??? Can it be frozen? 

Very surprised the recipe wasn't reworked from the industrial amounts needed in a restaurant or wedding catering setting to that needed for a family or couple.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: A way to extend the fridge life might be to mix all ingredients and add the crushed garlic ad hoc.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I. made this because of the rave writeup, than tasted and thought meh. My wife however raved about it and we took to a pizza dinner the next night and three of the six people wanted the recipe. Much better on the second day and I'll definitely make again. Did not know anything about all the raw garlic comments and I had wondered why only good for three days.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Thanks -- i'm using Chrome but can't find the scalable icon anywhere.   although the idea of making the whole batch and freezing portions make sense. Would still like to find the scalable icon.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Neal Hemmelstein The recipe yields about 3 cups. The nutritional information assumes 6 servings. That comes out to half a cup per serving.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: So much work: not worth it. I think the different steps are too much.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Because I do not normally stock some of these ingredients, I would say there was not a lot of flavor reward for a rather complicated recipe. And it does make a lot of dressing. Wish I had cut it in half.

commentBody: Since it is going into a blender, can you use firm tofu instead of silken?

commentBody: I cannot find this either (desktop). Perhaps some people are using an app??

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Elkmtnman It does not appear there on my screen. I, too, have used every browser and do not see it. I appreciate you taking the time to be specific about where it is located. I suspect this has something to do with ad blockers.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Barley miso contains gluten!!! As a celiac, I always hate to see hidden sources of gluten in recipes — I’ll try white or red miso?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Used only one 250 gram cube and did lots of substitutions; dark soy miso, tehina with roasted sesame oil, etc. But I found it to be the consistency of a paste, not a dressing. I didnt want to add water, so I added more oil, more sesame oil, more tehina, more vinegar; in short a lot more of the other ingredients in proportion to the tofu. It’s still pretty spread-like consistency but tasted great. I wonder if the original proportions would come out too bland. And how does one use this “spread” on delicate greens?  It’s more a dipping sauce.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can't wait to make, been looking for a creamy style dressing that doesn't have dairy or mayo. For those saying it makes too much & if you use half the tofu, what to do with the other half; some brands make twin packs of tofu, including shelf stable packages. I've bought those, use half immediately & toss the other half in the freezer. If you bought a whole pound of tofu, you could probably quarter the packet & recipe. Use one in this recipe and freeze the other three.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The amount of —perishable!— salad dressing that this recipe produces, as written, seems scaled for the restaurant where it originated.  So, I agree with NE and other commenters... divide all the measurements by six for ordinary home use!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
How about using some simple math techniques to reduce the volume to 1/4 of 3 cups… like 3/4 cup of finished dressing?? 
Simple division by four. If the garlic gives you fits then just adjust to taste. 
Then plan on adding tofu to a salad  or making ma po tofu or browning cubes of it for a stir fry. Endless possibilities. 
Reduce, reuse, be creative. Don’t just create three cups of something you may find to be less life altering and really is just glop …

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Describing serving size, in "Nutritional Analysis" by weight (e.g., in grams) gives me no helpful information regarding serving size. Using units like teaspoons, tablespoons, cup as  done in the recipe would me much more informative to me.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This recipe sounds good, if under seasoned for the amount. The one item that seems really out of place is so much EV olive oil. That's definitely not a typical Japanese ingredient and it has a very strong taste. Did anyone find this to be an issue? I was thinking peanut oil or unroasted sesame oil might be better?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I cut back to 4T, didn't miss the other 2T. Probably could use 3T and it'd be fine.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: For the anchovy bread crumbs you can follow Eric Kim's recipe for Radicchio Caesar Salad where he uses the oil from the anchovies to toast the bread crumbs. It's delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I know there’s likely no acceptable substitute, but for someone with a sesame allergy, would the dressing still hold up without the sesame paste, or is there some other ingredient that could perform a similar function?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: NE commented that this makes a large amount (3 cups). I haven't made this yet, but I think it would make a delicious base for a vegetable soup. I would add some broth and cook the vegetables as you prefer (roasted, sauteed or cooked in broth) and then add the leftover dressing at the end to reheat it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I can only source white miso paste , standard Trader Joe’s tahini and firm tofu in my area. I did reduce the amount of tofu slightly, and added a bit of water to compensate. We thought this dressing is delicious and enjoyed the lower fat profile compared to many easily available salad dressings.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I am a tofu lover and this salad dressing left a lot to be desired.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Tahini and sesame paste are wildly different.  Just toast white sesame seeds or use toasted white sesame and grind or use a food processor for it.  Also think this could be extended to 1 week in the fridge.  Nothing in this is perishable that quickly

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Also a good source of protein, which is uncommon for a salad dressing.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Add the garlic later before use to avoid contamination

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This would seem to be a recipe that someone should have made their own for the home cook instead of giving us restaurant amounts

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This sounded good - tofu, miso, sesame paste... I made the full recipe with the talk of using it for crudite, pasta sauce, etc.  But it ended up looking and tasting like mayo.  Very thick and only salvageable as a salad dressing with lots of lemon juice.  I had no interest in putting this on veggies or pasta and ended up throwing most of it away.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I love the idea of this dressing, but it is completely impractical to use this amount in 3 days unless you are having a party or you want to eat this every day for 3 days. I can't buy silken tofu in smaller amounts and it is not freezable so this unfortunately is a no for me.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Shelf-stable silken tofu comes in 10.8 oz (307g) little boxes in most grocery stores. I found this dressing as written to be pretty bland - the tofu taste dominates. By using the lesser (10.8 oz) amount of tofu and increasing every other ingredient by about 1.5x (except for the oil), I found this to be really tasty.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I bought sesame paste at a Japanese grocery. I don't read Japanese so I didn't know I was supposed to massage the pouch it came in before opening it. So all the oil was at the top. It came in a pouch like baby foods. Probably I should have emptied the pouch, stirred it all together and put it in a storage jar. I don't think I would bother buying it again. For 1 teaspoon I would just use tahini. I do think the barley miso made a difference. The recipe lived up to the hype.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I found this tasty but a little bland (only had white miso) so I added a knob of ginger and a tsp of Genevieve Ko's chili crisp--and you should too! In addition to salad dressing, I will use it in a brown rice ramen noodle bowl with tofu and veggies.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Creamy and delicious!

Toasted 1 tablespoon sesame seeds and ground with mortar. It suffused the dressing with a wonderful nuttiness.

Used salt to taste, skipping miso second time around, for creamier and lighter dressing. The miso had overpowered the nutty sesame aroma and lightness of the tofu.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Make this ahead of time! I found it a little meh at first but the next day, the flavor intensified and I couldn't stop eating it. Made as instructed but with 4 tbsp white miso subbed in. Used as a dip for veggies and mixed into soba noodles and veggies.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is really delicious and easy to make. I used red miso and regular tahini and thought it tasted great. No need to source the more obscure specialty ingredients. Beware that this recipe yields a LOT of dressing. There's no way my small household can use it up in three days so the next time I will probably make a quarter recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
My first foray into the world of tofu. I'm a pretty novice miso guy too. This is yummy.

I had a tablespoon of white miso and bought some more red.  I erred and used all three tablespoons, whipping this up after a couple of cocktails. Pretty bold but  I added another teaspoon of honey and it toned things down a bit. Delicious with red bell pepper, carrot, and celery crudite.

I let the blender mince the garlic, and I used untoasted sesame seeds and a little toasted sesame oil in lieu of the paste. 

I'm wondering if some lemon juice wouldn't be good.

Awesome way to add protein to veg!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Does anyone else remember the spread served with bread in SF at Now and Zen vegan restaurant in the ‘90’s? This is a near replica (minus the honey). Miyoko Schinner, of more recently Miyoko’s Creamery, was the OG of vegan ‘mock’ foods. A genius! And many people will not appreciate her significant contribution to creating plant based ‘dairy’.  This spread was insanely delicious. I will make it in honour of her.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is delicious. I am eating it by the spoonful. I made a third of the recipe and it seems like a manageable amount for a small household. I also added a 1/2 a teaspoon of chili crisp.  So good.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious! I used on shredded cabbage salad to go with chicken cutlet. Yummy. I skipped the salt and used less miso for lower sodium levels and it wS scrumptious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this with my homemade lumpy sesame tahini.  My husband said it was delicious.  I froze leftovers in small containers and it was still yummy.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Coming up we were always told to not eat honey with tofu (likely depending upon the coagulant that was used) so I will probably switch to another sweetener.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
This was delicious though I didn't quite have the ingredients called for: used red miso, not barley miso, and the stuff I thought was white sesame paste turned out to be black sesame paste (bought the wrong thing—very little of the label was in English). Though the taste profile must've been different from what was intended, it was great. I'll make it again.
Made a half recipe; took advantage of the leftover tofu to make chocolate tofu pudding!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @BklnDF you can make delicious desserts with the black sesame paste (I think it’s largely the same taste as its white counterpart, just more striking in color). Black sesame panna cotta comes to mind. Ice cream is my personal fave, but harder to commit to in home kitchen.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: A favorite! My hair stylist’s son is the chef at RVR. When you’re in Los Angeles, go there!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: After all the comments about the issues regarding the safety of garlic and the amount of dressing this makes I decided to make the recipe without the garlic and without the oil.  This allowed me to keep a ‘base’ in the fridge.  Then I was able to spoon out what I wanted and add garlic, oil, chili paste (because everything is better with chili paste) or whatever seasonings worked with dinner.  It also made a fantastic dip for vegetables.  Will definitely make this again!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Your nutritional information is way off!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This sounds delicious and I can’t wait to try it!

commentBody: How long will this last in the fridge?

commentBody: no ingredients/portions given.  publish them

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I haven't made this yet but I'm going to get some silken tofu tomorrow. It looks like it should be amazingly good.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I am seeing that it is 217 cal per serving. But I don’t know how many tablespoons that is. And of course, I know that the calories depend on the ingredients – as written for the recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It says that it makes 3 cups, and the recipe makes 6 servings. 3 cups is 24 ounces, a tablespoon is 15 ml or 1/2 of an ounce. So this would make 48 tablespoons. Actually impressivly less calories than one would assume by looking at the original numbers. I'd use an ounce for a nice sized salad, so figure 24 servings. When I multiply the 6 servings x 217 calories, I get 1302 overall calories in the total of 24 ounces, that divided by 24 for each ounce, seems to be 54.25 calories or 27.2 per tbsp

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Lizzy isn't 3 cups divided into 6 servings 6 half cup servings? one serving, one half cup?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Would red miso work better than white?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: can this be frozen?  i would pour it into an ice cube tray and take out portions as needed.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@marie, I’m no expert here, but in response to you and others who’ve asked about freezing:  I would think it *wouldn’t* freeze well be/c of the moisture in the tofu (even blended), and the fact that it’s emulsified … that emulsion of the oils/fats and the water moisture from the tofu is temporary … it might be quite difficult to get a good texture after freezing. The oils and water are likely to separate, and even re-blending might not get that original lightness.  
    I’m planning to make some of this dressing and use the rest of the block in a smoothie or two or perhaps another recipe. Have to think on the latter be/c I usually like to saute, cook, or bake with firmer tofu. But the dressing sounds so appealing … worth figuring out what to do with the rest of the tofu.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Could you keep the dressing longer if you didn’t add the garlic until shortly before serving?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Probably not.Tofu spoils if you don't store it in water that you change at least daily.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: how long will this store?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
My long-held understanding learnt from a Belgium restraunteur, is that the risk of unintentionally blooming Botulanim spores  is if garlic cloves are suspended in olive 
(or any other) oil and left to marinate at room temperature. Wouldn’t the refrigeration of this luscious-sounding concoction cancel this risk? 🧄

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Advice please. I used tahini and added sesame oil as I couldn’t find the Japanese paste and this was suggested as the sub. I also used unsweetened rice vinegar. Somehow my batch doesn’t have much flavor and has a slightly bitter undertone. I would love to doctor it up into something good and welcome any suggestions. Thanks

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The only things in the listed ingredients that I can imagine having a bitter undertone are the tofu, if it is not fresh, and the garlic, if it’s old and/or sprouted. Everything else leans to the sweet side. Maybe increase the black pepper? Or you can try my fix-it remedy for most flat recipes: a teaspoon or so of sherry vinegar.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Curious about substituting brown rice miso.  Thank you!

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Cathy-So if we substituted the raw garlic with jarred store bought minced garlic, would the shelf life increase if kept refrigerated in tight lidded mason jar (IYHO?)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: People are complaining about the amount of dressing. It’s ann easy recipe to halve, even though some of the elements get reduced to minuscule  amounts. But why not try freezing the unused portion? I see no reason, looking at the ingredients, that it cannot be frozen and re-emulsified in the blender after thawing (in the refrigerator). If the raw garlic worries anyone, use cooked garlic instead (if you keep roasted garlic around, as many do), or use garlic out of a tube.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Any suggestions for those who don't want to consume tofu?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I would suggest a completely different recipe such as a buttermilk or yogurt/sour cream dressing which you could modify with asian inspired flavors if that is what you're going for.  Plain Greek yogurt would have the benefit of having a good amount of protein and creaminess, and comes in a range of fat content from zero to at least 5-7%. My personal recommendation is Fage.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I would suggest blue cheese with toasted nuts and bacon on a wedge of iceberg. @BK

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I wonder if you substituted garlic powder for the actual garlic if it would change the flavor much and/or let you keep it longer in the fridge?

commentBody: no the texture would not be creamy@Martha R.

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Sooo good!

commentBody: I added some Gochujang paste when adding the garlic and ginger. It worked great. I also used basmati tide (cooking the rice only for 15 minutes)

commentBody:
1. I have nothing I can find to change the yield
2. I would have to specifically buy the first four ingredients and then anchovies. 
3. I'll have the wedge salad with blue cheese thank you.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I can't find silken tofu these days.  Does anyone have ideas of how to use firm tofu in this recipe?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Quite delicious. I doubled the garlic and added chili powder. We are very fond of spices. Timely recipe since I have two containers of silken tofu that I was figuring out what I wanted to do with. This recipe hit the mark. Had it with salad as a side to jasmine rice and a Thai red curry. Now to figure out what to go with that other container of silken tofu…….

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Everyone has loved this dressing. I added some balsamic vinegar for increased flavor and variety on 3rd day. Excellent. Maybe the acid will even increase shelf life????

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @June haha sounds so easy! I'll just need to dig out my Japanese style m&p! : )

commentBody:
Genevieve,
I used this recipe as a dipping sauce for my chicken wrap. Delicious!

commentBody: Errr...just made this.  It's not good.  I think it was the brand of silken tofu I used.  Any recommendations for a good tasting brand?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this today! I liked it a little more tart so added some extra vinegar and tossed some toasted sesame oil as recommended, otherwise followed the recipe as presented. Took a minute but I think once it sat and the flavors melded it was really easy to eat a cup with cucumbers and bell peppers for a snack.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This felt like too many pricey/hard-to-source ingredients for the end result, which resembles (to me) miracle whip with garlic.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Do you think the general public has access to all these fancy ingredients? Only in the big apple not in the rest of NY State!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Needs lemon juice!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made with ingredients I had on hand which included tahini.  My result may have been different than what was expected but was delicious nonetheless.  I add a sprinkling of nutritional yeast to balance out the miso umami.  It is a lot to eat in three days.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: disappointing.  very little flavor.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
After being reminded about the dangers of fresh garlic in an anaerobic solution (and sharing the thought that I’d never use even 1/2 a recipe in 3 days), I tried garlic powder. A bit disappointing, but it may intensify as it sits. Maybe a solution is to make the dressing sans garlic and add a touch before serving. 
I also think the dressing needs more tang. If it’s suggested to spritz with lemon before serving, why not add the lemon juice to the dressing in the first place?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Excellent !

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made 1/3 recipe to see if we liked it, and we do! My only advice to taste before adding the kosher salt. Next time, I’ll cut salt in half. It’s also vastly improved with the addition of a good squeeze of fresh lemon juice at the end.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Goodness! This salad dressing feels so naughty and it's so delicious!😋

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Really nice to add a good amount of lemon juice at the end

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
The problem with the proportions of this recipe is that it calls for 14 ounces of silken tofu, and I find it difficult to find silken tofu in smaller units. If this recipe is halved or quartered, perhaps there is another recipe that could use up the remaining silken tofu, which doesn't freeze well.
Also, I don't see the yield adjustment icon on my screen either - I'm using Firefox.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: For the anchovy crumbs, how about plain panko?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: We made this over the weekend as written. We halved the recipe and it was enough for two salads for three people. We liked it a lot, and we will certainly make it again. Needed to increase the liquid just a bit to get the right consistency, it was a bit thick when made per the recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I halved the tofu, skipped the sesame paste and honey and added some fresh herbs. It was delicious. Two days later I made it again with the other half of the tofu because we had consumed the first batch. This time I did add sesame and left out herbs.
Also delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made it and it’s a great all purpose dressing. As the author says, adjust for taste by dialing up some of the flavors once you’ve tried it. I used 14 oz of tofu and will try using less next time to let more of the other flavors shine through. I like Genevieve’s recipes because they introduce me to ingredients I haven’t tried and will experiment with more in the future. I never knew about sesame paste! I’m in love with it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This recipe asks for a lot of very specific ingredients.  I'm not quite sure who it's targeted to, but I know I don't have most of these in my pantry.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I served this sauce over grain bowls with lentils, couscous, kale, and almonds. It was delicious.  We ended up using it as dip for our sourdough on the side.  I did reduce the ingredients slightly to account for the silken tofu only being 10 oz. Will be making this again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I thought this was quite good. I made a half recipe, using white miso and roasted sesame tahini. I added about a tablespoon of water to the blender just to get the mixture moving, as it was too thick to blend properly. I ended up adding a bit more tahini, plus 2-3 teaspoons of lime juice and a shake of cayenne. Very nice on spring mix with bell peppers and cucumber. Will try it on steamed broccoli and cauliflower.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I made a half recipe to try it out.  Froze the other half of the tofu. I used tahini, so it does taste a little bitter.  This seems like a good basis for experimentation. I'm going to omit the tahini the next time, and sub miso. I think more garlic, and a good amount of lemon juice might be good. Kudos to the developers for coming up with this recipe. A creamy, healthy salad dressing has been a long-time goal for
 me--my husband loves creamy salad dressing.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was amazingly good instead of missing around with different types paste, I just used toasted sesame oil, super quick and easy to make. I will try it with other flavors instead of sesame, maybe ginger or different herbs in the summer. I think this will be super versatile thank you for the great recipe

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I halved this or maybe a bit less. It was OK. I added some Mirin and I liked it better (I just added a little then a little more-maybe a tsp. Or less in the end). I’m going to let it sit for a bit and try again. I also considered ginger and even peanut butter (one of my favorite foods😂)!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I tried this dressing and will never make it again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is waaay healthier and much tastier than "standard bottles" (whatever that means) of salad dressing. This household of two can easily go through the amount of dressing the recipe makes by using it as a dip for snacking on crudite, fruit, and toast.  We are lucky if it makes it through a day.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I’ve made this 3 times in two weeks. It’s wicked good. 
We had toasted sesame seeds on hand and whizzed them up. Not ideal, but it’s what we had. 
I could, and did, eat this with a spoon. Plant-based eaters — not to worry about the shelf life! 
Oh yeah I added powered greens to one batch and that was good also. 
Just make it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I always use raw garlic when I make vinaigrette, and make enough for a week or more. I never knew this was dangerous. I would say I've been doing this for at least 10 -15 years with no ill effects. I'm not recommending this, just reporting on my experience.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Roberta Acid kills botulinum. If your dressing is acidic enough, whicha vinaigrette is likely to be, you’ll have no problem. This recipe has very little acid, so it’s riskier.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I had high hopes for this dressing, but it was just ok. Nothing tasted terrible, just bland. It sounded so good that I ordered barley miso and the white sesame paste and made it as directed. This is one of a very few NYT recipes that wasn't spectacular which is surprising because I love Genevieve Ko's recipes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I was passing by a Japanese market, so I picked up the barley miso and white sesame paste. Made this as written and it was really good, though it's a lot of dressing! Should have halved the recipe. I look forward to trying other ingredients when I run out of the paste and miso.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Heeding others concerns, I used powdered garlic, with a bit of dried parsley for color- turned out great- I adjusted the acid a bit, etc. Also, this doesn't make 3 cups of dressing- logically there's no way 14 oz of tofu turns into 3 cups.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This lasts much more than three days in the fridge - going on two weeks and still perfect. Divine recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Be careful -- as several others have noted, garlic kept in oil (an anaerobic medium) can yield some very nasty bacteria, and it won't affect the taste or smell.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used white miso and regular tahini and it came out great. I made the suggested adjustments of adding more miso and toasted sesame oil. I used this dressing on a leafy salad and as a dip for crunchy veggies. I did not make the breadcrumbs. I would make this again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I found this a bit bland.  I used tahini since that’s what I had in the pantry.  A healthy squeeze of lemon helped somewhat.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is delicious!  Creamy and umami-filled, but not a lot of calories per Tablespoon as far as dressing goes.  I made it as directed.  My taste buds cried out for more vinegar.  I added 2 additional Tablespoons of sherry vinegar -- because I love it - and decided I would do all sherry vinegar next time.  We ate it on chef salad dressing on day one, then as a topping for quinoa, roasted veggie, and shrimp bowls the next day.  It was even better on day 2.  Even my tofu-hating husband liked it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Followed the recipe closely and the olive oil was the dominant flavor. I added quite a bit more miso than the recipe called for to compensate. This recipe generally is a good idea for a healthy but very creamy dressing, especially if you’re looking to get some extra non meat protein into your diet. Next time will tweak for my own palate.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I only used white miso and the dressing was good. I might add a bit of sesame oil to the recipe just to add a little interest.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: What kind of blender is used in the video? Haven’t seen a blender like that nice enough to keep on the counter!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I found it! Thyme & Table at Walmart. Love it! Haven't made the dressing yet:-)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious! I used a good amount to whip up an easy pasta primavera, which helped use up the large quantity this recipe makes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If you're worried about the garlic, soften it in the oil over heat before adding it to the blender or food processor.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This dressing has to be consumed within 3 days because it ferments, like cider -- and the results are not tasty.  Our small family cannot use so much dressing, so this one is out of my rotation.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I have successfully frozen tofu-based mayo that had roughly the same major ingredients. The texture was slightly thinner after thawing but satisfactory.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: People saying this should only be kept in the fridge for 3 days! What then? Down the drain? Absurd. Please don’t waste this great tasting dressing, just modify the recipe, there’s no law against it, by adding more acid either vinegar or citrus.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: this is delicious. made with the addition of about 2 tbsp grated ginger and 4 tbsp chopped shallot. whizzed in my vitamix. i used about a cup on my farro salad with mixed veg. the volume produced will not overwhelm and will not last more than 3 days since this is not to be used miserly on a salad, it is to be "laid on thick," as they say. Next I will consider adding some red boat fish sauce and switching out some rice vinegar for some citrus juice along with some grated zest, lime or mandarin.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I bought all the ingredients, read the reviews, whipped this up and well, meh, I really wanted it  to be life changing but it just wasn't. Salad dressing it was only pretty good but as as a dip I'd recommend Sheldon's Simeon's  Shoyu dip with Sesame Crunch. And I didn't have to shop specialty stores for products I'm unlikely to use again...

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is the first NYT recipe I haven't liked; after hundreds of recipes, I knew this day would come. You can use white miso, so the barley is unnecessary. This was bland and I had to dress it up in order to finish the enormous amount of dressing this recipe yields! I didn't consume within 3 days; it was in my fridge for 2 weeks and finally I had to throw out the rest. Even though I won't make this again, my advice is to cut the recipe down to yield 1/2 cup (1/6th).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Love this - we use as a salad dressing, dip, generally delish sauce for cooked veg, grains etc. it’s equally good with fresh herbs blended in - basil, mint, cilantro.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Added 1 tbsp of nutritional yeast.
Used apple cider vinegar as I ran out of rice vinegar.
Really good recipe

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’ve just made this for the second time. I make a half recipe at a time to avoid the garlic issue. I froze the second half of the tofu and defrosted it to use— you can squeeze the water out when it’s still half frozen. Also, I subbed peanut butter for the     Tahini— couldn’t find my jar of tahini. I likenit better with the peanut butter— it’s more mellow.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I've made this, as is, and it is delicious and healthy. No problem using it up in a few days on salads, bowls, and vas a veggie topping. I'm about to make it again and realized the simple, just in case solution to whether I can use it in 3 days to avoid garlic spoilage, is to leave it out and then add a bit when I do use it. Obviously it won't last like a vinaigrette, but it will give me a few more days.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is so easy to prepare, delicious, and good with so many different vegetable and crackers that it doesn't last three hours in my house.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This does go bad pretty quickly, even it two days. The texture is good, but I gave it two tries and the family wasn't going for it.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Made these with my 11yo for Lunar New Year. We cheated and used a food processor for all of the chopping. They were delicious and so easy to make. A great starter dumpling!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody:
I used ground pork as I could not find chicken. Turned out great. I could not find Thai basil because, February. Italian basil worked fine. 
Used the suggestion to use a food processor instead of manual chopping. Saved a ton of time. 
Instead of frying, brushed with canola oil and cooked in on convection at 375 for about 15 minutes. Less mess, less oil and delicious. Totally making this again.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: I just tried this recipe, and learned the hard way there is a reason people don’t normally cook spring roll wrappers. I have a strong range hood, but it was not even nearly enough - my house smells like really bad fried food. The taste wasn’t good either, aside from the sauce. Total fail.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: @Kim you can lightly spray them with oil and air fryer them too.  That might help if the range hood doesn’t pull enough air.  (Also starting the range hood a good 5-6 minutes before you start cooking helps!).

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: It sounds like your oil may have gotten too hot, past its smoke point, and burned, which can smell quite rancid. It should be shimmery before you add the dumplings, but not smoking or close to smoking. If it starts to smoked, turn the heat down to medium. Hope that helps!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Some people add a tiny bit of sugar to the dipping water you use for the rice paper wrapper which assists in the wrapper browning when fried. I do this for spring rolls as well. I have seen a few recipes on Instagram where, instead of cutting the wrapper into quarters you use it whole and make more of a “slider” sized dumpling that takes more than a few bites to eat. It will all taste the same!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: A local restaurant uses spring roll wrappers for their egg rolls.  So folks like me have a gluten free option.  Tastes great, so don’t be afraid to try this

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody:
These were delicious. The rest of my family is GF, and my daughter needs a low FODMAP diet and these adapted beautifully with the small changes of use of pickled garlic, and the dark green of scallions in place of the shallot. I think other herbs like a cilantro and mint combo would also work if you can't find the thai basil, but use the full 2 cups - so good! 
I will be using this rice paper technique with other fillings in the future, and the spicy  dipping sauce (I went 5 chilies this time)!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Love your FODMAP adjustments and so happy you all enjoyed!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: These were excellent! Thank you!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Instead of trying to cut those gooey rice wrappers, I used a super sharp chefs, knife, and cut them in 4 like pizza. So much easier!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody:
We made this and it was a total success! The flavors were great. We used a neutral oil with a high smoke point for frying so had no 'burned' smell at all with the vent hood working well. 

The sauce was perfect, our little dumpling packages were not lovely but they were tasty. We both have used rice paper before but each time we do, we learn more. 

I used a piece of cheesecloth to get all the moisture out of the cabbage/scallion mixture and it worked so well.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Question: I’m vegetarian what plant-based ingredient can I use instead of chicken? (Even if it’s a plant-based “chick’n” product - suggestions?)

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: @PatsyKB Firm - Extra firm Tofu

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: @Rudi Xeno second this—grate it and marinade it

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: @PatsyKB I was thinking of trying TVP and adding some spices to give it flavor. Marinated tofu could be good too.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: As a Thai Basil Chicken MEATBALLS recipe: this is fantastic. Great filling, great use of that clamshell of grocery store basil before it quickly turns. I was attracted to the idea of the crsipy rice paper wrappers, but not worth the effort. Add a tablespoon of peanut butter and just make this filling in other wrappers or cook the filling and use for an inexpensive week of rice bowls.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: I tried this and substituted Tamari for fish sauce because we have a gluten issue in my home.  I struggled cutting the rice wrappers into four pieces so I made clunkier ones by only making 2 dumplings per wrapper.  They were delicious.  My kitchen commercial style hood which exhausts out of the house did keep up with the smell.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: @suz None of the brands of fish sauce I have ever bought have gluten in them. So you should be fine using it. There is gluten in soy sauce, oyster sauce, hoisin sauce and even some tamari. For these sauces there are gluten free options available at most Asian markets, as well as US markets. Just read the labels.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: @suz Noma projects makes a Dashi Rx that I use as soy sauce substitute, it is great and use much less. https://nomaprojects.com/collections?srsltid=AfmBOopn9KTVzcOV9zowfNvXjwGE9Iy3ethIzNR_7eQT8wC1kiU4MLNk

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: What can I do to reduce sugar?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: @Shelly add less of it…

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: These look wonderful. Could they be steamed or gently boiled?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Unless you live in a place where room temperature is 100° I highly suggest using warm water or sticking the bowl in the microwave every now and then

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Just saw an IG video where she updated the method to covering the dumplings with sheets of rice paper (rather than wrapping them) and added water to the pan to steam them. This should solve the burnt dumplings  problems I see in the comments.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: @Blue. Did she have to cut the sheets of rice paper after the cooking?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: @LinSR She cut them before just as in the original instructions. Then lay each piece over the individual meatballs in the pan.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! Chopped the cabbage and shallots in the food processor, wrung out in a clean dish towel after salting/resting. Will play around with adding lemongrass, makrut lime, cilantro to the filling next time.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: This is a great gluten free dumpling option. My kids loved it too. I added chopped shiitake mushrooms and had to use Italian basil, but once my Thai basil comes back I will definitely use that.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: The instructions are crystal-clear and the results are incredible! I made them a second time with ground pork instead of ground chicken and that worked beautifully as well. Take the time to watch the video so you can feel confident going into the assembly process. These were probably the most forgiving dumplings I have ever made, and SO delicious!!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Such a delicious recipe; my whole family loved it (without chili). The Thai basil really shines.  The chicken stays very juicy and flavorful.  I love the rice wrapper technique as someone who is GF.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Great base recipe as a spring board for a million variations, especially if you have the chicken and rice paper and nothing else. I used some leftover grilled Hokkaido pumpkin, barely cooked fresh baby spinach and cilantro leaves and the little fried bundles came out super special. I added more acid to the dipping sauce as lime and sour, fish sauce.It too was perfect. Such an easy and tasty light dinner. The drink was your mocktail Negroni. Perfetto! Grazie.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Oh wow, these were stellar! Mine were perfectly imperfect and still addictive. And the sauce—sublime!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Made this last night for dinner.  Although very good, I did feel it was somewhat labor intensive. I got better at rolling the dumplings about halfway through the filling.  My husband doesn't do spicy, so I omitted the chilies, but added some Sriracha in my bowl of dipping sauce to make up for it. I was a little concerned about the amount of fish sauce because it was smelly while I was assembling. I thought maybe I should have used half. Wrong. They tasted great with the exact amount.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Can these be baked instead of fried ???

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: @Kim or steamed with parchment paper in the basket?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: My wife and I are pretty adventurous in the kitchen and we’ve been cooking a long time. The rice paper wrappers almost broke us. This might be the hardest thing we’ve ever prepared. Also,  all the moisture in the wrappers makes them uh… exciting to pan fry. Tasted good though, in the end.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: This filling works with regular dumplings wrappers too. For the rice paper wrappers, just dip them in water for a bit. They shouldn't be at all soft or floppy, just pliable enough to bend a little. That ensures that they won't be wet when they go in the pan. By the time they're filled and wrapped, the outsides should feel dry to the touch. If you do get splattering, simply cover the pan with a lid.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: I didn’t have ground chicken so just cut chicken breast in cubes and put it in the food processor together with all of the other ingredients and pulsed. It was perfect! Also, I think I might’ve overcooked the sauce but it ended up being caramel consistency and that was perfect to stick on to these lovely little dumplings. :)

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Can these be frozen before frying and fried at a later point?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: @Cynthia Yes! (The details for how to freeze and cook are in the tip at the end of the recipe.)

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: I have ground pork on hand. Can I substitute it for the chicken?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: @DJ Ground pork would be great here!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: These were excellent! I used a mix of habaneros and Serrano peppers since that’s what we had. Don’t let the stickiness intimidate you! Once you get the hang of rolling the dumplings, assembling them is almost meditative. My husband and I devoured these! Served with air fried baby broccoli and really enjoyed our Friday evening.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Scrumptious! My dumplings were not perfectly shaped but the flavor was delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: These turned out great! I steamed them a little first and then browned them in a skillet to get them crispy. Way less oil this way.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Made these with my teens and their friends, super fun lunar new year group activity. A few tips, get dumpling skins from Asian super market if you can, very easy to fill, fold and pinch. I steamed them before pan frying lightly. Endlessly versatile, next time will switch basil with Cilantro and chives, or add water chestnuts. The sauce is amazing, made exactly as written (Fresno chilli instead of Birds Eye) and it elevates the dumplings.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Amazing! Make sure to prep all the ingredients instead of as you go for time efficiency. My husband and I thoroughly enjoyed these dumplings along with the dipping sauce. Yum!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: I found these delicious! So satisfying. Also, if you’re like me and don’t want to cut your spring roll sheet into fours, you can cheat and just cut it in halves. I did try using a whole sheet and then pan frying. The result was yummy but messy.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: These little dumplings are SO good! Very easy to eat.   The rice paper made for a lighter dumpling than a traditional wheat flour dough.  Served them with a simple fried rice.  I'm thinking they would be great to pass around at a party.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Loved the flavors but the rice paper burst on almost every dumpling. Also, there was a LOT of splattering. If you have leftover dipping sauce, try it as salad dressing - delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: I just made this subbing in ground salmon for the chicken. It worked great! I think the key peice to this recipe that surprised me is just that you need a lot more vegetable than you think because it shrinks so much. You really can’t add too much basil.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Yes, it's messy, but these were amazing!  First time making dumplings.  My subs were savoy cabbage, regular basil, TJ's Calabrian Chili Sauce for the Thai peppers, because that's what I had available.  The video is super helpful, and the dipping sauce is fantastic!  Thank you, Genevieve Ko!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody:
These were a lot of work but fun to make w my toddler and tasted wonderful. 
I barely had 1 cup of Italian basil and added 0.75 cup of cilantro - wondered if I’d want the 2 full cups of basil, but maybe Thai basil is different. 
The filling was plenty salty as it were so next time I’d cut the salt by half.
