The dipping sauce at half the amount was a whopping amount, so make 1/4 of it unless if you plan on drinking it!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Made these using traditional (store bought) dumpling wrappers. We make bao and dumplings fairly regularly, so happy to have a different flavor profile.  Happier still to have a recipe using up the thai basil, cilantro and mint left over from making fresh spring rolls.  I bet these would be delicious as a bao filling.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: These worked perfectly and were delicious. They tried to swim towards each other in the pan as if they had magnets but I managed to separate them with a wooden spoon.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: I made these after seeing the video on Instagram suggesting to use this recipe in the style of the viral dumpling hack—where you dollop the filling in a pan and cover it with the dumpling wrappers. The filling itself was delicious. The rice paper wrappers I had were a little too thin to be cooked. But it didn’t really matter because it was so tasty. Really nice dipping sauce as well. Would definitely make it again.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: @RBCinNYC update on my review: any extra filling can last up to three days in the fridge. My new way to cook the leftover filling was to just sear like meatballs and serve with cold rice noodle noodles and sauce. Absolutely delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: looking for an equally favorable result with only veg (shiitake,cabbage,lemongrass +/-tofu) or veg+shrimp...anyone have suggestions based on actual experience with the approach used in this recipe?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody:
So good and adaptable what you have on hand! 

Used a combination of cilantro and parsley and scallions for the herbs and shallot, ground turkey and assorted chili flakes, cayenne pepper, and Sriracha to bring the heat.  Pan Fried in a film of avocado oil, no issues with smoking. 

Will definitely make again!

Recipe makes a lot!  Froze about half separated on the sheet pan with parchment to cook later.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Labor intensive but yields DELICIOUS results! I was definitely glad there were two of us to split the tasks and expedite the drying process

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: SO MUCH FUN to make, thank you! I had a terrible week and this recipe really cheered me up. It is definitely a project for people with my skill level but I made it exactly as written and it was really really good. Thank you!!!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Ps the video was really SO helpful!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: OK, so I decided to make these because the recipe intimidated me. But it’s like magic how they all turned out. I used ground turkey, bok choy, and Serrano chilies (instead of chicken, Napa, cabbage, and Thai chilies) and it turned out great. I’m really proud of myself. This recipe is fantastic. It also helped listening to Fleetwood Mac while the magic was happening.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Excellent!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: We loved these! My daughter has celiac and misses dumplings so much. We did cut back on the spice, but besides from that made them according to the recipe. I had some leftover filling and fried it up in a pan like a meat pancake. It will be delicious on some rice, or on a sandwich!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: This is my first Genevieve Ko recipe and my very first comment. I’m an absolute fan of dumpling week and this is the first of this year’s recipe I’ve completed. I made the dumplings and sauce as directed.  My family and I absolutely thoroughly enjoyed them! Next time I plan on imparting lemongrass flavor to the dumpling mixture so they have even more of a Thai taste.  Definitely second all the comments that mention using Thai basil, don’t substitute unless absolutely necessary. Can’t what to make these again…next week!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: These were delicious - light and bright. I had to grind chicken thighs in the food processor as there was no ground chicken at the grocery. I also pulsed the cabbage and shallots after they had been drained. Don't step away while these are in the pan - mine took less time and a batch suffered because I stepped away. These were a wonderful addition to dumpling week! Will definitely make again.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: I LOVE these, but I NEVER use vegetable oil. It has a chemical in it (during processing) that burns my eyes, regardless of the brand! Trust me, I've tried and tried. So, I have been absolutely resolute in using coconut oil. High smoke point, creates great crispiness and adds a bit of sweetness to the dish. I don't mind the flavor and it doesn't make my kitchen (with NO fan exhaust) smell like a bad chemistry experiment with an underlying thai smell.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: I have a tip for working with the rice paper, which can be a little tricky. If you dip it in water for a couple seconds then let it sit on your cutting board for 30 seconds it will fully absorb the water and be pliable enough to pinch into little parcels. I found pinching easier than folding.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Excellent just as is. Watch the video it helps.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody:
These were delicious- I had great success in the air fryer. 

When trying to shallow fry, my dumplings got stuck to the bottom of the pot. Is this because my oil wasn’t hot enough?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Easy to prepare and absolutely delicious!!!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Skipped the sauce and used soy sauce to simplify and still so good

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: I liked these. Next time, I'd use a food processor for the veg & fill the dumplings with half as much meat. I appreciate the rice wrapper, since gluten free dumpling recipes can be hard to find. The filling was flavorful. I might add diced trumpet mushrooms to it.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: These were great, so much flavor. I definitely got better at frying them in the later batches. Next time will use a food processor to speed up prep.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody:
so good. they reheat well after cooked and refrigerated.  use a paper towel between layers in a airtight container to store then pop into a nonstick pan on med heat until heated through.  i wonder how many calories are in just one including the dipping sauce which is pretty sweet.
labor intensive but worth it.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: If you click on nutritional information just below the recipe, it states that each dumpling is 48 calories.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody:
Very tasty dumplings! They do take a long time to make and it gets messy, I found the sauce a tad too sweet. Rice paper can be tricky to work with but it’s worth the effort. I found it helped to 1. Dip the papers 2. Cut 3 lay on board 4. Spoon filling 5. Attend to other things such as the batch in the pan while it sits for a few minutes to soften up before you attempt to fold them. 

I was maxed out before I could use all the filling so I froze about 1/3 of it for another time 

Note it doesn’t mention in the recipe itself but only in the video that if you are using Morton’s kosher salt use 1/2. I used the whole amount and it was fine but could be a bit salty for some people

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Is the sauce shelf stable and how long will it keep.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: I will never manage those rice paper thingies... They get either too gooey or break apart. I ended up making ricepaper burritos, which I fried up in just a little oil. Both filling and sauce were delicious, even though I have to say that I am not a friend of fish sauce. This would be good just over white rice too.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: My family and I live in Denmark. And we were craving NYC Chinese food. This recipe delivered!! Besides the authentic flavor of the filling, it was the crunch the fried rice papers gave! Wow- just delicious! And the smell brought me home to NY. Thank you!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: This is a hit. Delicious, i served it with some jasmine rice topped with scallions.  Ill try it again and cook them in an air fryer and see how this works

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Yum -- We ate them in wrapped in lettuce, which helped catch dripping sauce and offset the oily texture from frying. Excellent flavor.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: maybe i messed up, but this turned into a goopy mushy mess. the flavors were great and i’d definitely make again using regular wonton wrappers or just having them as meatballs. having a nonstick pan is also crucial imo (i used cast iron and it was not great).

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Made with ground pork and leek. Delicious! Slightly salty, will omit 1/4 tsp in mix next time if I use pork again.  Only used 4 Thai chilis in sauce, not quite enough. Use more than 1 Thai chili in the mix as well.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Made as written, and the struggle was real!  It was my first time making dumplings, and I found cutting the rice wrappers tricky. After a while, I cut the wrappers into four and put just 1/4 at a time in the water and filled.   This was more manageable.  I lost some of the rice wrapping during frying....If I make it again, I'll use the optional fry or bake.  Hopefully, this will keep the dumplings intact.  I used (2) serrano chilis in the filling and (1) whole w slit in the sauce.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: The prep time is not 15 minutes…

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody:
These are SO GOOD! We made them with pork and Italian basil. Added some cilantro we had in the fridge. The rice paper was easier to work with than I anticipated- just watch the video; it’s so helpful. 
Loved the dipping sauce too. This one will be in the rotation regularly!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody:
Made the recipe as written (although we used supermarket basil in place of Thai basil) and they came out restaurant-quality superb! Cannot recommend highly enough and can’t wait to make again. Best new dish I’ve tried in a long time. 

If your first batch is too brown (as mine was, though flavor wasn’t affected) or smells burnt, turn down the heat. The rest of mine came out perfectly.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: AMAZING! Takes a while but totally worth it!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, but it took my teen and I close to 3 hours to make (a double batch). And my kitchen was covered in oil afterwards. Would maybe try the viral cooking method if we did it again?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: So good and easy! I love the rice paper wrappers instead of wheat!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody:
I modified by cooking the plain meatballs in the air fryer with a small amount of oil; cooling them a bit then wrapping them with the softened rice paper quarters. Then it’s into a covered non stick pan on stovetop with a little bit of water. Somehow they crisp up once the steam has disappeared and the fat from the filling helps cook the rice paper. They were outstanding fresh from the stovetop but also yummy cold for breakfast.
I think a vegan version with just vegetables perhaps with crispy roasted chick peas for texture would be v good.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Will these do well if I freeze them?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: @skip the potatoe and go kielbasa I froze half of mine and they still cooked up great. There’s info about freezing them in one of the tips.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: Made lots of subs w what I had — ground turkey instead of chicken; grated beets, zucchini, and minced collards instead of cabbage; white pepper instead of black; chili crisp instead of fresh; added ginger. Very good!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: I didn’t have Nappa cabbage. I used chopped water chestnuts and grated carrots instead of I also used red pepper flakes instead fresh peppers. The sauce was too sweet. I suggest decreasing the sugar.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: This dish is perfect. I cooked the recipe almost as written. The only minor change I made was doubling the portion of cornstarch. Incidentally, I made my own ground chicken from boneless, skinless thighs.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: I'd also suggest buying the small size rice paper wrappers. That way, you can use one rice paper per dumpling. They sell smaller, 8.7" round ones at H-mart.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Thai Basil Chicken Dumplings With Rice Paper Wrappers Recipe
commentBody: made exactly as directed.  excellent.  bought the pre chopped cabbage and carrots from trader Joe's.  lovely!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: In the midst of a winter storm in the mid-Atlantic today and so the only chicken left on the grocer's shelf yesterday was a pack of 5 drumsticks. Followed the recipe exactly because I wanted something different from my usual chicken soup (carrots, celery, bay leaf, etc). It was amazing with a splash of soy sauce and a squeeze of lemon. The skin on a drumstick is thin and added just the right amount of fat; the bones are dense which made for a welcome creamy gelatinous texture. Thanks for sharing

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: Terminology question:  When Ms. Ko calls for "4 chicken drumsticks" is she referring to what might otherwise be referred to as "whole legs", which include both leg and thigh?  Four drumsticks sounds like an awful small amount of chicken to make a rich broth with 8 cups of water.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: thighs would be a much better choice than drumsticks

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: My husband is recovering from a bad cold, so I was excited to try this recipe. I made it exactly as written, except I had to substitute green cabbage, and I added an extra carrot. It's a delicious soup that can serve as a base for many different flavor combos depending on the condiments you choose. I used a few dashes of TJ's chili crip seasoning. It's A nice warming soup for a cold evening as well as a simple and meditative recipe to cook.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody:
The recipe is delicious, but maybe a bit precious. Unless you run a restaurant or need to impress guests, there's no need to discard the still-flavorful/healthful ginger and the protein-rich skin and foam. (If you added chicken feet or wings as suggested, you'd end up discarding much of the protein.)
RE: chicken feet: As cooked for dim sum, these need pressure (or extended slow) cooking to break down the tendon collagen into unctuous gelatin: 30 mins regular wouldn't suffice. Ditto for backs.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: I made this by accident the other day using whatever I had which included Napa cabbage and the rest. If you haven’t tried Napa cabbage in soup you are in for a treat. My big difference was adding white beans, also an eye opener.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody:
You cannot make a chicken soup in half an hour. It’s simply not possible; it takes 6 hours for the nutrients and collagen to be extracted from the chicken bones, and the drum sticks, btw, has no collagen at all.
What you’re making here is slightly flavored water.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody:
@Nils Wetterlind

Agreed.  Doesn't anyone believe in slow-cooking bones and/or a carcass down anymore and freezing the resulting broth to be used in future soups, stews, and anything that would benefit from it?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: @MariaB. I do it all the time!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: @MariaB I do as well!  I also save rotisserie chicken carcasses- freeze them- then make bone broth for both us and our dogs. Enjoyed by all!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody:
Coupled the carrot still not enough.
Pretty bland, and don't discard the ginger. 
Maybe add celery, although not very Asian.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: In what alternative universe does barley cook in 10-15 minutes?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: @Peter  Use pearled barley. Like the tip says, it has the outer husk removed so it cooks faster.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: @PK Pearled barley and quick cooking pearled barley are different. Quick cooking barley (like Quaker) is like using Uncle Ben’s instant rice. Steer clear.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: @Peter. Agreed!  My pearled barley package said to cook for 50 minutes!  I simmered the whole soup for 1.5 hours (added barley after 30 minutes; cabbage even later). I also started with packaged organic chicken broth and not water. It was tasty with these modifications.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody:
This is very good. The broth is subtle, and that could be the problem. It’s like a minimalist dish in an unstable and scary world that demands bold flavor to calm us down.
Tue condiments listed at the end became my seasoning, especially the soy sauce and toasted sesame oil.
Overall, I loved the meditative vibe in the making and the comfort of having a pot of chicken soup bubbling on the stove. I used bone-in skin-on chicken thighs and was glad because it provided more meat for the dish.
Next time I’ll increase the ginger and possibly use 4 cups of water and 4 cups of chicken bone broth.
Overall a delicious chicken soup and a fun variation of a cold-cure soup in this behemoth winter.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: Yummo.  However, I did the following:  sautéed carrots and onion and leeks first, then followed the recipe with chicken broth, not water.  I agree with other comments that the pearl barley wasn’t completely cooked, but I ate it anyway.  It’ll be great tomorrow.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: Four stars? Unless you douse this recipe with salt, it won't taste like much of anything. Four drumsticks and a few veggies do not a gallon of chicken broth make. One would need at least a couple of rotisserie chicken carcasses to have much effect on that much water.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: @John Did you make it? Or are you surmising based on reading the recipe?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: A gallon is 16 cups of water, not 8!  If you used 16, no wonder it was tasteless!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: This reminds me of my grandmother's chicken soup. She cooked the Acini di Pepe on the side to avoid a mushy starch in the delicious leftover soup.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: Bill, regarding terminology. I don't know what the chef intended here but from our experience making chicken soup similar to this, we use one entire leg which I would define as the drumstick and thigh. This adds plenty of meat plus a little more fat that enriches the soup broth.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: Homemade chicken soup, my beloved nemesis. So delicious and so very time-consuming. And sadly I do not find the cooking process soothing at all. But our family loves it, especially our son with autism who likes very few foods, so I make large batches and freeze individual containers for him. This recipe looks wonderful but personally I would add celery because I love it and chicken flavor Better than Bouillon to enhance the flavor. If that is blasphemy, I apologize. I will be trying the recipe soon.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: Great healthy and clean recipe.  Saute onion and carrot to sweeten them up - this is classic French prep for soup veg.  Use 100% chicken broth (homemade best!).  Cook barley separately to your taste and add when serving.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: I LOVE this gingery chicken soup!  Light and very satisfying. Great served with a dash of soy sauce.  I made it with 5 drumsticks.  Next time I make it I'll 1) add the barley first (I want the barley more tender), 2) double the carrots and 3) add the napa cabbage at the very, very end (for more texture).

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: Keep the bones in the soup, thigh or drumsticks, as some of us love to chew them after a long boil to get to the delicious marrow.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: I used 4 chicken thighs, farro, and regular green cabbage because that's what I had. Excellent  soup. I did add some chicken base to up the flavor.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody:
Ok, my 2 cents… first terrific recipe! My husband said it was the best chicken soup he has ever had- albeit, l listened to the suggestions of others. 

Start with homemade chicken broth or store bought cook broth for added richness. Cook drumsticks at least 2 hours (not entire leg as some have said- there will be plenty of meat! I used 3" ginger root and was subtle, strain out & pick apart chicken & remove ginger root. 

Sauté carrots along with (l added celery) & onion for added richness.  Cook until soft- set aside & add back when pearl barley & Napa cabbage is added- cook 15 mins or until tender. Add chicken Serve with a squeeze of lemon & cilantro. delicious- will make again!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: Just to clarify, the drumsticks are raw?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: could i use sorghum i/o pearl barley to make gf? or would you suggest boring rice? maybe le puy or beluga lentils?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: Made according to recipe (except used thighs on hand and a couple of feet) and was delicious flavorful light broth. Husband getting over flu and swears it helped:) Soothing and comforting.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody:
"Roll the carrot a quarter turn and cut another chunk; repeat."

This is a classic Chinese cutting method called "roll-cutting."

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: Do 4 cups chicken stock, 4 cups water to get a bit more flavor, and cook lid on. Turned out great!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: Where has this soup been all my life?? Oh my gosh, it is delicious. Strongly suggest following the recipe, except adding the extra chicken, per the notes, and serving with a bit of chili crisp from the dead and soy sauce. I only wish I’d made this when I was dealing with a cold a couple of weeks back. Seriously delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: Made this soup tonight, and it was fantastic!  I did make a couple small adjustments - I used chicken breasts as we are watching our cholesterol.  I also used homemade chicken stock instead of water.  Otherwise, everything else was the same.  Served with a splash of soy sauce, sliced green onions and chili crisp.  Perfect winter soup!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: Terrific, but "large saucepan" isn't big enough. I used a 3.5 qt pot, and the liquid was at the top before adding the cabbage, so I transferred to a 5.5 qt Dutch oven.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: Very bland and tasteless despite addition of chicken wings. Amped up with Better Than Bullion and soy sauce addition and more cooking time. Agree with previous comments about a quick chicken soup recipe is not necessarily tasty or appetizing. End result was satisfactory thanks to tweaks.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody:
Pro-tips:
1. Use chicken stock, and twice the amount, no way this broth would be flavorful enough without something extra
2.) used 5 chicken thighs
3.) added 2 tbsp of rice wine while chicken cooked w/ ginger
4.) skim the scum (or scuzz?)
5.) added 2x ginger bc we used twice amount stock/liquid 
6.) used 4-5 star anises
7.) added daikon radish, 2 small ones or 1 large one cut into 1 inch slices
8.) doubled carrots and barley, and used a bigger napa
9.) added 1/4 soy sauce bc stock we made previous night was not super salty
10.) use sambal or sriracha for the extra vinegariness to help lighten the soup up a bit. Either directly to soup or dip for chicken/daikon

OH added snow peas from our fridge that were about to go during the last couple minutes of cooking for some extra crunch.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: So how do I invoke the new scaling feature for this recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: As far as I can tell, the scaling feature is only available if you use the phone app.  I can't figure out how to scale recipes when I am using my laptop.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: On your phone.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: What substitutions can you make for barley? Rice?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: @Marlene Dodes-Callahan yes. Read the intro

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: Can’t you use chicken breasts? I don’t like dark meat as well as light.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: Can I use rice or quinoa instead of barley?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: It's your soup! Do what you'd like.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: Does anyone have suggestions for removing all the chicken bones after you have wanted their collagen and flavor to infuse a soup? Should they be kept in some kind of bouquet de Garni bag? It seems like my husband is always pulling a few small bones out of a soup I make with a full carcass

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: @Cordelia you can put all the soup contents into a second pot using a colander to catch all the solids. Remove whatever you want to (bones, skin…) then dump the rest back into the broth.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: @Ron A. I thought it was a Japanese cut. I've never seen it from the Chinese (takeaway and internet recipes). But definitely from the Japanese's curry recipe. It's so it cooks more evenly or soaks up flavour better. It has a specific name that Google reminds me is called rangiri

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: You can tell this recipe is easy and good by perusal.  Pearled barley for substance, ginger, carrots, and cabbage for health.  The photo green looks more like spinach or kale than Napa.   For richer, I'd  augment with BTB - but watch it -  that latter is very salty.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: Should one leave the lid off all the time? I found the liquid cooked down a lot when I left the lid off.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: A bit bland even after adding powdered bullion.  Tried fish sauce, soy sauce, and siriracha, with the hot sauce the clear winner.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: What did I do wrong?!?!? Followed the recipe and it is so bland :(

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: I like to add fresh dill and Italian parsley with a few bay leaves for flavor. I take it out after cooking is done.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: A tip from another recipe would apply here.  Boil the pearled barley in it's own pot for 15 minutes before draining and adding to the soup and then cook as directed.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: I made this soup today as written. Very bland. As a commenter here said, it could act as a soup base.  I won’t make it again.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: Don't dismiss drumsticks here.  They are cheap and the skin and cartilage helps make nice rich stock

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: Made the recipe as written and it is a mild but savory broth which I find in most Asian inspired broths.  If you plan on discarding the ginger be careful not to over mash it as it dissolves which in my world was perfectly acceptable.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: My husband picked this recipe - I said surprise me. I was too tired to look through and choose but still felt like cooking. So happy it only had a couple of ingredients (most of which were just hanging out in the fridge). When I went to get the protein, whole chickens were on sale. The butcher happily parsed it up for soup. The prep and cook were such a breeze, the simplicity was welcome. I didn't have to read the recipe a hundred times. Condiments made the dish sing. Clean and comforting meal.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: I made this exactly as written, and it was delicious. Added a lot of salt, but no extra condiments.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: This soup is absolutely the ticket for February doldrums.  Made it as directed and enjoyed every bite - the ginger, barley and cabbage render a hearty bowl of chicken soup. Will add it to our Winter rotation,

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: Nourishing, quick, simple and delicious. Easy to adapt- the first time I made it with a leftover chicken carcass (straining out the stock and adding back the cooked meat), this time I made it with thighs (no drumsticks on hand). Such an elegant recipe for when the whole house is sick. Thank you for such a great recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: This was one of the best and easiest soups I've ever made. I had a parsnip on hand and added that, which I think made the flavor sweeter and more interesting. My supermarket did not have napa cabbage so I just used plain old cabbage (purchased preshredded for cole slaw). SO good and SO easy!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: Turned out great. I ended up using about 1.5-2x more chicken legs than the recipe called for, kind of like using wings for a richer stock. After about an hour of simmering, I cut the meat off the bone and set it aside, returning the meat to the simmering stock for another hour or so. Then removed the bones and re-added the meat. Rich, thick broth blended nicely with the softened barley.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious and a hit in our house! The broth is delicious and it's a filling  and healthy soup. The chicken could have cooked a little longer than directions state (about 15 mins more) and would have been easier to debone. We added ramen noodles to the leftovers which was also a huge hit. Will definitely add it into our rotation.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: Excellent recipient. Only changes I made were half chicken stock,half water. Added a chopped Fresno chili and a parmigiana rind.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: Delicious change of pace for chicken soup! I used chicken thighs as we both are not fond of legs and the stock was rich as expected. Went to 3 different grocery stores and all were out of napa cabbage, so I picked rainbow chard as my substitution. This soup was beyond excellent, soothing and very flavorful. It’s going on my soup rotation.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: Is it important to use Napa cabbage or will any cabbage, including red, work in a pinch?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: Not bad but a bit too "subtle" for my taste which leans toward assertively spicy and savory dishes. Even jazzed up with the lemon and some Sriracha it seemed porridge-like.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: The key to a tastier version of this is chicken stock in place of water, a bay leaf early on in cooking, browning and seasoning the drumsticks beforehand, and use of condiments.  I used soy sauce and a small amount of toasted sesame oil in the end.  Delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody: A very easy and quick soup that packs a lot of punch.  For me, it needed more liquid - maybe my cabbage was too big.  I added more water and chicken stock to stretch it. Scallions, lemon, soy sauce and chili crisp on the table so everyone could add what they liked.  It was a hit!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Chicken, Vegetable and Barley Soup Recipe
commentBody:
From some comments sounds like other types of barley were used instead of pearled (quick cooked, not a whole grain) barley called for in recipe. I like hulled barley which is lightly  processed but still considered a whole grain & takes 50 minutes to cook. 
Flexible recipe: I added some shiitake mushrooms & snow peas that needed using. Delicious, easy soup.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are FABULOUS! I’m a very nervous baker and was hesitant to deal with a yeast dough, but this dough was a dream to work with! The timing of this recipe was spot on. I was over an hour late to a party because I messed up the rising time; all was forgiven because these buns were a huge hit! I think these would be extra good topped with or dipped in chili oil. 10/10!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Anna make me them!!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: What instructions are needed to mix the dough by hand?  Just follow the mixing instructions and do everything manually.  I make all types of dough, including bread and pastry, by hand.  Use a big bowl, a bowl scraper and a bench knife.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Janet CO thank you! I don’t have a mixer so always need instructions on how to mix by hand for baking recipes

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made it last night, was perfect with the viral dumpling lasagna. And made an extra batch of dough to make my kids Nutella rolls for breakfast this morning. Perfection!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This recipe sounds interesting, but I would appreciate a more realistic estimate of prep time in the recipe.  There is no way this recipe can be prepped in 5 minutes!  (Just do the math with the recommended time for each of the prep steps, not including rising.)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: True especially if one is including slicing 2 bunches of scallions into tiny pieces.  But could someone please share the quality of this recipe instead of just complaining about the instructions?  Five comments, I still have no idea if this tastes good!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @D Seriously, the prep time for this is at least 25 min.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Vicki VERY Tasty. The rolls would be an excellent accompaniment to a green salad at a summer’s brunch. I actually did not find it at all difficult to make. Yes, it’s  time consuming but that’s true of all yeasted pastry doughs.  And the recipe accurately reports that in the instructions. Next time, however, I do plan to double the pastry recipe and freeze half to minimize prep time next time out.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It has been said before that when the ingredient list describes items that are peeled, chopped, sliced, minced or whatever (like the scallions in this recipe), the prep time for the recipe does not include the time it takes to prepare those ingredients.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Linda, you’re right, but that’s not how home cooks think about time.   It’s “I’m starting now, and I need to get this meal on the table by X am/pm.   Can I do it in time?“.   NYT should change its approach since they are talking (mostly) to home cooks, not professionals, in this forum.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I liked this! It’s best the first day but is pillowy and soft snd delicious. Made it without a kitchenaid (just kneaded by hand) and still plenty good. I think I would have made twice as much filling if I were to make it again, I added chili crisp to the filling but still wanted some more. King Arthur has a similar recipe with tahini and honey in the filling which also sounds great.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I'm so rushed right now and these look delicious .  Can I just start with puff pastry as the dough and continue the recipe?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @LittleBird you can try puff pastry, but store-bought pizza dough or another store-bought bread dough (like crescent rolls) probably would work better. I haven’t tried it but I’m excited to hear how it goes!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It's a yeast dough, so maybe use premade pizza dough?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @LittleBird - I was thinking the same thing. Pizza dough can be tough, especially from a grocery store and crescent rolls have a strange aftertaste.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
These were excellent! I was making these to enjoy the following morning and decided to use my bread machine to mix, knead, and proof the dough.  I used my food processor to incorporate the butter into the flour before adding it to my machine. 
I also sautéed the scallions in 3 Tbs oil and added 1 Tbs toasted sesame oil off heat.  After refridgerating overnight, it took the buns about 1 hour to 2nd proof before the bake. 
I highly recommend the toasted sesame oil if you want to boost the sesame flavor!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These were quite tasty. The dough is easy to work with and baked up beautifully soft. I needed more oil than called for and still didn't have any left to brush on top. I added gochugaru flakes to the scallions (about 10, 2 small bunches) and put sesame seeds inside. I used non-dairy milk and I baked them like cinnamon rolls pressed together in a pan and liked the softness from less exposed edges. Although the dough rose on proofing, the final product did not rise much, and the buns were small.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Gluten free- I tried this with Bob’s Red Mill All Purpose Baking Flour (the blend with garbanzo flour), and they turned out great! I used kefir instead of milk, and did an overnight second rise because the garbanzo flour smelled so raw and dry. The dough was stiffer and drier than it would have been w wheat flour, but it did rise eventually, and the finished flavor is fantastic.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Em in the NW Note: The gf flour I used has no binders, so I added 1tsp xanthan gum per cup of gf flour as recommended on the package. This gave me a pliable dough that rolled out easily and rolled up nicely for slicing.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made these last night and they were a hit. I would have preferred a measurement of the scallions since not all bunches are the same. My Aldi bunches were a lot so I only used one. I also didn’t have any extra oil to brush on and ended up adding extra.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Currently making these. Far too little milk in the dough to make a soft sticky dough. I added at least an extra 1/4 cup of water during the kneading process and the dough is still quite stiff.

author: Genevieve Ko
Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Kelly thank you for your thorough answer! I will weigh next time and see if it makes a difference.
I’m live in a very dry climate so that may have had an impact.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Prepped these the night before and baked them in the morning. Was amazed at how easily they came together, how well they baked through, and most importantly, how good they tasted, especially with a chili crisp topping! Will certainly be making again, but next time with extra scallions :)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I reread the recipe to find measurement for cheese & was surprised not see any. Has anyone tried adding grated Gruyère or Fontina, which I think would be lovely…

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is based on the traditional Chinese scallion pancake recipe, as stated in the introduction. That is why there isn't any cheese.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are SO good!!!!  The dough comes together quickly and easily in a stand mixer.  I used instant yeast, since that's all I had on hand.  Doubled in size in 1.25 hours, rolled out easily.  Did a 40 minute second proof and then baked for 23 minutes, rotating the pan once half way through.  Next time I might brush the tops with an egg wash to help the sesame seeds stick and to give them a shinier bronzed finish.  But the final product was out of this world tender, flaky, flavorful and delicious

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I'm wondering why this recipe was formulated with active dry yeast, and if instant yeast can be substituted.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @thoughtful reader I just tried with instant yeast (twice!) and totally didn’t work. Not sure why…

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this recipe exactly as written with the only variation being that I toasted my sesame seeds before using them.  The rolls were excellent! They were flavorful and soft, delicious with the scallions and sesame seeds. I didn't have any oil left from preparing the scallions so I drizzled chili crunch over the rolls to serve. The recipe made 12 as promised and my husband and son polished off 8 as soon as they came out of the oven!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Please some hint on what constitutes a "bunch" each store seems to decide differently sort of like a handful = male or female hand -- thanks

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @ThomasJames, agreed! I’m in France where a « bunch » of scallions is 3 measly stalks. Weight would be good.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: So nice to see a savory recipe like this with no cheese! Severe dairy allergies mean I'm always making substitutions (I'll need to use soy milk) or, if cheese is the star, just skipping it. Its nice to see an option.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’m looking forward to making this Christmas morning! I’m curious though, what is the reddish sauce in the video clip?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @CE Genevieve says in the video that it’s chili oil and is optional. If you search in the NYT cooking app there is a recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @CE looks like chili crisp

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @CE It’s chili oil.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wondering if anyone has tried to make these with sourdough starter and if so how much starter did they substitute?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Elise exactly the question I have as well!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Elise I am thinking I’ll use some sourdough discard and still add yeast (I save it all—I can’t throw it away!). Maybe 30 g starter or discard max and keep all the other recipe proportions the same?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Followed the recipe closely. Since bunches of scallions vary in size I settled on using 2 cups chopped scallion. This was a good amount. I added a bit too much flour to reduce sticking, which made the dough easier to work with but also a drier bun. Next time I'd err on the side of a wetter dough. Though not difficult it took a lot of time. Would double the recipe if making for a crowd.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Very nice and soft dough with scallion flavor. I think next time I bake this recipe I will incorporate some sesame oil in the cooking of the scallions.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Use 25% less instant yeast than active dry. Instant can go right in with the flour. Or use the same amount as active dry and expect a quicker rise.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
These are perfectly delicious and easy to make. The dough is a pleasure to work with. I sizzled the green onions in browned butter (I used about an extra tablespoon) instead of oil and used only 1 bunch of green onions. I may use chopped shallots next time. Or no onions and add some grated cheese instead. Very riffable on the flavor profile you want. 
I rolled out the dough to about a 11x17 inch rectangle and thought that was plenty thin. A 12x20 inch is much thinner than 1/4 inch. Also err on the side of underbaking.  I baked 20 minutes and they were slightly overdone (my oven is accurate).
My cooking and baking mantra is to use a kitchen scale. I use mine almost daily. You’ll be happy you paid the $25,  Results in accuracy of ingredients, fewer dishes, measuring cups, bowls and much easier cleanup!  And a recipe is easy to scale up or down with a scale and  simple math.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The reader who suggested rolling out the dough between two sheets of parchment was spot on. No sticking!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
These came out delicious! I do wish I used more scallions (2 bunches will vary by store so it would be helpful to note the weight). I made the dough based on the weight of milk and flour and it came out perfect. 

I needed more oil at the end to brush on so I added maybe a tablespoon into the pan with the straggler scallions and heated it for a minute. Then I let the oil drizzle from the brush onto the buns instead of brushing because brushing them was making the sesame seeds come off.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can these be prepared, but not baked, and then frozen for baking later? If so, then thaw, let rise/puff, then bake?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I loved these! I definitely needed a savory bake after all the sugar of the season, and these hit the spot. I thought two bunches of scallions were going to be too much, but it was barely enough! Next time I might add a bit of sesame oil to the scallions. The one thing I need to do better next time is drain the scallions sufficiently to have that oil to brush on the top of the rolls. These are def going onto my make again list!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious! I had only one bunch of scallions, so I added some finely chopped shallots as well, and that worked. I also had some cooked bacon, so I added bacon crumbles. Highly recommend!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: now i'm eyeing my bacon grease wondering if i should sub for butter.  lol

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this recipe exactly as written. No dckin around. They were fantastic. Just watch for the dough to double and spirals to puff a little rather than relying on strict rising times (as is the case with all breads) and it will be great!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Recommend extending the second proof a bit longer. Could also use a wash of some sort before baking. Delicious nonetheless

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: For the non-bakers amongst us - I took The suggestion to use store bought pizza dough in the tube and it turned out delightfully! I let the dough come to room temp, unrolled it and rolled it out. Fast, easy and delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Heather Hi! Which brand did you use? I actually tried Trader Joe’s pizza dough and it was awful….

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are incredible! I made the whole batch but froze half after shaping the rolls. So excited to have them to look forward to! I found the dough super easy to work with and very forgiving. I used a stand mixer for the kneading step. The shaped rolls, which had been refrigerated overnight, needed a couple hours at room temperature to get puffy.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Accidentally ordered 2 large bunches of scallions and decided to make these buns.  Scallion buns were absolutely delicious.  All 12 buns were devoured quickly.  The buns pay homage to the traditional crispy Chinese scallion pancake flavor is a fluffy bun format.  I will definitely be making again.  

My only recipe feedback is to use kosher salt vs fine table salt.  I used fine table salt and it was a touch too salty for my taste.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My two benchmarks in a Chinese restaurant are scallion pancakes and Singapore noodles. These scallion buns are sooooo good, even better than scallion pancakes. I mixed the scallion oil used at the end with some chili crisp and that added another dimension! Wow!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this buns and the whole process was easy to make from making the dough to assembly the instructions were very clear. My problem with the buns though is that they were overwhelmingly salty so salty that my tongue was tingling. My husband and I tried to find ways to bring the saltiness down by using kimchi, duck sauce, but nothing worked because those flavors ended up cancelling the delicate flavor of the scallions. I think that a full teaspoon of salt in the scallion mixture was too much. I would totally reduce the amount of salt by half next time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
alternative to using serrated knife: use unwaxed unflavored floss (h/t Stella Parks - search for her cinnamon rolls).
As others have noted, it would be great to have more guidance on the scallions, since "a bunch" is not a standard unit of measure. I would have also appreciated having a temperature for the oil.
With that said, this was a really nice dough that I plan on using as directed, as well with other fillings.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can this be made without a standing/dough mixer?  I only have a traditional electric hand-mixer (i.e., for making cake or brownie batter, etc.)....

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Huge hit with 3 teenagers! The dough was very easy.  I kneaded by hand to 10 minute timer & only had rapid rise yeast. The flavor of the dough was fantastic~milk, butter and egg  paired well with simple scallions and oil.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These were pretty good, basically a savory onion cinnamon roll. I found that I didn’t have enough oil to drizzle over them at the end, so if I make them again I would add more oil. I like scallion pancakes better though.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious! I added some sesame oil into the scallion mix and let them rise for an hour once formed. Served them with chili crisp at a brunch and they were devoured!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: taste is ah-MAZ-ing! i found it very easy to follow. Would weigh out flour next time as my dough was too dry. I used fresh, local scallion bunches that were huge and very fragrant so the taste was just....so good.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The recipe says to roll the dough into a rectangle 20 inches wide and 12 inches long “with the wide side facing you.”  There is no possible way to interpret that other than to have the 20 inches long side facing you.  So as you roll away from you, you get a 20 inch long roll.  After watching the video, it appears that the 12 inches long wide side should be facing you, so that you end up with a 12 inch roll and hence, wider, flatter rolls.  Mine were narrow and tall, and therefore not at all as advertised.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @EMS yes! I did the same thing. Is it supposed to be the opposite?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @EMS I interpreted it the same way. I didn’t like how they looked as I was cutting them so I cut each in half again so they were shorter and flatter, like pinwheel cookies. I think they baked better that way, but I sacrificed the fluffy, larger bun described.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Does anyone know if the egg can be omitted/swapped for a flax egg for an egg allergy?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: A prep time of 5 minutes? How does that square with step 2 of the recipe, which calls for 10 to 15 minutes of kneading with a dough hook, not to mention the other instructions.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This sounds so delicious, and Genevieve Ko’s recipes are always outstanding. I have all the ingredients except instant yeast not active. Can I use instant or should I make a trip to the store for active? Thanks!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made these exactly as described and they were perfect, fluffy pillows of deliciousness.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These were a big hit in my house. The dough was easy to work into the desired shape. Someone had used one of my scallion bunches so I supplemented one with some fresh dill that I needed to use up. Light, fluffy and delicious. Definitely a recipe I’ll make again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These tasted amazing, especially if you like savoury salty flavors! The scallions were delicious and definitely worth the prep work. I used safflower oil instead of grape because I had it and it also has a high smoke point. I would add a bit more oil to the pan next time as I didn’t have much left for brushing the buns. Very straightforward and simple buns to make. Ate two of them right out of the oven!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Really delicious! A few notes - read Step 5 carefully, I didn't realize you were supposed to "separate" the oil out, so I put all of it into the buns. I don't think it made a huge difference, but when I got to Step 9 I was confused about the reserved scallion oil. Also, mine took much longer than 20m to bake - mine were closer to 40m before being finished. And 5m prep time is ridiculously short. But tasty and excited to bring these to a potluck!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Great recipe! For those without a mixer, I found the dough really easy to work with and was able to knead by hand without any issues. Buns came out plush and delicious, and were a hit for a New Year's Day potluck! Great dough to riff with other ingredients and flavors, too.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Being a lazy cook, I tried using pizza dough per someone's recommendation, and I don't recommend it.  It was like a scallion calzone...not bad, but nothing special.

I did not rate this because I did not follow the recipe so it is 100% my fault.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Is there a gluten free friendly substitution someone could recommend?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Anna, GF yeasted breads, rolls, and buns are very challenging to manage. Especially using pre-mixed GF flour blends. Check out Nicole Hunn’s gluten free on a shoestring website for tips and tricks. Her advice is based on some pretty rigorous testing and experimentation. Personally, I haven’t been brave enough to implement her bread recipes. But others I’ve tried, especially her DIY flour blends, are totally doable and are among the more reliable I’ve tried. Good luck!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can I use puff pastry or any other readymade dough from the grocery store instead of the dough made from scratch?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are absolutely deliicous!!! A good friend made them for me up through Step 7 and brought them as a gift to dinner (for our breakfast the next morning). I completed Steps 8 & 9. Wow!! We all thought they were so fantastic that I’m inspired to make them again.

author: Genevieve Ko
Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This recipe sounds delish, and after reading the comments, I'm ready to make them since I love scallions and bread--and there's no cheese!  (I follow a plant based diet.)  Will a plant based milk make much difference?  Please, someone respond to me.   Thanks.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Tante Stacey Plant based milk will not make much of a difference.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Fairly to new to baking, just got a Kitchenaid Stand Mixer 4.5qt tilt. 

I was short a .5 cup of AP flour so I swapped in .5 cup of bread flour and added an additional tablespoon of milk for moisture. 

I followed the instructions using my flex paddle to start and then switching to a new stainless steel dough hook on speed 2 but my mixer started smoking around 7-8 minutes in. Im looking online and seeing some advice that maybe I pushed the machine too far, max 5 minutes for dough hook. I did have it running for the full 5-7 minutes that I made the milk mixture prior. Any suggestions on what I did wrong here and how I can avoid in future? 

On another note, the dough came out smooth and was a little stiff but once it cooled down, had a nice elasticity, even after pulling it out a few minutes early. Perhaps with the stainless steel dough hook, I didn’t need the full 10 minutes or the bread flour made a big difference?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I found the dough a little bland. I made it a second time and substituted in some whole wheat flour and added some (didn’t measure but not much) ground garlic and snipped fresh rosemary leaves. Much better.
Then, at a friend’s suggestion, I made it using 1/2 t cinnamon, 1/2 t vanilla in the dough and again substituted in 1/2 to 2/3 Cup whole wheat flour. For the filling I used one can whole berry cranberry sauce, drained, and a peeled minced apple. Awesome.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Loved that the dough was a little sweet which seemed to complement the salty filling. Bread was denser than I expected. I would use more yeast next time and let rise longer. Really appreciate reviewers' suggestions to add garlic and other herbs and spices.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Definitely needs double the filling. The dough needed more salt than the recipe calls for. . I didn’t love this one… I think the idea is solid, but I think this recipe needs tweaking.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The enriched dough was a dream to work with. I think the filling needed more scallions and perhaps, sesame oil. Pretty excellent recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
These were quite delish!  I added a splash of sesame oil to the scallions while they were sitting.  Also, rather than the serrated blade, I use unscented dental floss to cut..
slide the floss under the roll and quickly bring the two ends together like you are starting to tie a knot...super easy.  I cut 12 out having rolled the dough on the long edge, 1.5 inches each.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’d like to prepare these for our holiday dinner, but oven time is tight and I’d like to make or prepare them ahead of time. How do you suggest I break this recipe up for a multi-day process?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Rachael I’ve made 2 batches and froze them after I cut them.  I baked up the first batch yesterday morning after thawing/proofing and they are absolutely delicious. I thought the dough was very easy to work with and I used no extra flour when I rolled them out between 2 sheets of parchment paper.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Any suggestions for making this with gluten free flour that is wheat starch? I have heard that gf flours might require slightly more liquid.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This looks delicious! I wonder what is included in the prep time? The post below says it doesn’t include prepping the ingredients. Is there somewhere in NYT that defines the terms?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Katherine Basically NYT and every other recipe site I know of starts the recipe time calculation with the ingredients prepped as listed. If the ingredients call for “2 bunches scallions, cleaned, trimmed and thinly sliced” then the timer starts after the ingredients have been prepped to that level. If the ingredients lists “3 grapefruits, peeled and segmented” for example, then the peeling and segmenting isn’t included in the recipe time. They do this because everyone preps ingredients differently. A chef may slice and dice an onion in 30 seconds, but that could take me 4 minutes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Brooke

You are the only person I’ve ever heard who has given me a reasonable explanation about this. Thank you!

I agree that it would be nice for NYT to publish a meal “prep time” definition. To build on that, I’d love for the NYT food app to have a continuously growing definition of terms :)

In any case, my cousin made these and they were great!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can I use puff pastry instead of the dough made from scratch?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: these are delicious, even though I didn't read the recipe thoroughly and didn't reserve the oil to brush on top.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These remind me of flower rolls, but baked instead of steamed.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My local bakery here in Orlando, FL, Chiffon Culture has something similar if not the same. I’m grateful knowing I have this recipe if I’m ever craving them. Thank you Genevieve!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Thoughtful Reader I have not made these yet, but the conversion from active dry yeast to instant yeast would yield about 4 g instant yeast.
@Corine As formulated this dough is about 60% hydration - to me, that is a very stiff dough. I would be increasing the milk to 150 - 160 g to increase the hydration to a bit north of 65%.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @CE -- What video clip?!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Ok. I haven’t made this yet but wanted to say there was a Hawaiian bakery in Berkeley called Ono Bakery that made something similar to this recipe except the buttery scallion bread also had a delish miso filling. OMG, it was good. For those who want inspiration for this bake…

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I tried this recipe and found it easy to make.  While it resulted in a perfectly pleasant dinner roll, I didn't find it nearly as exciting as described in the accompanying article.  If anything, it was a bit bland and uninteresting.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My dough turned out beautifully -- not sticky at all -- but did take longer to rise. I had no where near enough scallions to cover the rolled out dough. Will use more next time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’m not much of a baker, but have found that a scale to measure amounts in grams instead of cups has upped my game considerably. These rolls were awesome! I didn’t have any scallion oil at the end to add so I used a Trader Joe’s product called Crunchy Sesame Salsa Macha to brush on top.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Tried spacing them in a smaller pan as I would for cinnamon rolls. The result was many underdone centers. Next time I will follow the actual directions. Would like a second filling like barbecue pork. The dough is delicious and easy to work with. However, very soft, not like my idea of a traditional “morning bun”.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: in the chinese markets, they have pork floss. a dried shredded pork commonly eaten with rice porridge  now i'm wondering if that can be sprinkled on with the scallions and baked together.  mmmm.....

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Anyone know the gram measurements for the butter and sugar?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Cat the butter is roughly 42.5g and the sugar is 37.5g - if your scale doesn’t measure to the .5 of a gram it won’t make any difference to round up or down. It is annoying though! How am I meant to get cold butter into a tablespoon?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Sophia Butter usually comes in sticks with the tablespoon measurements on the wrapper. No need to put into an actual tablespoon to measure.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this today for a New Years Day party. I can't tell you about the taste because I have Celiac disease and couldn't give me them a taste.  I made them as an appetizer and cut them into 24 pieces. I served them with mascapone,  thinned with a bit of cream, and dill. I also had some smoked salmon available. The guests loved the rolls. I will most definitely make them again.  Great recipe, well of course it is. It's the NYT after all.b

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are good but actual scallion pancakes are easier and imo more interesting.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Beth I would agree with this comment.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: i am a big fan of scallion pancakes and like better as well.  But!  this is easier and can make more volume in less active time.  very tasty will make again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It sounds so tempting and I would love to try it. How would it be to use toasted sesame oil instead of butter?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Actually, they remind me more of an interesting twist on the classic Chinese steamed scallion roll. I should have measured out the flour (I rushed it), so the dough became elastic too quickly and didn't rise as much as I would have liked. I decided to go ahead and complete it anyway. I brushed some egg wash on top to get them golden brown. They were yummy little rolls!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These taste amazing and are a great rift on scallions pancakes. The dough was very easy to make. Love that the ingredients are typically in my fridge and pantry. My dough took a lot longer to proof. Even though I had it in my oven on the proofing setting it still took 3.5 hours. In order to have scallion oil at the end, I needed to lift the scallions out of the pot with a fork. (With a spoon, there was too much oil on the dough.) Spiral proof: again, took more like an hour.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious with half the salt plus extra scallions!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
These are truly delicious! I made the dough entirely by hand and was absolutely sure that I had messed up since the dough was perfection after only 10 min of kneading. But it all turned out great and the recipes got rave reviews from the family. Only change I made was doubling the scallions and next time might try 2.5 times the amount in the recipe. 
I also topped a few of the rolls with chilli crisp oil and those were great but the scallion oil topped rolls were the best.
I think this dough would be great with a sweet filling too so might try cinnamon sugar for an easy cinnamon roll riff or even Nutella!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Use a smaller pan, make sure they nestle together

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used 3 bunches of scallions from my normal grocery store and that was slightly too few. Also, I agree with other comments - watch the salt level. I used what the recipe called for and it was almost too salty, and I love salty. Tastes great with spicy chili oil, and it acts as the icing for these savory buns.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
1 tsp salt for scallions was too much for me 
Otherwise lovely recipe!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: For some reason the dough never rose for me. End product was ok, a great sesame pancake is easier to make and more of a crowd pleaser

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Tried making the dough twice, using a scale and following the directions exactly, using fresh yeast for the second batch. Both times I got a stiff, stodgy lump that failed to rise let alone double in size. Something off about the quantities of butter, egg, sugar for this enriched dough?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Allison I struggled with the rise too - after 3 hours it was a hard lump, on the dry side. It was a cold day, so I decided to give the dough a jump start before throwing it out and starting over. I put it in the oven, upper rack, with a bowl full of boiling water in the bottom rack. With that warm, steamy, humid environment, the dough doubled in size in about 90 minutes. The bake came out great.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made the recipe as directed and found the buns far too sweet for my taste. If I make them again I would reduce the sugar from 3TB to 1t. I would also double the amount of scallions and perhaps increase the salt.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These were oh so good! I am making another batch tomorrow. They keep in the freezer!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I really wanted to be blown away by this but it’s more like a scallion flavored dinner roll than anything else. Presentation wise, fantastic. Just a little lackluster. Kinda wish it had more of a filling.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These were amazing! I think I didn’t do as good of a job rolling out the dough thin enough, and I should have saved more of the scallion oil to brush on at the end. I also felt that maybe 20 min was too long for my oven. I should have kept an eye on them, but otherwise this was a very good recipe, and I would make them again with the three minor tweaks for myself!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: these are outrageously good. I am serving them again on Easter instead of hot cross buns

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These were really great! I accidentally made 13 buns, so I let one rise and baked it the same date, and let the other 12 rise over night in the fridge. I thought the overnight batch had a better, fluffier texture.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I followed the recipe as written, and they came out lovely. At first, I only used the white and pale green parts of my scallions. Then I saw the video and noted they included the dark green bits. I went back and added in the green parts.
After I arranged them on my baking sheet and let them rise for the second time, I used two chopsticks to gently pull them apart before baking. This worked very well. They were a huge hit at my party and all were devoured quickly.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Great recipe!  For my taste, could use 1.5-2x the scallion filling and the chili oil is a must.  The 2nd, in-pan, proof took nearly an hour since it was cool in my house.  Baking took 30-35 min at 375F to get the buns to an internal temperature of 205F, which is fully baked for this kind of bread.   

Two of us have already eaten 6 buns and they have only been out for 1/2 hour!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I have made these a few times. Tasty!! Also, dry the leftovers and make bread crumbs. I use them in my beef and pork meatballs. UNBLEAVEABLE.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Little Bird — or with a sheet of commercial crescent dough.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: go by the description of the final product, which would be a smooth, elastic dough @Janet CO

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: How many total scallions should be used?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Too much salt.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Thanks. This recipe is delicious and versatile.  The rolls would work well at brunch, lunch, or a party buffet, and they are much easier to make than scallion pancakes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are wonderful, the bun is so light and fluffy, very tender (even after rolling it to 1/4 inch thickness).  The dough was very soft and sticky.  I did let the buns proof longer after forming, about 45 minutes.  I used 2% milk because I had it on hand, didn't seem to detract from the softness of the dough.  I think I will use some sesame oil in the oil next time, just to give it a more Asian taste profile.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Currently making this recipe and I love the idea behind it ! I’m sure it will be a great snack for NYE or upcoming celebrations. 
I followed the metric measurements for the dough recipe and unfortunately, my dough barely came together, it wasn’t  just slightly sticky. So when using the metric measurements, I recommend adding about 20gr of flour to end up with a dough that can be formed into a ball and later be spread out. Also, since the dough remains sticky, using a bit of cooking spray on your hands makes handling the dough a lot easier. 
Thanks for the recipe!

I ended up adding quite a bit more liquid to get my dough the right-feeling hydration but I chalk that up to variety in ingredients and humidity.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Buns looked great and were delicious. The recipe was easy to follow. Agree that more scallion is needed to brush the buns after baking. My roll accommodated 16 slices and I felt like that was the perfect size for a crowd. My family suggested adding pork floss to turn it more like a Vietnamese bun but it is still great without it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Accidentally got chives instead of scallions. Still turned out great. Dough is easy to work with. Looking forward to making these correctly next time!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These were delicious and very much appreciated by my large Easter crowd (I doubled the recipe).  I refrigerated them over night before following the instructions and baking them. I would advise that a good bit of space should be left between rolls for the final rise and baking so that they can brown on all sides.  Also, don't worry if the dough seems too wet when mixing.    Once the dough rises, the texture is perfect.  I'd use the dough for sweet rolls in the future.  A fantastic dough!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made these dairy-free using Nutpods original in place of whole milk and Melt unsalted butter (until Miyokos returns to shelves!!).  I ended up making the yeast, sugar, milk mixture three times with two different brand new active yeast until I discovered that it wasn’t going to become as foamy in five minutes in a nut pod as it might have been in whole milk.  Simply took much longer (20-40 minutes) but my research said I didn’t need to wait until it was foamy as long as I was confident it was alive. That ended up being correct and they turned out beautifully. I served with a pimento cashew cream which has a little heat. Rave reviews.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made these yesterday following every step religiously.  The five-minute prep time is fantasy, but I had the morning free, and used much of that time in preparation!  Dough was sluggish in rising.  I used "proof" in my warming drawer, and my second rise took well over an hour, I gave it the time it needed.  They came out beautifully, & were a hit at the dinner party.  Regretted using sesame oil--too strong a smell, but took them elsewhere for dinner, so smell did not linger. Good leftovers too!
