author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are awesome!  I have made them twice, both times with white whole wheat flour and only 1 Tbsp sugar.  The first time I used skim milk and butter, the second time pea protein milk and Earth Balance.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Many have said the recipe was too salty. It calls for 1 tsp “fine salt” but I used 1 tsp Diamond Crystal kosher salt and it was perfect. Also used 1% milk since that’s what we drink, and it didn’t seem to negatively affect the recipe. I nestled my buns into a 9x9 pan so most of the edges stayed soft like pull-apart cinnamon rolls. Will make again!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Ok, totally different recipe, but I rolled packages of crescents (3 cans for a 9x13 baking dish) with pistachio cream and chopped pistachios. Each cab rolled separately then cut into 6-7 spiral rolls. Baked for 22 minutes at 375, brushed with butter and sprinkled with more nuts. A-plus breakfast. I just post it here because making the scallion rolls from scratch last weekend inspired the riff, and I could see doing all kinds of things with these, with or without homemade dough.

author: Genevieve Ko

These came out looking gorgeous, but the 1 tsp salt in the scallion mix is too much.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’ve made these twice and neither yielded enough scallion oil for me to brush the tops of the buns with. This isn’t necessarily a complaint, but I feel as though the texture of the buns would be more palatable with the brush of fat. The buns are a little on the dry side and don’t seem to develop much color during the 20-minute allotted bake time. The first time I made them, I thought it was user error but the second iteration yielded the same results. I now understand that these are inspired by scallion pancakes which are supposed to be fluffy and slightly dry so maybe these aren’t for me. Also odd since this uses a dough enriched with fat. The second iteration, I added sautéed ground beef to the filling. I drained the rendered fat (which was a mistake!) because trans fat is bad. But retaining some of the residual fat may have helped the buns develop more color. Also, mincemeat rolls!!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I recommend Abbott’s Vegan Chorizo. It isn’t oily or greasy, has a nice texture for mixing or layering and is mild to medium spicy (depending on your taste buds). If I am heating it at medium I add a little oil as it is a bit dry. You can get it at Fresh Thyme and some Target stores and I understand Whole Foods is stocking some Abbott varieties. It freezes well so I buy several at a time. A serving has 15g of protein so it’s good for vegans, vegetarians and carnivores!

commentBody: It seems rather arbitrary, but the 5 min of "prep" is straining tomatoes from a can, dicing onion and mincing garlic, and scrubbing potatoes and cutting them into cubes.  The 55 min is for all the rest, which is "cooking," including the 15-17 min in the oven.  Start this dish about an hour before you want to serve it, and chop the avocado, jalapeno and cilantro while it is in the oven.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I agree with substituting corn tortillas for flour, which I find absorb too much moisture in casseroles such as this and become too soft, almost gummy.  You can lightly fry the corn tortillas, or simply pass them through the sauce if you are rolling the enchiladas - either will make them more pliable for rolling.  Easier still - lay the tortillas out in the pan, cover with a layer of sauce, a bit of cheese, then repeat.  Three layers makes a decent stack.  
I’m considering using beef taquitos, sauce, cheese and topping with eggs - easiest of all, especially if these are served as breakfast for supper.  And before the “processed food” comments fly-no, we don’t eat convenience foods often, and an occasional meal of them hasn’t yet done us in - we’re in our 8th decade on the planet, active and healthy.  A little won’t kill ya!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: You could probably do this in a rectangular metal pan.  Make sure the pan you use is metal, not glass or ceramic as that will affect how fast the pan and batter heats up and cooks.  You can use thinner layers of batter and crumb and a shorter baking time, maybe 35-40 min instead of the hour for the much thicker bundt pan but check for doneness with a cake tester.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Joyce, a tube pan or a loaf pan would be your "next best." The reason being that you need some depth to facilitate the "cinnamon sugar" swirls that give the cake the "cinnamon roll" feel. Should be fine to try either of those. Just make sure you give some thought to how you will layer in the cinnamon crumbs.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
This unorthodox cake immediately intrigued me but it’s a siren song of a recipe and I washed up on the baking beach.  Total fail. 

5 eggs gives this cake an odd bouncy consistency and a distinct fortune cookie flavor to the crumb. Even using cake heating rods, it didn’t cook through, which is more on me but I did bake it 60 minutes. And by definition cake is supposed to be sweet, but this made my teeth hurt.  I also don’t think there’s enough acidity and liquid (no milk, sour cream or buttermilk??) I didn’t bother with the frosting.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I made this yesterday using Nordic-ware Bundt pan and was done at 1 hour, to get crumbs on tester, should have tested at 50 min. 5 eggs no problem and I used extra large. I believe the cream cheese made up for no milk, etc. and I think panko in cinnamon mix kept it not overly sweet. My husband and neighbor loved with cream cheese frosting. Will definitely make again but less time in the bake.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
Made this for tomorrow’s Christmas brunch. Looked wonderful coming out of the oven in my tube pan. Got waylaid and forgot to turn it out onto a rack after 15 minutes. Oops. Fully cooled cake was firmly adhered to the pan. Cut it in half and pried it out with a spatula. My son and I scarfed the collateral damage chunks that fell off. Delish! But next time I will definitely set a timer to turn it out on time.

While I’m here I might as well mention how otherwise forgiving this recipe is.  Since I only saw it this morning and we got a foot of snow on the Maine coast last night, I had to make do with what I had on hand because the plow guy didn’t show up until noon. Subbed about 70/30 mix of sour cream/greek yogurt for the cream cheese in the cake. Also cut the sugar to 1 1/4 cups because of all the sugar in the crumbles. Saved the 6 oz or so of cream cheese left over from Thanksgiving for a half-batch of frosting. The Christmas brunch crowd will just have to fight over it tomorrow. That’s life folks.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe as written and used a Bundt pan. Like others, I found the baking time too long. The cake was a tad dry. The cream cheese frosting helped compensate. Next time, I will err on the side of a shorter baking time. The instructions for testing doneness were a little unclear to me, and the crumb portion deceptively stuck to the tester giving the wrong impression of the doneness. The cake was a big hit at a dinner for 10 last night, with 4 leftover servings. Two now are gone and were delicious for breakfast. Tips if you make this: 1) bake for a lesser time than recommended; 2) allow more time to make the layers as the cake batter is a little difficult to work with without disturbing the crumb layers; 3) be sure to spread the batter layers all the way to the outer edge of the pan and the inner tube portion to ensure that the crumb layer is well contained after baking; and 4) consider using slightly less than 1/4 of the batter for the first layer, and thereafter gradually increasing so that the last layer of batter is more than 1/4 because the pan is narrower at the bottom and it will become more and more difficult to spread out the batter to cover the surface area of the crumb mixture.

commentBody: Can this be made with a hand mixer? Don’t have a stand mixer, but would love to try this recipe!  Thanks.

commentBody: This might be helpful to you, it's how I make most cakes (and will make this one): Wet ingredients creamed and mixed in larger bowl. Dry ingredients mixed in smaller bowl, then added to wet, mixed by hand til just blended. Old school I guess, less mess and the bakes come out deliciously!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Loaf pan report! I couldn’t find my Bundt pan so I used two 8 x 4” loaf pans, resulting in 2 cakes about 1.5” tall. I’d recommend using one 9x4” or 9x5” or so pan and I bet you could get all the batter in one pan, with nice height. The smaller cake baked in 45 or so, larger closer to an hour. I used a parchment paper sling and frosted upside down. Cake is good, cream cheese is nice addition and love the panko use. It’s a solid breakfast cake. Definitely would have been more dramatic in the Bundt pan! But I think calling it cinnamon roll cake does it a disservice, it’s really a cinnamon crumb coffee cake so your expectations should be for that, maybe that’s an obvious comment!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @Jourdan chiming in to say this cake ages beautifully! Made it Xmas morning and it’s getting better and better each day. It is also more “cinnamon roll” like today (day 3) than it was freshly baked. Would definitely bake again!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Here to add to the consensus that I only needed 55 minutes to bake. So perhaps start checking at 50 min. mark.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @Anne 300 grams of sugar for the cake and 85 grams for the icing is not really a lot of sugar for a cake.  Most iced cakes have a lot more.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Checked at 55 minutes and edges were burned. Left the cake in the pan as recommended for 15 minutes and even though I sprayed it WELL with cooking spray, the cake would not come out of the pan—it’s in pieces and chunks. So much for Christmas morning breakfast!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Ah darn! I've seen bundt experts recommend melted shortening with a pastry brush instead of cooking spray, then the 20-minute heat release method: Instead of just letting it rest in the pan, pour boiling water on a towel in your sink, set the bundt pan straight from the oven onto the towel, leave it there for about 20 minutes, then flip onto a plate to release. Good luck!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @Susan That happened to our cake, too!  I wondered how many people had to dig it out. The bottom of the pan had pieces stuck to it.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @Kate Thanks so much! Super helpful.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Double the frosting if you want the frosted cake to look like the picture.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @Waman I was thinking that would be the case. Exactly the note I was looking for before frosting the cake in the morning. Thank you!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
Excellent cake for brunch.  I like the reverse cream method, although the dry mixture was wet enough to ball up in the beater, which made me worry about the cake getting tough, so I rushed the eggs in, probably didn't get the mix quite right. 

I strongly recommend that the bake time should be edited - reduce to 50 min.  Mine was overbaked at 1 hr.

I also reduced the cinnamon to 3+ tsp, added 1/2 tsp cardamom.  Love the spiced panko trick!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Used King Arthur measure for measure and gluten free panko from Target and it was great. I imagine that the regular AP flour version would have a better crumb, but it was delicious in this GF version.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Just delicious. Had no problem with the baking time or the cake coming out dry. Followed directions to the letter and it took 1h05m in the oven. Low effort, high reward. Will make again!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Slather the interior of the Bundt pan with baker's goop and wait 10 minutes after taking the cake out of the oven. Cover pan with a plate and invert cake onto the plate. Make baker's goop by mixing one part flour, one part vegetable oil, and one part shortening.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
Time number 2 (of 3):  Made it in a loaf pan, but made the rookie mistake of filling it up to the brim.  Rose high and thought it was done, but inside was liquid!  I was able to salvage the edges.  They were all caramelized and really yummy.

Time number 3:  Split it into 2 loaf pans, did not frost.  Was really tasty but did not have the caramelized edges.  Did not frost, and it was a good solid cinnamon pound cake.

Next time:  Bundt pan, will increase ingredients (perhaps by 20%), & will frost

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
Dangerously good! 
Added chopped walnuts (~1/2 cup) to the cinnamon filling, and orange zest to the frosting. Both elevated the flavour and texture.
Baked in a 10" Bundt pan so was done around 55min (started checking at 45).
And the butter+breadcrumbs trick posted earlier worked to release the cake from the pan!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @Sj 6 for the cake + 10 for the frosting = 16, or exactly two 8oz packages.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Will this recipe work with a mini bunt pan (6 small bundts)?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: No self-respecting cinnamon roll needs frosting, even cream cheese frosting. And who needs all of the sugar. The goal, whether in a traditional cinnamon roll or as a bunt cake, should be to let the cinnamon shine. Do yourself a favor and leave off the frosting, not just in this recipe but in every cinnamon roll you make or buy in the future.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @ChasPDX completely disagree

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Couldn’t find my Bundt pan so used 9x13 at 350° for 40 minutes. Came out perfectly.  Froze both the extra cake and the extra frosting and have been enjoying for several weekends now.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Definitely 5 stars! The crumb was moist and tender, and the crunchy, cinnamon-y crust was divine.  I used matzo instead of panko and salted butter instead of unsalted—what I had on hand. I baked on convection at 350° to an internal temperature of 210°. It took 50 minutes. I cooled for just 12 minutes. I used the leftover cream cheese for a drizzle: 7 T butter, 2 oz cream cheese, 1.5 c powdered sugar, a pinch of salt, generous splash of vanilla, and enough plain yogurt to thin it out and make it tangy.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Will light brown sugar be ok to use? No dark on hand

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Of course you can use light brown.  It won't have as much oomph but won't suffer.  If you have molasses, add a couple of teaspoons to make up the deficit. That's all brown sugar is -- white sugar with molasses in it.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I don't have a stand mixer. Suggestions and how to use my handheld mixer.To make this work?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: You don't need any special directions.  Just make sure the cream cheese is truly at room temp.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I wonder if there is a place to get cinnamon that is certified as free from lead? This has been an issue with unscrupulous vendors adding cinnamon colored lead compounds to the powder. I’ve taken to grinding cinnamon sticks and sieving the powder for consistent fineness.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @Baja Bob You may want to check out Mountain Rose Herbs www.mountainroseherbs.com.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @Linda Penzey's Vietnamese. There is nothing better and you can trust that brand.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Penzey's

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: m in Brazil and can’t find panko. Any substitutions

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Could this recipe work to make muffins instead of a large cake?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I somehow forgot to check my panko and I only had seasoned (didn’t assume the recipe would benefit from Italian herbs and cheese) so substituted pulverized wasa bread and it turned out great, all the party attendees raved about it! Also I was low on cinnamon so rounded it out with cloves and nutmeg. I only baked it for an hour and turned it halfway through but it was drier than I would have wanted, will start checking at 50m next time.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: For all cake bakes, I coat pans with “cake goop.” One part solid fat (Crisco or butter), one part liquid fat (vegetable oil), one part flour (gf for gf recipes). Over the past few years, every cake I baked has plopped out of the pan perfectly. I probably read the goop recipe here.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Loved the flavor, but my cake was the opposite of "tender", as described.  It was kind of dry and hard...I followed the recipe exactly and wondering what I could have done better.  Still, delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @Robin- this sounds like it was over baked.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
I have made this 3 times so far and I haven't finished, it's not perfect yet (it will get there) but I would say that this is probably my favorite cake ever.  And that's saying a lot.  It's like a perfect cinnamon pound cake, and the cream cheese frosting makes it amazing.

Time number 1:  Made exactly as written
Cake seemed overbaked and I was worried.   Despite that, it was the best cake I've ever had.  But it didn't seem "tall" enough in the bundt pan.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: A wonderful cake - but it only needs 50 minutes bake time, as recommended.  The proportions, however, for the cream cheese frosting are way off and need good deal more powdered sugar.  You should consult another cream cheese frosting recipe . . . .   Otherwise, a lovely cake.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: This is in my oven now; it smells heavenly. I simplified the cake part of the recipe, as I often do. I used my Cuisinart to soften and blend the cream cheese and butter, then added the sugar and blended for another minute or so, then eggs and finally the rest of the ingredients were added. I find this makes a little less messsthan mixing the regular way.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: This recipe was not how I imagined it…really dry, and not much like a cinnamon roll. A bit disappointing for the amount of work and ingredients

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I agree with the title and the comment; more like a cinnamon coffee cake bundt, than a roll. And it was fussy enough to be not worth it to me. The fussiness wasn't worth it in the end, whereas with cinnamon buns, the payoff is substantial. There's another cinnamon coffee cake recipe on here by Lidey Heuck, and that was much better than this.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: As others mentioned, was done a little early—couple minutes before the 1 hour mark! I used Pam perfect release baking spray, started panicking as I read the comments while waiting the 15 minutes to remove from my Bundt pan—and it came out so easily no trouble at all! Removed it at about 12 minutes due to said panic lol. Very easy to make, looks and smells heavenly! Will make extra frosting as noted in the comments!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe to the letter, including using a Bundt pan, except for decreasing the baking time based on the helpful notes. It was good, but was a bit dry, even though I baked it only 45 minutes. From the aroma in the kitchen (amazing!), I’m sure it was fully baked at between 35-40 minutes, I just couldn’t believe that it would have been enough time as the recipe recommends baking between 60 to 80 minutes.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Planning to try this out this weekend. I have to wonder if the comments on cake dryness are related to this unconventional mixing method. I've made a lot of pound cakes and can't say I've ever started without "cream butter and sugar".

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I had high hopes for this cake, but it turned out pretty dry and bland. I even cooked it 10 minutes less than the recipe called for. The cinnamon part was good, frosting good, but cake needed more moisture!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Was going to make cinnamon rolls but saw this recipe & was curious. I take umbrage with the claim that a cinnamon roll is only good freshly baked, pop it in the microwave for a few seconds and ur good. But the cinnamon rolls I make are elite so they kinda great no matter what. Anyway, was curious so made it, and it's pretty good! Solid Bundt cake. Baked for 50 mins, removed from pan after 15. 5 eggs seems like a lot but I actually really liked the texture of the cake itself and would maybe use the recipe again in something else. I think cinnamon rolls are actually easier and take less ingredients than this but I may make again as a cake to take to an even or smth

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
There were not nearly enough crumbs. I didn’t get swirls, just odd bits of cinnamon-flavored something that tended to migrate to the sides of the pan. If I make it again, I’ll triple the amount of crumbs.

My frosting came out thick. That seems to be the intent of the recipe, based on the photo, but I wanted a creamier frosting. So I added milk to it in very small amounts until I got a consistency I liked.

The cake was overbaked at just short of an hour, but that may have been my oven.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
I added juice from half a lemon and I felt that gave it some extra tang and the suggestion to sub nuts for breadcrumbs was appreciated 
Nice recipe overall. Two thumbs up

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I swapped regular flour for GF and added 8 oz sour cream! Typically GF flour requires more fat and it worked out perfectly. Was insanely good.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Add a little sour cream until consistency looks right, and use a spring form pan instead of a Bundt. Zero complaints from me!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Nice flavour but, as noted by others, check at 50 minutes rather than 1 hour.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I followed it to the letter, and it was only okay. It was fine, but not particularly moist. I don't know that I'd make this again, but it wasn't terrible and my coworkers seemed to appreciate it. I'd be curious to see what it would be with buttermilk or the like used. FWIW I used a regular bundt cake, it was still raw-ish at 50, but perfect at 60.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
I’ve made this twice in a Nordic Ware Bundt pan. It was done at 45 minutes. More than that and it would be too dry.

I made the frosting the first time, but skipped it the second time. We actually prefer it without.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: A good recipe would’ve adjusted the frosting to 8 ounces of cream cheese, a standard package. Just a suggestion.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: You may note that the cake itself requires 6 ounces, and the frosting requires 10 ounces for a total of 16 ounces, or 2 standard packages of cream cheese.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Ah, but with the 6 oz in the cake batter, it comes to 16 oz (two packages) as written. So maybe on that basis it is "good"?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
Sj: maybe you didn't read the full recipe.  The cake batter uses 6 oz and the frosting uses 10 oz, so it's a full 16 oz, which is 2 packages.  They couldn't have made it more friendly.

Also (and this is me) I'd rather the recipe use the right amounts than adjust for packaging.  But maybe we have different ideas of what makes a recipe "good"!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: For the lactose intolerant, can I substitute oat milk?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: If you're asking to substitute oat mild for the cream cheese -- no.  But you can use tofu cream cheese.  You can't substitute a liquid for a solid.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @Angelica if you live near a Trader Joe’s they have a lactose free cream cheese (in a tub), or at Whole Foods they sell Green Valley Lactose Free cream cheese.  Both are dairy products with lactase enzyme added.  I’ve been using them for years and they’re fantastic - no weird taste or texture.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone made this gluten free?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @Anne V I made another cake recently (olive oil pumpkin!) and followed a commenter’s recommendation for replacing the flour with a 1/2 & 1/2 mixture of Bob’s Red Mill GF + almond flour, it turned out great! So I might try that here, too. I’m not sure how I’d replace the breadcrumbs in this recipe—maybe just omit it and see how it turns out?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @Allison they do sell commercially GF bread crumbs and panko

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: What can I use instead of panko (which is not easily available where I live in France)?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @Eileen Powis look on Amazon.de. They sell panko.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: White bread or brioche– tear the crumb apart (no crust) and dry in the oven, then pulse into fine crumbs in a food processor. You might find panko online or in a Japanese grocery.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
@Deborah I had wrote to NYT cooking about another Christmas Bundt recipe, here's the response I received. Hope this answers your questions.

When using smaller Bundt pans, you'll want to adjust the baking time as they will bake more quickly due to the increased surface area to volume ratio. Here are some tips to help you:

1. Temperature: You can generally keep the oven temperature the same as the original recipe unless your pans are exceptionally small (in which case reducing the temperature by 25°F might help prevent over-baking).

2. Baking Time: Start checking for doneness earlier than the recipe suggests—perhaps after 20-25 minutes depending on the size of your pans. A toothpick or skewer inserted into the center should come out clean or with a few moist crumbs when they’re ready.

3. Preparation: Be sure to thoroughly grease and flour each pan to prevent sticking, as smaller pans can sometimes make it trickier to release the cakes.

4. Filling the Pans: Fill each pan no more than 2/3 full to avoid overflow as the cakes rise.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @Anne - Thank you for your review! I had the same concerns and ended up using a different cake recipe (that uses sour cream). I added a tsp of ground ginger and a quarter-tsp of cloves to the crumb filling and otherwise followed the recipe. Delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Thoughts on halving the recipe? Although I suppose I can give huge slices to neighbors!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @SS it literally tells you how to halve the recipe on the top of page

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: (@Sharon maybe no need to say “literally” which sounds snarky? We all miss things that seem obvious to others!) @SS there is a drop down feature at the top right—click on “1x” and you’ll see options for halving or doubling the recipe. I imagine you could use an 8x8 pan?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @Susan. You aren’t the hall monitor.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Anyone try subbing almond flour for the panko?—I never have panko on hand :(

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: So I would love to make this for a daughter who loves cinnamon but who is unfortunately allergic to eggs....does anyone have any advice on what I could do, if possible? Thank you.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: This may sound frivolous but it’s a serious question. This is the second recipe I have seen recently that instructs the baker to whisk together the eggs and vanilla “in a large liquid measuring cup.”  Why a large liquid measuring cup? No more liquid is added to the eggs and vanilla, so the “measuring” feature isn’t relevant. Is it because it’s more easily poured from a measuring cup?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @Gilda Simon Yes, so it’s easy to gauge when you “pour half.” It’s a bit silly imo and not practical.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: try lemon zest, not juice, for the delightful lemon flavor. @DanF

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Merry Xmas & Happy New Year. I do wish the editors would participate and provide detailed alternative instruction for paid subscribers who do not use a stand mixer. Thanks.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: This recipe should be called “gloop city” due to the characteristics of the batter and icing!!!!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @Alan I don't own a stand mixer and have great results with my old hand held mixer.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: OOPS, I forgot to add that Vietnamese Cinnamon is more robust than ordinary cinnamon, so reduce the quantity by 25% to 30%. For example, if a recipe calls for 1 teaspoon of regular cinnamon, use only ¾ teaspoon of Vietnamese cinnamon.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Really delicious. My niece and I made this over Christmas and it a was a huge hit. We used sour cream instead of cream cheese in the cake batter and it was moist and delicious. No cream cheese (hence the sour cream) so no frosting, and no one missed it. The cinnamon swirl was sweet enough. A winner!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
Used a hand mixer, initially mixed with a large spatula first as I was mixing the eggs and dry together. Made sure the cream cheese was very soft by kneading a little. Melted about two tablespoons of the butter added to dry ingredients, along with remaining softened butter. 
For the breadcrumbs I added a tbsp of butter kept them moist based on others saying they were too dry. 
This was a hit, very good! Will definitely continue to make!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: This recipe was amazing. Just be careful to not use too much batter in the first layer even if it doesn't fill up the whole bottom because you won't have enough for the rest of the cake. Also if it is cut up and wrapped it stays better.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I liked this cake but can’t figure out why I didn’t love it.  Followed directions to the ‘T’. Removed at 1 hour - I agree with other commenter to check at 50-55 minutes. Timed the removal of the cake for 15 minutes and still had a little sticking.  Frosted as instructed and while it looked great - I felt it was missing some ‘oomph’ - nots nuts I never use nuts - just missing something.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Loved watching everyone relishing their cake, then quickly heading back for seconds while raving about it. Keeper of a recipe. Worth the fuss of layering in a bundt pan.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Made it and very good. Next time will check after 50 mins, one hr was too long bec it was a bit dry. Also I will put half cinnamon mixture instead of thirds. Last cinnamon layer on bottom was over baked. But will make again

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Last night, I followed this recipe exactly as it was written and it turned out pretty well. The only problem was that I had never used the oven in our vacation home (we haven't had it that long) and I am glad that I checked after 50 minutes because it was done early. (I went out and purchased an oven thermometer today.) The only thing I might try next time is to only put two layers of cinnamon crumb. I may not like it but it's worth trying. My husband has asked me to make it again!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly, and it came out perfect. No notes. Love the subtle crunch from the panko and the not-too-sweet cream cheese icing. Delish!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
Oh. My..
Wonderful..

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Made as written, done in 55 minutes in my finicky oven. Generously buttered and floured the Bundt pan and it came out easily. I was afraid I didn’t leave enough dough for the final layer, but it came out beautifully. Delicious, not too sweet, very cinnamon-roll-esque.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
So- I’m one of those people that could be considered “over the top” with some of my foods. 
I TRIPLED the amount of panko mix.
No regrets.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I will definitely make this again but next time I’ll frost the cake while still warm so it can run down the sides somewhat. The frosting is quite thick.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
Made this cake, and the icing.
The taste of the cake was fine, but the texture seemed a bit heavier than we like in a bundt.

Cream cheese icing was very loose and soft, even when chilled overnight. Still, it made a lovely, fluffy contrast to the cake. 

Crumbs were great, by the way. Made extra and sprinkled them on the cake when served.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Baking time was way off (and I have a newer oven and thermometer so they are not off) should have been much less. I put it in for one hour, smelled great, at 53 minutes it started smelling burnt and it was in some areas. I was going to try to resurrent it, but by morning it was rock hard so I didn't want to serve it to others. May try again with a lower temp and/or shorter cooking time.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
Recipe a bit fussy for what is basically a coffee cake.

More importantly, mine was  impossible to remove in 1 piece (or 10 pieces!) from teflon bundt pan prepared with cooking spray. COMPLETELY stuck.

If I made it again (probably won't) I'd use a tube pan for ease of removal.  The icing obscures the bundt flutes anyway.

Cooking to was 50m, a bit short of the recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Use Baker's Joy spray.  It's what works for bundt cakes.  My cake came out with nothing stuck to the pan.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Amazingly good and freezes great!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @Cornelia I used a vegan vanilla cake recipe, not adding as much sugar as recommended (1.5 c of sugar to 3c of flour. I think I used 1+1/4 c). It was really tasty!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: A happy coincidence, I only had 4oz of cream cheese - so I subbed in the extra 2oz with sour cream. Also, added chopped pecans to the cinnamon sugar mixture. It actually came out beautifully, lots of compliments from my guests! The cake was not dry at all, very moist and more dough like than cake.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, but bake time was way off for me. I pulled it out at 50 minutes and honestly could’ve taken it out even 5 minutes earlier than that. It was a touch over cooked. Fantastic flavor, crunch, and icing, though!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I made it last following the instructions as listed (except for bake time) and it turned out perfect. I love that the cream cheese frosting is not very sweet, because the cake is plenty sweet. My Bundt pan was 10 inches and only needed 35 minutes to fully cook. Use Penzey’s Vietnamese cinnamon and thank me later when your entire house smells amazing.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: delish! the panko bread crumbs made for the most delish crunch. I read comments and baked for 55 minutes; it was DEFINITELY done. next time I will plan to check at 50!!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: made it in a Bundt pan greased with Pam. could not remove the cake in one piece

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Mine was done with no crumbs on the tester at 1 hour and felt a bit overdone. That said, it's phenomenally delicious even when a little over and I plan to make it again this weekend. I'll just be checking starting at 45 minutes or so

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Good cake, but I needed more frosting. Was more powdered sugar the fix for that?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: This is a recipe for people who like coffee cake because it has the same consistency. The majority of baking I do is to be shared with coworkers and neighbors and they loved it. However, I found it to be underwhelming and I won’t make it again. Per another commenters suggestion, I reduced the cooking time to 50 minutes and still found the texture to be on the dry side. This is probably the only 5 star rated recipe that truly let me down.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: This is a good recipe, but was a little bit dry.  It did however not taste like a cinnamon roll as described . I do recommend doubling the frosting , as there was not enough to cover the whole cake. Leftover frosting of this type freezes very well. Happy baking!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Cinnamon Roll Bundt Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Definitely test at 50m, 60m would be overdone.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I really like the flavor of these cookies, without the royal icing. With the royal icing the sugar overwhelms the matcha flavor. So, next time I either leave out the icing entirely, or if I keep the icing I add a half teaspoon of matcha to the icing mixture. The recipe instructions were spot on. Will make again!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: i made these cookies as is and loved them but my son couldn’t sleep because of the caffeine.  so i substituted 2 teaspoons of golden milk powder from diaspora co.  the yellow from the tumeric was perfect for his construction vehicle shaped cookie cutters.  delicious too.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: who has room in their less than full size to refrigerate fully rolled out sheets of cookies!?!?!? really? will have to do the old fashioned way and use flour.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @dLynne Haha! I usually use my back porch as an extra fridge in the winter!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @dLynne If you’re lucky enough to live in a cold climate, the garage works well for chilling large items!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Alton Brown just commented on his latest video for gluten free sugar cookies - he uses a sheet of ice pads under the pan and covers with clean kitchen towel.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wow! these were delicious.  I had some matcha that was about to expire, so I figured I’d bake with it.  These are not very sweet which I love.  I will make these for my annual Christmas cookie decorating party.  They are perfect for decorating bc the light sweetness compliments the sugary icing.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: frosting was not really needed for taste

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are pretty. We added some almond extract in addition to the vanilla for flavor (it needed it). We found that we needed more liquid than just one egg yolk so added more egg. You don't need to put the dough in the refrigerator before rolling.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: is it safe to use the egg white?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Queenie  In the past, I’ve used pasteurized egg whites that are available in “milk” cartons.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Are the amounts right? I cannot see how you can get 18 cookies from this recipe, much less36.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Really cute cookies! The matcha was very subtle and mostly for color, you’d probably need to use a high grade matcha or add a little extra to get that flavor to come through. Definitely a small amount of dough as written – I doubled the recipe and ended up with 30 standard sized trees (3”).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I added a little less sugar (-5g?) and slightly less flour (-10g) in making the dough and underbaked them just slightly for a slightly softer and chewier cookie with less “snap”. Also be sure not to over cream the butter and sugar bc I did on one batch and they turned out greasy

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: None of my tree shaped cookie cutters were 2".  They were all 3-4".  So I only got 16 cookies out of this recipe. That said, they were delicious!  I just need a smaller cutout to be able to use this recipe for a cookie exchange.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly and they came out kinda dry. However, my regular sugar cookies came out perfect. I feel like it would be better to just add matcha powder to a standard sugar cookie cut out recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: i doubled the recipe and it came out to about 24 square (2 in by 2 in) cookies. the salt might also be a bit much. overall, pretty good! make sure to really incorporate the flour into the butter at that step!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Haven’t made this TBH. But it is very similar to one in Cooking Light from 1983 that is a much-loved recipe for me.  The differences are 
=1/4 cup granulated sugar vs the amount here 
=3/4 cup “margarine” (I substitute butter 1:1) instead of oil. They stay soft- see below. 
=cinnamon,clove, ginger spice mix (gotta say, this spice mix sounds excellent)
=using a cookie scoop to form, and rolling in a small amount of granulated sugar instead of icing. 
=baked at 350F X 10 minutes and taken off the cookie sheet immediately which I think is the secret to keeping them soft. 

I’m offering this info in the spirit of simplicity, but I am sure this is a wonderful recipe as well.  This makes 3 doz.  Each cookie has 70 calories FYI (even with all that butter!) for those who would like to know that.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Adjacent I like the idea of rolling them in sugar instead of the icing. Thanks!

author: Genevieve Ko
Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Though I’ve not yet made these cookies may I suggest white pepper for the chai blend. Big fan of chai & always went with white pepper as it adds a more perfumed smokiness. I’ll let you know how they turn out .
Thank you

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @frani thanks for the tip, looking forward to hearing how it comes out for you!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @frani Instead of black pepper or in addition to?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @frani It would help if you could follow up and mention how much white pepper was used.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: NYT newsletter remarks: “After testing nine different versions of gingerbread, I landed on this one, which my colleagues at The Times’s Los Angeles Bureau deemed the best. It also happens to be the quickest. Using oil in the dough not only saves time, it also keeps the cookies moist for days and allows the complexity of the spices to shine. You can start with a store-bought blend (this is my favorite) or mix your own (the proportions are in the recipe tip).”

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Sylvie if rolling out should we chill instead of leaving at room temperature?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Very nice.  Made this morning with homemade chai spice blend as outlined in the 'tip' note.  First bite and the taste was subtle but soon enough, quickly bloomed.  Did not make icing as I don't prefer a really sweet cookie.  Definitely would make again, perhaps with a more aggressive spice blend.  Dough was easy to come together and work with; and the bake came out nicely cracked and round..

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: blackstrap molasses comes from the 3rd phase of making molasses. so they come from the same ingredients but one is lighter and sweeter, while blackstrap is more robust, less sweet and a tad bitter. You  can definitely use it but regular molasses will yield more universally enjoyed flavor. But we cook for our own tastes so go for it! @Heather

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These cookies are easy and delicious, as promised! Followed the recipe almost to a tee; my only change was using slightly more than called for--2 teaspoons--of the chai spice blend. Drizzling the icing over the cookies used only about half of the icing.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I think we’re the first to comment having actually made this!

We made as directed, using our own chai spice mix (~2 parts cinnamon:1 freshly ground cardamom:1 ginger: 1/3 nutmeg: 1/3 black pepper:1/3 allspice). 

Loved how they turned out. The texture is perfect. But next time we make it we’ll add at least twice the spice, maybe 3x. 

The icing is nice, but the chai flavor is *very* faint. I think next time will also experiment with ways to concentrate the flavor a bit - maybe squeeze in a few drops of ginger juice or use lemon juice for a bit of contrast to the cookies.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: What's the difference between molasses and blackstrap molasses? I have blackstrap and would like it use it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Heather blackstrap is much stronger flavor. It is quite bitter. It can seriously over power baked goods. It also is less sweet.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Heather I would not use it. Made that mistake once thinking I could directly substitute and it ruined the dish.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Heather If you’d like to use the blackstrap molasses you already have instead of purchasing additional regular molasses, I would suggest using less. Perhaps 1/8 cup blackstrap and substituting the other 1/8 cup with maple syrup or agave syrup, depending on what you have at home.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Ok this is going to elicit some horror but does anyone use the contents of a chai tea bag instead of the spices? I’m ridiculously lazy.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Larry hi Larry! Definitely wouldn’t do this since a chai tea bag has black tea leaves and not just the spices. I’ll tell you we just dumped a lot of spices into the bowl that we already had. Close to doubled the recipes spices and they’re nice! Best of luck.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Larry I bought chai spice from my local spice shop but usually you can buy powdered chai spice in tea shops or coffee shops. Amazon certainly sells it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @LarryThinking the same thing myself….

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I only had blackstrap molasses so I added 2 tablespoons of brown sugar to counter the bitterness. Also rolled in sugar vs making the glaze. Baked 11 minutes. Very easy and good!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious ginger cookie. We added a teaspoon of the chai spice blend to the icing and felt it enriched the flavor of the icing.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
“The cookies will keep in an airtight container for up to 3 days.”

Am I the only one who finds these number always too low??  A cookie in an airtight container will last many more than 3 days, probably about a week in my book.  Am I wrong?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: You are right.  They start drying out, but they are still tasty.  I probably wouldn't give them as a gift on day 4, though.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Even in an airtight container, there's a rather radical texture drop-off in the first 24 hours. Days 2-3 have been relatively similar, the glaze/frosting helps a bit with that. It depends on whether or not you want them to taste as close to their potential chewiness out of the oven. If you don't mind something a bit more brittle, they still taste OK (not stale) so far 3 days after baking stored air-tight.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can this batter be rolled out and used for cookie cut outs?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Kelsey Woodward I just finished making these! In my opinion the dough does not have enough structure to roll these out, they barely hold their shape after scooping them into balls. Perhaps if you added more flour and chilled the dough in the fridge for a couple hours you could though. The finished product tastes and looks great though!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Note for substituters: I made with half regular flour / half almond flour and did half regular sugar / half coconut sugar and it turned out amazing!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Substituted garam masala (Morton & Bassett) for the chai spice which makes for a wonderfully intriguing cookie! Next time I’ll reduce the sugar, though. Rolling the dough balls in sugar results in a nice little crunch.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Are we sure that 1/4 c of molasses is 117g? Which molasses? My dough is way too wet. I weighed all of the ingredients, used G’mas molasses and the maths don’t seem to be mathing. Thinking it should read more like 85g.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@BeagleKnievel I asked Google “1/3 cup molasses in grams” and it said:

A 1/3 cup of molasses weighs approximately 112 to 113 grams, though it can vary slightly by brand, with some sources giving 112g (Sweet 2 Eat Baking, ~80ml), and others 113g (Southern Living, Allrecipes, Cotswold Flour, ~4oz).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Thank you for including a recipe that uses oil rather than butter.  These cookies are good!  I didn't pay attention and accidentally dumped in the full spice mix instead of 1.5 teaspoons.  I would do that intentionally next time. :)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Debbie same thing happened in our kitchen! We reached the same conclusion!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Rolled in sugar the second time I baked these, instead of using a glaze, and much prefer the result! These cookies are so easy to make and they're so tasty.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Gluten and egg allergies, so I used GF flour and flax. (The good thing about GF flour is you can BTHO and the dough doesn't get tough; downside is that it can be drying, so I had to add a few extra tbsps of water.) The cardamom in the spice blend kicks up what is otherwise a pretty basic molasses cookie.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Tricia
Which brand of GF flour did you use?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made these this morning and they are delicious!  Maybe good enough to make onto the yearly Christmas cookie list.  I made the chai mix as directed, added a bit more to the batter, and also added about 1/2 cup finely chopped crystallized ginger to the cookies.  For the glaze, I mixed the rest of the chai mix with the confectioners sugar instead of brewed tea (used water instead).  The color contrast isn’t there but the taste is deepened.  These are un-fussy winners.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Love how quick and easy these were and the flavor is really good.  The chai tea in the icing is a nice complement.  I used GF flour and they turned out well.   Otherwise, followed the recipe except used blackstrap molasses since that's what I had.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @KH I did the same. This is an error in the recipe unfortunately.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Substituted 2T blackstrap molasses and 2T honey . Turned out great.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are excellent. I did not even add the frosting. I let the dough sit wrapped for two days in the refrigerator and it was so easy to handle. Great cookie!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Scrumptious cookies!!! Bigger dough balls made the yummiest gooey sweet gingery center! Honestly liked them more without the glaze but the glaze is good for style points.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Could not be more obsessed with these cookies. Everyone is asking me to bring them to everything now. Here are my additions that make them much better IMO:

- USE TWICE THE SPICE MIX!!! (Except the cardamom which is intense!)
- 1:1 honey:brown sugar instead of molasses - better flavor and texture!
- 1/3 cup Applesauce instead of egg - softer and chewier!
- Bakes 14-16 min in our oven until fully cooked through (still deliciously soft and chewy)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Do they freeze well?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made these vegan by subbing one flax "egg" (1 tbsp ground flaxseed whisked with 3 tbsp water) for the egg; otherwise followed recipe exactly. They are delicious! Chewy, soft, spicy - haven't even iced them yet and I may not!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: In the market for a really good gingerbread cookie, but...two strikes against this recipe from the first graph: 1) soft and chewy. Yuck. Who wants a soft and chewy cookie? 2) The product you recommend is $14 which makes no sense. So you use some and the tin sits there forever. I don't get these recipes with ingredients that are not useful in this day and age of outrageous non-affordability. Ok. So I'll make the cookies anyway.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Joe in Wisconsin
Do you mean the Chai Blend is too expensive? Make a cup of tea and enjoy!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Joe in Wisconsin: I ordered the tea and with the 10% discount it was still $17.60 😂 Hopefully, I can drink the rest of the tea for the next month 😊 or two. . . You might try the Earl Grey cookies, they are excellent although it requires shortening 😬

author: Genevieve Ko
Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Lois it’d taste different, but did you read the Tip at the bottom of the recipe? It gives you directions to make your own chai blend

commentBody: Would this dough be good for rolling out and cutting into shapes?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I just made these… and wish I’d followed my intuition. I would sub for half butter and make them less sweet.

commentBody: Gluten free version not so great.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can you use avocado oil?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Amy I used avocado oil and the flavor was fine.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Husband & I are not "sweet-sweets" people. And I am a lazy baker. So we LOVED this recipe. Made without the icing. Sooo quick, apart from letting the dough rest. Simplicity itself!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: can I use butter instead of oil? I just love butter

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The texture won't be the same. In this case, the oil serves a particular purpose in the bake. They'll be less chewy and moist if melted butter is subbed measure-for-measure for the oil.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Convenient this recipe was posted on the newsletter today; I was looking for a cookie recipe to bring to our dairy-free neighbours. Used a 2 tbsp scoop and 10 mins was still perfect to get them cracked and puffed. Too lazy to brew tea for the glaze so just used chai spice and a little oat milk to thin. Surprised how good the consistency of the cookie was, I've done other oil-based cookies before and they weren't nearly as good. This one comes close to my standard, the molasses cookie from the silver palate.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’m dying to try this with slivered roasted almonds (ala TJ) and a “few” mini dark chocolate chips… and likely just half the icing.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Just completed as written but used white pepper as suggested in chai spice mix. I agree with the others I will add 2-3 times chai spice mix to dough. Chai taste just wasn’t strong enough for me in original recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @MSG I used the chai spice in the tip, which is 3x the recipe. So I hope it doesn’t gag my guests…

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I liked these very much----the ease of making and baking them, the perfect roundness of the cookies and the taste!  The flavor is mild, so not sure the name is a good fit? You'll be disappointed if you are expecting any tanginess. I didn't ice this first batch because I've got to buy some chai tea tomorrow but they are sweet enough on their own. I am going to add some diced candied ginger to the remaining dough just for fun.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made these today and followed the recipe for the cookies exactly. They turned out great! Soft and delicious. I didn’t have chai tea for the glaze so I just did a little bit of chai spice and water. 10/10 would make again!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Add chai spice to the frosting, it’s a little weak with just the tea for flavor. Otherwise fire 🔥

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Recipe did not work for me. The molasses refused to be whisked into the oil-sugar mixture. No matter how hard I whisked the mixture, the oil separated out and pooled on the surface. Even using an immersion blender did not work.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: 1/4 cup molasses = 84 grams not 117

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Trish I asked Google (note recipe calls for 1/3 cup molasses, not 1/4) how many grams 1/3 cup of molasses is:

1/3 cup of molasses weighs approximately 112 to 113 grams, though it can vary slightly by brand, with some sources giving 112g (Sweet 2 Eat Baking, ~80ml), and others 113g (Southern Living, Allrecipes, Cotswold Flour, ~4oz).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Trish I asked Google:

1/3 cup of molasses weighs approximately 112 to 113 grams, though it can vary slightly by brand, with some sources giving 112g (Sweet 2 Eat Baking, ~80ml), and others 113g (Southern Living, Allrecipes, Cotswold Flour, ~4oz).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious and easy. Appreciate the person who recommended adding extra Chai spice to the glaze. The tannins in the tea really pull everything together.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Mine came out with a VERY strong molasses flavor. I think two factors were at play: I made these by weight rather than cup measures, only after the fact reading comments that the molasses weight as published was incorrect (should be 85g, not 117g!) AND it turns out my molasses was blackstrap but only identified as such in the fine print on the back of the label. Oops! The texture is lovely, though, so I’ll try them again with less/different molasses. Also: rather than icing the cookies, I rolled them in powdered sugar mixed with chai spice before baking and they crinkled beautifully. Err on the side of underbaking to keep them soft and chewy!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I have a tin of loose tea, chai spiced.  Could I use that in the dough instead of just the chai spices alone, or would it be too harsh tasting?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: You could try that, although the listed substitution for the premix spice blend does work well (I used it). If you do opt to use loose tea, be sure to grind it down very finely before adding it to the dough, as bigger leaves aren't always the best texturally in baked goods. Wouldn't use more than 1-1/2 tbsp, and would add maybe a touch of citrus zest as a precaution to balance potential bitterness from the tannin?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: How would one freeze these for making ahead? Would freezing the dough balls or the baked cookies be better?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I think you'd risk dehydrating the outside of the dough through freezing depending on how you'd do it. Depending on your timeline, with a light coating of neutral oil and thoroughly wrapped in saran, the dough will keep in the fridge for a bit (I'd comfortably say 3-4 days having dealt with the dough). If you opt to freeze the baked cookies, do so in as airtight a container as you can manage without glazing them first. Add glaze when thawed.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I froze the unbaked cookies, then popped them in the oven when needed. They were perfect.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I can’t believe all the comments from slackers who haven’t made this yet want to tell us how to improve it. They should collectively pound sand. Recipe comes together quick; followed it to the note except I left out the two T of chai tea-I can’t imagine that makes that big of a diff. Will make again!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this pretty much as soon as the recipe hit my inbox as written, save for amounts (please note, it's been updated since I baked): by weight, the grams-for-vol for the AP, molasses, and sugar were off. I redid the g conversions while baking. That said, this is easy to make. Lightly oiled, wrapped, and fridged mine for 4 hrs before baking. Texture day-of was incredible, day 2-3 -- only good to OK. Would recommend small pinch of flakey salt on the icing!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Such a great cookie! I didn’t have chai tea but swapped equal parts cinnamon and cardamom, probably 1/2 tsp each in the glaze. They were the star of my cookie box.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Joy the recipe calls for 3-3/4 teaspoons of the chai spice. Do you mean you used less? I ended up using a little more at 4 teaspoons because the spice blend is one of my favorites.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Followed the recipe EXACTLY - seems there were some edits made a few days after the initial recipe was posted, and I used the updated recipe (1/3 cup molasses and 3.75 tsp chai spice) - I did weigh the ingredients that indicated measurements in grams. These turned out great, even living at high altitude I didn’t make any adjustments and had no issues. I got just about 24 cookies from the recipe, next time I might make them just a tiny bit smaller to try and get closer to 30 cookies.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are delicious! I was fine using blackstrap molasses + a bit of extra sugar. Using chai to mix the icing is a magical extra touch. I plan to make these several more times this season!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Just made these - I doubled the recipe and had a couple sheets to bake. I put the dough in the fridge for about 2 hours and then rolled out all the balls. The first sheet baked perfectly with nice big cracks but the 2nd and 3rd sheet had a lot less cracking and almost seemed like they were too warm going into the oven. I would recommend chilling the dough before if you want the traditional ginger cookie cracks. 

Otherwise they were delicious! Although I haven’t decided on icing them yet.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These were SO good! I doubled the spice mix provided in the “tips” section for a single batch, because I love it spicy. I used canola oil, and I did not add a topping or glaze, and I thought they were delicious without the extra sweet. I get locally milled wheat and rye flour, so I did a cup of AP, 1/2 whole wheat, 1/2 rye, with just a splash of egg nog to make sure it was all moist enough. In my Minnesota gas oven, they were plenty baked in about 8-9 min.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These cookies came out perfect! I used gram masala and ground ginger for the spice, which seemed to work fine; texture was moist and soft. Will definitely keep this recipe in rotation.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The softness and chewiness of these grew on me (as compared to regular gingerbread). Used all of the gingerbread spice that the "tip" made, as per suggestions--and that was the right call. As per others' suggestions, rolled the cookies in sugar before baking--perfect! (so didn't ice). Suffice it to say DH and 20yo son want me to make more.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’ve made these three times in the past two weeks for three different crowds, including a batch that were gluten free (King Arthur GF flour). All three groups said these were the best gingerbread they’ve ever had, and I agree! I used the King Arthur Chai spice blend as a handy shortcut and the taste was perfection. I also made a batch with a maple glaze due to running out of tea and it worked beautifully as well.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These caught my eye immediately when posted and I knew  I’d be making them for Christmas this year. I followed the recipe exactly with one exception based on reading comments - I doubled the spices (I used the DIY chai blend in the “tip”). I’m so glad I did - these are sooo good with chai tea glaze drizzled on top! Although it took a little longer I followed the advice of putting only one pan in at a time. I did make a mistake with one tray and left it in about 1-2 mins too long by accident and ended up with dry/hard cookies with burnt bottoms. The 10 mins listed was perfect, not a minute longer. I’m tempted to make a second batch …

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
These felt so much easier than other gingerbread cookies and will now be my go-to recipe. (Sure, not for fancy shapes but round works just fine and tastes the same.) 
Doubling the recipe worked great and yielded 40 generous cookies with 1T batter each. 
I skipped the glaze and rolled them in sugar and that was perfect for my cookie gifts. 
Also subbed white pepper instead of black pepper in my spice mix and it worked very well. These cookies have the right balance of spices and are soft and moist.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this gluten-free using 1.5 cups Robin Hood GF flour and 1/2 cup Costco almond flour - both sifted into measuring cups. I also doubled the chai spice mix and used the bulk of it for the cookies - with remaining in the icing. Let it rest on the counter for 30 minutes as per instructions. Mixture was sticky but did form a smooth ball and released quite easily from my hand. Baked on middle rack, one tray at a time for exactly 10 minutes at 350. Removed from oven and immediately slid the loaded parchment onto a cooling rack. Made 28 cookies ~2.5 inch wide cookies [flat-ish but still fluffy and moist]. Drizzled with icing spiked with ~1 tsp chai spice mix. DEE-licious!! Bringing to an Xmas dinner hosted by a gourmet cook - who will doubtless be as impressed as we are! My new favourite recipe - thank you! I think the 1/2C almond flour adds a nice chewiness to the GF cookie as well as helps bind and stabilize the dough.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My teenage granddaughter and I didn't want to wait, so we cooled the batter for a few minutes and made it into rounds. They came out crisp on the bottom and pillowy on the top.  The spice mix was perfect as was the icing. I admit to using oat flour instead of white, because that's how I roll.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I followed the recipe exactly, except that I was a little short on molasses, so it wasn't a full 1/3 cup. Still tasted great!

I used a #40 cookie scoop which made 24 cookies; I did bake the 2 trays separately as directed, 11 minutes each.

If you are looking for a good chai spice, I highly recommend King Arthur's Chai Spice. They generally carry it in the fall and through the holidays.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@“Neutral Oil” Sub 
You could use neutral (non-aromatic) coconut oil. (The regular, non-neutral coconut oil will likely impart a coconut flavor / scent). Olive oil is not a neutral oil; it has a distinct flavor and aroma.

commentBody: Molasses is a key part of the flavor and texture for these particular cookies, so I don’t recommend subbing. However, to your point on only using a little bit— molasses pretty much lasts forever. As long as it’s stored well it can go for years. I only make molasses cookies around the winter holidays and it’s nice knowing it’s always there when I need it!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Do not overbake! I never saw cracks in my cookies, and when the tops were dry they were still extremely soft— and it turns out that’s what they should be if you want them chewy. The spice flavor blooms more with the softer cookie, although dipping a crunchy one in milk is a bit of a remedy. Fun enough to make that I will try again, perhaps as others have suggested with a bit more of the spice blend.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I did it by weight and had no issues @BeagleKnievel

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: As I compared this recipe to my favorite spice cookies (the internet-famous elevator lady cookies, originally from the I Hate to Cook book), I noticed that the weight of 117 grams for 1/4 cup of molasses is a good bit more than the 85 grams I got (or see in other sources). For others who measure by weight, take note! Back to the comparison: the two recipes are close, with these chai cookies having a bit less sugar, spice, and oil, which makes sense as it yields slightly thicker/ chewier cookies than the  "elevator lady" recipe.

commentBody: Delicious! I followed the recipe (including spices in the "Tip") and they came out perfectly. Quick, simple dough and easily doubled. For the icing, I used Tazo Skinny Chai Concentrate (in the carton) and added a splash of milk -- super easy and boosted the chai flavor. Recipe states they're best day-of if iced, but I think they were even better the second day. Once the icing glaze dries & hardens, store them in a closed container and they will stay very moist and soft.

Is there something wrong in the ingredients list?  I double and triple checked to see if I had made a mistake.

Go easy on the salt in the icing! A pinch is probably perfect, I used closer to a dash. That said, I will definitely make these again!

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Fantastic! These will be a Holiday go to and often in between! Crispy outside, chewy inside. Flavor is bright and balanced. Small if you use a little cookie scoop - so perfect two bite. I let the dough sit wrapped in parchment on my granite counter overnight and then baked. Really easy recipe - appreciate the tip to make my own chai spice. I did not glaze or sugar - they are really lovely

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: So easy and just perfect! Only switch was to add a teeny bit of fennel to the spice mix. And spiced up the glaze a little more too, but will add still more next time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Loved these cookies. The spices smell amazing when you’re making the dough. Thank you to other commenters who suggested rolling in sugar prior to baking instead of icing. Yumm!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I'm not a particularly accomplished baker, but I have an empty nest this year and more time than usual. These cookies surprised me with how simply the dough came together. I wrapped it up and put it in the fridge, intending to bake the next night, but I ended up with a cold and didn't actually make them 'til the *next* night. By then, I had read the 1st few comments here & decided to just roll them in sugar instead of drizzling w chai tea icing. Delicious!!! I'm so proud to give these as gifts!!

author: Genevieve Ko

Someone suggested peppermint ice cream would have been good as well.  I will make these again, and make sure I have the correct molasses.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made these cookies (mostly) as directed, I had to chill the dough overnight for lack of time to bake. These were AMAZING! The chai glaze was incredible, but I’d double the spice in the cookie next time I made these. They disappeared at the Christmas party I went to and came together so quickly.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Blue Lu density of oil and molasses are different hence the different volumes/weights specified

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made these yesterday. They’re good, but as others have noted they need more spice. I made up the spice mixture in the tip section and ended up using all of it in the recipe after reading some comments (probably a good teaspoon more than called for). I made up some more spice mixture and, instead of making the icing (too fussy for the time I had available), added about half of it to 1/2 cup of sugar and rolled the dough balls in it. This probably boosted the flavour but I guess I like my spicy cookies to bite back more. Next time I’ll make regular ginger snaps and probably add chopped candied ginger to it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These were an absolute hit on Christmas Eve! Texture is 10/10 and the flavour is to die for! I can’t recommend these enough.

Tasted really blah, not gingery enough or something.  Used exact recipe. C. Boring texture, and hard as nails later in the day. Sorry person.

So much depends on the spice proportions, freshness,  and amount used - I went with an Indian store bought chai tea masala but would try making my own blend next time per the hint. I liked the suggestion of adding chopped crystallized ginger and will also try that next time.

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made these as written with the spice mix described in the Notes and the cookies have a great flavor and texture.

author: Genevieve Ko
