author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’ve made this twice; once as written (which was good, but not exactly what I was going for when I first saw the recipe), and the second was some tweaks ( wanted more chai, less gingerbread flavor and a firmer drier cookie). Second iteration- didn’t have molasses, so I substituted 1/4 C dark brown sugar and a bit of water (this was a good stumble because it made the taste less gingerbread); added a bit of almond flour (it was probably about 1/3 C), went a little heavier on the chai spice, added in about 1/2 C of slivered almonds.  It came out pretty much exactly as I was hoping! It is a very forgiving recipe which was great for someone who is not into precision cooking

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: FYI for those who prefer to cut biscuits, I took my dough out of the freezer and put it in the fridge for half an hour. Then I took it out and sliced pieces off, pinched them slightly to make them rounder before putting them in the oven - came out in perfect circles.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Make spice mix to your taste (I added ground fennel and subbed white pepper per a poster’s rec) and do double it. It looks like a ton of spice, but it isn’t too much. Try some of these right out of the oven! I kept a bit of the spice mix to add to the glaze, but they don’t need glaze, so I didn’t bother.

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
These came out really delicious. I used yuzu for the citrus, and it was lovely. 

I have to say, there is some serious miscalculation in this recipe though when it comes to how many cookies the dough makes. I saw the original and thought, “72 cookies?! That’s too many!” so I cut the recipe in half. I rolled out my dough to 1/4 inch thick, maybe even a little less, and I was able to eke out 22 cookies total, and that 22 includes one at the end that was a crazy scrap shape like in the video, and they were so tiny! I used my baby sized cookie cutters. All of them fit with no overlap on one standard-sized dinner plate. 

So keep that in mind if you choose to halve the recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Taylor- my thoughts exactly.  I made a full batch and rolled and sliced and got 24 regular size cookies.  I laughed out loud at your mini cookies description on the dinner plate!  They did taste really good though.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Kathryn of course this will depend on the definition of “regular size.” These are shortbread rather than sugar cookies, so are meant to be smaller. I got 56 1-3” cookies with cookie cutters and a very inefficient use of my dough. I would definitely have been able to eke out 72 if I were better at geometry.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made Ms. Ko’s Holiday Citrus Shortbread and icing yesterday and it was the best experience I have had using cookie cutters.  Everything went as Ms. Ko explained, and the timing of all of the factors was great:  30 minutes to let the dough rest, 1 hour or so in the refrigerator before cutting, etc.  The cookies are delicious!  Thanks for the recipes and the video!

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko

Genevieve Ko breaks down the recipe in steps that allow the shortbread to be managed really well.  The added bonus was to mix without a hand mixer.  Two dozen made and ready for icing with extra dough for another round in a day or two.  So appreciate the well thoughtout breakdown of the recipe, thank you Genevieve!

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Lovely tasting and some nice techniques  (I like doing things on a tray to keep the flour and mess in one place), but no way I'll get 72 cookies.   I'll grant you some of mine are larger than 2 inches (small gingerbread woman, menorah), but a lot of work for not as many as I need.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Does this icing harden so the cookies can be boxed/stacked?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Yes, but you should leave them out overnight (covered) before stacking. I started to stack them before I went to bed and that was a mistake. They were not quite hard enough after baking and icing during the afternoon. This morning the icing was hard enough and the cookie was still delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These sound great- can we do some dough without citrus an some with or does that require tweaking recipe more?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Mandy I read the article with helpful tips for this recipe and, as I recall, the citrus zest is optional. You could just leave it out or divide the recipe as you like and use ingredients proportionately to get some with and some without.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Mandy I think you absolutely could just skip the orange zest if you wanted to and do a batch without. No further modifications would be needed as the zest doesn’t change the moisture levels much. If you want to do some of both, then a half batch of each would make sense to me.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Mandy
A bit of almond extract is delicious in similar recipes I have made

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Great recipe, I separated half the dough out to taste without orange zest, used zest of half an orange in the main half. 
Love both, but especially the zesty ones. I added colorful sugar before baking, and they arent as perfectly decorated as in above photos, but finished in one afternoon. 
Try using good quality chocolate sprinkles if you skip the icing. 
The last round of leftover dough scraps? I beat them into submission, let the dough warm before cutting, and they aren’t tough at all.
All held their size and shape after baking, barely any spread on the pan. 
Will make again!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My cookie dough was just too dry. I added a few drops of water, which made it possible to form them. Anyone else struggle with this?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Renee mine was a little dry as well. I added another tbsp of butter. It probably would have been fine once it was chilled, but I was worried and it turned out great.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Fussy and not all THAT great. Edges spread a lot, even with refrigerating the cutouts. The orange zest gave the cookies a lumpy surface. This recipe made only about 15-18 midsize cutout cookies, so I don't know where the yield of 72 2" cookies comes from. I would not make these cookies again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Thank you for the outstanding video! I've avoided cutting out cookies for 40 years because they've always been a disappointment to roll, cut and decorate but im inspired by this video. The video picture, though, needs to be expanded or other mechanism to place the caption lower on the screen so we can see Genevieve's hands and the work bowl on the screen in all frames. The caption box sometimes obscures this part of the video when it would be helpful to see. Thanks again for an outstanding lesson.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
In the commentary you say not to roll up and use the scraps because they get tough, but in your actual recipe you say the opposite. I can’t imagine wasting that dough that can make more cookies. Ive been making similar cookies for about 65 years and they’re just fine 
Small tip that may elicit a duh! from  readers. If you’re making multiple batches you    don’have to wait for cookie sheets to cool (and never put new dough on hot sheets.) Just run them under cold water in the sink for a minute.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I think the detail that is left out of the written instructions is to "gather the scraps" as gently as possible.  In the video, she squeezes them back together while keeping them in flattish, rectangular shape (just enough to get rid of any holes); then flatten and even it out with a rolling pin.  Cookies are more likely to get tough if the scraps get completely balled up and then re-rolled.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Julie given that there is only butter flour and sugar, and no added liquid, rerolling the dough shouldn’t make it tough.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: this is a great recipe, very buttery.  I do sub out lemon for orange, rum, pistachio, cinnamon.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used lemon zest instead or orange and it was yummy. Liked the instructions  on cooling the dough to make using the cookie cutters easy!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Even with all the chilling, the dough didn’t hold intricate details of the cutters well. Flavor is good, but save your delicate cutters for another recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My experience exactly

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Do you think I could use the 1 to 1 gluten free flour with this recipe?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Best cookies I’ve ever made.
I wonder if the dough can be frozen?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: We made this recipe yesterday and the dough came out very dry and crumbly. We watched the video and followed the directions. We made it twice, and the second time weighed the ingredients, and the result was the same. So disappointing!  We haven’t baked them yet. Had to add some moisture (dabs of orange juice) so the dough would stay together.  It’s chilling now. Hopefully we’ll be able to cut some shapes out and bake. We’re having below zero to single digit temps right now and the air is very dry. Could that account for the dry, crumbly dough?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’ve found my holy grail Christmas cookie recipe! Very similar to the ones my mother made when I was a child (which I never realized were technically shortbread), but easier to make and sooooo delicious! Not sure how much flavor the orange zest added - I might try using more next time. Like other commenters, my yield was lower than the recipe stated - closer to 4 or 5 dozen 2-inch cookies.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made these yesterday and they are fabulous. I will say it needed more flour during the rolling and cutting stage. Quite a bit more - as the dough was just too soft and sticky to cut and lift the shapes. So I needed to add flour and re-roll each half after refrigerating. Maybe Australian flour is thirstier…either way despite frustrations handling the dough initially the end product is definitely worth it and I would make again with extra flour.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Jenn This was my experience as well. Way too sticky and difficult to work with despite using a silpat mat. With enough flour and continually scraping off my rolling pin, I produced delicious cookies. Next time thinking I’ll use pastry sock and hope it doesn’t ruin the texture on the top of the cookies.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are delicious. But, I for the life of me cannot understand how to make the icing look like on the picture. Every time I decorate my cookies, they end up looking like they were made by a three-year-old. Oh well.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @TH That was my experience with the icing as well! It was delicious, but the cookies looked like my Maltese dogs did the frosting! Perhaps next time I’ll use only half the lemon juice.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: would this recipe work well for making cookies with an embossed roller, cookie stamps, or cookie cutters that have imprinted/patterned details?  wondering if the pattern will still be sharp after baking. (I realize that they should be chilled/frozen when dough goes into oven.)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: that video is so great.  I usually would skip it because I am an experienced baker, but I learned a lot.   thanks.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Hi, the video and techniques are great, but my cookies did not have much taste at all. The orange flavor somehow disappeared. I followed the recipe exactly. What could have happened?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These cookies seemed extra fussy with the 2 step refrigeration.  Wish I would have read the whole way through.  Just wanted to make sugar cookies and eat them with an easy recipe.  this one is a let down, though I am sure if you follow all the steps precisely they will be good.  We give them only one star.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Melissa Wolaver this is not a sugar cookie recipe; that could have been the source of your disappointment. These cookies (and the technique) are excellent.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I followed all the steps, but my cookies did not come out with crisp, well-defined edges.  Far too much trouble for too little payoff.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These cookies are very delicious and such a huge improvement over regular holiday sugar cookies.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can you just put holiday sprinkles on prior to cooking like in sugar cookies?  The icing is WAY too much work if you make a dozen other cookies for the holidays.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Dr Vicki These shortbread are tasty without anything on top at all! If you want a little sparkle, sparkling sugar sprinkled on top before baking would be lovely.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It works fine.  I would decrease the salt the next time I make this.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made the mistake of using too cold butter and once I added the flour and mixed, it never formed a dough. It’s just loose powder. Do I have to start again? Help!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @lisa no need to start over! Stir an egg yolk into the mix to bring it together. If it’s still crumbly, add the white too. You’ll end up with a cookie with a sandier texture, but it’ll still be tasty!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wowza! These cookies are good. I made a batch for my son’s birthday party but they didn’t last the night. Two more batches cooling now. Perfect level of sweetness and the most delicate crumb.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made these for an art gallery holiday party and they were a hit, even with a foodie crowd. The orange is subtle, they’re the perfect amount of sweet, and if you’re gentle with mixing by hand they come out crisp! I added no more flour, rolling out between layers of parchment and cling wrap.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Great recipe!! Question: if you have extra dough (the part that remains after using the cookie cutter) can that be re-rolled, and if so, does it need to be chilled again? I did re-roll it, but was sort of crumbly and couldn’t get many cookies out of the second roll. I ended up just balling up the extra dough into five or six rounded tablespoons and experimentally threw those in the oven. Still delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: So yummy and fun to decorate! Don’t skimp on the zest and careful they cook fast. 10 minutes was just right.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This recipe did not work at all. Used the cookies for crumbs on ice cream

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is very good and essentially combines an iced sugar cookie with shortbread with a lemon bar.  A slightly thicker cookie worked better in my opinion.  Cutting the cookies while the dough is cold is essential and you might even want to re-chill the dough after rolling out the scraps.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Maybe I rolled the dough too thin but i found the cook time to be too long

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I find the cookie cooking times in the NYC always too long. Perhaps it is my oven? In general, I set the timer a bit short and check.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can the dough be made and stored (in logs) in the freezer until ready to bake (up to a week or more)?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Excellent cookie making video.  I will definitely try both recipes this season.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I normally add meringue powder to my royal icing to help it set up hard for the cookie boxes I prepare. Can I add it to this recipe given there's lemon in the icing?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I just made these cookies and they are delicious! The frosting gives them a tangy zip, but they are wonderful without the frosting as well!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Excellent video!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: What an excellent video! I learned so much.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can you use GF measure for measure instead of AP flour??

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Susan I made these yesterday using King Arthur Measure for Measure and they came out perfectly!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Adding to earlier note - reallyt delicious and they really do not need icing as the shortbread is yummy.   I'll make again, but just use the tip and make slices.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These look wonderful! Is it possible to freeze this dough and thaw to use when you are ready?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Thank you Genevieve for such helpful hints. I thoroughly enjoyed your video. I can't wait to try these with my grandkids after school today!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I'm in the middle of making these and feel very skeptical. The dough was just powder until I added a whole egg per a tip in the comments. Now it's in the fridge, but I can see large pieces of butter even though I've been mixing it for ages. Will it work?!?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These would be good for citrus-loving adults but I would argue they are not super child friendly. Adults comments were "WOW! Very lemony!!" A milk icing would be favorable to younger palates.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Just made as written.  These cookies were so fragrant as they baked.  Smelled beautiful and tasted divine.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My dough is pretty crumbly maybe a bit too dry suggestions?  Thanks!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The cookies were lovely (made with the orange zest) but the glaze was overpowering. I wouldn’t use this glaze again… just let the delicacy of the cookie’s flavour speak for itself.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: this was super yummy and easy and the video tips meant it all went smoothly.The only thing I would do differently next time is double the icing because I didn't have enough to pipe every cookie fully like I wanted, which is personal preference.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Dough was so dry and crumbly. A huge disappointment.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Maybe I’m just not a shortbread fan but my cookies came out greasy, flaky and too crumbly to decorate. I did appreciate the video and tip with the orange zest though!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are the best Christmas cookies I’ve ever eaten. Can’t wait to make them again. I followed the recipe exactly as written.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Great recipe! I made them in cutout rounds, used a small offset spatula to deliver a quick and easy swirl of untinted icing, then topped with crumbled freeze-dried raspberries. No piping required. We love these festive little flavor bombs.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I added 1 tsp. Boyajian's orange oil (even with the vanilla) and this intensifies the flavor.  True--with only 2 C. flour, you might get half the amount of cookies stated in the recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The flavor of the orange and soft texture make these a treat to eat! I threw cut out cookies in the freezer for 10 minutes before putting them in the oven, but they still didn't have the sharpest edges. If taste is your main objective, this is a great recipe. For more manicured cookies, try Susan Spungen's Best Sugar Cookies, also from the Times.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: To respond to all the queries about making these gluten free: YES. I used King Arthur Measure for Measure and my cookies are tender and buttery. I followed all other instructions to the letter!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this with lemon zest instead of orange, but otherwise followed the recipe exactly and it was perfect! I went with the option to roll it into a log and bake it as rounds. The cookies had a great flavor and were tender and delicate

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Cookies were very tasty. Frosting too lemony - overwhelmed the shortbread, IHO. Can you replace part of the Lemon Juice with another liquid? Water, perhaps, or does it need something acidic?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Great recipe! I switched out the orange zest for bergamot zest (very delicate and wonderful), and I used the bergamot juice (very citrusy) in the icing. OMG. They were delightful!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Excellent. The lemon icing is a revelation.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Well. We made this recipe with high hopes, and the cookie dough is to DIE for.  Everyone in the house was stealing scraps as I cut out cookies. However, what an aesthetic disappointment in the oven! Each cookie melted out shamelessly to twice their size. An absolutes delicious mess, with the icing project for the grandchildren scuttled. Even I’d they hadn’t’ spread, these cookies are way too fragile to manipulate at least with French butter, all purpose flour and sugar. I’m going to dig out our usual Joy Rich Roll Cookies to recover the situation ;-)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These cookies are delicious but I had a lot of trouble trying to roll out the dough as it was super sticky. Sounds like I need to watch the video, so next time it’ll be easier!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I followed the recipe and my cookies spread out a ton after baking. What the heck did I do wrong?!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
These cookies came out delightful. I followed the cookie recipe exactly. 

The icing is a bit tricky for a newb like me - my only recommendation is maybe to consider sifting the powdered sugar and THEN adding the lemon juice tbsp by tbsp like she suggests.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delish! My fav shortbread recipe and no icing required! ❤️

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made these cookies two days ago. The first batch is fine although I like a thicker shortbread cookie. One eighth of an inch produced quite brown and crisp cookies; one quarter of an inch produced only slightly less browned and crisp cookies. But on to the icing, I was not able to gauge how much coloring to put in so I ended up with very blue and light pink and green. They look awful. For the scraps I gathered them into a log and refrigerated them overnight. The orange zest turned bitter and when finished they don’t taste the same as the first batch. I’ve been a good baker for 40 years, but these bested me. Sadly I won’t be making them again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is the best shortbread I’ve ever had! Word of warning: WATCH THE VIDEO. Never having made shortbread outside the confines of a pan, I was unprepared for how crumbly the unchilled dough would be. I hadn’t watched the video, and didn’t form the dough while already on the plastic wrap. Trying to move that crumbly square my pastry mat to the plastic wrap was one of the most frustrating experiences of my life. But that was user error, and the final product is absolute perfection!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Quick help if anybody happens to see this on Christmas morning: I accidentally put the zest into 1/2 cup of sugar, not 1/3. Can I leave out the powdered sugar so they're not too sweet? Or will that change the texture as powered sugar has some cornstarch in it. Ideas? Halp. :)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Best cookie recipe by far. I have a 2 year old and yesterday while she took her nap, I made the dough and left it chilling in the fridge. And today, xmas day, as she naps, I baked them. They smell SO good, is ridiculous good!! And taste magnificent *insert chef kiss*

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are the perfect sugar cookie cutouts! I've tried so many over the years and this is the stand alone best. Cookie has a delicious flavor that stands up to frosting and is a great mix of a little crunch and soft chew. Love

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is an awesome recipe. Perfect sweetness and texture. I added 1/2 teaspoon of fiori di sicilia to boost the orange creamsicle flavor. I couldn't make the glaze in time but I'm sure that would've been great too. The cookie is not too sweet so it's perfect for families that don't like things too sweet. And the cut out shape holds very, very well. You can also cut the dough in half immediately and then put the half into the freezer for 10 minutes, and then freeze for 20 minutes after rolling.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Oops- I meant to say 1/4 tsp of fiori di sicilia.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These cookies are outstanding! The video is exceptionally helpful- I learned new tricks that undoubtedly contributed to the cookies' deliciousness! Cookies were gone in 1 day!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The cookies are very tasty, but the recipe doesn't make as many as it says.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
The yield is more like 24, 3-4 inch cookies.  I followed instructions in the video closely and found that I under mixed the flour+butter/ sugar mixture.  
Very tender cookie.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I normally make cut out sugar cookies each year but decided to try these this year as I liked the idea of shortbread. The video was very useful and I  had no trouble cutting out the cookies. I thought the undecorated cookies were delicious but the icing ruined them for me. Way too sweet, it overpowered the delicate buttery cookie. Made it taste like lemon and sugar. Also, we found the cookies broke quite easily. In the end, they were disappointing considering the amount of effort involved. If I were to make them again I would stick to very basic shapes that might not be as susceptible to breakage. And I would just decorate with a bit of colored sugar and forget the icing altogether.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I had to make a second batch since I only got about 40 2-inch cookies. These were great! They were pretty sweet on their own, so I might try lowering the sugar next time. The lemon in the glaze was a nice unexpected surprise. Fresh tasting and different cookie choice for Christmas.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Excellent. The lemony icing was quite a hit.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I never got around to frosting ours, and I am SO enjoying the plain cookie as a minimally sweet treat. The flavor is incredible— I love shortbread, and these are stellar. I attribute the wonderful texture to mixing by hand in a bowl, as I’ve never gotten such tender results using a mixer.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This recipe is great! Best christmas cookies ever.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made these cookies with my grandchildren last week. I watched Ms. Ko’s video and followed the recipe to the letter. The cookies turned out so pretty and even better they were delicious.  This will be my go to cookie recipe in the future. Thank you, Genevieve!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Broke the curse! Prior attempts at shortbread were a fail. Video and details helped immensely. Used Kerrygold butter. Cut into 1” x 2.5” rectangles. Dipped the ends of several in tempered dark chocolate and added a light sprinkle of Maldon. Absolutely delicious and will go on repeat for next Christmas.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious! Rolled in a log and cut slices.  Thicker better.  Used lemon frosting.  Delightful.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Perfect! Used Bob's Red Mill 1:1 gluten free flour.  I choose to do slice and bake instead of cutting shapes but that would have been easy to do.  I made the icing and drizzled in a zig zag over the tops instead of covering them in icing.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I left it in the fridge overnight, and now it’s very firm. How do I soften it?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I added the zest of 1.5 lemons to the dough and 4 drops of orange extract to the icing, took it up another notch for me

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Done it, almost weekly now. We've used shrimp instead of pork & subbed honey for the brown sugar. You can empty your veggie bin, we've added Kale, spinach, broccoli, snap peas, just about anything.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I make a vegan version of this by using textured vegetable protein (TVP): Don't soak the TVP but rather just brown it, dry, in a lightly oiled pan, and then just treat the TVP the way this recipe treats the pork.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @John we did it with crumbled tempeh! Delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Jessica Though I’ve been vegan for years, I have yet to find a way to enjoy tempeh. Maybe this dish would be flavorful enough to mask the bitterness, so thanks for sharing this idea!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Karen I have trouble with that aspect of tempeh too (exception: that time I made my own during the pandemic; an utter faff but so delicious with no bitterness at all). It’s a little fussy, but I find lightly steaming the tempeh first and then proceeding with the recipe really helps cut down the bitterness. Saltiness is a natural antidote to bitterness, so the sauce will help too. 
I think soy curls would also be nice in this recipe, maybe hashed up a bit first—might try that if I lose the will to make it with tempeh!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Genevieve Ko included a 15-minute vegan no-recipe recipe in a recent newsletter. https://www.nytimes.com/2025/11/12/dining/easy-dan-dan-noodle-recipe-for-cold-weather.html Here it is: Mix Chinese sesame paste (or tahini mixed with toasted sesame oil), soy sauce & chile crisp with a pinch of sugar in the bowl you’ll eat from. Adjust seasonings to taste. Stir in boiling water (maybe pasta water?) until runny. Cook your noodles, add whatever veg. Top with peanuts &or pickled veg.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: You can really use any type of ground protein. The meat is so heavily seasoned with the other ingredients it hardly makes a difference. I use ground chicken since I prefer to avoid pork and it comes out tasting virtually the same.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Rice noodles work for those who avoid gluten. My favorite brand is Tinkyada. I also use assorted mushrooms (shitake, enoki, etc.) in place of pork. I don't go all vegan, though. Some bacon fat and either a half pint of homemade chicken broth or high-quality jarred product (e.g., Better Than Bouillon) give depth -- and a nod towards tan tan men. I use fresh, grated ginger, too. It is worth it to find Sichuan peppercorn -- toasted and ground about 3/4 of a teaspoon.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Jon G. Tikita is great, but since Jovial hit the shelves, I only buy them for gluten-free pasta now.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Jon G. do you sub out the chili crisp for the peppercorn or do you use both? I want to make sure I get that numbing mala effect when I make this

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious, and for the folks who are worried about the salt, fat, preserved vegetables, or whatever; get over it. You're not going to eat a dish like this every day. It's a pleasant indulgence. For sure, this is less salty and fatty than most of the dan dan noodles I've had in the LA area, some of which are served without meat, actually. I've also never had a bowl with the preserved mustard greens...usually it's served with just steamed bok choy.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Chris in Redlands LP p

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I fell in love with Dan Dan Noodles while living for a short time in Chengdu, capital of Sichuan province, twenty years ago. Very hard to find it in New York area back then. I am very happy to see this dish becoming so popular.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I avoid the packaged pickles because they all have preservatives. I’ve found picked green beans are a relatively easy sub. Fermenting my own mustard greens is the ultimate goal though! Don’t skip entirely because they really make it and cut the richness of the sesame paste.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Val look there are benefits to a low-processed diet but the pickled greens are incredible and a few servings aren't going to kill you

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Val I was disappointed with the on line pickled mustard, and subbed raw bok choi slivers tossed in Marukan seasoned vinegar, kind of a salad topping layer.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: For those requesting a vegetarian option, I would recommend making this other tofu-based NYT recipe "I Can't Believe It's Not Chicken" (https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1027269-i-cant-believe-its-not-chicken-super-savory-grated-tofu) and sub that in for meat. I think it'd work beautifully texturally and flavor-wise.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Jake J. I can confirm that this is a delicious meat-free option. Made it and wish I has doubled the batch.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Jake J. I love this tofu recipe. It makes my heart sing.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Mmm.  After a night of hunting through my local Uwajimaya, and a last-minute run on the neighbor's soy sauce of all things, I was able to make this exactly as written.  Delish.

...Incindentally, the roots of your hair tingling are normal, right?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If you can't get to a local Chinese grocery, you can use yami.com.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: For gf veg (vegan/vegetarian) version use one of the impossible beyond meat packs. They're not the healthiest thing on earth, but in moderation is fine. Also there is Quorn. A meat sub that is made from mushrooms. Shiitake and minced portabella have a similar flavor and texture profile. Properly seasoned they would work too.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I think this will become one of my regulars.  I added Szechuan broad bean paste and used kimchee for the pickles.  Next time I may use caramelized cabbage and rename it Dan Haluski.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I will share that I've made noodles twice from scratch and actually bought a larger rolling pin because they take some effort. They are very easy to make! And they are delicious. Now to try this recipe. The noodle recipe is from YouTube, "How to Make Chinese Noodles at Home" by Souped Up Recipes. Also, I must sing the praises of the NuWave Induction Wok. I think Costco is starting to carry them (note: it gets hot enough to make blackened salmon!).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Vegan weeknight tweaks: 
Make the sauce anytime ahead, adding lime juice for spark at last minute
Sheet pan any veg( cauliflower, carrot are good)
Layer noodles into bowl with some sauce
Layer sheet pan veg, dribble with sauce
Sprinkle with peanuts
Top with arugula or julienned bok choi tossed in marukan seasoned vinegar, an inauthentic functional sub for pickled mustard greens.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this with rice noodles, but otherwise followed the recipe and it was just delicious. The sauce feels like something you could double or triple and keep in the fridge ready to toss with just about any protein, veg or carb. It's that good. Will add this to the rotation in our house.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Pretty good. I had to substitute some. Used capers/cornichons for the mustard greens, white wine for rice wine, and hoisin. I skipped the rinsing of the noodles step and that is what I didn’t love. The Lo mein noodles were too starchy and gave a gummy mouth-feel. S-O really liked it so it’s worth trying again. The flavors were really good but I also want to try it with the preserved mustard greens.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is a very good recipe that also works with thin linguini or similar what pastas.  The substitution recommendations are right on - tahini with added sesame oil and hoisin.  The “ sauce” I did adjust to out preferences.  Definitely add the optional veggies as that makes it a more “complete meal”. Very tasty and satisfying.  Garnished with black sesame seeds and peanuts…added nice crunch.  Thanks to the authors!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I don't understand "8 to 12 bok choy." Do you mean 8 to 12 large leaves, small baby bok choy heads, certainly not that many large bok choy heads? Please clarify

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: or is it 8-12 ounces bok choy?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: somebody please answer this question!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Roger

The number of vegetables is a range because the size can vary a lot. It also depends on how much vegetable you want in it, so it’s partially personal preference. 

The recipe intends for us to use the entire vegetable (but trim off the bottom). It cooks quickly. 

My method is to keep the chopped leaves and chopped stems separate. When it’s time to add the veggies to the water, I add the stems about thirty seconds before the leaves. 

Mt take is that bok choy is milder and Chinese broccoli has a slightly firmer texture and more robust flavor. It just depends on what you want and what is available to you. 

I usually prefer Chinese broccoli in this kind of recipe. But lots of people are adding other vegetables as different riffs on the recipe and enjoying it. I’ll probably throw in some asparagus into my leftovers since they’re in my fridge.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
In Italian cooking it is very common to finish a sauce with a splash of starchy water from pasta pan. This makes the sauce shine and come together.

I notice in this recipe this step happens before the noodles have been cooked - i.e. it will just be plain water.
Would it benefit the dish to use the same methodology? 
I.e. Move step 3 into step 5?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: For those of us who live far from fresh Chinese wheat noodles, what is the best easily accessible substitute?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Sarah I’ve always used spaghetti for Dan-Dan, the quality of Italian ingredients is much higher where I live and wheat noodles are wheat noodles. The noodles don’t know they aren’t Chinese!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @gabriel Chinese wheat noodles are made without eggs, so they are actually different. But spaghetti does work fine in this recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @StuckInNJ most commercial dried pasta is also made without eggs. Just FYI!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Following this recipe resulted in one of the best things I’ve ever cooked and eaten. The pickled mustard greens in particular— smelling and tasting them made me feel like I was ascending to higher level where all the answers were before me.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: We went to our best local Asian market to get authentic ingredients, which we generally do. We followed the recipe exactly using these ingredients. The final dish was inedibly salty. We suspect that the fermented mustard greens were the culprit since salt was the second ingredient. You are listing ingredients from all over the world that you do not have control over and cannot warn about them all. Guidance of what to watch for would be helpful.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Chris

Yeah, I sensed that this might be rather salty, so I used low salt soy sauce. Even with that, it is a bit salty, but that’s part of its charm. You might consider adding more sesame paste the next time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: A pound of dry noodles (the recipe doesn't specify fresh noodles) is too much.  The 2nd time I made them, I used a package of Assi Organic Udon--9.53 oz.  I also added  a half cup of Better than Bullion broth to the sauce, plus some pasta water at the end.  Didn't have Bok choy--used fresh spinach instead.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If you can't find Chinese sesame paste, it is very easy to make. Toast sesame seeds til lightly browned, cool, then whiz in a food processor until it's the consistency of peanut butter. Be patient... It takes a while.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this with butter pickles and capers combo and thinest spaghetti in my store. Turned out fine. Most important item in recipe is using a LARGE bowl to make the sauce. I did not use one - I figured, “why do I need a large bowl to make half a cup of sauce? The reason is this bowl is what you ‘slide’ all the pasta and meat into at the end of the recipe. Don’t make my mistake!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious, and don’t be afraid to add more chili crisp, but as with many similar NY Times recipes, the amount of noodles is excessive compared to the other ingredients. Also, it’s unclear what “8 to 10 bok choy” actually means. A great recipe, needs more veggies and less noodles.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This js one of the most delicious things I’ve ever made or eaten. I had to sub based on market availability: kimchi for ya cai, rice wine vinegar for Chinkiang vinegar, and mirin had to stand in for both Shaoxing wine and tian mian jiang so I used only 1tsp brown sugar in the sauce to keep the dish from getting too sweet. Will make again and again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Vegan Dan Dan - https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1023347-vegan-dan-dan-noodles-with-eggplant?smid=share-url

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this a vegetarian dish by substituting Japanese eggplants for the pork. i cut them into about 1" chunks. For the ya cai, I used half chopped capers and the other half sweet pickle relish (like hot dog relish). The dish turned out fantastic!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Meh.  
I added porcini and broccoli.
Very bland

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Penni this is one of the most flavorful dishes i’ve tried on here! did you miss some ingredients? the ya cai truly brings it to the next level

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious! I found the proportion of noodles & veggies to be off - recommend 8-10oz of noodles and 4-5 bok choi to end up with 4 servings

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used Del Allo gluten free spaghetti noodles for this and subbed extra firm tofu for the pork. Delicious and great as leftovers too. I only cooked some of the bok choy initially and for leftovers I tossed everything in a pan with a bit of water and sesame oil and added the freash bok choy to cook as I reheated everything else. Covered for a few minutes and it all came together again nicely.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Made this exactly with no substitutions.
It is a very flavourful, but a surprisingly subtle dish.
First bowl went a bit overboard on the condiments ... Szechuan peppercorns and additional crispy chilies.

Less is more.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is very good. I look forward to trying it again and not leaving out the two ingredients I mistakenly left out.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I can't figure out what she means by:

"Step 5
Slide the noodles and bok choy over the sauce..."

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Jason I had the same issue. So I think we may have made the same mistake: making the sauce in a small cup, like the small measuring cup where I mixed the sauce. No way I could “slide” a pound of cooked noodles over that! Had the sauce been made and kept in a LARGE bowl, the instruction would have made sense. Live and learn.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
- very flexible:  use shrimp, ground chicken, or pork; subbed honey for the brown sugar.
- you can empty your veggie bin: Kale, spinach, broccoli, snap peas, just about anything
- use Rice noodles (Tinkyada brand?) or GF spaghetti

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was delicious, I have to admit, we made substitutions.  We've been trying to use up a lot of sauces and other ingredients since we are about to move.  So...Leftover hummus (garlic), why not?  Made with tahini and chickpeas.  Had some marinated green beans (olive oil, lemon, and garlic) left over, chopped & used for pickled part. Had the soy, the sesame, and the chile garlic stuff, but missing the wine and the hoisin :( Used some miso with brown sugar...kale for bok choy, ground pork!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Melissa thanks for the miso tip! I used miso and coconut aminos to sub for the hoisin and it was so tasty! I likewise didn’t have the required wine but clearly this is a versatile recipe. I only had a bunch of ramps from my woods so used those instead of any other vegetables. Absolutely delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Brain trust - for a family that doesn't eat pork, do you think ground turkey (dark) would do the trick?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can you skip the meat completely and just do the bok choy and mustard greens?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: For those of you who asked, I found this vegan Dan Dan Noodle recipe from NYTimes. https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1023347-vegan-dan-dan-noodles-with-eggplant?campaign_id=58&emc=edit_ck_20251112&instance_id=166239&nl=cooking®i_id=70251875&segment_id=210457&user_id=b0f5aa32ff03339384e0b02ced406477

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious, we will be making this again.  I used shirataki noodles to make it low carb and went with pork and shiitake mushrooms.  Fabulous :)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: For a weeknight? Have you seen the list of ingredients?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this tonight and it was very good. I have a couple of questions tho: (1) my Chinese sesame paste was black! It shocked me, but tasted fine. Anybody else have this experience? (2) what is the treatment for the bok choy? They were small, so i threw them in whole. Should I have halved or quartered them? They were clumsy to eat, and i used a fork.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this recipe as written but made a huge mistake which ruined the entire thing. Put the wheat noodles to boil, then added the bok choy, then the phone rang. Too long in the water. Tried to put the meal together but the noodles ended up gooping up, running everything. We ate it for dinner but through out the left overs. My husband is a doll and will eat anything I make and even he said, not the best. I think I am going to try regular spagetti noodles next time to give me a grace period.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I think, given the multiple specialty ingredients, this recipe is best made if you have access to a high quality Asian market. Tried with what I could cobble together out here in the hinterlands, but I suspect I did not do it justice.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Scrumptious! I'm fortunate enough to live in an urban area with tons of ethnic groceries. I went to my favorite Asian grocer, which recently moved locations. The new location is massive. The new shop felt like actually going to China. This is part of the fun of ethnic cooking! Instead of plain cooking oil, I made my own chili oil from scratch. I'm vegetarian so I used tofu and added some peanuts into the saute. I also made Genevieve Ko's chili crisp and added peanuts to that as well.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: So salty! Did I do something wrong with the recipe? Loved the dish but have to find a way of reducing the saltiness by 100%. I cook without salt normally so was really sensitive to the saltiness

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: In the 90s I used to order Dan Dan Noodles at an Asian street food restaurant in Washington DC that my coworkers and I frequented for lunch. The restaurant and the ad agency I worked for are both gone now, but not my love for these noodles. I made them exactly as directed but used ground chicken because I no longer eat pork. It was perfect. It took me a while to find the miang jang at H-Mart, but they had it and everything else I needed. Yum.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Before the rest of our family arrived for the holidays, our daughter came home from her place in SoCal. I saw this recipe and wanted to give it a try, especially because she’s a fan of Asian food. I’m a novice at Asian food. So I was happy when she agreed to lend a hand in the kitchen! She even cut the scallions on diagonal, they were beautiful and I admired them. She knew exactly how to get the noodles just right, even though I selected Thai wheat noodles, which made for a challenge. I am happy to say that that was the only substitution I made. (It meant ending up at an Asian market  50 miles away.)  We came up with a great meal by following the carefully laid out instructions. I was especially pleased since I got to see my daughter in action and I learned some new skills. We made a great team. I’m thankful. As an added bonus, my husband loved the Dan Dan Noodles!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I'm not one of those people who changes all the ingredients and then sings the praises of my version. I decided to make these on a weeknight when my daughter was begging for NYC takeout, had ground pork and dried noodles but had to use the alternate or substitute at least half the other ingredients. I expected it to be a big loser and ended up with a giant win.
Had no Chinese sesame paste so used Joyca tahini. No Chinkiang or balsamic vinegar so used sherry vinegar. No brown sugar! Had crystallized maple syrup.
Used the very last about 1/2 tsp of the Trader Joe's chile crisp. Nothing even approximating ya cai (Sichuan preserved mustard greens) and could not even start with the capers and pickles so tossed in a "vegetable topping" packet that came from some ramen package. No tian mian jiang (sweet wheat sauce) or hoisin in the house - used 2 tsp of fig mostarda from Eataly. Had Japanese rice wine. Love little baby bok choys but nope - sliced up red and green peppers and thirded a bundle of asparagus. No Sichuan peppercorns. I'm a pretty agile cook but [scratches head]. My 14YO daughter slurped it up! She thanked me! And it was indeed delicious. Foolproof. Heading to Chinatown this weekend for the right stuff.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: So I took the recipe here and didn’t make noodles but instead a stir fry. As I was eating it occurred to me that these flavors were from a dish I loved at House of Nanking in San Francisco where I’d go in the 1990s. Never experienced anything like it anywhere else - until now. Thank you for this!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
This is a personal top 5 NYT recipe for sure... and probably top 3 because of how delicious the leftovers were (eaten cold!) 

I subbed in ground chicken for pork and followed a comment to use capers in place of the pickled greens, which I couldn't find and didn't have the heart to go to more than one grocery store. Hilariously realized last minute that I didn't have the staple that is balsamic vinegar so used red wine vin! 

Truly can't wait to make another batch of this.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was well received but I found it a bit sweet with sugar in the sauce and hoisin with the meat. I‘ll use less sugar (2-3 fat pinches) and more pickles next time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Just on the ya cai or Yibin ya cai, If like me, you live a million miles from even the most basic of "exotic" foods. You can very finely chop some kimchi and sub that on instead.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Didn't have the preserved veggies in my pantry but wanted to make this so I just used some diced onion and scallion instead. I know it wasn't the same but it was still really good, and we all enjoyed it!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: You need to rinse and drain the ya cai before using it otherwise it is far too salty.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Tried with Banza garbanza noodles, excellent

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: i hate the bitterness of tahini and always sub peanut butter in my tantanmen and dan dan

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can I substitute Kim chi or giardenera for the pickled greens?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Absolutely sumptuous. Could not stop eating it. My only sub was chopped cashews for the peanuts. I’ve been cooking a lot of authentic Asian food the last six months so I had many of the ingredients on hand. This was for sure one of my favorites!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Any suggestions for a vegetarian version?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Clark Jones - if you do a search on these comments there’s been a lot of suggestions for vegetarian substitutions.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Could I use ground chicken or turkey? I have a red meat allergy, but this recipe sounds so delicious and I’m not vegetarian.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If you can find it, I find ground duck is a great pork substitute (it's a "red" meat but ok for alpha-gal allergies

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Sharon Absolutely. Ground chicken or turkey, or any meat substitute like Beyond is an easy swap out. If you have access to an Asian grocery, I would also suggest buying a tray of fried tofu cubes to cut up in smaller pieces for the protein. Would be delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
For those of you asking about substitutions for the ground pork. tofu, ground chicken or turkey will work. Pork is pork though so the flavor will change and it would probably be fine.

As a personal note, gosh I love dishes like this - it's the sugar + fat + salt that = chef's kiss. Wish I could eat more of them!!!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: We all should eat less meat for health reasons.   But ground pork puts coal in the furnace.   And it's inexpensive.   1/2 pound here would feed two adults and two kids.  You have to choose your purveyor wisely.   TJ's is superior to supermarket chains.  It says a lot about the inferiority of the American food supply when Costco is quality for pork chops supped by average folks.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Wordsworth from Wadsworth Totally agree. Ground pork is always in my freezer. I buy a pound at a time and weigh it out into 1/3 or 1/4 lb portions, wrap in wax paper, then freeze them in a Ziploc bag. Terrific to have on hand for stir fry recipes like this.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: how much would the bok choy/chinese broccoli weigh ?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I add a teaspoon of whole Sichuan peppercorns to the meat plus some toasted & ground peppercorns to the sauce.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this tonight, as written. (well, I may have used more vegetables) It was delicious and better than any I’ve had in a restaurant. For those concerned about additives in store bought preserved mustard greens, making your own isn’t hard. I had some on hand that I’d made from a recipe in Ever-Green Vietnamese by Andrea Nguyen. They were fabulous in this dish, and keep in your refrigerator quite a while.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I don’t comment on recipes I have never made, but as good as this one sounds it involves ingredients that I don’t usually use and would end up in the bin 6 months later.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Sad to say I can’t call this an “easy” dish with the ingredient list being difficult in my rural area. Yes, I could probably get them online but that doesn’t work when you’re searching for a quick meal today. That said, I do love the NYT recipes just struggle sometimes because I don’t live in NY.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: What kind of sesame oil?  The ingredients just says “seasame oil” but the tips mention toasted sesame oil.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I made this almost exactly as written. It was good, 4 stars. The only thing I couldn't do was the pickled mustard greens. 
I live very close to The Asian Grocery Chain. I thought it would be a slam dunk to find 'em, but the pickled mustard greens were not available, nor did anyone working there seem to know what I was talking about. I did buy dried kind and rehydrated a few of them to give it that deep saltiness. I'd appreciate another note/tip on the mustard greens!
I think the balance was off

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wow, a great treat.  I used regular tahini but I’d like to use the Chinese type next time. I used ground turkey and chicken and also subbed soba (buckwheat)noodles for Chinese wheat and cabbage for bok choy. I did not have the ha chai and improvised with capers hot cherry pepper slices mixed with sweet pickle relish—crazy but it worked. Had both adult sons here and husband and they were very happy.  Chile crisp was essential.  I also sprinkled sesame seeds on top with the scallions.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
This is delicious!

I made it with chicken, which I prefer. 

It took some organized prep work, but now that I’ve made it once, I don’t think it will be that big of a deal to make again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: We have a favorite restaurant that made this dish, it was the only thing I ordered for several years, and this was even better. I can't wait to make this for my kids in a month. it was fantastic. it might be one of my favorite dishes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this for the first time and it is a keeper. So delicious. I am lucky to have an Asian market nearby so I had the authentic ingredients. I agree with the other comments that this could easily be made as a vegetarian meal and the other proteins would combine well with this dish. One note would be that this is quick to make, but it does involve some cleanup with the bowl, colander, wok, pasta pot etc. however, it was well worth the effort. The generous portion  could be another meal, maybe with some added veggies when reheated. Loved this!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Outstanding and the Yibin yacai is essential (half the fun is trying to find it in our international market)!  In the future, I’ll increase the sauce, especially when using fresh Chinese wheat noodles.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are really wonderful.   The crispy Szechwan peppers are the key ingredient

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: LOVE this recipe. We used a plant-based ground 'meat', and went lightly with the chile crisp and peppercorns so it wouldn't be too spicy for our kid, which worked out perfectly. Will definitely make this again!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Excellent

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It was okay

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious!  Used Napa cabbage for my veg and some grated ginger plus at the end a squeeze of lime juice.  I used Japanese ramen noodles (very curly), but might use skinny egg noodles next time. Chopped peanuts on top a must!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was excellent. I couldn’t find some of the very specific Chinese ingredients they listed in my everyday store, but their tips seemed to be excellent swaps! Tasted like the real thing at the end. Excellent flavor that you can really tailor to your spice level and add just about any veg you want.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Add air fryer tofu in a little cornstarch

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Sauce was spot on. Definitely go to an Asian grocery store for the mustard greens and fresh noodles. Make sure you only cook the noodles 1-2 minutes. Mine probably cooked 3 and they were gloopy.  Would make more often but this dish has so much sodium- definitely one to not put in a rotation of meals but definitely a good recipe

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Have been addicted to Fuschia Dunlop’s dan dan recipe for years but excited to give the addition of sugar and peanuts a go!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: No clue what ya chai is and the Asian supermarkets didn’t even have it. Anyways, it was 5/5 without it, and can’t wait to see what it takes like when I find some ya chai.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: meh—I like the idea but it has too much sesame paste in it—it starts to get too peanut buttery for me

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was fantastic.  It was my first time making an authentic Asian dish.  I went to the Asian grocery and got everything except for hoisin - they didn't have it so I made my own from scratch which was not hard and much better than the jar sauce at your regular grocery store.  I was skeptical of the mustard greens - but they were delicious.  I also added some broccoli for added veggies.  Will 100% make this again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I go sauce-heavy by cutting back on the amount of noodles.  We like it so much better that way.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I really like this!

I’ve made this three or four times now. Here’s my take:

It’s so savory, and the mustard greens take it from tasting like a facsimile of a Chinese dish to tasting like an authentic restaurant dish. 

Once you’ve got the ingredients, it’s easy to make, but it’s sort of time consuming. Organizing is important for this one. 

I do find this salty. I’ll use low salt soy sauce from now on, and I might rinse the mustard greens before using them, and include a lot of vegetables. 

There’s a big range about how people feel about the amount of sauce. I think it’s a lot of sauce. I also thinks this makes a very big quantity, so I’ll try to remember to halve the recipe unless we have guests coming.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Followed the recipe and did not find it salty. Definitely use the ya cai in the amount stipulated. Used bok choy. Great as leftovers too.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was amazing. Followed all the Western substitutions and made with udon instead. Such a satisfying weeknight meal

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Double pork and halve pasta.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Turned out bad. All I did was substitute pork for extra firm tofu and greens for Napa cabbage and rice noodles for wheat noodles. And I didn’t add the sauce to the bottom of the bowl but poured it over the noodles (not what you’re supposed to do). I guess it really pays to follow the recipe?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The sesame paste and ya cai were delicious in this dish. Plain old spaghetti worked perfectly well. I used diced chicken, which I thought worked also, though it didn’t absorb flavors quite as well as pork would have. While eating, my partner made those tiny involuntary vocalizations of pleasure that can’t be faked, so… five stars!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Amazing dish. The one complication that I encountered was when adding the ya cai to the hot oil in the wok. The ya cai that I purchased (from an Amazon seller) came in a foil envelope. There was obviously sufficient water content in the ya cai such that a nuisance amount of spattering occurred. Have a spatter screen ready.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Made this a few days ago now, using double the pork called for (I had frozen pork in 16 ounce bags in the freezer).  It was great and worth the effort to acquire fresh lo mein ya cai (aka suimiyacai), and the right sesame paste from a local Chinese grocery.  I cut a dozen baby bok choy in quarters, producing reasonably-sized pieces.

Next time I'll use less pork - the balance between meat and noodles was, unsurprisingly, off.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Wow, we loved this. I did the capers-pickles blend and was scared because I have a husband who dislikes capers - so I only did 1/4 cup. Next time I’ll do the indicated 1/2 because it disappeared completely into the dish.

Next time I’ll chop the bokchoy after it boils. We like things bite sized. Also could see myself doubling the meat, because even with 12 oz pasta instead of 16 I wouldn’t have minded more tasty little chunks.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is a go-to, regular rotation, super flexible, crowd-pleasing, amazing leftover, all in one delish dish. Apparently it’s all the rage w the young people too, and I think— this time—I agree!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Crestfallen to find my tahini had expired ... so I subbed in with smooth unsalted peanut butter.  Highly recommend!  Vegetables this time: bok choy, fine broccolini.  Will repeat!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is a weekly recipe in our household now. We sub sautéed cabbage for the bok choy (cooked in the same pan after setting aside the cooked pork). Quick and easy and incredibly satisfying

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Quite delicious.    I made it nearly as written.  Went a tad higher on the sugar because it needed it.    One tbsp chili crisp was perfect.      Def brown the pork.    Crispy bits are a plus.       I had no preserved greens .   I used a small amt of pickled carrots and peppers that I made.    I brought it together over heat and added some of the water then added it was the right consistency in just 2 min.   It worked well.    I’ll make it again for certain

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was so great. I subbed vegan sausage crumbles and used chickpea pasta (all I had). Added 2-3 Tbsp sichuan peppercorns too. Will be in the regular rotation!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Really tasty - mostly followed the recipe for the first try - few tweaks noted below.  Will make with a few adjustments -
First try tweaks - added cabbage with the bok choy; added Korean pickled radish (sweet) to the meat and topping, added "Spicy Black Bean Chili" (Chinese label translation) - perfect umam, really spicy!
Adjustments - Increase vinegar in the sauce, Increase mustard greens and add a bowl on the side for topping (I doubled on first try); quick stir fry on the protein CRUNCHY

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Yuuuummmm… I made with half pound of ground pork, half point of ground beef. Drained the fat after it browned, doubled the meat seasoning. Used pickles and capers. Might throw in some Mama L’ils next time. Served in individual bowls. Everyone was happy.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My sauce cones out too thick. Any advise?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Add the left over water from the noodles. Or hot water.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wow! This is my second time making and this recipe is really a hit! This time, I used ground chicken instead of pork, and used savoy cabbage instead of bok choy, as that is what I had on hand.  I tried, but could not find ya cai. The bread and butter pickle substitute didn’t sound appealing, but I really wanted to try this so I omitted this key ingredient. I want to say that even if you can’t find ya cai, this recipe is still worth making. (I’m going to search Asian markets in larger cities for it so I can use it next time). I topped the finished dish with chopped peanuts and a bit of cilantro because I needed to use it while it was fresh, and it was 10/10 Chef’s Kiss! Pro tip - the first time I made this, I doubled the sauce because there did not seem to be enough to adequately coat the noodles. This time, I thinned the sauce with warm water to make it go further. Worked like a charm and the overall dish was very flavorful without being heavy.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This recipe is missing Szechuan pepper corns.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Gordon They are listed among the last of ingredients.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this exactly as written-got the ingredients from an Asian market. A note on Sichuan peppercorns: I opened up the bag of them and the smell was heavenly. So I popped one in my mouth-and the taste was evil and my tongue and lips went numb. I googled "how to use Sichuan peppercorns" and followed instructions to toast and grind them. I put a tiny bit on top of each serving and we tasted nothing else except the peppercorns. Dumped that serving and tried again without them... much better.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Forgot to buy the proper noodles so I had to sub in spaghetti but it still came out great! Definitely going to try again with the wheat noodles, which I'd imagine have a better texture and hold the sauce and meat better.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My 4 year old devoured this. I shall be printing this out!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was fantastic, and an easier method compared to other recipes I’ve tried.  I tend to go heavy on the sesame paste and this hit the mark.  I’m a bit gobsmacked by the commentary around “disappointment” in the inclusion of preserved ingredients… perhaps some folks should get educated on traditional ingredients in the cuisine they’re cooking from.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Very tasty, but marking this down to 4 stars instead of 5 because of the handful of obscure ingredients it calls for, which are tough to find outside major metro areas. I'm good with adapting a recipe, but like to make it as written the first time (to see if I even like it!) and adjust from there.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used kimchi for the ya cai (I didn’t have time to make the longer trip to the Asian market) and tasted great. But I feel like I must have committed a major offense since it isn’t listed as a substitute?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Easy 5 stars! Super wonderful. And super grateful I live close to Asian super markets!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used rice noodles, however i never cook rice noodles and I just figured it would work for 4 portions (rice noodle nests) but I did not feel like there was enough sauce. Otherwise great, people had seconds.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I found this to be bland and overly umami without other flavors to balance it. I added some ginger and would recommend more vinegar.  Probably a “me” problem since I generally prefer Vietnamese food with fish sauce and brighter flavors. I would not make this again even though I have an opened pkg of the fermented mustard greens in my fridge.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious! Lots of prep time. Used fresh garlic & ginger in both sauces. Made with shiitake mushrooms 1/4 red 1/4 yellow peppers, 1/4 head of cabbage & carrots. Used the American sub.s for Yin ban… & extra oil cause used tahini. It’s a keeper.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this with about every substitution you could imagine - pickles for mustard greens, white wine for Shaoxing, regular old tahini, udon noodles, hoisin… and it STILL turned out awesome! This recipe is foolproof!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Subbed peanut butter as I didn’t have tahini though still added sesame oil.  Delicious! Used onion, eggplant, broccoli, and red cabbage. The longest prep was finding all the ingredients in the pantry!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Worth the trip to the Asian market! Will be making again. Hair tingling definitely happened. I used the full pound of ground pork, but otherwise no subs.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Excellent. Each component is wonderful in its own right and they blend incredibly. Using cayenne to season the fish is a key element. 

I disagree about using more nuts…..he is going for an elegant, gentle coating, not a crunch

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: While this recipe is fair, I found that the amount of tahini was a little too high. But MOSTLY, for Sichuan Dan Dan Noodles, there was no call for Sichuan peppercorns. To be missing that tingling numbing spice is to make this ‘any-old-noodles’, not Dan Dan Noodles. An unfortunate omission. Even a splash of Sichuan pepper oil (light or red) would be a fix. And if you can find Ya Cai, you can find Sichuan peppercorn oils.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Apparently there are two types of Chinese sesame paste: toasted and black.  The final dish, when made with the black paste, was... a sight. But tasty! I will definitely make again, but with the correct ingredient. Also recommend cutting the gai lan (if using) in half. They are very long and might not fit in your bowls.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: About a tsp of cocoa powder adds depth to any chili and it won't taste like chocolate, I promise! I also like to add a little dollop of spicy brown mustard if my chili starts to taste too "brown" and needs a little balancing acidity.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @KDA Love a tablespoon or so of cocoa powder in my chili!! Such depth!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Agree on the cocoa! I personally use Dutch processed and also add a tablespoon of good syrupy balsamic vinegar at the end. Life changing!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @KDA added a tablespoon, which I have never added to chili before and really liked it--thanks for the tip.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Perfect for a vegetarian. Swap the Turkey for Beyond Meat crumbles, vegetarian broth for the chicken broth, and we are done. If the Chiles are too mild, swap for some "heartier" Chiles...

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Gary V. Those meat substitutes are full of junk

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: i agrre with uou avout Beyond meat. I use TVP or Textured Vegetarian Protein in lieu or ground meat when preparing a meal for my vegan son. Dried soy protein that you hydrate and can use in all sorts of meals like chili or stew etc... much healthier than any fake meat.  @John

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Just eliminate the meat…

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I love kale in chili. It adds great flavor.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @JJ: yuck. I am so over kale.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @JJ such a great addition!  I wasn’t sure what to expect, but was pleasantly surprised!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Reader in Vermont use frozen spinach instead.  However much you can take before it starts to affect the taste.  I eyeball it each and every time - whatever you do is good!  It's a super easy hack, makes it healthier and lighter

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I loved this recipe! I subbed the Hatch or Cubanelle peppers for one (extra) jalapeno, the kale for frozen spinach, and added green onion at the end with the cilantro. Very hearty and didn't need rice.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: "Didn't need rice"... that's like saying you made gravy and it didn't need mashed potatoes. Rice is traditional as a bed for chili. (I also like it with elbow macaroni or over a baked potato.) Chili on its own is a heavy meal.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I wondered about substituting frozen spinach - it's easier to find in the grocery store. Thank you!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Richard X I never make rice with chili. In fact, I don’t know anyone who does.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was so delicious. I didn't realize I was out of chili powder and ground coriander so I mixed and matched taco seasoning with chipotle and cayenne. And, added fresh corn. Some how it all worked.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Too hot from chilies

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @john golden, Camden Maine If the Serrano or jalapeño chiles are too hot, you can use poblanos. Much milder, great flavor.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Madrid I love chili! But I'm not a fan of too much heat... they're is such a thing as liking chili w/o burning ones tongue off....

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The author seems to suggest that simmering the chili for a longer period of time creates a better balance of flavors.  If so, I'd use 3/4 cup of dried lentils from Rancho Gordo or other vendors in Step 4 and simmer for at least 25 minutes.  I would also use ground dried chilies instead of commercial products.  My go-to recipe calls for 3 ancho, 3 New Mexico and 2 guajillo chilies toasted at 350 degrees for about 6 minutes, then seeded and ground in a spice grinder.  Always have some on hand.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Jim S. Yumm. I’m a Rancho Gordo bean club member - so yes to this. Where do you source your dried chilies?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Kat Egancheck out the chili guy (somewhere in New Mexico or Arizona, but ships)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I still have fresh Kale in my garden. I was thinking of adding the fresh chopped kale instead of frozen kale. What do you all think?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @JanWhy not? That’s what I will do as I still have kale in my garden as well.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’m confused. Step 1 says to cook onions until deeply charred, but step 2 says to cook until onion is transparent.  Which is it?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Anne Asher thank you!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: After the bottom is charred, cooking until the rest of the onion is transparent.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: We really enjoyed this turkey chili. Served it with sour cream, cilantro and fresh sliced avocados and rice. Truly a fun dinner. Didn’t have the cubanelle peppers and it was still so good!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: How does one acquire ground DARK MEAT turkey? I imagine I’d have to do it myself or find a decent butcher.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Scott. Most grocery stores sell ground turkey. And they sell it at a couple of different fat levels from low - all breast meat - to high - all dark meat.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Sigh. So few people comment who actually MADE the recipe…my question is, how to swap dried lentils for canned? I don’t think I have seen canned lentils…and is there a reason for frozen instead of fresh kale?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @nycreb I will use dried lentils and cook them separately until they are almost done, set them aside and add in as the recipe states. I will also use fresh kale (from my garden) and blanch it before adding.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If you take the extra time to use a turkey thigh or drumstick instead of ground turkey, it's a real game winner! Put them in first to sear both sides and then pull the meat off the bones when the chili is done.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If you're watching your macros and/or trying to get enough protein into your diet, this recipe is a keeper.  Rich and hearty flavor that disguises how healthy this meal is, plus tons of leftovers for a quick weekday lunch.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Jared if you have the macros for this handy, would you mind sharing them?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I used a 4oz can of chopped hatch chilis instead of fresh and subbed a diced large red bell pepper for the serrano pepper. I picked up
fresh chopped kale from the grocery salad bar. Nice fresh veggie forward chili with just the right amount of heat for us. Also added a cubed avocado to the cheese, cilantro and sour cream toppings. We’ll definitely be making this again!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Avoiding spiciness and working with what we had on hand ended up great: 
No peppers
Sub ground chicken for turkey (equal)
Sub frozen spinach for kale (equal)
Sub parsley for cilantro (equal)
Sub tomatoe paste for diced tomatoes (4tbsp)
Only 2 tbsp chili powder

Tried right after making and wanted to add some paprika next time but trying it a day later it seems perfect and not wanting paprika or anything

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: A BIG HIT! I Followed recipe with one exception.  I had to cook my own lentils, which was easy.  Everyone loved it!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I was liberal with the spices, added a sweet potato that needed eating, used frozen spinach from freezer and can of chickpeas from pantry, and ate several bowls very happily. It was also a recipe that actually took 30 minutes. Easy, tasty, quick, will make again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Okay so here’s the thing.  This was surprisingly good despite ingredients that would never be used in Texas Red.  I actually measured the main spices because my wife has been on me about following the recipe (pshaw) but I couldn’t help but add dashes of soy, mountain sauce, maggi, a teaspoon of fermented black bean paste, unsweetened chocolate powder and, in homage to Uncle Roger, some MSG.  The main spice Ko recommends was about right although three TB of Gebhardts was perhaps a bit heavy handed.  

Here’s the thing.  In the US the ground turkey providers grind it so fine and leave a lot of moisture in the meat.  Her crazy process of not browning the meat alone, and first, pretty much insured it wouldn’t brown properly or retain texture.  So change that, I will if I do this again.
