But I realized that the texture usually provided by the meat was instead provide by all that kale and lentils.  Did I hate that?  No but I surely should have followed my instincts and browned the turkey in maybe two batches prior to starting the veggies.  That’s my main gripe about what otherwise was a pretty dreadfully healthy and tasty dish.  I think it’s more a soup or stew than a classic chili despite the classic chili seasonings.  That’s why I went 4 out of 5

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is so fresh and unique! It was easy to put together, full of flavor and a great mid-week healthy meal.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Excellent recipe!  However if anyone can do all that prep in 10 min I’ll eat my hat!!  About an hour+ should be allotted

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Prep shouldn't take an hour unless you're slaughtering the turkey!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Richard X
Well,  plucking all the feathers ALONE took quite a while!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I would like to try fresh kale rather than frozen. would you have to do anything besides strip, chop and rinse?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Bad Dog blanch it first and squeeze out the water

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Great recipe. It's not 30 minutes but an hour. Still, delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Modified for Instant Pot (yes I still use it):  Use one cup of dried black lentils.  At step 4, skip the kale and cilantro and cook at high pressure for 8 minutes, quick release, then stir in kale and cilantro.  Top with pickled jalapeno.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was such a nice surprise - a true clean eating 30 minute recipe with delicious depth of fresh flavor, and! packed with lean protein and! fiber.  I made it mild style: the chilis in the diced  tomatoes + half a seeded jalapeño were just the right amount of heat for me - perfect with greek yogurt, and lime.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My sister and I cooked this a couple days ago.  Followed the recipe but used 2 pounds of ground chicken because we prefer it.  Heaped up the spices to balance with the extra meat.  Also used about half a pound washed lentils, not canned.  Used my sister’s homegrown kale.  We very much enjoyed this recipe for dinner and my sister, a nurse, froze rice plus chili for her lunches.  Definitely a chili to enjoy as the days shorten!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can I use fresh kale instead of frozen? Would that affect the texture/cook time?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @SDS it would be much better and far healthier

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Sharon ... It is well established that freezing does not remove any nutritional value.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: WAY TOO HOT!  I wonder if the 2 TBLS of chili pepper were supposed to be teaspoons.  If it weren't for the over the top spice it would have been a great recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Pat H. Too hot for you.  I add more peppers.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The recipe calls for 3 tablespoons of _chili powder_.  This is not pure pepper, it is a mix of spices, including cumin and garlic (this might not be the case for some Mexican brands).  It is not the same as cayenne powder or ground chili.  My experience is that the brand name chili powders use a fairly mild chili in their mixes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I believe the distinction is generally “chili” powder which is dried chile peppers ground with other spices such as paprika and cumin versus “chile” powder which is straight ground dried chile peppers. What you generally find in the regular grocery store is chili powder which is not normally that spicy. When I make my chili recipe, I use both. I order my chile powder from the Santa Fe School of Cooking.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this with frozen spinach, one jalapeño chopped and one Serrano chili chopped and ended up having to add more than one tablespoon of chili powder. I am a weenie, and not very familiar with cooking with fresh chili. The lime juice makes a huge difference and this is a bright and flavorful chili. Perhaps my favorite chili recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: In answer to Judith's comment on canned lentils, I found them either at Trader Joe's or Sprouts if you have either of those stores in your state.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: To answer two comments:  Judith, GOYA makes canned lentils. Scott, EMPIRE KOSHER has ground dark meat turkey (as well as ground white meat turkey).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this last night subbing black beans for lentils and a pound of fresh kale for frozen, and that, I think, was the problem. So much kale. It reminded us more of a kind of shakshuka than of chili! Still, it tastes good, albeit very kale-y, so we’ll have it with an egg for lunch today!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Just making this now. I’m letting it simmer for the long haul, just to let the moisture evaporate. Too soupy for my chili taste. Doubled up on the spices and used some tomato paste to caramelize cause that’s how my chili rolls. Also added a can of beans for the same reason. Full of good fiber and protein!  Just for the record, I’ve never seen a can of lentils anywhere so I just cooked my own and added. The rice vs. no rice discussion is hysterical tho! Gotta be a geographical thing…

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Karen J Trader Joe’s has canned lentils.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made with fresh kale and black beans with jalapeno and lime since no lentils.Only powdered chili pepper and no coriander but we thought it was best chili ever!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is delicious! My store didn’t have hatch or cubanelle peppers so I used poblano. Augmented the listed spices with Tony Chacere’s Creole Seasoning. Used a mix of fresh kale & spinach instead of frozen kale. Only used a little chicken broth as there was so much liquid already that wasn’t quickly cooking off. I had trouble keeping the turkey separate from all the peppers and spices in my Dutch oven. As a result, with all the green ingredients, my chili is actually a little green, lol! Maybe I should have cooked the turkey in a separate pan as others have said they did and then combine them at the end?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I just made this and it came out really well! I didn't follow the recipe exactly - I used a regular sweet red pepper instead of the other peppers and used only 1 can of the tomatoes with diced chilis and added 1 can of regular tomatoes as well.  It came out great and is a great recipe for meal prep.  I also love that it's a good way to get fiber in - a lot of meal prep recipes are very low in fiber so this is a welcome addition to my meal prep rotation.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Exactly what it promises. Used black beans instead of lentils and added some roasted sweet potato. Delicious the next day scooped up with chips.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Used 2 tbs chili powder instead of 3. Used 1 cup cooked, dried lentils instead of canned.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @nycreb I just made this with dried lentils. I added them with the tomato and stock, put a lid on, simmered for the required time (with stirring ever so often), added the frozen kale 10min before the lentils should be done, and the results were fantastic I really really enjoyed this healthy, hearty dish

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: can you use fresh kale?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
This recipe makes a CRAP TON of chili! I wasn't prepared for how much chili I had! But that's not a bad thing because it's delicious. 

I did use dried lentils, cooked for about 20 minutes prior, but I think adding them in dry and either adding a bit more broth or just letting them cook would be fine. I haven't ever used frozen kale before but it was a convenient touch. I was craving kidney beans (and thought they were in it already?) so I added a can and it was fantastic.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: can you make this recipe in a slow cooker?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Be careful about the liquid amount.
The ground turkey I buy is from a large company, and they add a LOT of liquid to their ground turkey.  I recommend considering at least a half cup of liquid from the store bought prepackaged ground turkey so you don't get soup.
I do plan to try this, we make "chili" frequently and will use for hotdog topping and in mashed potatoes as well as "in a bowl".

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Not to be one of those people but I made this with beef and swiss chard instead of turkey and kale, added paprika and a tablespoon of cocoa to he spices, and cooked it for 45 minutes while I made some (terrible) banana bread. The chili was excellent!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was prepared by an experienced cook and it took an hour. Can anyone really do this in 30 minutes?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I can do it in 30 minutes and have done it often. Experiences doesn't necessarily mean fast.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Kind of bland, needs more seasoning and is too watery for a chili.  More like a mexi soup.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Yet so many others say it's too spicy!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I'm putting in black beans instead of lentils bc that is all I could find.  Do you think that will affect the taste at all?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Of course it will. It will taste like black beans, not lentils. But the result will be fine.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was delicious! I followed the recipe despite the shortfalls of my grocery store. No frozen kale, so I used frozen spinach. Used Anaheim peppers instead of Hatch. Finally found a can of lentils but it was quite a search. As always, the prep kept it from being a "quick" recipe -  chopping onions/peppers/garlic/cilantro and opening cans - but it is quick once it's on the stove. A healthy alternative to traditional chili and the heat was just right. Will make again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Very similar to NYT's Turkey Chili recipe by Pierre Franey (which is excellent).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used frozen spinach instead of kale and black beans instead of lentils. And who has ever heard of CANNED lentils? There is no such thing at my local grocery store. This chili is much better if you simmer it an hour or so -- 30 minutes just doesn't do it. Also, if you cook it in 30 minutes, your chili will be too wet and runny. As another cook suggested, I added a spoonful of baking cocoa.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: we had Goya canned lentils at our grocery store

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I respect the attempt to create chili in 30 mins. I also don't think that makes it chili. I didn't do much differently but I did let it cook for an hour and a half. I love chili. I think I'm pretty good at making it after 50 years. Yikes.
Swap veg broth for the chicken broth or maybe just go with a good beer
Two many peppers...maybe add some carrots too...but I do have to admit I bumped up the heat with 1/2 chipotle chili powder.
Really good though and a keeper. Easy.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Used cooked buckwheat instead of lentils, and leek instead of onion to avoid high FODMAPs. Delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I love this chili! I made w 3 cubanelles and 1 poblano, doubled the garlic, and for the chili powder I added 1tbsp each of regular chili powder/ancho chili powder/chipotle chili powder. Served over cornbread with lime and cilantro..have been eating it all week and it just keeps getting better. Will definitely make again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: So good. Subbed kidney beans for lentils (throwback chili look), various other peppers for specifics, and added some cayenne at the end to adjust heat. Also added some frozen corn, and topped with lowfat greek yogurt. Very rich in flavor.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wondering if anyone has used leftover cooked turkey?  I expect to have some post-Thanksgiving and am looking for ideas that go beyond a hot turkey sandwich!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Y'all know that rice has no nutritional value.  I've never served it with chili and rarely eat it at all.  There are many other grains that have vitamins, protein etc.  That said, I really enjoyed this recipe.  I'm sick of kale too, but I used fresh here and it was actually quite tasty.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made as directed but left out the chicken broth because I wanted it thicker. Turned out great!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I thought 3 tablespoons of chili powder made it too spicy.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious! Added kale right out of the freezer—no problem. I’d simmer it longer—maybe an hour—for best flavor.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Comforting, hearty and yet light and fresh. No heavy/greasy stuff-- the herbs and kale really brighten the dish. Took a bit more than 30 minutes, but easy and well-worth the outcome. I can see this becoming a staple in our home.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: FYI Trader Joe’s sells precooked lentils in a plastic package if you have time constraints.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Frozen kale and canned lentils not the easiest ingredients to find... My guess is they are included to keep this to the promised 30 minute mark. Whenever I try an nyt recipe, I make a point of following it to the letter on first go round, so in this instance it meant visits to three markets... hardly a time savings! I ended up boiling some lentils while chopping the peppers and letting the kale thaw. I substituted a poblano pepper for the jalapeno as another commenter suggested, as my husband not fond of very spicy chili. It was still too much for him. Not sure what the highest heat setting under the Dutch oven is all about either... enameled cast iron doesn't really like that extreme heat. What I liked about this recipe was the combo of kale and tomatoes (needs more) and turkey. if I give it a go again, I'll brown the turkey first and then season this more in an Indian direction, garum masala, etc. perhaps chickpeas.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Great recipe though I toned down the use of chiles as we can't take hot things anymore.  I used canned diced green chiles in places of the Hatch and jalapeno chiles.  I also did not use the full amount of chili powder.  I cooked Rancho Gordo lentils and used those instead of canned lentils.  The recipe was a little watery, but after a day's rest in the fridge, it was perfect.  It's a great quick chili recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I really wanted to try this but was missing a few ingredients.  I used some turkey breast diced up in small pieces instead of ground turkey.  I had NO peppers, so left them out.  I didn't have canned lentils, so instead I used canned beans -- 2 cans, at that, because it felt like chili wanted beans.  I used one can of white beans and one can of kidney beans.  I also used less chili powder than called for because we don't like things super spicy.  It came out great!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I love this chili. And I like spicy food. I think the recipe has a mistake. Recommend three teaspoons chili powder instead of three tablespoons. Also add sausage if you want something a little more sinful.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This recipe was way to spicy for me! I am new to cooking and did not know that cleaning the seeds out of a jalapeño pepper with your fingers could cause a capsaicin burn around the cuticle area. I suffered for 4 hours before it finally went away. Do people know they should not remove these seeds with unprotected hands? Maybe I’m just a novice.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this and tried to hold back a bit on spices. I required both Prilosec and Pepcid AC for the distress, and even honey, add sour cream, did not cover the heat to make it manageable for me. I used Anaheim chilies. Was that the issue?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: can you use fresh kale and dry lentils (not in a can)?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Tricia I used fresh kale, just add bag a quarter at a time and stir into mixture until dark brown. Repeat until all desired kale is incorporated and continue with the recipe!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If you're going to add kale to ANY recipe, be sure to add plenty of canola or corn oil ... it makes it so much easier to scrape the plates into the trash!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Tasty and quick to make. I also added a tbsp of cocoa powder as some commenters suggested and thought that was a worthwhile addition. Main critique was that it wasn’t super chili-esque. I’d make it again but as a Tuesday night “turkey dish” not a Sunday “game-day chili.”

commentBody: Right. Place a couple of tablespoons of Morton kosher salt in the bottom of a glass baking dish. Pour some liquid smoke [Wright's?] over the salt so it can spread over the dish. author: Genevieve Ko

commentBody: @S in VA  For those who would rather reduce or eliminate the use of what seems to be prodigious amounts of plastic in this recipe, one could use a round, square, or rectangular flat bottomed, somewhat deep-sided Pyrex or similar baking dish with a good-fitting lid, and proceed with the flipping, and turning of the fish is described in this recipe. One could store it similarly – in a appropriately-sized glass container with a tight-fitting lid. My husband makes cured salmon in this manner quite often and with good results. He learned this technique from his mother and doesn't recall plastic entering the equation – ever.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Smoked Salmon Without a Smoker Recipe
commentBody:
For decades, I’ve gone easy peasy: liquid smoke. Just two ingredients: water and liquid smoke concentrate. 

Two brands: Wrights and Old Hickory.  Color pale gold because no additives. 

Others brands are not liquid smoke: they are ultra-processed.  Eg ingredients: liquid smoke flavoring (whatever the heck that is, whereas my recommended brands are captured smoke from burning wood. Yum!),molasses, sugar,  caramel color (commonly made with ammonia, acids, sugars and salts). Yuck. 

Spray or brush it on the salmon. If I am saucing, add some to the sauce.

Same for anything you want to have smoke or high-heat grill taste. If wet brining poultry, add to the brine. 

Or not: brush/spray it on then cook. When poultry is resting, save the juices. Add liquid smoke to them and pour over the meat. 

Red meats: if you’ve the time, brush/spray it on all sides then add salt and let it rest back of fridge on a tray  uncovered 2-3 days to “cure”.

Or not. Making burgers? Mix it in when forming patties. Premade? Brush/spray it on then cook.

Steaks etc? Just brush/spray it on and cook.  Save juices from resting, add liquid smoke and pour over meat (if serving sliced, more surface area is exposed for the smoky juices)

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Smoked Salmon Without a Smoker Recipe
commentBody:
There's a big difference between hot-smoked and cold-smoked salmon.  The temperature of the smoke is the difference.  Hot smoked is cooked and it can come out tougher and can even be jerky.  It's cooked salmon with smoke (usually a fruit or nut wood to create smoke).  
Gravlax is brined salmon, uncooked except for the brine, which usually has at least some sugar and mostly salt (this recipe reverses that ratio).  If you take gravlax and add cold smoke, you get cold smoked salmon!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Smoked Salmon Without a Smoker Recipe
commentBody: BTW, cold smoke comes from hot smoke that's been transferred to a different container to cool off the smoke in a cool environment.  I have set it up with a hot smoker and a clothes dryer heat diverter as a chimney, delivering the smoke to another container where my brining fish is.  Presto.  Of you can add liquid smoke if it doesn't bother you.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! Subbed some red pepper flakes for the chile and homemade (baked) corn tortilla chips for the fried noodles.  Served on a bed of chopped romaine (tossed with lemony vinaigrette) instead of  lettuce wraps - much easier to serve and eat.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: @Sara Schoenwetter this couldn’t be further from the recipe. Tortilla chips and lemon are Mexican inspired flavors. Makes no sense to have them here

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: @Sara Schoenwetter if that worked for you and it made you happy, ENJOY! We all had to substitute lots of ingredients during the pandemic, so do what you need to do to make a meal and be HAPPY!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Agreed!!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: My go-to method of separating lettuce leaves -- using a paring knife, remove the core from the head of iceberg lettuce. Run the head of lettuce under cold water, core side up.  The whole lettuce leaves practically separate themselves!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: I worked in restaurants back in the day when iceberg was 'a thing' for most salads.  We always gave it a wack core side down on the prep table and then just grab the core and pull out. Easy peasy!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: @Cindy that’s the method they use at in n out to get big leaves for lettuce wrap burgers. works like a charm

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: We loved this! I used my mini food processor for the onions, chile and garlic and cooked them all together for 4-5 minutes with the ginger. I’m not sure I’ll do the fried vermicelli noodles again. Since there was already crunch from the lettuce, celery and water chestnuts, I didn’t think it added much. They were also messy to try to break up and get in the wraps. I might boil the noodles for an extra component in the wraps along with some fresh herbs. It really makes a difference to rinse water chestnuts, so I feel like that should be in the recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: @Mary Smith timing on recipes is usually estimated after ingredients are already prepped because chopping time and ingredient prep time varies widely between cooks. I can dice an onion in a quarter of the time my partner can, and meanwhile my friend who was a chef earlier in their career can dice an onion significantly faster than I can. Meanwhile, the actual cooking time is generally more consistent from cook to cook. While I'm pretty quick at chopping, this one will definitely take me more than thirty minutes total!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody:
All the chopping, including the meat, in 20 minutes?  Probably not.

But I think I will make it as it looks really good.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: The food processor saves some time for the finely diced items.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: @Mary Smith the recipes do not include chopping/ prep times if the ingredients are listed as “ chopped onion” for example. The time assumes all mise en place is done. Hope this helps

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: @Lynda, true, but the practice is not at all helpful when trying to grab a quick evening recipe. Why not include a prep time alongside the cook time then? NYT could use an approximation of the time an average skilled cook would need to peel and chop garlic and ginger, mince the pork, fine dice the peppers, chop the celery… aye,my wrist grows tired. Newbies would find their slower, knife masters would be faster, but everyone would know dinner won’t be done until 6:00 when scrolling through the recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody:
A superior choice is the Laotian Lamb Chaing Mai, which incorporates toasted then ground rice for
texture, and most importantly, butter lettuce, which rolls up much more effectively than iceberg.
Butter letuce is the goto for all forms of lettuce wrap.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody:
Lettuce wraps have become one of our favorite appetizers/main courses and while we usually do chicken, the pork option will be our next try.

The sauce recipe will probably replace the package mixes except when we are in a hurry.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: This was great although next time I’ll either leave the Fresno pepper seeds in or add a second one for a little more heat. Surprisingly neither of the grocery stores we frequent had iceberg lettuce, so I used romaine instead and thought the slight bitterness offered a nice contrast to the sweetness of the hoisin sauce.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: I used Jicama in place of water chestnuts. Worked great!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: This was great! I made nearly as written. I used a cubanelle for the Fresno because I had it in my fridge and my kids don’t like super spicy (yet). I doubled the recipe because we like having leftovers, but 2 lbs of ground pork is a lot of pork so we did 1 lb of ground pork and 1 lb of super firm tofu. Topped with chow mein noodles instead of the fried vermicelli for pure convenience. The sauce was very good (doubled it).

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: This comes together very quickly and is really delicious.  I used pork, romaine, homemade hoisin, and added extra ginger for good measure. Far superior to take-out.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Have done this several times as written, and it's really yummy.  Like it so much that I've tried to make it when I don't really have all the ingredients, with various degrees of success.  A couple of discoveries might help in a pinch though.  When out of chilis, a floop of gochujang in the sauce helped out.  (I believe there are about 4 drizzles in a floop.) Once even used crumbled up leftover meatloaf.  Not great, but surprisingly not awful either.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody:
@Weasel
I so love your measurements and it is helpful to know how many drizzles to a floop.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: This was excellent exactly as written. Did use the fried mei-fun rice thread noodles for perfect crunch.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Excellent! Frying the rice noodles intimidated me, yet it was simple. The noodles make this dish! Read the instructions first, and have everything ready before frying. My Cuisinart was great for all the chopping! I added garlic/black bean paste to the pork and veggie mix. A lot of liquid cooked out of the meat so I doubled the cornstarch slurry. Besides hoisin, chop more chilis, some cabbage, peanuts and add sweet Thai chili sauce to the sides. And summer cukes make a great thai salad!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Sorry to be that guy but has anyone made this with ground dark meat chicken instead of the pork? Should work, right?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody:
@CJ
Can always substitute turkey/chicken for pork or beef. May have to jazz up seasonings.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: The tip mentions other options (including dark meat chicken).

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: @CJ, yes, the recipe even indicates that ground pork, chicken, or turkey can be used. I used ground dark chicken (and chopped bell pepper instead of celery), and the recipe is great.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Just made this and it was excellent, like PF Chang’s lettuce wraps. Although I’m not a fan of substitutions for the first try at a recipe, I forgot to buy hoisin sauce so I substituted sweet and sour sauce which I had on hand. I hate sesame oil so I used a little canola oil with fish sauce. This is a flexible recipe you can riff on a bit and it’ll turn out great. A keeper!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: instead of pork, I crumbled high protein tofu from Trader Joe's, and if worked fine

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody:
Made with tofu using the cooking method from the Can’t Believe it’s Not Chicken Tofu recipe and it tasted the same as the pork. 
Having it with shredded lettuce like a salad was also good if you don’t like messy hands.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: @DarlingH did you put in the aromatics before adding the tofu? The cooking time from the I Can’t Believe it’s not Chicken recipe is so much longer than this recipe I am worried about that impacting the final product. But love the idea of using tofu instead of meat, here!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: I find the filling very salty and heavy.  Any recommendations for substitutes?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: @Ben, we just made it for lunch. I extended it with a half block of crumbled firm tofu and added fine chopped raw red cabbage. I used low(er) salt soy and hoisin and the cabbage because it’s what I had on hand. Yes, it was likely a bit less greasy and salty (savory) but still very, very tasty! If yours needed crunch, you could just chop some of the cabbage less.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: A total hit for my party goers, and real fast to cook once you get things chopped! I will say the iceberg lettuce was a chore to separate, so in the future I'll probably use bib lettuce or something a little looser so fewer get ripped up in the process.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Made this vegetarian (vegan?) for my wife by using Impossible Beef. Followed the recipe except I added an extra chile because it was getting old... freaking delicious! This will be going on our regular rotation and I'm going to print it. Fairly fast to make, spicy/sweet/umami, I added some Chendu Crunch -yum.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Salty but very tasty, ordered water chestnuts and hoisin sauce from Amazon.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Made this tonight and substituted soy chicken replacement for the pork. Holy guacamole, this is good! This is definitely going into our regular rotation! I always worry about there being enough sauce, but the sauce was perfect. I didn’t bother with the crispy noodles, as I prefer the fresh crunch of the lettuce and veg. I might up the quantity of water chestnuts next time, or maybe add some peanuts or pine nuts. The diced chilis added a nice hint of heat, but if you’d like these to be extra spicy definitely add at least 2-3 instead of just one.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: I made this with boneless chicken thighs and it was delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: For those looking to move recipe to less expensive or more heart-healthy, I make a protein/fat crumbles with chopped mushrooms (can be frozen or dried), chopped walnuts, and cooked lentils in equal measure--blitzed lightly or chopped together.  All around delicious, cheap, and healthy.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: How in the world is this nearly 700 calories per serving? Even with the vermicelli??

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Nice to see a Chinese-American's recipe that evokes memories of my late father making a version of this dish for family dinners in the 60's.  He did not make a separate sauce - all the seasonings went into the stir frying of pork with other ingredients he liked to use, including tiny dried salted shrimp. It was always delicious. Thanks for featuring this recipe!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody:
Wait, the noodles are optional? You're mistaken. Don't even eat them (but do eat them!). I called my kids down to watch the noodles exploding into a puffed ball - we laughed and cried. Seriously, this is the best thing that's happened in the kitchen all month. Optional...sheesh. 

Oh, the rest of the dish is great too. There's a handful of dishes that become favorites - easy, the kids love it and can do fun a date night. This is all of it. Amazing. 6 stars!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: @Gregor Yayyyy!!! I’m so glad you made the noodles and that your kids enjoyed watching that magical poof moment.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: So goooood!!! Came together quickly even with the little bit of prep…I made this with ground turkey (even I was skeptical) the sauce is awesome!! Saw a suggestion here to add peanut butter…definitely will try that with some chopped peanuts on top! 5 stars? More like 10!! ;)

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: My family loved this!! I used a jalapeño from my garden with the seeds left in and it was perfect. Didn’t have time for the noodles, but hope to add them next time. This will definitely be added to the dinner rotation.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! Didn’t have patience for the noodles, but added Thai basil, and it just soars! All three kiddos loved it too!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: I made my own tempeh recipe last night and it’s nearly the same (I use broccoli and mushrooms instead of celery and water chestnuts.) Anyone interested in making this vegan (I’m not but love vegan food) can follow GK’s exact recipe but simply use tempeh instead of the pork. Guaranteed to be delicious, especially with the fresh, crunchy ingredients she’s chosen!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Pepper seeds are not a source of heat. Removing them has nothing to do with heat. It’s a food myth and is debunked. I remove them because they look bad and are hard to digest.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Really, really good and really easy.  But I was shocked that no one wrote about what a mess it was to eat as a wrap -- maybe we put too much filling inside the lettuce leaf.  The solution:  serve filling on chopped lettuce with dabs of hoison on top.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Tried to make this last night. It was (sadly) a disaster. The one step missing is draining the fat before adding the sauce. (I know, I should have done it anyway). But it was a bit bland (as well as greasy). Jicama sounds like a good idea though.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Just like the Chinese restaurant dish made with minced chicken and water chestnuts. Delicious! Doubled up, all gone

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Excellent. Served with tortilla wraps.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Finding it really hard to believe this recipe was rated as high as it is. Family of three here and no one liked it. Also feel iceberg lettuce was not the right lettuce here. I will say that it was fun frying the noodles though and they did add a very pleasant crunch.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: This was pretty good. The flavor profile is great. I think I’ll try it again using ground turkey or chicken. I’m trying to decide if I like the texture of the pork. The whole family feels like the texture for that was off.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: I made this as listed, except I used Morningstar farms chik’n strips. It was fantastic! This is going into the regular rotation.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried these ground pork recipes substituting a fake meat like Beyond Burger?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Hey experienced people - the ‘dried’ rice vermicelli is throwing me off a bit: does this mean cooked and toweled off, then fried, or does it mean fry them straight out of the package, uncooked?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: @NS, the rice vermicelli noodles are sold dry in cellophane packs in Asian markets and some grocery stores. You drop them into the hot oil and they puff up instantly.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Was the sauce not heated? Cornstarch does not dissolve, just suspends until it is heated and expands. Otherwise the sauce sounds grainy.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: @Owen  Yes, the sauce is added to the hot pan in step 6. The sauce that goes on the lettuce wrap is just hoisin.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody:
Fried vermicelli - SO FUN!!  
Added diced shiitake and fresh peas - served with rice and butter lettuce (didn't have iceberg).  Mushrooms added great depth of flavor.  Definitely will make again!  Looking forward to trying with tofu.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Delicious!  We did ours over chopped Romaine rather than wraps.  The fried noodles were kind of a disaster - didn't end up using.  I think cut up peanuts would be easier and quicker.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Have not made this, but will say the fried vermicelli noodles are super easy, really quick, cook it in small batches.....AND they freeze really well!!! I make big batches and freeze in ziplock baggies for whatever lettuce wrap thingie I make, the crispy vermicelli noodles are always a must!!!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Do you need to drain the grease from ground pork? And when would you do it if you should?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Wow, this was incredible!!  We substituted ground turkey for the pork and we added paprika and bumped up the black pepper. So flavorful!! Definitely a keeper!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Husband calls this "restaurant worthy" and I have to agree--absolutely delicious. Only substitution that I made was to use jicama instead of the water chestnuts (per Denise's suggestion; 1/2 medium jicama.) Will make again.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: What cut of pork is recommended, besides pork belly?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: @Denise  that's a good suggestion, both have the same mouth feel so I can't imagine what would be bad about it.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Was going to double recipe, realized mom didn't have large enough pan only after prepping everything, so threw half the pork in pan, planning to use just half of all my prepped onion, ginger, garlic, pepper. Brain freeze set in and I threw it all in. Did not double sauce, thankfully. Tasted great!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Cooked as written and it was delish! The vermicelli is fun and adds great crunch! We snuck up on it, tossing in a piece until the proper reaction was achieved and it was cooked in seconds! Very customizable and we’ll add some more heat next time!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: I recommend using green leaf lettuce leaves rather than iceberg lettuce. That's how the Vietnamese restaurants here serve their fried spring rolls and green leaf lettuce works great. It's healthier than iceberg. And vegan chorizo crumbles worked very well as a vegan alternative to meat.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Ran out of soy sauce - used soy vey teriyaki + hoisin instead of the sauce and was still yummy

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Used soy vey teriyaki sauce and it was still delish

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody:
Step 7, "To eat, **swipe some sauce** into a lettuce leaf, stuff it with filling and top with noodles"

But in Step 6, the sauce gets cooked into the filling, "Stir the sauce, pour it into the wok and stir until everything is coated and the pan juices have thickened, 1 to 2 minutes."

What sauce is Step 7 referring to? "Set the filling next to a platter of the lettuce, a dish of hoisin and the plate of noodles" Is it referring to "hoisin" or the "sauce" in the recipe?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: @ChrisK, a fish of hoisin.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: The rice noodles: cooked first, then dried, or dried right out of the package?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: per the recipe, you fry them.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: @David Shepherd you fry the noodles right out of the package; they should be dry and brittle when they hit the oil. If the bundles are really big, you may need to break them into smaller clumps with shorter strands. (Just grab the bundle and snap it with your hands as you would a corn on the cob.)

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: This is delicious! I skipped the noodle bit - because week night time. Really good mix of flavor and crisp!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Excellent and adaptable! I used what I had, leftover pork BBQ. The smokiness went very well with the sauce. I chopped up some sugar snap peas and added them at the end for crunch to substitute for the celery and water chestnuts. Small Romaine leaves were perfect.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: this was pretty good. forgot the pepper and added sambal at the last minute to add the spice back. added previously soaked dried shiitake mushrooms that I diced to add more vegetables

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. Quite easy and quick, but it’s important to prep ahead prior to cooking. Used ground pork and followed the recipe (but used lots of black pepper and two Serranos). I used butter lettuce and vermicelli noodles (cooked per package instructions, not fried) and dry sherry.  Side note: I used the sherry because it was of a better quality than my rice wine… that may be a sign to find a better quality rice wine for cooking when I know it’s going to be a prominent flavor.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Wonderful flavor, would consider adding finely diced mushrooms and switching some or all of the soy sauce for dark soy sauce to enhance the color.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: I knew these as "Peking 46 Delights." From my not so local Chinese restaurant, Peking 46," from when I was a teen.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: I make this often using Eileen Yin Fei Lo's recipe which is basically same as this. Make sure to make the crunchy noodles, it's really easy to do! The sub of jicama for water chestnuts is a good one. Lo suggests fresh water chestnuts but you will never find them.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: 2oz of noodles is a LOT. Stop at a few handfuls. Everyone enjoyed this, I used grated super firm tofu and added a big pinch of MSG. Might try a softer lettuce next time, the iceberg is unwieldy.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: A very long time ago this dish was found in London in a few top Chinese restaurants, including the very popular fine dining The Zen where it was made with Quail & nuts. Utterly delicious. The book "Classic Chinese" by Yan Kit gives a recipe for it that is lightly different to the one given here. This one here by Genevieve Ko is far more appealing as it has the sweet hoisin sauce. Nuts can be cashew nuts, almonds, peanuts and even pine nuts which gives another texture to this dish. Thanks NYT.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: This was excellent; used turkey and red pepper flakes for heat and it was fun w lettuce rolled up; we decided the flash firing was too much work so we added rice to add to roll up and sauce was fully consumed by our crowd.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody:
Made this tonight.  I used chicken tenderloins that I chopped up in the food processor.  In fact, ALL of this was made with the food processor and a spice grinder/minichop.  It came together in 1/2 hour.

I subbed green onion for celery (ok that wasn’t food processor).

In addition to the lettuce wraps (Romaine) I offered mint leaves because I had them on hand.  Wow!  If you have mint, give that a try!  It really added a fresh note to complement the peppers.  (Habanero, black and white)

Great recipe, will make again.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: @Mrs Child I almost spit out my coffee, laughing, when I read your comment!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Can the filling be frozen, or will the celery and water chestnuts get too mushy?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: I would like to see a video of someone eating this! It was all delicious, but I think you should have included a photo of the lettuce "wrap" actually wrapped, instead of just the finished ingredients. Mine made an unholy mess on the plate. But, as I said, delicious in all its parts and smushed together in my hands and mouth.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Thought I had ground pork on hand, found out I didn't, so I subbed sliced shiitake mushrooms. Didn't use extra hoisin on top, instead used diced English cucumber dressed with a bit of salt and some unseasoned rice wine vinegar. I left the seeds & ribs in the chile, so the cucumber was a cooling contrast.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe

recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Excellent.  Family loved it - rivaled any restaurant version, but enjoyed at home!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: Made as stated, minus the vermicelli. Excellent!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: I made this as written but might have added a little extra Puerto Rican rum - my guests loved it and asked me to make it for our upcoming supper club. The recipe was easy to follow and I did not encounter any issues.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: I made this with Gardein spicy sausage - vegan crumbled sausage product. I used half a habanero, because that's what I had, and it came out DELICIOUS. I was eating it by the spoonful as I cooked. Very easy to make and easy to veganize. Lentils would work as well, but I would cook them in a broth with a cut chili.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: I omitted celery and water chestnuts and instead, used carrots, baby bella mushrooms, and zucchini; I threw these, along with the onion, jalapeño (I didn’t have recommended chilis), and garlic into the food processor until everything was small. I heated up the wok, threw the veggies and some squeeze tube ginger in, cooking for about 3 min before I added the lean ground pork. I mashed the ground pork into the veg and cooked until done. Added the sauce and served in romaine lettuce boats with ground peanuts on top. Everyone thought it was amazing and I ate the rest this morning for my breakfast/lunch. Lots of helpful comments (I always read those before I cook recipes from NYTC) which made this turn out spectacular!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: I subbed some crumbled tofu for part of the pork. Next time ill do half and half. Keeps the flavoir but healthier and less oily

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: This was good! I ended up using a medium sherry which I think changed the profile a bit; it would have been better with a dry sherry and I will change that next time, but there will definitely be a next time. It is quick and flavourful and fresh on acocunt of the lettuce leaf wraps. I might try adding fresh cilantro next time....

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: This was absolutely delightful, a dish that was new to me. I largely followed the recipe, the only variation being to add fresh garlic chives from the garden and to omit water chestnuts (which I love when fresh, but find unsatisfactory canned). I also used a Boston lettuce as that’s what was to hand, and I’m not that big on iceberg anyway.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: I sautéed some cremini and shiitake mushrooms until they were almost crispy and added them to the meat mixture just before adding the sauce. I also omitted the celery because it’s not a favorite flavor around here. The mushrooms were a nice addition to the dish and I’d definitely make it again. Very tasty.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: So salty…and I’m a saltoholic.  Cut the Hoisin and use low salt soy.  Trust me on this.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: San Choy Bao (Pork Lettuce Wraps) Recipe
commentBody: i added some time by cooking the onions a bit more to get them closer to soft, and used gochugaru because i couldn't find fresh peppers. still tasty!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
OK, so you bought a bag of masa harina, made some Pancakes, what to do with the rest of it?
I stumbled upon a recipe for masa balls. The recipe was for Mexican spiced masa dumplings in chicken soup which if one drops the chili and cumin and sub in some chicken broth powder makes a great gluten free "matzo ball".  I mean seriously good, like from you favorite kosher deli good.  
Google masa balls for the recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Fresh Tacos! The bag probably has a recipe on the side you can follow. I don't have a tortilla press. I took a gallon-sized plastic bag and cut open the sides. Then I place a ball of prepared masa in the center of on side, fold over the plastic, then press to the desired thickness using a pie pan. You could use a cutting board, plate, sheet pan, whatever. Then cook on a griddle or pan. Once you've had fresh tacos, it's hard to go back to store bought

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @G.  Try a clean used cereal bag, just rip at the seam. Also great for rolling tricky cookiiebdough.  As well as veggies,etc. Have not bought baggies for decades.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Lee Ellman when you make chili, mix masa harina with water 1:2 and stir it into the chili to thicken and give a hint of corn taste. Yum.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Hello. I am hoping for clarification about which form of fresh masa is best. The recipe lists three types( sin preparada, para tortillas, y para tamales) but does not specify which one to use. It just says “fresh is best.”

commentBody: @Clarpea it’s the sin preperada that you want.

Oh, sorry, I see the recipe has been updated with calling for the sin preparada masa. 
I still stand by my comment using the masa for tortillas as it is more finely ground than the masa sin preparada, and is more widely available.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I am a pancake person...I have made many recipes for pancakes since my childhood. I made these as described with Masa Harina which I have on hand for arepa and tacos. This is hands down the best pancakes I have ever prepared. Thanks to Yara, Sean, and Genevieve!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Mark

I agree!  I couldnt wait to put the next forkful in my mouth!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Great pancakes but RFK, Jr. would not approve of the cornstarch.  Trump and co. have ruined democracy, and now pancakes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Randy Moffett No cornstarch in this recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I make masa harina and quinoa flour pancakes, in a 2 to 1 ratio of masa to quinoa (for me a batch is 1 cup masa, 1/2 cup quinoa).  Add an amount of liquid (milk, oat milk, water) equal to the total amount of flour.  Add egg and yogurt (vegan is fine), baking powder & baking soda, and skip the sugar, vanilla and oil.  A 1/3 cup measure doles out manageably sized pancakes to the griddle.  Great with maple syrup or a fruit compote of whatever summer fruit is coming in.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Gracias por su recete!  Locally, I can only source masa harina, so I was grateful for the tips; the overnight wait was wonderful - I mixed up the batter, and headed to bed. The masa harina was fully absorbed and "lifted" so the "pancakes" were gorgeous - I cooked over medium, since the time was longer. Covered a square skillet with non-stick cooking spray and they were simply lovely! This is a recipe I'll make again and again; it's savory, so could be used as a base for "taco night," etc. Yum!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: A few shortcuts made this super easy. I didn’t see the point of mixing the masa separately. So I mixed the dry masa harina with the other dry ingredients in a large bowl. Then threw all the wet ingredients plus 70 g of water on top of the dry (without blending them separately). Just whisked it all up. Easy peasy, just one bowl, less mess. I didn’t refrigerate the batter - just let it set for an hour at room temperature since I planned to cook them right away. They were very good, fluffy and tender. Not a strong corn flavor though. Like if a buttermilk pancake and a cornmeal flapjack had a baby.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’ve been making essentially this same recipe for ages and it’s great. You can substitute Kefir for the Yogurt and that works well - adjust the amount of milk since Kefir is more watery than yogurt.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are so yellow and cheery, but Blue Corn Masa with a pinch of juniper ash is also soooooooo good.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Is there a way to make this a waffle recipe?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Spencer cut the milk down till the batter is the right thickness

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I have not made this recipe but I grew up in Appalachia Kentucky where my Mamaw made cornmeal pancakes and served some with supper or as a dessert with molasses. So very yummy. At least once every fall, I make just a few for me!  And I remember earlier times.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Loved this! I recently had to go gluten free, so I subbed the regular flour for King Arthur's GF flour and the result was amazing—so fluffy and delicious, hands down the best GF pancakes I've made so far! Letting the pancake batter sit for 24 hours made a big difference as well.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
To adapt into a waffle recipe:

Double the oil, double the sugar, double the eggs. With the eggs, separate egg whites, adding yolks into wet ingredients. Just before cooking, beat egg whites until stiff and fold into batter.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I pulled together the batter - with masa harina - last night and cooked for breakfast this morning and served with fresh blueberries and syrup.  They tasted fine, but neither my husband nor I grew up in food cultures that incorporated much corn.  We don’t dislike corn but rarely eat.  So I think for someone who loves corn based dishes this would be great.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Had to make a dairy-free version (alt yogurt and milks) and can confirm that it works.  Also made a batch with purple barley flour, blue masa, and ube halaya in place of the vanilla - a very tasty deep blue/purple pancake (great with berry compote to put another hat on a hat)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I tried something atypical and mixed the dry masa harina into the dry ingredients.  I planned on leaving the batter overnight, which I figured was long enough to hydrate both the masa and the flour sufficiently.  Also, since all I had was butter milk, I used it for the the milk, water and yogurt, 1:1. They came out beautifully!  the taste and texture is divine.  I used blue corn masa. Bacon fat and pine nuts next time!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I followed some comments here and replaced the yogurt, water, and whole milk (by total weight) with buttermilk, and I also mixed the masa harina with the dry ingredients (no blender required, just made sure to whisk the liquid ingredients really well before adding the dry stuff). This recipe, even with only an hour of resting, produced the lightest, fluffiest pancakes I’ve ever made, and they will be my go-to recipe in the future! I mixed the batter at night, tested a couple pancakes after an hour, and then I used the rest of the batter after an overnight rest in the fridge. Flavor-wise, I think it was slightly better, but not enough to justify resting for so long if you’re short of time. Also, while they were still extremely airy and fluffy, the pancakes made on day two did deflate a bit after cooking. In the future, I’ll use the same replacement of buttermilk and putting the masa harina in with the other dry ingredients, but I’ll only bother resting an hour or whatever works for same-day prep. Excellent recipe!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Try serving with Cheri’s Prickly Pear Syrup, which you can order from her website. Color and flavor like no other, a fun way to wow your breakfast guests!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Amazingly good. I made the masa dough. Also cooked two batches. The first were on a really hot pan and they got a nice thin crispy exterior. The second time the pan was cooler and they had great flavor but not the crispy exterior. 
Superior pancakes for sure.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Has anyone made these with a longer (like a day and a half) rest? I want to make up the batter and take camping.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Answered my own question. Made these 24 hours in advance to take camping, and the batter was excellent!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Lee Ellman, yeah, those are called chochoyotes and they are a type of dumpling in traditional Mexican cooking, not a "gluten free matzo ball." Also, if you have leftover masa harina, you can make ... tacos? tamales? atole? It's literally a staple of Mexican, Guatemalan, Honduran etc cuisine.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Too much baking agent! They have a weird bitter/soapy taste. And my goodness as someone who makes from scratch sourdough pancakes regularly, I have never had pancakes stick to a pan as much as this. I had high hopes but this recipe is a no-go.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Has anyone tried a vegan version of these? I just bought a bag of masa harina to try making cornbread with and am thinking these would be a great way to break in a new cast iron pan I just got. 

I'm thinking of doing these using vegan Kite Hill almond yogurt and a "flax egg," organic canola with maybe some evoo for cooking, and of course no butter for serving, just syrup (with maybe some organic Earth Balance spread if I'm feeling dangerous.)

commentBody: I had never experimented with Greek yogurt with Masa Harina, but I am not going back. Instead of following the recipe I went for pupusas. Thank you!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Perhaps I misunderstand the distinction between baking powder and baking soda but I do not understand the point of using baking soda and then letting the batter sit overnight.  Won't baking soda use up all its leavening power in that time--as opposed to baking powder which will retain that power until the batter is used?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are ok, not amazing, not awful. Followed the recipe with masa harina. They are very thin. I'd try another corn pancake recipe before measuring out 5 TBS of water again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Bob’s Red Mill also makes Golden Corn Flour Masa Harina which you may find in a store and certainly online: https://www.bobsredmill.com/product/golden-masa-harina-corn-flour

commentBody: Will Masarepa work?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Marty “work” as in yield edible results? Probably. When I have some time I might do a comparison. But in the strict sense, masa arepa will not yield the same results and definitely not the results that would come from fresh masa.

commentBody: Anyone know how to convert this to a high altitude recipe?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Could I please have a clarification on the directions? If I use dry masa harina, do I skip step 1, and put the dry masa in the blender with the wet ingredients in step 2? Thanks!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @cocomero I haven’t made this recipe yet but I make tortillas often. I think they’re asking us to do Step 1 if you are using masa harina (dry corn flour mix) in order to make the dough. Once you have the dough ready you can make the pancake mix with the wet ingredients. Fresh masa is already like a dough so you could skip step one

commentBody: "Cornmeal and corn flour aren’t adequate substitutes because masa is ground from dried corn kernels that have been nixtamalized, following the ancient Mesoamerican practice of simmering dried corn in an alkaline solution to give it a distinctive taste and texture."

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: There are some other great cornmeal pancake recipes here in the NYT! I like the one from Alison Roman with blueberries.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Quite delicious! Used masa harina and blended with an immersion blender

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I love the idea of tiny skillets but as a non-restaurant owner this is a no go. Even a four inch cast iron pan is a big ask

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I found one, Betty Crocker brand, at Dollarama 🇨🇦. I think it was $4. Try a discount store? Walmart or Target?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are good pancakes but require at least 90 minutes of prep time, so I would only make these for special occasions.  I’d love to see a quicker recipe for masa pancakes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I cooked this using fresh masa para tortillas (all I was able to find) and I thought it was spectacular. I do love a good pancake and this was one of the best I’ve made.  Served with our standard pancake fixings: syrup, cinnamon sugar and frozen strawberries microwaved to just warm then blended into a sauce. 

I don’t have the prescribed pans so I cooked them on a griddle like regular pancakes and they were fluffy and light. 

I did half after an hour and half the morning after. I thought the one hour version was every bit as or maybe even more delicious, though that could be because I think I slightly undercooked the morning after ones, maybe partly because the batter started out colder. But in my experience baking powder doesn’t keep its oomph overnight. 

Not sure if anyone else experienced this but  it smelled awful right when the wet and dry ingredients were mixed - like a fart. It smelled better once the ingredients had melded in the fridge.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: On the latest Chef's Table a master chef from Mexico plays with vanilla and cinnamon as savory flavors adding cumin as well. I don't like pancakes for breakfast becayse they are so sweet.  I'm going to take a page from her and swing this savory which also widens pancakes to lunch even dinner fare. I'm encouraged by the masa ball soup idea and will definitely try that also.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Anyone try making waffles with this recipe?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can corn meal be substituted for the masa?  Sadly, I do not have access to fresh masa where I live.  Thanks for your help

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Joan In the Tips section below the recipe the chef says that “Cornmeal and corn flour aren’t adequate substitutes because masa is ground from dried corn kernels that have been nixtamalized, following the ancient Mesoamerican practice of simmering dried corn in an alkaline solution to give it a distinctive taste and texture”. However masa harina is a great substitute. It is shelf/stable and readily available at most grocery stores or online. Well worth the effort to buy a one-off ingredient because these pancakes are truly a treat!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I use a package of mini peppers, deseed  them and cut them in half . Cover them with some oil and add salt to taste. Cook them in the air-fryer till the edges brown. Add them to the vinegar, oil and garlic while still warm. The heat cuts the raw garlic. I leave the skin on where a lot of the nutrients are. Easy and delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Has anyone tried this recipe for waffles? I’m guessing that the batter will have to be thinned, presumably with milk. Or maybe the kefir suggestion without reducing the liquid?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: There's at least one mention in the thread.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Three stars because the baking soda will have largely reacted with the yoghurt if kept in th fridge overnight.  The CO2 generated might still be trapped in the batter and give lift, but it will  escape to some extent when portioned into the pan.  I'd consider adding it after sitting overnight, but that might overmix the batter.  That said, I'd opt for letting it rest for a half hour or so.  Somepone else needs to weigh in on this.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Exactly what the title says. Pretty good taste, but given the extra work and steps, I probably would not make again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My household loves atol-drinkable porridge  for breakfast. I keep jar of masa on the counter for that purpose. I stir in my collagen powder or protein powder for a morning snack.

commentBody: @Arjay answering my own question: subbed buttermilk for the whole milk and added an extra tsp of sugar to balance out the flavor. Used Masienda brand white masa harina and refrigerated overnight. They were outstanding.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Arjay I followed another comment and replaced the milk, yogurt, and water with an equal weight of buttermilk. It worked perfectly, and didn’t require using a blender. I’ll make this again exactly the same way, and I highly recommend it!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Or you can go the Venezuelan way and make Cachapas…

I don't have a 6 inch pan, but they came out fine.  Only flaw is having to plan in advance: to go buy masa and make the batter hours before.

The crispy, crunchy edges are the best part.  I have leftover batter in a container in the fridge and will test tomorrow to see if it's still viable.

commentBody: I made these the first time. Awesome. The second time I was distracted and make masa with 2/3 cup of harina rather than 1/2. Astounding how much of a difference that made. It was cement. I threw it out and started over.

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko

A note on the wait time: I made a few straight away as a midnight snack and the rest after letting the batter sit overnight. The ones I made right away were more tender and crispier on the edges, which I much preferred.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Since I was using Masa Harina, I kept expectations low. Maybe it was the fact I gave the batter a full twelve hours in the fridge and stirred minimally when cooking, but they were incredible fluffy and easily the best pancakes I’ve ever made (and I’ve made a LOT!). Those edges!! We served them with the cinnamon and pure maple syrup as well as with a homemade strawberry compote. They’ll be a weekend staple, and I hope to try them using the fresh masa soon.
