author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wow, lots of comments about calories here. I am a mom of two toddlers here. I'm constantly trying to get them to eat healthy fats and protein, rather than grains or fruit. This bar is perfect! Perhaps,  not for the overweight 30-50 year old trying to shed some pounds.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Caitlin agree, I’m always trying to get my 1yo and 3yo to eat anything with protein at all. This is a lot like an energy ball recipe I use but seems potentially less messy as the energy balls are more like no-bake oatmeal raisin peanut butter cookies.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: You can whiz regular rolled oats in the food processor or blender if you don't have quick-cooking ones.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can I use less maple syrup or will that ruin it? Also, can I add a scoop of protein powder to the mixture?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I understand that many of you see these as non"protein" bars. Perhaps they should have a different name. But there is one thing they aren't. They are not a boxed amalgam of preservatives and chemicals that often exist in "healthy" snacks. Do they have a higher fat content?  Yes. Are they a donut? No. There are benefits in eating all of those nuts and fiber and even natural sugars instead of corn syrup when it  comes to your glycemic index. Not everyone in the world is paleo. And that is okay.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I followed the Recipie exactly. Left in refrigerator for three hours. Attempted to slice and entire pan crumbled. Tastes good and is good in yogurt but it became granola. Would probably not make again if I wanted bars.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Laura Levy , exactly my outcome, having to eat spoonfuls from a bowl.  Makes a nice coating on a banana.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made these for the first time following the recipe except using whole rolled oats, and reading others comments I added 5-10g more maple syrup, used an 8x8” pan (so a bit thicker), and let them cool on the counter longer before refrigeration so as to help the thicker oats absorb more and adhere together better. It worked, with the occasional crumble of a corner. Also they are quite delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can I use regular rolled oats rather than instant?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I tested these with regular rolled oats and they're very tasty, but because they're larger and thicker than quick-cooking oats, the bars don't hold together easily. If you're ok with the bars crumbling a bit, you definitely can use rolled oats!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Genevieve - how about steel cut or pinhead whole oats? How could I modify to use these instead? Thanks!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Genevieve

I put regular oats in with the nuts and ran through a couple pulses in the small processor. Presto!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If you're looking to up the protein in the bars: chia seeds, wheat germ, egg white. For a great substitute for maple syrup, agave syrup is excellent and doesn't need to be reduced or cooked. Microwave nutbutter and sweetener in big bowl and then mix in rest of ingreds.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Why parchment paper its wasteful and absorbs flavor away from nuts silly
Easier and more ecological to rinse pan afterwards

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @J the parchment paper is for lifting it out of the pan in one piece for cutting it. I reuse my parchment paper if it’s still clean.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @J, I suspect that the parchment paper is less about making clean-up easy as it is about ease of getting the product out of the pan & onto the cutting board to portion. If that feels wasteful to you, you do you!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @J King Arthur sells square silicon mats with "handles" that you can use instead of parchment paper.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: A quick spin in a food processor for the nuts makes quick work of the chopping.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Matt et al, unless you’re trying to lose weight, I wouldn’t worry about the fat, which comes from the nuts. They are a healthy fat compared to animal fat.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I would like to be able to cook for  Energy Bars, but unfortunately I am handicapped and
I will not to be able to stay on my feet in the kitchenv for so long!
Is there a faster recipe to cook these bars?
Thank you!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Have made this recipe twice. The second time I measured each ingredient and followed the recipe closely.
Both times  ended up making granola as they never formed into a cut table bar.. not sure what I did in error

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Maryanne Adame Mine crumbled too. I am going to crumble them up and mix in more of the nut butter-maple syrup mixture and see if that sticks it together better.  If that works, next time I will either halve the dry ingredients or double the wet.
I used almond butter and walnuts.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was really easy and good, more of an "energy bar" than a protein bar, used honey (1/2 amount and plenty sweet) and peanut butter, wonder if a flax egg would bind it better; will definitely make again, great after a long hard hike!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can anyone think of a way to double the protein? Egg whites? I don’t object to a nut-based bar that’s even up to 400 calories (a meal replacement), but like others have said I opened this recipe looking for good ways to add protein more than I’m looking to reduce my intake of highly processed food (though that is desirable too).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Jon im not sure how processed, but i have had some success substituting the oatmeal component in other recipes partially or wholly with peanut powder (pb fit) pumpkin seed powder or protein powder.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Jon egg whites are a good idea and you can buy dehydrated egg whites, which don’t need to be cooked to be safe to eat.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made these after reading comments: blitzed regular oats in food processor, also nuts. Used agave syrup instead of maple. Let the pan sit out at room temp until completely cool for better absorption, then refrigerated overnight. The bars held together and taste good.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I measured my ingredients by weight and had no issues with the bars falling apart. I used homemade peanut butter (just unsalted peanuts ground in a blender) and old-fashioned oats. For the add-ins, I used 50g each of cashews, peanuts, and pistachios that I pulsed in my mini blender. I had about 1/4tsp ground cardamom in the pantry that I wanted to get rid of, so I tossed that in, too — love that addition. I would remove the black pepper next time, though. I enjoy sweet and savory, and I’ve been known to crack pepper into sweet oatmeal in the morning, but here, it just stings my throat in an unpleasant way.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Some didn’t crumble, most crumbled. Maybe I didn’t stir long enough. Too sweet for me.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made these with peanut butter and cashews. I used maple syrup and honey (low on the maple syrup) and it was delicious. I agree, these are energy bars. Oh, to the folks that had binding issues, be sure to boil for 2 minutes, then again 1 minute with the nut butter. (It will form the chewy caramel to bind to the oatmeal.) Hope that helps.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
My wife requested raisins be added, so I plumped a 1/2 cup. 

Also, I used a hamburger press to compress and smooth the dough. Worked well.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can you grind he old fashioned oats in a food processor to make them work in this recipe?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: That normally works in cookies, but those are baked. I think you’ll wind up with smaller pieces of raw oats.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wow! I made these with half maple syrup and half honey. I used peanut butter, cashews and sunflower seeds. (what was in the pantry) They are delicious, a little crumble on the bite, but well worth it. Will make again and again. (the full boil on the syrup is crucial; 2 minutes...then the second step bubbling; one minute is vital to bind the ingredients...I refrigerated for one hour, sat at room temp for about 15 minutes to soften for cutting.) Perfect

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: First time I made this it never set up…2nd time was a charm since I made sure to not overcook the syrup (2min is good) and made sure to wait for the syrup to go to low before adding the nut butter otherwise the nut butter is likely to turn to powder and not form the necessary “glue” to bind the nuts.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Too much sugar!  1/2 cup of maple syrup is way too much.  Maybe 2 tbsp.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
This is a sugary calorie-bomb that lacks complete protein.

I might make this for a backpacking trip when I'm burning a massive amount of calories, but I wouldn't make it otherwise unless I were severely underweight and needed to put on some bodyfat.

This has protein, but is not a "protein bar" in any real way.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made these exactly as written and then once in the pan, I sprinkled flaky sea salt over the top. They are delicious! One bar was incredibly satisfying and kept me full from 7am till lunch.  It is like a healthy, whole food version of a Nature Valley Protein Granola Bar.  For the detractors, my usual breakfast of 2 egg whites has 12 grams of protein, this bar is infinitely yummier with 10 grams of protein, so I am willing to consume a few extra calories to mix up my boring breakfast routine!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These tasted good, better than one of those “Perfect” bars which are probably more processed. I added some vanilla whey to up the protein count. Blended in amazingly. Don’t trust the comments. When it comes to protein, haters gonna hate.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I am trying hard to get more protein into my diet, while avoiding processed foods as much as possible. I cheated a little here, in hopes of increasing the protein and reducing the fat. I used powdered peanut butter, which someone kindly suggested, along with a single scoop of protein powder. The result is aa bit crumbly, but the flavor and crunch are quite satisfying. I suspect that the protein powder took something away from the recipe overall, but as I said, I need to increase where I can. Powdered pb seems to work just fine.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These sound delicious, but at a ratio of 23:9, these are Fat bars, not protein bars.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: if you have two of the same square pans, use the base of one to press the mixture down in the other

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: How would Cashew butter be instead of almond and peanut? Also how would collagen powder blend as an add?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Mine was crumbling and didn’t come together. I went over the ingredients and measured correctly.  I have a bowl of crumbs. Maybe put some with plain yogurt.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Did anyone add protein powder?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is not a “PROTEIN BAR”  it has a minimal amount of protein and mostly sugar and fat!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is not a protein bar. It's a snack bar with some protein in it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I followed directions but ended up with a granola rather than bar consistency. The almond butter didn’t bind well. It’s very tasty but I’ll use it as granola.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I added more oats to mine and added protein powder and water to the mixture holding it together. Also added chia seeds. Still higher in fat bc of nuts and pb but now high in protein and fiber. Also more oats make it higher in carbs. When I eat these bars I’m looking for a meal replacement to get me through work so I don’t mind the calories, but want a balance of fat, fiber, carbs, and protein.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can this recipe be made using powdered almond butter?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Not if you're trying to make bars – the nut butter is needed as a binder. See the note / FAQ at the bottom of the recipe. If you did use the powdered form, you'd definitely want to decrease the amount (otherwise the final product would be very dry) and you'd end up with something more like loose granola than a compact bar.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: How about zizzing up those whole oats just briefly in a food processor?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can I use quinoa flakes instead of rolled oats?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Do they freeze well?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I started making my own protein bars after I realized how many store brands contain sugar alcohols. I add agave syrup instead of honey for a lower glycemic index.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I thought I had all the ingredients on hand when I started-I had to make some adjustments and the bars turned out perfectly. 1-I used old fashioned oats and blenderized them as someone suggested. 2-I did not have enough maple syrup so I made up the difference with honey. 3-I used less almond butter than the recipe called for. It just didn’t seem right that 3/4 cup almond butter was equivalent to 217 grams, so I used 145 grams of almond butter.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This recipe is rubbish!  It does not stick together even after more than a day in the fridge.  It would seem to me that there is simply insufficient liquid in the recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Was excited to try these, but knowing my protein obsessed kids, they will not eat them unless the "macros" are better. Has anyone tried adding protein powder?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: With 2.5 teaspoons of sugar per bar, plus the fat and calories, I think these are a relatively healthy dessert rather than healthy snack food. As a vegetarian, I eat a lot of seeds and nuts, peanut butter and oats, but without the added sugar. if one skips sugar, food naturally tastes sweeter. I also make granola without oil and sugar, just water to make it clump. I would make these for a very active person, but as a senior, they are not for me.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I followed the instructions and they came out well. If I make them again I will definitely toast the nuts.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made these with almond butter, quick-cooking oats, 150 gm of mixed nuts, and pumpkin seeds, in an 8x8" pan, then refrigerated for about 18 hours. The taste is wonderful. Two of the 16 bars crumbled a bit, but the other 14 are intact. The pumpkin seeds are not sticking well in the bars, so next time I may chop them so that they are smaller.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used mixed seeds in place of the pepitas ( mix had some pepitas ) perfectly fine.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It’s not helpful to offer a “Protein Bar” recipe that is really just a snack bar- 300 calories and 9 grams of protein.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made them precisely according to the recipe. After one hour in the refrigerator, I found that the mixture had no cohesion at all. Just crumbled into granola. What did I do wrong?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Made these with really no modifications. Used peanut butter. Since the instructions were really big on talking about the nuts being well chopped, I ran my pre-chopped nuts through my nut chopper just to get the pieces a little smaller.
Love the texture of these; they're a little fudge like.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are tasty but should not be called a protein bar when a majority of calories come from fat and carbs.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Did anyone have trouble with the bars

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The mixture seized up after I added the peanut butter. maybe because it was the regular highly processed kind not natural. Very crumbly. Will make a great yogurt topper but can't send it in the backpack with the kid as hoped.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @APR mine did the same and I used organic PB! Mixture was completely dry and all crumbles after combining. In desperation I added a couple of splashes of water and it came together beautifully.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Has anyone tried powdered peanut butter (reconstituted with milk or water) with this recipe?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Did not stick together. Really just granola to put on top of stuff. Tastes good, but I would categorize this as more like a snack or dessert. It's definitely not a meal replacement bar.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: We all loved these. I used old-fashioned oats, and just ground everything a bit in the food processor. The bars pressed nicely into the pan and held their shape well. I may cut back on both the almond butter and syrup next time. Cut into 24 instead of 12. What a nice treat to have waiting in the fridge!  I may tryba handful of currants, orange zest, or butterscotch chips next time to make them extra decadent.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I see a lot of folks had the same thought I had...not enough protein. I'm thinking I'll add some high quality protein powder to the mix and these will be perfect. Has anyone tried that?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: From the intro in Jan 17 newsletter: To ensure they stay totally intact, bake the pressed mixture at 350 degrees for 10 minutes before cutting it into bars. They’ll be firmer and chewier than the original version.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I tried this, and it was an expensive failure.  The results are a crumble of nuts, almond butter and sweets.  I am unable to recommend.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made these twice.  First batch - completely fell apart after refrigeration. Like crumbles.  Didn’t hold together at all. Made a second time but increase the maple syrup and peanut butter and made sure to coat everything - result? - see above.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: What a complete waste of ingredients. The instructions said to boil the maple syrup for two minutes. I did it at least two minutes longer, because it wasn’t getting thick, though finally it was thicker. But obviously not thick enough. I now have a pan full of slightly sweet chopped up nuts and seeds mixed with dry oats that could be breakfast cereal if you like peanuts and almond butter with oats for breakfast. Maybe the birds would like it. Oh, well. Live and learn.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This has become a staple in my house.  I toast the oats and nuts first for a richer flavour. I also add dried cranberries. In the last batch I mixed in some toasted sesame seeds

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: followed recipe exactly. super tasty, but crumble to pieces. had to use as a pseudo granola topping instead.

author: Genevieve Ko

The crumbles were tasty, but not even chunky enough to use as granola.  I had pressed the ingredients into the pan with the back of a large serving spoon, but it didn't help.

commentBody: Probably best to look for a different recipe in that case.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I added Thompson raisins and dried cherries - good, but I still had a problem with crumbling. Don't think it was the fruit, though.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The maple syrup started to scorch before it reached the consistency of honey - possibly because I used a large pan with a broad surface area, so it was spread out. I ended up transferring it to a smaller pan and adding water to the almond butter and oats; otherwise I would not have been able to mix the ingredients as the mixture was too dry (the almond butter never bubbled, it was too thick). The taste is good, but the bars so far are not holding together very well. I'm going to try again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: What is the purpose if the black pepper??

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I make my own maple syrup with Mapeline. This substitute Doesn't Work!! I had a 9x9 pan full of granola!!! Use the real stuff.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Pepita seeds come from only certain varieties of pumpkin. Ergo, all pepitas are pumpkin seeds, but not all pumpkin seeds are pepitas.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used good quality maple syrup......boiled it....did not thicken.  Bars are now granola.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Love the taste but mine fell apart as I cut them. I used peanut butter so I did not expect this outcome. It’s more like “delicious chewy crumbs” to me.

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I would love any advice and ideas on making a savory version of these. I’m overhauling to an anti-inflammatory diet, and am in the process of coming up with DIY options for a very picky eater with complex nutritional needs. These look perfect, and I’ll definitely make them as is…but there’s only so much sweet one can take sometimes :)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This recipe has ripped off Angela Liddon's recipe for Glow Bars. Bad on you, Genevieve Ko!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Could you use quinoa flakes instead of oatmeal?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I agree with comments of seeing these bars for the fatty content. Recipes touted as protein rich should, at least list, amount of protein/fat/calories per unit in order to be helpful. Though I'm aware that nuts and seeds have fat (typically 15g per serving) content, they are my go to get enough protein to meet my 72 yo active needs in the morning, while avoiding eggs, meat & gassy whey products. Along with the fat/protein in my chosen low-fat yogurt or cottage cheese for my morning mix, I measure in a mix of enough nuts and seeds to attempt to exceed 20 grams of protein. I take another 10-15 gram mix of nuts in my pocket when going to the gym. About fat quantity complaints here, unless comparing to a processed "protein bar" I don't know how to get protein without fat - for what is appetizing to me at breakfast. I'd love to find the perfect mix for a "simple" home made protein bar.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Way too many calories for the protein. Sorry. Not gonna go on my playlist.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: making these contain a real protein content can be accomplished by adding protein powder - then, of course adjusting the liquids & butters. its an experiment that is worth the try - if the bars are too wet then keeping in fridge helps. delicious..

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Love these!  Great for a snack, breakfast or dessert (with a little vanilla ice cream!). I make them with steel cut oats. Extra crunchy

commentBody: I had the same problem.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious but make sure you have someone else who will eat them so you don't eat all the calories yourself!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly, but as soon as I tried to cut them, they crumbled. I'm not sure what went wrong.  I wouldn't mind a baked recipe if they would hold together better.  I guess I'll eat it with yogurt, or take it hiking as trail mix.  Otherwise it's largely a waste.  Tasty, though.  Any hints for better adhesion?  More maple syrup mixture so that it hardens better? author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious. To make the nuts and seeds crunchy, I lightly toasted them prior to adding to the mixture. To bump up the protein content from 10 g to 22 g, I threw in 12 (you could do less, or more) servings (scoops) of my grassfed bovine collagen powder.. (collagen protein holds up well to heat, and does not degrade.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this twice using volume measures, worked perfectly. Made twice using weight measures with a scale - didnt hold together either time. (Peanut butter every time). Not sure what the issue is, but I guess ill make by volume and wiegh it next time to get the rel weights.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Ours crumbled too but I loved the crumbles on yogurt or on top of my morning muesli. They’re perfect for me: I find granola to typically be too crunchy and too sweet for my taste.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These didn't hold together at all for me.  Using it now as a topping for yogurt.

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Proteins often have naturally occurring fats, essential for energy, cell development and maintenance and vitamin absorption

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’m very excited to make these!  According to my very healthy, fit and sane 26 year old daughter - we need fat carbs and protein.  She helped me shift my mindset about eating healthy (does NOT mean eliminating fat and carbs!).  I’m healthy and have more energy.  My ideal weight at 63 is no longer determined by my nostalgia for my younger body but instead determined by my health.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Genevieve Ko says, in the NYT: "To ensure they stay totally intact, bake the pressed mixture at 350 degrees for 10 minutes before cutting it into bars. They’ll be firmer and chewier than the original version."

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can you freeze these bars so they will keep longer?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Is baking not recommended? Wouldn't that make the crackers even crispier?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Like so many others, mine turned out to be a delicious granola, not bars. I followed some of the suggestions in the comments: I added more maple syrup, I let it boil for much longer, I left the mixture on the counter for an hour before I refrigerated it to allow the syrup and peanut butter to infuse, but none of that helped at all. My next move is to follow the suggestion to halve the oatmeal. It is so yummy, I really want a bar of it, but if this many cooks are having issues with a recipe that actually does not involve cooking ( just boiling?), I urge the NYT cook staff to look at it again to figure out what the problem is.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@lisa the problem is that you followed the comments instead of the recipe in the first place.
It works if you do what she says—cook it for the time she says, use the ingredients she says.
Cooking longer makes it hard crumbles.
Telling them to change a recipe you didn’t make because for some reason you obeyed rando commenters instead of a professional recipe developer and didn’t like your results is not appropriate.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are fantastic. 9 grams of protein per bar is great for someone who does not enjoy eggs or yogurt- or breakfast in general!   The bars hold together nicely if they are firmly pressed into the pan and certainly do not need baking.  I mix up the nuts and seeds, toasting all, as well as the oats, in advance for a richer flavour. A few raisins or dried cranberries is also nice, but reduce nuts to keep the same volume.  Re fat and calories, yes, they are there, but they are healthy and necessary

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Josiah k. If you check out other protein bar recipes, they all come in over 300 calories with abut the same amount of protein.  You'll have high calories because of the nuts which they all need.  Throw in some protein powder.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Would advise against making these bars.
They did not  hold together, no matter how much almond and peanut butter we added.
Consulted with  several excellent bakers for  help, and am now finally throwing the ingredients out.

Agree, it was like sawdust.
Please redo the wet ingredients or remove from the file.
Shelly Feb 2026.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I substituted all the nuts and rolled oats for 1/2 cup of muesli and also added protein powder. I agree with the comments that this is not a protein bar but it's tasty and I was able to reduce the overall calories to around 150 a serving. next time I'll use either honey or less maple syrup. I don't love the syrup flavor but it does make it a sweet treat that isn't as bad as eating a bunch of candy or ice cream.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: We cut these into 1 inch squares and eat as a dessert.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Tried this (using maple syrup and peanut butter, and a combination of almonds, pistachios, and cashews) and it was the consistency of sand once I stirred everything together. For the life of me, I can't figure out what went wrong. Any ideas?

This wasn't just crumbly at the edges, it completely couldn't hold together. Amounts of everything have been triple-checked...no errors there. 

Would love to try again, but not if it results in this mess. TIA for any advice!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Really good! I made one change based on Kim Boyce's granola bar recipe in Good to the Grain. I just toast the oats for a bit in a dry pan and then proceed with the recipe as is! I'll definitely make again!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Half of the pans bars crumbled during cutting despite following the recipe and providing a day of chill in the fridge. It tastes fine, not fantastic. The clumpy granola recipe I use tastes better and has a healthier nutritional profile. I will eat the crumbs using a handful for a serving size but won’t be making this again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I wanted to reduce the sugar and increase the protein while keeping the bars vegan. I decreased the maple syrup to 1/3 cup (112 g) and added 3-4 Tbsp of water. I also added (1 scoop 31 g) of plant based protein powder. I didn’t cook the maple syrup water mixture as long. It’s a better amount of sweetness with a bit more protein. The bars stuck together well, but might be a bit too wet. I might try 2 scoops of protein powder next time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I made these bars and yes, I agree that the oats should be whirled in a blender of food processor to make them so that the bar holds together better.  
Honey is not vegan?  Really, bees get there nectar from plants.  Plants are organic.  So how can honey not be vegan?  Bees are not killed nor is any part of the bee used in making honey so explain to me how honey is not vegan!  Picky, picky.
