commentBody: Carrot cake is great, but I have to watch my sugar intake. Any suggestions about how much can be cut back here whilst maintaining the integrity of the recipe?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @Michael, I suggest the flourless carrot cake recipe by Martha Rose Shulman for that: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1013341-flourless-carrot-cake I pair it with a dollop of Greek yogurt sweetened with honey or maple syrup. Not quite as satisfying as the real thing (sugar and all), but tasty nonetheless!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Agreed, two cups of sugar is insane!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: This has some good info on how to reduce sugar without impacting the cake too much. Good luck! https://www.kingarthurbaking.com/blog/2017/04/26/reduce-sugar-in-cake

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
Made exactly as written. Just put that puppy in the fridge for the 30 minute rest so the frosting can stiffen and the cake set. Ms Ko, the sheet pan hack is genius! Never again will I be intimidated by the prospect of baking rounds to stack (clumsily!) as this was easy, and the results delightful. 
Could you possibly adapt the hack to other layer cakes? Lemon, perhaps, or German chocolate? I'm not great at adjusting recipes and achieving perfection, but I will faithfully follow your every step.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
Hmmm, 2 cups flour (260 grams) 
2 cups sugar (200 grams) 
Which means less sugar than flour apparently? Luckily I have a scale, otherwise using measuring cups it doesn't seem right to use equal amounts of flour & sugar.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: 2 cups sugar = 400 grams; I suspect the recipe meant 1 cup sugar/200 grams.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @Ed, you’re right and as of my viewing, this seems to have been corrected and is showing as one cup/200 grams.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I am a  retired pastry chef and my carrot cake with cream cheese frosting was always in great demand. I wince  when I see cream cheese frosting with 2 cups of confectioners sugar in it. I use one cup (1/4 lb )  and it's plenty sweet enough and don't find any need for cream sour cream..

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I substitute puréed butternut squash for carrots.  Just as delicious and easier on your knuckles.  It also freezes well in glass pint jars so extra is always available.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Is there a reason to avoid the grater attachment on my food processor? My hands cramp up with repetitive motions like using a box grater. That's why I have the attachment. I wouldn't bother for just one carrot, but three....And I'll be using a mixer with the whisk attachment for the cake, then with beaters for the frosting.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I use the grater on the food processor for all carrot recipes that require grated carrots. The carrot strips are a little longer using the food processor, but that has actually been better for most recipes.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @Janet I use jarred carrot baby food- works perfectly.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @Athena#8  I use the processor. Works just fine and so fast!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Wouldn't it be easier to spread frosting if the top side is facing up? That's the smooth side

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Made this beaut yesterday and it is amazing- tastes great as well as beautiful to present. Left walnuts out of the batter but put some on top of the finished product. Grating the carrots finely is a must, thanks for the hot tip!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Made this for Mother’s Day ‘25 as suggested and it was a huge hit. I used avocado oil for the cake and used a fine grater for the carrots, per the recipe. The result was a light and moist cake unlike some carrot cakes, which are dense. I swapped in Melissa Clark’s cream cheese frosting for this one, and in that recipe, used lemon instead of vanilla for flavoring. The presentation of this cake is delightful and mouth-watering. Make it!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Carrot cake in any form is wonderful. I prefer it without frosting to mask the cake’s yummy flavor!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: This recipe calls for 1 cup sugar (200 grams) for the cake and 2 cups powdered sugar (230 grams) for the frosting.  Maybe they corrected the recipe, but this is how it reads now.  Actually, depending on the reference you use, once cup of powdered sugar weighs 115-130 grams, so the weights in this recipe seem to be correct.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Yes, the corrected the recipe from 2 c sugar to 1 c sugar.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I find that powdered ginger tastes more like dust than ginger, so after following the recipe I painted some ginger juice onto the baked sections of cake before frosting. Instant taste boost.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Used fresh orange juice instead of lemon and it was incredible. The orange comes through so nicely

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @Janet I used 5 carrots /my 14c Cuisinart for shredding, then wiped the bowl, inserted the regular blade, and prepared the batter.  Minimally pulsed until just mixed.  While the cake baked I cleaned the bowl and then made the frosting.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, and perfect for the big summer solstice party I attended.  The floral notes of the cardamom, coupled with the brightness of the lemon in both the cake and the frosting, give this cake spring/summer vibes. I wanted to make a layer cake for a crowd, so I tripled the recipe for the cake, and quadrupled the frosting recipe.  Would make this again in a heartbeat. People went crazy for it.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Would Greek yogurt work instead of sour cream?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: This is easy-ish, tasty, and a bit lighter than many carrot cakes.  I love the fun bar cake shape and easy layers. That being said, I ended up with shredded carrot EVERYWHERE. But it's a really forgiving cake (yes, mistakes were made in evenly cutting the strips; it still looked great when iced). It wouldn't hurt to take a bit stronger hand with the spices and lemon zest (and juice in the icing), and as other reviewers suggest, pineapple or perhaps better a dried fruit would be a nice add.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Brilliant idea of stacking thin layers! The cake was dark, dense and moist. Loved the lighter, cream cheese-with-sour cream icing. Added my preferred ingredients like fresh chopped, drained pineapple, shredded coconut, a half cup of shredded zukes, and subbed date sugar for the white. used half olive oil. Also subbed a quarter cup of almond flour and physillium husk for part of the AP flour. My new go-to! thank you.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: The frosting ruins this. It’s got twice the amount of cream cheese to frosting PLUS unnecessary sour cream and it makes it goopy and sticky and slimy, rather than the promised “silky”. Ruins the cake because the layers slip and slide all over the place. Had to scape off the frosting (even though cake and frosting were chilled in the fridge overnight), add way more powdered sugar and redecorate for it to have any sort of structural integrity.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: This was a very touching piece about motherhood. A question. I love carrot cake but my daughter has severe nut allergies. I’ve yet to encounter a good carrot cake recipe without a substantial amount of nuts. Will this recipe work without the walnuts?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I nominate any single or combo of tart cherries, golden raisins, shredded coconut, and chocolate chips.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I’ve make delicious carrot cake for years without the nuts. What’s missing from this recipe is a small can (8 oz) of crushed pineapple - drained.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: You definitely can omit the nuts!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I love to alter NYT Cooking recipes to make them vegan and whole food/less processed, this is my mutated version of their carrot cake. (Comment 1 of 2 because of the space limit) When ready to make the carrot cake, place foil sheet on foil tray, spray sheet with oil, mix whole wheat flour, whole grain flour, a tablespoon of grated carrot, whole sugar, baking powder, ground cinnamon, ground cloves, coarse salt, stir, then add just enough applesauce, agave nectar, and unsweetened soy milk...

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I made this recipe exactly as written for a long family weekend. Everyone enjoyed it. While it is different from my usual go-to carrot cake which is moister and includes chopped pineapple, this cake was still delicious and very easy to make. I liked baking it in the half sheet pan  and then stacking the cake thirds to make a three layer cake. And I especially liked the cream cheese icing. I was skeptical of the lack of butter, but the flavor was terrific. I used Neufchâtel cheese. The frosting was a bit loose but firmed up great after refrigeration. I’m sure I will be making it again.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I made this with King A’s GF cup for cup flour, and the flavor is good, crumb is nice but it’s a bit crumbly for building a three-story layer cake. Ended up trimming it to an easily liftable size (with two large pancake turners) and all was well, more of a squarish cake, with oblong snacking pieces of trim. Reduced sugar all around by 25%, with good results.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I used gluten-free one to one flour, and it was great

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
I followed the recipe with these changes:
* 1 15 oz can pineapple
* used 3 round cake tins
* added 10 mins. to cooking time
The cake came out great!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: This cake broke a long personal streak of poor cake bakes, so first of all - thank you. I subbed orange instead of lemon and put a dash of Fiori di Sicilia in the cream cheese icing, and it was absolutely divine. Not too sweet, not too fussy, and dead simple to assemble. This recipe is totally dialed in.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Delicious cake! Made it with for my birthday with  Bob’s cup for cup GF flour since I am gluten free… everyone (the non GF guests) loved it! Didn’t have ginger powder but had crystallized ginger and added that (yum!). I used extra virgin  olive oil, and did add 1/2 C of unsweetened shredded coconut with the walnuts. The cream cheese frosting is heavenly!! This cake is so good and really easy!!! Make it!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Made it exactly as directed. I was underwhelmed by the lack of flavor as I was snacking on the trimmed edges and thought it was too much frosting as I was assembling, but changed my mind after it was frosted and chilled a bit in the fridge. This is an awesome cake and I love how it looks when frosted with “naked” sides.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: So what if the mother is gluten intolerant?  Any ideas?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Looking forward to making this ... I have reduced sugar up to half the amount, and you can du applesauce as the author suggests she did for her toddlers.  But, can you reduce the amount of powdered sugar in the cream cheese frosting?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Way too much powdered sugar for my taste but halving it made the wrong consistency.  Very gloppy.  I recommend using a more traditional cream cheese frosting.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Trying to eat healthy, I would love to see the applesauce version recipe, and the possibility of substituting Greek yogurt for sour cream.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
Excellent cake and easy to make.  The presentation is striking!

For those who are curious about gluten-free substitutions, and those who don’t love cloyingly sweet cakes, my alterations:

— King Arthur Measure for Measure GF flour
— 50% less sugar in cake
— 50% powdered sugar, 50% powdered erythritol (“monk fruit” or “swerve”) for the frosting.

Cake turned out great, plenty sweet.  It was a bit crumbly (because of gf flour and less sugar), so I had to move it carefully with a spatula when stacking the layers. 

Next time I’d use slightly less sugar and less erythritol in the frosting, personal preference.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Could I cut the recipe in half? I am the only one who loves carrot cake.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I love the idea of the sheet pan for layer cake. I was hoping for applesauce and less than 2 cups of sugar however…

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Fix this - 1 cup granulated sugar = 200 grams. You call for 2 cups, so which is it?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I think I will cut back on the sugar and add crushed pineapple. It’s cooked in a sheet pan, so that’s  forgiving. Don’t skip the bit about rubbing the zest into the sugar. It really makes a difference.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious made exactly as per the recipe- using a sheet pan and then cutting and stacking was a great trick. Will definitely make this again.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Does the frosting require blocks of cream cheese, or could you use the same amount, by weight, of the whipped cream cheese?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I have now made this recipient twice and it was lovely each time. I did lower granulated sugar to 175g with no discernible effect. Weigh the carrots because what I think are medium carrots are clearly smalls. Half grated fine, half shredded worked for me and the cake was very moist, but not wet. It keeps well on the counter (covered) for 3 days but it is gone by then so I have no idea about longer.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I made the braised pork and the carrot cake. We loved both. The carrot cake worked out perfectly with just the right amount of cake and icing even though I was a bit worried since I once had some trouble with the proportions in Genevieve’s cranberry lemon bars 4 years ago. Always delicious results however.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: If you don’t want a lot of frosting, you might try Nigella’s Venetian Carrot Cake (it’s on her website). It does have a companion recipe for mascarpone cream, but you can just ignore it.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: See also Cook's Illustrated, which first popularized carrot cake baked in a half sheet pan for easier layered cakes.  It also included a tip for buttermilk powder to reintroduce the tang ( sometimes lost in beating cream cheese) for the frosting.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @Patricia G. cashew cream isn't tangy so you'll lose flavour. I can find vegan sour cream in UK, use that for the best results!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Loved the idea of baking in sheet pan and very happy with the frosting but disappointed in the carrot cake itself.  Next time I will go back to my mom's old recipe although the cake was a big hit with my book club.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Recipe has been corrected: 1 cup sugar, 200 g

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone made this recipe in 15 minutes prep time including preparing the cake and the frosting and assembling?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I've made this cake a few times now, and it's delicious as written. I've tweaked the recipe (of course) and now omit the nuts, use 1/2 the sugar, 1/3 more carrots and 2x the lemon zest, cinnamon, cardamom, and ginger (ginger ground as written + freshly grated). The frosting is perfect, though bits of candied ginger on top is fun. For a gathering, I use a bigger-than-cake/smaller than half-sheet pan, leave the cake as one layer, and cut it into bite-sized pieces. Rave reviews!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Easy -especially if someone else grates the carrots- delicious and absolutely gorgeous in a rustic way. Thank you so much for this recipe. I used half the confectioners sugar in the frosting and one regular cream cheese/one low-fat cream cheese. It was rich and tasty and more than enough frosting. I seriously don’t think it could’ve been better.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Step 3 states save the lemon for the frosting but the directions do not call for lemon. Has anyone added lemon to the frosting

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @Debra C If you don’t use Philadelphia cream cheese, the tip says to add some lemon for tartness.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
Replaced walnuts with toasted almonds
Frosting: 
Used same amount of cream cheese & sugar(16 oz cream cheese & 2 cups confectioners sugar), added 10 tsp unsalted butter, 1 tbs lemon juice, 2tbs sour cream, 1 tsp vanilla, 1/8 tsp fine sea salt

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Have tried this with AP flour and cake flour now. With cake flour I found the height was improved, but even with the shallow layers I got using AP flour, the finished cake is a good size — once stacked and frosted. Blitzing the carrots in my food processor rather than grating makes it truly easy. The sheet pan trick is stupendous and I never want to use round pans again!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: This so delicious. I didn’t have sour cream so used leftover buttermilk and a little yogurt, didn’t have powdered ginger so grated in fresh ginger. Only had a cup of all purpose flour so made up the rest with spelt flour. Read a suggestion in one of the comments about adding fresh pineapple, which I did, about half a cup and added a little more baking powder and flour. Incredible, the lightest, most delicious carrot cake ever. Only caveat, the icing looked way too much - it is, cut in half.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Made this with King Arther 1x1 gluten free flour, added drained crushed pineapple.  Great cake, very tender crumb.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I made this for Easter dinner dessert- we all loved it! Sheet pan way is brilliant and easy to make and assemble. Substitutions/adjustments that worked: I used coconut oil, Greek yogurt instead of sour cream, and only used 1.5 cups of powdered sugar for the frosting and wish I had used even less. Upped the lemon juice. We couldn’t wait for it to cool so hacked off a couple inches off the bottom of the big sheet pan piece and layered that with dripping frosting and out rest in fridge for later. No regrets. Fresh! Delish!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: How on earth would you cut a cake in a sheet pan into thirds?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I'm glad that before I made this cake, I read through the comments and was alerted to the issues with the icing recipe. I made the icing and agree that it is too loose. One pound of cream cheese and 60 grams of sour cream is not sufficiently strong for this cake. It might work on a sheet cake. Instead of the icing I'd made per the recipe, I immediately pivoted and made anther batch, the old standby (8 oz cream cheese, 1 lb powdered sugar, 4 oz butter, teaspoon vanilla).

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Delicious!! The lemon zest is the secret ingredient! Also used frozen diced carrots blitzed in food processor instead of fresh, added a bit of coconut and increased the spices by 1/4 tsp plus 1/4 tsp allspice. I think this recipe would be great subbing a bit of almond flour for the AP.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Can the cake be baked in a 9x13 glass Pyrex dish?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: The frosting was disappointing for me.  I put in half of the powdered sugar and it was already super sweet.  Without the rest of the powdered sugar, the texture was off.  More like whipped up sour cream rather than a thicker frosting.  Very disappointing - I'll be going back to a traditional cream cheese frosting.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I think the measurement for the sugar is wrong. One cup of sugar is about 200 grams. I made it with 1 cup of sugar, and it was just fine.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Amazing, fam made it for mother's day, not too sweet, and super easy because of the sheet pan approach! More of these recipes please, I want it for chocolate and vanilla cake too!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Thank you Genevieve for this amazing recipe! I made it for my family for our Mother’s Day Brunch, which had 2 soon to be college freshman and their soon to be empty nester mom in attendance. Everyone loved it! It was not too sweet, too much but perfectly balanced and satisfyingly wholesome! I sub’d in ½ cup of buckwheat flour and used Greek yogurt vs sour cream. It all meshed perfect!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: So fun and easy to make, I did decrease sugar by half but added cranberries.  I used vegan cream cheese and it tasted better and was very fluffy. I will definitely make again.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: My first try was too heavy . I think this recipe likely has too much carrot and way too much sugar. The icing was delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Wondering if i can replace the sour cream with cashew cream.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Who has a 13x5 pan??? What other to use?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @KAW 13x17 cut in 3 and stacked so cake is~ 13x5

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: There aer no baking trays this size in the UK. Any suggestions of how to adapt?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I would use the frosting from Dorie Greenspan’s best carrot cake recipe. When I made this frosting, it was way too runny and frankly wasn’t a very good taste.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: The spice mix has to be part of the cake, right? Dumb question but I just don’t see at what point it’s added to the batter. Hoping it doesn’t matter that much when.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: The spices are added to the flour in Step 1.  The resulting flour mixture (albeit referred to only as "flour") is added to the wet ingredients in Step 4.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Would this cake withstand an outdoor event in warm weather, or will the frosting melt? I’m wondering whether to go with this or Ms. Ko’s other carrot cake recipe. This seemed more festive looking so I was drawn to it.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: You would probably have to bring it out right before serving.  Cream Cheese frosting will melt a bit if it gets too warm.  However, this cake is definitely worth making.  Best cake recipe I have tried in years.  Both the cake and the frosting are outstanding!  enjoy

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I followed this recipe and this cake was absolutely one of the very best cakes I have ever made or eaten. It was a grand hit at my sweetheart’s birthday party.  Thank you thank you!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: This came out very flat, flatter than even a single layer out of three should? Haven't stacked yet but very concerned. It's like a cm and a half

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: The thinner layers stacked on top of each other and filled with icing makes a nice sized cake in the end. I chose to separate the cake batter into three round cake tins and cooked them for an extra ten minutes.  I didn't trust myself to cut the cake to make the layers.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I used sheep’s milk yoghurt (full fat and richer than most yoghurts), and it was delicious. My husband doesn’t like too much sweet icing and loved this one. I will say that, if I’d chosen to ice the side, I would’ve needed to increase the recipe by at least 1/3.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Can I freeze this? Before or after frosting it?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @Dr umbrella wrap it well in plastic wrap, then overwrap that in foil — BEFORE frosting.  It’ll keep for a good 3 months.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Can you use Grapeseed oil instead of vegetable oil?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Grapeseed oil is a type of vegetable oil.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Replaced 2/3 of oil with homemade apple sauce, yogurt instead of sour cream + more lemon (just to not have to run to the store). Reduced granulated sugar to 3/4 cup. Was delicious. Still a bit sweet. Used (2) 1/4 sheet pans and cut each in half.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Thoroughly enjoyed making this cake. However, the ginger and cardamom obliterate any other flavors. I added crushed drained pineapple and golden raisins. Made for a moist cake but it held together well. 4 teaspoons of lemon juice helped the icing, but I’ll cut down the powdered sugar next time.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I made this cake for our Easter dinner. I generally follow a new recipe to the letter and this recipe was not an exception. EXCEPT: I used 1-1/2 cups of powdered sugar in the frosting which was a perfect balance of sweetness to lemony tartness. I love this recipe!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: This felt like too much lemon for me. I had high hopes for this one but it’s just not my fave…

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I read all the notes and this was a super easy cake to out together (I used ground walnuts that made texture better imo and I topped with toasted pecans) however the assembly was more challenging than anticipated (ie getting layers expertly aligned etc. However the result, even uneven was delicious and using a sheet pan is a great hack!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
My kid asked for carrot cake and I used this recipe because it said it was easy.  It also was not tasty.  The frosting was good but the cake was dry and the thin layers were just boring.  I might have overcooked it but that is bc it is really easy to over cook because the layers are so thin.  

None of the kids at the party liked it.  Barely ate it. Would probably be good for a book club with a bunch of people who like to diet and have not had a really good fattening, soft, sweet carrot cake in 20 years.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
can I make this in an 8 x 8 inch glass pan?  I don't have a 13 x 5

thanks

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: You're not cooking in a 13 x 5. You're cooking in a 13"x17", like a cookie sheet

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
I love the recipe from NYT, I normally keep my bad comments to myself, as everyone taste is different
This time it is a math issue 13x9 is 117sq in. the recipe called to cut it in 3 section os 13x55 in it equals 213.5 sq in. we are missing some cake!!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: It says 13"x17", so we're talking more of a cookie sheet, not a a 9"x13" baking dish

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @JF they call for 13x17 in pan. I also don’t have half sheet but will use 13x9

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: If you have time the flavors were honestly better the next day. The spices had time to develop. I also swapped out browned butter for oil and infused the cardamom and ginger into the butter. Swapped out full fat yogurt for sour cream. Sublime and the use of cut sheet cake was so much simpler than three full cakes.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Could you just make the cake in the sheet pan and cut into squares after icing?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I halved this (except spices, added cardamom) and made 12 cupcakes.  Yum!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Made this yesterday, tried it tonight. It is replacing my favorite carrot cake recipe from Americas test kitchen. I did have to add extra lemon juice to the trader joes cream cheese I used - glad the recipe mentioned that might be necessary. Total winner!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! I only had a 9 x 13 cake pan, which made for kind of a tall cake once assembled. Next time I’ll probably leave it as a sheet cake and halve the frosting, because I had quite a bit leftover that I didn’t use. Took others’ suggestions here to use half the amount of sugar in the cake - the frosting was plenty sweet enough and I thought the cake had a really nice flavor! Will definitely make this one again.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Easy Carrot Cake With Cream Cheese Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Followed this recipe exactly and thought it was absolutely delicious and easy to execute. The only reason I gave it four stars is because I like pineapple in my carrot cake, but was scared to add it.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious! My only complaint was that the sauce was too sweet for my taste; next time I will cut the honey in half and add a salty element, perhaps miso? The cucumbers, however, were perfect. My husband and I cleaned our plates! Served with asparagus, drizzled with olive oil, seasoned with salt and pepper, then roasted along with the salmon.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody:
@Mama Liz
Cutting the honey and using white miso works, but try substituting the honey with marmalade with thinly sliced grapefruit rind cut to about 1-2” added that has sat overnight.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody:
@Mama Liz
I agree, way too sweet for me, disappointed. Glad I didn’t make it for Easter dinner as planned.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: @Katwe thought it was too sweet as well.  We served to guests this weekend.  I wish I would have read reviews first!  Next time we will cut the honey in half.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: I cooked this late on a Friday night for my 35 week pregnant wife. To call it a rapturous experience for her would be an understatement. After scarfing down this Succulent Salmon dish, as well as some buttery rice that I served on the side, the young woman recounted that “her perception of time was altered, 5 minutes could have been an hour, an hour could have been 5 minutes. My mind was consumed with salmon.” She was completely incoherent for a good half hour, consumed as she was with gluttony for the flaky, rich flesh of the noble fish. I thought it was pretty good, I put less honey in the glaze than was called for.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody:
That conjures up the movie "When Harry Met Sally..."

"I'll have what she's having."

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: What if you don’t like dill??

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody:
Jennifer, you might substitute another fresh green herb like chives, thyme, parsley, or rosemary. Or, thinly sliced scallions?

Dill seems to be a traditional herb for salmon dishes. I don't think I like it very much, either.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Tarragon works well with salmon, albeit not as well as dill

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: there are a million other recipes for salmon - use one of those

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Very delicious, but the sauce browned up too mich as it was cooking.   Next time I think I will just soften the butter and add the honey and a good amount of zest from the lemon(not the juice).  I will smear this on the salmon to cook...and perhaps add a couple of teaspoons of juice over the fillet once cooked.   Also, I halved the honey...and it was plenty sweet, but a nice balance.  The cucumber salad is outstanding and very easy.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: This is excellent. But unless your fish is very thick (or you like fish jerky), do NOT cook your salmon as directed at 425 degrees for 15-20 minutes.  That's way too long -- even for the old days before slightly rare fish was fashionable.  The Canadian rule of 10 minutes per inch is sensible and maybe even a bit long for this temperature.  I cooked mine for about 8 minutes and was very satisfied.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: @Stephen F canadian here. You are so right ! Frankly I like mine a little “wet” but I really don’t understand the discussion because like steak just cook it to your preference . ( I do confess I do not understand those who like anything well done but as the french say “ à chaque un son goût” )

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: I frequently make roasted salmon with everything bagel seasoning on top, honey mustard mix,  then top with overlapping zucchini slices. nice presentation and delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: @Sandy what is honey mustard mix?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: @Sassywilk maybe honey and mustard mixed?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: @Sassywilk I was wondering the same, but guessing a mix of honey and mustard ? 1 tbsp honey to 1 tsp mustard?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: What if you don't like salmon?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody:
@Garbanzo
If you don’t like salmon, why are you reading a recipe for making salmon? But w that said perhaps cod, halibut or sea bass?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: @Garbanzo perhaps Arctic char or steelhead trout

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: can you use ghee instead of butter?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: @Peggy W That’s what I was thinking I’d do.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: @Peggy W or @Law Chick Did ghee work?  Or has anybody mixed oil and butter?  My partner is averse (not allergic) to butter.  Buttery subs don't work.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: I buy salmon from a small company Alaskan fishing that does not remove the bones from the filet before flash freezing it.  I bought a fishbone remover but still had trouble removing the bones. The salmon was tasty but still full of bones yet it looked like I had macerated it before cooking it. Guests plates were full of bones. How embarrassing.  I wish The NY Times Cooking folks would include a link to a video to show how to take the bones out of the fish without turning the whole thing into salmon flavored mincemeat.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: How frustrating, especially because a fillet should be boneless by definition! If I get a fillet with pin bones, a gentle rub with my fingertips exposes the ends, which I then pull out with needle-nose pliers.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: @P R I buy salmon sockeye fillets with the bones removed from Alaska Seafood Company.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: @P R some Japanese markets sell stainless steel fish bone tweezers. The tip on mine is actually a full 1/2 inch wide so it allows you get a tight grip and a very strong tug to resistant  bones, but it’s also very finely edged to grab onto even the thinnest bones. It’s an amazing tool. Having said that, we love the big  fatty, buttery farmed raised salmon  that is so easy to filet, no fish bone tweezers necessary! Hope this helps!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody:
Delicious recipe! 
Just a tip for the pickles. Add some thinly sliced onions, crushed all spice berries, and fresh dill (all spice is a trick I picked up in Sweden!).

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Salmon was $38 a pound, so I got Chilean Sea Bass ($27) and made it per this recipe.  And I used English cucumber because my store does not sell Persian.  Excellent dinner.  Will try it with salmon when the Copper River salmon is available.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: @Matt Temmel. Wow. I live in WNC. And we get regular shipments of wild caught salmon for around 20-24 Lb.  Sea bass is very expensive here as is halibut.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: The harmony of flavors is insane.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: @Levente I couldn’t agree more, the absolute perfect recipe- have made it at least 25 times!!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: If I put the pepper on the salmon before the sauce, the pepper will run off of the fish.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: You're spooning it on top, not blasting it from a hose. The pepper will be fine.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody:
We tried this side-by-side (I had both on hand) with both wild-caught Alaskan salmon and Norwegian farmed salmon-- the Alaskan wild-caught definitely benefitted most from the delicious sauce! I'd say this is an ideal recipe for wild-caught, and 4 out of 5 stars for non-wild. 
And the pickles! Fabulous. 
To Jennifer below: I think any fresh green herb you like could work-- oregano, chives, mint, tarragon might be lovely. I bet Thai basil would be amazing in the place of dill.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: @Tory such a helpful and thoughtful experiment, I’ve switched over exclusively to wild-caught as opposed to farmed salmon. However as the latter is the most readily available I often wonder if recipes are aimed at the flavour profile of the farmed variety, so it’s great to read this. Thank you for taking one for the team!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: I use a trick when roasting salmon others might find useful. I line my rosting sheet pan with foil but do NOT grease it and put salmon on it skin side down.. Coat as you prefer . Cook at  425 or 400. When done, after sectioning into desired sizes if not already in serving size filets, use a metal spatula to serve, leaving skin behind by reaching right under the fish .  If you love  crispy skin, not for you.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: @Sally fish spatulas are my secret weapon!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: I have never commented before but this was soooo delicious! I followed all directions except that I cooked for 12 minutes and it was melt in your mouth perfection. Cucumbers were amazing. I didn’t have mustard seeds so I used g egg pound mustard seeds.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: I realise this is designed for the American palate, but to my European tastes this is simultaneously inedibly salty and cloyingly sweet, and 75 ml of honey overwhelms the taste of the salmon. The addition of the pickled cucumbers is genius, however, and works well with the dill.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: @Farncombe thank you for this comment!  I detest sweet meat or fish so that is why I came to the comment section.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: At what temp is it still done but still rare in the middle?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: This salmon slaps - easy to make and even the kids ate it, which is RARE. Following the advice of some others, I halved the honey to 2 tbsp but also added 1/2 tbsp of white miso paste. I made the cucumbers as directed and they were a bit too sweet for me but my wife liked them. If I were to make them again, I think I'd probably halve the honey there too.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: As written this recipe was good, but we had some leftover salmon that I turned into a pasta salad, which was even better. Added some chopped orange bell pepper, cucumber, red onion, capers, and fresh dill. Dressing was olive oil, lemon juice & zest, salt, black pepper, chili flakes, and sun dried tomato powder (not necessary but yummy). Love a recipe that works well for 2 distinct meals!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: I agree!  Love to get TWO meals from a meal, especially with today's cost of meat etc.  I get three meals for two people from a 4lb chicken.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: With any seasoning I cook salmon in the microwave about 3 to 3 and a half minutes maybe sacrelige but sure is easy usually once or twice a week

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Instead of using the oven, I grilled the salmon over charcoal (without turning). It was spectacular!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody:
I make this twice per week when i first saw this come out many months. Incredible.
I use the trader joe's Norwegian farmed with no skin and it is incredible. I did some research about farmed vs. wild and the norwegian farmed is in some ways better than wild (and sustainable).

I use 1 tsp of dried dill if i didn't have to buy any and I skip the cucumbers. Takes all of 5 mins to prep. I also broil for 3mins at the end to give the top a bit of a slight crust.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody:
We enjoyed the level of sweetness, pleasantly contrasting with the dill and cukes.  We did spoon a bit of lemon juice over the salmon (as a condiment), which may have helped avoid excessive sweetness.

This is now in our “when salmon prices aren’t insane” rotation!  Thank you!!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Y’all. The salmon is delish, but more importantly, I was inspired to tip a tiny spoonful of the brine into a freshly-made vodka soda, and it is DIVINE. Would be just as wonderful in a mocktail. Never discarding brine again without a little mixology experimentation first.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: made it exactly as written. Family loved it --- age range 4 - 71. Definitely would make it again.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Giving this five stars, because it is delicious and scalable. Don't like the sugar content? Reduce it, substitute, or omit. Don't like the butter - cut it back or omit it, too. It just works.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: What if you don't have yellow mustard seeds?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody:
@AW
You can use 1 tsp of either whole grain mustard or smooth Dijon mustard. Both will work.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: For P R: To help remove pin bones, try laying the salmon fillet skin-side down across an inverted bowl (adjust bowl size so fillet is nicely rounded) -- the pin bones will pop up from the rounded flesh so you can grasp them with fish-bone tweezers (or needle-nose pliers). For small fillets, bending it over your fingers works.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: As other users commented, a bit too sweet. Also even though cooked much less than instructed WAY too done. Won't make again. there are other wonderful salmon recipes on this site.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Can you use oil instead of butter?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Used half the honey suggested and cook at 400° until internal temperature reaches 125 came out perfect if you like it a little bit on the raw side. Cucumbers were perfect. Great spring recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Made this with about half the honey and added good dash of chili flakes to the butter, lemon, dill mix.  Fantastic and easy....make sure to salt the salmon well and let rest a bit prior to baking....cooked the salmon to about 135F to get just a touch of browning on top.  That sauce that is generated with cooking is killer.  The cucumbers go perfectly with this salmon as well as cilantro lime rice I made while salmon was cooking.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody:
Is the salmon skin on, or skinless?  I’ve found that skinless cooks quicker.  

Also, I’ve gotten used to using an instant read thermometer on salmon with much more consistent results.  Does the 125 degree rule still hold true to get to medium rare?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Thermoworks says: "The USDA says that salmon needs to be cooked to an internal temperature of 140°F (60°C) for food safety. If you have any immunity issues, that’s a good idea, but for the tastiest salmon, most chefs call for a pull temp of 125°F (52°C). "

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody:
@JimPi
I always understood that wild salmon, Internal temperature was 120 and farm Salmon should be 125 to 135. My research shows 140 is for well done which I don’t prefer.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: @JimP  I read in the comments section from a different salmon recipe from The NY Times Cooking by a person who lives in Alaska and fishes for salmon for a living is that one of the reasons for cooking the salmon to 140 degrees F is to kill the worms. Ugh.  So I cook it to 140 degrees no matter what.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Allergic to honey. Is it necessary for the recipe?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: I made this exactly as it was written.  I found that the cucumber salad was much too sharp from the lemon zest and vinegar.  I would try with 1/2 the amount next time of vinegar and add just a bit more of honey.  This is the opposite of what another person found so it's all personal tastes.  Experiment accordingly.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Can you do this with sockeye?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Easter lunch. Upped quantities to serve 12. Ditched the dill and pickled cucumber and used an Herb de Provence seasoning and topped roasted fish with chives.   Kerry Gold butter, Meyer lemon juice, local Arizona honey.  Rave reviews from 3 generations.  Served with NYT  Lemony Orzo with Asparagus plus peas,  Ottolenghi's  Iceberg Wedges and Smoky Eggplant Cream,  Everything but the salmon done ahead and in reality, the salmon so moist it could be served at room temperature.  Fist class meal.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: This was pretty good but the sauce burned to the bottom of the pan so there was none to spoon on top. Next time I think I’d set some aside to add towards the end. I also halved the honey.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: This was easy, delicious, and perfect for a new approach to Easter brunch. We dubbed it "Salmon and Waffles" and my family ate it all. Definitely a keeper.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious, but a note to say that raw mustard seeds can cause digestive issues in some people - it did in my house - so be aware. I would omit them if I ever make this again.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: While the sauce was delicious, the butter burned in the oven, causing lots of smoke, and the salmon came out overcooked.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Loved this and will make the salmon again. Due to lack of time, I only prepped the salmon, added baby broccoli (TJs) and left over Carbonara Orzotto to the sheet pan to cook/reheat ahead of the salmon. Only cooked salmon slightly less than 10 min. Perfect. To counter sweetness, I added a dash of Soy Sauce and a couple dashes of Tabasco to the butter/dill.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely terrific. The only issue I found was that the honey seemed to separate somewhat from the rest of the sauce and it over-caramelized on the sheet pan (ok, it was pretty much burnt). I followed suggestions and used just a tad less honey in the sauce, and we all loved it. Not too sweet for us. Otherwise followed the directions and will definitely make this again and again. Perfect for company or just for a weeknight meal So, so good.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Can anyone share tips for taking this from sheet pan to serving dish?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: I used a spatula and then did my best to use it to scoop up the sauce. Maybe a dough scraper would serve one best?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: turned out delicious and much quicker prep than i anticipated!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: The cucumbers were delish, and I'd make them this way again -- but not the salmon. I love Genevieve Ko's recipes as a rule but this merely okay, unexciting, like an afterthought. I followed the recipe exactly, as I always do the first time. And I will say the timing is accurate and the parchment paper is an essential suggestion since the butter and honey glaze slid off into a black mass (no pun intended).

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: I made the "single person's" version of this.  I used a single serving salmon fillet that was about 4 1/2 oz.  I mixed up a small amount of the sauce and put it on the fish before cooking in a toaster oven at 400 degrees for 15 minutes.  The result was amazing.  As long as I have some fresh dill around, this will be my new go to way to cook up some salmon.  I had leftovers of NYT's French Lentil Salad and that was my accompaniment instead of the cucumbers.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Really enjoyed this. Served over brown rice and it was the perfect light, mid-week meal but totally filling. Can't wait to try the leftovers tomorrow! Only had about 1 T rice vinegar and filled in the last 2 T with sherry vinegar for the cucumber: worked out very well. Next time I think I will let the honey butter mixture (for salmon) cool a while, maybe the whole hour or longer, so that I can smear it on the salmon instead of it running off immediately.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: The sauce ran off onto the pan and began to burn.  I had a kitchen full of smoke.  Honey at 425 is not a good mix.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: To be honest, I did foresee that possibility and made mine in a porcelain baker just so that I did not have salmon house. Worked out great.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: can you use neutral oil instead of butter?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: My whole family loved this! I made it as written and it got rave review, "Better than IKEA..."

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and light! I made it as is.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: This is SO good.  It was a great first "spring" meal for me.  Next time I'll throw some dill in the pickles (I love dill), but honestly, no notes!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: We aren’t used to writing fan mail, but this salmon was surprisingly delectable—sweet, savory, and a contrast in flavors that was delightful. We used slightly less honey than called for and cooked a little less time, served with green beans and lemon rice. Perfect! We are adding it to our regular rotation.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Just a beautiful dish. For the salmon sauce used at most 2T honey and the juice of 1/2 (moderately large) lemon. Also, put the pepper on the fish after dolloping on the dill sauce. For the cucumber salad, used two entire English cucumbers for the alloted amount of "dressing".  Served along with boiled red potatoes. Definitely one for the rotation!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Going to save this for a second mortgage purchase…price of salmon soooo expensive here!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody:
My 10 year old made this almost entirely on her own, and her six year old brother said it was straight into his 'top 5 dinners'. 

Not just delicious, but kid friendly on all fronts too - a solid 5 stars from us.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: The idea of adding miso to the marinade, as others have suggested, is interesting. The fermentation enzymes + salt in miso (especially when combined w/sugars), even for only 20-30 mins of marination, break down fish flesh, making it amazingly silky and melt-in-your-mouth tender, while also somehow firming it up. Other methods (added fats, slow cooking time, etc.) can help keep salmon moist + nice, but I have never found another marinade ingredient to replicate miso's spectacular results.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Another approach to the flavorings: In Thailand, an accompaniment to a huge variety of dishes, including roasted anything, is prik nam pla (pepper-spicy fish sauce). It goes well with roasted salmon. To 1/4 cup of fish sauce (Red Boat is the best brand), add a pinch of chopped hot peppers with their seeds (if you can tolerate them), minced garlic, and a generous squeeze of lime juice. Spoon the mix over the roasted salmon. Leave out the hot peppers if you don't like spicy-hot.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: I made this using fresh frozen dill and it worked fine.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: The cucumbers are the star of the show. And the salmon is pretty darn good, too. I'll be making this again.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Perfection.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: We indeed made this for Easter lunch (with cucumbers, a green salad, and roasted baby potatoes).  Very well received!  Had a thinner filet so cooked at 425 for 6 minutes and then let it rest in pan while we spooned sauce over.  Perfect. Only comment is that sauce clumped up after mixing (as butter cooled) and before placed on salmon.  I reheated in briefly in microwave (on “soften” setting), stirred and it was fine.  Would absolutely make again.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Really delicious.  I was afraid that it would be too sweet, but we thought it was just right.  I cooked it for 15 minutes and it was rare in the center, which was fine for us, but next time I will go for 20.  Looking forward to the leftovers tomorrow.  Yum!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: We decided on this last minute and used a big farm raised fillet from Costco. Delicious. Salmon isn't usually my favorite and the dill wasn't overpowering--just gorgeous.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody:
Used about three pounds of salmon divided into two large pieces. Delicious! And so lovely for a celebration when presented like the NYT image. 

I used regular Danish cucumbers. To make them a bit less likely to overdilute the sauce with their water content, I lightly salted them and drained them after ten minutes, and then proceeded with making the pickles as called for in the recipe.

Everyone ate the salmon, including the small children, and most ate multiple helpings.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: This was excellent! A big hit on Easter and wouldn't change a thing.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Sensational. Would definitely make again. Perfect for easter brunch.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: This was really good! I reduced the honey to 2 Tbsp. I made the spicy cucumbers from Alison Roman's Weeknight Salmon (also this site), which includes 2 Tbsp Aleppo pepper and 2 Tbsp olive oil. Served with jasmine rice and roasted Ontario asparagus. I'll be making this again soon!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody:
As @Mama Liz said, the cucumbers are perfect. I served them as a condiment with burgers. Also put them on a sandwich with the roasted salmon which I had made into a salad. They got rave reviews.
.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: When making marinades I taste and adjust as I go along. In this case, a little honey at first and more if necessary. A bit of mustard might mellow it out too.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody:
"Quite warm to the touch" ... sorry, that's an absurd way to approximate how "done" the salmon is, at least suggest a visual in terms of opaqueness or a temp to check with an instant read thermometer.  Salmon is very forgiving, I grant you, but lots of people fear cooking fish for exactly this reason - not knowing how "done" is done.

And 5 Tbsp honey is crazy sweet, unless you're serving this for dessert!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Great salmon, did change up a bit of the recipe though

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Loved it the way it was.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Yum. I adjusted the salami sauce by doing 1/8 cup white miso and just under 1/8 cup honey and added half a shallot. It wasn't too sweet and the shallot tasted great and added a nice purple dash to the salmon. I served with the cukes, dilled rice and lightly sautéed spinach.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: This dish is delicious and so flavorful. I will definitely be making it again. I followed the recipe exactly, but cooked the salmon in a shallow baking dish instead of a sheet pan. This saved the sauce so that I could pour the leftover onto the brown rice had I cooked as a side. It was a great addition everything tasted wonderful!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Both the salmon and the cucumbers were delicious and easy to pull together. The salmon was perfection - just barely past rare. The cucumbers were such a hit, that they will be made again and again - especially over the summer as an accompaniment for salmon and hamburgers. Next time, I think I will skip the dill in the sauce. I love dill, but couldn't really taste that which was cooked; I think adding it at the end and maybe cooking just a little will yield more taste.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Did anyone cook leaving the sauce as is without any sweetener

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: I splurged on a pound of wild King Salmon so cut back a bit on the ingredients for the sauce. Be generous with the fresh dill. The flavor gets muted when it's cooked. I think dried in the sauce would be okay too. Also make the cucumbers in advance. They were too sweet for my taste initially but over time the mustard seeds got soft and the vinegar came forward. Served with roasted baby potatoes. So good!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: @Alex The salmon is baked skin side down and doesn't crisp up due to the amount of liquid. I left the skin on the pan when I served the salmon. Wild is better in my opinion but skin-on is not a factor in this recipe so use what you can afford and enjoy.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Agree with others that it was a little sweet, despite subbing for hot honey. I liked that it’s kind of poaching in butter a bit. Realized half way through making the pickling dressing that i didn’t have cucumbers so subbed for shaved carrots and worked well.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe! I am cooking for two, so I used an 8 oz. portion of salmon and adjusted the sauce ingredients downward--especially the honey, based on other reviews. I cooked the fish at 400 in the air fryer on a foil tray sprayed with avocado oil. It only took 7 minutes to get it the way we like it. Absolutely beautiful and delicious! I served it with brown rice, a simple salad and those divine cucumbers ... the cucumbers put it over the top.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Delicious.  Used Trader Joe's farmed norwegian which was perfect. Only change I will make next time is to lower cook temp and shorten time, so I can low broil at the end for caramelization of honey on top but medium cook of the salmon.  With the pickles, my husband and I loved it!  I also added some smoked sea salt to the sauce, which always makes things tastier.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: This recipe yield a wonderful salmon and cucumber dish. I wasn't so convinced before I started that lining the pan and putting salmon on top would make much of a difference. It really did. Moist and delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Invest in a good digital-instant read thermometer. Guessing the internal temperature of salmon will almost always result in overdone fish.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Can you substitute oil, such as olive or avocado oil, for the butter?

author: Genevieve Ko

commentBody: I am a relatively new cook. Can someone please tell me how long I can keep the leftover salmon in the fridge if I put it in a tupperware? I really appreciate your input! Thank you.

commentBody: I would say 2 days after cooking.

author: Genevieve Ko

commentBody: Why the pre salt.? I’ve never done this; on a low sodium diet so just clarifying. Does it serve a purpose?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Excellent recipe. Will make it again. We're not fans of cucumber (Persian or otherwise) so I substituted green and yellow beans. Also realized I had no mustard seeds so I used a smaller amount of horseradish. It was really tasty.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody:
Made this tonight as a practice run for Easter lunch.  I used 3 skinless salmon fillets and the juice of 2 lemons as i worried it might be too sweet. I cooked it in a ceramic baking dish with a bunch of asparagus underneath the salmon
 It was delicious! For Australian readers i cooked this at 220c for 20 mins and it was moist and not over cooked. Pickles were excellent

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: The honey part sounds disgusting. Has anyone just left that out?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Surprisingly delicious. Perfect combo of flavors to complement the fish. Will definitely make again.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Second review of the week. Made for Passover x 5. Let sit with smoked salt in fridge for 4 hours. Cooked at 375 for 10-11 min. Low broil 4 min. Topped with the quick pickles. Everyone asked for the recipe. Stunning.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe but not crazy about the sweetness of the honey - may try Mike's Hot Honey next time to give it a kick.  The cucumber salad as fantastic, too.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: I made this for Easter and everyone loved it! I have no leftovers!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Very good and easy. I cut honey amounts by half for both the salmon and the cucumbers and very glad I did. Roasted asparagus in separate sheet pan at the same time. My very large farm-raised fillet (almost 3 lbs) needed 16 minutes, possibly the 2nd sheet pan in the oven increased time needed. Also served brown rice. Great dinner party fare. Will try with wild salmon when it’s available fresh.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Excellent, and easy. We wrapped it in foil and put it on the grill and then opened the package at the end for about 2 minutes. Other than using grill, made it exactly as it says.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: I would agree with others that the sauce was too sweet. Cutting the honey for the salmon by at least half might salvage the recipe.  The ingredient proportions in the cucumbers was good.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: this did not turn out well. I like dill,  but the sharp off sweet is an issue.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Would you.kerp skin on or off?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Salmon was great but the honey burned—I would cook in a ceramic or glass dish rather than baking sheet to save the surrounding sauce.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Amazing! Easy prep, had all the ingredients except the dill which was fantastic and the cucumber side dish- delightful!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: i cooked my 1.5 inch salmon for 10 minutes and it came out really dry. i definitely recommend cooking for less time and keeping an eye on it so as not to overcook.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Marinated cucumbers were delicious. Used a different marinade for the fish.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe

author: Genevieve Ko
