author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Made per the instructions. I didn’t particularly enjoy the salmon, the sauce came out sweet and watery. The texture of the fish was good though.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: There are better salmon dishes out there.  The combo of honey & lemon just didn't do it for me.  As for the cukes, the real "agurksalat" from Scandinavia is 1/2 vinegar, 1/2 sugar - tastes much better than this version.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: Delicious-we are only 2 so halved recipe with1 lb of salmon. Perfectly cooked, perfectly delicious. Thank you Genevieve Ko!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Lemon Butter Salmon With Dill Recipe
commentBody: This was great! I doubled it for company and had one 1.8oz filet and one 2.5. As other commenters suggested, I used less honey. Cooked at 350 for 15 minutes to an internal temp of 125 and it was absolutely perfect.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Re serving-Should the apricots be sliced before, or after, the pickling process?

author: Genevieve Ko
Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The recipe is for dried apricots, not fresh. California apricots are already sliced in half. Turkish aren't--maybe slicing those in half would be good. Trader Joe's has excellent dried apricots.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This cake is delicious! Very buttery and rich. I made it as written with the kosher salt amounts, but found it to be quite salty so would reduce the salt next time, particularly in the glaze.

author: Genevieve Ko
Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I’ve been making this pudding since (not to be too presumptuous) before the writer of this recipe was born. And yes, the quantity of salt is wrong. Halve it.

As for the apricot pickle, that’s just silly.  I understand where the chef was coming from — South African apricot chutneys (yes, made with vinegar and sugar) are a thing – a big thing – but don’t really belong on the same plate as the pudding.  It’s a bit like pairing champagne with pot au feu.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Lakebluffer Agreed! Wish I’d read your comment before making this.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Has anyone tried this with Cup4Cup or similar gluten free flours?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Mimi Yes, personally I’ve used King Arthur Measure for Measure GF flour and it turned out great.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Easily halved in a loaf pan! Good way to try things in small households.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Which of orange marmalade or fig preserves is the best substitution for apricot jam? I have both on hand.

Checking this week's grocery budget, I cannot afford buying a jar of apricot jam only to use one tablespoon.

Thanks.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Gus, NYC

My preference would be strained marmalade.  Maybe you could run it through the food processor to make a jammy substitute. Pieces of fruit in the batter would change its texture.  (Of course, you still might like it, but it would be less like what is described here). Just my humble opinion.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Gus, NYC Funny you should ask that. As soon as I read this recipe I thought that I would put in some of the white fig jam that I recently received as a gift. I think it will be a little less sweet than the apricots and more to my liking.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Gus, NYC I recently made the NYT roasted chicken thighs with carrots, which called for apricot jam, and it just so happened I had some. It was delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Try peach preserves or orange marmalade maybe. To avoid weird colors from darker jam.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Any thoughts on replacements for apricot jam. No one in my household likes it so I’d rather not buy a whole jar just to use a single spoonful. Would mixing in strawberry jam change the flavor dramatically?  Ideas welcome!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Cindy D I think you’ll want to consider the color of the jam — will the batter be pretty and pink, like strawberry, or weirdly grey, like blueberry or grape? You might use peach jam, if you want to keep it closer to the original concept.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Cindy D i agree with the suggestion of marmalade as a better substitute for the apricot jam than strawberry

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Cindy D I mostly use apricot jam as an ingredient - it makes an excellent glaze. If you do decide to buy a small jar, you could use it on Easter ham,  chicken (google sticky apricot chicken and broccoli),  salmon, etc. Sesame-apricot tofu was a hit at our house recently. It doesn’t have an intense apricot flavor jn the finished recipe. Adding a bit of apricot jam to BBQ sauce for glazing chicken drumsticks is also excellent.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Was pleasantly surprised! The cake batter smelled of cider vinegar so was doubting how it would turn out. Made the glaze and the pickled apricots (which I also doubted because of the smell!) and dinner guests loved it! Very light in texture and taste. Was missing the ice cream which would have been a nice contrast with the warm cake. Did use less salt. Easy & quick recipe. TY

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This looks delicious and similar to a tres leches cake (my all time favorite.) For readers who don't want to invest in a regular sized jar of apricot jam, try looking for a miniature one at World Market in the picnic section of the store.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Thank you, Sam; strained orange marmalade in place of the apricot jam, it is!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: maybe lemon curd instead of the apricot jam?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I haven't made this YET.  But I always read through the comments before i do. Regarding saltiness, beware that not all kosher salts are created equal.   If you have Morton's Kosher salt instead of Diamond, you should only use 1/2 tsp - the same amount if using table salt.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made the cake as directed. It baked up exactly as described, but I found it to be quite fatty (not in a good way) from all the cream and butter and rather bland in flavor. Just fat, sweet and not much else. The texture was good on the first bite, but became monotonous to eat. I had a small slice and didn't want to eat more and the husband just had a spoonful. Wouldn't make again, but enjoyed trying something new.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: interesting, do mean by fatty it's bad tasting or just too 'not healthy' tasting, in which case I'm going to try it! but perhaps you find tres leches cake too fatty tasting too. I think if you love butter like I do that I will love this cake, but if one is worried about fat (not that nutritionists are anymore) then you might like ABC pudding, it's interesting and might be right up your alley.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I was somewhat disappointed in my version of this delicious-sounding dessert, using the recommended ingredients and - as a minimally-experienced baker - followed the directions slavishly. My big issue: cake was too soft in the center (it cratered at the center and was mushy even after baking for 34 minutes). Will try again after getting an oven thermometer, and/or reducing the volume of milk a little, baking closer to 40 minutes,  and being careful not to over-whisk. I would also try to make it less sweet and might add some more vinegar and take the option of adding the option of pickled apricots to temper the sweetness.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It's not you. I'm a very experienced baker, followed the recipe exactly as well. Mine cratered a bit in the middle, had very little flavor and was generally underwhelming. Why bother trying again?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Yum -- sounds kinda like Tres Leches, but with fruit. If someone were to buy a cake mix from the store to approximate the cake instead of making it from scratch, which mix would it be? Asking for a friend.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
There is so little jam in this recipe that it doesn’t matter THAT much which kind you use. If you don’t like or have apricot, use something else. I was in a place with no grocery store and some strawberry jam in the fridge - cake turned out great.
I did not have a problem with the cake not absorbing the liquid, as another reviewer mentioned. I followed the recipe exactly on timing, poking lots of holes, using warm (hot) liquid, etc.  That said, next time I might experiment with using 1 TBL less water and substituting 1 TBL of water with 1 TBL of amaretto. 
Most American recipes have, IMO, far too much salt so I used a big pinch of table salt in the cake and a small pinch in the liquid. I also reduced the amount of sugar in the cake.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: An 8" square cake is going to feed 8 to 16 people? Not in this lifetime. I suspect the serving size has been adjusted to lower the calories. Other recipes for malva pudding come in at about 800 calories per serving, with the same ingredients in approximately the same quantity, but with normal serving sizes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Liz It works out to eight 2 inch squares or 16 1 X 2 inch squares.  For a very sweet cake with fruit and cream topping, that’s a normal serving size for 8 for a plated dessert or 16 tray snack sizes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @JW Actually, cutting an 8”x8” cake into 2” squares gives you 16 pieces. If you cut it into 8 pieces, you’d have 2”x4” rectangles. I agree with you, though, this sounds like a really rich cake and the “servings per cake” number given seems reasonable!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I am an ex South African who has been making Malva pudding for 3 decades using the original NY Times/Mount Nelson Hotel (I proposed to my wife there) recipe https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/4925-malva-pudding?q=Malva%20 to rave reviews from our friends and family in NY and Pennsylvania. The original recipes that I have do not call for salt, and nor have I ever tasted salty Malva pudding in South Africa. So for a more authentic South African taste I recommend not adding any salt at all.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wow, that is a lot of hype over nothing. Very basic cake, pouring a lot of butter and sugar over the top doesn't make it more special. I didn't even use some of the sauce, it was all too much. You surely don't taste the apricot jam (I used 100% fruit jam). I added some Grand Marnier to the rest of the sauce to pour over to add some flavor.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’m surprised by some of the negative reviews of this cake. It was excellent: well-structured, moist, creamy, and incredibly simple and quick to make. I followed the recipe exactly as written and had no issues. The salt level was just right — not too much at all. I suspect those who found it too salty were using a different kind of salt; if you’re not using Diamond Crystal, either get some (it’s worth it!) or adjust accordingly. The cake baked perfectly in 25 minutes. Def will bake again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Too salty, kind of bland.  Not even close to the “best dessert” I’ve ever had.  It doesn’t make the top 50.  Tried it, tossed it, now I’m going to forget it!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Sounds similar to a cake my mother made in the 1970s with cranberries in it.  The sauce is kind of like a melted hard sauce, without alcohol.  The pickled apricots would probably be good in chicken salad sandwich filling, too.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I've made this twice in the past week! I've eaten it warm, cold from the fridge, with ice cream, with my fingers, you get the idea. It's that good.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Did anyone else find this too salty? Next time I’ll use half the salt.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This looks right up my soaked-cake street.  The pickled apricots in particular lit up my brain -- I know I'll be trying those soon, cake or no cake.  They sound like they'd be heavenly with almost any kind of roast or braised pork dish.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This looked so good. I had no issues with the amount of salt.  My complaint is flavor. There is none! I even added apricot preserves to the glaze. Should I try it again, I’d add vanilla and maybe cardamom?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Love this recipe!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I didn’t do the pickled apricots, just the gelato, and it was delicious.  I cut it into 12 servings which was just about right.  We had left overs, and I tried melting a square of dark chocolate over the top of one piece.  That was really good.  Next time I might add the chocolate before the sponge cake is completely cooled.  I might try the pickled apricots for something else, maybe a salad, but not for this dessert.  I’m not sure why it’s called pudding.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Pudding is 'british' for dessert.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I can't wait to make this with this year's apricot jam from my trees later this summer.
Apricot-raspberry jam would be fantastic in this.
I think I would want to put some jam in the glaze to bump up the apricot flavour.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious little treat.  So easy to make.  Everyone loved it and many of them requested a copy of the recipe.  One person said “It tastes as if a sticky toffee pudding and a tres leches cake had a baby”.  The apricot jam doesn’t really lend an apricot flavor at such a small amount.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I added 3 tbsp of apricot jam ti the glaze in place of the water and it upped the apricot flavor considerably. Also made the pickles. Try it again! ❤️

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’m surprised by some of the negative reviews of this cake. It was excellent: well-structured, moist, creamy, and incredibly simple and quick to make. I followed the recipe exactly as written and had no issues. The salt level was just right — not too much at all. I suspect those who found it too salty were using a different kind of salt; if you’re not using Diamond Crystal, either get some (it’s worth it!) or adjust accordingly. The cake baked perfectly in 25 minutes. I’ll definitely be making t

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: What do we think about adding apricot jam to the glaze for a bigger apricot punch?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: A pleasant, homey and very moist sponge cake with delicate flavors, reminiscent of tres leches. I served it with sliced strawberries and tart lemon curd for acid, rather than pickled apricots. The recipe might benefit from a hint of vanilla or more pronounced fruit flavor but as written it’s a sweet, comforting dessert. I am not too frequent baker so I followed the recipe to a tee and found it to be straightforward and satisfying, with the right amount of (Diamond Crystal) kosher salt.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made it as written, minus the pickled apricots, because apart from that we had everything in the house. Everyone loved it, will definitely repeat.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this recipe as written, but found that my sauce never really sank into the cake, which was disappointing. I was expecting something akin to tres leches and wound up with more of a very thick, kind of unappealing glaze. I do think this dish benefits from the pickled apricots, as the apricot flavor is otherwise undetectable.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Absolutely delicious, I wouldn't change a thing!  Easy too and definitely serve warm.  I served it with vanilla ice cream but it really didn't need anything with it.   I did not halve the salt and it didn't seem too salty to anyone.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wonderful recipe!  Thank you!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I didn't like the doming I got from the original recipe, so I made several changes: Lower baking temp to 325, bring eggs and milk to room temp, whisk eggs and salt and let sit before adding milk and sugar, whisk instead of beating, use a bain Marie, bake until a thermometer reads 190-200. My malva pudding is nice and flat with those changes. 

I also made slight ingredient changes: I added 1/8 t pepper to batter and 1/2 t vanilla and 2 T golden syrup to glaze, which increases complexity.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious and so quick to make. However, I did make a couple of changes: strawberry jam (only type I had), coconut cream to replace milk/heavy cream. I'll definitely make this again - and likely double the recipe because I ended up sending most of it to my husband's office. :-)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I have 2 apricot trees that provide an abundance of fruit so I am always looking for new recipes. Must I use dehydrated apricots when pickling? Could I use sliced, fresh apricots?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I'd use apricot jam!
Seems very Irish. My mother always used apricot jam on her cakes, a simple addition would've been a tin of condensed milk & butter.  Lots of Irish were in South Africa fighting (on both sides) during the various wars, afterwards missionaries, brides sent out after WWI. 
Beautiful country, well worth a visit.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can I make this in a 9x5 loaf pan? If so, are any recipe or baking changes required? Thanks!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: How can this be made gluten free?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Bea S.—You could try substituting gluten free flour for the flour in the recipe and reporting back.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Mimi We made this with Measure For Measure gluten free flour, and it was phenomenal. My whole family raved about it. I'm positive Cup4Cup would work just as well.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I was really skeptical as I was making this and…it was heavenly. I do agree it was on the saltier side but I personally liked that. Next time I think I’ll experiment with putting a little apricot jam in the soak—I wanted to taste the apricot more, and I didn’t have pickled apricots to serve it with.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I find using a fork instead of the toothpick helps speed up the process!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this almost exactly as the recipe stated. some of you may be happy to hear that making it with half-and-half as I did rather than full fat cream was still incredibly good , but a little bit lower in fat.  I did increase the recipe by one and a half times  so I could make it in a 13 x 9 Pyrex pan. it was still done in 22 minutes. Since I wasn't planning to serve it with vanilla gelato or anything else I decided to add 1 teaspoon of vanilla to the sauce. it was good if a bit basic, and a little too sweet (which I know is part of its character). So I'm going to make it again probably with a little less sugar in both halves of the recipe and with maybe some lemon or orange peel and I may even throw some freeze dried raspberries into the sauce.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It's been over an hour, and I still have glaze pooled on the top corners. I'm thinking pour on half the glaze, then once it's soaked in, add more in stages.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this cake twice and both times the cake sunk around the edges. I wonder if the flour amount is correct. I’m using the metric instructions (I live in England).  I used 125 grams of plain flour.  There was basically no body to the batter. I also cooked it at 180c. Besides the sinking issue, the cake is delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I serve mine with the traditional South African pourable custard. I asked all the guest house cooks for their custard recipe. They sheepishly brought out the container of powdered custard.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Bird's (Custard Powder) for the win!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made it for a dinner party this weekend & everyone loved it. The flavors are delicate and unusual in some ways - the pickled apricots almost have a floral note - and it was heavenly drenched in the glaze. Baked it for 30 minutes and had no problem with the middle sagging. Served it with the gelato and the pickled apricots which definitely made the dish more sophisticated. A super easy dessert. Drizzled a bit of the picking liquid on the gelato bc it kind of thickened up almost like a syrup. Mmm!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Traditional Malva pudding relies on the reaction between baking soda and vinegar as its leavening agent, and does not typically include baking powder

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is delicious, but it creates a dome instead of a nice flat pudding like shown in the photo. To help prevent doming: Use a water bath, bake at 325 for longer, and preheat FULLY.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This pudding is a really high concentration of pure sugar. Would love to see an adaptation that is less sweet.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I found this to be very bland. Needed lots more flavor for me.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can the cake be baked and allowed to cool for 6 hours , after which the glaze is added, and the cake is then warmed up in the oven?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Maggie Pepler invented this pudding. She was a family friend. A legend.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Soooo good. I think I added slightly more jam and cut the sugar in the cake and the glaze to 100 grams each. Perfect warm and as is (no ice cream needed)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was delicious and the anti-salt brigade needs to cool it. The balance of sweet and salty is perfect. Highly recommend!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: A little scary to make these with no comments at the time, but they came out well. I didn't have whole milk so used 1 cup oat and 1/4 cup heavy cream. I added cinnamon and used more cardamom than called for. Batter is very liquidy, but they cook well and stay moist even when cooking in tin. I have eaten five.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: The way to avoid a thin batter and chips that sink to the bottom is to let the oats sit in the milk for 15 minutes. The oats expand and soften, yielding a thick, oatmeal-like mixture. You then combine this with the wet and dry ingredients, resulting in a tender, fluffy, slightly nubby muffin with wonderful cardamom aroma. My muffins needed only 15 minutes in the oven; don't overbake or they'll be dry.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: @Lauren that is so helpful, thanks!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody:
@Lauren Thanks for the tip! Wonderful texture.

Note: Red Mill bean-based GF flour + xanthum gum subs great, but needs less baking time.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: Made this recipe today and they were pretty good. I did not have any cardamom, so subbed a bit of cinnamon, nutmeg and vanilla.  The oats give a wholesome texture but I think some chopped nuts would have given a nice contrast. I liked the tip of using the 1/3 cup measure to scoop the batter - came out exact! They don’t rise a lot, so it’s okay that the tins are quite full. The batter is quite liquid, so don’t be alarmed about that!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody:
Very enjoyable! The large amount of oats in this recipe gives these muffins a satisfying bite.

I was looking for a quick breakfast bake recipe and tried them this morning. They come together in less time than it takes for the oven to reach 400F.

I shall be making them again.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: Just came out of the oven and look great. I didn’t have paper liners so buttered and floured. I used mini morsels and added a bit of flour to them before adding to batter. Not sure if it helped but there seems to be many morsels on the tops of the muffins. Can’t wait till they cool!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody:
Part 2:  Chips were well distributed and muffins had a nice rise. If you like cardamom the recipe needs more. I think I would prefer vanilla and cinnamon…..and nuts! The consistency is somewhat chewy. 
These muffins are okay, but do not think I will make again.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: The oats gave these a nice texture. The flavor was good as well. My muffins stuck a bit to the paper liner so I would oil the liners next time. Quick muffin for a weekday morning!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe! I swapped that cardamom for a 1/4 tsp of vanilla. Texture is great. Not overly sweet. Will be make these again!!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody:
This recipe's biggest "plus" is the cardamon! The resulting flavor is very interesting and good!

The biggest issue with this recipe has already been noted by several others here--the batter is too thin to support the weight of the chocolate chips so they all sink to the bottom! I attempted to alleviate this problem by letting the batter sit around awhile to thicken and by using mini chocolate chips. Neither helped! Maybe less milk next time....

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: These are very good. Next time: a little more cardamom and some almond extract. I buttered and floured the tin instead of using paper liners, and, because I didn't have enough chocolate chips, used frozen wild blueberries instead. (I poured a bit of batter into the tin, then some still-frozen berries, then more batter, more berries. That way, the blueberries don't sink or turn the batter gray.)

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody:
GF modifications: 
- added 1/8 t xantham gum
- subbed in gf flour from Anson Mills (45% brown rice, 45% oats, 10% sesame) (by weight)
- also subbed 1/2 t cinnamon for cardamom and coconut sugar (by weight) for brown sugar because that's how I roll.

They were done at exactly 20 minutes in my oven.
They were really good and I will be making them again!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: These stuck to the paper so badly that 1/3 was lost. The chips were all at the bottom. This recipe just did not work for me. Luckily, all the other NYT recipes I’ve used were perfect.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: They were done in 15 minutes

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody:
Typically, I cringe when someone makes significant changes to the provided recipe, I’m a follow the recipe guy. But, I was craving a choc chip muffin & decided to make these on the spot. I was missing oats from my pantry but had an ample supply of corn meal, so, after a quick substitution search, found that corn meal is a valid sub for oats, just use a lesser amount & make sure cooking time is at least twenty minutes. So, I subbed  cornmeal for oats & used 1/2 the amount (& ultimately ended up baking for 25 mins at 400); these muffins came out superb! Crispy exterior, sumptuous interior, and, just the right amount of body to make an ideal breakfast muffin.
If you have cornmeal hanging around your pantry, try this sub, take it from a reformed no-substituter!!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe closely but the chocolate chips sank to the bottom of the muffins. Not ideal.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: @Jonathan Same here! The muffins were tasty but the batter was too thin to hold the chocolate chips.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: Can’t believe nobody has mentioned that without a little oil in the muffin paper, they end up sticking to the paper and can’t be removed!  Need something to make them slide out easily.  Now I’m eating paper.  Don’t like the way they tasted either, way too much chocolate.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: Sugar doesn’t need to be decreased. Could use cinnamon instead of cardamom. Add some vanilla? Maybe some raisins? Very quick to make.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: I made these muffins exactly as directed and did not have any problem with the chips (Guittard) sinking to the bottom. But although they were evenly distributed throughout, I was underwhelmed with the final result. A whole tbs. of baking powder did not suffice to make the muffins light and fluffy; nor did the tiny addition of cardamon prove to be a game-changer. What some would call a "nubbly" texture, others might identify as "leaden."

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: I found this recipe to be pretty easy to throw together, but I cut the sugar in half, using coconut sugar instead. I also use for the flour a mixture of  sprouted red wheat combined with Kamut wheat flour. I also cut the amount of chocolate chips in half substituting with good quality, dark chocolate, chopped. they baked well in about 18 minutes. Very nice and chewy in texture and not at all sticky and plenty sweet. Much more wholesome as a result.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: I doubled the recipe and used cinnamon and vanilla instead of the cardamom. I soaked the quick oats in milk while assembling the other ingredients. I had no issue with the chocolate chips not staying in place. I cooked for 20 minutes (I live in Denver, so elevation can impact the cooking times.) These were so good that I'm currently making a second batch. No issues with dryness at all. I have the feeling that's due to making sure they didn't over cook.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: Used half whole wheat flour as it was all I had and an extra TBS of butter and a little vanilla extract . They were delish

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: These were tasty and filling! I read the comments re: thin batter and used 1 cup milk total instead of 1.25. Tossed the chocolate chips in flour to prevent sinking. These only took 15 minutes to cook for me, not 20. The cardamom adds the slightest hint of ginger/nuttiness. Will make again!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: Made as written with old fashioned oats (pulsed for a few minutes as per tip). I also soaked oats in milk as suggested in comments and chocolate chips didn’t sink to bottom. These came together quickly and very a big hit with the kids. I’d also recommend not subbing something else for the cardamom as it adds a very unique flavor

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: Lots of great variations in the comments. I made these as a hybrid with the 5-star “Banana Bread” also in NYT- basically made the banana bread batter but added the oats and milk in the same ratios from this recipe, included the choc chips of course, and used the same temperature/baking time from here. Delicious and highly recommended!!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: I let the oats soak in milk for 15 min and the chips didn't sink once baked. I mixed all the wet ingredients in that bowl and followed the rest of the instructions as written. The batter was on the thin side but fit all 12 muffin wells and baked up to a decent height. I gave it 4/5 because the flavor seemed bland. I am also one who doesn't like too much chocolate in a oatmeal/chocolate or even chocolate chip cookie, so if you are like me, reduce the amount of chips.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: These came out beautifully for me and were delicious! (In my oven they only needed 17-18 min so check early) I used mini chocolate chips (smaller amount than called for) and didn’t have any issue with them sinking. If you like a less sweet muffin, I cut the brown sugar by half and still found them plenty sweet (was also able to sub half whole wheat flour and use 1% milk with no ill effects). The cardamom flavor was just right to me - definitely noticeable without being overwhelming.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: Thanks go other posters for alerting to too-wet batter! I cut the liquid to 1 cup (reducing by 1/4 cup) and the batter was the perfect texture. The chocolate chips (I used mini CC) were evenly distributed, not sinking. I doubled the cardamom and it still wasn't enough for my taste. Would double it again to 1 tsp and/or add additional spices (nutmeg, cinnamon, mace). Overall, with my mods, a solid recipe!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: I also shared some of the trepidation (see other comments) -- the batter was thin, so I added a bit more oats and flour until I got a consistency I felt more comfortable with. The cardamom really makes these special and I encourage people to use the spice. While these are nice muffins, and allow for nuts and raisins as substitutions for chocolate chips, I really prefer zucchini chocolate chip muffins.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: Meh, despite the enormous quantity of butter this stuck to the liners. They were greasy and bland IMO

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: These have only four tablespoons of butter, hardly enough to constitute an "enormous" quantity or render the muffins greasy. Perhaps you misread the recipe?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: Four tablespoons of butter for 12 muffins equates to 1 teaspoon of butter per muffin. I would hardly consider 1 teaspoon of butter per muffin "enormous!"

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: Wondering what if any proportion of oat flour could be subbed for any of the wheat flour to make them even oatier?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: Made as written except subbed dried cherries for chocolate chips.  The batter is super thin and I was doubtful they would come out, but they cooked into lovely, dense chewy muffins.  Not gummy at all.  The dried fruit did not sink to the bottom so that worked well.  Used silicone muffin cups so they did not stick and popped right out.  I will make these again but plan to significantly increase the spices.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody:
A really lovely breakfast treat that also kept us full until lunch (two muffins each and a small bowl of strawberries)!

I made some small changes: I used whole wheat instead of AP flour. I didn’t want to wash my blender so simply soaked regular rolled oats in the milk in a big measuring cup as I assembled the rest of the ingredients. And slightly increased the milk to compensate. 

Great as-is but might top with a sprinkle of flax, almonds, walnuts, pepitas in the future!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: I roughly chopped the chocolate chips (I also used up some white chocolate chips in the fridge). I think this helped them mix better and not sink to the bottom as much as other people mentioned. The muffins did stick to the wrapper, though. Overall good!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: I added the chocolate chips after pouring the batter into the muffin tray to keep them from sinking to the bottom — and it worked!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: My kids loved these. I subbed whole wheat flour and also had to use skim milk (although I did have a little less than a 1/4 cu of cream, so I added that).

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe that takes modifications beautifully! I used olive oil in place of butter; half cup brown sugar; one cup chocolate chips; half cup chopped pecans; and GF flour!! I would increase the cardamom (it's the perfect way to bring out chocolate flavor), maybe to a third tsp. Rounded tops, lightly crunchy fresh from the oven, hold together beautifully (not always the case using GF flour in muffin recipes). All in all, KA has done it again! Thanks for this stunning breakfast staple!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: I only buy regular rolled oats, will those work?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: I really enjoyed this recipe, because we normally make the ready-to-prepare mix from Quaker.   My mods: I used slightly less sugar -- partly because that's all we had, and partly because I would like to use less sugar if possible.  I also used half and half whole-wheat to white flour.  And we don't eat eggs, so I removed the eggs and simply added an extra tablespoon of baking powder.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: These turned out wonderfully! I made them gluten free: Swapped out all purpose flour for half almond flour, half GF multipurpose flour, and added less than 1/8 teaspoon of xantham gum. Used rolled oats (not quick cook) and soaked in milk for 20 minutes, as recommended by @Lauren. Added vanilla extract and a smidge of nutmeg along with cardamom. Will make again! My kids loved them. Somehow I ended up with 11 muffins not 12.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: Batter is definitely runny, but they cook well and make great little snacks. I added some vanilla in as well.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody:
These were so good! I agree with the commenters who suggest doing something to prevent the chips from sinking to the bottoms—either letting the oats sit in the milk or covering the chips with a dusting of flour.  

I subbed butterscotch chips for chocolate because they were what I had on hand, and they all sunk to the bottom of the very thin batter. However, this was amazing with butterscotch, as they made for caramelized-butterscotch bottoms for the muffins.  Seriously, so good. But I can see that with chocolate chips, it would be best if they didn’t all sink.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Best Oatmeal Chocolate Chip Muffins Recipe
commentBody: Cut 1/3 of the sugar out (so used 2/3 of what the recipe calls for) and took advice from one of the comments to let oatmeal soak first, and then bake for 15 min only. Recipe still works — sweet enough from the chocolate chips but the muffin itself is not too sweet! Very nice!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I'm going to be That New Englander and ask if maple syrup will work in place of the honey.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @jess bee I actually tried it since we didn't have honey and it was delicious :)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Jess Bee or that Canadian!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Human Being ha ha was just going to say that!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
A couple of additional tips. When cooking pancakes, flip once and only once. Once flipped, do not press them with the spatula. Multiple flips and pressing leads to tough pancakes.

When to flip? I flip once the tops stop bubbling. At that point they will be nice and brown.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Panhead who presses pancakes?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Marjorie. My husband did it for years! I couldn’t believe it either. It took a while to teach him otherwise.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Marjorie, haha, I do 🙋🏻‍♀️😂! I don’t even know why. There’s something very satisfying about it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used old fashioned oats and pulsed them as suggested and used melted butter in place of oil. I also halved the recipe as I was only cooking for one. These were delicious! I don't like eggy pancakes but find pancakes without egg missing something. You couldn't even notice that egg wasn't present. i will definitely make these again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Celine me too, and as a vegan it was annoying! I’ve started adding the smallest pinch of black salt to my pancakes and it is so satisfying.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Celine I always use butter in my pancakes, better tasting, and the edges crisp up a bit.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I have a long-standing recipe from Good Housekeeping that uses regular oats and lets them soak in the buttermilk for 15 minutes or so before putting the ingredients together.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Marc Kagan Also, a quarter cup of wheat germ.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Marc Kagan could you share this recipe?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Jecz - I use thick cut oats and soak in oatmeal a few hours or overnight - then add everything else in before cooking. Depends how much time I have at each given point. 

Looking forward to trying this version (org recipe)

I don’t know how to attribute the recipe as it scratched on the back of an envelope from my mom. Please see below, it very forgiving so I use my hand to measure small ingredients. 

2c old fashioned oats (I prefer thick cut)
1/2c all-purpose flour
3T sugar
1t baking soda
1t baking powder
1/2t salt
2.5c buttermilk
2 L eggs
1/4c melted, unsalted butter
1t vanilla

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This recipe, tweaked with the substitution of flaxmeal for eggs, is the one we call Grandpa Ed's Oatmeal Pancakes, the childhood pancakes of my husband's Bay Area 1950s family, straight out of the red & white checkered Better Homes looseleaf cookbook that was the other great American cookbook of its day (Joy of Cooking was in our CT kitchen). Nice to see it updated for flax (and a little honey). I usually make these with whole wheat flour, or white/whole wheat and they are still delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Yes. I have used and love the Oatmeal Pancake recipe from the Betty Crocker cookbook. My edition is from 1956 (inherited from a grandmother) , cherry pie, a baked turkey and other foods on the cover. My tweak is to add cinnamon and diced apples (with peels). I'll make the same change when I try this recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Sally. Still one of my favorite cookbooks!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Good to know.  Unfortunately, Oatmeal Pancakes must have been removed from our late-60s edition of the BHG New Cookbook.  Fortunately, the "Corn-meal Griddle Cakes" recipe makes up for the loss. Most pancake lovers will make these a family favorite, as they are in our family.  Caveat:  the cornmeal pancakes are a moist style with a crisp shell.  If you prefer a pancake that has the interior texture of a biscuit, they may disappoint.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I usually eat Quaker Old Fashioned oats that cook in 5 minutes.  Will these work?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Ken K soak old fashioned oats overnight in the buttermilk. You might need a bit more buttermilk than the recipe calls for

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Ken K You can pulse them a couple of times in a food processor if you don’t want to plan ahead for an overnight soak.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Ken K
I only use old-fashioned, I cover them with just enough water and zap them in the microwave for two minutes before I start doing anything else and by the time I need them they’ve cooled down enough to add to the mix .

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Would this recipe work in a waffle maker?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Frank
I haven’t tried it with this recipe, but my theory is that you can adapt any pancake recipe to a waffle iron with the addition of a little butter or neutral flavored oil.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @carole Agree. Should defin work in a waffle maker.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These sounded really good in theory. In practice, they tasted somewhere between a bowl of oatmeal and an underdone pancake.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Rachel hah, yes! It tasted like bready pancake. I wanted so much to like them

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Rachel same! Really strange recipe

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: For those who don’t usually have buttermilk at hand,it is available as a powder near dry milk or baking supplies.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Susan4 I keep this on hand and it is a life saver. But keep it in the fridge or airtight container after opening if you live in a damp climate like Florida. It goes rock hard quickly if not sealed well.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Tip on canned buttermilk powder I have this as well. Yep. A lifesaver.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Susan4 I didn’t have buttermilk on hand, so used plain goat milk kefir. It worked well and the pancakes tasted amazing!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I never have buttermilk on hand and don't see the need. I used an unsweetened nut milk blend, and GF all purpose flour.   I added a handful of bluerries  but didn't add any honey to the batter. Delicious and healthy!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Patty Y   A different recipe, in other words. But sounds good.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Patty Y - I rarely have buttermilk on hand, and simply use the substitute of 1 cup of milk & either 1 tablespoon of white vinegar or lemon juice, which you allow to sit for 5-10 minutes then stir and add to your recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was meh. King Arthur flour has a fantastic whole grain pancake mix recipe using oatmeal and wheat flour that I keep in a ziploc bag in the fridge. Lasts forever, one scoop, an egg, buttermilk and a short soak on the counter and it blows the socks off this recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I really want to try these. I'll probably make them for myself as an experiment, and try using plain yogurt with a splash of vinegar to replicate the buttermilk tartness. 

But I'll also be ready to put on lots of good maple syrup to make up for any deficiencies in the final pancakes - plus maybe some premium strawberry jam!!!! (LOL)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If I’m dairy free, do you think I could substitute oat milk or something like it for the buttermilk?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Kat I do it all the time--just a tablespoon of apple cider vinegar or white vinegar to your oat milk and let it sit for 10 minutes to make it a buttermilk substitute!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Kat. In my experience it will definitely not be the same. There is a chemical reaction between the buttermilk and baking soda which results in a lighter and fluffier texture causing the pancake to rise more and the buttermilk results in a different taste profile. If you substitute for the buttermilk I would add one teaspoon of baking powder also. (I have not tried this recipe yet)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Kat, I was going to try it with some cashew or coconut yogurt. 😄 🌱

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’ve been adding oatmeal to my pancakes for years, I don’t use flax or honey but do use corn flour along with wheat flour. And I cut my milk with equal amounts of plain yogurt and milk.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I subbed quick oats for at least half of the flour in my overnight sponge for sourdough pancakes on an impulse last night.  Was delighting in the last  bite of excellent pancakes this morning as I read this piece.   Made breakfast even sweeter.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @lazy baker Recipe? Do you use the King Arthur recipe for sourdough pancakes?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Absolutely a keeper - so so good. I think this is one of those recipes that you have to pay attention to the details and cook precisely as written. I ended up with 7 slightly crispy flavorful pancakes with beautiful texture and scent. My husband loved them as well. We ate them with berries and maple syrup. Yum! I can imagine that if you over stir or don’t use butter generously or have the griddle too hot that you will have all manner of disasters in texture and flavor. My recommendation is don’t use powdered buttermilk or a different kind of oats or something else in place of flax… or any other of the myriad variations NYTs readers love to indulge in and you will have a delicious easy to cook pancake to add to your morning rotation. One recommendation: they take quite a bit longer to cook than a regular pancake - a full 5 minutes. So, cook them all at once if you are able to or you will spend 35 minutes in front of the stove like I did :D

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @NLF I have now made these pancakes twice and each time I felt that the bottoms were browning too quickly, way before it was time to flip. Anticipating that that might happen again I decreased the heat under my black skillet and they still browned too quickly? Maybe I’m trying to cook them like wheat flour pancakes?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Flax seed and oats are gluten free. I will try this recipe with gluten free flour mix.  As flour is a smaller percentage than in most pancake recipes, I think this will be fabulous.  Sure am tempted to add and egg for loft.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made these for breakfast this morning and served with Vermont maple syrup -- delicious. And fun to make, including watching the baking soda fully dissolve -- bubbling up and transforming the batter! Next time, I will serve with a layer of orange marmalade topped with sliced bananas and strawberries -- and topped with Vermont maple syrup.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made my own buttermilk (1 cup milk/1 T lemon juice) and presoaked the oats in it, then gave it a good hour with all ingredients. It needed another 1/4 cup flour on top of the suggested 2/3 cup. With the heat on low-medium and using butter, these pancakes cooked much like any other pancakes - and I found them delicious. Added vanilla to the second batch and found that also delicious. I'll be making these often. This recipe's a winner.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
These were good, but not outstanding. I liked that they were egg-free. 

One tip- some stores sell powdered buttermilk, which I like to keep around for recipes like this. It works beautifully and lasts forever.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Professional chef here - don’t waste your time or money making these - much better oat pancake recipes elsewhere.
These are flavorless and flat. If you do make them do yourself a favor and add some spices at the very least.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Bijipop and can you please give us the links?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Flavor was okay but the interior remained soft and gummy. They took a long time to firm up enough to flip, while the exterior got quite brown. Nice concept but there are better recipes for oatmeal pancakes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This recipe also works well for a waffle maker. (I like to stir in some frozen blueberries first, pancakes or waffles). Just be sure to grease the waffle irons well, even if they're non-stick. And maple syrup will work just the same as honey.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: In the early 80's our kindergarten classes "cooked" each Friday (science module and introducing children to new foods).  For "F" week, we made Madalyn Brogan's Friendly Frog's Fantastic Flapjacks.  Very similar, but I think a tad healthier: 1 1/8 cup buttermilk, 1 cup rolled oats, 2 T oil, 2 eggs, beaten, 1/2 cup wheat flour, 1 tsp baking soda.  YES, you may use milk soured with vinegar.  YES, you may sub 4 egg whites for 2 whole eggs. YES, you may add blueberries, or your desired fruit.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
In place of the 395 grams of buttermilk, I used 305g of whey leftover from making Greek yogurt.  The pancakes were delicious and I’ll definitely be making them again.

FYI- Whey is thinner than buttermilk, so the King Arthur Flour website recommends using 20 - 25% less when substituting. For this recipe, that’s 300 - 316g of whey.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I always have quick cooking, steel cut oats on hand, do you think these will work?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
This looks like a great recipe.  It caught my eye because it referenced a "secret ingredient." 
I find pancake mixes and restaurant pancakes severely lacking in flavor. Most resembling something like cardboard.  My secret ingredient is VANILLA and a scratch recipe (most are quite easy).  1 teaspoon of vanilla per batch of any kind of pancake or waffles really makes the flavor pop.  I look forward to trying this recipe and adding vanilla.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Needed waffles & used this recipe in a Breville waffle maker (non-stick surfaces). Added 1 t cornstarch with the baking soda for some heft & lift; subbed toasted walnut oil for omega 3's and taste. Just fantastic, lovely browned result and completely done inside.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: You say Let sit for at least 10 minutes and up to an hour. The longer the mixture sits, the creamier and more tender the pancakes. But can you do the night before and store in fridge overnight?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Good recipe but somewhat bland.  May try adding vanilla (?) and serving with a fruit sauce.  I used whole wheat pastry flour to improve nutrtional profile without adding density. It worked well but needed to add an additonal 1/2 cup because batter was thin.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Denise. Seems like the changes you made in the ingredients (pastry flour?) may have affected the outcome.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Denise try adding a little cardamom and cinnamon. Also I ate these with maple syrup.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Looks good but I think I will stick with Staffordshire Oatcakes, an English speciality from Stoke-on-Trent that kept my student body and soul together over 50 years ago, and has been a family favourite comfort-food ever since. They are fine oat-meal based crepes/pancakes and go excellently with fried eggs, bacon, mushrooms and/or tomatoes, and black pudding if you can get it; tough in Alexandria when I was posted to Washington.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Made these this morning with substitutions: I had no more ground flax so used 2 eggs as advised, melted butter instead of oil, and long-cook rolled oats that I soaked overnight in the buttermilk.   Results were still excellent: a wonderful roasted oat taste in a pleasingly textured pancake -- certainly not gummy as some experienced.
Tips:  Use lower heat to accommodate the longer cook time of 2-3 minutes: 325 on an electric skillet. Don't flip until the tops are covered with tiny gas bubbles.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If anyone makes these on a griddle, I recommend heating it to 325 instead of the standard 350 so the honey in the batter doesn’t burn. Excellent pancakes!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: A keep a bag of buttermilk powder to make buttermilk for these occasions. I know you can make your own with milk and acid but this has been a gamechanger in our house.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Icelandic pancakes. Served to observe the beginning of spring. 
We use steel cut oats, small but nicely chewy. In Iceland, they would sometimes serve them as a savory entree, with smoked fish and an herbed topping.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made these with the eggs and instead of buttermilk used yogurt. Also added frozen blueberries to the batter. Best pancakes we’ve ever had. They also freeze and then microwave well.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
These pancakes are thick and hearty.

The second time I made them I tried to thin them a bit with more buttermilk. DON'T DO IT!!!

The result was really nice porridge. As pancakes they were completely un-flipable. Even after adding an egg, not flippable.

Good porridge, though.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Caveat:  the ratio of volume to weight  of regular  oatmeal vs instant oatmeal is significantly  different.  2/3 cup of regular oatmeal weighs only about 55 gm, so you need almost a cup of regular oatmeal  to achieve 75 gm.  I found one egg sufficient and didn't  add honey or sugar, maple  syrup on the finis pancake  giving enough sweetness.  The pancake has more texture  than made with flour alone.   I used whole wheat flour.  Chopped apple also good for texture  and flavor.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Thank you for an eggless pancake recipe. I used chia egg instead of flax. Two tab rested for 10 mins in 3 tabs of water. Added to batter and rested for 10 more mins. Very fluffy and tasty.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: We thought these pancakes were good but not great, unless of course, the price of eggs makes them totally unaffordable at some point in the future! I've got a couple gluten-free oatmeal pancake recipes in which the oat taste comes through more, and both of those produce a better texture (since they use eggs). So, these were interesting but probably not going to get re-run in our household....

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: When we got married 45 years ago we had a "Recipes for A Small Planet" and loved the oatmeal pancakes.  Now I sub out milk & use almond milk/nonfat greek yogurt/1 tbs vinegar mix.  Also use this milk sub in cornbread.  Except for the vinegar measure I just mix up enough almond milk and yogurt to get the volume I need at a thickness that looks right.  Other than that it does not matter the ratio.  Easier than keeping buttermilk on hand. I did try instant dry buttermilk. Yuck.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Terrific! Blowing your minds by saying that I made it exactly as written, except it rested for about 30 minutes instead of 10 while I was waiting for sleepyheads. Genevieve’s description is correct: it tastes like porridge and looks like pancakes. As such, it could be fun to drop some raisins into the batter.
Our family really enjoyed the change-up.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It's important to let it rest so that the oats can soak-up the liquid. I used Bob Red Mill Gluten Free Protein Oats and let it sit for 30 minutes, which was not long enough. The flavor was great, but they stuck to my cast-iron pan and wrinkled. I will give it another try, but the first attempt was a was a flop.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I love these bad boys. I've made them with ground oats as well as quick cooking and they both taste great. I've also subbed GF flour once and they were great! I use kefir as my buttermilk and serve them with stewed fruit and maple syrup. I might be deluding myself but they feel wholesome and delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: What does "crackly with butter" mean?  Butter doesn't crackle.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Hi Todd! "Crackly with butter" means that the outsides of the pancakes become quite crackly because they're cooked in lots of butter, which you can taste in that crackly shell. Be sure to use a generous amount of salted butter when cooking -- a lot of the pancakes great flavor and texture come from that step!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is similar to an oatmeal pancake I have made for years.  If you only have old fashioned oats, mix them up the night before and let them sit in the fridge until ready to use instead of grinding to smaller pieces.  May need to increase the buttermilk as these oats can soak up a lot! My recipe used an even amount of buttermilk to oats and by the next morning all the buttermilk was soaked up.  Enjoy!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: i replaced milk with clean soy milk, and flour with amaranth flour .. didn’t add honey yet added ceylan cinnamon and pumpkin protein .. and some nutmeg .. gluten free oats also. and on top i added tahini and glazed blueberries … divine!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: So, it's a completely different recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is so great!!! Legit!!! My go-to!!! My biggest change is that I get a 7-grain blend from my Amish neighbor; I pulse it into flour and use THAT... It serves for both "oats, flax, and flour." (all included in 7-grain blend...!) but proportions are right: natural sweetener, baking powder for lift. Freaking delicious!!! Reminiscent of IHOP's multigrain pancake (that's not the name, but they have one!!! These really remind me of it!!!). Anyway, it's absolutely lovely! Thanks for posting!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious. For gluten and other restrictions, I used gluten free flour,sprouted rolled oats, avocado oil, maple syrup, unsalted butter and oatmilk soured with vinegar. My husband and I finished off the 12 pancakes with mixed berries on top. They tasted just as Genevieve stated a buttery crackly outside with creamy oatmeal inside. You could probably refrigerate step 1 overnight.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: has anyone made these sheet-pan style?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Made it exactly as per recipe and loved it. A few notes:
- gently adding flour until JUST mixed in is important so as not to knock out the air that makes it fluffy.
- the batter is pretty thick, getting 10-12 small pancakes is optimistic, but possible if you use a spoon or scoop. More like 6 palm-sized pancakes using a spatula or small ladle.
- consider under-cooking it a little bit. It doesn't contain egg so that won't cause harm, but fully cooking through can make it quite dry.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I always love reading this New York Times recipes partly because there's such a wide range of readers and users of the recipes. It varies from people who apparently have tons of experience with cooking and substituting ingredients and throwing ingredients in without even measuring them. I am one of those people.  You can imagine how I smile when I see people saying: can I substitute 9.2 g of milk instead of 9 g?   Hahahaha  (slight exaggeration on my part here 🤪).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Also i keep the Saco brand powdered buttermilk on hand.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My ground flax was expired so I substituted with eggs. Batter was very runny and took forever to cook.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
The second time I made these, they were a revelation! The salty butter makes all the difference- make sure they get crispy and crackly and don’t skimp on the butter!  Definitely not low-calorie, but definitely delicious!

The first time they were a little soft and I realized I had just used enough butter in the pan to keep them from sticking. They really need to sizzle and crackle, and get a nice crunchy buttery edge.  
With maple syrup- well, if this were my last meal, I’d die happy!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I love the ingredients!  However, I found the batter became difficult to manage - very sticky and not cohesive - on a hot frying pan coated with Canola Oil. Tips please ! :)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made for an easy Christmas breakfast by subbing cheap grocery store eggnog for the buttermilk and omitting honey. Served with lingonberry jam. Yum!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I soak old-fashioned rolled oats overnight instead of using quick oats for more texture and flavor. i add the ground flaxseed and other ingredients at that time as well as any fruit I may desire. The next day this can be cooked for oatmeal or thinned a bit for pancakes or a little more for waffles.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: We’ve been using recipe for many years after first seeing a version of it in Gourmet magazine. It works ever better if you add berries or other fruits directly into the batter.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Even better if you make batter the night before and / or make the batter early in the week and use it up over the week

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @AA good to know… I wanted to make the batter tonight, but wasn't sure if it would be sitting for too long.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Are you kidding me?!  Easily one of the best recipes I've ever tried from NYT, despite, or maybe because of its simplicity.  These pancakes are a complete homerun, and I couldn't believe the combination of the slightly crispy exterior along with a cooked but almost custardy interior due to the oatmeal and flax.  I'm not a fan of people giving their adjustments to these recipes, but I did add one egg, just to experiment.  And I think the advice to let the mixture sit for a while is spot on.  Yum!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My new favorite kitchen implement is a Cuisinart spice (and nut) grinder--perfect for pulsing regular rolled oats. These were delicious (made w/ egg); no need to add honey to the batter! Served with plain Ellenos Greek yogurt (Seattle area treat that is supposedly now available "across the US"), thawed blackberries that were picked last summer, and maple syrup, of course.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Ellenos is super delish. They used to only sell in the Pike Place Market, and you could come with your own empty container to fill.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used 2/3 cups + 2 Tbs of Trader Joe's "organic oatmeal with ancient grains and seeds" instead of instant oats and ground flax. I soaked it in buttermilk for a couple of hours without pulsing. The result was amazing! Felt healthier than my usual buttermilk pancakes, yet delicious in a different way. I'm definitely making again. One tip, I cut down the salt by a third but it was still salty enough.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
This is my 2nd time making these. The first time, I followed the recipe as written except that I added some vanilla & cinnamon & used sprouted rolled oats/old fashioned oats and enjoyed it a lot which inspired me to make it a second time but with some changes! 
So this time, I used whole wheat flour in place of regular AP flour, I processed sprouted rolled oats because I don’t eat instant or quick cooking oats (I allowed the processed oats to soak in the buttermilk for an hour & 15 minutes), I used a good tbsp of monk fruit sweetener instead of other sweetener, I added 1 egg and cut the flax meal down to 1tbsp and I also added half tsp of baking powder. Vanilla & cinnamon to my liking. Not only did it look beautiful like regular pancakes, they were fluffy, tender & insanely delicious! This was a huge hit with my love ones and it works better for folks with diabetic & pre diabetic issues. I LOVE this version & will be making it many more times!! I’m sure regular AP flour with this version will be awesome too but I like whole wheat better for its higher fiber content!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’ve made this recipe twice in ten days, as I crave these pancakes. So soft and delicious with just a drizzle of honey. I didn’t have flax seeds so I used chia seeds for extra fiber. They freeze and reheat really well.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used ground up oats instead of all purpose flour to keep pancakes gluten free. Added one egg just for pep.  Everyone happy!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can you suggest a gluten-free flour alternative for the all-purpose flour?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Nelson. King Arthur’s gluten free would probably work.  I use it in some of my bread recipes. Give them a call - they are very helpful!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I didn't have buttermilk so I used whole milk yogurt. I added a couple of sliced up ripe bananas and reduced the honey to one tablespoon. absolutely delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Excellent. I used flax milk plus vinegar instead of buttermilk.  Maple syrup instead of honey. The batter was VERY runny, but I proceeded nonetheless. The pancakes took a long time to cook, even after they browned on the first side.  I let them go for awhile, then carefully flipped.  The second batch of four came out much better, as is often the case with pancakes.  Next time, I'll reduce the amount of liquid.  And perhaps use a non dairy yogurt instead of the flax/vinegar.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I tried this recipe today and was delighted with the outcome.  I opted for a shorter buttermilk soak time (10-15) minutes and followed the recipe precisely (also pulsing Quaker rolled oats).  The flavor was delicious and the texture was satisfying (with little bits of oat and flax).  Even my wife, who is not a big fan of pancakes enjoyed them.  I may try in the future with regular milk, but I believe the buttermilk lent flavor and texture to the pancakes that milk would not.

author: Genevieve Ko
Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I substituted in maple syrup for honey, and it worked out great! I used old fashioned oats (pulsed in my spice grinder a few times), and added a tablespoon of hemp hearts. Where the measurements were given by weight, I used that. The batter did come out a little runny, but it puffed up and browned nicely, though yes the edges did splatter a little when flipping. It made 7 pancakes slightly larger than my palm + 4 half-dollar sized (for my baby).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Alternatively, have you investigated Staffordshire Oatcakes? Very similar to pancakes, though made with finely milled oats and using yeast as the raising agent.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Another buttermilk substitution is to thin out plain yogurt with milk until it’s the texture of buttermilk.  I like nonfat Siggi’s or a Greek yogurt - and any type of milk works.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I followed the recipe the first time and it was good. Second time I was missing a couple of ingredients. I substituted whole milk Kefir for the buttermilk and omitted the baking soda (didn’t have it). They turned out better than the original. The pancakes were fluffier and a little sweeter. For both recipes I topped with bananas, blueberries and walnuts. No syrup. They’re sweet enough for me with just the fruit.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Just here to say that I don’t think this would work gluten free. I have yet to find a GF flour mix that works as a pancake except for Pamela’s which has flax seed and almond flour in it. If you are looking for a fantastic buttermilk pancake look for it in the store. I use a ratio of 1 cup Pamela’s 1 cup buttermilk and 1 egg. Adjust as needed.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Tara S Try millet flour. I have nice results with pancakes. Or King Arthur gluten free measure for measure flour.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I often make pancakes for my breakfast, but I generally use a buttermilk pancake mix. For a quarter cup of the mix, I add 1 TBSP flax meal and usually 1-2 TBSP oats. I like quick cooking steele-cut, but have used rolled oats. Lots of fibre and a nice "Chew". When I have them, blueberries are a good addition. Since I cook for just me, this makes two 5 in+ cakes and that is enough for me. I do indulge with real maple syrup.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’ve made these several times now and they’re so fluffy; my partner and I honestly prefer them to regular pancakes! The oats give them a warm, nutty flavor with the added benefit of a little bit of extra fiber. Definitely a big fan! Also, I’ve used regular old-fashioned oats instead of instant each time (without pulsing them) and it hasn’t been an issue; I just leave them to soak in the milk mixture for at least 30 minutes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: In New England, we can find Kate's brand of real farm #buttermilk.  When I shop at Market Basket,  I am sure to pick up several large bottles.  I find it does wonders for my gut.   Try it and you will find it superior to the imitators.  and fab in your oatmeal pancakes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If you use Gluten Free oats (and, yes, that's important if you are coeliac or gluten intolerant) this becomes a great GF breakfast!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Has anyone tried this gluten free? (GF oats and flour substitute)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Aren’t oats / oatmeal gluten free?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Margaret H. GF oats are the same thing as regular oats, it’s just the factory plonking them into packaging that’s different.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are Staffordshire oatcakes, well known in the English Midlands

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’m looking forward to making this recipe. Has anyone tried using gluten free flour?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
This is really runny, and I've let it sit an hour. 
You need to specify full fat buttermilk -- I used reconstituted and it doesn't work.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I was excited to try this recipe this morning because my husband hates oatmeal and I love it. 
I followed directions exactly and used oil, not butter to fry. They turned out fine for me but much too “gooey” and “difficult to swallow” said my unsuspecting partner. He wouldn’t finish them. So I made him some new ones from Aunt Jemima Pancake mix and he was happy. I’d give them a “4”, and he would give a “1” to the recipe. I don’t think I’ll be making them again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These sound delicious. I've made your genius flourless fudy brownies several times. They're always a hit! Plan on making the pancakes tomorrow AM. Seems like the perfect meal when you're housebound after a snowstorm!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Hey it's Recipes for A Small Planet!  Main difference, RASP uses whole wheat flour and egg instead of flax.  Since I almost never have buttermilk on hand I just use some combo (50/50?) of plain yogurt and almond milk with 1 tbsp of white vinegar substituted in the volume needed.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: GSince my partner was allergic to wheat four I have made oatmeal pancakes for a years but  always buzzed  up the oats in the blender.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can you make waffles with the same batter?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I was skeptical but these were just awesome!  I cut the recipe in half and added just a bit of cinnamon. I love walnuts w my oatmeal so I served them w chopped walnuts and some maple syrup on the side.  So good and didn’t make me feel too full afterwards like regular pancakes always do.  A half batch made 5 pancakes (2 generous Tbsp of batter each).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Recipe wise it's simple. 
PROBLEMS: Make sure you use a thick/thicker buttermilk.  A runnier buttermilk, such as can be found in Australia leads to problems.
Preferring less sweet, I omitted the honey step, opting to add maple syrup at point of eating rather than point of cooking.
Overall, it cooks, but I'm not a fan of this one.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Followed this recipe to a tee but the batter was too liquidy and everything fell apart. Disappointed because usually the NYT cooking recipes are spot on!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are fantastic. I was skeptical because I have a buttermilk pancake recipe that I've been using for decades. How could a pancake with oatmeal, flax seed, and no eggs be delicious? I shouldn't have worried. By all means use salted butter to grease the griddle, keep the pancakes on the smaller side, and let them brown fully on the bottom before turning, otherwise they may fall apart. Delicious and a healthier alternative to traditional pancakes! We enjoyed them with maple syrup.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I make a similar recipe from my mother's 1950 Betty Crocker cookbook.  It has more oats, much less flour and uses the 2 eggs (a few other small differences).  I'm 71 and have been eating them all my life.  Definitely a very satisfying comfort food!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Really nice with Lyle’s Golden Syrup instead of honey, oat milk + a tbsp of lemon juice for the buttermilk, and our homemade vegan butter. 
Will be making again!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this as written except soaked regular oats in oak milk overnight.  Added a little yogurt to counter the lack of buttermilk. They cooked beautifully and freeze nicely.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used oat bran (already fine, more protein and fiber) 1:1 by weight and these turned out great! Really fluffy, moist (oatmeal-like, as other reviewers pointed out), tender (careful when flipping), and flavorful. Have subbed whole grain flours like rye for the all-purpose and that adds to the nutty flavor.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This recipe resembles the one by Walter Willett in Eat, Drink, and Be Healthy. I modify it by using 1/3 cup oatmeal instead of millet and barley,  and 2T flax seeds, plus sunflower seeds 3T.  I use white whole wheat flower as a fiber source, and 2 eggs. More milk is added until it is a suitable consistency for the griddle. Marvelous.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Ken K I always chop thick, organic rolled oats by hand when making pancakes and use a microplane to grate toasted almonds, substituting this combo for one third of the flour.

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: While I enjoyed the flavor of these pancakes, they have a spongey texture that I’ve come to expect from pancake recipes that are mostly comprised of oatmeal instead of flour that I don’t find particularly pleasing. I don’t know that I would invest the time and effort to make them again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Disappointed.  Not fluffy.  Maybe my baking soda is old or powdered buttermilk doesn't work for this recipe.  Very thin pancakes and if you use a full 1/4 cup, they are quite hard to turn.  Added more flour for substance.  Will stick with other recipes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I have made other oatmeal pancakes, did not encounter the heaviness problem.  My recipes all included granulated sugar which, when it melts, leaves pockets of air that warm and help with rising.  Might make this adding the sugar.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If you like the breadiness of pancakes, just do regular pancakes. If you, like me, like the idea of pancakes more than the cloying sweetness and gastrointestinal discomfort that comes from actually eating them... this recipe is for you!

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko

commentBody: These pancakes tasted good.  However, the batter was incredibly soft/loose and the bottom cooked faster and tended to burn before the top was set.  This also made the pancakes extremely difficult to move around the pan and very messy to flip.  Will not make again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This recipe may taste good but eating these  spikes your blood sugar which can lead to unwanted weight gain and increases your risk of later getting (type 2, or non-genetic) diabetes. Quick cooking or instant oats, unlike whole oats or oat flakes, get digested too fast, spiking your blood sugar, as does plain white flour and honey. To your body, these pancakes are just like eating sugar. Use some healthier ingredients and enjoy!
