commentBody: Made them during snowmageddon here in Massachusetts to nourish the shovelers in the family.  Maple syrup, of course.  Good heavens I don't think any of us will stop craving them forever!!!!!!

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Author's tips: Even though the ingredients are pantry staples, they may not be what you stock. If you don’t have plain quick-cooking or instant oats, you can pulse old-fashioned oats in a blender or food processor into tiny bits. Plain yogurt, sour cream or milk mixed with lemon juice can replace the buttermilk. Ground flax gives these a nutty richness, but eggs can bind the batter, too. Maple syrup or brown sugar can stand in for the honey, and unsalted butter or oil work for cooking.

commentBody: Read that oatmeal helped lower blood pressure so thought this recipe might be more enjoyable for my oatmeal grummping sweetie. He ate 6 and said, ”They’re not bad". Hight praize from this carnavour! So it’s a keeper. The only change I made was using whole wheat bread flour, have no all purpose, so they didn’t raise as much as I thought they should. author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is similar to my oatmeal pancake recipe.  I usually toast the oats (I use regular rolled oats) and then process them in a blender until they are finely chopped instead of using quick cooking oats).  Also use brown sugar instead of honey (but I plan to try honey next time), and a combination of baking powder and baking soda for better lift.  Soaking the oats for at least 15 min. is critical or you will end up with leaden disks.  My husband likes these better than regular pancakes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These were absolutely delicious. Like others, I found my batter was thinner than described: maybe because I "made" the buttermilk with milk and lemon (buttermilk is unheard of in southern France), which was maybe too liquid. But no big deal: I just made bigger pancakes in my skillet. With loads of butter and indeed leaving them to brown well on the bottom, they were a treat. Husband loved them too, always a bonus. These were a big hit!

author: Genevieve Ko

My son, who usually turns his nose up at anything resembling whole grains (“mom stop trying to get me to eat healthy things, they’re gross”), gobbles them up and is always eager for me to make them again.

author: Genevieve Ko

commentBody: I have made these twice now, once with eggs, once with ground flaxseed. Both are keepers. I prefer the texture of the egg version, but the flaxseed version edges ahead in flavor.  I used leftover buttermilk that had been frozen, then defrosted. My experience with the powdered buttermilk (from long ago Sunday pancake breakfast with the kids) is that pancake batters made with it are too runny.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My tip after decades of cooking pancakes:  (1) Dedicate a non-stick griddle to pancakes that is never oiled--ever. (2) Preheat over medium heat while you are preparing the batter.  (3) For each batch, set the timer for 1 minute per side.  If the pancakes are not done in approx. 2 min., your batter is too thick for good interior moistness.  For that batch, you will have to reduce the heat and cook longer = breadiness.  Meanwhile, thin the batter--I use water by the tablespoon--for the next batch.

I would make the oatmeal pancakes again though!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These were great. I used powdered buttermilk, because that's what I have in the pantry. I used ground flaxseed, though I'll try subbing eggs next time. If I made pancakes often, I think I'd stick to a homemade mix or a simpler recipe. I don't think these were quite worth the extra time and effort of making the oatmeal mix separate and allowing it to sit. We did like these a lot, though. So 4 stars.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Best pancakes ever in terms of taste and texture.  Fluffy and airy.  Easy to make.  I might try adding more honey or substituting maple syrup.  My husband and i had these for lunch today, and we only had two left out of the entire recipe.  They were delicious.  I didn't have flax so used eggs instead.  I'll definitely make these again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @MG do about 2/3 Greek yogurt and 1/3 milk or water to sub for buttermilk. If you’d like to get more precise, King Arthur Flour has a whole write up on buttermilk substitutions.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I use sweetener-free muesli as oatmeal, so that's what I have on hand this snowed-in week. I can't grind up the muesli because of the dried fruit and nuts in there, so I just soaked it in the buttermilk for an hour. Came out great! I want the eggs for the protein and I can't use honey because I'm prediabetic, but this recipe was great inspiration to play around with my oatmeal and pancake routine. 10 out of 10, will make again and again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Menelik use a nonstick pan! Heat the pan before adding the batter, not too hot. As from another comment, test the heat by adding a drop of water to the pan, it should dance. Then wait until the cake is bubbly before flipping. There’s probably a million videos on yt.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If you have someone in your household that reacts to propylene glycol, choose the egg version over the flax seed version.  Cooked flax produces a compound similar enough to propylene glycol to trigger.  That’s why it works as an emulsifying egg substitute.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Have enjoyed the results of this recipe at least half a dozen times now. Used Quaker ‘Oat so Simple’ (UK) rapid cook rolled oats, which come in convenient 36g sachets (that is, use two per batch recipe). Quaker also make a Golden Syrup flavour variant which really adds to the flavour of the pancakes. A separate 1:1 mix of honey:butter, melted, makes a complementing finishing syrup poured over just before serving. A handful of blueberries mixed into the batter have also worked well. The crunchy bits around the edges are simply and moreishly beaut!

I’ve found even lower and slower than other pancakes works best and I also make them slightly smaller than other pancakes. I have plenty of quick oats that need using up so I’ll probably make them again. The real test will be if I decide to buy more and keep going when I’m out.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I made these pancakes twice. The first time I followed the recipe exactly only to find my batter was too liquidy. (I was also using 2 eggs in lieu of flax seeds.) I added roughly 1/3 cup flour to that batch and produced thin but decent pancakes. The 2nd time I reduced butter milk to 1-1/2 cups which gave me much better consistency and produced pancakes that looked like pancakes.
Also want to note that if you use clarified butter (ghee) when cooking the pancakes, you will get better tasting pancakes with slightly crunchy edges.

commentBody: The simple on hand ingredients  are the beauty of this recipe. I used boneless, skinless sardines in olive oil, hence  less than 1/4 cup of mayo was needed. I use one can of skinless boneless, 1 hb egg; 2T mayo, 1/2 tsp dijon mustard and a dash of Worcestershire.

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko

Excited to try this recipe.  One of my favorite sandwiches growing up was a sardine, hard boiled egg, and onion sandwich.  A little mustard on the bread topped by the other ingredients.  My mouth is watering just thinking about it.

Hat's off to the old Fat Man restaurant in Paterson, NJ for introducing this culinary treat to me.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Using a full cup of mayo in this recipe would be madness. I agree with the tip to start with 1/4 cup and add from there as needed. Also, I’ve started using a combination of greek yogurt and mayo in cold salad recipes, to maintain creaminess but reduce fat. The tang from the yogurt is typically a plus.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Kelly I always combo mayo and sour cream for many of the same reasons, and it's what I reliably have on hand (the sour cream).

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made it this evening as written. Delicious!  But I will add a tablespoon of chopped onion next time.  It seems there are many variations possible. Definitely a keeper.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Love this easy combination.  I also added scallions, fresh dill & a touch of sumac; sumac adds a flavor that is slightly lemony.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Lena Richard made her own mayonnaise. This is from her cookbook:
1 egg yolk
1 tablespoon lemon juice
1/2 teaspoon salt
1 tablespoon vinegar
Few grains cayenne pepper
1 cup olive oil or other salad oil
1/2 teaspoon dry mustard if desired

Have mixture bowl and all ingredients very cold. Stir the yolk with a fork, add a portion of the lemon juice, stir and add oil drop by drop, stirring constantly. Add other ingredients, and as mixture thickens add alternately, lemon juice and oil.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I'm not a sardine person so I made Lena's Lamb Chops with Pineapple from Lena Richard's Cook Book and they turned out - excellent! The cookbook includes recipes from everything from cocktails to soups and gumbos, bread and biscuits, compotes, desserts, candy, beverages. . .Chocolate waffles and divinity fudge sound delicious! They're on my to-do list. Paula Rhodes, Richard's granddaughter, loves the okra gumbo.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I loved this! I subbed paprika for the cayenne because that's what I had on hand. I added mayo by eye and a freshly cracked black pepper. Totally agree with other commenters to add lemon and to eat the final product on crackers.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: my Dad would onion

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Sardine and egg is one of my favorite spreads. I add a teaspoon each of mustard and lemon juice and the zest of a lemon for flavor. I pulse it in the blender for a smooth consistency. Delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This sounds amazing!  I am also going to try with some pickled herring in sour cream!  Very keto too on the right bread.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Jen JG My wife and I often enjoy an even easier way to eat sardines. You can use dressing, of course, but the lemon mixes with the sardine oil to give it plenty of flavor. I have a slice or two of buttered toast with mine.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Dainty sardine sandwiches were my grandmother’s appetizer of choice at dinner parties during the 1930s. They had long gone out of fashion by the time I came along, but I’m always happy to indulge in some canned sardines while I think of my grandmother’s parties.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Sounds delightful and I will try (with minimal mayo).  Reminds me of the school lunch sandwiches my mother made for me. author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I love going thru the "Easy" recipes. This certainly is easy and quick. I didn't have any mayo, so I substituted sour cream (about 1/4 cup), added chopped banana peppers, and seasoned it with garlic powder, cayenne pepper, and some paprika. Mix and then spread on a potato roll. It was a very satisfying lunch. High in protein. I may add some celery next time. This is a keeper.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I was skeptical but this will definitely go into our favorites list.  I did replace the cayenne with Cholula because we are enamoured with that atm, I made a double recipe with a tin of sardines in EVOO and a tin of Sardines with lemon in EVOO.  Followed the directions and it was fabulous.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Used tinned sockeye salmon and hungarian hot paprika instead.
Worked really well, although no need for salt with it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This makes a good quick lunch. Made as directed but next time I will add onion and maybe some red pepper flakes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: You left out thinly sliced raw Sweet onion on top !!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I believe every Italian American Cook has a version of this in their repertoire. As the family cook, being that I was a teacher and got home an hour and a half earlier than my wife, I would make a very similar sauce: ground beef browned in the pot, caramelized the onions and a carrot, if I had one sitting around, open up a can of Cento crushed tomatoes, empty it with a little bit of water. The kids would come home, absolutely ravenous from their after school activities and devour it, and if we were lucky, with a big dollop of ricotta cheese and Parmesan on top. It was an absolute lifesaver, highly recommended for working families. I’m going to try the red curry paste, it sounds delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Charle A.  yum!  such a lovely comment . you make it sound even more delicious.  can’t wait to make it and eat it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Zeldie Thank you, Zeldie. :)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Is this Thai red curry paste, asks a reader in sunny London, UK

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I'm guessing yes, but I came here to post the same question.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: All of the red curry paste in my search results are Thai red curry paste.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Teresa Green It's probably red curry paste but I used Panang curry and it turned out delicious

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It was quick but didn't care for the Asian flavor which stood out to me. I like the idea but will try tomato paste and crushed red pepper flakes next time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Colleen I don’t eat spicy so I left out the curry completely and I also replaced the heavy cream with coconut milk and it was delicious

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @opinionated Did you double the tomato paste instead?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
It's quite good and easy. Probably a dis-service to term it "Bolognese." Not for reasons of purism; I'm fine with recipes that mess with classic preparations. But just because...well, it doesn't really taste like a Bolognese. Granted it doesn't taste at all like curry, either. So...

Well, in any case: easy prep and very good...whatever you want to call it!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @AC I feel the same. It’s delicious, honestly I’m not a big bolognese girl anyway so I might like this better. But it tastes like a creamy tomato curry sauce with beef and carrots. Super cozy, super easy, super tasty, but a little misleading to say it tastes like a long-simmered Italian sauce IMHO

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Nah, this doesn’t do it for me. 

Don’t use Thai curry paste. 

Asian flavors are too strong. 

And I’m half Asian, so I love the food but this doesn’t work for an Italian dish. 

Why not just use Calabrian chilly paste.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Ellen I was thinking the same thing. Also some red wine added will give it some depth.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Ellen
Did you try it?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Ellen no she didn’t try it

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Turn down the heat when adding the onions

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made exactly as instructed, used 2.5 TBSP of Thai Red Curry paste, and it was great! The red curry adds an umami richness, but sadly, no heat. I'm going to try 3 TBSP next time. This is great comfort food for a weeknight recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Nevada Rose If you want some heat, sub in (red) Penang Curry paste.  It’s got such a wonderful heat! I think you will LOVE it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Would adding red wine make sense? Also tomato paste - can it be used as a substitute to curry?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The red curry paste seems to be a substitute for time, so that the bolognese is complex but quick. If you want more of a slow-cooked flavour, try roasting your tomato paste or using something like sun-dried tomatoes. It might alter the flavour, but it'll give you a boost of complexity.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Craig c I do a version of quick bolognese sauce and sauté the onions, grated carrots, minced mushrooms and then add  a couple of tablespoons of tomato paste and sautée together till tomato paste is fragrant. Add splashes of red wine to deglaze and simmer for a few minutes and it adds hours of depth I find!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Craig c no don’t add wine or tomato paste that is a different dish

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Oh muh gawd. This was outstanding. I followed all instructions, but subbed a high protein pasta and puréed 2/3 c cottage cheese with 1/3 c half and half for the cream. Not a molecule left on anyone’s plates.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: What a beautifully written article, Genevieve Ko. Wow. I was going to make meatballs but now I'm making this. I will skip the curry paste, tho, and I don't have carrots on hand so won't use those, either. Thank you. Our girl, 18, and her friend(s) are often around. Though they still love the health nightmares like McDonald's and Wendy's and large sweet drinks from Starbucks if and when they can get their hands on them, they do seem to love Italian food.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: You can make pretty good Bolananse ala Marcella in a hour although the multi hour version is better. I don't get the curry paste ? Use a heavily reduced Tomato paste from Italy. Cirio comes to mind.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Did you try ths with curry paste? I bet you did not.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Supreme effort-to-flavor ratio. Endlessly riffable. I had a half pound of pasta, but didn't mind the extra protein. Sub'd in gochujang for the red curry, and tomato paste plus extra pasta water (instead of canned sauce) to make things saucy. Will definitely make its way into the rotation.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Cody gochujang is such an excellent idea!!! I really liked the red curry (didn’t taste like curry in the end) and I suspect I’d get the same result with gochujang…depth of flavor.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: How about Harissa paste rather than Curry?  It’s what I have on hand…

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Kathy Sure works fine, too! I’ve been making a similar sauce adding some gochujang. Lovely flavor nuance!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Kathy
Great idea! And I have on hand (no curry)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Kathy I was thinking this.  Going to try this weekend

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My mom always used mild Italian sausage, which I think adds a ton of depth of flavor, as well as chili powder.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I sprinkle a bit of baking soda on ground meat before browning. Turn the baking soda side down when placing in the pan and then leave undisturbed. Greta results - no more grey ground meat.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@sf cook

ATK claims baking soda helps the ground beef hold moisture better. Did you notice that?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Adding the red curry paste is a similar trick to how I add umami to my kid-friendly bolognese: I use miso, fish sauce (patis), and shaoxing wine. (I'll pour the shaoxing into the empty jar of tomato pasta sauce that I use and shake it to get all the remnants of the sauce out.) Umami tricks are plentiful (even the mushroom powder from Trader Joe's works well!), and I also sauté dried herbs and fennel seed with an onion to start the cooking process to add even more depth.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I like to substitute whole milk plain yogurt in recipes that call for cream. It gives the richness with that extra tang and aids in digestion. A Win Win

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Yogurt won't aid in digestion if heated to the point that it kills the culture.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@H Ball
Careful, yoghurt separates if it’s heated too high ! Maybe try heating up a little to see what happens

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: what about gochujang??

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I was just thinking this might work. Maybe "hoping" it might work is more accurate. It's what I have in the fridge.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Annie
I was think the exact same thing.  I eat Korean red pepper paste all of the time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Andy can confirm - works great!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: What nondairy option do folks think would be a good substitute for the cream, for those of us who don't use dairy in meat dishes?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My observant Jew friend uses Richs products to keep things kosher. In this case it would be their Non-Dairy Topping, assuming you can find it. I think there is also a coconut cream available that might be easier to find.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Marcus, maybe cashew cream? Lots of recipes for that online.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Marcus
Country Crock Plant Based heavy cream

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I cooked this last night and it was excellent. I used the Thai red curry paste available in many supermarkets. It’s quite mild so three tbsp was not spicy even for my spicy-adverse child. However, it definitely added depth. It was still a lot of time spent chopping given how small the carrot and onion pieces need to be so this is not a weeknight dish for me personally. I used 15% fat beef and 3/4 cup of heavy cream which still worked out wonderfully. I also used lentil rotini for a nutrition boost. My spouse said it might be the best thing I’ve ever cooked.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Kylie M I started with shredded carrots which saved a lot of chopping time!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Kylie M food processor while ground beef is browning.  Not usually a fan but great for this

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: In my experience many red curry pastes have shrimp as an ingredient. Anyone who is allergic to them needs to be warned. Not something you would expect to find in a Bolognese sauce.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was easy and delicious. More like this, please!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Seems like so many readers feeling are kinda punchy. About pasta sauce??  Cook something and share a meal with others if possible. Or enjoy the process of making something and savor the opportunity to eat what you put some effort into.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I sprinkled a little sugar and some fish sauce just to amplify the flavor and give it some mid-range and it went from good to can't stop eating it. Anchovy paste would probably work equally well

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Dorie I almost forgot the sugar, a staple in my usual Bolognese sauce

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was easy and delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Red wine instead of curry paste.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Eileen Red wine to deglaze with some caramelised red onions. Yum!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Red wine in addition to, not instead of, the curry paste.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this as set forth in the recipe and thought it was excelent. A few notes on the techniques in the recipe.  The carrots and onion call for a fine dice. Next time, I wil dice finer.  You want them to almost melt into the sauce as this is a rich, creamy sauce. Note that you are to add the beef to a hot pan and smush it in a single layer.  This allows the beef to develop a great crust, like a smash burger. It's a great and quick recipe.  I got eight servings out of it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I've made a recipe like this from NYTimes with great success going the few-drops-of-fish-sauce route rather than the curry paste.  We made the sauce in 20 mins (emergency cooking) and it was great!  I missed the simmering smell of an all afternoon bolognese, but the eating was just as satisfying.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: We don't eat beef - any thoughts on using ground turkey or vegan crumbles?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Edith
 no

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Unfortunately I don't have experience w/either ground turkey or vegan crumbles, but I wonder whether ground chicken might work. I've only used it to make meatballs, so I don't know how it would stand up to the high-heat cooking.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Yes, I sub ground turkey for beef all the time! If using white meat, or a blend of white and dark, you may need to add olive oil to the pot when browning since turkey won’t have the fat content of ground beef. Try it, and enjoy!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This makes a lot. Big flavor and delicious. I never would have thought to use red curry paste but the flavor was fantastic. I added a bit of grated nutmeg in honor of Marcella’s original recipe. Just lovely!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I really enjoyed this (very easy) recipe and it was nice to finally use up those almost exactly 2 tbsp of thai red curry paste in the freezer. I added some regular tomato paste, and some thawed spinach for extra veggies and I used ground turkey because it was what I had. I will say though that I can taste that it tastes like curry, though perhaps I needed to cook the curry paste down more. That said, I don't mind one bit--I like a hybrid recipe!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Used a scant pound of pasta (rigatoni, all I could find) and subbed 1T harissa   2T tomato paste for the curry paste. Delicious and quick.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Excellent!  I always try to prepare a recipe as written the first time I make it.  Because we had an 8 oz. box of pasta for the two of us, I prepared the sauce as directed, then divided it in half and saved half for a future meal.  Added 1/2 cup of cream to the sauced pasta and stirred together.  No pasta water was needed to thin the sauce, it was perfect as is.  This recipe is a keeper.  No changes needed for our tastes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Let's not get hung up on the recipe's lack of bolognese-ishness. Seared meat (I added some prosciutto) with Maesri curry paste and cream was totally delicious, whatever we call it. Try a pipette/shellbow instead of a more classic papparadelle.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Made again tonight. Used ground turkey, added some unsalted butter and neutral oil at the beginning to compensate for the fat. Instead of tomato sauce, I used a can of diced tomatoes in their juice, and some tomato paste browned in the pot along with the curry paste. Deglazed with a little water, might try dry white wine next time. Used about half the pasta, which was right for us. I had only about a 1/2 cup of heavy cream so i added some pasta water to break up the solids in the carton, which worked well. I didn’t have any spinach or swiss chard this time but I would have chopped it up to add in the last few minutes if I had. 
Overall this is an easy and delicious recipe and it’s fun to tweak with whatever you want.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: For those w/shellfish allergies - be aware that many curry pastes include shrimp as an ingredient, but curry powder doesn't.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Tomato paste would be a much better substitute than curry powder :)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Terrific little recipe - eschew the haters and try for yourself! I did deglaze the carrots and onion (and added some garlic in the mix) with a little red wine, and added a TBSP of tomato paste along with the 3 TBSP red curry paste. It does indeed have a subtle heat and roundness without tasting like curry! 

Only used a little over half the cream, which was plenty, but also about half the pasta! Sauce ratio felt right there to me 

For my veg partner, I used butter and olive oil to brown the carrot and onion, and added a pack of TVP crumbles for the last ~4 mins of cooking. I had beef in my bowl as well and we were both very happy!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Finally got around to making this. Not to be that person but I definitely made a lot of substitutions after a tragic incident in which I broke a jar of curry paste and spilled all the heavy cream. Swapped those for what I had - Gochujang and coconut milk. Still an absolute banger of a recipe. Already looking forward to leftovers for lunch tomorrow!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Very good as is. I have made many meat sauces over the years and will use what’s around—chicken, a small piece of beef,  ground beef and a small piece of salt pork or pancetta to get started. They all work.  To me this presents as a creamy arrabbiata (only 2 tbsp of curry paste) and as such it was spectacular . Fyi also I used passata in lieu of tomato sauce and found it made things a bit less acidic.  These are marginal comments because in all I thought it was great.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Hey all, I made this with impossible meat and it was quite good. I reversed steps 1 and 2 sautéing the onion and carrot first, then adding the meat, 2T of tomato paste, and 2T of chili paste. I only used 1/2 cup of cream but used more pasta water. Really quick and satisfying.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I only used 1.5 T of red curry, added crushed tomatoes, and subbed half the cream with milk. I threw fresh pasta into the sauce to cook, which created a quick, 1-pot, flavorful dinner that my teens went crazy for. There was no curry flavor; we just tasted a dish with depth and complexity.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Greasy and heavy.  Not at all complex set of flavors. I don’t understand the increasing enthusiasm for beef fat in so many recipes.  Nothing healthy about it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Used fake beef and added a small can of diced tomatoes but otherwise followed exactly. This was so so so so so so so good. Went with 3 big tbsp of curry paste. The end result was deeply savory and robust but nobody could identify the “secret ingredient”. It was a superb ratio of sauce to pasta. I used mezzi rigatoni.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is a fantastic dish, if one suspends one's disbelief a bit and just enjoys it for its deliciousness without trying to compare it to classic Bolognese.  I used half white wine and half heavy cream, adding the wine with the veg to reduce a tad and the cream towards the end, also reducing slightly.  Also minced a couple of garlic cloves to add with the veg.  Used Thai kitchen red curry paste, which is not too spicy.  Simmered it to unctiousness and served over penne.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Hi Jennie - not trying to be snarky at all, but wondering what you mean by unctiousness? I didn't recognize that term and wanting to learn, checked dictionary app and online search.  What came up was unctuous, defined as "excessively or ingratiatingly flattering; oily." Maybe your comment is a victim of autocorrect because not sure that jibes with the rest of your comment? Thx

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Deborah: maybe you are a victim of a shallow search. Unctuous is defined (definition 2) as "(of food or drink) having a pleasingly rich taste." You can see the definition of unctuousness here: https://www.thefreedictionary.com/unctuousness

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Tastes great! Requires a lot of heavy cream, but who wants to live forever?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Love it! 
I doubled the recipe, though. In doing so, I used 16 oz. Ground Beef, 16 0z. Ground Pork, then twice as much of all the other ingredients (5 Tsp Red Curry).
Used Pipe Rigate pasta; didn't double the pasta though, cuz it really holds the delicious sauce.
Anyway, always have a loaf of Italian bread on hand to toast and scrape a clove of garlic across for mopping up. Clean Plate Club!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was delicious and easy. I didn't have red curry paste so I used a dollop of tomato paste mixed with red chili flakes and curry powder. I also subbed the heavy cream with sour cream and whole milk. Soo good!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My mom made a terrific bolognese sauce, back in the day. She used traditional ingredients, but would say that tablespoon of curry powder was needed for depth. She was right!! Probably Mccormick's...

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was just fine. Tasted more like hamburger helper than bolognese.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Used gochujang instead of curry paste and Mexican sour cream instead of cream. It’s what I had on-hand. Came out SPECTACULAR. This one’s going into the regular rotation for sure.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wow.  While I love to cook and don't mind when things take hours, the simplicity of this was just outstanding. I can't help myself so I added a few anchovies, a couple tablespoons of the anchovy olive oil and red pepper flakes, and used a locally made Thai red curry paste, and the spice was perfect. SO easy, and my son and I both gobbled it up and really enjoyed it.  A definite keeper, and it will be in the "go to" list for quick and easy dinners, especialy for a crowd!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I did not have any curry paste, so instead I caramelized a healthy dollop of tomato paste in the pan alongside the onions and carrots, and then added a big dose chile-garlic crisp (a great condiment that's having a moment these days)... and it was glorious! So rich, warming, spicy, comforting, satisfying... I believe this may become a staple. I liked my subsitutions so much  that I'm hesitant to try it as written... but I guess I'll be responsible and do the proper diligence :) YUM

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Cooked as is but added more seasoning (garlic, basil, oregano, parsley). Delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Really yummy! For the Aussies - I used 3 tbsp of Marion's Red Curry Paste and it was very good!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Great recipe. Gochujang paste works as a solid substitute if you don’t have curry paste. And halving the cream doesn’t hurt either.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Fabulous. I let the smushed layer of ground beef sear a bit—like a smash burger!—before breaking it up. Used half and half instead of the heavy cream. Doused the finished product in grated parm. Feel like this could also be great with ground pork or Italian sausage.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Given the title and accompanying article, I was able to see past “but it’s not really bolognese!” I am reminded of an oft-repeated family phrase, “This is not real veal cutlet,” said by a relative while we were eating at the equivalent of a Denny’s. 

What it is is adaptable and something I made in about 30 minutes on my lunch break. I used ground pork because that’s what I had, subbed tomato paste for the curry paste because, again, that’s what I had. Finished with 2 percent milk since I didn’t have cream. Surely cream would have made it better, but even with the substitutions, I enjoyed it and I would gladly make it again. I’ll save the “real bolognese” for a day when I have the time and ingredients.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: delicious, subbed with meat substitute italian sausage and used greek yogurt instead of heavy cream 1:1 substitute

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This recipe turnout out great - with the red curry paste! To make it extra meaty I reduced a whole pack of thick cut bacon, chopped into small pieces, into which I added a mixture of ground beef along with ground pork. I was very generous with garlic powder and crushed red chili flakes. Everything else was done just as described. The entire family devoured the meal! I’ll definitely make this again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Unfortunately, my family was not a fan.  One child's comment, "this dish looks like Donald Trump." While I was optimistic about how quickly this could come together, the flavor made me think this is what it must be like to judge Chopped cooking competitions.  The sum of these parts did not make something delicious.  It didn't help that the cooking time is so short that the carrots didn't have enough time to soften and meld into the sauce like a traditional bolognese. We'll use our leftover curry

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: surprisingly delicious!  We didn't detect a curry flavor at all.  Used 3 tbl Thai Kitchen Red Curry which added no real heat.  Will add red pepper flakes and garlic next time.  used 1/2 & 1/2 because I was making "healthy choices," lol.  Great, simple dinner.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: A cup of heavy cream added at the end? Ugh! That will pretty much kill the complexity. If you must, add a third or scant half a cup well before the end, just to smooth out the flavors. The curry paste is intriguing, but my favorite flavor bomb in a quickie Bolognese is Rao's Marinara sauce with a good pinch of red pepper flakes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Brilliant recipe. I was a bit sceptical at first seeing red curry paste as an ingredient. After taking on this recipe tonight with no substitutes, just followed the recipe, I can say WoW! Just follow the recipe. You will love it

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Excellent recipe, quick and easy yet delicious and flavorful. Followed the recipe except I swapped the red curry paste for jarred Calabrian chili since that’s what I had on hand. Turned out great and will be making again!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Fantastico! More to our taste than bolognese we recently had at a fancy Italian place. Mods:

-Used sweet Italian pork sausage (from Trader Joe’s)
-Drizzled Dutch oven with a little oil before browning
-Only used 1/2 cup heavy cream
-Garnished with fresh basil

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made as written with 2 Tb. Thai red curry paste (it was plenty; more would have been overwhelming and we are not spice-averse) and about 1/4 C. pasta water to thin out the sauce. I used rotini because I had a box on hand. A really good recipe. Makes a goodly amount. You could make improvements, but as written, it's perfectly satisfactory, with a good ratio of effort to result. Comfort food.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It’s funny I read comments all the time and I’m always so proud of the people that substitute things because they don’t have the exact ingredients in their pantry. I’m always too afraid to do that! but I they’ve inspired me. Instead of running to the store I used the hot spicy sausage I had  instead of hamburger and green curry paste because that was what I had on hand and it was spicy and delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Could this be made with plant-based meat, such as impossible or beyond burger?  Maybe with a different fat, like butter?  (Not trying to make vegan, just avoiding red meat.)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I can't see why that wouldn't work, especially with addition of butter!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I've made this about five times by now and it's amazing. Takes very little time but tastes like you've been cooking it for much longer. I use tagliatelle pasta and then whatever dairy product I have in the fridge instead of the cream - greek yoghurt, sour cream, and even cream cheese have all worked before.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: What an amazing addition, red curry paste?  LOVE IT!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Beware that, for some curry pastes (I used Mae Ploy), 2 to 3 tablespoons will be way too spicy. I like spicy food, and yet I wish I had used just 1 tablespoon

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: if it's not in my pantry i don't consider it weeknight-friendly...i don't have red curry paste in there but i do have sun-dried tomatoes as suggested, thnx!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I only used 2 TBSP of the curry paste and it was too spicy for me, even though I generally like spicy food.  The flavor was good but I prefer a traditional bolognese. Probably won’t make again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Garlic, people, garlic.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Appreciated the notes and tips in the comments. Went with 2 T of red curry paste and one T of tomato paste. Rinsed out the tomato sauce can with some red wine. Added a splishy splash of Worcestershire sauce. Such deliciousness! And plays well with tinkyada GF pasta!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this minus the red curry paste. I used Italian tomato paste and added some red chili flakes. Added a splash of red wine. It turned out really really good.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The recipe is so easy and the meal was delicious and satisfying. I did not have red curry paste and heavy cream handy so skipped both but I used some half and half. I will definitely make this again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Sour cream substituted for heavy cream.  Wonderful.  Will be making this at least once a month.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If you don't have red curry paste you can use tamari or soy sauce with cayenne.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this with 3 TB. Gochujang (brand O'Food) and 1/3 c. cream. A big hit and will be making it again! I am intrigued with the red curry paste and may try that too.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I do not like red curry so I roasted tomato paste in olive oil with a pinch of ground red pepper.  I still felt it lacked flavor so I added Italian seasoning and salt.  I loved it after the substitution and additions. I feel like I will go to this often for a quick, meaty pasta dish.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is definitely an awesome dish to make the day after using curry paste in something more traditional. I never use the whole can (I'm usually cooking for two), and most other curry paste recipes would feel redundant in the same week. I was so curious about this after seeing the recipe and finally tried it last night. My wife and I both loved it, I thought I could taste the curry paste (a hint of ginger is what stood out), my wife said she couldn't taste it at all. I didn't want to buy heavy cream so I substituted with a half cup of half & half   a half cup of full fat cottage cheese, blended to break up the curds. Three tablespoons of Maesri red curry paste didn't impart much heat but certainly brought flavor. Will definitely make this again next time I use some red curry paste in, say, a panang curry. Thanks, Genevieve!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this recipe and it was very easy to make, as well as very tasty.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Probably my favorite NYT recipe ever. So easy and delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Ten out of five stars. The flavor is just fantastic.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I really liked this! It doesn’t taste like curry at all. I used Thai red curry and added Calabrian chili paste cause after tasting the red curry in the jar it wasn’t spicy at all. I made this out of curiosity, not cause I was pressed for time so I followed the ingredients and the order of the directions, but I used some garden tomatoes instead of tomato sauce and so I let it simmer for an hour. I also let the onions and carrots go past translucent to almost browning then browned the curry paste til it was quite dark. This would be an awesome meal for after sledding or time spent in the cold where everyone is hungry for something warm and filing and there’s not a lot of time to satisfy that craving. Excellent short cut to deep flavor.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: So great and a great weeknight sauce that the flavour comes together quickly. I subbed harissa paste instead of the curry and added a splash of red wine. Two thumbs up!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: As others suggested, I used gochujang because that’s what I had in my fridge. Worked really well. Because my toddler was going to eat it as well, I did 2 tsp instead of 2 tbs. Gave it just enough of a kick to not scare off the toddler but would use more for just myself.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I read the comments and felt a little dubious. However, I followed the recipe as written with the substitution of ground local lamb. It is delicious!!! I used Mike’s Organic Curry Love Red Thai Curry paste from the Brattleboro Food Coop. Try the recipe, it is very satisfying!. Buon’ appetito!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Made this tonight! 
Pros: Able to execute this dish mostly as-written while solo parenting a 1 year old. We are both nourished. 
Cons: To me, it tastes sort of like ketchup (could be a pro to some?). I used Maesri brand curry paste, which may be a different brand than Ms. Ko's.
I found that only 15 oz of tomato sauce (or, in my case, tomato purée) was insufficient so I used 28 oz. I did not have cream, so I used whole milk.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Lucy strongly suggest using cream next time or even evaporated milk if you have it.  I might well add more tomato sauce too but that could account for it tasting a bit like ketchup.  Between the milk substitution and the extra tomato, I’m surmised you didn’t end up with soup :-)  I’d suggest using diced or even whole canned tomatoes.  I’ve found that the already puréed tomatoes often lack flavour.  Maybe they’re made from the leftover bits and pieces?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I only wanted to make 1/2 lb of pasta and foolishly added my 8 Oz of rigatoni to the sauce. It was swimming haha! 

But it’s not a crisis. I will just boil more tomorrow night for supper.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Honestly could not believe how good this turned out!! The time spent VS the results make this recipe a solid 5 star! Highly recommend adding the Thai red curry, I don’t taste the curry at all, but somehow it adds a depth to the flavor! I also did not use as much heavy cream as the recipe suggested, the flavor was perfect without feeling too heavy!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: i messed up by using milk. didnt have curry paste so i used gochujang + miso + tomato paste. was still good. maybe my ratios were off put 1lb of pasta was a lot for the sauce.....

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My family loved this so much that we made it two nights in a row.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Quick Bolognese: 
- Gently fry the ground meat (beef, pork or ground chicken) render fat, pour off and save.
- Add slurry of onion/carrot/celery made w mini-chopper or stick blender.
- Add milk (don't need cream); add fat later as desired, cook 2 mins and add small amt tomato sauce/paste, then parmesan and simmer another 15 mins. Boost umami if you want w MSG/anchovy paste, a little wine or a tiny amount sugar to taste.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used Mama Lam's Malaysian curry (the best) and I loved the additional complexity of flavor! Also added garlic and little bit of fresh ginger. This calls for way too much pasta, though. I didn't even use the full pound and felt it drowned out the delicious sauce. Next time I'll cook less and also add it gradually to the sauce until the ratio feels right. Topped with parmigiano.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: We made this as written and it was really very good - especially considering how easy it was and how quickly it came together.  We will definitely make this again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I would never have thought of red curry paste, but I had some Thai Kitchen brand languishing in the fridge, which is a pretty mild red curry paste. I also did add some tomato paste, and in place of the cream, which I didn't have, I added a generous dollop of mascarpone cheese.  So, yes, I didn't make the recipe, but my riff on it was delicious, and I assume the original would be at least as good. Next time I will add a little red wine, since hubby and I aren't drinking it fast enough these days.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Didn't have Thai curry paste & substituted gojuchang sauce, topped with chili crisp & a sprinkle of cilantro.  Loved it & so did my family.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Used 3 tablespoons yellow curry bc that’s what I had on hand. Was tasting a bit too much like curry (which is fine, just not what I was in the mood for) so added about 1/3 canned marinara sauce. Turned out amazing!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Tasty! We used turkey instead of beef because that’s our preference, just added some olive oil when adding the onion and carrot. First time used the whole cup of cream and felt it was too much — used 1/2 cup the second time and that suited our taste better.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Loved this recipe as is, but loved it even more using ground lamb.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Turned this vegetarian by using minced mushrooms!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: So I got out the Thai red curry paste languishing in the back of the fridge- BB date 2011! I used it anyway-with 1 Tbs of Red Miso (in date)  plus a tin of pureed tomatoes instead of tomato sauce, a splash of fish sauce and a splash of Worcestershire sauce. It tastes very rich and good. I think I might get some new red curry paste before the next time though :-) . I think a splash of red-wine/sherry vinegar or lemon juice might jazz this 'bolognese' up a little bit before serving.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious! No other comment needed!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
This is a great recipe! I don’t know how someone could say it tastes “too Asian”. I added 2 tbsp of red curry paste and thought it brought an almost smoky flavour to the sauce. I also really enjoyed the cooking method and will use it in the future. 

Next time I may experiment with replacing the curry paste with garlic, sun dried tomatoes and oregano. 

Fantastic recipe. Thank you!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was delicious. Not a traditional Bolognese (if that’s what you’re looking for), but a surprisingly complex and hearty sauce from so few ingredients. I used roughly 2T of red curry paste. Will definitely make again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Excellent recipe. I used curry powder instead of curry paste. It worked well. 
Very abundant, it serves 6 to 8 well, plus leftovers.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Easy to prepare and very tasty! I will try it with a little more of the red curry paste the next time. I dropped the pasta to 12 oz. instead of the 1 lb. in the recipe. This way, the meat to pasta ratio was perfect!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: For the curry part I used pre-made jarred tikka masala paste and it made for an incredible result!! The sauce was transformed, it got all smoky and a bit sweet and had that umami note. Delicious, and I say all this as someone born and raised in Bologna!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Holy Moly the red chili paste truly kicks this up a notch! Banger recipe. Recommend three heaping spoonfuls to get a good bite of spice.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Vile!
The red curry paste taste is not discernible as curry but the flavor is truly awful.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Didn’t have curry paste like I thought when I started cooking this, so I substituted with tomato paste, curry powder, and a touch of jarred chili oil paste. This was delicious and my entire family gobbled it up.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This recipe was very easy to make, as well as delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used 2 1/2 T of curry paste and an 8oz box of high protein pasta. Next time I wish I had more finely chopped the veg, but super tasty topped with parm! Will make again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: WOAH wow. This recipe is so flavorful. Spicy red  sauce & red wine is my love language. This is my first NYT recipe review to leave because it’s that good. <3

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Really delicious recipe. The curry paste gives it an interesting and complex flavor. I liked it a lot, but it is definitely a different take on a Bolognese. Make sure to use a large pot or skillet to speed up the cooking process. Made almost exactly to the recipe but added 2 celery sticks and used beyond beef vegan ground beef. Got 6 servings out of it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Perhaps it’s because we don’t often eat red meat at home and so a ground beef dish feels luxurious, but I’ve made this dish twice now on to applause all around. Simple, fast, with good flavour. It’s a weeknight winner.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used gochujang instead of Thai chili paste and it was a nice substitute.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I've done it, it's really really good but, as an Italian, I don't understand why call it a Bolognese? I mean, we don't usually put heavy cream in it, we do use wine (red)..just call it like "beefy magic", I don't know whatever you like but please not "Bolognese" because Bolognese is another dish all together:)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Pretty good especially if you want something quick to make! I don’t know if the red curry paste is necessary though. I feel like you could omit it and use tomato paste instead.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Instead of curry paste, use Calabrian chili paste and fried fennel seed, and a small handful and fresh rosemary

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: this was so good and easy!!! could not taste any curry flavor (i did 2 tbsp red curry and 1 tbsp tomato paste) and the sauce had a great depth of flavor. my only issues were that i had to simmer the sauce for a good 20min to get the carrots fully cooked through and that i found 1 cup of heavy cream to be way too much. i think i would make it again with 1/4 to 1/2 cup of cream to keep more of the tomato flavor.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Super good! Substituted harissa for the red curry paste and half&half for the cream because that's what I had. Also added about 2T tomato paste with the vegetables for a little added depth of flavor.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I didn't have curry paste. Looked on the interwebs for a substitute and apparently it's ... curry. So I used curry, kept tasting and adding until I got some kick. It's madly delicious! (Otherwise cooked exactly as ordered!)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It was delicious. I did not use the red curry paste as we are not curry fans. It was a mild and soothing dish! I will make it again!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Hello from snowy Idaho, USA to Teresa Green who asks from "sunny London, UK" if the red curry paste in the recipe is Thai red curry paste, I believe the answer to your good question is yes it is intended to be that!  That is my plan for the ingredient when I make this.  I may start with a tad less of it and add to taste for heat level.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this yesterday, and I didn’t have Thai red curry paste, so I used Gochujang instead …. Was curious to see what this recipe is all about. Since I had the whole afternoon, I just added a 28 can of crushed tomatoes and half a can of water and let it cook for a couple hours. I didn’t use the heavy cream at the end, but I can definitely see how that would be an added boost if you had made it a little quicker. It’s a good hack: the spice paste added a little bit of warmth and some depth.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
This reminds of recipes that accompanied our Instant Pot. We tried a pot roast recipe that dumped all kinds of prepared sauces and heavy spicing in with the roast. It was spectacularly awful. The Instant Pot sits at the back of the cupboard until donation day at GoodWill. 
This recipe will taste like red curry… there is no way that it can’t. A better shortcut is to dress up a bottled sauce than this mess…

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: We didn't taste the red curry at all.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Nope. It works! Doesn't taste like curry, tastes like a spicy-ish sausage pasta.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: How would this taste with ground lamb?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Julie V I don’t know about ground lamb, but I make mine with ground turkey and it’s perfectly fine. I think it will work if that’s what you have on hand.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Lamb bolognese is classic.  La Colline in DC serves a delicious version.  Go for it, but you might want to drain off some of the fat since lamb is much fattier than beef.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: 5* We LOVED! But I'm going to be one of "those people" who rates recipe & lists all my changes! (Sorry) Didn't have ground beef, so used 1# frozen boneless, skinless chicken thighs. Pulled off most fat & rendered it 5-10 mins, then added diced thighs & sautéed-stirred 15 mins. Then followed recipe except only used 1.5 T. Mae Ploy red curry paste (still too much!) but added 3-4 T. tomato paste. Warning: not all red curry pastes are the same; Mae Ploy is SUPER hot! Thai Kitchen MUCH milder. YMMV

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I had all the ingredients on hand and made this spur of the moment last night. Mostly followed the recipe exactly but added red chili pepper flakes to increase the spice and used a 26oz can of tomato sauce (because that’s what I had) without the extra water called for. However I did need to add a few ladles of pasta water towards the end to get it to the consistency we like. I also grated some fresh Parmesan in at the very end. Served with French bread and a salad. Super fast and everyone loved it. Warning: it does make a LOT! I fed 2 big eaters and 2 rational proportion eaters and we still had about 1/2 left. It was promptly eaten up for lunch though!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made as written - curry paste I had made it way too spicy- otherwise delicious! Will cut back curry next time

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If you are looking for a deeper flavour in your standard Bolognese recipe - try my favourite secret ingredient: Kecap Manis (sweet soy sauce). A dash of it doesn't make it taste Asian at all, but it does add a depth and richness of flavour that goes perfectly with deeper tomato and/or red wine pasta sauces.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Used a can of fire roasted diced tomatoes instead of sun dried. Delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this last night as written, and it was such a fun twist on pasta with meat sauce, which we have frequently. It quick and delicious and my husband and I were both excited about the leftovers for lunch!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Made this last night, with few modifications. Used dark meat turkey instead of beef - i used a combination of butter and olive oil to make up for the extra fat. I added the pasta (cavatappi) in stages until the sauce ratio was what we like - guessing i used maybe 2/3 of the pasta. The whole pound would have been way too much. 
Added some spinach to the last 5 minutes of simmer time before the pasta. 
We liked this recipe! It’s pretty easy to do and relatively quick - although like all NYT recipes that I’ve tried, everything takes longer than estimated.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used chili crisp instead of curry because that's what I had on hand. Delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can you substitute cochujang for red curry paste?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Lois I had the same thought! And it turned iut out very well

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Loved the Red Curry addition! I didn't think it tasted too Asian at all. I used half Italian sausage which also adds a lot of complexity and half ground chicken, delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I plan to use perhaps pre-soaked/cooked brown lentils in place of the beef to make vegetarian. Can anyone recommend a brand of red curry paste?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If you are making this recipe with brown lentils, the brand of curry paste probably doesn't matter.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: We subbed in gojuchang for red curry paste and used linguine— we loved it! It was a really good weeknight 30-minute recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The quintessential American art of ruining a traditional Bologna dish for the sake of saving time. Barbaric. You can naturally make any concoctions u like, but do not insult a whole nation calling it ‘Bolognese’ please!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
No one called this traditional bolognese; there are other traditional recipes on this very site.

The beauty of America is that it's a melting pot of cultures. Maybe try the recipe instead of getting caught up in the name. It's not that serious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Ay Yai Yai Roll. They called it a bolognese. They used the word. Words have meanings. This is not remotely a bolognese. It’s like an abomination such as an espresso martini. A martini is a martini. Feel free to make an espresso cocktail, but why use a name that just confuses things?  This is actually closer to what has been called American Chop Suey. The writer probably didn’t want to use that name because it’s so déclassé.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: So good. We made as written using the full 3 tbsp of chili paste and then sprinkling with red pepper flakes. This isn’t an Italian dish don’t try to turn it back into one. The Thai red curry paste makes this dish. Everyone went back for seconds!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I added a rib of celery and a couple tablespoons of tomato paste, and cut back on both the cream (used just 1/4 cup) and the curry paste (used just 1.5 tablespoons) and it was delicious. This was a perfect dish to make on a night that kid sports take up most of the dinner prep time of night. I made the sauce as directed up until it was time to let it simmer, at which point I covered the pot and placed in the oven at 225F for 90 minutes until we got home from swim practice. We all devoured this!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Would half-and-half work instead of the heavy cream?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: What do you mean by "work?" Of course half-and-half would not cause this recipe to be a failure. Even milk would "work." Orange juice would not.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Very good, quick and easy to make. 
Tip: Add 1 TB of the Thai red chili paste first to taste as some brands can be very hot. 
We reduced the cream to 1/2 cup and also added 1/3 cube of beef bouillon for flavor. 
Will use this on repeat.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was a tasty, easy pasta dish that was on the table in less than an hour.  I used 2 1/2 T of Thai curry paste, ground Italian sausage (mild), and added 3 sun dried tomatoes with the oils and Italian herbs, some minced garlic, and GF quinoa and brown rice fusilli.  It was a hit meal!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I used Ellen's suggestion and got Calabrian chilly paste - worked great! I could only find the whole peppers but ground them up and added them. Nice heat. 

For @Blueberry Girl and others: You can always add less heavy cream! I almost never add the full amount. But this gave a nice offset to the heat and rounded out the flavor for a quick-cooking recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I was initially put off by the idea of an entire cup of cream.  But I found that 1/3 cup was plenty. If you are trying to stretch this to feed a crowd, you may want more.  But we thought it was excellent with just 1/3 cup.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: 1/3 cup of cream might be plenty for you.... but not for everyone.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was outrageously good, especially when you consider how easy it was to make. To save time, I briefly pulsed the onion and carrots in the Cuisinart to finely chop them. I ended up using  2  1/2 TBS of Thai chili paste... probably could have used 3, but it was fine. I added a can of petite diced tomatoes as well as a few chopped sun dried tomatoes in oil that I had left over in the fridge. I used about 2/3 cup of cream, and that was just right. Will definitely make again!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was super easy and quick. Flawless recipe if you have young kids (just use a mild version of the curry paste like the Thai Kitchen brand).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: As a Marcella Hazan Bolognese devotee I only tried this because Mr. Spouse forwarded it to  me. Made it last night per directions, using 3 tbl. of curry paste. HE LOVED IT. So, it's a hit and delicious even if you don't have time for the Hazan version. The cream made it special.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I've been bastardizing my spaghetti sauces and chilis with madras curry powder for over 40 years.  Just a teaspoon or two.  My kids love it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is the easiest most delicious dinner ever. Could not recommend more highly. Also good with Spaghetti Squash. I also made it without the cream/reduced cream and both turned out incredibly well.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Super easy, does what it says on the tin, but make sure you’re tasting the sauce and MODIFY ALONG THE WAY. It could just be the brand of red curry paste I had (hot Thai kitchen) but I got next to no spice with 3 tablespoons. Added red pepper flakes at the end. Also I’m a big believer in adding sugar to tomato sauces so of course I recommend that here. Not a ton just some.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Sugar is necessary in tomato sauce only if the tomatoes are excessively acidic. Good quality canned tomatoes are not. And, of course the brand of red curry paste will affect the spice level. If you got next to no spice, perhaps you added "just some, not a ton."

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: i tried this mostly as written (except just half the recipe as we are only 2), and it was very good! i subbed half-and-half and only had crushed tomatoes vs. tomato sauce. i’m not understanding the complaints about an asian flavor due to the curry paste, but mine had none so i assume it’s either concern from folks who haven’t actually made the recipe or it’s highly dependent on which brand you use.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly, and it was fabulous. I made Billy Parisi's homemade pasta cut as pappardelle, and it was a perfect accompaniment. The broader style of noodles allowed the sauce to stick to the noodles. It tasted like a gourmet meal that took such a short time. Would serve to guests. We used 3TBL of the curry paste, and it was spicy. My husband said he'd try two next time to make wine pairing easier.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I have made this twice. The second time I omitted the cream. It was really good, both times, but I prefer it without the cream.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was just ok.  And it did kind of taste like Thai food.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: was ok, definitely less carrots for the kids

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Or just grate them. The kids will never know!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Forty-five minutes to one hour  on a fast simmer with a traditional Bolananse ingredients , sofrito very fine, maybe pasata in place of whole tomatoes,  say from Marcella, is pretty good as it is.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This kind of tasted like sloppy Joe mix to me. It was nice because I haven’t had sloppy joes since elementary school. It was not quite bolognese, but it was still pretty good. Not sure I’d make it again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
* Maesri curry sauce
* Reduce heat when adding onions
* Used 2.5 TBSP Thai Red Curry paste & was great! I'll try 3 TBSP next time. 
* In lieu of curry: use a heavily reduced Tomato paste such as Cirio.
* In lieu of heavy cream: Try milk (a la Marcella Hazan), preceded by white wine. 
* Quick bolognese sauce: sauté onions, grated carrots, minced mushrooms, then add 2 TBSPs of tomato paste & sauté together til tomato paste is fragrant. Add splashes of red wine to deglaze and simmer a few min.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I followed the recipe as written. I'm not the best cook, and every love it! The perfect comfort food, neither bland nor super spicy. Next time I might up the red curry paste from 2.5 to 3.5 Tbs. Thanks for the recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My Sicilian grandmother used to sauté a couple of diced anchovy fillets with her onions to add depth. When I’m in a hurry, I use a teaspoon of fish sauce instead, added right before I start simmering. I also add a pinch of ground fresh nutmeg.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I have not made this dish. It sounds yummy, but it has very little in common with actual Bolognese--which is named so for a reason. Let's give it a different name.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: You can call it whatever you want. I'm happy with "weeknight Bolognese."

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Not having red curry sauce I put some ground red pepper (or red pepper flakes)
I also added maybe 1/2 c red wine and reduced before the tomato sauce.  Big hit at the ski house

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was incredibly easy and tasty. I didn't find that the sauce needed an entire cup of cream so just kept pouring until it seemed right to me, which was about 1/2 cup. I like spice but 3T curry paste didn't do it, so I added about 2tsp of chopped [jarred] Calabrian chiles and it was just right. Will keep this in the rotation for sure.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: While cooking this I (of course) sampled everything at each step of the preparation. I found that the last addition - heavy cream - unnecessary. When using high-fat ground beef, the sauce was plenty rich to begin with. Adding cream just made it heavier without adding much more in the way of complexity. Ironically, it seemed to diffuse it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Used a whole tin of Maesri red curry paste cuz I like the heat, and it delivered! Didn't use a full cup of heavy cream (2/3? 3/4?), but something along those lines is necessary to temper the rest of the dish. Next time I might try coconut milk to really lean into the Thai side of the dish. Maybe some fish sauce? I also deglazed with a splash of mirin, but will try Shaoxing wine next time. Last tip: stick to a smaller pasta--used rigatoni and combining in a 5 qt pot at the end got a bit dicey.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
This recipe is amazing! An unlikely but delicious marriage of things I typically have in the house.
So glad I tried it - no modifications and it’s great.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was SO easy to make on a Monday night where we just wanted some cozy pasta. The only change I made was halve the number of carrots in the recipe because I'm not crazy about a ton of them in bolognese. I loved how the red curry paste enhanced everything.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I cannot say enough about the ease of the sauce, and how versatile it is.  We normally use jarred sauce because of time, but this came together very easily for a weeknight dinner.  I didn't taste the thai curry I used, it just tasted yummy!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this tonight, and it was absolutely delicious!  A great substitute for the tired old spaghetti dish I make all of the time.  I added three tablespoons of curry paste, and the full amount of cream.  Is it the healthiest dish?  No.  But it is delicious and I will make it again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My husband and I loved this. We used a curry paste that had zero heat (even though the packaging stated otherwise) and needed to add some red chili flakes to compensate, so I’ve since done some research to find a spicier paste for our next attempt.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Added an extra carrot and a third tablespoon of the red curry paste. A bit sweeter, a bit more tangy, and a bit more filling. Love this recipe!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made it with one tablespoon of the curry paste. Good flavor and spice was tolerable for me. Im not a spicy fan but I like the flavor. I wish I could post a pic! Great recipe!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Tasty, but be very careful with the heat. Even with beef with a higher fat content, 4-5 minutes in a pot preheated on high heat scorched my first attempt pretty badly. Undercuts the prep speed of this recipe a bit, but a slightly lower, more controlled heat let me cook the beef and vegetables more evenly without adding a heavy note of carbon to the flavor profile.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If you want good chili or bolognese put some Worcestershire sauce. No one can pick it out. There’s the umami. The cream is essential! Personally I like to let it set overnight in the fridge to deepen the flavor. But real bolognese is more complex with other types of meat added. One trend lately in restaurants is using wagyu beef. Yuck. Turns it into baby food and adds no flavor.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I always add Worcestershire Sauce to my Bolognese! I also add a good sprinkle of celery salt. (Texas people look away now) I add the same 2 umami ingredients to my Chili con Carne :-)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Tried it.  Best Italian sauce this side of Mumbai.  But too schizophrenic for this recent visitor to Rome.  Better sauces can be had by simply shopping wisely for better canned tomatoes and adding a handful of herbs.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Liked it, but it missed depth. During simmering I added some bay leaf, thyme, garlic and a splash of red wine, which did the trick perfectly.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made with a mix of tomato paste and Harissa as the comments suggested (instead of curry paste). Delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Really enjoyed this. I used a can of crushed tomatoes rather than tomato soup. Next time I will make sure the beef is caramelised more.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Sarah, it doesn't call for tomato soup; it calls for tomato sauce

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Weirdly soupy? Not sure what happened but don’t add the water to it!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’m a huge one-pot-meal person and love anything with pasta, so when I saw this recipe a few months ago, I had to try it. I’m all about something easy on a weeknight, but it must be delicious!  This dish can cook up in well under an hour. The red curry paste adds a nice zingy spiciness. I’ve improvised several different ways - it’s an easy recipe to memorize - and it’s always YUMMY!  My family, whose pallets are maturing with age, really likes the spiciness from the curry and the creaminess from the cream…winning combo! We always serve with a buratta ball for extra creamy cheesy goodness!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made as directed. Used 2 Tbsp Thai red curry paste Too spicy for my wife. I loved it. Will cut curry paste in half next time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I love this recipe! It's simple, yet so flavorful and easy for busy nights. It makes a ton of pasta, so we can eat it for a few meals. My only advice is to be careful of the red curry paste, as different brands have different levels of spice. Start with 1 tablespoon, then go from there!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My husband said this was the best bolognese he's ever had! Going into a regular rotation.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: What is canned tomato sauce? Passata? (Australian here!)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Jessica R
You could use a can of Leggo’s tomato puree. I think that would be fine. I’m also in Australia and was wondering the same thing.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: A solid recipe, but unrealistic that the listed prep time is just "10 minutes."  I have made a similar pasta source for 40 years now.  It is inconceivable that one can cook 1 pound of hamburger meat (cutting, as the recipe appropriately calls for, the large bits of meat into "tiny bits"), finely peeling and chopping onions and carrots, adding numerous other items, ensuring that the recipe comes to a boil and changing color, and, finally, boiling the pasta noodles, in 10 minutes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The prep time is chopping the onion and carrots; everything else is cook time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It is inconceivable that you read that the recipe would be completed in 10 minutes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Using red curry paste means this is not a bolognese. If you want to deepen the flavor - do it like the Italians (the people who invented the dish) do and just add tomato paste

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Not the same thing.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Our go-to "fast bolognese" recipe, also from NYT cooking, uses worcestershire sauce to add the umami depth. We often choose a larger pasta than spaghetti, and now I want to add some cream at the end! https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1020996-easy-spaghetti-with-meat-sauce

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I modified pretty heavily - added anchovy paste, pinch of nutmeg, pinch of sugar, tomato paste, red pepper flakes, and splash of mushroom broth.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
We've been in AirBnB, with all the kitchen limitations that suggests. Looking for simple versions of familiar favourites. I've cooked a million bologneses (sp?), and loved them all in their own way, but this was by far the best we've had. 
Rich, deep, verging on uxurious, but not tipping over. Only change was simmering longer than suggested (1 hour vs. 10 mins) and only a couple of tbsp of cream vs  a cup. Two tsp of red curry sauce vs. three. Genevieve Ko with an all-time NYT classic.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Simmer the sauce for an hour. Then I added half a cup of cream to the sauce. Then I poured the sauce over the pasta.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Easy & absolutely delicious! Made as directed. Didn’t even wait for the pasta to be made before I downed about a cup of it. Used 2T of the red curry paste (Thai Kitchen brand) and didn’t notice any “heat” or unusual flavor as some others have mentioned. Will make this again and again and again…

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: A-ma-zing! The dish is so simple that it tricks you into thinking it needs more. In my humble opinion it is perfect as it is. The noodle is key in this dish. If it doesn’t have a scoop or a twist the dish will fall flat. Highly recommend this one!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made exactly as directed. This is very good reheated, so don't worry that it makes a big batch. Sprinkled a generous amount of fresh Thai basil on the reheated leftovers and that really gave it a flavor pop.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was delicious. I added some beef bouillon and also substituted oat milk for cream since I can’t have dairy. I let it evaporate a bit longer since it was more liquidy because of that. I didn’t have tomato sauce so I mixed so a lot of tomato paste with water. So rich and creamy. Served it with homemade pasta!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Subbed Curry paste with Harissa paste (I make my own & vary the type & quantity of dried chilis.).  Used Greek yogurt , cottage cheese, almond milk mixture in place of heavy cream.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I'm not a fan of this recipe at all.  Strange flavor combo.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I found the combo of tomato and curry paste a little weird, slightly bitter and acrid when I first tasted it. I was regretting making the dish, but I added a pretty large amount of salt and some MSG (if you don’t have it, you could add bouillon or better than bouillon) to get rid of the bitterness. That worked, and it ended up being a huge hit, even with my Italian-American husband! If you’re finding the taste slightly off-putting, I would recommend the same.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made it as written, including the red curry paste.  Easy and delicious!  Will keep in weeknight rotation.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Why would you not just make a proper Bolognese ragu but batch cook enough to freeze weeknight portions??

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Curry paste? Really? It may be something but it’s not bolognese.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Lovely recipe! Used Marcella Hazan's tomato sauce recipe for this and used harissa paste in place of the red curry paste.

author: Genevieve Ko
