commentBody: I made this with harissa in place of the curry paste and it was fire. 10/10 taste for effort ratio.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I had a pound of pasture-raised, 85/25 ground beef that needed to be cooked and I was cooked from a long week, so I needed something simple. This recipe hit the spot for a fast and yummy meal, and helped me clean out the fridge! I only had a red onion, which worked out fine, and after the carrots and

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This came together quickly and was very tasty. More of an elevated hamburger helper than bolognese though… The only change I made was deglazing the pan with a splash of wine before adding the tomato sauce. This made 8 servings for us, so next time I’ll either cut in half or freeze half of the meat mixture before adding the cream. I did 2 TB of curry paste but next time I’ll do a little more. My husband and two kids happily ate this, so it’s a keeper for sure.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can it be frozen

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Interesting to find out how this will turn out by substituting the red curry paste with Harissa or Gochujang.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Left off the curry and it was very good:  creamy, easy to make.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used the Mekhala Thai red curry from Whole Foods and it turned out delicious. I wasn’t sure what to expect but the curry added a subtle spice without tasting like straight curry. My end result was a bit salty though and I’m not sure if it’s because of the paste or just user error but I would watch out for that!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Didn’t have curry paste/didn’t trust the idea- so used a tiny bit of turmeric, some Italian seasoning, some paprika, garlic salt. Splash of red wine. also had like a cup of marinara leftover from something else so used it up. Didn’t have cream so splashed some half and half. Truly a great “use a bunch of random stuff” recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Why would you want to make this recipe when you don't have most other the ingredients that are called for?????

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Loved this!!! Added 5 cloves of garlic to the onion and carrot mix. Also added about 1/2 cup of red wine and let the sauce simmer for 30 mins. Used the full 3 tbs of red curry paste. Topped with Parmesan. Will make again for sure.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wow. Great flavor, and so quick to put together. This recipe delivered! I admit I was skeptical at first with the red curry. Used 2T. There's a hint of spice but there wasn't an overt curry taste. Though I imagine the sauce flavor might differ depending on which brand of red curry paste you use. I also used pork instead of beef. Perfect weeknight meal!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Loved this easy  recipe. Used turkey meat. Also used fancy single origin red pepper flakes instead of red curry.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: so delicious! used Kroger brand Thai red curry (maybe 2.5 tbsp) - a little bit of heat and no curry flavor

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I can’t believe how good this dish was. I ended up cutting it in half since I was just cooking for myself and I will have at least three servings possibly four. The red curry paste was perfect. I ended up putting in 2 tablespoons for half a recipe wished I had a little bit more beef, but it still was really good. I only put in 3 tablespoons of the cream and for my taste it was perfect.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Making this for the 2nd time. I dhave canned tomato sauce at home at the moment, but I do have Rao’s marinara sauce. Wondering it that would work as a substitution?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I have made this multiple times and my family loves it.  Recommend using some calabrian chili to up the spice level -- its fruitiness pairs well with the red thai curry paste!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is a fantastic recipe. So easy and so delicious. 2 TBS paste and it is flavorful and rich but doesn’t taste like curry. Grate the onion and carrot instead of dicing. Much easier and better result.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Love this recipe, especially using the pipette pasta as opposed to the tagliatelle that I usually use. Searing the hamburg was a great step.  I added equal parts of celery, which I like in a Bolognese.  Also can’t beat how quick this all comes together. This will definitely be my go-to Bolognese.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this with Moose burger.  I doubled the curry paste and used a large (28 oz) can of San Marzano Tomatoes from Costco.  It was quick and delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Don’t call it bolognese, but it was great. We decided it was like a very elevated version of hamburger helper. The red curry paste definitely takes it out of bolognese territory, but the flavors were balanced and rich, and it was easy for a weeknight.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: A non-spicy substitution so my kids would eat it: I swapped the red curry paste for ~1 tbsp tomato paste and ~1 tbsp maple syrup (plus some garlic powder). The flavor was great!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Cleaning out my freezer, I had a half pound of hamburger, so I tried this recipe last night -- technically, a half recipe. Wow, this was so much better than I expected. I'm having some of the leftovers for lunch right now

I used a cast iron ski that was probably a bit too big. Wound up adding a bit of extra tomato sauce, and most of the pasta water at the end. The seared hamburger was a little terrifying in process, but worth it. The Thai brand red curry paste wasn't hot-spicy...delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Very good. Don't skimp on the curry paste. I also added the Calabrian hot peppers. Wonderful depth.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this per recipe and it was delicious! Made six generous servings. Leftovers were perfectly matched with peas.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I had a packet of Thai red curry paste in my cupboard so I gave this a go, but hybridized it — added two stalks of finely diced celery along with the carrot and onion, and also added some finely diced prosciutto, since I had it on hand and wanted to use it up. Was mostly curious about the red curry paste — it works! Adds some complexity as though this cooked longer than it did. It also adds some Asian spice hints that are different, but not in an overwhelming way, more of a whisper. The cream hides it. This isn’t a Bolognese sauce as I know it, but it is a very nice weeknight pasta.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This needs to be renamed. "Bolognese", it is not. I followed directions religiously (as a new cook, I do not play around). It was awful. I have successfully produced palatable Bolognase following other recipes. It is not difficult.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this last night, was fantastic.  Even my mother, who would never entertain the thought of eating curry paste (and I didn't tell her it was in the recipe), really enjoyed it, citing it as one of the best dishes I've made.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This one really did not work for us. The curry and cream gave this an off-putting flavor that rendered the dish inedible. Weird as so highly rated. I am not sure how to fix beyond subtracting the curry.  All said, I love curry but not in this dish.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was good for how little time it required. I used 2 tbsp of red curry paste (just basic Thai Kitchen brand) and couldn’t pick it out in the finished pasta, so it had the intended effect. The only thing I might try different next time is using Italian sausage rather than ground beef.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: 10/10 recipe. It is so good. If you’re unsure, start with 1 Tablespoon of curry paste and 1-2 Tablespoons of tomato paste.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Added one bell pep for extra veggie. Used ground turkey + a teaspoon of bacon fat since it's what I had. So yummy.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
For those wondering whether to use thai curry - use Gochujang, which is not as potent as tahi but still adds some heat

2 tablespoons is enough to cater to people who don't have a taste for good that's too spicey while still adding depth

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I sent this recipe home for the kids to cook while I was at an appointment. They managed it quite well, and it was delicious. A homeroom as far as we are all concerned.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Used gochujang instead of red curry because that’s what was on hand. Absolutely delicious! Will make again using the same substitution.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My kids (10 and 7) call this dish bolognyum because they love it so much. It’s quickly become a weeknight staple that is quick and easy!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I just felt it was missing some depth somehow—felt like it fell short on Asian flavors and Italian flavors both, and my brain didn’t know where it was lacking. My husband said it was solid, but it just wasn’t my thing.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this as is and loved it. I debated using the curry paste but decided to just try it and see what all the fuss was about. I think if I was a guest being served this I wouldn’t be able to put my finger on it, like a really good secret ingredient. I’m glad I tried it. This will go into the rotation in our house.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: You can riff on this recipe and make it to your taste. I deglazed the pot with red wine after sauteing the onions and added a heavy teaspoon of both Thai red curry paste and gochugang,plus about half of a small can of tomato paste. I loved the taste, like it had simmered all day.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I made this recipe per instructions. It was delicious. Next time I will add another teaspoon of red curry paste and about one-third less cream. 

Also my wife, who is quite spice-avoidant, didn’t detect any heat whatsoever. This isn’t an issue.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Believe the hype - this is simply outstanding. Two notes to share - I swapped gochujang in for the curry paste, as I had it in the fridge and like the flavor. It worked beautifully. And I cut back the cream to 3/4 cup and found it plenty rich/creamy still. I’ll probably try a 1/2 cup next time and see if that’s sufficient. Overall - cannot wait to make this again!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: In sum, this is a genius recipe

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My wife and I make this with crushed Calabrian chiles to keep it Italian and oh my it’s so good. You don’t even have to cook the paste or jarred peppers off you can just mix it right in with the tomato sauce.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Could you make this with ground turkey? What would you change?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: A great midweek short cut! You can also sub a big dollop of sour cream for the heavy cream.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: excellent! very creamy

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: For kosher folks, use Beyond or Impossible ground "beef" and then you can use the cream. Soy or almond milk or Rich's frozen producrt never taste right as a substitute for real dairy.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The red curry addition is fabulous.  Great recipe, definitely a keeper.  I added a tiny bit of garlic to the meat while it was cooking.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Question for the NYT hive - can someone chime in about using Fish Sauce in a dish like this?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I love Thai curry and I keep three kinds of paste in the fridge. That said, while I liked this dish, I wasn't wowed by it. I used less paste than I would for my taste; my wife doesn't care for spicy foods. But the curry taste still dominated. I appreciate the effort and the simplicity, but I'll skip this recipe next time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Depends upon the brand of red curry paste. Thai Kitchen is brand I find in my local supermarket. It's actually quite mild. I started buying Mae Ploy from the ubiquitous online megaretailer. Much better and much less expensive per ounce. But it's 3-4 times punchier, by our taste buds. So I'd use recipe's quantity of Thai Kitchen, but only ~1/4 as much Mae Ploy. I substituted some tomato paste for the curry paste I omitted, and it turned out wonderful! 5* (also added some dried Italian seasoning)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Kevin B Having the same issue with Mae Ploy. But I have FOMO so I used the suggested amount anyway 😂 I wanna taste all those aromatics!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: What would substitute for the cup of cream?  Tx

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I often use coconut milk as a substitute for cream.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I make a similar version with pasilla chili powder to give a rich, earthy flavor. I’ll try this as written and see how it comes out.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Terrific recipe.  I seared/carmelised the beef and vegetables separately then combined them with tomato sauce.  It's passata bottling season here in South Australia and all of our Italian neighbours have so much they give it away to each other (same with plums and lemons).  Sweet freshness.
Used a yellow curry paste since that's what I had in the fridge. Turned out ultra-good.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used carrots and an onion, substituted everything else. It was just okay. But the bourbon (one of the substitutions) was stellar.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: GOOD! Sub'd the Curry with Tomato Achaar and the cream for 2 heaping TB of Greek Yogurt. Cooked it down a longer then called for (got sidetracked- but caused no issue), wound up infusing the flavors very well. Used pasta water to thin @ end. Garnished with about 1c of thinly sliced baby spinach leaf strips (a handful for 4 plates) when served. Everyone loved!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Where I live, tomato sauce means catsup. Instead, I get tubes of Italian tomato or vegetable paste and then add pasta water.  Any idea  how much I should substitute to achieve the same effect?  Thanks.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Use passata or a can of diced tomatoes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used super concentrated tomato paste because I ran out of curry paste. Made up for the loss of heat with red pepper flakes. I will make it again because it's one of the only things my children have eaten without complaining. I still think it's missing a certain something. Maybe balsamic.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: So delicious! We're eating yesterday's leftovers, and we both agree this recipe makes 6 big servings, or even 8 "sane" servings.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I had no real success with this recipe. I mean if you are  single digit kid it is fine. First, I found the recipe to be vague. What size carrots? Ihad baby carrots and sort of guessed how many would eqqual two, size unknown, adult carrots. Second, what kind of red curry paste/ I used atleast three tablespoons and while it added some flavor it added no heat. Is it a particular kind that produces heat? As a result the dish was bland but rich. My wife hated the carrot pieces.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Outstanding weeknight meal. The whole family went back for seconds.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Amazing. Used Country Crock plant based heavy cream and 1 1/2 T of gochujang. Having second round tonight.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was very tasty.  I used ground Turkey and some diced mushrooms.  I didn’t have heave cream, so I used milk.  I would use 3 tbs.  Of curry next time.  It’s a little too mild for me.  I like heat.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Very easy and tasty! I used only 2 tablespoons of the curry paste and it wasn't spicy at all, fyi. Would recommend!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made as written. Easy, quick enough for a weeknight, and delish!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was really good.  Had to make some substitutions, but it didn’t suffer for them.  Small jar of crushed tomatoes, part of a tube of tomato paste and water for the tomato sauce.  Non-dairy sour cream for the cream, with extra pasta water to thin it out.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Don’t know about curry paste but way too much tomato. I’ll give it a try with less.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was a huge hit and definitely will be a part of our weeknight dinner rotation!  Simple, quick and flavorful.  Everyone enjoyed this!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I guess you could call this a Bolognese, but since it does not have the olive oil, wine, or even the celery to accompany the carrots, much less some decent San Marzano tomatoes or parmesan cheese, I am not sure why you would bother.  Why not just put in the coconut milk instead of the cream and call it a Thai dish? There is a Tuscan dish called Penne Strasciate, which is quicker than a full Bolognese and actually tastes similar.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this exactly as written and it was excellent. A bit spicey, warm and comforting on a chilly week night. However, next time I will reduce the amount of cream. I thought a full cup was too much and diluted the complex flavor of the sauce. Overall, though, this recipe is a keeper!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is a wonderful recipe and incredibly easy to make. My only modifications were adding the celery and nutmeg from Marcella Hazan’s original bolognese recipe.  Perfection at a fraction of the time, no one has to know!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I Loved this!  I didn’t realize until halfway through preparation that I didn’t have any red curry paste.  I substituted with 1Tablespoon of Sambal Oelek chili paste and 1/2 tsp of pickle masala, my new favorite condiment.  I withheld cream to 1/2 cup. The final result was delicious!  It will become a new favorite, no doubt.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used the Thai kitchen brand red curry paste (curry paste can be so variable brand to brand) which isn’t really a spicy product so I was able to customize the heat level with chili flakes. It had a long cooked taste and was really delicious with the carrots. I froze half of it before I put the cream in and am very excited to eat it again!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: To Phoebe: How do you roast your tomato paste? Thanks

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: To be honest, if I used 20% fat ground beef and a cup of heavy cream, any Bolognese I made would taste sumptuous, without or without the addition of red curry paste. The real talent is how to make this dish taste sumptuous with 5% fat and half fat crème fraiche for example, because the NYT version is my idea of a huge treat due to the calorific value (or you eat microscopic portions which I've no wish to do) and not something for mid week after the gym.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Most of us know we don't eat something like this every day.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Used about 1.5 TBS of red curry paste, 1/2# dark and breast turkey, and 2% greek yogurt instead of cream. Delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It would be nice to know what red curry paste was used in the recipe? I used Mae Ploy and the bolognese was very spicy (which I liked) but tasted mainly like the curry paste (which I liked less).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Veronika
I used the Thai Kitchen brand available at our local, average grocery store. It’s hardly spicy at all and blended completely into the sauce. It did not add an Asian flavor to the taste.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: For those of us with severe shellfish allergies, adding hidden ingredients to meat dishes can be a real problem.  I’m seeing more and more dishes using Thai paste or fish sauce for umami and it’s a problem!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Anyone with a severe allergy should carefully check ingredients when making anything.  If you are the cook surely that is what you would do.  I am not quite sure of this concern as my red curry paste doesn't have any fish in it at all, and my fish sauce only has anchovies.  I didn't realize anchovies triggered shellfish allergies as they aren't shellfish.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Obviously, anyone making food for you should be notified of your allergy, but surely you're not saying that people should stop including in their recipes anything you might be allergic to?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Easy to make, especially when I don’t have time to make traditional bolognese. I liked the flavor, but the hint of Thai seasoning, was good but not Italian! I will try roasting tomato paste or sun dried tomatoes as a commenter recommended.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
This turned out phenomenal. Complex, delightful, rich and satisfying. Will be making again for guests, but with a longer simmer for tenderness.

Doubled the recipe. Used Maesri red curry paste(full 4oz can), substituted coconut cream for the cream, and used gluten free jasmine rice pasta.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly with excellent results. Might try it with a bit more curry paste next time. The relatively quick preparation time only adds to the pleasure. Two yums up!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I would replace the red curry paste with gochujang. I think it would make the whole dish more moreish.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Perhaps it was the Thai curry paste I used, but my wife (lovingly) referred to this Pasta Tikka Masala.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I substituted plant-based "beef" crumbles and used whole-wheat pasta - and it worked well! I also substituted tomato paste, adding curry powder, cayenne pepper and smoked paprika to taste.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I didn't have red curry paste so I used medium spicy gochujang, and rigatoni and it was delicious!  It was fast and delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made it per the recipe w/3 Tbs of paste, plenty spicy.  It was creamier than expected but delicious.  Topped it with fresh grated Parmesan and it was delicious.  I did use Sfoglini Pasta, the  cascatelli shape and loved them!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I made a few substitutions due to what I had on hand:
I used 93% lean ground beef plus two thick slices of bacon cut up and placed in the skillet beneath the ground beef.
Instead of heavy cream, I used 1/2 cup each whole milk plain Greek yogurt and milk.
I used gojuchang instead of curry paste.
I mixed only 8 oz of pasta into the sauce (pan was too small), served the rest of the pasta on the side, and had plenty of leftovers. All 4 of us liked the high sauce-pasta ratio!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Fabulous!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: We made it the other night exactly as written, it was delicious. This result is rich, hearty and the depth of flavor is extremely satisfying . I could eat the whole pot! Than you!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I did not have red curry paste,  so I used harissa.  Used a 660ml bottle of strained tomates La San Marzano (coulis) instead of tomatoes in a can. 

Absolutely delicious and, oh, so easy!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Used white miso and sundried tomatoes since I didn't have the curry paste.  It came out great!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I forgot the carrots, halved the pasta and halved the heavy cream. The result and leftovers are well worth the effort! I’m excited to try it again with some of the suggestions like Calabrian chili paste, tomato paste and red pepper flakes, etc.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It's surprisingly good and very fast/ easy.  First out used 2 tsp. curry powder and fine.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Was hesitant using red curry paste and cooked without it. Enjoyed it but will try the paste next time and gage the difference! Btw the true Bolognese recipe I followed from the Times is a labor of love and a true hit. My father overcame his Parkinson’s symptoms  and ate an entire bowl on his own over the holidays.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: What are commenters' thoughts on using a different curry paste, such a massaman?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: So good and easy!  Made as directed and my kids and wife loved it

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used harissa paste & curry powder instead of red curry paste and it was phenomenal!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I was disappointed. Using curry paste was an interesting idea and it didn’t taste like curry but I added red wine which helped but it was too bland for my taste. Adding red pepper flakes helped.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made it. I liked it. But not as much as the all-afternoon, even better next day recipe!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can't wait to try this one exactly as written!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious. I used a generous tablespoon of both the Thai chili paste (that’s all that was left in the jar) and tomato paste. This gave the sauce a really rich deep flavor. Big hit with my family!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: So delicious and easy. I only had 8 oz of tomato sauce, so I used half tomato sauce and half jarred marinara, and it worked perfectly.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This recipe was excellent. We all gobbled up our first servings, then gobbled up our seconds! The red curry paste (I used about 2.25 T of Mae Ploy) gives it just the right amount of heat and pizzazz. My husband thought it was too spicy the first night, but the leftovers weren't hot at all. I used 2% milk and butter instead of heavy cream, and it worked great. Looking forward to the next time I make this.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Absolutely incredible, no notes. Great flavours and so simple to make.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wow! This is a wonderfully, tasty, fast dinner! Thank you!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: So quick and easy! I added two cloves of fresh garlic and some dried fennel seeds in the ground beef and it’s amazing. Grate some fresh Parmesan into it too. Delish delish!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This recipe is fast becoming my weekly favourite. What an irreverent and delightful way to make a ragù taste good, quickly. Bravo!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Loved the simplicity of this dish. We added approximately 5tbsp of red curry paste, and a little more salt. Next time? Adding spinach. Can’t wait!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My husband and I loved this. We doubled the meat the second time we made it just to have more protein, and left everything else the same, and I thought the ratios still worked well. Quality of the red curry paste you use matters a lot here, so use an authentic one for the best flavor.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is an incredible and unique take on a Bolognese sauce, and it's very quick. I can see there's some controversy over the red curry paste in the comments, but I honestly thought it gave it a very delicious kick. It might not be for everyone, but it was a hit in my house. I also added some Italian seasoning to it. I used cavatappi pasta instead and only about 12 oz instead of the full 16 oz, and I think the sauce to pasta ratio was perfect there.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is delicious! I added just 1Tb of red curry paste because I have a 5 year old and almost-2-year-old and the kids loved it. I would have preferred it a little spicier for myself so I bet 2Tb would have been perfect. The curry paste adds a depth to the sauce that the tomatoes can’t in such a short amount of time. So good, make this!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Didn’t have red curry paste so I used anchovy paste and a tablespoon of chili crisp- was perfect!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Esy, tasty and a great alternative to the Bolognese sauce.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
1 tablespoon max of curry paste for family, maybe 3/4 tablespoons. 

1 medium size yellow onion is plenty

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was so good and will be a new staple in our house. We used 2 tbsp of the red curry paste which felt like enough of a kick while still being universally appealing to spice level tolerances. It's not a typical "spicy" taste in any sort of overwhelming way, more depth of flavor.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this exactly as written. My boyfriend and I both agreed that it somehow had a seafood flavor, almost like a crawfish étouffée. Curious if anyone else had this experience? Flavor wasn’t bad, but definitely odd. We both thought it was much better leftover the next night, after flavors had mingled and no longer had the crawfish flavor.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Some curry pastes contain shrimp paste as an ingredient. This might be the cause.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’ve made my sauce for years with a few liberal shakes of Franks hot sauce (I find Tabasco too vinegary). It doesn’t taste right if it DON’T do that now

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: We don’t like curry paste, what about mild chili crisp instead?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: How could you make a tasty bolognese if you are lactose intolerant?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: You could try subbing lactose free half & half. (Organic Valley is the best, but it's also the priciest. Land O'Lakes makes a good one, too.) To thicken it, take 1/4 cup of it and add some corn starch, whisk it; heat the other 3/4 cup to a simmer, add the 1/4 cup cornstarch mixture, whisk until it thickens slightly. Alternately, you can try coconut cream.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: What could be an alternative to the red curry paste?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Red curry paste IS the alternative!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I like the red curry paste, jazz’s it up in an interesting way. Used 2.5 tablespoons, nice heat but not spicy. Used half and half cause that’s what war in the house and I think richer would have been nice. Also used a meatloaf mix of meats, tasty!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wait! No anchovies??

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made with ground dark meat turkey with added olive oil for the browning. 2T curry paste was plenty. Everyone devoured it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: What can you substitute for heavy cream if you’re dairy free?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Coconut Milk Cream?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made it exactly as written, and thought was delicious!!!  I used three tablespoons, and gave a kick but did not feel it tasted Asian at all.  My teenage son devoured it.  Next time, for my personal preference only., I would sub half and half, or try the cottage cheese hack referenced in the above comment.  Super easy and soooo good.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
This recipe lived up to the hype!

Don't be fooled, at first it doesn't look like much--like the "slumgullion" or other hamburger casseroles of our childhood. However, the red curry paste and liberal amounts of carrot and onion really elevate this dish. Definitely a keeper.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Very tasty—and unlike others, I loved the curry paste flavor, which I found subtle—but next time I’ll double the sauce for a pound of pasta.

If you use an induction stove (and probably if you boil water in an electric kettle before pouring it into your pot), this recipe has you starting the water boiling way too soon. I think it’s assuming a gas range.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I think the recipe assumes each cook will know how long it takes for their equipment to boil water!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made a happy mistake tonight while making this dish for the first time. I grabbed red CHILI paste and not curry paste out of my fridge (they have the same bottle!) and used 2-3 TBSP, plus a large pinch of red pepper flakes. The result was smokey and a little sweet, with a kick of heat.  Thinking next time I’d like to split that addition into a chili/curry paste combo since I have yet to make this recipe in its intended written form. My husband and I both really enjoyed the chili paste version though!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I make this often but I use tomato paste (not curry). My grandmother always said to fry that paste til its brown - THEN you can add the tomatoes or sauce. (And that woman could cook like nobodies business)!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I really love the use of red curry paste here.  I didn’t have tomato sauce, so I used a can of tomato paste thinned with homemade chicken broth.  I browned the tomato paste a little, then added the curry paste as instructed, then added the chicken broth.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I went full NYT Cooking reader on this one, "followed the recipe" but used Rao's marinara instead of tomato sauce, added a nice big clove of garlic and red wine instead of water.  Probably 2.5 tablespoons of the Thai curry paste.  It was outstanding!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: wonderful!   To make it even better, add garlic   sugar   Worcestershire sauce

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used 2 Tbsp gochujang in place of curry paste, because that’s what I had. Also puréed 2/3 cup cottage cheese with 1/3 cup half in half in place of the heavy cream. Hubby is trying to lose weight. Someone always hate something about my meals here, but everyone approved this one.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Did what others suggested

Good tip on the cottage cheese / half and half. I tried that and it worked perfectly!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: At first I was skeptical about the red curry paste, but it worked! It didn't give it an overpowering 'curry' flavour, and it was very delicious. I used 2 tbsp of the Maesri Thai red curry paste brand found in most Asian grocery stores. I also subbed in coconut milk instead of cream for a healthier option. Would make again!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I mean this to be complimentary, but to me it seemed like an “adult hamburger helper.” It was very comforting and the red curry paste added an immense depth of flavor without tasting like “curry.” I don’t have kids, but I could see how this would be a wonderful and quick meal for a whole family with low effort. I did sub the heavy cream for 2/3 C cottage Cheese blended with 1/3 C half and half for a bit more protein / lower fat, and I think that worked out perfectly. I’d make it again, but it is NOT a substitute for true bolognese. I give it 4 stars.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made as advertised and it was quite yummy..the red curry added the richness that happens with a traditional Bolognese but also added a unique flavor. Will be in the rotation

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Simply loved it. Thank you.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I was surprised how kind of normal this was considering the red curry paste. I ended up with way to loose of a sauce. I won't add the water to ribse out the jar next time. I certainly didn't need the pasta water.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I was skeptical of the red curry paste. Born and raised Italian. Not gonna lie, this was delicious. Family loved it especially the younger kids. Planning on making a large batch and freezing portions so all I need to do defrost, toss with pasta and add the cream.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I won’t be making this again. Usually, adding cream to any dish improves its flavour but it only dilutes it here. Plus, the cooking time is off - the carrots were still undercooked even after I gave the sauce an extra ten minutes. In fact, cooking carrots in tomato sauces generally makes them tough. Don’t know what the cookery writer was thinking here.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this with a plants based meat and subbed cream with whole milk.  OMG. Quick, easy, and so delicious. It also made great leftovers. Next time, I think I'll sub the meat with planted-based Italian sausage to kick it up a notch. I think Marcella might be giving me the side eye ... Bless her.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wow, the chili paste gave this such a good flavor. I did not make any substitutions and made it exactly as directed, no complaints.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Didn’t love this. Might have been better without the curry paste. Tasted out of left field for me.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Perfect as made per the recipe. Only adjustment was cutting the amount of pasta in half.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious and easy.  A winner for the whole family!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Cooked this last night and it is awesome!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: when doctoring a bottled sauce like this, i always swish out the can with the dairy, usually milk. won’t using water just make it…watery?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I make a similar dish, instead of beef, I use shrrimp and instead of carrots either okra or spinach.  Delicious over farro or other whole grains

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Yeah, it's not Marcella's Bolognese, which I've made and is wonderful.  BUT, for a quick meal - which  I made as written - it's delicious!  In the rotation, for sure!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Thought it was delicious! Thank you for giving me another idea to feed my teenage boys! I used two tablespoons of curry and it gave just the right amount of heat. I used less heavy cream, too. Quite easy and satisfying. I’ll be making it again!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used Indian Mutton Curry Masala powder— I don’t think Thai curry paste was the intention, but I bet it is also good. Also, I suggest only using half of the pasta—it took away of the delicious sauce.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Deserves every accolade I can think of. This was a big hit with my husband and SO quick and easy!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I didn’t have heavy cream or red curry paste, so I substituted with coconut milk and harissa. Turned out great! Top with parm

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious!! I made it exactly as written, maybe a little less than the cup of cream. 2 1/2 tbsp of the curry paste. The flavor was developed and hearty with no Asian flavor. Everyone absolutely loved it!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
This recipe was so intriguing, as well the comments, and I could always use another solid pasta recipe. It definitely is a winner. Yes, if I was eating this without having made it, I would get that there was a twist of something but I would not know what. Very good, very easy, few ingredients.
I did add garlic, and used creme fraiche instead of cream as I do with most all recipes.
Ratio of pasta to sauce is good. Added a cup of pasta water to end.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Would prob cut down the red curry paste from 3 to 2T next time to make it a little more subtle. But still came out really delicious and satisfying!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I was curious about this recipe because I had some hamburger meat left from the 4th of July and half a jar of red curry paste in the fridge. I ended up only adding 1/4 cup of cream, but the ragu was silky and delicious. Very quick to put together.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: absolutely delicious! I have been adding curry powder to my meat sauce for years but never curry paste. the paste made all the difference! it also turns out that I am not a fan of ground pork (all they had in the store) but the sauce compensated that greatly and the dinner was a hit!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was easy and delicious and actually took the specified amount of time for a week night!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious, my husband loved it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
yummy
forgiving
i think it tastes like adult hamburger helper
my husband says what chef boyardee wants to be
either way , would make again

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I cooked this recipe, delicious! and it freezes well for leftoers! Thank you!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Love a quick whip-up meat sauce. I halved the cream to ease the artery burden, and it was still tasty. My grandmother taught me to add 1T balsamic vinegar and 1t sugar to any red sauce. The acid gives depth (much like red wine), and the tiny amount of sugar cuts too-tomatoey flavors.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I feel like cream doesn't add anything relevant to the bolognese. I'd rather just not use it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Great flavors. The curry flavor is subtle, but definitely present (maybe one with less lemongrass would make it taste less like a curry). That being said, I really enjoyed it and like the fusion.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I make the classic Bolognese in my Instant Pot. Amazing. Quick and as if you worked on it for hours!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Super quick and delicious! I used apple cider vinegar instead of white vinegar which tasted fantastic.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Curious, vinegar isn't even a listed ingredient. Why?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is a really great recipe. It does prove one thing, people making judgement calls don't bother to read the introduction or what the recipe was originally featured in. "Featured in: A (SORT-OF) Bolognese Fast and Easy Enough for Hard Nights," emphasis on "sort-of" is mine. You want the real thing, cook the real thing. This recipe is packed with flavor, actually pretty close to the time listed and unpretentious. Hat's off, Ms. Ko for a true hitmaker!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
The heat of the red curry paste can vary, mine is very hot so 1 TB is plenty, Add accordingly. 
I also added a little bit of beef bouillon paste for a bit more depth. Overall, easy recipe and quick for a Sunday night dinner.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I guess I’m used to adding Parmesan to all pasta but don’t see it in the recipe or anywhere in the notes! Would you recommend a sprinkle of parm on top? Any herbs ?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made with my college-aged daughter who heads back soon, to give her another recipe to make with her friends. Thought it was outstanding ! Only addition was some red pepper flakes. It’s not bolognese, but unlike others who commented, I wasn’t expecting that in 30 minutes

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was a delicious and easy weeknight meal for a busy family with teenagers.  We have long been fans of the African Spice Bolognese at Moro's restaurants in the finger lakes, and this recipe approximates the warmth of the spices and the depth of flavor, and is manageable to pull off on a weeknight.  As another poster noted, we also added red pepper flakes, as I was using a mild curry paste and wanted a little more heat.  This recipe will definitely be added to the rotation!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: 3 Tbsp of Red curry paste was too much. Next time, I am going to stick with 2. Other that that, outstanding.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Not spicy with red curry paste!?… I guess if you were born on the sun!!!

Skip it… rather, add tomato paste earlier and cook it plenty to add flavor and color.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is so good and easy! The curry paste is a great addition. We'll be using this for future meal prep as it's great reheated and easy to pack for lunch.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this recipe as is - but halved and in place of canned tomato sauce, I used half of a large heirloom tomato that I blanched and skinned (as per Marcella Hazan's instructions for preparing raw tomatoes for sauce). So delicious - but yes the red curry does give this an "Asian flavor" as other commenters note. That said - I found it entirely delightful. A splash of white or red wine might've made this taste a bit more traditional perhaps.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If I were to make it without adding the pasta right away, so that I could freeze it, should I hold off on adding the cream until I defrost and add it to the pasta? or add it before I freeze it?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
to Ken:

Ken
6 months ago
All of the red curry paste in my search results are Thai red curry paste.

I used Thai Kitchen the first several times. Good flavor.   Looked on line and Mae Ploy Red Curry Paste is touted as best by many.  Available at Amazon.    Much hotter than Thai Kitchen brand.  Good flavor, I just need to use less!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
A wonder! Thank you GK!
I used Greek yogurt instead of cream and it was perfect. 2T of red curry paste hit the right balance for me. Didn't bother chopping up the carrots and onions that finely, I just covered it and let it cook for a bit longer while I cleaned up. It's a very forgiving recipe. New favorite weeknight pasta.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: also I was worried it would need garlic. no need. curry paste does an incredible job of infusing so much flavor and depth! I would almost recommend against adding garlic to this one (something I have never said).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Use 3 tablespoons of the curry paste (the heavy cream makes the recipe mild) and really give it the full 3 minutes to darken. It makes a difference and deepens the flavor!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Highly recommend this recipe!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delish! Would only do a half cup of cream next time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made as directed, it was fantabulous. Yes, it’s not really bolognese, but the red Thai paste added a great depthless. Topped with grated parm. Will be on repeat for us.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made a half batch last night. I had previously purchased a Costco pack of Banza chickpea rotini, and I did not love it. This sauce promised to be flavorful, so I thought it might be good to drown out the chickpea pasta flavor. I was right! I also have a big tub of Mae Ploy red curry paste on hand, so this was perfect. For the half recipe, I used one heaping tablespoon of curry paste (probably close to but a little under 1.5, tbsp). Also for chickpea pasta, I cooked it for 6 minutes exactly and rinsed it with cold water. A little al dente when I pulled it, but it absorbed a little sauce at the end and turned out perfect. Made some steamed broccoli with garlic, butter, and lemon as a side. Great weeknight dinner.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I loved this the night I cooked it but found it didn't hit as well as a more traditional bolognese when served as leftovers. The sauce develops a consistency similar to ketchup and mayonnaise. Definitely worth a try though

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this according to the recipe twice.  It’s a family favorite and and quick.  I doubled carrots to add nutrition and served with parmasan cheese and a side salad.  Yumm!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Comes together surprisingly quick and is delicious.    We're currently a household with late summer colds and no energy at all.

This was perfect.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I have made this recipe twice already and it is easy and my husband loves it.  We like a higher spice level so I use 3 tablespoons of the curry paste.  I would recommend a bit more than this if you want even more of a kick.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly and used Thai Red curry paste. It turned out tasty and not spicy at all. It did seem to be missing something to add richness, next time I’ll try the suggestions and add red wine, tomato paste, and maybe beef broth.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Dumb question….1 can tomato sauce? Is this like a jar of Rao’s or just a can of tomatoes puréed?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Sarah S good question! You’ll actually see a 15 oz can in the grocery store labeled “tomato sauce” in the canned goods section - that’s the one you want (not a whole big jar of Rao’s or other marinara brands).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is a delicious recipe except I think I will use less cream next time. Also, I found it really nice to add 3 cloves of garlic in the middle of cooking the meat and red pepper flakes for a little extra spice!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Great recipe! Chucked in some shitaake mushrooms that were on their last legs in my fridge after the onion/carrot mix had cooked down a bit. 3tbsp of the curry paste (maesri) left a very pleasant heat in the mouth during dinner. The sauce is incredibly luscious and makes for great leftovers. It all came together in about an hour and a half-mainly because I forgot to get the pasta water boiling ahead of time. Looking forward to repeating this soon!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Tried the originally recipe and found it ok so switched recipe up slightly using the idea of the red curry paste as inspiration
Just used onion and garlic, added 2 Sweet Italian Sausages to ground beef mix, coated meat, onions, and garlic in small jar of red curry paste mix, added 22 oz can of crushed tomatoes and tablespoon of brown sugar. Simmered the mix for 20 minutes - my wife said it’s the best ground beef sauce I’ve ever made!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Loved it.   Made as directed but with only 2 tablespoons of red curry paste.   Served it with oven toasted French baguette.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was... odd. The combination of Thai flavours (keffir lime etc) in the curry paste, cream and pasta was very strange to me. It was okay, but not one I'll repeat. I prefer Thai curry paste with coconut milk and noodles.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: So don’t strain the beef after it’s cooked? Leave the rendered fat in the bowl?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Love this! The first time I made this, I didn't have tomato sauce, so I used spaghetti sauce. The second time, I made it as written and found it bland. I made it the third time tonight and went back to spaghetti sauce. I’m keeping the spaghetti sauce!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This one has no taste or flavor

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used yellow curry paste — a little more than 3 Tbsp, somebody stop me! — and it gave it a cinnamon spice that tasted more savory and rich than hot. Loved it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was the perfect cold weeknight dinner and could not have been more delicious. Skipped the curry paste and added a splash of red wine and red pepper flakes. Kept it Italian.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I didn’t have curry paste, so I made it without. Then just to see what would happen, I topped a serving with chili crisp. It’s amazing both ways! Will try the curry paste next time - and there will be a next time!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Super easy and delicious! I used two tablespoons of red curry paste along with some tomato paste and anchovy paste - can't have too much umami, right? - and let that toast pretty hard before mixing it. The curry paste adds a really flavorful background note and just the right amount of spice. Invite a big group over - this makes a LOT.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Great recipe. I made a few changes in the prep. I grated the carrots and threw onion into food processor.  I followed recipe exactly and found that the thai curry paste I have is quite spicy. I enjoyed it, but would have only used one tbsp when cooking for kids

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Made this vegetarian with some veggie crumbles, thinking you could also maybe use another substitute/tofu/roasted veggie.
I used oil to cook the crumbles and veggies. Next time, I think I'd start with the veggies to get the carrots a touch more tender. 
Even though I didn't rate this (I don't like to do that when I've significantly changed a central ingredient), I'd give it 5 stars - it was filling, satisfying, and really easy. Added some roasted broccoli on the side for a busy weeknight meal.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I thought this would cure my pasta craving…..NO NO NO. Down the disposal we go.  The curry paste is wrong. The suggestion of the Calabrian chili peppers excellent.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: "Push the meat to one side, and add the onion and carrots to the beef fat on the other side. Sprinkle with salt and pepper, and cook, stirring occasionally with the beef..."  I'm confused.  Am I cooking and keeping the onion and carrots separate from the beef and stirring each separately, or am I combining the onion and carrots with the beef and stirring them together?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This looks absolutely delicious. I hope mushrooms in lieu of dead cow works.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Great recipe! My wife and I are vegetarian so we used Beyond Beef grounds, which come in 16oz packages. Worked great, though I suggest giving the veggies a few minutes head start, since the BB cooks a little faster than the genuine article.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I will substitute the curry with red pepper flakes

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This dish was easy fast and absolutely delicious with just the two of us and a pound of pasta there are leftovers – yay!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Truly delicious, and it was a hit with my kids. I used Italian sausage instead of beef.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this with my 11yo daughter as written and it was a hit! 2Tbsp of red curry paste was warm and zingy but not too spicy for my kids. 11yo enjoyed cooking with me. 7yo also had a generous helping. Served roasted cauliflower on the side. Next time I might add peas for extra veg. We also had some Georgian feta lying around and threw that in.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Super easy and delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Followed some other tips to use harissa and tomato paste instead of the curry paste. Started with the onions and carrot and two cloves of garlic, added in the meat, the pastes, and a whole lot of sauce, and some extra water, and sauted for a long while. Added kalamta olives generously, which PERFECTLY balanced it all out, and a drop of chili crisp on top. Used calamarata pasta! Substituted cream for coconut milk and nutritional yeast to make it a kosher Shabbat dinner. Delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Holy cow! This is every bit as good as advertised. I ended up not using the water with a tomato can but using pasta water instead because I had a feeling it would be too thin and I think I was right it was so flavorful and came together so quickly this is definitely going in the rotation.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I substituted the heavy cream with full-fat coconut cream, and it came out as a lovely fusion of Thai and bolognese — would recommend this substitution for dairy free. Potentially only 1-1.5 tablespoons of curry paste as it’s a bit strong.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Parents and kids (12 yo + 9 yo) loved this! We went with 3 TB of red curry paste - it had good kick, but not too much. So comforting and delicious, with heat to keep things interesting.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Nice easy recipe. Added 3T of the curry paste and it certainly had quite a kick to it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made it this weekend for guests and it was an enormous hit!  I deviated just a tad: I added 4 crushed cloves of garlic, and also about 1/2 of leftover "au jus" from a French Dip.  Otherwise followed it to the letter.  Definitely keeping this on the A List.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
This came together incredibly quickly once prepped and had nice depth of flavor. It wasn’t very spicy despite my using the full 3 tbsp of red curry paste, but I suspect that has to do with the brand I used - I’m in a rural area and was lucky to find it at my grocery store at all. I didn’t detect any overwhelmingly “Asian” flavors as some of the other comments noted; to me the curry paste just lent some richness and complexity. I finished it with some finely grated Parmesan and red chili flakes, plus lots more salt and pepper.

My husband is a bartender so I’m always looking for meals that will reheat well when he gets home late, usually a few hours after I’ve cooked them. This one will definitely fit the bill - I suspect the flavor will intensify as it sits. It also makes a ton! We’ll definitely have leftovers.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I skipped the curry paste simply because I didn’t have it. Loved how fast this can come together and how I had every ingredient already on hand.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used coconut milk as a dairy substitute and it turned out amazing!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Did not use curry paste. Used red chili flakes instead. Also added generic Italian seasoning, and a clove of garlic.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Is this Asian red curry paste?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: A good recipe if you have young kids who are picky. Take it up 2 notches and add ground spicy italian sausage to the burger meat.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Take it up 3 not notches by adding garlic to the onions and carrots - along with 1lb hot italian sausage. Same amount of cooking time. Easy recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: East of the Atlantic we do bolognese more like this most of the time, but cut back on the simmering to about an hour or so. :-) https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1015181-marcella-hazans-bolognese-sauce?smid=ck-recipe-android-share

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Instead of using red curry paste, I just sub a jar of Rao’s for the tomatoes. Lots of flavor already in there. Add some veggies and ground meat and a drizzle of cream and it’s delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: When I cook something, I need it to be EASY! This was as easy as cooking spaghetti but it tastes so much better! I am sensitive to spice and went with 2 tbsp of the curry paste. I don't know that I could handle any more than that. I used mini shells as the pasta and it was perfect with it. I will say, I did not include a carrot in this recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: For the Europeans... what exactly is tomato sauce? Passata (aka pureed/strained tomatoes) or tomato paste or something else entirely?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The stuff that comes in a 15-oz can, as called for here, is just pureed tomatoes (and maybe salt).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’m not gonna lie I didn’t really like this recipe and wouldn’t make it again. I do enjoy when there is an “unexpected” ingredient, but this flavor profile just didn’t make sense to me taste wise. The curry paste did not add anything positive to the dish, if anything it took away. Simple cayenne would’ve been better than this attempt at creativity.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Having made so many versions of Bolognese over the years.  The red curry paste was a brilliant add on - definitely adding going forward!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The curry paste was interesting but a little too spicy.  Likely I just shouldn't have added as much.  Also just felt like a odd flavor in there.  Not bad just not great.  No one really wanted me to make again and was too spicy.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wow, what a delightful weeknight meal. This exceeded expectations. The red curry paste adds depth to an otherwise very quick basic tomato sauce. A few simple pantry ingredients and 35 mins is truly all you need. My 7yo came home from basketball and inhaled this, exclaiming over and over again how good it was. The only change I made was to reduce the heavy cream by 1/4c  just because. Don’t try to substitute the red curry paste.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious and easy. I did not have red curry paste and used tomato paste and added in red pepper flakes. I used a rotini protein pasta (lower glycemic for my diabetic husband) it was quick, easy and delicious will make again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was good! I made a few modifications. I used probably 2 tbs red curry paste along with 2-3 tbs tomato paste and a dollop of harissa. Added a bit of garlic and onion powder and a dash of Worcestershire. Everyone went back for seconds. This makes a large amount, and I look forward to leftovers!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Easy and delicious! I'm incredibly white and sensitive to spice, and the 2 TBSP of red curry paste I used (Thai Kitchen, the least authentic variety) wasn't enough, so next time I'll add 3 TBSP (unless i try a different brand). As long as you have the patience to dice the carrots and onions into teeny tiny pieces, you're golden. I made this with cavatappi and it was very tasty.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Added a dash of Worcestershire, everything else I followed as written! Delicious, and fast! Thank you

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I love love love this! It tastes like Hamburger Helper with a nice kick (I only used 2 tbsp of curry paste), but without the cheese and poverty flavor. Added to the repeat recipe list!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was a big hit. My husband isn't a pasta purist, but he certainly has his preferred way of making a meat sauce and this pushed him out of his comfort zone. That said, he commented multiple times between bites how much he enjoyed it and it made great leftovers! We added several Tbsp of tomato paste along with the curry paste, which turbo charged the tomato flavor a bit more and dialed back the Asian flavor while retaining the kick.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I've made this almost a dozen times. It always hits the spot for me: warming, indulgent, quick, and very tasty. I use pre-chopped mirepoix and meatloaf mix for ease and added flavor. I've used a few different red curry pastes, and while I have my favorite, every brand I've tried works well.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I have made this several times, and 90% lean and whole milk work just fine as substitutes! I have taken to using my food processor to chop up the onion and carrots to get them finely chopped quickly. My go to when I need a simple and comforting weeknight dinner.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used the Thai curry paste, 3 tablespoons and honestly wasn’t that spicy.  I guess everyone’s tolerance for spice is different.  I also completely omitted the Creme and it was still very tasty.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I added Calabrian Chile Paste instead of the curry paste and it was over the top.  Add the Calabrian paste a little at a time as mine was potent!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I guess I like curry.... and knowing that I'm adding it to the sauce I understand that it's going to be different than the traditional Bolognese sauce. But it's a good kind of different. The way this is a no fuss, low prep recipe is what cinches it for me. It's great and if you like a spicy kick, you should try it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This worked way better than it had any right to! Amazing! So clever. Thank you!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I passed on the Thai curry paste. Most have lime and ginger in it which can overpower a sauce. I opted instead for gochujang which has fewer invasive flavors and gives the sauce the depth of flavor that it needs. Threw in a few cloves of garlic, oregano, and some pecarino to round it out.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was good but not a weekday recipe. Chopping veggies and prep took me 15 minutes before I even stated.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: cooked it exactly as shown and it was delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: instead of meat i used finely chopped portobello mushroom caps. i used four for a half-recipe and cooked them until all the water released and mostly evaporated. then i added some butter and olive oil along with the carrot and onion. i added a little soy (to taste) for umami and a little coconut sugar (to taste) to help make up for the high-heat caramelization on the meat. the rest was exactly the same! it turned out INCREDIBLE.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Family loved this. I simmered the sauce for awhile longer pre-heavy cream, aiming for a reduction and thickening of the sauce. Might give it a hit of red wine next time after the meat browns.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
The recipe, as is, is delicious. The Thai red curry paste doesn't really add any "Asian flavor" at all -- there's just a kind of subtle additional umami to the bolognese. This sauce has the benefit of being a very quick sauce with a flavor that makes it feel like a sauce that cooked for awhile. The carrots add a little sweetness as they cook down and disappear into the sauce. 

Great recipe!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Omg the raves were warranted. So good, perfect comfort food!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was easy and comforting pasta dish but something was definitely missing. Not sure what but just wasn't super flavorful.  But super easy to whip up and serve the family, will definitely make again but need something to boost the flavor a bit.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is not a Bolognese sauce. Give it a name but certainly not this classic beauty. It’s a disservice to the name.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: FANTASTIC flavor and delicious-to-effort ratio. I used turkey instead of beef and doubled the carrots for more veggies. Also found that the Thai curry paste didn’t add any heat at all even after 3 tsp so I dumped in 1/4 cup of red pepper flakes and it was still mild, so don’t be afraid to go heavy handed with spice if you like it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It was okay. I think it needed garlic and parsley to round it all out. Maybe less cream as well.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this vegetarian by subbing cooked green lentils sautéed in a hefty amount of butter for the ground beef. It was DELICIOUS!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Everyone makes this differently! I use a  1 1/2 inch thick just over 1 lb. ribeye last week. Dry brined it for 12 hours, crushed peppercorns with a mortar and pestle, lightly coated both sides 1 1/2 hours before cooking and left out to get to room temp. Preheated oven to 440. Preheat cast iron for 10 minutes, set on medium-high on gas stovetop burner put a little olive oil and butter in, quickly got steak in skillet for a little over 3 minutes, flipped, 3 more minutes, took pan off burner and put in preheated oven for 1 1/2 minutes. Remove steak to warm plate, set aside to rest. After a couple minutes start 3 parts brandy to 1 part Worcshestshire sauce plus quarter stick of butter in the skillet

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’m not sure what I posted about before but I followed this recipe exactly except added 1/4 pound of Italian sausage I get from Graziano Bros Italian store in Des Moines. Everyone that ate it raved. Just a little more spice and a little more meaty Bolognese.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made exactly as written, using 2 T of the red curry paste. This was simply amazing - and a great substitute for my favorite restaurant’s bolognese.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: 5 stars just for the crispy thin layer of meat trick

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Used Maesri brand“karee” curry paste (technically yellow, but looks red, not spicy) and subbed gnocchi. Will be making this unusual and addicting version of bolognese again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
RED CURRY
Here’s the thing. It def means Thai red, as most other versions are pretty obscure. Brand matters a lot. I used Mekhala red paste, normally a favorite, but three tbsp waaaaay too intense. Thai Taste or some milder brand would’ve been fine. But 3tbsp of Mekhala has it tasting like actual Thai food, very gingery and lemongrassy—kind of a weird vibe for pasta bolognese. Still tasty though! Gonna use a mellower paste next time or honestly Calabrian paste and some sun-dried tomato paste is probably the pro move.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: "Smush". Keep living, keep learning.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
This is a great recipe, few ingredients, good effort-to-result. This time, I used turkey thigh with coconut oil and canola instead of ground beef. Added a bit of worcestershire as the turkey was browning to add a little beefier flavor.
 Didn’t have tomato sauce, used crushed tomatoes with some tomato paste to thicken. We liked the resulting chunks of tomato in the sauce. Used greek yogurt after taking the sauce off the heat instead of cream, which worked fine. Freezes well, we like to keep the pasta separate for that purpose.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly and was not disappointed at all. It was delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was delicious! I usually put more spicy ingredients than the recipe calls for but in this case, I wish I had only put 2 tablespoons of the curry paste! Trust Ko on this one! 3 tablespoons of curry paste if you like it real spicy.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Definitely a weeknight meal that is delicious.  I did use Calabrian chili peppers in oil per one cook's recommendation and it was the heat/spice combination I was looking for.  I also accidentally used ground pork instead of ground beef but it turned out amazing anyway.  I used DeLallo's girelle pasta, a thick and curly pasta that balanced well with the sauce.  This recipe is quite versatile!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I added a can of tomato paste.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was so. Yummy. I love the curry paste flavor. I also did a little deglazing after the veg with some white wine I had in the fridge. So tasty.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I didn’t find the Asian flavors too strong, but if I made this again I’d omit the cream and use 1/2 a pound of pasta. 1 pound is a lot! And it would be easy to substitute Beyond Beef or any vegetarian meat substitute. Would it be Bolognese? Hey, we’re riffing on an Italian meat sauce. Purists can make the 3+ hour version. Modern cooks need to adapt. I’d also cook the onion/carrot mixture first, alone. I prefer them slightly brown and not translucent, which takes longer than 5 minutes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I absolutely loved this recipe! I had to substitute green chili paste because my grocery was out of red. I used TBSP and a splash of red wine and my fiance said it was one of the best meals I’ve ever made! We will definitely keep this recipe!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I just used regular tomato paste, no curry, and it was excellent. Super comfort food!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: No carrots. Subbed cream for milk. Subbed tomato sauce for jarred marinara. Used Thai curry paste and harissa. Delish!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Follow the recipe but skipped the curry paste, added paprika and a few spices. Worked as advertised, easy weeknight meal and our daughter got seconds.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Thank you for this gluten-free dumpling. Sounds yum!!! An Indian version would be stuffed with coconut, jaggery (brown sugar) and cardamom. 
But I do have a question: Any savory filling recommendations for this glutinous rice dumpling? Thanks!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Rahel I think red bean would work well! Or maybe salted egg yolk.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Rahel Coconut and jaggery sound great! Any mixture that can stay bound together easily, like a blend with ground meat or seafood, would be easiest to wrap with this (gluten-free!) dough. Just place a ball of the mix in the middle of a flattened round and gather and pinch the dough around the filling to seal (pleating if you want). Steam or boil until the filling is cooked through and the wrapper is pearlescent. Enjoy!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Five minutes prep time?  Seriously??

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Yup! Prep time is the time is takes to prep the ingredients in the ingredient list (so in this case, chopping chocolate if not using chips and measuring out ingredients). It takes about an hour and a half start to finish if you work without interruption.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Genevieve Ko thank you so much for that clarification! For years I have enjoyed this app but basically ignored the prep time as an unachievable dream. I always assumed that prep time was supposed to equate to the active time spent on the steps listed in the preparation section. I have always discounted it, thinking I must be a distracted or slow prepper but now I’ll look at that prep time a little differently. Such a small distinction but this clarification will make me feel a lot better about my prepping performance :)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Christine I’m pretty sure you’re right and prep time is prepping the ingredients to the point of cooking (in this case it would include rolling out the mochi and wrapping the chocolate). Of course if NYT Cooks is using two different definitions, it’s confusing!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These were great. I found the glutinous rice flour dough very easy to work with and the filling was rich and delightful. I personally probably would prefer a smaller amount of filling in each dumpling but my kids felt they were perfect as it is. I may increase the toasted sesame oil in the next batch as I prefer more of that flavour.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Turned out exactly as described. I found it easier to use a rolling pin to flatten the dough. Tastes like Nutella with sesame instead of hazelnut and a wonderful chewy wrapper almost like mochi. Serving these with ginger ice cream for Lunar New Year!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These were delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Even after resting, my dough ended up really dry and wouldn't come together. But, I moistened my fingers with water throughout the kneading process, which worked and made for a nice dough that worked well in the end. I live in Phoenix so perhaps the really dry climate had something to do with it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I tried and failed making the dough 3 times. 

First two times followed the instructions and video, but each time ended up with a dough that was cracking and crumbling, incapable of being flattened into dumpling disks. (Second time I did not apply as much pressure in forming the dough or kneading it, but did not make much of a difference.)

Third time used 2/3 cup water. Dough seemed more moist and pliable, but also ended up cracking and breaking apart when attempting to flatten into disks.

 Alas, three strikes and this recipe is out for me.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this with the kids for Lunar New Year. This was easy enough that an 8- and 10-yo could pretty much do everything (I helped with the kneading) and we found the work went quickly. I’m not saying the dumplings looked good (the boys were not interesting in pleating) but we liked the taste! I did use the sweet rice flour as I couldn’t get the glutinous rice flour in time and they might have been a little chewy (also possible that it was because the kids didn’t get the dough rounds super thin). But they were tasty with a great combination of flavors.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @michelle lol, I need your kids help because I could not - in three tries - get the dough to form properly. Kept breaking as I tried to flatten it into disks. Glad it worked for you!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: i dont know what i did wrong. but I felt the dough was really hard to work with.... maybe to dry.... to thick but it was hard to get super thin without the dough breaking. the dough was tasteless to! what did i do wrong.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @dianne I had same issues!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The dough IS going to be tasteless-- it's pretty much just flour and water.  Can't really help with the texture but I'm guessing it was too dry and/or you let it sit too long.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: We had the same problem but as we handled the dough with warm hands it got more flexible and eventually formed.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Dewey rice flour and glutinous rice flour are the same thing. Differences will be due to milling practices. Asian milled flours are usually finer and while finely milled rice flour or sweet rice flour is desirable, some extremely finely milled flours may behave differently in cooking of making.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: would these be any good with pre-made wonton wrappers?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Sue premade wonton wrappers would give you a completely different result.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I found the dough to be really tricky. Ii had to add a bunch more rice flour and even then was impossible to not get sticking to my hands.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious!! Made it as it called for and it turned out great — perfect balance of sweet and salty! Our guests loved it, too.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Put less  toasted sesame oil

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: dough ended up being impossible to work with.  was very dry and stiff and couldn't be used.  these made for delicious truffles that I served with a miso caramel sauce

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made these for our Lunar New Year dumpling party. There were super tasty, reminiscent of warm chocolate mochi balls. Had a lot of trouble with the dough - it would break apart/split, so tried kneading in more water, then more flour, then more water, and just couldn't get it right. We were able to mold it around the chocolate balls though, just no pretty pleats. Very yummy and so cute!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’m thinking of adding sweet black sesame paste in (partial?) place of the condensed milk. Anyone think that’s a good/bad idea?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: When I heard the recipe name I expected a sesame paste to be mixed in.  (I was thinking tahini, but that's quite runny... unless you let it separate and use the thick stuff at the bottom.)  So yes, I think it would taste great.  I think the issue would be the oil content of the sesame paste.  While being steamed, it might get runny and ruin everything.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Check out her video "How to Make Dumplings Two Ways" where she has a variation of this sweet dumpling using black sesame paste.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Can these be frozen?
Can they be reheated?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: When you worked the rice flour dough on the countertop which was sprinkled with flour, was that wheat flour or again the rice flour from which the wrappers were made. Thank you.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I was wondering the same, but I assume it's rice flour.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It's the same rice flour! Use as much as needed for the dough to stop sticking to your fingers.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used parchment to line my bamboo steamer and the dumplings were stuck like glue to it. Any suggestions?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I want to make these ahead of time and freeze them. Anything I should know about steaming them from frozen?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Could coconut milk with honey be subbed in for the dairy intolerant?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Is that an unsweetened chocolate bar you are using in the recipe?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’m excited to try these, but I’m on a lactose-free diet. Any suggested substitutions for the condensed milk?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Rahel  The Chinese make a savory chopped peanut "mochi", fyi. delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Why can't one use cut up rice paper?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Couldn’t wait to make this recipe and it came out very delicious. I think the next time I make it, I’m going to play with it to get it a little more tender and with less char on the crust.
