I marinated for 4 hours, but think it could benefit from a full overnight fridge. I’m going to try covering the roast in the oven for the first hour and then uncover for the second hour. My crust formed nicely during the first hour of cooking, but got a little overdone and ugly during the second hour.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Thank you for this tip—I will follow your suggestion to cover for the first hour!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The secret is out! I have been marinating meat in miso for years. The results are incredible!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: What if the roast is one the bone? Undo it or just rub the miso marinade on the outside?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @john golden I took the bone out of mine but probably didn’t need to. It does make for easier carving and presentation to have it boneless

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This looks like it would work well in a slow cooker. I would love to get specs from anyone who has done so.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wow. This was fantastic (and we from the pork-producing state of NC are picky!).  We used shoulder. We used a pretty big roasting pan and rack b/c we had to buy two 2.5 lb shoulders to get 5 pounds.  Needed much more stock in the bottom to keep onions and rosemary from burning up. I like Robbin's idea of longer marinating and covering for first hour, though ours only needed two hours of cooking total (maybe because it was two separate roasts and  we were aggressive butterfliers).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Mashed potatoes and Asian cucumber salad.  MP goes wonderful with Miso and the salad cuts the richness.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Beth try making shio koji mashed potatoes. https://justonecookbook.com/mashed-potatoes-with-shio-koji/

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was so, so, so easy and the results were fantastic! Definite WOW factor when it hit the table. The au jus it made was as good or better than the cranberry-pear sauce (more of a compote or chunky salsa, really) but most people used both. Again, this is a 10/10 recipe and I will mke it again, for sure. Cant wait to try it on a standing rib roast!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Any thoughts on whether this roast can be cooked in a slow cooker, either on its low or high setting?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Chris We marinated the roast over night, tied it in the morning and put it in the slow cooker on low until it reached 165 internal temperature. (We had a 7  pound roast with double the marinade and it took about 7 hours on low.) My husband then seared the outside in pieces in a cast iron pan, though I’m sure it could be seared in a high temp oven. The roast carved well into soft slices and small chunks of meat. It was some of the best roasted pork we’ve ever had. The marinade is unbelievably good.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Would a pork tenderloin work?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Lisa Howick A tenderloin would need no more than a half hour or so to cook or else it will be really dry

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I would think pork tender not to be a direct translation for the pork shoulder/butt.  The miso marinade would be delicious regardless the cut, but the roasting/cooking would need some thought and crafty adaptations.  Pork tender is very lean, and the shoulder has a fair amount of fat.  Tenderloin cooks quickly relatively speaking and at higher heat (400°F?) does well, while the pork shoulder benefits from the lower 325°F and slower cooking to render out the fat and the connective tissue.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @ChiaviDiBasso what temp would you recommend for tenderloin?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Could I substitute a couple of pork loins for the shoulder roast? 
If yes: how must I adjust your recipe? 
TIA/Happy Holidays

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Caly I think this recipe is about pork shoulder. Pork tenderloin is an entirely different conversation.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Caly pork loin and tenderloin are 2 different things. The loin has a fat cap and is larger so i think it would work. Just watch the internal temperature for cooking time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I accidentally overcooked it but it was still devoured. I thought it was too sweet. I added more onions which may have contributed to it. I boiled the pan juices, added some red wine, soy sauce and tapioca starch for a scrumptious gravy. Served with mashed potatoes, roasted fennel, and citrus and radish salad.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @RE wow this gravy is a great idea, I’ll definitely be doing that next!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Think this would work with a pork LOIN roast? Just stop a135 internal temp?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious!  I used tenderloin and yellow miso followed tip and reduced brown sugar.  Marinated for 5 hours, roasted tenderloin for 40 minutes at 400 convection, to 160 on instant thermometer.  The flavors were beautifully melded and the meat took on a depth of flavor unusual in pork tenderloin.  Such a simple easy to cook dish but so good. Will definitely make it again, would make a great dinner party main dish.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Are there any suggestions for side vegetables?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @John I’m going to serve this with roasted Brussels sprouts and sweet potatoes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @John. Sweet potatoes or winter squash- both could be roasted in oven along with the pork.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Roasted delicata squash, greens sautéed in olive oil with garlic and a pinch of red pepper flakes.
For the greens, I think broccoli rabe would be good, chard, kale or spinach would work too.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this as specified (with no sauce). The thermometer read 165 on the dot at the specified time. It's probably a good idea to occasionally add a bit of extra water (stock or wine) to keep the marinade from sticking on the pan, burning, and making cleanup challenging. I thought the result was OK, but a bit sweet. I probably would not make it again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
5-star results despite some head-scratching directions (suggested mods below). Flavors are excellent: a lovely Christmas Eve meal!

1) No WAY anybody needs 6 cups of that (delicious) cranberry sauce / compote. Halve so you need just ONE standard bag of fresh cranberries (12 oz). I wish I was into canning given copious leftovers, but we’re going to try to immersion blend it into a Christmas Day cocktail.
2) 1/4 cup of [insert liquid] in the roasting pan is WAY too little. We put in at least 2 cups worth of stock, red wine, and butter as well as some whole garlic cloves. Kept everything moist and yielded a delicious gravy at the end!
3) MOST egregious were the “cook ‘til” temp and cook time. TLDR = if you want it fast, stick with 325F for no more than 1.5 hours in the oven; if you have time, go low and slow at 200F. Longer story = we (by which I mean my husband, obvi) had a combustion predictive thermometer aiming at a core temp of 145F; after just 1 hour, our 5 lb roast was nearly cooked through and starting to burn! We’d been planning the rest of dinner assuming another 3+ hours so ended up cooking it at 150F for the remaining time and rotating it a quarter of the way every time we basted it. Took it out when the internal temp was 140, let it rest for 30 min, and it was perfectly cooked. 

Thanks for coming to my TED Talk…!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @DM83 Thank you for the tips and I respect and appreciate your wit!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Worked out really well. Thanks

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I wanted to try this recipe but circumstances required some changes: no time to marinate my 1 lb pork loin so I adjusted the amounts of miso marinade and simply rubbed the paste on to the meat; adjusted the temperature to 425 and the cooking time to 45 minutes. Added sliced garlic to the sliced onions and added stock and red wine to the bottom of the roasting pan, which resulted in a delicious sauce with the consistency syrup to pour over the sliced tender meat.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Not a cranberry sauce person. Any suggestions for an alternate sauce to serve it with or accompaniment?  Thank you!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Mdmlarsen: This article provides recipes for 5 alternatives for cranberry sauce: https://www.huffpost.com/entry/cranberry-sauce-alternatives_l_5dcc2404e4b0a794d1f8ff04 In general, a chutney should work.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Try lingonberry sauce or lingonberry jam.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Mdmlarsen I grew up eating pork with apple sauce. It’s a great combo! No need to make from scratch.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I followed the recipe and cooking time and the pork turned out perfectly.  I didn't make the sauce but used canned cranberry sauce instead which worked well.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is a fine and simple recipe, so yay! Next time I will not go to 165 degrees. I'll try for around 150 or a bit over.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This recipe was made for a huge roast with a beautiful layer of fat.  I had just a 3lb loin roast.  I used 1/3 cup white miso (what I had) and about half that much brown sugar (plus the pepper and rosemary) and didn't butterfly it. Only had 3 hrs to marinate, last hr at room temp. I had a hard pear, and roasted that alongside, w an onion (with some water and broth added now and then when I checked on it). Roasted at 400 for 10min, then turned down to 350. Took an hour or less. Made a pan gravy w more water and a little flour. The roast was moist and delicious! The pear was amazing w it. Also had homemade cranberry sauce leftover and that was ok but not needed w it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was excellent and easy. I'm mostly vegetarian so don't have a lot of experience with roasts. I was nervous making this for the first time for Xmas eve dinner but it was outstanding. Will make it again this year. I used a 4 lb bone-in roast; marinated overnight. Used about 3/4 cup of vinho verde in the bottom of the roaster and had a good amount of drippings which were excellent. Covered it loosely with foil for the first hour, then uncovered. It took about 2.5 hours to cook.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Note that you can wrap the pork tightly in plastic wrap before refrigerating. It will keep the marinade adhered to the meat and make less of a mess. Also, if having trouble with over-browning, cook at 300. These are both techniques used to make bo ssam, which is a similar (although very differently flavored) dish.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was amazing!  I took the other cooks comments and covered with foil for the first hour.  I did mine on convect roast at 300 for the same time and it came out super juicy after marinating in the fridge for 8 hrs.  I used my immersion blender to puree the onions, rosemary and pan juices, added some more pepper and thyme to serve over the pork.  Made it next level!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I wonder how this would work on a smoker? Anyone tried it? I'd think the miso paste would add some good umami.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this with pork shoulder and pork tenderloin - both were a success. I marinade for longer, and put way more onions and broth than the recipe calls for.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: So easy, so good. You don't need the extra sauce. The gravy from all that roasting is plenty. I roasted the pear with the onion and the pork. That gravy from the roasting juices was plenty and perfect

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was really good. Followed another’s recommendation for adapting recipe for a pork tenderloin. Marinated overnight (added lime juice) then roasted at 400 convection bake in the marinade :40 minutes or until internal temp measured 165. Not dried out like tenderloin can be. Sauce was delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Used pork tenderloin and added apple cider vinegar to make the paste more spreadable. Loved by kids and adults.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Lindsey C. Would you please share the details? Amounts, cooking temp/time? TIA!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It cooked way faster than the recipe stated. I strained the cranberry/pear/wine sauce- much nicer! I added a bit of honey for sweetness- it was somewhat sour without it. Roasted some asparagus and used some leftover stuffing from the buttermilk turkey brine I made last week. Really good and I prepped everything yesterday so super easy for me today!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was excellent! I cooked it in a cast iron pot (dutch oven) without a lid in the oven at 350. The only marinating I did was in the hour it sat out before cooking. I used far less miso, a heaping tablespoon, which was perfect. I poured white wine in the bottom of the pan and also put in some garlic and kept replenishing the wine for every basting. The meat was tender with a nice crunchy top. I had no cranberries so used apples. With the ginger and a cinnamon stick the sauce came out like chutney — mouth watering.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I have made this twice and it was terrible both times. Both my husband and I are experienced as cooks. Granted, we used a 3-lb roast, but we made different adjustments for that. The only thing good was the smell. The meat was dry and tough, the sauce was terrible and did not go with the pork at all. Off our list.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If you substitute pork tenderloin for the shoulder it should only be cooked to 140 degrees. At 165 it will be very dry

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I have a 6 lb rack of pork. I think it’s a rib roast? Do you think this work for this recipe or should I make adjustments. Thanks for any tips!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The meat was tender and tasty but quite sweet and very fatty. It reached 165 after only 2-1/2 hours in the oven, so was ready well before I expected it to be, which meant it was luke-warm when served.  Disappointed after all the raves — would not make this again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Mimi
Totally agree.  The meat is ready 1 hour and a half early.  I’m lowering the oven temp to 200 until we sit at the table.  Any other suggestion?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Absolutely delicious. Will make again. Filled a family of 5 with leftovers for one person.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: One of the best NYT recipes I've ever made! Covered my 7 lb bone-in roast for the 1st hour, then uncovered and turned the heat up to 350 because I put it in a little later than I anticipated. Couldn't find cranberries so I opted to strain the pan drippings and reduce for a quick gravy. Delish! My guests raved about it and demolished most of the pork. Just have a little for leftovers. Served with maple roasted delicata and acorn squash, and a simple watercress salad. Yum!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Jamie M. I was one of the fortunate guests … so darn delicious! Still talking about it!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious. Marinated for two days (busy life!). Convection roasted for about 40 minutes. Used boneless 3-pound pork tenderloin. Sliced into 1 inch rounds. Served with roasted carrots. Leftovers tomorrow, with roasted fennel and boiled potatoes. No brown sugar to conserve carbohydrates. Made cranberry sauce with candied ginger. Very nice.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
This was delicious! Here are my adjustments:
- Halved the recipe for dinner for 4
- Marinated 2 pork tenderloins for 10 hours, removing from the fridge for the last hour
- Roasted in a baking dish over onions at 400 for about 50 min
- IMO the sauce is a non-negotiable :)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: May I use Korean soybean paste in place of miso?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Is this skin on or off?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Would state in the recipe that boneless or bone-in pork roast can be used, a point which was clear in the accompanying article that my husband had not seen. This unfortunately resulted in him deboning the bone-in roast I bought to reproduce what was shown in the recipe photo! The final product was great, but there was unnecessary waste and lacked the increased moistness from a bone-in roast.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This recipe, including the cranberry sauce is the tastiest, most perfect flavors. Don't mess with it. This will be my go to cranberry recipe from now on! Only tweak I made was cooking it at 300 hundred for six hours until the pork was 190. It really made it so tender. But I'm going to dream about this marinade for weeks!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I made this with a 3 rib pork roast, otherwise closely followed the recipe with a few exceptions -- made the miso mix as specified and marinated overnight.
-- cooked at 325 F until temp in thickest part was 140 F -- and rested 30 minutes.  It was done, but not rare.  It needed much more fluid (used white wine) ~ one glass +
-- Made the sauce as suggested -- it was stellar -- amazing combination -- do not omit.
DLC

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @DLC Agree, it needs more liquid. But delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was one of most disappointing dishes ever. It should have been braised rather than roasted. It was tough.  Tasteless. And a mess to make. Still cleaning up the mess.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was SOOOOO delicious!  Made it for our family for our New Year’s Day dinner and everyone loved it.  Super easy to do.  I followed the suggestion of other cooks who recommended adding additional liquid to the pan.  Roasted root veggies and sprouts and made the delicious side of cranberry compote.  Served with another ace NYT recipe - radicchio salad with fennel, red onions and citrus which offset the rich meat perfectly.  5🌟!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I cooked this for New Year’s. It turned out delicious. I made the fruit sauce with tart pie cherries instead of cranberries and would do the same the next time. The au jus was also a big hit. The cooking time for my 4 pound roast was just a little over 2 hours. I served it with Martha Stewart’s mac and cheese. Delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
"cut along its natural breaks to butterfly the meat without cutting all the way through" - What does this mean?

And if it is a bone-in roast, do you unfurl or not?

commentBody: Where is the onion used?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: We used an immersion blender for au jus.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Use large oven even x 2 lb. Cover w/parchment paper as soon as needed

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made it with a rib end loin roast and adjusted the cooking methodology accordingly. Cut the sugar in the marinade as I usually do with sweet/savory pork dishes. Wanted to love it but didn’t. The marinade was blah and the pork was tender but had a slightly unpleasant spongy texture, suggestive of over-brining. I may try again with the intended cut of meat and reduce marinating time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Pork shoulder roast is tricky to keep moist. Try adding buttermilk and a little smoked sea salt to the marinade and go overnight in a large zipper freezer bag. Using a meat injector to get some of it deep into the roast would be even better. Cook it low and slow; 275-300* for about an hour per pound, tenting with foil after browned. An internal meat thermometer set to 180* followed by resting helps to make a tender slice.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I followed all directions and it came out beautifully moist. A hit with my guests. From comments i feared it would be too sweet and I’m not fond of sweet meat dishes but it was not too sweet. I should have patted dry the meat before the miso paste spreading, it was not easy. My5.5 lb bone in but roast cooked in 3 hours.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Has anyone tried this marinade and sauce with pork tenderloin??  Might try that

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Nobody shares that the portions work for 15-16 people. A 4.5# roast would shrink a little and give every ona slice or two ?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this with a 7 lb roast removing large chunks of fat and served it to eight. There were barely enough leftovers for two, with lots of side dishes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: From the email: “ I’m not sure why I said the cranberry sauce is optional — technically it is, but you’ll want it. It brings a tangy pop and the hug of ginger. Spoon the meat all over mushroom and tofu sticky rice or tuck it into milk bread rolls, and blend up this creamy sesame-ginger dressing to toss with greens or shaved cabbage to balance all that richness.”

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The marinade makes for a delicious roast. I love the pepery bite alongside the sweet. It's not difficult to make but requires advance planning; I marinated overnight. I recommend a slower oven, say 300, and adding small amounts of liquid while roasting. At 325, the onions charred rather than carmelized. Check the roast for doneness regularly after th first hour to avoid overcooking. The roast will continue to come to temperature during the resting period.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Miso is my new favorite marinade (find the white miso pork chop recipe)!  It was VERY easy to make.  Following reader tips, I applied the marinade paste the day before for longer saturation.   And in an attempt to make easier and find the right size container - I used a clay pot to cook (avoiding basting).  The meat was perfection.  I did not wipe off excess marinade (hard to waste) - and had no regrets.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this using pork tenderloins weighing a little less than 4 lbs. It marinated longer than the recipe called for - simply because I forgot that we had dinner plans the day I intended to cook it. In any case, after marinating for 2 nights I cooked it for about an hour at 350 and it was delicious. Served with rice & mixed vegetables. I actually used dried tarragon in the marinade instead of rosemary because I didn’t have any rosemary.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can we give this ten stars?  Oh, so good!  Needed to adjust the recipe for 2 people instead of 12-16 servings.  Got a boneless 2 lb. roast.  Butterflied it and cut the marinade in half.  Marinated it for 4 hours.  Did not make the cranberry sauce.  Only needed to cook it for a little under 2 hours.  This was so delicious!  My husband said it was the best pork roast he had ever had!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious and tender! The secret was cooking it in a dutch oven with the lid off, with lots of stock and wine - probably about 1.5-2 cups total, in the oven, to get the best of both worlds: tender but crispy on the outside. For the rub, I used only about a heaping tablespoon of miso and glad I did - it was salty enough. I only marinated it for the hour or so it was sitting out on the counter, which was also enough. For the sauce: apples since I didn't have cranberries. Will def be making again!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wow. Easy but special enough for holiday buffet. Leftovers equally delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: In the oven now. Smelling delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used about half the sugar and added one chopped habenero. It was great, no notes. Definitely want cranberry sauce

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I love cranberry sauce, but one could easily cut the sauce recipe in half and have enough.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Nothing about this dish worked. The meat should have been braised as you would with any pork shoulder. If it wasn’t for the cranberry chutney the dish would have been dry and tasteless.  the miso marinade added nothing.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Are there any alternatives to miso that is soy free?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Does this have to have red miso specifically or might another miso be just as good?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Oops... the answer is in the tips.  Mine is marinating now. Thanks for all the great suggestions.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: GREAT recipe.  Everyone LOVED it and went back for seconds.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I tried an overnight marinade and made sure to use the parchment paper after the first hour. Worked perfectly. Really stellar and great leftovers.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I mean, of course its possible to screw this up. For instance if you have a baby at home which refuses to sleep and you have to interrupt the cooking process at 60 degrees. After that, the roast never made it past 63, but was still very very done. And dry. But the marinade is pretty ace!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is completely flavorless. I am a good cook and have had great success with other NY Times recipes. This is a waste of time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this for NYE and it was excellent! Only issue was that I had to keep adding at least 1/2 cup of broth and water every half hour! I used a 7 lb. shoulder but not enough fat drippings to keep pan from burning. Loved the sauce! Definitely will make this again!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can you do this recipe with pork loin?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’m going to give this a whirl tonight. And I’m going to try it with shio koji mashed potatoes. I’ve been wanting to make these taters and the roast gives me the perfect set up to do so.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: What are some good ways to manage leftovers? I’d like to make this for four of us … should I freeze half the (uncooked)  pork or try to freeze once it’s cooked??

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I make a gravy with the drippings and my family LOVES hot pork and gravy sandwiches for lunch!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Leftover roast pork is incredibly useful for all sorts of things! Sandwiches, yes, but I like to toss slivers into quick noodle soups or stir-fries, or on top of salads--or on top of noodle salads! Cook the pork, and if you won't use up the leftovers in a few days, freeze small packages of slices or slivers in the quantity you'll use for a single meal. They defrost quickly.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made it exactly to the recipe without the sauce. Absolutely simple and delicious. Next time I will reduce the sugar. I’m not sure why every pork dish seems to be sweet and call for a sweet sauce, but I prefer a more savory flavor. I served 10 with a 5lb roast, and there was not a morsel left.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this and cooked on a traegger bbq in a roasting pan.  Only addition, garlic in marinade.  Total winner. The roasted onions ended up in a bowl next to the sliced meat, a great complement to tender, flavorful pork.  Started to wonder if this could be a Xmas dinner protein, it was that good.  Didn’t even consider making the sauce but for Xmas eve….? Elevate it!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The pork roast was great. I usually do it with garlic and fennel seed, and then crisp that fat until it shatters, but this was just as good. The standout element was the cranberry sauce recipe. I used an apple instead of pears. This is going to be my go-to cranberry recipe from now on.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was great. Just enough for 10 with a 5 lb bone-in shoulder. I put two big onions in the roasting pan and afterwards took the drippings and onions to make a sauce. Cranberries were a hit too, will use that recipe for Thanksgiving.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Where is miso found in the grocery store?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Joy At my grocery store, it is in the international Food aisle.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I found some in the asian section. But I could only find brown so going to have to try it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Does this work with thyme instead of rosemary?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Recipe calls for either bone-in or boneless--but seems to be more for boneless, no? And wouldn't there be vastly different cooking times for both?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Anyone use monk fruit brown sugar version sub for the brown sugar?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My 5 lb roast was done in about 21/2 hrs. It was tender but sliced i full slices as in the photo. Would like to go a little longer in the cook time and get it even more fall apart tender, but afraid of over cooking. Has anyone done this? I also covered the rose for the first hour and I think that was a good idea. I also defatted the large amount of delicious juice I had and poured that over and it was really good.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: How do I adjust for a boneless loin roast? I’ve had trouble in the past with these roasts being dry.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can you share with me the amount of ginger you have used?  7 slices seems vague and depends on the size of the ginger root.  Can I grate it instead of mashing it?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this for Christmas dinner - yum. So good. Swinging by the comments to talk about sides. While Brussels and potatoes are a good idea, I recommend something on the stove top. The cooking temp is hard to reconcile with other dishes. Also, I used a rack…while the meat was amazing, and the pan jus great (used a combo of homemade chx stock and white wine), the onions were tasty but the texture was mush. Maybe sheet pan is better for the veggies? Anyway. It was great.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Tried this for the first time for our Christmas dinner.  Flavor was excellent but the cut of pork (shoulder per recipe and purchased at Whole Foods) was difficult to roast. Even though securely tied after rubbing with the miso mixture, the meat roasted unevenly.  I’d like to try a pork loin next time or try another reviewers plan and place in the slow cooker then sear.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was absolutely outstanding.  I was surprised.  I used a red miso from a Japanese grocer that seemed quite dry so the paste didn't seem like it was going to be an effective "marinade" in the classic sense.  I rubbed it best I could and it ended up turning out wonderfully.  Served it with mashed potatoes that had the Japanese seasoning Shio Koji.  Best mashed potatoes I've had.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this and it was fantastic! I substituted raspberries for the cranberries in the sauce as my son hates cranberries so I cut back on the sugar a lot since raspberries are already sweet. So yummy!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is a delicious roast.  The only thing I would change is that I would make far less of the cranberry sauce, half or even less.  It's delicious, and a nice addition, but even a quarter of the recipe would be enough.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
delicious! Pork was boned, I had a tough time butterflying it (it didn't "unfurl"). Followed the recipe but marinated overnight. The 4lb butt took just 2 hours to cook to perfection. That was much earlier than I needed it so I wrapped it in foil and placed it in a warm oven for about another 2 hours. 
I had to continuously add liquid as the pan kept drying out, but that was okay because the pan drippings even with just water was super tasty!
Used thawed leftover cranberry sauce from Thanksgiving and served with jasmine rice, NYT roast carrots/thyme, and roasted broccoli.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was really good. I had a bone in piece and I removed the bone and marinated for about 4 hours because I ran out of time….longer would be better. Mine didn’t brown up as much as the pic and other comments about needing to cover with foil….I used white miso because that’s what I had. Overall it was a really great and different take on pork.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Prepared a 1-lb rolled pork roast for two with white miso (no sauce) on New Year’s Day. We enjoyed it very much. I Googled cooking time and was presented with a 30-minute estimate (I suspect AI was involved). It actually took between 1.25 and 1.5 hours 90 to cook. I’ll definitely make it again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Make as directed, with a smaller boneless roast. Made the full marinade recipe, slathered it all over, and tied the roast. It was easy and very delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Followed the instructions mostly, but skipped onion and used air fryer. Put some water on the bottom tray but did not baste.  Turned out great!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can this recipe be found in a cookbook???

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Thanks for all the tips re tenderloin and convection times. Perfect! My pepper grinder broke so I substituted Szechuan peppercorns for 1/2 the pepper because it was easier to mortar and pestle.  Wowza- that added a great fizz to the pork and you could still taste the richness of the miso.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this recipe this past weekend for a dinner with friends. I followed the recipe closely since it was my first time making it and I think 325 degrees for the oven was too hot. I liked the flavor from the marinade, but the meat was tough. I want to make it again and try 325 degrees for the first half hour to get a little browning on the edges, but then reduce it to 250 for the duration. It also cooked much faster than I expected.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Amazing recipe!! One of the best things I’ve made from NYT Cooking. Thank you.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I made this exactly as directed, even though re-rolling the pork shoulder once butterflied was awkward and resulted in about half the miso marinade rubbing right off. It smelled amazing almost from the minute it went in the oven and everyone's mouths were watering waiting to eat it.

It was bland and flavorless, with the external half dried out and barely edible and the center seeming slightly undercooked. The fat did not render at all. Quite possibly the worst thing I've ever made from the NYT.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I just finished baking my first batch.  These are without a doubt winners!  I think paired with some sharp cheese on an appetizer board is going to work perfectly. Just sweet enough.  
Also, what a great recipe.  Just follow along!  This is definitely one to use your stand mixer.  A food processor will not work to cream the butter, sugar and egg yolk. One thought is to not skip the egg wash.
I will be up late tonight mixing up additional batch to be certain to have enough to share these.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Judy, your cookies may have spread because the dough was over beaten/creamed. Shortbread dough should be just-mixed. Maybe try mixing everything by hand, if you used a mixer the first time. 

Also, I have found that some parchment papers are more slippery than others. I used to bake butter-heavy shortbread on an unbuttered pan! You might try a different paper.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: In Wisconsin, old-fashions are made with brandy as well, so I substituted that for the whiskey. Husband declared them delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Very tasty.  I opted out of the brandy and bourbon because I didn‘t have any.  Refrigerated the dough log overnight and they sliced up beautifully but they spread out while baking instead of retaining their shape.  Should I increase the oven temp?  Any ideas?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Name Judy Definitely steer clear of silpat mats, which can cause cookies to spread too much.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Name Judy same here. I froze the cut cookies on the sheet for 10 minutes and used parchment. I think it’s because of the huge proportion of butter to flour, which is much more than other shortbread recipes. What a waste of work and ingredients.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Name Judy. You need to make sure the oven is hot when they are put in.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are wonderful, and I love the bourbon-soaked cherries. I couldn’t find any candied orange peel, so I took a large grater to a navel orange and it worked great.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Great cookies! Followed the recipe pretty closely. Weighed everything rather than use volumes. They sliced easily (I used a serrated knife) and they did not spread. Not sure why some are finding this recipe difficult. The two best guesses: better to weigh the ingredients, and perhaps oven not fully preheated--I find it best to wait about 15 minutes after preheating to start baking most things. One tip: toss the soaked fruit with 3 TBsp of the flour before adding to the dough. Mixes in better.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Are these crunchy or cake like or scone like cookies?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: For the love of god, don't do what I did and let the bourbon boil a second too long with the dried fruit!!!  It caught on fire pretty quickly ;/ I think I'll do what others did the next time and skip the bourbon or rye!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can the logs be frozen and then sliced when ready to bake, like many other refrigerator cookies?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wondering if it would be ok to swap in pecans for almonds, or better to just leave out the nuts altogether?  Trying to work around an almond allergy, but stay as true as possible to the recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Absolutely.  This is America and our ancestors left Europe to escape from the food police.  Swap roasted* pecans for almonds or use peanuts or pinioli/pine-nuts or whatever floats your boat.

*Best way to roast nuts is in a toaster oven for 10 to 15 minutes.  No burning!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Maine Cookie - Golem's "this is America..." about absence of food police so do what you choose is  probably attempting humor.  But, I think you want know if it impacts the integrity (texture, structure, flavor) of the cookie to swap something in for blanched almonds.  I think there are several things that could work.  If you can use roasted and skinned chopped hazelnuts  those might be nice and they are white'ish like the almonds.   Pecans could be tasty too, a Bourbon BFF, and add brown fleck.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Maine cookie - one more thought for the nut swap you need to do from almonds due to allergies.  Added to my previous suggestions I would add pistachios.  Raw pistachio nut meats, not roasted salted pistachios.  I buy mine at Fiddeyment Farms (online and via Amazon) and they make great nuts for baking and cooking.  I think pistachios would be great here.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Maybe use rum? Gotta use something that I'll be likely to use again and I have outgrown bourbon.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Suzy Young I’d recommend you buy the bottle of bourbon because you can use it solely for making these cookies again and again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Suzy Young I use rum for this, too!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Perfect balance between cookie, chewy bourbon infused fruit bits and slivered almonds.
Did not have enough dried cherries and candied orange peel, so filled the gap with dried cranberries, candied lemon peel, and currant raisins.  Absolutely delicious!  Worth every step.  
Brushing the cookie dough with the bourbon fruit syrup egg white wash before baking is essential and results in an attractive sheen on the baked cookie.
Making again with just cherries and orange peel bc it's that good!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Anyone freeze and bake at a later date?  Tan k you

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: First time making them and they came out great.  Only thing I think I will do differently next time is to cut the cherries into smaller pieces. They were delicious but kind of took over the cookie.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Also thank you Genevieve for listing ALL ingredients (including almonds, etc.) by weight. No need for any measurement cups - bonus points in my book!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I LOVE these cookies.  I should have weighed the flour as the dough was too crumbly to form into a log.   A bit of water solved that problem.  Cookies held their shape on parchment paper.  I didn't soak in brandy, just hot water to plumb up the cherries and orange peel.  Next time I will definitely soak in brandy.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
The first time I made these I soaked cranberries in Krupnic (a spiced honey based Liqueur from Vancouver island) over night and it made the berrie really aromatic and rich tasting. I forgot the orange peel (don’t like vadied orange peel anywa) so I grated zest into the egg white  and liqueur mix! Yum!

This time I forgot that I was soaking the berries and the orange zest, for a second batch, so they sat for 48 h in bourbon!!  Tastes amazing! I recommend soaking the fruit rather than heating it up!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Many bakers have been commenting that butter is now contains more water than in the past, especially cheaper butters. Try adding a bit more flourer next time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Should be called shortbread, as it contains an egg yolk? I wonder if that itself might contribute to the cookie spreading when baked. And though Peaches is correct that shortbread dough should be "just-mixed," the recipe calls for beating to a "soft, smooth mass" without any cautionary notes about over-beating.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are so perfect. I made a double batch and they were a huge hit at a holiday party. The recipe is foolproof.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: What can I substitute for the bourbon?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Brandy.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: You can skip the bourbon and all the steps with it (soaking the fruit, draining it and using the bourbon for the egg white wash). You can add 2 teaspoons vanilla extract when you add the egg yolk. You can then just stir in the dried cherries and candied orange peel in step 3, along with the almonds. You don't have to then brush the rounds with the egg white, but if you want your cookies to have a little shine, you can whisk a teaspoon water into the egg white.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Genevieve Ko I actually prefer the look and taste of this method, whether adding vanilla or bourbon to the egg yolk. I had nice soft dried fruit on hand, so not soaking allowed the chunks to be more distinct in the finished cookie. Also froze cookies for 10 minutes before baking, and they kept their shape well. Unexpected bonus was my kitchen smelling like my mom’s the many years that she made Christmas stollen. Thank you!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Any ideas for almond substitutes? Family nut allergies…..

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I thought these were good, but not knock-my-socks off. I suspect if I’d had the cherries (I used dried apricots instead) they would have been improved with the added tang.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Brandy, cherries, and oranges are natural partners. Just make sure the orange peel is fresh. Last year's dried fruit (unless you freeze it) gets a musty taste. Also, I didn't have dried cherries but I had cherry-infused dried cranberries. Worked really well. I'm not sure cherries would have been better.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This recipe is a disaster, and I wish I had listened to my instincts about it. Way too much butter to the amount of flour and they dissolved into crumby puddles. After the first tray I cut the rest 1/4 inch to see if they would help. Marginal improvement. So disappointing because of the wasted time/work and ingredients.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I put on an untreated regular cookie sheet rather than using parchment hoping that would stop the spreading.  Definitely not overworked but still really spread.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Then did athe remainder of the batch on Reynolds’s parchment.  Didn’t spread as much. Note that pans were chilled in that they were in my cabinet which is on an outside wall. It’s around 12 degrees here.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I really loved the flavor so made another batch. Sliced thinner than recommended and froze them for about half an hour. Used parchment and they didn’t spread!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I have been making shortbread for years and never had such a disaster.  The batter spread even after being treated gently and cooled sufficiently….a waste of good ingredients.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used orange marmalade in place of the candied orange peel and added about a teaspoon of orange extract when cooking the fruit, but no bourbon, though I did add vanilla extract to the egg yolk as suggested in the comments. I needed to use a bit more flour (maybe 1/2-3/4 cup) to offset the added moisture, and also used a bit of vanilla extract with the egg white wash. They turned out great and people were asking about the recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
These tasted great, but the texture was more like a heavy cream scone than a crisp shortbread. I used rye. Mixed with hand mixer, then spoon, then finished with hands (light kneading). The soaked fruit adds a lot of moisture. If skipping the alcohol, I recommend plumping the fruit in juice or water, to keep batter from being crumby. 

Dough was sticky and soft. Formed a rough log, chilled an hour, rolled again to 2” log shape. Chilled another hour. Cookies did not spread.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are great and I’ll make these every year. Used Kerrygold butter, brandy, dried cherries, and dried orange peel (it’s what I already had in my supplies). Did not use egg wash.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made these w/o the almonds, and everyone agreed they are delish…but we all also agree we can’t taste any bourbon! Wondering if it’s just us? Also wondering if I could take the rest of the reserved bourbon and brush it on the hot cookies as they rest, or will that compromise the shortbread texture?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I loved this recipe! (I'm always disappointed that my bakes don't seem to look like the photographed version, however.) I refrigerated the roll of cookie dough for 2 days before baking. It was easy to cut. Perhaps I overbaked it but the edges did seem too brown. I will try it again. The crumbliness and the crunchiness of the shortbread was divine. Seriously good!!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made these as a part of 13 different cookie recipes. While the flavors of the cherries & oranges with the shortbread were good, I cookies were just okay. If you make them I recommend caramelizing some Demerara sugar on top to continue with the “old fashioned” theme.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Second batch. Sooooo good. I did everything by hand ( no mixer), no problem.
This time I double the cherry orange mixture to have extra to put on top of a vanilla ice cream scoop next to the cookies when served for desert.
Thank you for the recipe!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I don’t have space (or money) for a standup mixer but I’ve always gotten around it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wow these are good. I would coarsely chop the cherries and almonds next time. The leftover bourbon syrup is so good that I might just make more of it to add to a bevvie!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Great recipe - also wonderful with candied yuzu peel and pistachios vs orange peel and almonds.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: So so good! Made as written, except I got 32 cookies instead of 24, and had to adjust the bake time down accordingly, to 13-14 minutes. Next time, I will soak the fruit for even longer.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are so lovely. I made them to use up some homemade candied peel from my Christmas bread and they made my whole house smell like the holidays. I didn't have cherries so I used dried cranberries plus a little candied ginger. Delicious.  And perfect because it doesn't make an acre of cookies.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Flavor and texture were excellent; it could have used more bourbon flavor.  Next time, I'll use more to soak the fruit and I'll soak overnight. 

 I'm new to baking so any other suggestions as to how to increase that element and keep the balance of the other ingredients are most certainly welcome.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: could one use dried cranberries instead of sour cherries?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @louise yes! Made these yesterday and couldn’t find cherries or candied orange peel, so I used regular ol’ dried salad topping cranberries, and crystallized ginger. Also added some chocolate chips to counter the bite of the ginger. Followed the recipe otherwise and they were sooooo delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
These are all "add-ins", so replacement of one nut for another will not affect the integrity of the final product, only the taste. The same is true for the dried fruit or the spirits. Make your swaps to suit your taste, aversions, allergies and pantry availability. Orange juice would probably work instead of the spirits, if one prefers.
Keep in mind that potency will make a difference. Vanilla, for instance, is far more potent than rum.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Will it work to use other mix-ins (different dried fruit-- can't find the ones called for) if I'm not using the bourbon, and replacing with vanilla as Genevieve Ko said in response to someone else? I keep reading through the various shortbread recipes on the app, but many don't have weights so I don't bother, and others call for baking in a pan into bars, etc. (Also, does anyone know how we an be alerted if someone responds to a comment?)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Has anyone tried these nut free? I can't think of a reason the nuts couldn't be left out, but I don't want to ruin the texture of the shortbread.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Rachel did it without nuts   Came out great

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I think the sliced almonds are importantly for both the taste and the texture. They look pretty, too (I used sliced unpeeled almonds, so there were specks of brown). The almond taste could be replaced by a smidgeon of almond extract (it is not made with almonds!), but what for a slight crunch? Pumpkin seeds? Toasted cashews (they are not nuts, but fruits)? Or maybe toasted coconut, and rum for the booze?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Peaches 
Agree with you to add almond extract. My former Danish grandma-in-law told me she used it as a replacement for vanilla extract in all her baking recipes, which were always great. Plus these days, almond extract is much cheaper than vanilla...

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Excellent and simple treat. Deliciously light with just a hint of the bourbon.  As with any cooking/baking, don’t go cheap on the ingredients, even the whiskey. A keeper.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Needed a nut-free cookie for my book club.  Didn't have dried sour cherries or bourbon.   So I used up the last of the dried cranberries and soaked them with the candied orange peel in Grand Marnier.   Skipped the almonds.    I love that they can be frozen and sliced for baking later.   Adding to my Christmas cookie list but next year I will make sure to have the dried cherries and bourbon!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are very tasty. I swapped out the butter with margarine due to the cost. I’ll have to try with butter next time as I found it difficult to cut into slices without them looking a bit like mounds- which maybe that would be a cuter look if I can’t cut clean round coins of these

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Loved the cookie but did not like the mottled look of the glaze and felt it did not add to the flavor profile and left it off the second batch. Do make your own candied peel, so good!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Followed recipe, refrigerated overnight. No issues with spreading. Delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is a flawed recipe. The cookies spread out into a mess-- total bust. I read other shortbread cookie recipes, and it seems this recipe calls for a much higher butter to flour ratio.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: So delicious and unusual. No issues with the directions or the prep. I doubled the recipe and am so glad I did. Everyone loved them and they are definitely going into the holiday cookie hall of fame to make again. I used my homemade candied orange peel.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: For sure weigh everything.  Still had problems with spreading until i tweaked as above. And oven was well preheated.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made no changes to the original recipe - used bourbon to soak/simmer the orange peel and used dried cranberries instead of cherries and they turned out so well. So crisp tasty. The cookies didn't spread either and I used a Silpat. Fantastic recipe and amazing how short the cookie is considering it's granulated sugar used, not powdered sugar and no corn starch. highly recommend.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wanted to share that i also had issues with spreading in this recipe.  But the cookies are still delicious! Instead of candied orange peel (which i couldnt find) i used candied oranges from Trader Joes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I normally I scoff at all the changes people make … I mean find another recipe…but I did the same here. Used brandy instead of bourbon and pecans and golden raisins instead of cherries. These cookies were fantastic! Thanks nyt!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Love this recipe!  Used pecans bc I did not have almonds, otherwise made as per recipe.  
Adding this to my list of Christmas cookies to make each year.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I couldn’t taste the bourbon, so may leave it out next time. Otherwise fantastic!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’ve made these several times, and they never come out the same! After reading deeply into the subject of shortbread, I’ve decided that the temperature of the softened butter may be the most important variant. I am going to try this with melted butter, and add some cornstarch or rice flour. General observations: you need to squeeze out the fruit really well, and the cookies come out better if slightly overbaked.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’ve made these twice now. They’re so flavorful! With the second batch, i rolled the log thinner so i could get more cookies — these are the perfect nibbling cookie, they don’t have to be big to be satisfying. Do you think i could freeze the dough? Not sure what it would do to the fruit chunks.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Amazing! I subbed out almonds   dried cherries for fresh cherries, and orange for lime to make summer time cherry limeade shortbread cookies - were a hit! Had to let the cherries cook down a bit more to dry them out

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Perfect Christmas cookies

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can anyone clarify: after cutting the long in 1/2 slices and separate 1” apart do you leave the cookies standing upright or lay down flat?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The important part is to have every cookie as close to the same weight and then form into a round shape w/the same thickness. Your hands will warm the dough so handle as little as possible. If you find the dough getting softer as you work it, stick the dough (formed & unformed) back in the fridge for a few minutes. They should be laying like flat coins on the sheet.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: As a lover of shortbread and old fashioned, this cookie is an absolute delight! I used Jack Daniel’s (and in the process I’ve learned it’s legally a bourbon but not marketed so) and Trader Joe’s candied orange slices (as I couldn’t find peels), diced. I followed Claire Saffitz’s advice and rolled the dough (it was a bit sticky) in parchment paper, which worked well. Refrigerated for 2 days, I think. I have a 5-yo and a pregnant relative so I asked ChatGPT if this cookie following the recipe is safe for them to consume and the short answer is yes, having one or two is similar to a trace amount of alcohol found in a ripe banana, yogurt, etc. so rejoice! Cheers!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Had trouble finding a store w/candied orange peel as fast as I needed it; used TJ's dried mandarins. Was still really good and easy. Will do again. Do let cool on cookie sheet as they need time to set-up.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: was it worth making the candied orange peel for this? maybe not unless it's a special occasion. but what a fun twist on a shortbread cookie!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These finally made the holiday cookie spread this year and were one of my favorites. I'm wondering how to keep the rounded shape. Mine were deformed after coming back out of the snooze in the fridge. I beat on them and tried to roll them a bit again with marginal results.  One suggestion is nesting into a sliced open a paper towel cardboard. That would require planning ahead. Folks have other ideas (aside from purchasing more equipment that I don't have space for)?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: FABULOUS! I did not have rye whiskey, so I used Jameson Irish Whiskey. I added candied (not crystallised) ginger & candied lemon peel to the fruit mix. For the glaze, I used 1 TABLESPOON of the Jameson syrup to the 1 egg white to amplify the whiskey. My slices were 0.64cm thick, but they took longer to bake b/c of the fruit content (25-27 minutes). At first, I was scared b/c they looked overdone, but weren't. Delicious results. Everyone at the craic thought so too!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I'm all for soaking things (especially dried cherries) in bourbon, but I also prefer really crisp shortbread.  These were excellent to start, but within an hour had already lost crispness.  And the morning after, well, they are yet softer.  Yes, I drained the cherries thoroughly.  Think I'll try the same recipe without fruit (but keep the glaze) and see what happens...

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are so good! I'm on my third batch because my brother and his kids devoured everything in sight - and found the ones I had stashed in the freezer.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Diane . Sure! Great idea to freeze the dough. I'd defrost a few hours at least B4 baking, but you could also cut from frozen (strong, sharp knife?) and consider it will take longer to bake. Have fun!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: When this popped up in NYT Cooking feed it was "bingo" for Christmas Eve dessert.  I only need 4 servings and I don't want a lot leftover.  So I'm going to try this "small batch" technique of cutting this recipe in half and using a 6" springform pan to bake it in.  My experience with all kinds of cheesecake recipes that call for 9" springform, as this one does, it a 6" works so well for a half recipe.  We'll see with this one too.  Just an idea for others needing small bake size.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I'm here to tell you this is to die for. Absolutely love cheesecake and this, well fix it and you'll find out. Did use Pamela's Honey Graham crackers as one daughter can't have gluten. Defy anyone to tell the difference from regular. Orange blossom water is a must as vanilla would significantly alter the flavor. Mandarins are the road to go vs clementines.  For the compote I added Cointreau which upped the flavor. Mon dieu!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Luther. How much Cointreau did you add? Sounds like a great addition! Did anyone else like the cheesecake without any compote? (The clementine taste got overpowered by the cranberry compote to some degree—so a few guests preferred it without the compote).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @LPD I added almost 3 Tablespoons of Cointreau and it was perfect!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I would love to make this, but don't have a food processor. Any help with making it unplugged would be appreciated.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Maria Catherine
Any type of blender or hand mixer will do. It will take longer but you can achieve the same smoothness.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Maria Catherine for the ginger snap crust, put the cookies in a plastic bag and crush with a wooden spoon or rolling pin.  For the rest just mix really well with whatever you feel works - a whisk, Wooden spoon or fork - I never use a food processor - it takes a little longer to get the texture right but it’s hands on and quieter!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Maria Catherine  I’ve read this recipe & feel familiar with the ‘why’ of a food processor for this type of cake. I’m sure it’s possible (with a lot of strength & determination) to combine the ingredients by ‘hand whipping’  but as a seasoned cook I imagine it would be quite a feat due to the thickness & resistance that these particular ingredients present to fully incorporate & get everything evenly distributed. Perhaps a friend could loan you their food processor? For almost 20 years I used my grandmother’s broken old Cuisinart (I had to manually engage it due to a broken plastic part :) …finally I got a lucky sale & bought a new one. I consider it a kitchen must for so many meals. If you happen to live near Asheville,NC I’d happily give you my grandmothers. I’ve not managed to part with it :)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’m assuming the crust should go up the sides as well?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: From the picture it doesn't look like it. That browning you see on the sides is cooked cheesecake.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Jim - The crust in this is intended to be only on the bottom based on recipe instructions.  See Step 2:  "Press evenly into the bottom of the prepared pan."

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Holy moly is this amazing. My only adjustment would be to make 1.5x the crust in order to ensure having enough. It was a teeny bit skimpy. But goodness! The flavors are incredible.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made it gluten free with gf graham crackers for the crust and my kids and declared it a 9.5/10!  Easy to make ahead and pull out for a holiday meal. Fancy looking too - easy win. Personally I would up the ginger but I followed the recipe and everyone liked it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is a keeper, loved by everyone! I don't usually like cheesecake, but for some reason this recipe looked so good I wanted to try it. I found it took much longer to cook. I added 20 min at 250. It was still jiggly in middle when I removed from oven. It was good that night, really excellent next day after 24 hours in fridge. Next time I would bake a day ahead. I cut sugar to 1 cup each for cake (white sugar) and topping (coconut sugar) and it was delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I made this recipe for our family Easter celebration, but tweaked some of the holiday seasonal ingredients to make it appropriate for spring time and wanted to share because it came out perfectly!

1. I replaced the mandarins with Meyer lemons. I used the rind and juice of 3 whole lemons.

2. I replaced the cranberry compote with a blueberry compote.

Otherwise I followed the recipe exactly. It was marvelous!!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Unqualified success! So delicious! My oven runs a little cool, so it took 70 minutes. I used Grand Marnier instead of vanilla. Other than that, followed the recipe exactly. No cracks on top, lighter than a cream cheese-only cheesecake, and so, so, so delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Used key lime juice/zest instead of the oranges (same proportions) and used graham cracker crust. The extra zest and acidity of the limes helped balance the heft of the cream cheese. Very pleased with the result.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Ridiculously delicious! What a find. People who normally don’t rave were asking for seconds. A bit time consuming but worth it. The ricotta and clementine flavor really comes through. And I wasn’t sure the jiggling would go away just leaving the “unfinished” cheesecake in the oven but cook it did! Only addition was some whole cloves in the cranberry which needed that addition.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: For the best crust with an Italian Ricotta cheesecake, use Amaretti cookies.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Not sure that this recipe would halve well. A 9 inch springform divided evenly would be 5 1/2. Is that okay to use. 6 inch?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Julia - Yes, a half recipe of this cheesecake should work well in a 6" springform.  I do that often with cheesecake and other recipes specifying 9" springform when I cut them in half.  It has always worked.  This is on my Christmas Eve dessert menu so haven't tried it yet, but the 6" for half recipe is what I'm planning.  The area of a 9" round pan is 63.5".  A 6" is 29".  It works.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I had to cook the cheese cake considerably longer - 30 minutes more-and it looked beautiful when I took it out of the oven. Then it cracked badly - should be ok since the cranberries will be on top!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Has anyone made this in “steps”? E.g., will the compote hold up made 2 days ahead?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Sara - Yes, I think the compote will hold up made a couple of days ahead.  In my experience all compotes do.  And this is a sorta, kinda cranberry sauce and that is always a good make ahead candidate.  I have this on my Christmas Eve menu for dessert and I plan to make the compote on the 22nd and bake the cheesecake on the 23rd.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: How do you get the rolled edge like in the picture?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I don't think it's a rolled edge. From making cheesecakes in the past, the filling will puff up, sometimes well above the edge of the crust. As it cooks, that bit cooks a bit quicker than the rest and sets up more. When the center, which isn't as set (as in the directions), gets cool, it'll shrink/fall some. That will sometimes make that "rolled edge" effect.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was so good! I got nervous about how jiggly  it was in the middle, even after letting it rest in the oven.  So I turned the oven back on at 250 for about 10 more minutes . It did end up cracking in the middle, but the texture was perfect.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Tatyana this was really good advice! Cracked a tiny bit, but was fully set and the texture was good.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was fabulous. Followed instructions except used 10 inch pan. No adjustment in time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My mom just made this and strained the chunky cranberry topping into a really pretty gelee that I think looks better and more refined/dessert-like

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My favorite ricotta comes in a 12oz container, so I 3/4th the recipe and it turned out great!  Baked in a 9" pan so not as tall but the cook time was still the same.  Brilliant color!  Added to the Thanksgiving menu last minute because how can you not?!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Family did not like the cranberry topping, cheesecake was delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This looks so good, but I live at 7000 feet and am at a loss as to how to proceed with timing, temperature, liquids.  Any tips?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used limes instead of mandarin oranges.  I skipped the orange water and vanilla. I put a tablespoon of lime zest in the crust and a tablespoon of lime zest in the filling.  Even for the topping, I used lime juice and zest.  I ended up with a lovely sweet/tart cheesecake and topping.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this for a friend’s birthday and everyone deemed an excellent and absolutely delicious recipe!  A very festive appearance, as well!  I was nervous trying this, as I hadn’t baked a cheesecake in many years.  But it was easier than I expected.  It does seem that it might need baking an additional 5-7 minutes before turning oven off.  Would also suggest that using Fiori di Sicilia instead of the Orange Blossom Water might be a variation worth trying… a little less floral, but still with a citrus profile.  A tablespoon or so of Cranberry Liqueur is also a lovely enhancement to the compote topping. Will definitely make again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Was made as directed with the exception of a touch more juice and zest and a ¼ cup less sugar. 
Loved it! Has me wondering if it can be frozen and thawed without sacrificing flavor or texture?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Where is the ricotta in this recipe?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Very good. Followed the recipe exactly. Made three days in advance and still good on the third day. Richer than I had expected for a ricotta cheesecake, taste and texture more like that of an all-cream cheese cake. Clementine flavor really comes through.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Did only a bottom crust and the sides browned beautifully just like the picture, very nice! Didn't use food processor, but did use a stand up mixer.  Could be done with a hand mixer with care!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this just after Thanksgiving as a test for Christmas dinner. For Christmas I made it a day ahead. It was a huge hit with everyone.  My husband is a T1 diabetic and even with full sugar this dessert was manageable for him.  Very tasty and has a wonderful presentation without being fussy.  I will definitely make this again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Excellent recipe! Have made this twice now!  For the baking time, I found that it took more like 63-64 minutes to get to the amount of jiggle called for.  I also left it in the turned-off oven for about 18 minutes instead of 15.  Would highly recommend trying Fiori Di Sicilia extract from Italy (available through King Arthur Baking Company, among others) as a delicious alternative flavoring in place of the orange blossom water/vanilla extract.  FOR THE COMPOTE:  Agree with several other cooks’ suggestion to reduce the amount of sugar in the compote to perhaps just 1 cup or even just 3/4 cup.  I also recommend using about 1 tablespoon of grated fresh ginger… if you have it on hand in place of the ground ginger.  Finally, a tablespoon or so of Grand Marnier or a Cranberry Liqueur makes the compote even more delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Could this cheesecake be frozen before adding the topping?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Jane I can’t comment on this specific cheesecake but in my experience many cheese based items,  once frozen,  get a weird gritty texture….at the same time I’ve bought some pretty decent frozen cheesecakes over the years. I’m not sure what’s added in more commercially sold cheesecakes that eliminates that gritty texture.. good luck! :)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Love cheesecake
I'm making this for Thanksgiving, straight forward recipe, you could sub out juice oranges for the clementines, mandarins imo.  I've never put cornstarch in a cheesecake, so I'll need to come back and rate the recipe after the family taste/texture test.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Melissa please do; I hesitate to use cornstarch also, as four eggs should the trick at firming up this cheesecake. Let us know! Did anyone else out there skip the cornstarch?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Id like to make this gluten free. I guess I'll just bake the cheesecake without the crust. Has anyone done this?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Jean just made this with Mi-Del gluten free ginger snaps and it worked perfectly for the crust!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Use Pamela's honey graham crackers. Gluten free. It's what I did because one of my daughters has to have gluten free. Absolutely can't tell the difference. Whole Foods

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made it gf with a graham cracker crust (used Schar brand gf crackers and added powdered ginger). Came out great!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: So wish this was a 100% Ricotta recipe - no cream cheese. Would make it lighter & healthier.  The NYT does have a Ricotta cheesecake recipe that is to die for & not difficult to make.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is an amazing recipe.  Everyone enjoyed it after a lasagna dinner because it was light and flavorful.  I did make two adjustments regarding clementines, mine were rather dry, so zesting was not possible. I peeled them, scraped the interior off and julienned the peel for both the cake and the cranberries.  Also had less juice than requested for the cranberries so added 1/4 c Cointreau.  Excellent flavor the day of, and more citrusy the next.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Would a mixer work instead of a food processor?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If you don't have a food processor for the crumbs, wrap the cookies in a kitchen towel and bash them with a rolling pin!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Do I bake the thing on a sheet pan? So the spring form pan goes on a sheet pan for the whole baking process? This is my first cheesecake, but I thought a water bath was used (like crème brûlée). 
I made the compote already and it’s so good I wish I made double.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this yesterday for a dinner party and it was a huge hit.  I ground up the cookies in a small blender and used my stand up mixer to make the filling.  My grocery store didn't have the orange blossom water but I found it at Total Wines. I used slightly less sugar and added a dash of ground cloves and cinnamon to the topping.  Other than that the instructions were perfect and it turned out beautiful and delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is so delicious! Earned rave reviews at its debut at a party last night. Perfect holiday dessert. I also let it go a little longer and then let it sit in the hot oven an extra 15 minutes. Added some nutmeg and a shake of cardamom to the compote, along with a little rum. Strongly recommend the Trader Joe’s triple ginger gingersnaps for the crust.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: A very good cheesecake and topping. We made it for our holiday/solstice dinner. For the crust, I used McVittie's and fresh ground ginger (about 1 tsp) and put it up the sides. For the filling, I used 200g sugar, some creme fraiche because my package of ricotta was only 12 ounces. Instead of orange blossom water, I used orange extract. For the topping, I used 200g sugar and fresh ground ginger. Clementines are late this year in the Bay Area, so I used satsumas.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I am going to try this with part skim ricotta. Will that be okay? I bought it by mistake.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It should be fine. You could add a few tablespoons of sour cream if you need the fat or the consistency isn't to your liking.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I assume the “sheet pan” in step five is the same as the springform pan in step one? It makes it sound like it could be a different pan which makes no sense. Not many questions on the string here ask about substituting if you don’t own a springform pan, such as in glass or Corningware, which probably impacts cooking times.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @E Scott no I think the idea is to put the whole springform pan on a sheet pan - so if it leaks, any liquid ends up on the sheet pan rather than your oven floor

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Heather thanks!! Helpful

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was an unqualified Christmas Eve winner ❤️Everyone said it was the best cheesecake they ever had.! Hits all the Christmas flavors-definitely do ginger snaps for crust . Wonderful

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this for Christmas dinner dessert and wow, it was among the best cheesecakes I’ve had. My family loved it - I will definitely be making again. The store was out of fresh cranberries, so we used fresh cherries instead and cut the sugar by half (probably will cut more next time). Amazing!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wow. When this thing is fully chilled it is a glorious cheesecake. We tried it first 5 hours into cooling and it was good. Things hadn't set right just yet. A day later though, we couldn't stop eating.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this for Christmas Eve dinner and it was fabulous. We had homemade cranberry sauce with the meal, so for the cheesecake I made a raspberry coulis instead. 100% will make this again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can anyone tell me why my cranberry glaze turned hard when I left to cool to room temperature. I thought I followed the recipe to the letter but now I can’t spread the glaze

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: FANTASTIC!!  Yes the top of mine still cracked but the topping DOES hide it and YES it really is delicious!!!  Don't change a thing and you'll love it!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Loved this recipe. I followed it as written and it came out beautifully and was delicious. The orange blossom water was interesting, but next time I might substitute orange oil or try the vanilla. I will definitely bake this again! Thank you Ms.Ko

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Easy recipe but wow does it impress! Delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Lessened sugar by 50 g, could have gone a bit less. Used a simple almond and butter crust and it was perfect, just 1 Tb brown sugar. Didn't have orange blossom flower so used vanilla and very glad I did... orange flavor was strong enough as is, and vanilla added a different depth. Didn't get a chance to do it for Christmas so did an orange/ blueberry compote with it, was excellent!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @AM Would be interested to know how you made the crust. I don’t have any cookies or crackers on hand but do have almond flour and would love to make this for a church picnic. With rhubarb compote!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious! And very festive. I made it for Christmas dinner (making it the day before).  It was a big hit, and I’ll make it again when I need a rich and fancy dessert.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I am really in need of a ricotta cheesecake with NO cream cheese. This looked so much like one my mom made once when I was in grade school, but never made again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Way too sweet for me and, rather than having a rich, dairy-forward density, it leans toward eggy. The sweetness is adjustable, but the texture makes this a "not again" recipe for me.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Critterina look for ricotta cheesecake on Italian websites with Google translate enabled (Google Chrome internet uses a plug-in to make this simple). Italians have made cheesecake for centuries without (American origin) cream cheese and I agree the texture/flavour 100% ricotta gives is a completely different dessert

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: We followed this recipe to a tee and it came out perfect. It was the first cheesecake that we made and while it did crack a bit no one noticed with the cranberries on top. No one missed the pumpkin pie at thanksgiving and people took slices to go

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Are we supposed to place the springform pan on a sheet pan? And when do we take the cake out of the pan? After it’s coated w cranberries and chilled?? Thanks for advice here!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can you is this recipe for cheesecake bites done in a mini muffin pan?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: sublime

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I would like to make this in a mini muffin pan. Has anyone tried it. Want to present it at a cocktail party as a pickup dessert

author: Genevieve Ko
