commentBody: What could I garnish the top with to make it look a little more Christmasy? Mint leaves? Basil leaves?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Rebecca Kern
I think you might try making very thin strips of candied orange peel that are twisted a bit while still soft would be both pretty and tasty.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Made the batter recipe as written. Added more crumbs.

After tasting, we all agreed that the orange flavor isn’t pronounced enough once it’s cooked. I would add more zest and another teaspoon of orange blossom essence. 

I’m a fan of basque style cheesecake. This cheesecake is not my favorite. I give it a solid B.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Crust subs: a sleeve of sugar ice cream cones (stale, from summer) + two thawed molasses cookies we made last month or the month before + quarter cup panko. 25 shakes of masala chai spice dust. MacGyvered Christmas concoctions are the best concoctions!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Definetly needs to bake for longer than 60 minutes...a jiggley center is not a good indicator of appropriate doneness as stated in the recipe. So embarrassing when I served this Christmas Eve. When I cut into it, the center turned into a soupy puddle...tasted yummy but seriously underbaked!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The topping A+ the actual cheesecake needed way more cooking time and although I cooked an extra 15mins and left in oven 15 mins off it still was runny and undercooked in center.  Not my favorite cheesecake recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My new go-to cheesecake receipe.  I'll try it with raspberries, cherries, who knows what else?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this as written and we honestly thought it wasn't that good.   The cranberry sauce topping is nice and tart, but the cheesecake suffered from blandness. (I used orange extract instead of the water, but doubt that would have changed it much.)  But the texture is good and I would add more citrus flavor or liqueur to liven up the cake.  We used a processor and observed the baking times: you can fearlessly turn off the oven when the center is still runny.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Not only is this by far the best cheesecake I've ever made, it might be the best one I've ever eaten.  Leaving the cheesecake to cool in the oven after turning it off and opening the door helps prevent cracking, but you won't care if it does because it is covered by the cranberries.  I'll likely use fewer cranberries next time, but only to reduce guilt not to make it taste better.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this over the holidays for dinner with friends, it was delicious. Mine did crack on top, despite following instructions, but it really doesn't matter because the cranberry covers that up. My husband said it was spectacular and our friends took a couple of slices home. The gingersnap crust works really well with this, I'd stick with the recipe and not use the usual graham crackers!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was so lovely, and doable in my parents' kitchen (which has almost no kitchen gadgets—yes, I did miss the food processor.) There was a touch too much orange flavor for me, but I'm guessing a lot of that is down to the size and juiciness of your citrus.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I already reviewed this delicious cheesecake, but I wanted to add a tip. I found the crust really sticks to the botton of the springform and it becomes very difficult to get it out. The last time I made it, I buttered the bottom of the springform, then put a layer of parchment with the edges slightly sticking out, then buttered the parchment. It came out of the pan like a charm and the parchment peeled off easily! Everyone raves about this cheesecake!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Shrimp Cocktail With Cranberry Cocktail Sauce Recipe
commentBody: This time of year (Thanksgiving) we have access to fresh cranberries. I probably would cook the fresh cranberries first until they pop then add the rest of the ingredients. Then puree. The cooked sauce will keep longer. Sorry for the thought experiment.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Shrimp Cocktail With Cranberry Cocktail Sauce Recipe
commentBody:
So frozen, not fresh cranberries...  
Can we use fresh cranberries that we have first frozen ourselves (I always have bags of cranberries in the freezer), or is there something about the processing of store-sold frozen cranberries that makes them better for this recipe?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Shrimp Cocktail With Cranberry Cocktail Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Yes, absolutley you can use cranberries you've frozen yourself. I happened to have one cup of them in the freezer and the sauce came our perfectly!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Shrimp Cocktail With Cranberry Cocktail Sauce Recipe
commentBody: From the longer article this recipe was in, the explanation for the frozen cranberries is a bit more clear: "...He added that there’s a molecular change that occurs when the berries are frozen that makes them softer and easier to blend." And also a note that fresh were "too dry to break down completely and end up imparting an astringent bitterness." Sounds like the ones we freeze ourselves might be OK?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Shrimp Cocktail With Cranberry Cocktail Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Just prepared it as is, it was Perfect, having it with cranberry is an acquired test. Thank you

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Shrimp Cocktail With Cranberry Cocktail Sauce Recipe
commentBody: This was good and easy to make but was a little sweeter than I would have liked. I'll reduce the amount of maple syrup next time.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Shrimp Cocktail With Cranberry Cocktail Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Excellent recipe. I will add that it would be good to use large food processor as the frozen cranberry takes a bit more time to reach the jam like texture. I also suggest just a bit over two minutes for poaching the shrimp to be more chewy. Other than that, this is such a fabulous recipe and the sauce is the king.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Shrimp Cocktail With Cranberry Cocktail Sauce Recipe
commentBody: When should the shrimp be peeled and deveined? Before poaching?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Shrimp Cocktail With Cranberry Cocktail Sauce Recipe
commentBody: @Deanna. Thats the way that I read the recipe, and the way I plan to make it.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Shrimp Cocktail With Cranberry Cocktail Sauce Recipe
commentBody: After.  The shell helps from overcooking and adds flavor. You can find deveined shell-on shrimp at the grocery store.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Shrimp Cocktail With Cranberry Cocktail Sauce Recipe
commentBody: @Pat I don’t think there’s a way to devein shrimp without peeling them first, so if you buy shrimp with the shells on, be prepared both to peel and then devein them.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Shrimp Cocktail With Cranberry Cocktail Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Why do you suppose we’re not to use fresh cranberries? Wondering if it’s a consistency issue and if I can chop & freeze ahead of time?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Shrimp Cocktail With Cranberry Cocktail Sauce Recipe
commentBody: From the intro to the recipe:  "Be sure to use frozen cranberries straight from the freezer, which will purée into a smooth, tasty dip. (Fresh cranberries won’t break down as much as you try to blitz them and their sour bite is too sharp.)"

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Shrimp Cocktail With Cranberry Cocktail Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I always freeze extra cranberries when they’re available. Recipe doesn’t say anything about washing & draining the cranberries. I guess, since I don’t wash them prior to freezing, I should give them a quick rinse before preparing the rest of the sauce?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Shrimp Cocktail With Cranberry Cocktail Sauce Recipe
commentBody: It's probably not a good idea to use any good that has not been washed. Do you normally wash your berries before eating them?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Shrimp Cocktail With Cranberry Cocktail Sauce Recipe
commentBody:
I don't have a food processor, will my blender be up to the task?  
I am spending Xmas alone and planned on shrimp cocktail so finding the alternative sauce recipe is perfect timing. It sounds yummy!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Shrimp Cocktail With Cranberry Cocktail Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I can see the need to start with frozen cranberries but don’t understand the comment about fresh cranberries being too tart.  My frozen cranberries have no sugar added so should have the same tartness as fresh.  Am I missing something?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Shrimp Cocktail With Cranberry Cocktail Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Started out with the basic recipe, but wanted more color/flavor/texture at the end.  Next time I'll simply buy pre-cooked "fancy-looking" shrimp for a better presentation.  BUT, when it came time to make the cocktail sauce the color was lacking.  Added about 1/4 cup of shredded raw beets and two drops of red food coloring.  Another tsp of lemon juice.  BAM.  Beets added more texture and "woodsy" sweetness.  A keeper with the changes.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Shrimp Cocktail With Cranberry Cocktail Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I'm in France and frozen cranberries are hard to cone by. Would lingonberry or red currents work?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Shrimp Cocktail With Cranberry Cocktail Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I enjoyed making this recipe for my very non-traditional Christmas Eve dinner! It's a really fun flavor twist on a really familiar flavor, and I loved it! Even my oldest son, who "hates" cranberries (news to me), enjoyed this refreshingly flavor-adjacent match to the traditional cocktail sauce! And it's super easy - which is always a treat!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Shrimp Cocktail With Cranberry Cocktail Sauce Recipe
commentBody: What a hit at our holiday party! Everyone loved this change of pace cocktail sauce.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Shrimp Cocktail With Cranberry Cocktail Sauce Recipe
commentBody: This looks great, but Ann is allergic to horseradish.  Any ideas for a substitute?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Shrimp Cocktail With Cranberry Cocktail Sauce Recipe
commentBody: This was a total success.  I served it at Thanksgiving.  Everyone loved the twist on cocktail sauce and it tasted great.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Shrimp Cocktail With Cranberry Cocktail Sauce Recipe
commentBody: Letting this dip marinate for at least 24 hours enhances its flavor and COLOR tremendously.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Shrimp Cocktail With Cranberry Cocktail Sauce Recipe
commentBody: I love everything cranberry but was totally surprised when I came across this recipe! Cranberries in cocktail sauce? The recipe is terrific & is a wonderful change of pace with shrimp. I will definitely make it part of my cranberry repertoire.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Thanks to all who made these! So, I usually don't prefer big cookies, but in testing, I liked the crisp-chewy combination of rounds that spread and baked into 3 1/2- to 4-inch disks. (That's what you get if you follow this recipe.) If you want to make these smaller, I recommend baking them at 375 degrees until the edges are brown and the center no longer looks wet. (For 1-ounce balls shaped with a #40 scoop, that'll take 11-13 minutes.) Enjoy!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is great information, Genevieve. You should add it as a note to the recipe so more people can see it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Genevieve Ko Maybe I’m the odd person out here, but I think the size is great. Eat the whole cookie, folks!

author: Genevieve Ko
Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I will never understand the giant cookie thing. A recipe with this amount of butter/sugar/flour etc. is a 3- to 4-dozen cookie recipe. If you really want to eat three or four cookies in one go, have at it! I do, sometimes. But it's so much better for the digestion, and the waistline, to make them a normal (small) size, and eat them one at a time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Margaret The size of a drop cookie really does have a significant effect on the texture. It is difficult in a home kitchen to achieve the perfect crispy-edge, chewy center with a smaller cookie. I really only struggle with the giant cookie issue when entertaining: no one wants to take on a cookie the size of a saucer at a party!  Otherwise, I bake at the size that optimizes the texture (for my taste) and cut the cookie into quarters.  If the recipe sounds good to you otherwise, maybe try a sheet at suggested size and another at a smaller size. If the textural difference isn’t a big deal for you, then by all means downsize! It may take a little testing to see how small you can go before you lose the chew/crisp balance. Enjoy the process!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I so agree w you on the cookie size issue.  Thats a lotta cookie.  I’m chilling my dough now - I formed it into a log and plan to do a messy slice and bake - but not so big.  Will get at least 24 cookies.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Dede, you HAVE TO tell us how the cookies from your cookie dough log did!  After chilling, was the log easy to slice?  Did the cookies turn out crispy on the edges and chewy inside?  Do you have cookie dough log regret or would you do it again?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I think that people read these recipes with no intention of making them but love to scold. Giant cookies are a lot of fun--perfect for sharing. If you make them smaller, they won't be the same. Nothing says that one person has to eat the whole thing on their own. Geez. I, for example, make a batch, and freeze them already dipped so we can have fresh cookies on demand. These, by the way, are delicious, and like I said, perfect to share!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I imagine adding a cup of yellow raisins would make this cookie irresistible

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Nancy Lauka - yes, I tried this and the cookies were wonderful!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Nancy Lauka agree. We wish we put chocolate or raisins in it

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Nancy Laukai I added a scant cup of Ghirardelli’s bittersweet chocolate chips. These were so good and the chocolate chips took the cookies over the moon. I made them smaller, slightly smaller than golf ball-sized and baked them at 375 for about 12 minutes and got 20 cookies. Perfectly crispy on the outside and chewy in the inside. Exactly how I love cookies.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are good. I followed the recipe, except for the yield.  A #24 scoop yielded 15 cookie dough "balls." They spread to 3.5" in diameter and had a nice height when baked.  As an experiment, I reduced the size of 1 dough ball to fit a #40 scoop.  Interestingly, it also spread to a 3.5" diameter. It was still chewy, but because it was thinner, it was much crispier. We preferred the slightly thicker cookies from the larger scoop.  They had a nice crunch on the bottom with a lovely, chewy center.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Thank you for sharing! Huge help. Was wondering how they would turn out if I use a #40 scoop instead of my #20.  Good to know!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Loved this recipe, made it crispy by using convection oven at 350 and baked for 10 minutes on lower oven rack and 8 minutes on upper oven rack. It came out perfectly formed with a tan color and got a lot of requests for the recipe. Now I double the recipe which yields 55 cookies. The fact that there is no spice or raisins in it gives this cookie a "universal" purpose. I also did not add the salt topping. Love the pecan flavor it generates.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
This recipe is perfect. I made it exactly as directed, portioned 12 balls with 3 TB/1.5 oz scoop, baked them 6 per half-sheet after chilling 60 minutes, and the results were as promised, crisp on the edges and chewy in center, with lovely toasted pecan flavor. 

However, 5 minutes prep time is wrong. Even if you had everything chopped and measured before starting the prep-time clock, it takes longer than that to brown the butter and pecans. Allow 15-20 to measure, chop, mix, portion.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Excellent.  It's nice to have a cookie that's got lots of flavor but isn't relying on vanilla or chocolate.  And I'd venture this is more for adults than for kiddies.  I used a #40 scoop, and I got 13 cookies (plus a tad left over).  They baked up to about 3-1/2" in diameter, so 5 on a cookie sheet.  And you probably have every ingredient in your house already.  Thank you, Genevieve!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I made these last night. I followed the recipe and they are delicious. One thing I did change was refrigerating on a cookie sheet instead of a plate. I wouldn’t do that again…as I think it impacted the texture. Not the pan versus plate, but what I did next. 

I froze ten of the cookies dough scoops, and baked the last two on the too cold baking sheet. Texture was maybe a bit off.  That’s what you get when you just don’t want to wash another anything after dinner. 

Next time, and there will be a next time,  I will double the recipe and chill on a plate/or other pan. And I will bake on a room temp pan (and definitely bake more than two).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can these be made with gluten free flour (like 1 to 1 by Bob's Red Mill)?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Yes. Same volume.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: What about if you subbed Almond Flour? Is that still 1 to 1?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @phred I prefer to substitute 1 1/3 cups gluten free oat flour per 1 cup wheat flour. No taste difference to me, makes a great cookie, and has the added benefit its whole grain!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made per the recipe, 12 cookies in all. Size was perfect! Don’t let the small cookie team dissuade you from your large cookie ways. It’s a great recipe and I’ll be making it again. I plan on doubling the recipe - this is half of a standard cookie recipe for me. I like how oat forward these are. Letting the cookies completely cool on the pan is the key to the crunchy sugar structure. I baked mine 17 minutes and the centers looked a little undone when I took them out. After they cooled, it all made more sense - edges crunchy, center a bit fudgey.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: For those of you who have never browned butter -- do it on lowish heat. What happens is the milk solids separate from the fat and drop to the bottom of the pan. The clear butter on top is clarified butter, or ghee. The milk solids that drop to the bottom of the pan will color, creating browned butter. Make sure to use a spatula to scrape out all the bits. That's where the flavor is.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I have made enough cookies in my lifetime to recognize a good recipe when I see one. I am making these the instant I get back home from my trip out east to visit election-result grieving family. 
It always makes me snicker(doodle) to read the comments from people who change the recipe and then complain that it didn't work. FIRST do the recipe as it is written. THEN make changes when/if you make it again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I made this dough today.  It’s chilling in my fridge now.  I used Bob’s Red Mill "thick & chewy" oatmeal so am not sure how they’ll turn out.  Thought I’d chill the dough so the oatmeal can soften.
I couldn't taste the maple syrup flavor in the dough at all.  It seems a bit precious to include it? Long on romance, short on reality?
I will probably add dried Montmorency cherries to the cookies before baking, since everything tastes better w cherries.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
How did Bob’s Red Mill "thick & chewy" oatmeal work out?

I think the maple syrup is intended to be a caramelizing factor akin to those old Lace Cookies made with corn syrup.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Made these on election day and, much like many things that day, they were a disappointment.

Perhaps I made them wrong, but the ratio of oats to flour made them fall apart easily and tough instead of delicately chewy as I expect of an oat cookie.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Lauren in the midst of wanting to do anything but laugh—you made me laugh. Thank you.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
They are stupendous.  Even though I made the cookies smaller than the recipe calls for, they were still chewy with crisp edges.  
I used mostly whole wheat flour and added Heath toffee chips, just a bit.  Oh so flavorful!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are delicious! I was delighted by the genius technique of toasting the nuts in the browned butter and letting the oats soak up the flavor. I made my cookies smaller, only for convenience (my favorite cookie scoop is smaller) and ended up with about 18 cookies. Still had a lovely chew with some crispness on Day 1, and I loved how the flavors melded and chewiness increased with time. I made them with walnuts, because I had them on hand and wanted to make them right away. As other reviewers commented, I loved how the maple and nut flavors shine through without spices or vanilla to dominate. We enjoyed them all last week! The perfect afternoon pick-me-up for challenging times. Thanks, Ms. Ko!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I’ve never posted before, but when my husband said these were the best cookies I’ve ever made, I thought I better report it.   I thought they were amazing as well…and for dietary reasons, I have to use gluten-free flour (Cup 4 Cup)…still worked well. 
One of my sons has a nut allergy, so my next attempt will be subbing sunflower seeds for the pecans to see if I can get any kind of resemblance to this perfect cookie. 
Highly recommend making this as written though!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: What is "milk solid bits"? We are talking about butter.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @GS when browning butter it will foam and specks of the milk (butter is made from milk fat) solids will come to the top and bottom.  The foam will cook off the milk solid bits will brown at the bottom.  They burn quickly so watch them closely - they add to the yummy brown butter flavor.  Always stir and watch the butter because burnt butter is not yummy.  These cookies sound delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I couldn't help myself---tried so hard to follow directions without deviation---but I added 3/4 c dried cranberries.  This is the best cookie I have ever eaten.  And I've eaten a few....LOVE!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Best cookie ever! It is very cold outside right now in CT so took the premade balls out to the porch where they chilled nicely. Made these on Election Day and I’m sorry to say that we are going to need many more of these to get us through the days ahead…

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
So I followed the article's headline and made these on Election Day then distributed them to friends in my building.  All agreed they were the best part of Election Day 2024.
Hope to make again sometime soon.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I thought these were very tasty and a nice departure from the usual. The browned butter and pecan went so well together. Like others, I thought the cookie size was too big so I just dropped it down, baked for 15 minutes, and that seemed to go fine. I would recommend removing them from the baking sheet right away. I didn't do that if they got a bit too crisp.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My hubby can’t stand butter so we substituted for shortening and these cookies turned out rock hard. My mother always made cookies using this method growing up because of butter shortages in Ireland (we call them Chewy Shortening Cookies) but these were not good and too hard I couldn’t even taste the pecans and they cost so much. Very disappointed with this recipe

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Hippocrampus These cookies have “butter” in their name, and yet here you are, making them without butter and saying it’s bad.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Jasmine
But Hippocrampus didn’t substitute oil for butter because of cost but because of taste, so I’m 100% with Susan. Some substitutions work in recipes but others are just not going to when the whole point of the recipe is browned butter. You can’t expect oil to flavor the same way. With regard to the sensitivity to economic limitations, I don’t know about you but on my fixed income I find a great many recipes that assume that more costly ingredients are just part of the recipe. Sometimes ingredients can be substituted sometimes not. But the NYT across the board assumes a more affluent readership (check out Wired sometime and see what they consider “affordable” lol) so I don’t expect anything different in the recipes. I clip any recipe that looks appealing whose ingredients I can afford and enjoy reading the rest in the hope that I may someday splurge!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @JasmineBut Hippocampus wasn’t subbing for economic reason but because “ hubby”  dislikes butter. And who knows what shortening was used. My gran used to make oatmeal cookies with leaf lard (NOT fatback) and they were great.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These cookies are not too big. I portioned by weight, dividing by 12, and got a perfectly reasonable 59 gram cookie. These are good. I added a touch of Mexican vanilla, and might add a pinch of cinnamon next time, but these are definitely going into the rotation. Perfect compliment to a cup of coffee! Really good!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Terrific recipe. One suggestion: the dough is fairly wet, which makes scooping out the dough kind of a mess. Instead, I refrigerated the dough after step 4, which made scooping much easier. It didn't affect the consistency of the cookies (I made both ways).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made these today while waiting for my lovely old horse to cross the rainbow bridge. The recipe seemed a bit daunting with all the steps and ingredients, but it was therapeutic to concentrate on the recipe and they turned out wonderful. I doubled the recipe and  I put the maple syrup in a heavy saucepan on low heat for a couple of minutes, swirling the pan the whole time. The syrup foamed up and became darker and thicker and really deepened the maple flavor, which I prefer.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: nothing is made better by raisins. @Nancy Lauka

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Sometimes too many ideas make for bad cookies.  I made THE recipe.  THEY turned out wonderfully.  No need to read the comments below.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I like the brown butter and toasted pecan concept but these cookies, in my opinion, had a flat taste. Luckily because of my addiction to chocolate, I added 3 oz. chopped chocolate to half the recipe, which made them delicious and interesting. I also played with rolling some in Demerara sugar, some in refined white, some topped with Maldon salt and some nothing before baking. My favorite were the Demerara topped. The result was a golden cookie with a light crunch. The recipe calls for just salt but even those did not brown nor did it really add anything.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: We made these as written, but omitted the pecans due to a nut allergy and they turned out perfectly. We also added a 1/2 tsp of cinnamon and 1 tsp vanilla.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Family enjoyed the original, but adding coconut and chocolate chips made them exceptional.  New family favorite

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I doubled the nuts and halved the sugar. Excellent.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Great recipe, cookies turned out amazing! They have a wonderful caramel-y taste and I love the texture..crispy edges and soft centers. Yum. I did not have pecans or any other nuts so I subbed pistachios (I only had 45g sadly) and golden raisins that I soaked in orange juice. I used bobs red mill extra thick rolled oats as well. These would make great ice cream sandwiches imo! Next time I’m going to make them with pecans or walnuts. I will say 14 cookies was probably my sweet spot for this recipe. Maybe the addition of raisins/the extra moisture from soaking made them a little more fragile the larger they were ..a couple cookies needed to go back in the oven a minute or 2 longer.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: A good oatmeal cookie. I was expecting fireworks from the comments and I was underwhelmed. But it was a good oatmeal cookie.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I made these cookies the day my thirty year old Morgan mare crossed over to the afterlife. I needed something calming to concentrate on while waiting for the veterinarian and the kind man with the backhoe and this recipe with its various steps was just the thing. 
The crossing was uneventful and peaceful and both veterinarian and undertaker sent messages thanking me for the amazing cookies baked under such sad circumstances.
This is a wonderful recipe and I made them for friends and family for the holidays, happier times indeed, and they received rave reviews all around.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Is there a world where this works without pecans? Maybe coconut flakes instead? I have a nut allergy but these look so good!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wanted to ask the same, coconut sounds delicious! Maybe it will come out like a macaroon...

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I just left out nuts because grandchildren allergic and it worked out fine. Maybe not as crunchy. I’ll be making some with nuts not meant for them.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Sunflower or pumpkin seeds?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Love this recipe. I added homemade candied ginger and dried cranberries

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: "A recipe with this amount of butter/sugar/flour etc. is a 3- to 4-dozen cookie recipe." There are 8T of butter for 12 cookies. Calm down.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
These cookies are terrific! I always make them in "regular" cookie size, so they are crisp, not chewy, but they are wonderful nonetheless. As another commentator noted, the recipe yields three or four dozen smaller cookies, so there are plenty to share with others, whose reactions are universally enthusiastic.
But the "Prep time" is a lot longer than five minutes. It takes at least a half-hour to measure the ingredients and do the melting and mixing. Do I not understand what "prep time" means?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Amazing cookie, made as written and added a cup of chocolate chips.  Even better the next day, I will mix the flour in with the mixer next time as two of the fourteen needed up as florentines, full 6” spread and cooked faster than the thick ones.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I love giant cookies. There is more crispy edge to nibble at, and a couple of good center bites...like a cookie filet! And I never eat the whole cookie at one time so it allows me to feel virtuous. Can't wait to try this recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made these using a #40 scoop and baked at 375 - watching until the edges were well browned and the centers just starting to dry out.  They turned out with the combination of crispy edge and chewy center and were about 2.5" wide.  A big hit, but I would reduce the 1 tsp. of salt to 1/2 tsp. next time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @geneviveko these are my favorite cookie.  Ever.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: What’s the approx. diameter of the unbaked cookie as per the recipe?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Texture is very different if you refrigerate the dough for more than 1 hour. I was sad to discover they weren't crispy anymore (though the dough was much easier to work with).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wow these are delicious!! Added mini choc chips and it was 20 min in the oven for me to get a crispy chewy cookie with great flavor - so yummy.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Only alterations were adding 1 c semisweet choco chips and using hazelnuts instead of pecans since I live in Oregon, Hazelnut Heaven. Chilled dough after mixing and used a #30 scoop to make 23 cookies, baked at 335F convection for 18 minutes. Very good, though maybe a bit too much salt, even for me, a salt-craver. Next time I would use only 1/2 tsp fine salt in the dough if I planned to top with the coarse salt.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I just made these cookies mostly according to the recipe except I used Bob's Red Mill GF 1 to 1 Baking Flour. Also, I melted & browned the butter in a glass container in our microwave, then added the chopped pecans to the browned butter and browned them in the microwave, too. Finally, rather than 12 large cookies I made 22 large-ish cookies that  after about 20 minutes in the oven were plump and golden with chewy centers and crisp brown edges. SPLENDID RESULTS!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Brown butter flavor comes through, and I have terrible taste buds! I've tried many recipes with a lot more brown butter that didn't taste like it at all. These have that lovely butterscotch-like flavor. 

Reduced sugar to 150g and found them too sweet. But maybe that was because I subbed chocolate chips for pecans (my beloveds don't like nuts in their treats).

Used whole wheat pastry flour as usual. 

Formed balls after dough sat 1 hour in the fridge and they fell apart. Any tips?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are very tasty. I did substitute a couple of tablespoons of maple sugar for 2 tbsp of the light brown sugar to increase the maple flavour. Next time I might try adding some chopped dates.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made these twice. Second time around I doubled the brown butter quantity and they were outrageous. Still excellent both times

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I sell cookies sometimes at our farmers market stand. This cookie received rave reviews. My search for the close-to-perfect oatmeal cookie is over. I use our sugar cane syrup in place of maple.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delish as described!  I may try a adding a bit of orange zest next time, just because I love orange-oatmeal cookies.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
These had good but were kinda pain to make. The dough was almost too crumbly to scoop into balls, so the refrigeration is a necessity. That said, with a half hour fridge time, they did not spread at all. I forgot the maple syrup, but added some Guittard super cookie chips. 

For me, the flavor was great, but the texture was off. I think if I was to make these again, I would reduce the oats by half cup and increase the flour by 1/4 cup, as well as cream the sugar and butter a bit more.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I followed recipe exactly and made smaller cookies that were chewy and crispy and just wonderful.  Chilled for a few hours before making into balls. Thank you for the pantry friendly recipe!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Absolutely brilliant. Always desirous of learning any new (to me) cookie cooking knowledge.  NYT cooking increases my voodoo cookie magic .

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’ve made this recipe three times in the last week alone.  Delicious and very easy to make it gluten free with GF flour.  I didn’t have enough light brown sugar so I subbed half the sugar with my dark muscovado (ahhhh) and it really enhanced the flavor.  I only used 3/4 cup of sugar and it’s still plenty if you’re thinking of cutting back on the sugar.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Don't forget to sprinkle the flaky salt on top before baking! These are fabulous cookies. I did make them smaller as they were for a large party. They were probably a bit more crisp than they would be if large but still delicious! Golf ball+ sized scoops made 52 cookies. I can see adding chocolate or dried fruit too, but the caramelization of the brown sugar/maple syrup/browned butter and pecans was sweet enough. A winner!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I just made these cookies and followed the recipe exactly; I wasn't planning to bake today, but the recipe sounded so good, and I already had all of the ingredients, so I did it! I hadn't used my stand mixer in a few years and had never made cookies with browned butter; I'm so glad I did this today. These cookies are so good. Like others, I considered making the cookies smaller next time so I can share them, but I do like the crunchy outside and soft inside, so I'll make them exactly the same.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Reading is fundamental, but I didn’t read that you divide the butter and I browned all of it. Whoops! Cookies still turned out great!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
These cookies can take a lot of add ins… including couple of heaping T almond butter, coconut, dates & dried cherries. Also subbed almond flour 100% for flour.
Delicious , hi protein/fiber cookie with these subs. Awesome taste. Daily breakfast cookie. Also 1T cinnamon .

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Since it didn't look like anyone reported back on results making these as roll/slice cookies, here's my experience: I put the roll (prob. 14" long) into the freezer for almost a week (I was accommodating holiday travel), then thawed on the counter for an hour or so. Sliced fairly thin - got 36 cookies. Had to do a bit of reshaping - the dough is a bit crumbly slicing that thin. Baked at 350 for 12 minutes. Very good, chewy with a little crisp at the edges.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Has anyone tried these with quick cook oatmeal? I don't have old fashioned in the house :(

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I love these cookies because of the flavor and also the clever hack for browning butter and toasting nuts together. Will use that in other recipes. One thing I did change which made a difference is resting the dough. It’s because of the oats. In spite of being in the butter, I felt the cookies were a little dry the first time I made them. The second time I added an extra T of butter to the oats in the pan. Then once mixed  I scooped the dough into balls and let them rest covered overnight so the oats had time to absorb moisture and soften a little. Much less dry and chewier all around.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: We've made these following the recipe to a T (5 stars) a bunch of times. This week, we were mid-cookie and discovered we were a bit short on nuts and oats. Posting to say the recipe tastes great with a mix of cashews and pecans, and 1/2 cup of brown rice krispies for the oats.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: We enjoyed these cookies. A couple of observations: 1) hard to do because they're so tempting to eat immediately, but I found the taste, texture and chew were superior on day two after baking (will remember to bake ahead of serving or gifting); 2) I coarsely chopped the pecans on my first go-around, next time will chop fine; 3) love the idea of adding dried fruit (raisins,  cranberries and especially dates, which would add to the caramel notes, are all candidates).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I wonder if you can substitute molasses (which I usually have on hand) for the syrup (which I never have on hand).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
You certainly can substitute molasses for the maple syrup.  They are a similar consistency and sweetness.  But! Make sure you use unsulphured molasses.  Blackstrap molasses, which is sulphured, is very bitter!  Bet you know this already since you stock molasses in your cupboard.

You're not going to have the same flavor complements that maple, brown butter and pecan will have, but the cookies are still going to be outstandingly tasty.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @TriciaPDX I added a tsp of molasses in addition to the maple syrup. Over the top delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can you make these without nuts and would you increase flour or oats and by how much?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Severe nut allergies in our household, so I ask your question often :) For this recipe, I think subbing flour or oats for nuts might throw off the ratios too much (oats and flour, in addition to having completely different consistencies, are also much drier/less oily than nuts). Pumpkin or sunflower seeds might be interesting swaps, or searching for an Anzac Biscuit recipe (essentially all the ingredients above, but coconut instead of nuts) that uses brown butter.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: On the second try, the cookies turned out very good. I even like them, and I'm not a fan of oatmeal cookies. I reduced the brown sugar to 150g (at125g the first time, it was an oatmeal breakfast "bun," not a cookie). I reduced the in-cookie salt to 3/4 tsp and put no coarse salt on the top (not a flavor that we like). After 10 minutes of baking, I flattened the cookie, and then continued baking. Perhaps this takes away some of the chewiness, but they are still plenty chewy, and good.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Perfection achieved with the addition of dried cherries poached in apple cider until plump and then strained❤️

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Huh. Flavor on these is absolutely fantastic but they certainly did NOT spread to 3-4 inches… after all the hullabaloo in the comments about size I balled up 12, determined to try 2 the big way and go from there. I had refrigerated a couple hours, so I squished the top a little as instructed and they just…. Stayed that way. At 16 mins in the oven I flattened them a little more because they were literally still 1.5 inches tall. Turned out a thick, regular sized cookie…. Delicious, but definitely not huge! I ended up cooing 19 mins total, and couldn’t do more without the pecans burning, so not sure what happened here. Will try letting a few come up to temp before baking the next batch!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I mean brown butter and chewy crispy cookies, what’s better? I left the nuts out entirely and they turned out fine. Added a splash of vanilla to my wet ingredients and some dried cranberries and a few chocolate chips at the end. Yum!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I unfortunately didn't have pecan at home and did it with dark dessert chocolate and an excellent chestnut brown honey. The result is different than what you proposed but still very nice to taste and I really liked the oat absorbing the noisette butter. 
Next time I'll try you're authentic recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious. I thought they tasted like pecan pralines.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made the smaller cookies ( following baking time instruction), added cranberries and omitted the pecans bc of a nut allergy. Wonderful, wonderful cookie with a chewy middle, crisp edges. A definite keeper. Thanks, Genevieve.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Followed the recipe.  My dough was too wet to form into balls, so I refrigerated it first, then formed balls before baking.  Remove from the oven when the edges are brown but the center is just dry - a little tricky, but the center will continue to cook as the cookie cools.  Lovely toasted butter, oat and nut flavor; not too sweet.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious! Added 1/2 cup dried cherries and 1 cup chocolate chips. Used walnuts instead of pecans. I think of recipes like this as a starting point…

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: All of the discussion about size misses the main point-these cookies are some of the best cookies I’ve ever eaten. They are absolutely delicious, with the perfect combination of textures and a rich sweet/salty flavor. I used the recipe exactly as written. I have made them twice; the first time I made them as directed, quite large, and the second time I made them a little smaller. Both were perfection. The big cookies seemed like an extra special treat, though.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I don't like big cookies, so I make 24 smaller cookies - about 30 grams each.  The secret to getting the crisp edge and chewy interior with smaller cookies is to chill the dough for 3 hours or so, so the dough balls are thoroughly cold going into the oven.  Cooking for 15 - 16 minutes results in the desired blend of textures.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Absolutely delicious. I make these all the time - they are really forgiving, bigger or smaller, less or more time in the oven, white or whole wheat flour, always delicious. And they last for a week or so in a cookie tin. I use only 140 gr sugar, which is still plenty sweet.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Truly impressive cookie for all the mundane ingredients! I didn’t even use very dark maple syrup (just regular) and made them small (per Genevieve’s comment) and they’re spectacular. The butterscotch brown butter flavor goes so well with maple syrup and pecans, giving caramel notes. The dough comes out pretty wet before chilling but fear not, after chilling, it looks more like cookie dough. Easy five stars

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Why can’t you just put the cookie dough balls on the baking sheet and put in the freezer instead of using a plate and then transferring to a baking sheet?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Dawn 
Because in a different recipe someone complained that they were sick of recipes calling for putting the entire sheet pan into the freezer in order to freeze the dough.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: What kind of oats...quick or old fashioned  or does it not matter?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The recipe calls for old fashioned rolled oats, but if all you have is quick oats it will still work.  They might be less chewy, but maybe a couple pinches more of the quick oats will give the cookies more body.  Cookie dough is pretty forgiving.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was easy to make, with really good results. I had salted butter, so cut the fine salt by 1/3, then discovered I was out of flaky salt. Even so, the saltiness was perfect…any more, and it might overshadow the other flavours. Thanks, NYT.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I would have liked them sweeter and less salty. I might try them again with chocolate chips and a fraction less salt. Good texture though.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I was nervous about these because my dough was very wet and sticky, even after measuring out my ingredients using a kitchen scale (maybe my butter was too soft/warm?). However, after allowing them to chill in the fridge, they shaped up well & made great cookies. I wish I could post a picture! Great cookies!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious.  Recipe is around 700 grams.  I love everything being weighed.  A dozen cookies means a two ounces and two grams.  Not like an especially large cookie.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I would definitely make these cookies smaller-they took at least twice as long to cook as directions instructed. I normally bake a 2" cookie and will do that when I make this recipe again. Cookies are delicious but the smaller size is more preferable to me and my friends.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Agree with dryness-made a double batch and pecans soaked up butter.  Refrigerated two days, hard to flatten (and they never did really).  Took 25 minutes to cook.  Pretty good!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Dede Wilder; the author specifically recommends the darker (robust) types of maple syrup specifically to overcome the lack of maple flavor you're complaining about. You can also amp up the maple by adding a teaspoon (or to taste) maple extract to the dough. That should improve your perception of the maple. But if you don't want to buy another bottle of syrup or an extract, you can either live with it or swap in honey (at a reduced quantity), cane syrup, or even (gasp) Karo syrup.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Fabulous recipe! Followed it the letter and the results were amazing. Only variation was I had to move the cookies off the sheet and onto a cooling rack, but this was easy and the cookies still came out with a good snap. Thanks for the lovely recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious. Will make them again even though I hate getting that many dishes dirty.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Add 1/2cup of Captain Morgan to spice things up.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: What is Captain Morgan, please?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Kmj Hello. Captain Morgan is a brand of rum.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Captain Morgan is a spiced rum.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I have not made full sugar white flour cookies in ages but to de stress today of all days I thought yes, these sound good, look gorgeous.  
I neglected to salt top, just out of my cookie routine, ended up with a way too sweet crispy and chewy cookie.. if I only baked  for 13 minutes… I used a full one tablespoon measure. 
Would I make again?  No, too sweet, slightly greasy and ine dimensional.
Am I glad I did on this day of days?  YES!  may not be my cookie, but my house warm and soothing

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
added 1 Tablespoon Diaspora Co. Masala Chai mix.
instead of 12 cookies, made 16 medium-ish sized cookies.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: AWESOME cookie for people who don't like overly sweet cookies. my one bit of advice is WATCH your pecans toasting like a hawk. mine burned a bit in a hot minute.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Definitely use a scooper of some kind to place the balls of cookie dough onto a plate or sheet. I tried to roll one by hand, but it was WAY too messy! I used a cookie sheet and was nervous about them touching each other while chilling in the fridge, but that wasn’t a problem at all. In the end, these cookies were INCREDIBLY DELICIOUS!!! Now that I know the process, making them a second time will be easier.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My grandchildren loved these cookies. They are usually not fans of oatmeal type cookies.  Because they are big they have to break in half and share a cookie. I’m going to make them small and see if they come out the same

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Recipe was seamless to follow and we got a winner. I do want to highlight though that I swapped maple syrup for molasses and only used half. A bit on the sweet side so next time will lower the sugar content to maybe half

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: But AM - Margaret didn’t mention anything about drop cookies. I agree with her; I think you can make smaller balls that will turn out just fine, perhaps with the kind of careful oversight you are suggesting.

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Depending on how finely you chopped them, the pecans may have exceeded the 112 gr. specified (baking is best done using weights rather than volume measures). It's a surface area to volume issue. For those who remember chemistry class, the smaller you chope something, the more surface area it has, hence the nuts in your case hogged all the butter, which coated them and clung to all the surfaces, so not enough was left for the oats. Next time, bigger nuts!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Margot Otway Definitely recommend using less salt and then taste-testing the dough. NYT recipes are often salt-heavy, I've found. Frequently it's because the recipes call for kosher salt, which measures differently than regular salt. Not sure about this particular recipe, but 1 teaspoon of fine salt does seem like a lot, especially since it also calls for flaky salt on top of the cookies.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I am not sure why there are complaints about the cookie size in this recipe. At 63 g per cookie, it is actually on the smaller end of the typical bakery range, which professional references such as Advanced Bread and Pastry: A Professional Approach place at approximately 50 g to 100 g per cookie. My standard cookie size is 60 g, and when I make artisan bakery-style cookies, they are usually 75 g or more. This recipe falls well within normal professional bakery standards.

And just an aside, browning all the butter will produce a far more aromatic and flavorful cookie than browning just 24% of the butter. And it will produce a crisp edge with chewy center. To be honest, you don’t even need to brown butter or use an invert sugar (maple syrup contains invert sugar) for a crisp edge and chewy center. My standard oatmeal cookie contains neither and was developed specifically for the crispy edge and chewy center.

commentBody: My experience would label these oatmeal cookies, rather than brown butter - the oatmeal makes them!  I made the smaller sized ones under Genevieve's instructions for higher heat/less time.  Perfect.  Also - and key for me - added dried cranberries.  They were chewy, pecan-y, and I feel the cranberries lent a sweet/tart softness. In the future, I would use this as a base for adding lots of different fruit types or even chocolate chips. The fam loved them too - certain to make them again!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Ok, this is exactly the oatmeal raisin cookie recipe I have been looking for. I followed the recipe exactly as described but added flame raisins to half the dough in order to compare. Honestly, both versions are delicious, but the raisin version is what I was seeking. Perhaps a bit too sweet, but close enough. Because of the added volume of raisins (flame raisins are large), my portions yielded 6 cookies without raisins and 8 with. Thank you Genevieve!
PS: plus, the recipe is very unusual and interesting to make!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: this was my first giant cookie bake and I've gotta say I'm a fan!  nice to split with a friend.  Soaking the oats in browned butter really makes this recipe.  Well played NYTimes!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wrong.  Chewy cookies are awful. The best are crunchy.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: They came out nice and the size was ok. They were 3 1/2 to 4 inches in size. I left out the pecans since I don’t like nuts in cookies. Splitting a cookie was just enough rather than making them small and hard.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Followed recipe to the letter ... was really hoping for better! Better flavor, texture ... just better.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Husband liked them, added a handful of raisins to butter pecan step but they were a little bland for my taste. Next time I’ll add a little nutmeg and maybe clove.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: chewy, nutty ~ delicious!! followed recipe as us, they were great,  family really enjoyed them! will make adhesive,  perhaps add some dark chocolate

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Make the cookie balls about golf ball sized. They flatten out a good bit.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: On my 2nd batch, I reduced salt to 3/4t.  Found that, with the added salt flakes, it was saltier than I like.  Prefer the flaky salt to the fine sea salt added to the dough.  Also,  added some chopped dates to the recipe.  nice!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Absolutely delicious! I will try cutting Pecans to 1/2 cup and add 1/2 cup raisins next time. Additionally i'll add 1 tablespoon of vanilla.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made these yesterday. I expected a stronger caramel flavor Too much oatmeal for my taste. I made exactly as written. Crunchy. I think I baked a minute or two too long.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: We love this recipe!  I add a cup of dried cranberries (“craisins”) which provide a nice balance to the sweetness. I chill the dough in the bowl from our Kitchenaid mixer for about a half hour, then scoop the dough, roll into balls and chill on a small cookie sheet for another half hour or so, before transferring them to larger parchment covered cookie sheets for baking. I flatten the dough balls somewhat on the cookie sheets before baking. I rotate the cookie sheets about 1/2 way through baking. The cookies don’t spread much, but that’s fine, they are delicious!  I get 14 cookies using a relatively large scoop.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’m new to baking cookies - can I omit maple syrup or should it be subbed for something else? It’s the additional moisture it adds integral to the process or just for flavor? Husband is allergic to maple and I’d love to simply omit if ok. I don’t keep other liquid sugars in pantry and don’t want to “have” to replace if not neede d

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: You could use Lyle's Golden Syrup in place of maple syrup. You can make your own according to this video - but you'd better make a lot of cookies with the quart of golden syrup produced! https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0Tw3XwFsIug

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
From the point they touch your tongue, you’ll melt with delight. My wife tasted my original batch and asked that the next time I include chocolate chips. Even better!

I also made the smaller size with the second batch (with higher temp and shorter bake time). Equally crispy/chewy. And helps avoid the sin of gluttony.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: We love these cookies as. As many do, we like to follow the recipes first and then tweak them. Following the original recipe yielded 12 cookies. About 1 C shredded coconut was added to the second batch and was also delicious. Not changing any of the other measurements of ingredients this yielded 14 cookies.  For both batches, we do not think of them as giant cookies as ours spread to about 3 inches, certainly not 4 inches. We enjoy having the cookies in the freezer and baking them "fresh" daily!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
To amend my initial comments:  We love these cookies as written.
Perhaps our size doesn't reach 4 inches because we do not flatten the cold dough balls.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Not afraid of large cookies, I followed the recipe as written and had 12 dough balls. I chilled them for 1/2 an hour and made - what I think was - the mistake of flattening them slightly, as they spread very much. One was almost completely flat. They are…. DELICIOUS! Chewy!  Crunchy!  Deep, deep butter flavor. Will make again without flattening.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These cookies are amazing! (I added chocolate chips.) Thank you Genevieve.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If a person is allergic to most nuts (pecans, walnuts, cashews) what should they substitute for pecans?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wow are these good and a keeper!  I'm not much for maple syrup but tried this recipe anyway and glad I did.  I'd describe them as akin to a chewy breakfast bar with the oats, nuts and maple syrup/caramel notes.  I used a Vollrath #40 scoop (.96 oz or 1/8 cup) and produced 18 cookies about 3" across.  Chewy centers with slightly crisped edges.  14-15 minutes at 350.  Still nice and chewy the next day. Great recipe, Genevieve :)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: We like to freeze the cookies we don't bake. Baking for 7 minutes, flatten and continue baking for another 10 minutes. Perfection.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These ARE really good! I can't believe they smell so good and toasty for days after making them.  I used hickory nuts which taste very close to pecans, 1 T maple syrup and 1 T Lyle's Golden Syrup plus 1/8 tsp maple flavor from King Arthur Baking. Our syrup is from a friend and it's not very dark; and I know Lyle's syrup will make cookies chewy. So, I blended the two. I cut the salt back to 3/4 tsp and I think they are still quite salty/sweet. Loved them!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I think this recipe screams for chopped dried fruit like apricots. I added 1/2 cup raisins as a post script and really liked the extra chew, tartness

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: absolutely delicious!  These will definitely be a go to favorite!  Im generally a choco chip kind of a gal, and these are good enough to change me.  I used Genevieve Ko's recommendation for a smaller cookie and baked at 375 for 11 minutes.  Still got the crisp edges and chewy middle.  My husband generally doesnt eat sweets or deserts, he went loved these!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are delicious! Mine did not spread, but they still tasted amazing. I have a kid who thinks chicane chips belong in most cookies, so he threw some in. I’m not sure they added anything to the cookie. I’d skip them next time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are my husband’s favorite cookies that I’ve made in a long time. I spread ice cream on one and ate it like you would a cracker with dip and that was excellent. But I still have notes. The main one is that the brown butter flavor was not coming through for me at all (yes the butter was truly browned and I included all the little brown specks). I want to make again, either browning the whole stick or at least half. Not creaming a softened stick could mess with the texture though, so I am open to thoughts.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Next time I’ll chop the bite finely. The cookie is a bit too chunky as is. Flavor is good.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Truthfully was a bit disappointed by this one. I found the effect of the spice mix crust overly salty and without great depth of flavor, and the chicken turned out quite dry. I did something unusual and actually measured out the spice mix exactly, used kosher salt as well. After 40 minutes the thermometer in the breast read 120 so I kept cooking for 6 minutes and took it out at 150. Rested 10 minutes as per recipe and served over same pan of roasted vegetables

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
1. Clarify BUTTER DIVIDED: In ingredients I would put divided in the butter. I didn’t see that it was only 2 tbsp that you brown in the paragraph under step 1 and had to start over after already using pecans and oats.

2. Suggest providing weight or measurement for cookie size as it seems difficult to achieve the desired larger size suggested by “eyeballing” dividing mixture. Another user commented to do 1.5 oz or ice cream scoops which I did but they were too small as I had much more than 24 (I 2Xed the recipe) balls at the end. 

3. Suggestions on handling the dough? I found it a particularly difficult dough to separate into balls, probably because it’s broken up by oats and nuts - any suggestions for forming balls neatly and without batter all over your hands by the end?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Bit too sweet for my taste, otherwise yummy!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I medicate for hypertension and am supposed to control my salt intake. It makes me nervous that all the recent cookie recipes seem to have salt sprinkled on top. Anybody out there feel the same?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Elliot pecans are available from B and B pecans in Fairhope AL. They are buttery and rich and fairly small. Mmmmm

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I never follow recipes. I am a cook by trade and profession but when it comes to baking I need guidance. I followed this recipe except subbed in almonds for pecans because I didn’t have any peacans and I had a 25 pound case of almonds. I also added about 200 g of dark chocolate chunks because I love chocolate.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are truthfully the best cookies ever. I made mine smaller (18 cookies). After chilling, baked half and froze half. I just baked the frozen half last night and while I didn’t think this cookie could get any better, something magical happened during freezing and they became an even more perfect consistency, extra crispy on the edges with a still perfectly chewy middle. Baked the chilled ones for 14 minutes, frozen ones for 15 (pulled them out at room temp while the oven heated up). Perfection!!! (I see I used the word “perfect” thrice in my review … they are THAT good!).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
But, you didn't make this recipe.
@Hippocrampus

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Has anyone tried these with walnuts??

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Used the trader joes sweet and spicy pecans, it was a nice twist. The heat in the spice comes out more day 2.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made the recipe almost exact, just added maybe 1/3 cup craisins. I didn’t feel like weighing so used a cookie scoop. 40 scoop yields 9 giant cookies. They turned out bigger than expected, so I flattened them slightly and baked them for about 19 minutes. Even better the next day, nice and chewy. Will definitely be making these again. They were a big hit at a family function.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: After microwaving, I put mine in the toaster oven at a relatively high temperature (400-450 °F) for around 5 minutes or even less. It ends up tasting like it was actually baked instead of microwaved.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @J.  I totally agree!   Just microwaving results in a somewhat tasteless and mushy potato.  The skin, a nutritious part of the potato is chewy, not crisp and is unappealing.  I have been using the toaster oven trick for years- after microwaving, I drizzle with some olive oil and salt up the skin.  They come out like you baked it, with the interior soft and fluffy and the outside nice and browned.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @J.   I have always microwaved then toaster oven.  But I think this misses the point. I also have found the center to be fluffy and outer edges overdone.  So I’m willing to give this a try.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My understanding is that we don't control the power on the microwave. lower power just means that it cycles on and off, slowing the cooking. Please correct me if I'm wrong.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Two words: inverter microwave

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: An inverter microwave oven is the only way to go.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Jean L: Whatever the technicality, the effect seems to be equivalent to lower temps.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
As simple as this seems - using the microwave to speed up a baking a potato or cook one when it is definitely not the star of the meal - I am grateful for this.
During a brief illness, I have had to coach 3  experienced cooks through cooking a potato. Or, tried.
Reactions  ranged from outright fear to how to wash a potato, how many times to prick a potato with a fork….
There seems to be a real loss of basic skills in food prep.
So, I am grateful  for a “recipe”  I can print. Food that comes without instructions seems to flummox more people than I could have imagined.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Experienced cooks don’t need these instructions but inexperienced ones definitely do. I also was sidelined for two weeks with a brief illness recently and was absolutely stunned by the coaching my stepdaughter needed to fix even the simplest dish. We ate a lot of packaged food those two weeks!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Bgk. All of our 4 grandchildren cook! All 4 of our sons cook albeit, to varying degrees of complexity. Our youngest could move into the chef’s position at any 3-4 star restaurant. It’s sad that so many young and older people can’t cook a good healthy meal with simple ingredients.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I love baked potatoes but they need to be crispy. After nuking one for maybe 5-8 minutes, I coat it with olive oil & bake in a hot toaster oven for about 10-15 minutes. Serve w butter, cottage cheese & broccoli. Classic!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Theresa the Great
I do the same thing! I preheat the toaster over while the microwave is doing its magic.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: An earlier commenter asked about power phasing in microwaves. There are two technologies. A more recent is the "inverter." These microwaves do not cycle on and off, but actually lower the power itself. I have found that method to be so much more successful. In-reheating meats and other foods, I often use power level one or two and allow the extra time required to warm the food. It is far more palatable to re-warm slowly than blast it in a short time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is a common food stuff in Britain known simply as a "jacket potato". Oven potatoes take way too long for no benefit compared to a microwave (unlike sweet potatoes). My new microwave now gives me stages of power which is much simpler to key in - high - 1000W, medium - 800W, low - 600W etc. I found medium perfect to create a soft and fluffy potato (on high it was terrible it's true). If I have the time I'll try a lower wattage but I have to say 800W is where it's at!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
After pricking it, try rubbing the potato in vegetable oil and sprinkling with salt and pepper, then wrap it in parchment paper and continue with the instructions. Excellent baked potato. Skin will be delicious, not crispy, and you won't care, because this is the baked potato you made for yourself in fifteen minutes, not some perfect thing for a party.

Also, yes, at 50 percent, the microwave runs at full power half the time and is off the other half. This is news to some people? So what?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Chris in Redlands
It might not be necessary for a potato, but inverter microwaves actually reduce the power, which is nice for many applications like softening butter or ice cream.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Chris in Redlands try searching for an “Inverter Microwave”…..  You’re Welcome! :-)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Tip. After the microwave, put cooked potatoes in the toaster oven for a few minutes until the skins get crispy, for those who think the skin is the best part of a baked potato.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It also works to microwave potatoes on high power, at 2 1/2 minutes increments, turning the potato over after each increment, until cooked through. Turning keeps the bottom of the potato from becoming soggy.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Simple, sure, single person heaven.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
have been preparing baked potatoes in a microwave for decades now.  using the method described here -- and short of preparing them in an oven -- Best. Baked Potatoes. Ever.

;-)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: After microwaving coat with olive oil, salt, put in oven at 400 degrees for 10 minutes. Magnificent

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I usually microwave baked potatoes then put them in my toaster oven on broil for a few minutes to sear the skin (my favorite part of the baked potato). Thank you for the tip on 50% power. I’ll do that next time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I remember when a baked potato was only considered well cooked if it has a crisp skin. After microwaving it, I put in a very hot oven or in a covered gas grill turned all the way up till the skin crisps up. Mushy skin on baked potatoes has become not only acceptable but the norm.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: FWIW, there’s a chemistry error early on. Microwaves don’t cook from “the outside in.” They increase molecular energy in water - eg, they heat it - wherever it occurs in the food. Baked potatoes and many similar foods have less water  in their skin than their flesh. As a result, the potato will cook on the inside “faster” than when fried or baked, which is truly outside-in. For the same reason, vegetables like broccoli can be blanched nicely in a microwave before stir frying.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Golly! I’ve been microwaving “baked potatoes” ever since I first bought a microwave in the mid 1980’s! Why half power? As above, except full power for 2.5 minutes, turn over and full power for 2.5 minutes. Double the time for 2 potatoes. Wrap in tin foil and in the oven at 175-200 until time to serve.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Jim Cave that’s how I’ve always done mine too!  I put olive oil all over the cooked potatoes then wrap in foil.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Shorten the cooking time on Side 2 by one minute; remove potato, cut in half and make "tracks" along both sides of open face potato. Fill with lots of small slices of mozzarella (or any cheese); back in microwave for one minute.  Voila!  Cheesy potato...delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Unfortunately the microwave can’t create the crispy skin of a true “Baked” potato. However you can pop a microwaved tuber in a 450 oven for a few minutes and correct that deficiency.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Thanks. I'll try it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: When I worked on an Idaho farm harvesting potatoes, we would grab a couple potatoes off the harvester that was pulled by a large tractor.  We would wash and lay them against the tractor motor in a small shallow pocket.  We would then resume harvesting.  An hour or two later we would take the potatoes off the motor and have it for lunch.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Same timing for two potatoes?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: what if you want to bake two potatoes?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I typically eat baked potatoes with grilled meat.  While the grill is getting up to temp, I nuke them for 7 minutes, then transfer (with tongs) to the grill where I put them away from direct heat while the meat cooks. gives me a flaky tender potato which I enhance with butter and sour cream. Delightful.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The key is temperature, not technique. The inside needs to read between 205 F and 210 F. If you want it crispy, salty, or otherwise, throw it in the oven for a while. It still needs to read over 200F.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I wish more people knew that turning down the power on a microwave oven can be the difference between a tasty food and something that needs to go straight into the trash.

The only time I use full power on mine is to heat water or maybe cook an acorn squash. Everything else I start at 70% and go down depending on the food.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I wasn't sure how this would work with see potatoes, but thought I would try. It took a few minutes longer than this recipe, but the inside was soft and fluffy, almost like it was whipped. Best sweet potatoes I have had in a long time!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: America's Test Kitchen says that 202 degrees Fahrenheit is a properly cooked temperature for a baked potato using an instant read digital thermometer. I've been using that for my baked potatoes and they always come out very good.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: There's a compromise: you can nuke the potatoes until they're about 2/3 done, then put them in the oven. If you slit them open like jacket spuds, the stint in the oven will dry them out enough so they don't taste and  like boiled potatoes. Skin's better too. Saves a lot of time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I nuke my Yukon Gold's, then I smash them on a baking sheet, sprinkle with salt, pepper, garlic powder and a liberal dose of olive oil and bake for 30 minutes. Delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I could be wrong but I believe my microwave really only has one power setting. Choosing 50% just means it turns it on and off such that it is off 50% of the time. That aside, “baking” a potato in a microwave will never match the taste of oven cooked. Microwave potatoes develop a cheesy/gummy taste, oven potatoes ate drier/flakier and have a crunch at the skin membrane. If you are pressed for time two minutes in the microwave followed by 30 in a good toaster oven will approximate the real thing.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I use an instant read digital thermometer and 202 degrees for a baked potato from what I’ve read and experienced seems to be optimal.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: For the record most microwave ovens do not cook at “reduced power”. They cook at full power for bursts of time. In this case 50% power actually means something like 100% power for 15 seconds then 0% power for 15 swconds. Only an expensive microwave will “lower power”. And yes, I jave found that it does male a difference.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: After many years of cooking potatoes in a microwave, I have reverted back to the oven.  Just better.  IMO

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I find russet potatoes to be flavorless and mealy. I prefer Yukon Gold. Am I the only one?

I also put microwaved potatoes in my air fryer after smashing them, with some pesto. Crisps them up nicely

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I will never accept that a potato cooked in the microwave is “baked”. Baking involves cooking food by surrounding it w/ hot air for whatever amount of time. It’s dry heat. Microwaving basically steams the food by stimulating the water molecules in the food. Totally different texture to the potato. Same problem w/ wrapping potato in foil & sticking it in the oven, it still steams & doesn’t bake.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is an amazing way for quick baked potatoes.  I wrap mine in a clean tea towel after puncturing and it comes out perfectly cooked.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Am I the only one? Wrap in a tea towel after baking or microwaving for a tender baked potato.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It will be cooked and moist inside, but will the skin be crisp?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is much too long to microwave a baked potato.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Even at half power? Have you tried following these instructions? How did the potato come out?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My 5-yrs-old microwave has "potato" setting, and for once of the few times with tech, that button actually works! Yes, of course, I poke the holes all over the potatoes before putting them in, and then in a very few minutes, they are done (whether Russet or Yukon Gold or other varities). BTW, you can hear the microwave adjusting the temp. My microwave is a NYT Wirecutter pick still.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I also microwave first and then finish in my oven or toaster oven on high heat to crisp up the skin.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
With all due respect, I find microwaved potatoes rubbery !
The method that works best for me is to get the potato wet. Then spread 
Kosher salt onto the surface. The water keeps the salt in place.
Bake the potato for about 1 to 1 & a half hours at 450F. My toaster
oven works well. The magic happens when you split the potato in half.
The steam comes out; butter & salt the interior with any other creative mixture,
It is well worth the effort & the wait!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If you want a crispy skin, lightly coat the microwaved potato in olive oil and put in an air fryer set to 400 degrees for a few minutes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I just buy mine at Wendy's, with some chili, and then I regret not getting a hamburger instead.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’ve been following a recipe in which I wrap the potato in a damp paper towel and then microwave at full intensity for 6 minutes. I also put it in a toaster oven for a few minutes afterwards to crisp up the skin. Still experimenting.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Thanks for 50%power tip. I also use microwave and then finish in hot oven or gas grill. First rub with olive oil and cover with kosher salt for a steakhouse touch. Maybe experiment with other rubs, herbs, etc.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: No need to poke a boatload of holes in the potato. Depending on size, one or two will do and that will keep the insides moist. I mic between 4-6 minutes then rotate and then 4-6 again. Meanwhile the oven is heating to 350. 250 if the other dishes are close to completion and 450 if I’m close to serving. Big potatoes are better - I only buy individual potatoes now.

author: Genevieve Ko
