commentBody: I rinse the potato in water so that the skin is damp, put it on a plate, and then put a thin damp towel over it (paper towel works or a fabric tea towel, dampened and wrung out).  The wet towel enables me to cook it on full power for 5 minutes without the potato cooking unevenly or drying out.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I put some water in a shallow bowl and place the potato in the bowl.  Maybe submerge 1/4 of the potato in water.  Doesn’t wrinkle.  I never learned how to use different power on the microwave.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Nuking a russet potato will never give it the pricelessly thick, leathery, uniquely tasty skin that 425 degrees in a real oven does. That skin is the only reason to eat a baked potato.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I agree but I usually use red or Yukon Gold because I don't have time to bake a potato.  I live alone & I'm not going to put the oven on for one potato.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I use the baked potato setting on my microwave, for 1-4 spuds.  Wash them well, prick with a fork, rub with oil, sprinkle with salt and zap.  They turn put great every time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I've gone back to using the oven for baking potatoes. If I am in a pinch, I will cook them in the microwave until they are nearly done, rub olive oil and kosher salt on the skin, then throw them in a 400-425 degree oven.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The one comment (a chemistry error) about microwaves adding energy to the water, and therefore cook a potato from the inside out is only partially correct. Microwaves are absorbed as the penetrate the food. The heating is distributed within the food, still a huge a time advantage over slowly being conducted from the outside in. I usually use partial power and a slightly longer cooking time to get more even heating.  Inverter type ovens are superior at partial power use.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My kitchen flooded - so the only thing I have for cooking is a microwave and a kettle while it is torn apart.  This recipe is perfect!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
This has been an extremely quick dinner for over 20yrs...
Microwave as described, immediately top with salt and good olive oil.
Then top with low fat cottage cheese, Rosemary or Thyme and cracked pepper. 
A bit more salt if you desire. It's so good and filing!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The truck with putting them in the toaster oven is a good one - what I do. When the potato is about done in the microwave, I take it out, oil it a little, put some coarse salt on it, and pop it in the toaster oven for about 10 minutes. It comes out like an oven baked potato in a fraction of the time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I cooked this exactly according to the recipe, even including the extra minute. Even though my microwave tends to run a bit high, I came out with an undercooked, inedible spud. I had to throw it out.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can’t believe you cut that with a knife! Poking with a fork in a Frankenstein/thunderbolt pattern, then pushing the ends together will open it up and produce a fluffy interior. And I agree with commenters suggesting a short time in a toaster oven or air fryer for crisp up.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I've mcrowaved a zillion baked and sweet potatoes and they come out perfect every time:

Wash thoroughly. Make about seven or eight knife slits all over the potato (they don't have to be deep, but they need to penetrate the flesh a little). Bake at full power for 6-6 1/2 minutes (seven minutes MAX for a really large one). Let it sit for 2 minutes. By then, the skin will have separated from the insides. Cut it open and add whatever you like. I top them with yogurt and a little seasoning.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I'm not a big eater, so I cut a 16-oz sweet potato in half, prick the skin a bit, place it on a paper towel skin side up, and nuke on high for 3 minutes. I don't know how well this works for regular white potatoes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Many contributors propose a final few minutes in a 'toaster oven' to crisp up the skin. Here in the IK we don't know what a toaster oven is, but I use my air fryer very successfully for the same purpose.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Microwave ovens actually provide heat all the way through - more so than a regular oven (conduction). The outsides heat more rapidly because the heat sink (conduction to other materials) on the outside is only to the inside; on the inside, it's surrounded by the heat sink on all sides. The initial heating likely makes water there more mobile and energizable by the microwaves. This method allows the heat to spread more evenly and not "run away". Similar concept with defrosting food.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
An easier and quicker method is to pre-bake the potato at regular power for about 5 minutes, then finish it off at 400 or so in the oven for a few minutes.  Results are perfect.
Arlene Holen
Washington DC

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this with two potatoes and did 10 minutes on each side, which cooked them perfectly. Followed it up with 10 minutes in an air fryer @425 to crisp up the skin a bit.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Slivers of parmesan during oven crisping!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: As advised in other comments, I finished this off in a 450 degree toaster oven, after coating the skin with olive oil and flaky sea salt. Topped it off with Kay Chun's Baked Korean Sweet Potato recipe toppings, using miso compound butter and plain yogurt (no sour cream). This takes comfort food to a new level!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I learned 7 minutes in the microwave, works fine.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The recipe and notes are valuable updates to Barbara Kafka's 1987 book Microwave Gourmet.  In it, she said microwave ovens then topped out at 800 watts, and many were only 400.  Considering the small space for microwave ovens even today that are mounted over a cooktop, compared to a Panasonic 2 cu.ft., Inverter at 1,250 watts, there is a lot of size and power variation.  Panasonic says a 6-8oz potato will cook in  3.5-4 minutes at 80% power.  Will try the 50% method.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Air fryers are also great for that final crisping, as are toaster ovens. No need to heat up a whole oven to crisp one potato.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: You could alternatively microwave the potato, then let sit for five minutes before flipping and microwaving again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Guy, what does that do for the potato?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Use your digital thermometer. It is perfectly light and fluffy and moist at 208 to 210 F.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Aha, now I know how Wendy's cranks them out so fast.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I rubbed the potato with olive oil & salt & microwaved it 5 minutes on one side 4 on the other!
Delicious with butter & sour cream!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Best baked potatoe ever. I never had the patience to wait over an hour for a baked potatoe what with the oven preheating and baking time. This is a great weeknight game changer.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: While a proper oven-baked potato is great, there is nothing like being able to eat a potato topped with whatever you like in under 10 minutes.  I’ve been surviving ok on microwaved potatoes for decades without piercing them and none have exploded! Love the idea of trying them at half-power.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Whichever way you choose to make your potato, be sure to heat the butter (microwave or stove top) until bubbling before you add it to the potatoes. The potatoes absorb the butter much better and you can get the same rich flavor without using as much butter.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: As a GenX who was abandoned to my own devices around 1979,  the microwaved baked potato became a staple part of my diet throughout the 1980's. I learned to add cheese, sliced ham, fried eggs, and many different spices.  To this day, after a long day of work, a microwaved baked potato still feels like a celebration of me-ness.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Finish on broil in the oven for proper skin, turning once.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Like the Cuisinart, the microwave is a tool that is superb, but only within its very limited range of capabilities. It's great for baked potatoes, either on its own or to save time in conventional baking. One thing people overlook when microwaving is that the volume of food has a direct correlation to microwave cooking time. Unlike a conventional oven, nuking two potatoes takes nearly twice as long a single potato. If you don't take this into account when scheduling your prep time, you will be unhappy. Too many people make this mistake, and conclude that reheating coffee is the only thing a microwave does well.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Done this for years. I put yellow gold potatoes on a bowl half full of water at full power for 5 min then turn over for 3

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: How about the potato button on the control panel?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: And if my microwave doesn't have one of those??

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My grandpa used to make microwave potatoes for me all the time when I was growing up. It was the pure comfort food that I'll never forget. I've never been able to replicate how perfect his potatoes would turn out and now I suspect it was the half wattage that I missed. Thanks for the tip!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Is the time different if you have 2 potatoes?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Do you need to modify this technique for a sweet potato?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Tasty

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Has anyone tried this method with 2 potatoes to see how long it would take?  Certainly works well with 1.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Almost double the time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I microwave at 2 minute intervals, rotating the spud after each interval. It usually takes only 2 and 1/2 turns per large tater. I then put them in the oven with whatever is slow roasting (225degrees) until everything is done. Microwaving is fast but the potato ideally should be a bit drier than the method allows.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wash the tater, wrap it in a paper towel, and wrap again in a cotton dish towel. Microwave it for the suggested time, and you've got yourself a PERFECT baked potato!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Does it matter what wattage the oven is? Mine's 700.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: cant wait to try this tonight... i adore baked potatoes, not so much heating a gas oven for one.  have been doing them in the microwave for years... my latest microwave is more powerful than previous ones and even using the baking potato setting results in either overcooked or undercooked.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Microwaves come in a range of sizes and powers, from 500W to 1400W. Small ones (0.7 cu ft) are generally 700W and the larger sized ones (1.2 cu ft) are 1100W. What size does this recipe assume you're using at 50% power?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Please define a "medium " russet potato - how much does one weigh?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: After washing the potato, I poke holes around the middle only, like a belt. Then I cook it in a damp paper towel for about 6-8 minutes on high. Take it out, pop it in the toaster oven at 375 for 35-30 minutes. Cut the potato in half along the fork "belt", scoop out the innards, and fix the "jackets" or skins up as you wish. Mash the potato and doctor it to your desire.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: What happens when I try this with a sweet potato?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It's perfect for sweet potatoes.  I do them for 12 minutes @ half power & they come out perfectly. Slit them first to release the steam as they "bake."

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I use olive oil, sea salt, basil, and garlic after piercing the potato all over then cook at 400 in the air fryer for 45 minutes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I use red potatoes which are small but fully cooked in four minutes.  Rest them a minute or two then add olive oil and sea salt.  Yummy and fast.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I've been doing this for a couple years now. I make deep cuts on top in the shape of an 'X,' salt & pepper it, and mike it for 10-12 minutes at half power. If it's not perfect, I'll flip it over and mike it for 45 seconds or more.
I use half power for most of my microwaving, especially reheating. My microwave allows me to program however many minutes at half or a third power, then switch to full for however many seconds or minutes I want to finish.  I'm not sure if they all do that.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I like the mixed texture of a full power microwaved potato. I then put mine in the air fryer on max crisp for ten minutes and it comes out like it had been done in the oven.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If it is microwaved then by definition it is not a baked potato.  The microwave cannot achieve the crispness of skin which has been baked.  For a decent-sized potato, the oven cooking time will be 60-90 minutes.  The product is 20x the experience.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: A countertop multi-oven at 400 degrees is a convenient way to give it that final roast.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
The really important thing is pricking the potato, so it doesn't explode.

I'll try this; I've usually been microwaving for 5 minutes, turning it over and letting it rest for a minute or two, then microwaving again for 4 minutes or until done. (on high)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’ve been microwaving my baked potatoes since at least the Nineties.  About a half-dozen years ago I read that poking holes in the potatoes prior to microwaving to keep them from exploding was an old-wives tale.  So I stopped poking holes in them.  None has ever exploded.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I had one explode in the microwave, even though pricked beforehand. It was a mess to clean up.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: You are lucky.  I thought as you do.  It doesn't happen often, but once you have to clean your potato-frosted oven after an "incident," you will swear by pricking your baking potatoes.  They really DO explode.  You can hear it in the next room or further.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I cook red potatoes in the microwave like this:
Medium (fist size) red potato - 
-If refrigerated, bring up to room temperature first at 30 seconds (full power)
-Wait 30 seconds (Start with step 3 if already room temp)
-Turn over and cook for 2 minutes at full power
-Let rest for 1 minute (It’s still cooking inside from the generated heat)
-Turn once more and cook for 2 minutes (variable depending on size) full power
- let rest for 1 minute + (it’s still cooking!) Halve and serve with butter

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: After microwaving the potato, slice open lengthwise, break up inside with a knife, add olive oil and smoked salt. Wrap in tinfoil and let steam for 5 minutes. Amazingly creamy!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: No. Microwave on high for 4-5 mins.  Immediately wrap in aluminum foil when it stops and allow it to cook on its own for 3-4 mins.  Easy peasy

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Make sure you put an ice pick or  knife through it before cooking.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Lots of folks have suggested finishing off the potatoes in a toaster oven.  I often use the regular oven if it's running to bake or roast other foods.  I also use the microwave (at full power) to pre-heat potatoes for roasted potatoes with rosemary or in Spanish omelettes.  This greatly reduces the cooking time and ensures that there aren't semi-raw, chewy bits.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Cook in a covered bowl. This retains the heat. A single potato needs 2 minutes on high, then you can wait 2 minutes for the temperature to equalize.
Cook for another 2 minutes, and wait for 2 minutes. 
You can then cut the potato in half, and dice up the internals. Add butter and cheese, and maybe microwave a minute.
Vary times as needed for your particular microwave.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Cut lengthwise in half.  Place 2 halves face down and microwave for about 8 mins. Take out, turn over to be face up and bake again for about 7 mins.  Take out, score deeply with knife, place butter, herbs and spices on face and microwave for about one minute.   Saves time and tastes great.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I agree with other posters - microwaving at lower power makes the insides fluffy and moist, but additional cooking in a toaster oven or broiler after coating in olive oil gives it that crispy skin.
It's quicker than the all oven approach which is like 45 minutes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This took twice as long as your recipe in my 1,000 watt microwave. 2 medium-sized potatoes. They came out fine -- after 25+ minutes -- but I've never had any texture or taste problems after nuking them at full blast for 10 to 12 minutes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Or you can buy a plastic microwave container called Delicious Potatoes, which allows you to cook russets on High, with a chart showing the appropriate timings for differently powered microwaves. I'm very happy with this simple gadget.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious. I’m trying to lower my cholesterol. A schmear of vegan butter and yogurt and yum.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I put the potato on full power for 8 minutes, then remove and wrap in foil for another 10.  It will steam itself.  I cut in half, dash of salt and drizzle of olive oil.  Potato heaven!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Has anyone cooked other white potatoes, golden and red, this way? I cook all three interchangeably and by the same method in the microwave (on high, flipped half way through, let sit — but I plan to try this half power method), and haven’t noticed any difference between the different potatoes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I punch fork holes in the spuds - maybe 2 down and on 4 sides - and then nuke them in minimal water. Perhaps 3 x 3 minutes at medium strength. Follow it up with 16 minutes in an airfryer. Perfect inside, perfect outside.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Also the best way to cook corn on the cob. 9 minutes high power

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: doesn't mention ti,ing for more than one potato, any suggestions?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Or poke your holes in the potato, take a very wet washcloth and wrap it around the potato and put that in the microwave, and it comes out moist at full power.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: How about for itty bitty new or red potatoes time wise?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I start a baked potato in the microwave and then finish it in the air fryer or conventional oven, if I already have something in there. This dries out the potato a bit so that the inside is fluffier and the outside is crispier, the way I like a baked potato.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Finally!  I made a great baked potato.  This is a wonderful recipe.  Thank you, thank you!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I had to add considerable time to get the potato soft.  Next time I'll try finishing in the toaster oven.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wrap  the pierced potatoes in  paper towels before microwaving.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Finally a great way to make a "baked" potato for one or two people.  The potato was excellent and after you stuff it with butter, cheese, Greek yoghurt, veggies and whatever it serves as a backdrop anyways. My go to way forever.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Jacket potato is merely an unpeeled “baked” potato.  Microwaving maybe quick but it ain’t a baked potato.  Best part of a baked potato is eating the crispy skin.  Not having a toaster oven (small kitchen) I run my potato with oil, prick and roast at 400.  Yes, time consuming but it’s worth it

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I wash the potato and put it damp in a cloth microwave pouch (no pricking necessary). Mic on high 6-9 minutes depending on size until softened. Prick with fork and cook in toaster oven at 425 for 30-45 minutes. Nice crispy skin and fluffy interior. Try it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I guess i’m the only one that doesn’t think this is a good way to make a baked potato.  I find the texture of poor quality.  Perhaps microwaving them for a few minutes and then popping them in the toaster oven would be worth a try.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can this method be used with sweet potatoes?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I always microwave sweet potatoes, whether eating them in their skins or scooping out the flesh for sweet dishes, etc.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: A potato sack is great for making them in a microwave.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Doesn’t this really depend on the wattage of the microwave being used?  Mine is only 1000 watts, which is a little more than 50% of the biggest ones. It works fine at full power for baked potatoes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: IMHO depends on the size of the potato. I prefer to slightly undercook at a lower speed because the item can continue to cook after it's removed from the microwave.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Question: Does this method work for sweet potatoes?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I'm going to try! But IMHO it should fine.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I sometimes bake potatoes in my air fryer when I don't have the oven in use for anything else. They are not speedy, probably 40 minutes at high temperature, but I figure less electricity and heating up the kitchen.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I always wrap the microwaved potato in foil for five minutes. This allows it to continue cooking until it is put on the table. Especially helpful if you’re microwaving more than one potato.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Most microwaves have a potato setting. You indicate the number of potatoes. As such, it increases the cooking time. The feature automatically oscillates the cooking power. I flip it over part way and usually run it a second time. I immediately wrap it in foil once removed to preserve the heat whilst the main meal is cooking. If the oven is going (usually the case) I put the foiled potato in the oven. I find the potato feature to be easier to use than changing power and time settings

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I do this slightly differently. I scrub the potato, slice in half lengthwise, coat both pieces  generously with olive oil & salt & pepper, place cut side down in a pirex dish, then use the potato setting on my microwave. Perfect & delicious every time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I cook several potatoes at a time in the microwave. My trick is wrapping them together in a damp dish towel, but in a single layer with them touching.  I just fold over and tuck in the sides, tightly so no steam will escape.  I cook on high for 3-5 minutes and then remove carefully and wrap in foil for cooking to continue.  They are divine and never dry.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I seem to be late to the party again, alas.
My spuds method, for the last 35+ years: No idea about my old microwave oven's power. Clean the potato. Poke a couple of holes in it if I remember (never had a problem when I've forgotten to). Put it in a fairly sturdy plastic bag and place in the oven on a safe plate. I give it 5:44 and then, with tongs, turn it over and give it another 5:44. (That number was easy for me to remember as an old hockey fan.) Easy peasy...

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Hmm... Not sure it's wise to use a plastic bag in a microwave. Toxicologists wouldn't approve.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: We like russets cooked so the outside is tough enough to have to struggle to get a serrated steak knife through it (very dark brown or almost black!). So, the way to do this is to microwave them on high for about 8 minutes and then finish them off in a 450F oven for about a half hour or so. Delicious! Crunchy skins and well done interior with butter and/or sour cream and chives. Delicious! Better yet, scoop out the insides and eat the skins separately. Use the insides for frying with something.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I use a microwave cloth bag, wash, dry but don’t prick the skin, cook at high heat about 6 min per medium size spud often two at a time in one bag. Someimtes i turn the bag once. Always works fine.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: As a kitchen klutz, let me offer my tips: scrub the potato under the faucet with hot water, while wet-sprinkle it with salt, wrap it tightly in plastic wrap, and microwave it. When done, immediately wrap it in aluminum foil- shiny side in. Prepare the rest of the meal. Then unwrap the potato and add toppings to taste. The extra time in the foil completes the cooking and evens out the internal temperature. Enjoy.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I don’t like to wait. I microwave my potatoes full power for 4-5 minutes after scrubbing them in tap water, stabbing them about 8-10 times, and chopping the ends off with a table knife. Moisture released helps its texture, and removing ends makes the entire skin edible. Try it once.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My ancient GE has a baked potato setting (as do most microwaves, I think) and it seems to work to perfection. One button.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I have been microwaving whole potatoes forever. I poke a few fork holes…. pop in microwave…push start, cook for 3-5 mins. depending on size…..
 Let rest about five mins in the microwave. Remove, add toppings. NO hassle. Tastes great.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Just slice it in two before throwing it in the microwave. One less step afterwards. Agree with others here placing it in a 400 degree toaster oven for few minutes to finish with a crispy texture.

From one who consumes the entire potato, In can't understand why some restaurants encase the spud in course salt wrapped in aluminum foil.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: A much better receipt, from my experience, is to microwave at Hi setting for 3 mins per potato, then in an oven preheated to broil, bake for 15 mins … potato’s come out soft on inside, with crisped skins …

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Tasted good, but didn’t like eating something that had been cooked in the microwave for that long

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: That's right Jean L.   Cooking on "half power" allows the heat to diffuse more evenly than if you supply the same amount of energy in half the time.   Thanks J. for that toaster oven tip!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If this is an organic potato and if you like to eat crispy baked potato skin, finish this in your toaster oven until the skin is as crisp as you like it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I do it this way: wash the russet; poke holes in it on both sides; a squirt of olive oil and a bit of sea salt on the potato.  Depending on the size of it, 5 minutes full power.  Turn it over. 3 minutes in full power.  Check the potato by sliding a sharp knife into it. If it slides easily, done. if not, another 30 seconds should do it.  Let it rest until ready to serve. Slice in half and butter it/sour cream it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Using a microwave to par-cook baked potatoes is the way to go, and a move I learned while cooking professionally if we had run out or needed one or two more towards end of service. My microwave has a potato mode. I microwave for 5 minutes, and then I throw it in the air fryer to finish off. Takes a 60 min baked potato or whatever down to 15-20 minutes and I bet most people can't tell.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Quick yes.  But those crispy skins that I love so much?  Don't think so.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Lots of helpful comments about different cooking techniques (which I can’t wait to test out). What about using the “potato” button on the microwave? Is that just a hands-off method of controlling the power?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I will partially "bake" a potato in the microwave and finish on the grill if we are using the grill for other portions of the meal.  BTW, baked potatoes covered with grilled juicy veggies (think zucchini, mushrooms, eggplant), with or without some grilled marinated tofu or haloumi, is a simply wonderful meal.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Doing exactly as the recipe directs, I got a terrible baked potato: gummy, soft, chewy(!). Back to the oven. where they don't come out perfect but at least one knows what to expect.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I just wrap a Russet potato in foil and stick it in the oven at 350 degrees. Time of baking depends on the size of the potato. This is less than a min ute of prep but sometimes an hour of cooking. And you can cook several potatoes at once.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Almost as good as Julie Child’s recipe but much faster. Inside of potato was fluffy

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: it worked for me, but it did take much longer. i just kept flipping and microwaving in 2min increments@Jill

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’ve always smeared my potato with real butter, ghee, or olive oil with a sprinkle of of salt, then wrap in a wet paper towel and nuke using the potato button. If I want a toasted skin, I nuke it first then place it in the toaster over for about 10 minutes, or for a real treat, place the potato(s) on the baking pan of my toaster oven, slit the potato and mash the inside with a fork, add butter and Penzey’s Mural of Flavor or other seasons, and cook at 400 degrees until bubbly. However the potato is cooked, the inner temp (using a digital thermometer) should be at least 200 degrees.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: You will need a silicone oven glove to shuck! There's a youtube video showing a really easy way to shuck: chop off the bottom inch or so of the cob, then (with your silicone oven gloves on) squeeze from the top until the cob slides out of the husks. Most of the silk will be left behind with the husk.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I usually leave the corn in the husk for about 5-10 minutes.  That let's the corn finish cooking, but also makes the whole thing easier to handle. No gloves necessary.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: @Claudia a potholder or towel is fine

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: @Claudia cut the bottom off before microwaving. After microwaving, the husk slides right off!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I do 4 min for 2, 6 for 3, and 8 for 4…

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: @DB Lincoln log style!  Me, too!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: This is how I always cook corn. Fast, easy, and you can leave it in the husk for 10-15 min and still have it be warm. I just use a dish towel to hold the steaming hot husk when I’m getting it off. Easy peasy and perfect corn every time.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: We’ve been doing this for years. Cut off the stem end before cooking. Press from the top and the corn will slide right out of the husk after microwaving. The added bonus is that all the silks stay behind in the husk this way.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: @Donna This is how I do it, too!  It’s fool-proof and reduces the usual mess of shucking and boiling.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: The time depends on the power of your microwave. We used to allot four minutes per ear for a 950 watt oven. Now we are at three minutes in a 1200 watt machine. We always wrap each ear in a wet paper towel to generate more steam.  We have had no problem cross-stacking them if there are more ears than will lay flat.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: @Robert The wet paper towel trick works for me every time!!!!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: They're easy to shuck if you cut off the stalk end about half an inch into where the kernels begin.  Then squeeze the cob from the narrowing end so the cob "squirts" out of the stalks, leaving only a few silks to pull off.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: 3 minutes per ear is all you need, because you need to let the ear sit for 3-4 more minutes to cool (and it will continue to cook a little outside of the micro during that time) as it will be too hot to handle until then.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: If you're cooking for a crowd, do 4-6 (stacked) ears at a time, about 3 minutes per ear. I put them directly on the microwave's glass plate. When that batch is done, put the cooked, not-yet-shucked ears on a rimmed baking sheet, tent with foil and do the next batch, repeating until all the ears are done. Then shuck them and serve immediately. Any leftover ears that  have cooled are then sliced off the cob and put into zippered freezer bags. I squeeze out as much air as I can before freezing.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: We husk it remove the silk wrap it in a wet paper towel and microwave for 5 minutes for two ears. Let stand on table to cool and use the warm damp paper towels as an end of meal napkin.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody:
This is how I cook my corn. For smaller cobs, you can try 2-1/2 minutes. I always let the microwaved corn sit in its husk for at least five minutes, since it provides time for the heat to be distributed.
IMPORTANT TIP: the corn might feel warm to the touch on the outside, but it’s scalding hot on the inside. I simply husk the corn under the faucet with a light stream of cold water. The corn stays hot, and your hands will not be cooked.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody:
This is the best way to nuke corn on the cob. It gives the corn a nutty flavor. It also removes most of the silk better than any other method. Another good trick is to soak the corn with husk on, and put on the barbecue.
My sibs think cooking in the husk is somehow dirty or unsanitary. They shuck the corn then wrap in copious amounts of dampened paper towels. The result does not taste as good, and then there is all the wasted paper towels. They also shuck & boil, which is a sin.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Cook it on the grill, much better.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: The way to get the ear of corn out of the husk without having to pull all of the silk off do this: gather the silk and the ends of the husk at the top of the cob, cut the bottom of the cob off, holding the whole air of corn only by the silk and the top leaves shake the ear out of the cob. It comes out perfectly clean and ready to be enjoyed. So your recipe was only half correct

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Inferior. The only way to cook corn on the cob for maximum flavor and crunch is to drop it in rapidly boiling water for two minutes.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: My favorite way when not in a hurry is to bring a pot of water to boil, dump in the shucked corn, cover, turn off burner and let stand at least 2-3 minutes.  Otherwise I wrap the husked cob in a doubled wet paper towel and nuke a couple minutes.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I find 1.5 to 2 minutes per ear is plenty enough - there's no reason corn needs to cook through, it just needs to be warm enough to enjoy.  And, I second the slip off husk/silk method - just need to be careful not to get scalded.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I live in dry country so I often soak the corn I water for 10 min to add moisture to the husks. My microwave takes only 2 min for one cob. I usually do 3 at a time and let them cool for 5 min or so before removing the husks. I've used this method for decades, since microwaves first came out.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: It may be more time and fuss but I’ll take three minutes in a rolling boil pot over the microwave. I tried the microwave route and didn’t find the corn as crisp and tasted a bit overdone.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I always have liked the gimmick of cutting the base off of the cob once it comes out of the microwave. You lose maybe one or two rows by doing this, but then you just hold the corn upright by the silk and the corn slides right out.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I shuck first. I tried the “shuck after “ method a bit, but there’s nothing fun about shucking boiling hot corn (or discovering a cooked caterpillar). Place shucked corn on a plate with another plate on top. Perfect!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Careful!   Friends did this in our microwave and ruined the microwave.  Too much moisture.  Do one corn at a time.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody:
How can "too much moisture" ruin a microwave?   People literally boil water in it.

PS.  I just checked online and "Excessive condensation can slowly wear down the interior coating of the microwave", but I don't see how cooking corn will cause excessive condensation.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Have been doing this for years as a solo cook.  You can get more of a steamed effect if you take off a couple husks and run the ear under water, then tie it back together loosely with a  thin strip of husk.  Nice presentation too!  Then cut off the bottom and squeeze out the cob - voila, no silk!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Been doing this for over 30 years, thanks to Mike of Mike’s farm market- simple and no pot to wash

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: This is my favorite method of cooking Corn on the Cob. No special anything needed, just the microwave. I am not sure that it matters but the one thing I do before I cook the corn is that I dunk each ear in cold water, separating the tip a bit and shake off the excess. I like to think that more steaming takes place this way. I have never had corn that is over or under done. Always perfect!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: The very best way to cook corn on the cob! I’ve microwaved corn on the cob for over 20 years.  However, I shuck, butter, then wrap each ear in waxed paper before microwaving. No salt. About 5-7 minutes. Tastes so fresh, like right from the garden.  Boiling corn in a pot of water absolutely leaches the flavor out. Grilling produces tough corn. I’ve tried microwaving the ears in the husks, but it seems extra messy. But either way, once you use this method, you’ll never go back.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody:
I shuck the corn cob completely.  Cut bottom stem end flat.  I remove remaining fibers.  I wet a piece of Bounty Paper Towel, rap it around the corn cob and place it into the microwave.  Heavier paper towel works best.  

For my microwave, 1 1/2 to 2  minutes produces a perfectly cooked corn cob. Trial and error for several ears, start out at lower times.  Test by biting into an ear.  A few times you’ll figure it out.   The chewed ear is yours. 
Butter then salt so salt sticks and enjoy.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Another approach: shuck the corn and wrap it in paper towel. Wet the towel and wring excess water out. Microwave for 3 mins per ear. Move around halfway if doing three at a time.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: @Mary we do this but top the corn with another plate, not the paper towel 1 to 2 minutes per ear and let rest. So easy!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I husk the corn and wrap it in wax paper.  Microwave about 4-5 min.  The best!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: We’ve been cooking our corn on the cob this way for decades. Cut off the ends up to the kernels and then zap away.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I use high (80-90%) for about 1:30 and then low (20-30%) for 5 or 6 minutes (per ear). So, I get the kernels hot and then let it sink in. The cob needs a little time to warm up. Next to fire roasting, micro is the best.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I have cooked corn on the cob in a microwave four years but I do it a little differently.  1. put unhusked corn on m,microwave and zap for 2 minutes.  2. let rest for 2 minutes.  3..Zap for 2 more minutes,  Husk and enjoy.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Very solid dish, loved making it, love corn so much im a big fan of corn i love corn

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I’ve done this method for some time.  Have a sharp knife handy.  I use hot pads layered with paper towels to handle the corn to husk, and use the husks to remove the silk.  Peel the husk back to make a “handle”, wipe the last strands of silk, then use the knife to cut the husk off.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Instead of trying to shuck a very hot ear of corn, I prefer to husk first and wrap each ear in wax paper before microwaving. If you’re concerned about cooking in wax paper, you could probably use parchment.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: As a luddite I was surprised by how much this method has improved my cooking life. No waiting for water to boil, no waste of water and heating up my summer kitchen. Shucking is easier and faster, and the corn tastes better, too. Even when grilling, it's helpful to premicrowave for a minute or two with a damp paper towel around the un-shucked corn to reduce the drying out and the time it takes to cook on the grill.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: For what it's worth, I shuck the corn beforehand, wrap it a wet paper towel and nuke for 3 minutes. Delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I have an even better way. Soak a couple of ears of corn in water for a minute or two. Drain. briefly, cut about 1-1/2" of the cob off at the base of the ears. Microwave for abut 4 minutes. Remove from microwave with oven mitts and using an oven mitt squeeze the cobs from the top of the ear and the whole ear will slide out of the husks almost silk free!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I do this, but I cut off both ends of the ear first before microwaving. This method makes it easy to remove the silks with the husks.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: When it’s 90° and you don’t want to grill outdoors, I can see using the microwave as an option. I tried it after reading this and I probably will do it again. However corn is far better grilled on the barbecue grill or just plain boiled. My guess is it takes some time in cooking to develop the flavor. A zap in the microwave will get it to the point where you can eat it, but the experience is far less enjoyable.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Try adding a few drops of water to the corn before cooking and make sure not to overcook it.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Been doing this for years.  3 minutes per ear.   Didn’t know about letting it rest.   Great idea.   If corn is FRESH you can cut off the stem end and push the corn out leaving the silks behind!!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: If I’m using pre-shucked corn (sometimes available from my farmer’s market) I wrap each ear in damp paper towels and cook for 3-4 minutes.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I did this for years, until I found out that most of the pesticides in corn are concentrated in the silk.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: The microwave method ensures that your ear of corn stays hot to warm longer, delightful eating!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I remove the husks, wash the corn, covered with a paper towel then microwave for 3 minutes, done ready to eat.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: We've used the microwave method for years. We apply 2 minutes per ear plus 2 minutes more. So three ears of corn x 2 minutes = 6 minutes + 2 minutes = 8 minutes. Cut off the "stalk" end and then squeeze the ear from the top. The cooked ear will appear and the husks will pull the silk off. Works every time.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Just boil water which is usually already going on the stove and drop the shucked corn in for two minutes. No microwave required. No gloves needed to shuck.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I take it one step further. I microwave with husks ON...and husks are easier to remove after.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: For already husked corn: wrap each cob in wet paper towel. 7 minutes. Leave in microwave to steam until ready to cut off cob or eat. This has worked for years and works well with multiple ears at once.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I’ve always removed the husks and wrapped the ears in damp-wet paper towels, microwaving in increments until the kernels reach the desired tenderness.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I did my graduate work in corn genetics. (My MS is in Plant Breeding and Genetics.) We often planted sweet corn as border rows between experimental plots of field corn. I ate a lot of that sweet corn — right off the plant — while working in the fields. Virtually everyone overcooks sweet corn. All one needs to do is boil a skillet of water, drop in the shucked ears, turn off the flame and let them sit for a couple of minutes. The longer you cook it, the more of the sugar turns to starch.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: You can also just wrap your shucked corn ear in a wet paper towel. My chef friend suggested it to me a few years back and that is the only way I cook corn now. And throw it on the grill for a minute or two afterwards for a little char.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I shuck the corn first then wrap it in parchment paper. T here ears takes about  six minutes in the microwave. Perfect every time.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I let the grandkids shuck the corn at home and they love to do it as I tell them about my life way back when down on the farm. And the kids do a really good job of shucking.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I have been making microwaved corn on the cob for years. I shuck it and wash it, and then i wipe and wrap the corn in a clean white paper towel. Cook for 2 minutes. Done. Much easier to handle this way. Eat as is -- no need for any embellishments when you have fresh, in-season sweet corn.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: We shuck the husk and silk off to start with, slather the ears with softened butter, salt, and pepper and then wrap in wax paper.  You can leave it in the paper until ready to serve.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I find it's an easier and less messy process to shuck the corn completely, rinse it off, and wrap it tightly in parchment paper before microwaving. Plus, you can check the condition of the corn before you cook it so there are no surprises when you strip off the husk.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Cut about an inch of stalk from the base, put the remainder in the microwave on high for about 4 minutes, use an oven mitt to remove the corn by holding to the top, shake the corn over the sink, the corn will slip easily from the stalk

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: This is a no brainer, stays moist and flavorful. Just cut ends off and peel a few husks to make it easier to handle.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Any ideas how this can be modified to cook already husked corn? Corn in their husk is available for a very limited time by me while husked corn is available practically ear round.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: wrapped in wet paper or plastic wrapped

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: My method is to shuck the corn, then add a bit of butter along one side, then wrap in wax papper.  Finally, microwave the corn for about 2 to 3 minutes, and then wrap in tin foil.  After a few minutes the corn is ready to eat.  Easy peasy.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Let the corn cool slightly so you don't burn your fingers then remove the husks and the silk will come right off. It's how I cook corn now.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Just need to cut the bottom at the last row of kernels.    Potholders also work to shuck them while hot.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Pro tip: After cooking, cut the bottom of the cob off and squeeze from the top (holding with a towel as it is hot) the entire cob, silk free. The steam allows you to slip it out easily.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I learned this from my mother, corn tastes so much better!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Perfect boiled corn is ready after a max 2 minutes.  To get the same result, hoe much time for 4 ears in the microwave?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody:
Be careful when removing the husks:
A lot of steam will be released and can scald your hands if you’re not wearing oven gloves.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I only microwave corn. Shuck first, moisten with water, wrap one or more in wax paper with some butter, salt and pepper, zap 4-5 min. Done.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I have been cooking my corn on the cob this way for a few years now.  It is perfect every time.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Revolutionary!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I have done this for years. The corn tastes sweeter when cooked this way. I vary this when cooking artichokes, standing artichoke in a dish, a little water in the bottom, cover with plastic wrap, and cook for seven to nine minutes depending on size of the artichoke. Cooks a lot faster and is flawless.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: We've been doing it this way for years.  We husk the corn under cool running water. The heat from the cob keeps them warm and the cool water makes it easy on the hands.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Wrap each ear of corn in a wet paper towel, and microwave for 2 minutes. Fast and easy.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I use the internet-inspired method of shucking corn, wrapping in a wet paper towel and then nuking it.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: This is so much simpler and neater than husking and boiling.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody:
I find 2 minutes enough to cook a very fresh ear. Important not to overcook! Can always cook longer if necessary but can’t uncook. 
I also like some others cut the stem end off before microwaving.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody:
I looked at this with interest because I do have some corn and was planning to barbecue today. The comments about cleanliness did strike home as I don’t think the microwave heats everything evenly. Maybe if we give the corn a good scrub before we put it in?

I did notice in the lead article that the writer was lamenting that his favorite restaurant was closed by the health department and was apparently planning to go back if it opened. Likely these guys have a lower bar than I do.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I don't eat fresh summer corn anymore because I can't find the old heirloom varieties of my youth.  Corn just tastes way too sweet to me now and sticks to my teeth.  I miss the complexity of the heirlooms.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: We have been doing this for years however we husk the corn first and wrap the cob in plastic wrap. Microwave for 3 min if one cob and add a min for each additional cob. Leave it for a few minutes after microwaving and it cooks in the wrap. This way we can keep wrapped cobs in the fridge and have a cob of corn anytime. Especially good for single folks or varied mealtimes for busy kids!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I do this all the time,  so much better than boiling and I like the husky taste that results.  The ears will steam in the husks as you let them cool.  It is great for on-demand snacks for grandkids.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: This is why I bought a microwave 40 years ago! Nothing better than corn in the microwave!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I've been cooking corn on the cob this way for years. More flavorful and juicier. Doesn't even need embellishments, i.e. butter, etc. However, depending on the size of the cob cooking time can be as little at two minutes.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: We shuck the corn and loosely wrap in plastic wrap. 4 -5 minutes and it steams perfectly!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody:
Here in Cape Breton (NS) we've been doing corn that way for years.

During lobster season we purchase 3 meals of lobster, steam them normally, then after the first meal save the rest bagged in the fridge for up to four days...two more meals. 

We reheat the lobster in the micro, one at a time, at about 3 minutes per lobster (in OUR microwave).  The lobster comes out sweet, hot and  as close to fresh as one can imagine.  Probably what many restaurants do.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody:
My sister taught me this amazing secret way to microwave corn -- important to keep the corn in its total husk -- I have found: microwaving 2 mintues 30 seconds for one corn on the cob in its husk- firm just beginning to be soft.

Very important to take the tail of the corn husk out with a pot holder - for it is super hot - stand over the kitchen garbage can - as you hold the tail with the pot holder double up a paper towel to move down the husk to de-fiber it. This is a clean delicious Corn!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I husk the corn first, then wrap it in wax paper, (like a piece of taffy) 3 or 4 ears for 5 minutes in microwave. No messing around with hot husks, just put in all those cute little corn holders we seem to accumulate.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: This method is a real game changer. It works very well.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Just tried this last night and am amazed at how well it worked! No silk left and kinda fun to squeeze out of the husks. Maybe not so great for cooking a lot of corn, since my microwave only fits 2 ears at a time, but perfect for just a few.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: After cooking the in-husk corn for 3 minutes, I quickly hold the cob under a cold-water faucet.  I can shuck the corn and pull off all the six without burning my fingers.  Once clean, the corn is still piping hot since the heat comes from inside the cob.  No need for gloves.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Best way to cook for tender corn. Easy peasy.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Have been doing this for years. Quick and delicious. Preserves more of the nutrients than boiling. Too bad can't use this method for a crowd.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I have cooked as many as 7-8 ears at a time in my microwave, giving it more time as necessary. I remove stems and silk first, so as not to handle the hot corn.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: We've used this formula for years, and it always yields perfectly-cooked corn in the microwave: 3 minutes for one ear, 5 minutes for 2 ears, 7 minutes for 3 ears.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I've been microwaving corn on the cob for years. Unfortunately, the supermarkets only sell husked corn. I wrap the ears in a wet dish towel. I wouldn't touch food with paper towels since I suspect they contain substances that we shouldn't ingest. (I once improvised a coffee filter with a paper towel, and the coffee was awful!) How long should I cook shucked corn?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Cut the STEM END to where you can see a full round of kernels (about an inch of the cob). Be careful and do this with a sharp serrated knife, rotating around the corn to line up the cut. After microwaving for 1-2 minutes per ear, grab the silk end of the husk with a hot pad or mitt and squeeze the corn out of the husk. Most of the silk will remain inside the husk. Less mess. Works great.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I have microwaved corn for at least 5 decades. However, I pull back the husks and remove the silks before I microwave and trim the stems if necessary. I find it easier when the corn is not hot. Then I rinse the ears, pull the husks back over the ear of corn and microwave until done, about 3 minutes depending on how many ears I am cooking. The kernels always delicious, moist and plump, a little butter or my new favorite, EVOO, salt and pepper and they are ready to eat.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: IMHO this is the absolute best way to corn on the cob! (Grilling is great too, but only if you slather it with butter or oil or whatever to keep it from drying out, so I avoid that.) And I've found that people who boil corn on the cob overdo it so it becomes mushy. This way you can eat it au naturel without adding anything at all, and it is fantastic! For the record, I microwave 2 ears just for 3 minutes and let them sit a minute or two before (easily!) removing the husk and silk.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody:
Finally, a recognition of the easiest and best way.  We've been doing this for at least 20 years.

All the sweetness is retained in stead of being washed away.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: A revelation!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Easy and amazing, especially for quick family veggie side. This is a staple now!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody:
Husk and de-silk. 
Wrap in wax paper. 
One minute per ear, rotate if more than one ear. 
 Cool for a minute or so. 
Butter up. 
Yum.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: This is the only way to cook corn (IMO). Never boil! And here is a short video how to shuck. https://www.google.com/search?client=safari&rls=en&q=how+to+microwave+husk+off+corn+on+the+cob&ie=UTF-8&oe=UTF-8 You can also under microwave a few min, shuck, and place on grill with some olive oil spray!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Have been doing this for years.  You don’t need 3 mins.  Approx 2:20 for the first ear and then slightly less for each additional ear.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Been doing it this way for years. I have always found that the ears of corn need much longer than 3 to 4 minutes per ear. If I put four ears in the microwave, they need about 12 to 14 minutes, total. But maybe that’s just because we like our vegetables more done.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Life changing!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody:
I husk the corn and wrap it in a half sheet of damp paper towel and into the microwave for 2.5 minutes. Perfect every time. You can even use the paper towel for subsequent kitchen uses. 

My Iowa friends remain aghast that I do this but it turns corn on the cob into a quick lunch or snack.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: @NancyinChi, what on earth could you use a dirty paper towel for?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: This method worked very well for me, following advice from some comments to cut the base and push out to remove the husk. So much easier than boiling to prepare corn salad! I wouldn’t have tried this method myself because I always worry the tips would catch fire. That happened to me with edamame tips.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Tried 4 ears for 8 minutes and after resting for 5-10 minutes shucked to find half cooked corn. No thanks, way easier to cook other ways.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I never cook more than 2 ears at a time, in the microwave. I do 4 minutes for one ear and 6 minutes for 2. I have a 1100-watt microwave.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I have done this for years.  Cut off the top silks and bottom excess. Best to do 1 ear at a time.  I usually do 2.  The first one cools for at least the 3 minutes while the other one cooks.  It is still very hot.  And that is the only drawback.  Peeling the hot husks.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I have been doing this for a few years.  I cook them in the husk for 4 minutes each, take them to the sink and run briefly under cold water as I shuck each one.  So easy, so much better than a whole pot of water boiled and then discarded.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: As soon as you pull them out of the microwave, cut off the bottom 1" and squeeze from the top (with a towel...steam is hot!). The corn will slip out as advertised, silk free.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Another great way to cook corn in the microwave is to shuck it and brush each cob with melted butter. Wrap each cob in plastic wrap, twisting loosely at each end. Cook for two minutes per cob (e.g., cook 4 cobs for 8 minutes), let stand for a few minutes and serve. Unwrap each cob just before eating so they stay hot.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I have had good luck with wrapping the husked corn in a wet paper towel and heating on high for 3 minutes.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I always cook my corn in the microwave. I trim the small leaves, cut off the silk, and cook it with the husk on. I usually cook a single ear for about 4 minutes, then cut off the fat end and squeeze the ear out of the husk (holding it with a towel). It slides right out completely silk-free.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Before placing into the microwave, cut off the stalk end of the corncob, place corn in a ziplock, cook --remove from microwave and take corn from bag and allow a minute or so to cool--then grab the silk end of cob with an oven mitt and the entire husk should slip off effortlessly. If the husk doesn't release, it's probably undercooked or you haven't cut the stalk end off far enough up on the corncob itself.   I cook for about 4-5 minutes for about 4 ears of corn...more or less.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I take the husked corn, lightly sprinkle it with water, then wrap it in waxed paper before putting it in the microwave

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I've done this for years, but only if I'm not grilling something.  If the grill is hot that is the easiest way to cook corn.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: We shuck the corn first, then wrap it in parchment paper with a slathering of butter and salt and cracked pepper. Microwave for 45-60 seconds per cob. Unwrap at the table just before eating.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Shuck them, insert plastic holders on each end. Add water to an Instant Pot. Pressure cook for 3 minutes. Let them sit until you’re ready to eat. Grab them by the plastic holders, which will not be hot, and move them to your plate. That is the easiest way.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Not as good as boiled fresh picked young ears from the garden boiled for three minutes. Not even close. The real problem is that stores sell mature ears past their prime. You need super sweet ears that have kernels big enough to eat but not fully filled out and large. My corn here in Candia New Hampshire should be ready in 10 to 14 days. I invite your chief editor chef or whoever to come by and try it. You will never microwave corn on the cob again, trust me!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: wrap it in wet paper towel first

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I have been cooking corn like this for years. To make this dish extra special, coat the corn with goat's cheese instead of butter before serving. It's delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Even easier yet:  leave all the silk and husks intact.  Cut off a half inch of the cob at the stem end.  Microwave as directed.  Squeeze the pointy end of the cob and silk and husks slide off the pointy end leaving a butter ready cob of corn.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: After you microwave, if you cut the tail end (the stem), you can squeeze the corn out of the husks, and the silk gets left behind. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AJ75-64Gm5w

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Air fryer works pretty well too.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I've been doing this for years.  The single person's "go to"

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Shuck under running water - it washes the corn and keeps you from getting burned.  Don’t run too long under the water, just enough to shuck.  It will stay hot and then you can butter/salt.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: You Must wrap them twice around in wet paper towels tp make them moist and steamed within themselves.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I found out this method works great but felt guilty about it. Cooking in the husk keeps the corn juicy, it only takes a few minutes and no pots to clean up. If cooking on the grill, I pre-cook in the microwave and then finish on the grill.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: A lot of corn purists are aghast at the use of a microwave but we have been doing it this way for years and it is great. And we don't add butter or salt or anything and just enjoy the natural sweetness of the corn.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody:
Great for one person. For a dozen cobs that would be 3 X 12 = 36 minutes, plus having to shuck them all while hot.

For 12 cobs, put a large pot of water on the stove to bring to a boil. With a chef's knife, cut off the top and stem ends of each cob & shuck them, pulling away as much of the 'silk' as possible. When water boils, add a generous amount of salt, add the shucked corn and  start timing 6 mins after the water comes back to a boil. Less than 30 minutes to shuck and cook a dozen.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Sometimes I end up with the corn without the husks- and then I use a damp flour sack tea towl to wrap the corn in, then microwave- Easy peasy :)

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: This is exactly how we make corn on the cob. If you microwave more than one, you need to add additional time. Enjoy.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody:
75 years ago my family always made corn on picnic cook outs by throwing the unhusked corn into the fire. That was the best and boiled corn never was as good as that. 30 years ago I started cooking the unhusked corn in the microwave and  there was the delicious corn knew from childhood! Both methods steam the corn in the husks. Boiling leaches out most of the flavor.
Broccoli and cauliflower also do well in the microwave.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I've been doing it this way for years, but if I have more than two ears I find it takes a three minute zap, then turn turn the ears and zap for another three minutes. (Beats my grandmother's way of making sweet corn which was to start boiling it in water with a cup of sugar at three in the afternoon so it would be "ready" by dinner time. Grandma wasn't much of a cook.)

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: This is how we have cooked corn for years. Cut off fat ends of corn. Microwave 3 minutes for 4 ears; add 1 minute for an additional 2 ears. Let sit for a couple minutes, but don't let it completely cool. While still hot, squeeze the smaller end (using a dish towel) to force the corn out of the husk - this will leave the corn "hairs" behind.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: do it all summer...always surprisingly perfect!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Astonishing how few people know this technique. I've been doing it for 30 years.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Get some of the microwaveable, screw-in corn cob holders that are available on line.  Slice off the stem end and screw in the holder.  It makes handling the hot ear of corn easier when you do the squeeze-the-top maneuver to remove the husks and silks.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I husk it first and wrap it in a damp paper towel- 2 minutes in microwave and turn it for another 2 minutes.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Simpler, but still overwrought. Leaving the husk on while heating accomplishes nothing but ensuring burnt hands. Shucking raw cobs then microwaving them works just fine. 2 minutes for 1 cob, check that it's piping hot, then give it another 30-60 seconds if needed. Done.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I have made corn this way many times. It has made eating fresh corn very doable; no big pot of water to boil and no waiting. I run the cooked corn under cold water before I shuck it. Still very warm to enjoy, but no burning skin.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I've done this for several years. Three minutes is way too long! I cook mine for 1.5 minutes and then let it set for about one minute. I peel back the husk under warm running water and the silks just slide off, probably because microwaves cook things from the inside out and they get pushed out a little. They are hot, crunchy and delicious, even without salt or seasoning.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: This has been my go to for corn in the summer as I don't have a grill.  Yum!!!  I do agree with folks that you need to protect your hands when shucking.  If you don't have silicone mitts, layer paper towels on the mitts or potholders to remove the husks and silk.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Three minutes is enough, but may depend on your microwave.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: @Elaine And the size of the ear.And the variety if corn. I start with 2 min./ear and go from there.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I’ve been doing this for years. It’s the best way to cook corn on the cob.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Fresh, local, in-season corn is delicious uncooked!!!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody:
Not for everyone.
The corn is not always thoroughly cooked, hence, possibly difficult to digest.
If a problem, boil.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I’ve been doing this for years.  Pro tip:  Run some water over the corn first (I get it in the open end).  That helps it steam better.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I soak my husked corn for a minute, then put the husked corn on a gas grill for 10-14 minutes.  Because it comes from the farm stand down the street, it needs nothing else.  No salt no butter.  Just pure sweetness.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Even I could handle this recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I learned this exact thing from a farmer selling his corn from a stand on the side of a road in PA.  It's amazing.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I've been doing it this way for years and years. The only hitch is, it’s not practical if you’re cooking for a crowd.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: The only way to go is to Microwave sweet corn in the microwave with a corn steamer such as the one from (and not necessarily the only site) from Walter Drake https://www.mileskimball.com/buy-microwave-corn-steamer-311459?srsltid=AfmBOorSmw4164mhyqjcHOtRtvsbcrpUqYmH35N80_V-0qJrO1SHdB19 Two tablespoons water in each compartment, place shucked corn in separate compartments then microwave for 5 minutes ... Perfectly "steamed," delicious delectable sweet corn with little or no effort!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I shuck them first, put in a deep dish with an inch of water, cover and micro on high for 3 minutes for one cob.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: This article does not address cooking pre shucked corn. I wrap mine in a moist dish towel  Works great.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Best part about microwave cooking on-cob corn, aside from eliminating vats of hot water, is that the ears are not dripping wet, so more butter sticks! Less mess!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I have been doing this for years. Steaming in the microwave maintains a more intense corn flavor than boiling the corn.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: You miss the final step. Cut off the heel of the corn with a sharp knife and then with a potholder, squeeze the top so as to push out the corn minus the silk! It works beautifully once you get the hang of it. Silk free delicious corn.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Been cooking corn this way for years.  So easy!  Another tip if you want to shuck and wash the corn before cooking, wrap each ear in a wet paper towel and cook as if they are unshucked.  Same result an no worry about shucking hot corn and burning your fingers.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody:
We eat corn often and simply using a knife cut a circle on the shallow side of the stem and microwave it for 2 minutes 12 seconds.Let it sit for 30 seconds and with a paper towel grab the husk and pull.You will be left with a clean cob ready to eat
Gene

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: One tip to make it even better: the "toothpaste method." After microwaving the unhusked corn, cut around the bottom of the ear (before it curves inward) and then squeeze from the top like a toothpaste tube. If you've placed the cut in the right place, it'll slide out easily and not leave behind any silk. If not, make another cut a bit higher and/or deeper and try again.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody:
Thoughts from 40 yrs microwaving cobs.
