These seem like "city instructions." If one buys corn from closer to the farm, the outer husk may have dirt and such to remove. Also "country corn" has longer stems and silks.

Cut off the extra stem and just over the top of the ear to remove excess silks and ease peeling and expose cleaner leaves. The top cut also vents steam wo peeling.

I don't open ears before buying--one doesn't pre-peel bananas. Feel the fullness of top kernels through the husk.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I don't open corn before buying either.  A farmer taught me to look at the silk.  If it is nicely full and brown, not blackish, pale, slimy, or sparse, the corn will be ready.  There is one piece of silk for every kernel so a full head of silk means lots of kernels and the brown color tells you it was harvested at peak time.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody:
Missing the most important step.  First, cut off the stem at the point where the ear gets largest.

Then when the corn is done, grab it from the silk end and squeeze out the ear.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Here's a tip for getting the cooked ear out of the husk and silk quick and clean: before cooking, peel off the outer layer and leave enough of the tasseled end so you can grab it.  Cut the cob off at the stalk end just high enough so all of the husk leaves are no longer connected at the base.  When the corn is done, hold it in a hot pad or clean towel, grab it by the tassel end and squeeze the corn out the other end.  It will come out silk-free and ready to eat.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I shuck mine first, put in a long narrow microwavable pan (one of those white things) filled with water.  Cover with plastic wrap, microwave for about 3 minutes plus more if needed.  Always comes out perfectly.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: By way of Martha Stewart and my wife Jamie. Half an inch of water in a pot. Place any number of shucked ears in the pot, then cover and turn on heat. As soon as the water comes to a boil turn off heat and let sit for 5 minutes. Then you're good to go.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody:
You call for the corn to be "unhusked". Then your recipe instructions state "peel back the top of the husk to see . . ."

I'm cornfused.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Unhusked corn means the husk is still present when you microwave it.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Three minutes is always enough in my microwave, which is a high powered one. I never boil or steam corn anymore.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Three minutes is way too long! I take off the outer leaves and cook one ear for 80 seconds. You want fresh corn barely cooked to preserve the tenderness and that little explosion of flavor and juice when you bite into each kernel.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: This is the easiest and one of the best ways to cook corn on the cob.  My dad has been doing this for years!!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Glad to see other people liking this recipe.  The microwave method is my favorite way to cook corn on the cob.  Have been doing this since the 80's when I found Barbara Kafka's wonderful microwave recipes/tips for cooking corn and other veggies.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Squeezing the hushs off never worked well for me

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody:
the best way is to take the pot of boiling water into the field and bend down the stalk and it is ready in 3 minutes. The absolute freshest and best.
I still prefer 3 minutes in boiling water. This Iowa transplant keeps it simple.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: This is pretty much the only way I cook corn on the cob now! A squeeze of lime along with a sprinkle of ground Ancho chile and Maldon salt flakes provide a bit of a street corn flavor.  You do have to wait a few minutes for it to cool down enough to shuck, but that works out well as I can cook the corn and let it sit while I prep the other meal items.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Am I the only person who loves the silk as well?  I always peel it off the husks after it is cooked and eat it - minus the brown tops, which I cut off before cooking.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody:
the ONLY way I cook corn!
My brother insists on roasting corn in the barbecue, but honestly I think it comes out drier and tougher than simply zapping it.
I cook a single ear for 4 minutes in my 1200 watt microwave.  Husking it after a minute to cool is bearable; I just split it at the top until I see the tip of the corn, then haul the sides down and cut it all off at the bottom.  If I pinch the split husk from the outside as I peel it off, my fingers don't burn.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: I much prefer this way of cooking corn on the cob: boil 4 quarts of water, throw your husked ears in the pot, turn off the flame, and cover for at least 10 minutes. Much easier than this labor-intensive way of microwaving.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody:
Easy Peasy….too easy for some who just enjoy trying to remove all those silks from uncooked corn without making a mess, and like to steam heat the kitchen by boiling water on the stove.
If you grill corn, microwave in the husk for a minute or two, then husk as described and grill to finish.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: How did I not know of this technique?  It's the only way I cook corn on the cob anymore.  So easy.  Yes, ears are hot so use a glove, oven mitt or hot pad holder to shuck them at the end.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Wow, where has this been all my life! I LOVE corn and don't make it as often because of not wanting to go to the hassle if it's just me eating it. This is sooooo fast and easy and works great.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: OMG what a game changer! After decades of shocking corn and trying to get rid of the dill, this was so easy and delicious! I’m sold

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: Easy and cooked to perfection!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: The ease in shucking is not worth the sacrifice in how grilling or steaming (in salted water) brings out the sweetness of the corn.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody: this is amazing! How have I not known this until now? I love corn and this is so easy!! WOW!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Microwave Corn on the Cob Recipe
commentBody:
I cook shucked local corn in boiling water for three minutes. Take them out with tongs and roll them in a little slightly salted butter Yum. 
Jean Nj

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If you zap them too long and they become tough, just eat those as quickly as possible while no one is looking and start over.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The comments I've seen have largely been positive about not having to deal with bacon grease and all I can think is, "why are people not saving their bacon grease"? Save your bacon fat, people.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Thin bacon for 45 seconds; thick bacon for 1 minute per slice. For less cleanup, place it on top of paper towels, with one paper towel on top to catch splatters.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is how I've always done it, the fat drenches both papers leaving lovely crunchy [if you like it that way], somewhat guiltless delight.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I use paper towels under & over the bacon for less grease.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I like to cook it through mostly in the microwave to get rid of most of the mess, and then finish it off in a pan for a little extra crisp. My bacon is done when the fire alarm goes off.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If you cook bacon in the MicroWave, how do you fry your eggs in the bacon grease?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The idea is nice but your microwave will stink after

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The oven method I use is identical to yours, but I put it in a cold 375º oven for 20 minutes for perfect bacon. Add a few minutes for thick-cut slices.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Hmmm... 20 minutes v. 2 minutes...

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It is by far the best way to cook bacon. Use paper towel on a paper plate and then there's no pan to clean up and bacon grease to dispose of. The whole process is quick and easy.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Parchment paper works better

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: In 970 watt microwave it takes about 1 minute per slice for medium doneness.   It’s the only way to cook bacon!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This, to me, is the best way to cook bacon. All the grease is left on the paper towel and the bacon is deliciously crisp. I do the same with pepperoni before adding it as a topping on my homemade pizzas. It's amazing how much grease is in the pepperoni, thankfully left on the paper towel and not deposited in my blood raising my cholesterol.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Not a fan of bacon cooked in the microwave. It just never tastes as good as bacon cooked in a skillet or in the oven.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I use the Makin Bacon Dish - super simple, easy and has less fat compared to other methods, particularly this messy approach.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
How to microwave bacon:
Place bacon in microwave.
Microwave.
Eat.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I learned this trick 40 years ago from...Craig Claiborne in the New York Time cookbook. He stated that it was the only thing the microwave was good for.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Scott It’s not reasonable to expect everyone to know that a truly influential person such as Craig Claiborne introduced a technique decades ago. But I really dislike it when contemporary food personalities pretend they’ve invented or discovered those techniques that they otherwise rightly bring attention to.  Every generation of new cooks will need to know the best ways to prepare food.  It won’t harm your brand if you give credit where credit is due when you’re writing or demonstrating.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: A nice convenience if you're in a hurry but to me the bacon gets tough and loses flavor compared to pan frying.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Don't use your good plates! Even if they are microwave safe, they will get extremely hot under the bacon. Two of my nice plates shattered.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I use a dedicated hard plastic microwave bacon plate, with ridges like a "grilling" skillet. Cover with a paper towel. 2-1/2 minutes for two slices (1100 watts). The grease collects in channels around the sides. You can pour it out, or keep it if you want to cook something in the fat. 

It cooks the bacon evenly, and quickly. Been doing it for 20-30 years.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I've been doing this for at least 25 years. It is the best, but you need plenty of paper towels. You can actually buy a microwave plate with ridges that keep the bacon above the pooling grease. I have one now and it is definitely worth buying,

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It works well if you like the microwave smelling of bacon every time you run it after cooking bacon.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Bacon in the microwave is fine but for ideal crispy bacon use your air fryer. Four slices - eight minutes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: "Save your bacon fat, people." (Robert, 3 weeks ago) YES! For me, it is best for cooking garlic fried rice to eat with the bacon... And eggs!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: An air fryer is the easiest, least messy method for me.  I don’t see why there aren’t more recipes using them

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Paper towels on top and beneath-- lets you flip your slices without dirtying a fork, and helps with grease.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: we need to cook the eggs in the leftover grease.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Steamed bacon fat coats the interior surface of the microwave. Thin coating of bacon fat/smell persist.  Mitigation is labor-intensive.  Pass.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was a terrible idea. The bacon was mushy, did not get crisp even after repeated tries, and was tough.  Not doing this again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Is this serious or a comedy skit? The only thing worse than microwave bacon is maybe boiling it .

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If bacon is cooked BETWEEN layers of paper towels it works much better will be very much less greasy.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Looks delicious. I’ll probably try it some day. Was looking for a less complicated recipe at the moment though

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: In my microwave, 2-3 strips take 37 seconds.  Medium crisp, just the way I like it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I've been doing this for some time.  It's the only way to cook bacon, but you must put towel roll on the plate, then the bacon, then cover with towel roll.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I have cooked bacon in the microwave for years.  Place enough paper towels on the plate to absorb all the grease.  One layer over the top to prevent splattering.   Discover the right time for your particular bacon and number of strips.  

NOTE:  regarding the paper towels:  a cheaper paper towel, with thin loose knit, works better than expensive thick paper towels.  I use Scott for this.  The expensive towels will stick to the bacon and could ruin your result.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I've been doing this for years.
I also put paper towels under the bacon (2 on top and under).
The more strips, the more time required. I only do 2 straps for 3.5 minutes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I followed this recipe, had to double the cooking time in my 1000w microwave as I desired maximum crispiness.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Put a paper towel both UNDER and OVER the bacon. Makes cleanup so much easier.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I've cooked bacon like this for 20 yrs.  I use paper towels under and on top of the bacon.  I simply cook until the strips are done.  The time varys depending on how thick the bacon is and how many strips you are cooking at the same time. Just keep checkiing. The paper towels soak up the grease. Microwave bacon makes great sandwiches or crumbles for a salad. Makes flat, straight bacon strips - that's okay with me. For large quantities, use the oven.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I like very crispy bacon and always cook a full package at a time. I put 6 slices onto 2 layers of paper towels, sitting on a Corel dinner plate. I put one paper towel over the top. I cook on high for 3-4 minutes, then repeat the process 2 more times for the full package. I put the grease-soaked paper towels in a fine mesh strainer and let the excess bacon grease filter through the paper for later use.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: You can absolutely do turkey bacon the same way. I prefer it with my morning breakfast burrito everyday - cook the egg in the microwave for 1 minute and tortilla with shredded daiya cheese on it for 20 seconds. Throw it all together with your favorite hot sauce and you have a nice easy lower calorie treat in less than 5 minutes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Put a piece of paper towel on the plate first, arrange bacon, then cover with second piece of paper towel for best results. Captures fat and bacon is crispy. If you do more than 5 strips you can add a layer of bacon and paper towel be aware that if corners of paper towel overlap the edge of the plate it will wick fat off the plate and into the microwave or the resting place outside the microwave. Care in positioning the towel will help avoid this.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Forget the oil and smell ... even in a microwave. Latch on to Costco/Kirkland fully cooked bacon. https://www.costcobusinessdelivery.com/kirkland-signature-fully-cooked-bacon%2C-hickory-wood-smoked%2C-1-lb.product.100462467.html Easy to prepare in 3 - 5 minutes in a toaster oven. Wonderful tasty, crisp, and perfectly prepared bacon. No greasy oil orders, easy to clean up, etc..

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: You should use at least two-three  paper towels on the bottom of the microwave safe plate to soak up the grease. Spray the paper towels with Pam so the bacon doesn’t stick. Put another paper towel,on top. Spray it Also. One minute per slice then check, press the “add 30 seconds” button , check each time until crisp.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I use paper towels under and over the bacon on a paper plate to make microwave bacon. No mess and no dishes! 2 minutes for two slices makes it nice and crispy usually.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: People are hating on bacon grease, but when I cook a sheet pan of bacon, I strain the grease through a coffee filter, and it provides a lot of flavor, and it's very stable in the fridge for long storage because all the solids are strained out. Use it in moderation like any saturated fat. Flavor!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I cook bacon in the air fryer. Much less mess and shrinkage.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: One problem is that the slices might cook unevenly, with the centers getting overdone while the rest is cooked correctly.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Mine came out well, the only downside being that the plate I planned to eat off of was too hot to touch. No grease-splattered stove, but two plates to clean - I call it a draw.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Air fryer is easier, can do lots in 6 minutes and the fat runs to bottom so can be used for whatever.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used this method for 25 years.  I found a microwave bacon dish on Amazon and it changed my life.  No more wasteful paper towels.  Cooks so much more evenly and my supposedly microwave plates were getting cracks.  The grease drains nicely into can or plastic cup (I have a septic system).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Nordic Ware makes a microwave bacon rack with a lid to cook bacon and save the bacon grease without making a mess of the microwave. The base is reversible with a ribbed side to carry away the grease of bacon and a flat side for cooking anything else. The clear plastic list covers both sides.I've used it for more than twenty years. Look it up on Amazon.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Hormel Black Label microwaveable is simple and great. 4 strips per pkg and 4 pkgs per box.

2 1/2 minutes (1200 MV )and you have great bacon. I like it crisper so I’ll use mine for 20-30 seconds more

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Microwave one to five slices - that's the wah to go. More than five, bake on parchment-lined sheet pan in a 390°F oven for 18 to 20 minutes. Save that rendered bacon fat.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Try zapping in air fryer.  That works too, and you can put it to cook outside if spouse doesn’t like the smell.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’ve been doing this for years . Thanks for the NYT validation!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: And your microwave will stink of bacon for a year.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: interesting.   We have a small combo oven (oven, convection, and microwave.   I currently cook bacon on a tray lined with tin foil at 385° until it's done.   Very little mess.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’ve been cooking m6 daily 2 pieces of bacon this way for over ten years. I use the ribbed microwave plates with lids and cook the bacon around 1 minute per piece. The plates clean up in the dishwasher easily, the lid keeps the microwave clean and the fat collects between the ribs. After cooking I remove the bacon and then let the grease cool a little before pouring it into absorbent trash in the trash can and then drop it in the dishwasher. I have two of these plates so one is always available. I will never cook bacon any other way unless I don’t have a microwave.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The easiest way to cook bacon is on a baking sheet lined with crumpled aluminum foil.  400 degrees for about 15 minutes, you can cook a pound or two of bacon, pull the bacon off the foil when done.  After the pan cools, you just throw away the crumpled up foil which has caught the bacon fat.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The easiest way is in the air fryer.  Lay them out, set the timer and voila, perfect bacon strips.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I have saved a couple of old Corelle plates and bowls specifically for microwaving.  They don’t get over heated, are lightweight, and can be easily washed in dishwasher.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Want it even faster? Buy pre-cooked bacon. It takes, literally, seconds for it to heat up in the microwave. I used to par-cook bacon in the oven, finishing it off in the microwave before serving. That way I could pour off and save the grease. But I do enjoy the convenience. Oh, and it's shelf stable at room temperature before opening.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I have always cooked bacon this way. Delish!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Works absolutely perfectly. Dream come true.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Maybe it's me or maybe it's my bacon but the bacon tasted really bland. It also took six minutesd for four slices in my cheap microwave oven.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It's a quick way to get to bacon, but boy do you lose taste along the way.  I prefer to stick as many slices as i need +2 on a cookie sheet, throw the sheet on the top rack of a 450 degree oven, and go away until about 2 minutes after it finishes preheating.  Not a whole lot of mess, plenty of bacony goodness and the slices come out flat and perfectly evenly cooked.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Use a bacon hanger with paper towel folded over the bacon.  All the oil drips into the bottom of the pan and the paper towel catches more oil/prevents splatter.  In our small, low power microwave, crispy bacon is 130-135 for 1 slice.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Have found that wrapping the bacon slices with several layers of paper towels eliminates all messy grease deposits on plates, micro, etc.  and bacon is crisp and delicious. Bacon must be laid piece next to piece  in the towelling by  and not stacled.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Use one regular or two half size paper towels, lay 2 slices of bacon across the width of one end of the paper towel then wrap the paper towel around the bacon, place on an upside down paper plate and microwave for 90 seconds to 2 minutes depending on thickness of bacon and desired doneness.  This will leave a splatter free microwave oven.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: You can microwave turkey bacon this way, too.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Great way to use up tomatoes this time of year!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Why not turkey bacon? It works for me.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The plates can get really hot!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Don’t overload. 6 strips is about the limit.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Don’t be afraid to try microwaving turkey bacon. It works just as well for the times mentioned for regular bacon. I do about a quarter lb which takes roughly 5 minutes for crisp slices. Keep,an eye on the final minutes in any case.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I do this for turkey bacon. No plate needed, just a paper towel on top and underneath.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My sister uses the microwave to cook her bacon, but she uses a lot of paper towels. Is there a less wasteful way of cooking?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: You could fry the bacon in a frying pan.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: You can also finish the strips in a hot pan. They crisp better and shed so little fat that you can just wipe the pan clean with a paper towel.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I avoid the odor in my home by placing heavy duty RWrap on a baking sheet & cooking to a desired level on my grill (I save grease) then place in plastic bag & refrigerate.  I remove what I want for the dish, double wrap in paper towel & finish cooking.  Odor is outside, no mess as grease cools it is easy to handle, remove aluminum foil.  No MESS!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Microwave works great for those of us that can’t lift their cast iron skillet any longer.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Just did my bacon this way. Perfect result and less mess!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I have made bacon this way since I was old enough to reach the microwave, always use paper towel on top and bottom, 1 min per slice minimum, use tongs to clean the grease off the microwave plate afterwards by grabbing the used paper towel and sopping the rest of the grease up with it, done!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This did not work at all for me. I just ended up pan frying.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Good to use a microwave cooking sheet designed to cook bacon. Catches all the grease from the bacon. Still need to use a paper towel on cover the bacon.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: So 45 seconds a piece. Then I’d Mike 4 pieces for 3 minutes?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Thumbs up for microwaved bacon!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I do the same, but only halfway, with paper towels on top and bottom. I finish them on the frying pan and save the rest in the freezer for my next happy meal. I can't eat the whole package for one meal.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I learned how to this in 1979 when my parents got their first microwave. My kids were shocked their college friends didn't know about this.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
My Dad microwaved bacon 30+ years ago. He had one of those thick ceramic ridged plates that were in vogue for microwave cooking.

I didn't eat bacon for a long time, but after my doctor put me on a low carb diet, I started eating it again. I cook it in the oven at 425 on a sheet of tin foil in a baking pan. Nice and crispy, but not real fast. I may tray the microwave again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I find frying in a cast iron pan gave the best, if messy, crispness.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I have used a Nordic Ware Large Slanted Bacon Tray for many years. I prefer the cold start cast iron skillet method but I seem to go back to the microwave method a lot.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: For really crisp and tasty bacon, nothing beats a hardwood grill.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The bacon grease MUST be saved, for a warm dressing for my spinach salad. Preferably curly spinach. Has anyone tried skipping the paper towel and covering the bacon with another plate? I don't have any bacon, or I would try this right now.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: And no more impossible cleanup job afterwards

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I quit using a paper towel in the microwave when I had a grease fire. Bacon can cook on the rows in the microwave safe dish. Then wrap in a paper towel when finished. Much safer and works well.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: We have a dog who loves bacon grease on his kibble.  I microwave the bacon on a plate with another plate upside down on top to catch the grease splatters.  When bacon is done I simply pour the bacon grease from the plate into a suitable container and use it for the dog ...or frying eggs the next day.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: 12 minutes in a 450°F oven also works really well

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: While the microwave does a great job you still end up with a house that smells like bacon. We cook bacon on the grill. It is supercrisp, there’s zero cleanup and the house stays fresh.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Easy and less messy for sure.  However the final product pales in comparison to pan-fried bacon.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: There should be a paper towel layer on the bottom as well as the top of the bacon, to soak up the grease, and the bacon should be flipped halfway through cooking, unless you like unevenly cooked bacon.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Be wary about paper towels coming into contact with food when cooking in the microwave. They could leach toxic chemicals.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: cooking in the oven is way less of a mess

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It may be faster, but oven baked is better for an easy clean up! I just put the bacon on parchment paper and bake at 400 about 20-25 minutes. No turning over needed either!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I buy precooked bacon bits at Costco.  Most of the fat is already cooked out of it.  I just put a power towel under it in a bowl and zap it for 30 seconds.   It's good in omelettes and other dishes that require bacon.   It's not a whole strip, but almost as good.  A bag Lasts months in the fridge and doesn't need to be refrigerated until opened.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Several layers of paper towels under the bacon with one towel layer over top works well.   Lower quality towels may stick.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Having microwaved and baked bacon--baking is better. Depending on oven size, you can get a whole pound done in about 25 minutes-- and you get to keep the grease. Think summer industrial production of BLT's. Or any bacon dish. Save a few scraps for the dog, of course. 

Admittedly, if you want two slices right  now, it's the microwave.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: For those worried about the fragrance (not stink) left behind, it is temporary and apparently  you've never heard of a squeeze of lemon juice in a little dish of water and running the microwave for a minute or two.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Shouldn’t have put my two strips on a Heath plate. The plate broke in two. A $30 mistake. Choose your plate wisely.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I use paper towels under and over.  The bacon is grease free and super crispy!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Only if I'm adding bacon to a sandwich. Bacon and evgs, fry the bacon first, then fry the eggs in the bacon grease. If you want to save the fat from the bacon in microwave, just put the paper town over the bacon. The grease will collect mostly in the plate and you can drain it off. If you don't care, then a towel below and on top of the bacon works well.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: for three pieces of bacon on high, you need around 3.5 minutes for well done

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Been experimenting with this since I read this very interesting (to bacon lovers everywhere) article two weeks ago. Here are my thoughts: 1. Even with the paper towel, I get a thin layer of bacon grease on the walls of my microwave. It's not much, but still. 2. The bacon does come out perfectly cooked! 3. Cooking in a ceramic bowl with a ceramic plate as a cover, with no paper towel, lets me save the bacon grease for later and also use less paper products.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Turkey bacon! I can attest that the Oscar Mayer Nitrate-free turkey bacon crisps up beautifully using this method.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: To have fresh bacon by the slice, roll your fresh bacon slice by slice in a length of plastic wrap and freeze it. The bacon can then be rolled out when you want a slice while the rest is returned to the freezer for fresh keeping.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Try putting bacon on paper , rolling up like a sleeping bag and microwaving 75 seconds. Crisp, flat, un-oily, and all fat is cooked crisp, too, and delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Been cooking bacon in the microwave for years! It's fast but uses a ton of paper towels.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is indeed the very best way to cook bacon.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Recently parts of the bacon stuck to the paper towel. Didn’t happen before with a different brand. Any ideas?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’ve been cooking bacon everyday on the microwave plates for over ten years and I cannot imagine why anyone would ever cook it any other way unless you’re working on a commercial griddle with a bacon press. But in that case you have someone else cleaning after up after you. 😊

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Must have to say that streaky bacon needs to be used and not back bacon as found on UK supermarket shelves

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is the only way I cook bacon. I've done it this way for years and years. No clean up!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is brilliant. Who knew?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The easiest scone recipe is 2 cups self rising flour and one cup heavy cream or Greek yogurt. Add rosemary and cheddar if you like. Mix the dough gently in a med bowl and roll it out on parchment about one inch thick. Use biscuit cutters. Brunch with melted butter. Bake 450 for 8 min.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is easy. But there’s no getting away from the chemical taste of self rising flour.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Self rising flour is a great way to make baking easier but is more flexible (and cheaper) than a boxed mix, and it is common in many parts of the world, including the UK. If you’re using any chemical leavener, such as baking powder, it too could impart a “chemical” taste, but that usually happens if there is too much of it. Self rising flour gives a better lift, with the leavener better distributed, than flour plus baking powder and/or soda.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Patricia Noll how much cheddar? Thank you!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’ve got half-and-half.  Will that work instead of heavy cream?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Kira yes! If you have it, add a teaspoon of cornstarch and swap the last 1/4 cup for either yogurt or sour cream.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Not as written, no. You'd be ~halving the fat levels which will impact the tenderness. You could add fat back in by adding butter, but then it's a different recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Kira While the amount of HC is slightly different, I make a similar recipe from Cooks Illustrated that calls for HC and I use half/half all the time.  It’s what I always have on hand for coffee. They seem fine to me. Can’t hurt to try.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Two questions for the scone experts:
1. Can the dough be rested just as successfully in the bowl. vs shaped and on a sheet (working with a small fridge so sheet is tough to fit)
2.  Any thoughts on the whipped cream approach to scone batter (folding in gently whipped cream that is) vs the liquid
Thanks in advance scone bakers.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @MS King Arthur has a similar cream scone recipe. You can freeze before baking. Use liquid heavy cream. Don’t over work.

author: Genevieve Ko
Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Perfect Sunday morning treat! Easy, relatively fast (after chilling the dough, in the bowl, for about an hour. Who has space for baking trays in the fridge???), and delicious. Used ½ & ½ (b/c who has heavy cream just hanging around?) subbing ¼ cup with Greek yogurt for a lively tanginess. No one in our house is into cardamom, so subbed 1 tsp. vanilla and the zest of 1 lemon. Will definitely be on our weekend rotation with whatever fruit is on hand.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@GreenSageSJ

Love the substitutions and comments you made, *except* for substituting out cardamom, which is one of my very favorite spices! However, I do know that it’s not everybody’s cup of tea :-)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: i made this exactly as written.  Made the dough in the evening, scooped mounds onto a cookie sheet, covered it with foil and refrigerated it overnight.  In the morning, I painted the tops with cream and sprinkled with demerara sugar, baked at 425 for 25 minutes.  They were perfect. Delicious and fluffy.  And REALLY EASY!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Unless you are opening a new box of currants, you will want to plump them in hot water for 10-15 minutes, drain, and pat dry before adding them to the dough.  Also, chilling before baking for several hours allows the dough to rest and is a very important step.  I probably would wait until the oven is heating before brushing the scones with cream and sprinkling them with sugar.  If I were using non-wild blueberries, I'd reduce the amount to ½ cup.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: How does one fit a baking pan into one’s fridge?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: You don't have to. I just kept it in the bowl and scooped out blobs of scone after about an hour of resting, while I walked the dog. Came out perfectly.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Easiest scone recipe I have made. Delicious. I added a teaspoon of cinnamon instead of cardamom.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This recipe can be even quicker.  Whisk all dry ingredients, toss in fruit to coat with flour to avoid purple dough, then add the cream stirring with a spatula and/or your hands.  You can make a ball, pat it into a circle, apply some cream to the top & sprinkle with demera sugar or cinnamon, etc. Then chill if you have time, cut into wedges & bake on sheet.  Mine usually take 13 to 14 mins until golden.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wondering if 1:1 ratio gluten free flour could be used as a substitute?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Elizabeth yes, it should be fine.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Elizabeth always measure the gf flour by grams and it should work fine.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: First time, so followed the recipe except used 1 C fresh blueberries. Also used fresh ground cardamom from the pods. Light and oh so tasty, blueberries and cardamom … a wonderful pairing. Thanks.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Subbing in part almond flour? Yes, absolutely! And mini chocolate chips instead of blueberries would also be delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I was suspicious, with how easy this recipe is…but these scones. Wow. DELICIOUS. A new go-to for me. Love love love.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: In recipes where butter is used, I was taught to cut the butter in by pinching with my fingers. It is fast and efficient, and requires no extra specialty tools.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
My first attempt at baking--I've plenty experience buying blueberry scones at coffee shops. These were much better than coffee shop muffins. I followed Genevieve Ko's recipe closely and I'll do it again soon.
PS My wife who is the baker in our family also thinks they're great.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I've been using this substitute for 40  years, thanks to my ancient Fanny Farmer Cookbook. 1/2 tsp. cream of tartar   1/4 tsp. baking soda = 1 tsp. commercial baking powder. No chemical taste. 
Can't wait to try this recipe 😋

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My new favorite scone recipe! So tender and moist. No rolling or cutting and easily adaptable to other fruit and spices. I found frozen wild blueberries at Whole Foods. Internal temp of 200-205F is easy way to test for doneness.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @KD thank you for the temp info. I have a temperamental oven  and the toothpick test isn’t always accurate.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I halved the recipe (just making a smaller batch) and used dried cranberries, a pinch of cinnamon, and swapped cream for kefir since that’s what I had on hand—they turned out perfect. Tall, golden, and with just the right amount of crisp from that sprinkle of sugar on top. These scones are everything you want in a quick bake—effortless yet impressive, perfect for breakfast or a mid-afternoon snack. Delicious and super easy to whip up!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I am SO excited to write this comment-I usually am on the other side of comments, scrolling endlessly for tips, tricks and recommendations from people who seem more skillful and well versed than myself. I am an intimidated baker, but I had a hankering for scones and wanted to give this recipe a swing. 

I replicated the recipe as written, and I added only 1 tbsp of granulated sugar + the listed 1/3 cup/70g granulated sugar-I know myself to know that I enjoy sweet, but I did want these to taste like a more traditional scone-tender, crumbly, crisp on the outside, more cake-like, and more subtle, if that makes sense. And when I tell you that, 1, the recipe was very clear, SIMPLE and easy to follow and 2, they are absolutely HEAVENLY, I am not exaggerating. And again, because I get intimidated easily, to have come out with these scones felt like such a win-something a beginning home-baker can easily do and yield bakery results. I highly recommend this recipe! I had them ready for breakfast by the time my husband and daughter were awake, and it was such a special thing to share with them on a snowy weekend morning. I cant say enough good things about this recipe!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Crispy-chewy on the outside, but otherwise a bit like eating a sponge. Perhaps my fruit was too moist, but I'll stick with cutting in butter in future -- these just don't seem like scones to me. (You can even freeze a stick & grate/stir into the flour mix, very easy.)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Mine came out crispy on outside but spongy inside too. How to make them less spongy???

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Use buttermilk for the most tender scones ever! Use a scoop(no triangles or drop method.) I’m a professional baker, my scones  have been for sale and at resorts for years. Must freeze dough at least 20”, scoop and freeze overnight, take next day at room temperature. Frozen blueberries are fine, add by hand, after dry mix is ready (with butter, pea size), buttermilk added last, again by hand, don’t over mix. Try it first an improvement.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used the Cup 4 Cup gluten free flour, frozen blueberries, no extra cream or sugar on top.  Turned out delicious and with great consistency.  The 70g sugar is conservative- which I prefer, but many others would want it sweeter.  Will definitely make again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Perfect recipe! So happy to not have to deal with the cold butter piece.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’d like to report my half buckwheat flour and half white flour experiment worked! (I tried a half batch first). I found I needed to slightly increase the cream and decided to gently pat and cut instead of dropping since the dough was a bit crumbly. Used tiny wild blueberries. These were excellent! Crisp exterior with a warm, soft interior and not too sweet! Easier than flipping pancakes. I gilded the lily by adding some maple sugar on top.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
This is much easier than the alternate scone version that uses 1/2 cup of grated frozen butter in place of a half cup of cream.  But it’s not as good.  This had a much more “biscuit-y” taste than the buttery crispy scone.  

I think the “strawberry drop biscuit” recipe is also easy and closer to the scone that we like…made instead with blueberries, of course!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I've tried these twice and the dough does not bind. It falls apart in small clumps. Any suggestions?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Only add enough liquid to make the shaggy dough as indicated in the recipe.  Another fix is to allow the dough to hydrate before forming it into the scones.  This simply means to let it rest at room temp uncovered so the flour has a chance to absorb more of the moisture in the dough.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used fresh blueberries, added the zest of one lemon and omitted the cardamom. Delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Buttermilk okay instead of cream?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If you use fresh blueberries, two tips: 1. Smaller berries (5mm diameter), like the blueberries you see in the wild in the Pacific Northwest, are better suited than the commercial ones (10mm or bigger) because they dont sink to the bottom of the dough, but stay well distributed throughout it. 2. Since most of us cant get wild berries, dredging the berries in flour and (optional) pricking them with a toothpick before folding in helps.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Used White Lilly self-rising flour and cream, was delighted with the flavor and texture. Left in the bowl overnight, scooped carefully to bake in the morning.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: White Lilly flour is not available in all states.  I've had good results with regular self rising flour with a portion subbed out for cornstarch.  For each cup of self rising flour I remove 1 tbs and replace it with a tbs of cornstarch.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious, incredibly easy and simple, not too sweet. I have made these four or five times already. Making them the evening before is great, so all you have to do in the morning is pop them into the oven. Used regular blueberries instead of wild and whole grain flour, so don’t be afraid to substitute, because they are still wonderful. I am planning to keep making them every couple of weeks until we are sick of them, which may be never.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I don’t know why we continue to insist on round biscuits/scones. If you work the dough in the shape of a rough rectangle, you can just cut it into squares & never have to deal/ leftover bits of dough again. The drop method also eliminates this problem.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I wanna sub some cornmeal for flour, a la this: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1016842-cornmeal-plum-scones I'm thinking 1/8 cup cornmeal, to be proportionate with how that recipe does it. Anyone know more precisely how cornmeal might affect baking soda, cream, etc.?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are so simple and absolutely delicious!! I had never made scones before and I was skeptical they would be as easy as everyone said, but they are! I made a blueberry batch as written (perfect!) and then a batch with currants and subbed 1 tsp of ground anise for the cardamom (amazing)! Highly recommend serving with fresh whipped cream on the side (I made mine with maple syrup and a little ground anise sprinkled in) :) :)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: FANTASTIC - these were great prepared exactly as described with fresh blueberries that were solidly medium sized (not small as the tip suggests). I sprinkled turbinado sugar on top before baking. Take the "don't overmix" instruction seriously - I just stirred a few times until there was no unincorporated flour, a dozen strokes at most.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I wouldn’t make them again. Crusty on bottom and not cooked in the middle

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Is there another berry that could substitute for blueberries?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Third time with this recipe, great as before. Have adjusted the recipe in 3 ways:  bake for 23 minutes (turned dough into 12 ample sized scones), large handful of fresh blueberries are great when baked … syrupy and yummie, 1/2 tsp fresh ground cardamom… the effort is worth it. Thanks

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this with gluten free flour and turned out amazing (bob's 1:1). I chilled overnight in the bowl and baked when the guests arrived in the morning. Followed recipe exactly weighing out ingredients.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made these with currants and used half Sonoran soft whole wheat flour. They are perfect! Instead of drop scones, I patted the dough into a circle and cut 8 triangles. I also sprinkled with a bit of turbinado sugar after brushing with cream. They are lightly crisp on the outside with a moist, fluffy interior. Will definitely make again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Trader Joes has some amazingly wonderful frozen tiny wild blueberries!!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Fantastic and so easy! Inspired by other comments, I added a half teaspoon of Vanilla Extract and used Cinnamon instead of Cardamom. Also I used a few more frozen blueberries, about 100g. I refrigerated the dough overnight in a covered bowl. A delicious breakfast (or anytime) treat!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Rinse and drain your frozen blueberries like the tip says. I used cardamom and I liked it. Before baking, I cooled the prepared sheet pan in the fridge for about an hour after moving a few things out of the way. A biscuity scone. Very satisfying when warm about 40 minutes after baking. Moist, nice blueberry ratio, not too sweet and a simple recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is a very easy ‘drop scone recipe;  chilling is necessary to solidify the fat in the cream prior to baking.  Used an all-purpose GF flour blend  (without xanthum gum in it); but added scant 1/2 t. Baking soda to help with an adequate ‘rise’ due to GF flour;  came out perfect—used frozen berries from our bushes which were thawed on paper towels—and used half n half as that’s what was in the house.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’m so glad I made these. They were perfect and so easy. Took about 22 mins in my oven, watching closely to ensure no burning. Tried really hard to avoid over-mixing and over-forming.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My dough turned out really wet-not sure what to do. Adding a little more flour and crossing fingers!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Please, some wise baker, answer these questions: 
Use self-rising flour and Greek yogurt? Can it be low-fat or no-fat yogurt?
Chill dough in the bowl?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Debora I used self raising flour but stuck to the cream. I chilled the dough in the fridge in a big bowl and then shaped the scones and they turned out really great

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: OMG… these are unbelievably great, especially using just picked blueberries from my garden! I did weigh the mounds before putting them on the sheet to be cooked and weighed them… about 45-50 grams each. They cooked perfectly at 20 minutes (slightly browned but not as brown as the photo that goes with this recipe).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Delicious. Used double the weight for blueberries, fresh instead of frozen. 

My go-to has always been Joanne Chang’s recipe for blueberry scones… but i think i have a new go-to for easier scones!!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I tried without cooling and using gluten free flour : works just as well and the scones were delicious

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I make an almost identical recipe all the time.  The chilling helps the scones keep their shape but isn't necessary if you're in a hurry.  I riff off the basics with all kinds of options (fresh blueberries & lemon zest, dried wild blueberries & lemon zest, apricots with ginger & almonds,
cranberries & pecans...let your imagination loose).  I do use heavy cream and these are always delicious & light!  I have subbed in gluten free flour or a mix of gluten free flour and almond flour.  It works!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Maybe it's just me, but I would call rhese cream biscuits. I think scones must have butter to be called a scene, to get that essential crumbly, crispy texture.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Liza Feltimo Yes, these look like what we’d call Rock Cakes, or Rock Buns, in the UK, and constitute most children’s first school baking efforts.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I love to cook, but baking (even easy baking) is intimidating to me. We can delve in to that in our next session. These were super easy and tasted great. I made them exactly as is (I don't change a recipe until I've tried it first) and I loved them.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Used cinnamon instead of cardamom and added about a quarter tsp of lemon zest. These scones are a family favorite and so easy!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: To reheat from frozen, I stuck them in the air fryer for 4 min at 325F

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Love this recipe. Very easy and highly delicious. Have made it several times and modified slightly overtime. I add the zest of a lemon instead of cardamom, use fresh blueberries and double the weight, and followed the advice of others and rest in the bowl as a ball. Have made into 10 and 8 scones, 8 produces better sized portions. Make at your own risk bc you will not be able to resist making frequently!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used eggnog instead of cream, and then no sugar except for sprinkling on top. Next time I may add a touch more sugar to the batter but the sweetness from the crust was just enough.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used plant-based cream; these scones are easy, delicious and a new breakfast staple for us.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are absolutely wonderful. I made them vegan by substituting TJ’s Coconut Cream for the dairy. I refrigerated a can for 24 hours, then removed the cream on top (dug it out :). It weighed in at the same amount as recipe calls for (I’m sure cans vary slightly, but for me it was just the amount I needed). I popped the hardened cream in the microwave just until the coconut cream was barely melted, and incorporated into my recipe. I only baked a few scones and froze the rest - we have been loving them! A new favorite plant-based recipe! Note- the coconut flavor stayed in the background and was subtle…I assume the cardamom neutralized it somewhat. But we love coconut so it wouldn’t have mattered!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Cut up fresh blueberries if they are larger. Add in more berries - I used about 95 g and could have easily used 125 g. I topped with a powdered sugar + water + lemon juice glaze to add a bit of sweetness to the scone. They are not very sweet as is, but I think a glaze adds better flavor than dumping more sugar into the recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Easy, delicious and just follow the recipe and you will be rewarded with scones as good as any you’d buy at a bakery.  I chilled the dough overnight in a bowl. Worked fine. The dough is rather clumpy and not all blended together. I just dropped the dough into same size balls and it all worked out in the oven.  Thus is a keeper!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: A quick and easy treat. Used up some cream that had started to turn, chose to use the cardamom. Next time I’ll add a little more and maybe some lemon zest too.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
This scone recipe is simple to put together and it is delicious! I’m a long-time baker and this recipe is a keeper. 
Next time, however, I will added less sugar, cutting the amount by 1/3. I prefer less sweetness in my scone.
Another recommendation: freeze leftovers. Thaw by toasting 2x in a toaster. It’s a luxury to have scones in the morning with little effort!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These were awful. You can blame me for using gluten free flour if you want, but I have made the Cream Scones recipe from this site (Molly O’Neil) and they are great. This recipe calls for more flour and less cream than the Cream Scone recipe, resulting in a dry, crumbly mess than never fully incorporates. Waste of money and effort.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Points for easy, didn’t find the cardamom added much nuance - and it was freshly ground and from Burlap&Barrel. Next time, will use the 1/2 and 1/2 a few commenters have suggested and also the lemon zest.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It's blueberry season here. Can you use FRESH blueberries instead of frozen one?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Amy yes, definitely. Much better in my opinion than dehydrated ones.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Excellent and simple. I used frozen blueberries, rested 30 minutes, baked for 22 minutes. I can see that resting longer would improve them but enjoyed them thoroughly as is.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Anyone try a dairy free alternative?  These look amazing.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made mine with vitalife vegan sour cream.  Next batch will try Siggi’s nondairy yogurt.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Easy, yummy and customizable.  I made vegan cherry-almond scones with vitalife vegan sour cream and organic sugar, 6oz dried cherries, 1/2 tsp each vanilla and almond extracts. Chilled overnight in fridge. Shaped into 8-inch disc  and cut into 8 wedges. Added sliced almonds to topping.  Baked 30 minutes at 425F.   Can’t wait to make other flavor variations.  Yummy!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I followed this recipe exactly as written. I didn't have wild blueberries which are smaller so I sliced regular size berries into quarters. Super easy and tasty! Next time I might try with raspberries.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I love this recipe! It's so easy (what a joy not to have to deal with butter), and everyone in my house loves the scones. I use regular frozen blueberries (without thawing) and toss them in flour before adding to the recipe. I usually make a double batch and freeze most of them. They reheat nicely in the convection toaster oven at 250 F for about 12 min.

I plan to experiment by making a savory version with cheddar cheese and Old Bay.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Did anyone else experience a highly bitter, nearly inedible flavor from the cardamom?  I tasted the cardamom directly from the jar and my tastbuds did not reacte the same way.  Ugh, I am so frustrated.  This is a good recipe otherwise.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This recipe is absolutely amazing. Storing them in the freezer is a game-changer because I can make multiple batches at a time. I also just throw the frozen blueberries in and make sure to toss in the flour before adding the cream & sugar.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Great recipe and came out perfect with King Arthur GF AP flour 🎉

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Anyone have any ideas about whether a different berry could be used (raspberry, blackberry etc, fresh or frozen)? Dislike blueberries over here but this recipe sounds great! Thanks

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Dried blueberries work well also

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can this be made vegan?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Patelloyd
I’ve made them with coconut cream (not coconut milk). They came out great.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Used fresh blueberries. Made 12 instead of 10. Yes, serve while warm. Yummy and easy.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Could coconut cream be subbed for heavy cream to make recipe vegan?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Jamie I realize it’s been two years since you asked this question, but I tried it tonight and it worked! I did need to use slightly more coconut cream than I would have used heavy cream. A few other comparison notes:
- they are a bit denser than the dairy ones, in a stick-to-your-teeth way…
- but they also melt a bit more in the mouth.
- it was a bit difficult to get the dough to come together. I should have added the extra coconut cream earlier on instead of trying to mix and mix. I think I overmixed a bit.
- they are a little dry and would do well with a spread (plant based butter or cream cheese).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
There is a restaurant called the Sideboard in the East Bay of CA that always makes their blueberry scones gf. They’re very good! I’m not gf, and I happily eat them, so I think it’s worth trying to make them gf. 

That said, I suspect that making them dairy free might not work well — I think the dough will be too gummy…

If someone tries any of this out, please report back!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Used large cookie scoop- 13 scones
My oven only 10 minutes

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I love scones and I made these today and had with clotted cream and lemon curd—glorious is all I can say.  So different when made with just the cream I shall not go back to butter!  Will try with the currents next time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Fabulous!! I didn’t have heavy cream, so I used 1 cup of half & half and 1/4 cup sour cream mixed together with a teaspoon of cornstarch. I also tossed the blueberries in a tablespoon of powdered sugar.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Just pulled these out of the oven and they are wonderful and flaky.  The only sub I made was to use King Arthur Pastry flour because that is what I had at home.   I laugh at home many of us home bakers can't resist putting our own touch on recipes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Yes, MS, you can rest this dough in a bowl in the fridge. I use two spoons to portion out scones for baking. I have also left some dough 24 hours in the fridge before portioning and although a little weepy on the parchment papered pan, they baked up fine.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made them this weekend, and they were pretty good, though if I made them again, I might substitute that last quarter cup (or more) of cream with sour cream or buttermilk. And I would add 1/2 tsp of cinnamon to the cardamom. They were very easy!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Excellent and, unlike many baking recipes, not too sweet.  I refrigerated dough in a bowl overnight (so I could fit in fridge), then separated into individual scones and brushed with cream and sprinkled with sugar right before baking.  Worked fine.  

Definitely don't overwork dough.  Shaggy is best.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: How long do you refrigerate the dough before baking?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Kathy Howard the recipe says 30 minutes up to overnight.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made these with frozen strawberries but didn’t know to rinse and dry them, so the dough didn’t cook all the way through the first time, but the second heating they are AMAZING

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Needed more blueberries, and a little taste of the baking powder? I added just under 1/4 teaspoon of ground clove instead of cardamon.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
How much is a mounded forkful of dough?  I ended up weighing the finished dough and then dividing by the number of servings, creating a needless mess.  
As a cooking note, I added small amounts of cinnamon, cloves and cardamon, and a half-teaspoon of vanilla.  
Tasty and should have been easier.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Just blob it onto the pan, whatever size you want your scones to be. Could not be easier.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: When I made this, the mounds were about 3" in diameter and 2" high at the center.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Just made this recipe. Whether you call them scones or biscuits they are easy and delicious. The little bit of seas salt really brightens the flavor.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Consistently delicious. I've made it three times. I don't refrigerate the dough, and they come out just fine. Huge hit with my teenage son.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: In my scone recipe, I use frozen hole cranberries, unsweetened. I cut them in half, frozen, and toss them in with my batter, along with small bits of crystallized ginger.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can the uncooked dough be frozen after it is formed into scones to be enjoyed fresh-baked from the oven later?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used the fork to just barely get these to stick together, then I dumped everyone out on a silpat with a tiny bit of flour. Folded all the dough over about 3 times, then shaped into a generous rectangle and cut into triangles. Really excellent scones! I did add about 15g extra sugar to the dough and topped with Demerara sugar for crunch. 2/3c of regular frozen blueberries was not too many. Rested in the fridge covered in parchment about 10 hours. Baked for about 21 minutes at 6600ft.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I am making these for a second time easy and delicious I like recipes that don’t call for a lot of butter 
These didn’t last long in my house! I did substitute half and half they make me happy!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: So easy to make and they are excellent. I have tried various scone recipes and this one the easiest.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Easy to make but meh.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
On first bites, I could taste baking powder.  
They are very quick and tasty. 
I let blueberries air dry on paper towels.  This created a beautiful pattern.  I am drying towels to safe for some use.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Quick and easy but not really scones. These are more like blueberry drop biscuits.  Not complaining- they are tasty! But if you are wanting a real scone these don’t quite get you there.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious and very easy recipe. Thanks for sharing

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Or, split the difference between wild blueberries, and dried currents by using dried blueberries. If you are in a hurry, you can skip the refrigeration and they’re still just fine. Cook a little less because, of course, they don’t go into the oven cold.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Super easy to make.  I used half and half instead of heavy cream and they turned out really well.  More of a cake consistency

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: LOVE these scones but, NB, bake time of 18-20 minutes may yield overly dark result even if dough is thoroughly chilled prebake. Suggest vigilance at 15 minutes mark!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: beautifully converted to GF with ATK gf ap blend. I added an egg so the protein would help bind and 40 extra g. flour to accommodate the extra liquid. also used a very scant 1/2 tsp guar gum for a half batch (would probably use 3/4 tsp for a full batch) and used full amount of leavening for half batch to accommodate overnight rest in the fridge after shaping. also, i mixed very very well to fully hydrate flour b/c there's no risk of gluten development. 5 stars!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Love these but I do pat into a circle then cut wedges.  The crispy corners are the best part!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Has anyone tried full fat coconut cream instead of heavy cream?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Keeper. Very easy. I have high standards for scones but always looking for one that is one bowl and can be done without cutting butter as that takes time .  I had wondered how they may cook as I had inconsistent size but they turned out fine. 20 mins and they were great.  I added zest of a lemon. ready and prepped in 15 mins.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Yes- no... most baking pans won't fit in most fridges
 ( without some serious  re-arranging  the contents currently occupying on the shelves. 

I guess you could place them on 2 plates, 5 scones each,  and find space in the fridge for that, stacking the plates if necessary by placing a bowl over the first plate and then stacking plate 2 on top of the bowl that's covering  plate #1.

 Hold the pan for baking in the morniong.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Ran short on heavy cream and subbed a 1/4 whole milk, baked straight away as misstep… holy cow, still delicious

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Meh....after trying these I now will never make scones again without butter.   Not sure what I was thinking in the first place.   These were very tasteless.  I used the Cardamom too but I think it was to old to impart any flavor

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is the easiest scone recipe I've ever made and they turned out perfectly! The only substitution I made was lemon zest instead of cardamom which I love but didn't have on hand.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Patricia Noll  - I used Brodie XOXO self-raising flour (Canada) and this worked beautifully.  No metallic taste and super easy.  I made this two days in a row.  I let the dough rest in the bowl in the fridge. Thank you for this tip.  Game changing for me!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These turned out wonderful and the heavy cream was so easy! I was a little shy on flour so used cake flour to get the full amount which of course produced an extra delicate scone. I do wish I had doubled the amount of fresh blueberries, I saw another comment about that after the fact. Still delicious though!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
How do you get a "shaggy dough" I couldn't get all the dry ingredients  mixed in.
I probably overmixed

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Simple, easy to pull together and clear instructions. I've made this recipe 3 times and it stands up.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Rumford baking powder- none of that chemical taste ever. Any other and I can taste the difference never mind the self riding kind. But if it tastes good to you- all the better!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was SO easy! I was in a time crunch and didn't refridgerate the dough at all, and they still turned out perfectly. The were DELISH!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: i will absolutely be making these again!!! i followed the recipe exactly and they are so good! i saw a few comments saying they were dry, but mine were moist and almost cake-like in the middle. i can't wait to make these again and experiment with other fruits and flavors!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This might be the most forgiving recipe ever! The cat & dog were wrassling, so I was distracted while preparing & did most steps out of order. I only had 1 cup ½ & ½, which I used w/ ½ cup light sour cream (which I think is basically yogurt) & added 1 tsp of vanilla bean paste. Fresh berries, not wild frozen. The so-what of it: Didn't matter. These were WONDERFUL w/ only a slight adaption for a toaster oven, involving lowering heat to 400º and baking for 22 minutes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made them at 10 pm last night and baked them this morning at 8.  Tip:  if you are going to top with sugar after brushing with cream, wait until just before baking to do this so you can get the crackly top.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly.  Use a kitchen scale to match the weight suggestions.  They are not very sweet - I like them that way, but if you like them sweeter top with course sugar.  I like this recipe - very easy.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These were fine the next day or so by warming them for 20 seconds in the microwave.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These were amazing fresh out of the oven. Once they cooled, they still tasted great but were a bit dry. With such a high ratio of results to effort, this recipe is a keeper, especially for when we have enough eaters to devour all 8 scones right away.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I've used this particular recipe since the mid 90's. It is the best, light, not biscuity at all.  Easy to change the flavors for whatever mood you're in.  I also catered high teas & these were always well received.Ca

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
My understanding is that scones have eggs and biscuits have NO eggs.  Whichever the golden rule, these are light airy "things" that are not as satifying as typical cut in butter scones.  And honestly, they take longer to make.  Go to Joy of Cooking, and just use the 100 year old recipe, for thick dense rich heavenly scones.  But if you want light fluffy things,  make these.
Different strokes for different blueberry freaks.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I'm sure this is a great recipe and the scones are still delicious. I glanced at the top it said 25 minutes cooking time - this is misleading as in the text it says 18-20 minutes - my scones are a bit dark now. Next time I will adjust... Served with Lemon curd.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this recipe exactly as written. LOVED it! Made it twice more by popular demand of my husband. Added mini- chocolate chips to the blueberries...delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It is so crumbly that half of it isn’t being worked in appropriately, should I add more heavy cream in? How do I get the rest of the flour mixture to fully combine without an abundance of flour in the bottom on a bowl?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The trick is to use a silicone spatula about halfway through the mixing with the fork. Bring the flour up from the bottom of the bowl gently in short strokes (as if you were going to fold it in), going all the way around the bowl, then finish mixing it in with  the fork. And keep the spatula handy to scrape off the fork when plopping the sticky dough onto the baking sheet.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Does anyone have an opinion on whether or not some portion of the all purpose flour can be substituted with almond flour?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I've used hazelnut flour for part of required flour in a similar scone recipe. I don't think that I'd sub in more than 1/2 C of almond flour (I'd start at 1/2 C), as it doesn't work like regular flour.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@Dee
I have often substituted almond flour for part of the flour in other recipes. Because almond flour does not absorb any liquid, has some fat, and is sturdier, you usually need to make adjustments to the other ingredients. My guess here is to reduce the cream (guided by how the dough comes together). What a great idea, even if the result is not exactly a “cream scone”, it will probably be delicious anyway.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can the blueberries be replaced with chocolate chips, I assume mini chips would be best?  And what other changes would be necessary?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This looks so easy! I love Joanne Chang's maple-blueberry scones (in the NYT Cooking collection). Can I substitute part of the cream in this recipe with maple syrup?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can you use whole wheat flour?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: can you add blueberry flavoring?  If so how much?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: can you use fresh blueberries?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Can I use raspberries instead of blueberries?
Thanks.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I'm sure you can use any berries you'd like, but raspberries may bleed more into the dough than frozen blueberries. Two other things: the recipe use frozen wild blueberries, which are small. That tells me that larger berries may make the scones fall apart more easily. And depending on the berries, they may add a little more moisture to the dough, so you might want to cut down on the amount.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @BrooklynDF. I used larger frozen berries and scones baked up perfectly. Since it takes fewer large berries to fill the measuring cup, next time (and there will definitely be a next time) I will add more, probably double the amount.  I did rinse and dry them per the tip in the recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can sour cream be subbed for the heavy cream?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Easy assembly, but my dough was not ‘shaggy’ so I patted it out into circle and cut into 8 wedges, separated those and now it’s resting in fridge!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Without butter it seems more like a crumbly like cup cake that disintegrates to the touch.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I agree that the hardest part of this recipe is finding room in the fridge!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious! A new family favorite and really easy to bake.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are similar to the scones I make but I use cold butter, (about 6 TBSP to 2 cups flour), not cream, which is cut in with a pastry blender. I think the butter keeps the dough from becoming too dense.  I usually do not add fruit, but rather, put preserves on the hot scones before eating.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Not having to cut in butter is the whole point of this recipe. It's a delight and makes the mixing so much faster and easier. And these are delicious. I don't find them dense.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @CJ I prefer a proper English cream scone over an American butter scone, not only for the ridiculous simplicity but because I find them lighter and more tender. The key is not to over-mix the dough and give them some time in the fridge (or freezer if you want to bake them soon after mixing). Totally worth keeping heavy cream around the house.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Any suggestions for making this sugar free for a diabetic diet?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Just leave out the sugar. Cream biscuits, without sugar, are a known tradition.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I doubled the recipe and one pint of cream left me a little short. I took out 1/4 cup of the flour mix and ended up drizzling melted butter on top of each before chilling. The dough wasn’t sticking together much but after the fridge chill it all came together! We loved these!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made these scones quickly, they were fabulous. I used wild blueberries in season which was extra amazing.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Haven’t made these yet but such a scone fan, I want to try. My small dilemma is living alone, how many scones can one person consume?  Do these freeze well enough?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I had an issue with the bottoms of the scones scorching badly. I chilled overnight and then brushed on (over-abundant?) cream. Perhaps this was an error. 
The scorching was even across the bottoms though, not more under the edges where it would soak in foremost.
So, a word of caution about too much cream/ application at the end of chilling. I will post back if I retrial! 
And I may consider using a tray as a partial heat shield on the lower rack. 
Would love to hear from anyone familiar with this issue!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I didn't have full cream, so I blended a little over 1/4 c cubed softened unsalted butter with milk to get 1 1/4 C (I used oat).  The softened butter/milk combo whipped right into 1/2 of the flour mixture, then I quickly mixed the rest in, then added blues.  With the frozen blueberries, everything got cold after mixing so I scooped the scones and let them rest in the fridge while the oven was preheating.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Substitute self rising flour for flour, baking powder and salt. If you want to avoid having a partial container of heavy cream hanging around in your refrigerator and you’d like to have  more than 10 biscuits, increase the flour to a scant 3 cups and the cream to 2 cups.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I can never make recipes which require you to put a whole baking sheet into a refrigerator. They do not fit going in sideways and for sure don't fit going in short end first. Frustrating.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I have switched up the refrigerator ‘categories’ as set up by the manufacturer. My crispers are now mid-height since the veggies semi-froze when they were at the original bottom ‘category’. At the bottom is where I store my eggs. THIS is where I place my baking sheets. Normally the eggs are 3 cartons high. To chill a baking sheet I change it to 2 cartons high and rest the baking sheet upon the eggs. If you are able, give this a try. I hope it is helpful.
