That craggy dough has a specific outcome that isn't good for me but we rated based on the scones connoisseur in our group.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I make these often, always with GF flour.  I use the zest of one lemon that I rub into the sugar to release the oil.  When doing this I forego the cardamom.  I also add two teaspoons of lemon extract, and typically use fresh blueberries.  I also discovered the other day, that placing the ingredients in a wide, ceramic mixing bowl allowed the dough to form more easily and thus quickly. I too struggle with pans in my refrigerator but it’s worth the challenge!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Added vanilla extract. Would benefit from lemon zest as well. As with all cream drop scones, low in flavour, but very tender. Should be eaten warm with salted butter. Turned out great even without resting in fridge.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These were lovely. I added loads more blueberries than the recipe called for (like, a lot more—close to a pint-because it’s berry season and I had them). Also, I was lazy and impatient the day I made these so I rest or chill them; they went straight into the oven. And? They were beautiful all the same. Tender and delicious and bursting with blueberries. I can only imagine how good they’d be if i made them properly.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I disagree with step 6.  I thought the scones tasted better the day after.  I wish I had not frozen them all, as suggested. (Although, it has kept me from eating them too quickly!) Made as directed but used homegrown blueberries and some blackberries, as I didn't have enough of the blueberries.  Can't wait to experiment with some other fruit and spices!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made these as a postpartum middle of the night breastfeeding snack for my wife. Used bittersweet instead of semisweet chunks since that's what I had on hand and it turned out great - less overwhelmingly sweet but still really rich chocolatey flavor. Impressed even my Asian parents and in-laws visiting - they said "not too sweet!" with a smile which is among the highest of compliments from them.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Derek, I just love every part of your comment.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made these today…VERY good. Was nervous about filling the muffin cups all the way, but these have a beautiful rise and gorgeous domed top, so no need to worry. For portioning, it’s a large cookie scoop of batter, then did 9 chocolate chunks before baking, 5-6 chocolate chunks after (mix of Aldi specially selected dark and semisweet chunks). Chocolate goo in the middle is a delicious extra yum. Next time I will put more filling in them because I had some left over. Five stars and a gold medal.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My husband dislikes coffee in his chocolate desserts (he’s a chocolate purist no nuts no berries no….). Can I just use water? Or maybe mix a little extra cocoa/sugar in a cup of water and use that??

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: You'll want to replace the coffee with something acidic, since it's not just adding flavor but also adding acid to react with the baking soda, especially since the recipe calls for Dutch process cocoa--processed with alkali to reduce its acidity. A tablespoon of neutral vinegar in a cup of water would do the trick, if you don't want to add extra flavor (as, e.g., red wine would). No need for extra sugar!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Ann while not as acidic as coffee, I subbed in equal parts earl gray and the 1/2 cup of white sugar for 2tbsp of raspberry jam and they came out perfect.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I am, sadly, allergic to coffee.  Any ideas for a substitute?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: You could probably use the same amount of water in place of the coffee. Just replacing one liquid with another. I don't think it will be a major change to the flavor.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If you don't want to try the water or wine recommendations, Stash tea makes a chocolate hazelnut herbal tea that might be a good substitute.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Cindy I subbed in for equal parts earl gray and it was delicious. I also subbed the 1/2 cup of white sugar for 2tbsp if raspberry jam and threw in some fresh raspberries too. Unbelievably moist and delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These muffins were so easy, with big flavor and delicious payoff! They are not cupcake-y at all. I did without the extra chocolate chunks on top and they still tasted just sweet enough.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These were amazing. I filled them to almost the top, and they didn't overflow like a lot of muffins do. They just made a nice domed top. Next time I think I'll try mixing the first 3/4 cup of chunks into the batter because mine didn't all sink, or maybe I'll put them on top but push them down a little. Otherwise when you add the chunks to the top after baking it feels like the top of the muffin is loaded and there aren't many chunks inside the muffin.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Any suggestions on something to substitute for the coffee? We are tea drinkers :)

commentBody: Delicious! Super chocolatey but not sickly at all.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The recipe indicates 1/3 cup (56 grams) cocoa powder. The actual equivalent is very close to 2/3 cup for 56 grams. Resulting muffins were delicious.

Despite a non-stick muffin pan and greasing the wells, my muffins stuck to the pan and could only be extracted in pieces.  Nonetheless very delicious . Made a second batch the next day with the requisite muffin liners and they came out easily. I also added a teaspoon of vanilla, but otherwise followed the recipe.  All my guests raved!

author: Genevieve Ko
Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Even better the next day. Deliciously moist crumb with a rich deep chocolate flavour with very subtle coffee undertones that really enhance the 'chocolateyness'. I added a ganache filling (not the one in the recipe) and it just took them to the next level. Very easy method to follow. A must bake.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: For the non-coffee people (unless you’re allergic): try the recipe at least once with the coffee, as it intensifies the chocolate flavour and doesn’t end up tasting like coffee.  My family’s fave chocolate cake uses coffee instead of water, and even my son who can’t stand coffee loves it. I also use coffee instead of water for boxed brownie mixes, and it makes a huge difference in chocolatey-ness.

author: Genevieve Ko

commentBody: Made these this morning and I was shocked by how good they were.  Along the way I had doubts about the methodology, specifically the flour, sugar, salt, and leavening agents going together before combining with the butter, coffee, and cocoa powder.  The batter was so thin, I had a yield of 16 standard-sized muffins.  All of this made me doubt I would end up with anything decent but, I was wrong.  The rise was perfect and eaten warm they were incredible, rich, and decadent.

commentBody: I am an avid coffee drinker, but sometimes baked goods develop bitter notes or an aftertaste with coffee. So I often substitute equal amounts of strongly brewed Assam black tea in recipes that call for brewed coffee. Assam tea also compliments the chocolate flavor.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was wonderful! I made it as written, except I didn’t have heavy cream, so used heavy whipping cream for the filling. I would not make the filling again, as the muffins were great without it ( I filled some & left others without); I felt the filling was too bitter ( I’m not a big super dark chocolate fan). I could not even taste the coffee. I followed the advice here and put some of the chocolate chunks directly in the bottom, and am glad because my chunks also did not sink. Will make again!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Enjoyed and approved by the Horsley Herd! My Dad said it may be the best thing he's ever tasted. Happiness!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Fabulous recipe, every single person that tried it was in awe over how delicious they were. Deep chocolate flavor and not too sweet, I would 100% make these again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I read someone is allergic to coffee and an excellent substitute is a strong chicory tea. 
Don't buy in the form of a blend (Teccino brand, for ex, is a no-go), get it straight, and make it strong, and it is a delicious substitution. 
I haven't tried substituting with Dandy Blend (another herbal coffee "sub", it's an instant drink powder) but that might be good, too. Some people are concerned about the presence of metals taken up by dandelions. 
Likely u can find at a Co-op or Natural Grocers

commentBody: Is the cocoa power sweet or unsweetened?

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko

I followed the recipe to a T, and they completely spread outside of the muffin tin, and between the melty chocolate chunks on top and the sauce, we basically had to spoon these onto our plates and into our mouths. They were very yummy, but overall I’m not sure I’d put the time or effort into the mess again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: do you have advice on how to adjust recipe for altitude about 8000'?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made these a couple of days ago, decided not to fill them.  Was expecting a heavy chocolate muffin,, instead they were amazing light and chocolatety.  The coffee added a mocha vibe.  Loved them, would definitely make again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I loved this recipe- so rich and delightful, the muffin was soft and moist while the crust had a nice crunch. I wouldn’t have added the chocolate chunks on top if I made it again, it made it a little too rich.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I really don't think this recipe is worth the mess. Just eat a bunch of chocolate.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Very fudgy and moist! It may as well been a brownie in muffin form. Delicious! Molten middle filling NOT optional :) well worth the effort

I used warmed cold brew and whisked the butter in. 

Highly recommend using a scale to measure out the cocoa powder.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Using higher quality ingredients in a simple recipe like this really makes it shine. I’ve used Droste Dutch cocoa powder ever since I saw it win taste tests on America’s test kitchen, and the chocolate flavor is so intense and smooth. Out of this world! I took these to work, without the filling, and everyone was raving about them. Going to try today with the chocolate filling on some, and pistachios cream on others

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I literally won a gold medal for "cooking" at an Olympics part in part for these muffins. Only note I have is make the ganache before baking the muffins - it needs a long time to cool and for me became a sort of frosting since it was still very liquid. The good news is, when I explained my mistake to people eating them they told me to shut up because I was interrupting their enjoyment of a top-notch chocolate experience.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made with ¾ of sugar amount for both brown & white resulting in a not too sweet verdict from the Asian family. Next time would not make the filling and would add all chocolate chunks into the batter

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I halved the amount of sugar that went into the filling- it really balanced the sweetness of the muffin!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I made them as written and I was halfway through the process I realized just how much chocolate there was in there! It was too late to turn back and so I committed to using the quantities stated and added the optional ganache filling because the chocolate train had left the station and we weren't turning back!

As written these were very good and pleasantly balanced and not too sweet! I would call these a lava cake rather than a cupcake or a muffin.  Would I make them again? Probably!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The chocolate chunks stayed on the bottom and stuck to the cupcake liner the first time I made them. The second time, I shook them in a little bit of flour and mixed them into the batter and they stayed suspended in the batter a bit. It was much better that way.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious! Didn’t have a piping bag so trying to fill them was tricky. Making them again today armed with the right tools. Rich, moist, chocolaty goodness!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: For those who don't like coffee but do like tea, use lapsang souchong leaves in a spice grinder.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These didn’t give me the intense chocolate flavor I wanted. Good, but not worth the hype in my opinion. If you’re a deep chocolate fiend (flourless chocolate cake, dark chocolate brownies, etc), these probably won’t deliver for you.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: great both with or without the chocolate filling

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Would recommend adding the chocolate chunks to the batter for a more uniform distribution

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My picky son declared them “perfect”! Made the recipe as is, perfect for chocolate lovers, will surely make frequently.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: well, this didn’t turn out as i hoped by my own error! basically, i advise against using a ziploc to pipe in the filling unless you carve out a hole first. i don’t have a piping bag but maybe it’s finally time. also, my chocolate chunks on top just melted and slid right off so i clearly didn’t do that quite right either- i think mini chocolate chips would work much better! taste was still great

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These muffins are DIVINE! I ate three immediately. My friend proceeded to eat two back to back. They’re amazing. They will be added to my rotation immediately!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @SAJ09 update: I made a double batch two days later because I ate all the ones I intended to share. They’re amazing! I didn’t make the ganache. They’re plenty chocolatey as is. The second time I added a little bit more flour to the batter and I mixed the chopped chocolate that went into the batter with flour the first time the chocolate sank to the bottom. This time it was more dispersed. Both times were decadent. I HIGHLY recommend making them and making a double batch because they will go fast.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It’s chintzy for them not to share that recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Way, way, way too sweet.  I’m not an expert baker bay any means, but these tasted more sweet than chocolatey to me.  I only had semi sweet chocolate on hand.  Would not make again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this with GF King Arthur flour, mixed in 1 cup of the chips bc I read comments they didn’t sink. It was the right move, the texture was good and chips distributed throughout! Overall these were good if you really really love chocolate, but I found the cake itself a bit bitter tasting, maybe I should have used better cocoa.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These were super tasty! But, they looked miserable. The top was flat, stuck to the baking tin, and lost their tops when I flipped them out. I read through the comments before starting to make sure I got all the good tips. Alas. Despite the recipe calling to fill the cups to the top, I would like to try the recipe again with filling only 3/4 full and greasing the top of the muffin tin. Despite how terribly they look (which is too bad because they're for a party tomorrow), they're YUM!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Followed recipe to a tee. They came out perfectly. In the baking Olympics, these merit a Gold.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I prepared these muffins for a winter 2026 Olympic watch party — definitely worth of a gold medal. They looked opulent and decadent with the chocolate (and Kit Kat) chunk topping. Everyone loved the slight crisp to the puffy muffin tops. 

In order to serve them warm, I made the batter earlier in the day and kept it in refrigerated until 30 minutes before serving. I also put all the ingredients for the filling (except the heavy cream) in a sauce pan on the stovetop so all I had to do was heat it up while the muffins were baking. By the time it was melted and I had transferred it into a bag for piping the muffins were cool enough to fill and serve.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: To add filling to any cupcake or muffin, use a serrated grapefruit knife to cut off the top of the cake. Insert the knife at an angle and go around, so you end up with a cone-shaped piece of cake. Lift that off and scoop out a larger "well" depending on the amount of filling you want to simply spoon into it. Replace the top; it will sit off the cake/muffin a bit but I've been using this method for years and it has worked great, especially for shipping "cake with frosting" without any mess!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: We were out of oatmeal so I made these. I regret nothing. They are spectacular. I highly recommend the chocolate filling--it's totally over the top, but you're not going to make these every day, and you'll feel like an Olympian after eating one. Genevieve Ko gets the gold!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Is the nonstick spray required? I hate using it and never have it in my kitchen.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I've never seen cooking spray in the UK, and (wrongfully*) assumed any spray oil will do (e.g. sunflower oil, airfryer oil - sunflower and avocado oils). I have also used "loose" sunflower oil/vegetable oil (rapeseed oil) and a tissue to wipe the it around (or a pastry brush with large cake tins). *US spray contains stuff + oil. As I say 100% oil for frying (not EVOO) works for greasing tins.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’m making these today carefully checking comments. I’m not sure about making the filling is it really necessary? I haven’t read any feedback on it, yay or no?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I am sure they are delicious. However, muffins are made by adding liquid ingredients to the dry (making a well in the dry).  The method here is to make a batter.  Having said that, yummm.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: They were fine. Chocolate on chocolate. I prefer a contrast. I recommend adding some sea salt on top after it’s baked.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Also can add a tablespoon of Instant Coffee to diffuse yet not eliminate the Chocolate but give it a flavor variety from the absolute Chocolate, and drop the sugar in half. Substitute a mashed Banana for the sugar and can eliminate most of the already too much sugar. Get the best Cocoa you can afford. Experiment with ingredients always.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Very good. The filling is very loose and leaks a bit. I would let it cool down before piping.

commentBody: I now understand why there is a need for chocolate chunks, and that is that chips will not sink to the bottom. Not that I'm complaining.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Anyone have a suggestion for internal temperature when checking whether the muffins are done? I always prefer temperature checks to the more subjective toothpick method. Most charts recommend 200 degrees F or 93 degrees C for muffins or quick breads in general, but I wonder whether that would be too dry for these muffins.

commentBody: I am making them now (just checked them in oven)...I think about 1/4 of the chunks sank. They didn't sink at all when the batter was raw but I figured maybe it'd happen in the oven. But not really. I'm not really going to fuss w/ adding too many chips on top bc I'm adding the filling. @genevieve any tips for this? maybe put them in the bottom or middle instead of on top?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Instead of piping in a filling - you could use chilled ganache (equal parts heavy cream and bitter or semi sweet chocolate) when filling the cupcakes.
If you serve them warm, it'll be similar to a lava cake. 
A restaurant I used to work at did this with chocolate bread pudding.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Loved these!  Very easy to make and turned out delicious.  Was worried they may have a strong coffee flavor but they don’t.  Used a Pike Place Kcup for the coffee and Guittard dark chocolate chips with Droste cocoa powder.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Used dark brown sugar instead of light; and instead of half the cocoa powder just in the muffins, a cocoa protein powder (by Mikuna Foods). Turned out great!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are delicious and SO easy, but (and this is not a criticism!) they are, in no sense, muffins. These are cupcakes. Whether you put the ganache inside and call it filling or put it on top and call it icing (which is what I will next time because I am not fussy), they are cupcakes. Before I started this recipe, I was expecting to wind up with a stiff batter that would yield a standard "crumbly" muffin texture. When the batter was as thin as my favorite chocolate cake batter, I knew: cupcakes!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Re: coffee: interestingly, the YouTuber Jordan Howlett posted this recipe recently as well and his version uses 3/4 cup milk and 1/4 cup water with 2 tsp instant coffee. Probably better for those who don’t care for the coffee flavor since you can just omit. 

Interestingly that version also seems thicker and doesn’t have issues with the chunks sinking. It also suggests not over-mixing the batter and using a spatula rather than a whisk.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can you double this recipe?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These were delicious!! Absolutely scrumptious when they come out of the oven. Sadly I will never be an Olympian, so this recreation will have to do!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
These are absolutely unreal. The cocoa powder I had on hand was double Dutch black cocoa so these turned out dark and delicious. 
A note for anyone concerned about the taste of the coffee- you don’t taste the coffee! It just brings out the flavor of chocolate in baking and provides the perfect liquid to bloom the cocoa powder.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Very tasty! My one gripe is that most of the chocolate chunks fell off the muffins. You either really need to use tulip muffin papers, or wait a bit until the muffins are slightly cooled.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I didn’t have a lot of sugar so I subbed the rest with honey….. because of the extra liquid I compensated by using instant coffee granules. I ended up softening the butter to compensate for not having regular cofffee to melt it with. This batter ended up pretty stiff but the muffins turned out great!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Super yummy muffins! Just keep an eye on them, for smaller tray don’t bake as long (~10 min).

commentBody: I filled to the top however mine overflowed . Still delicious but not as pretty as I had hoped . I’m baking at 7800’. Any suggestions for high altitude modifications?

commentBody: A solid chocolate muffin though I would only make again if asked. Easy enough to make! I imagine these would be great for a gluten free sub as they’re moist enough to minimize crumbliness of the 1 to 1 gf mix.

Half chocolate on top

commentBody: Coffee adds depth and flavour, however it is important to note that the finished product will be less sweet and will have that bitterness. So I would recommend keeping sugar levels as they are in the filling but a couple tablespoons more in the batter wouldn’t hurt. Overall, it’s a great muffin, but it needs some changes. I would have wanted to taste more chocolate which I found was lacking even though it’s pretty much a triple chocolate muffin.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My tweak the next time I make them: push the first round of chocolate down into the muffin cup. My chocolate chunks never sank and then there was no room on top of the muffin to add more after they baked.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Has anyone tried replacing the butter with oil? Would this be ok?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I wanted to make chocolate muffins but was running low on sugar and didn’t want to go back to the store. I had bananas that were going off so I replaced some of the sugar and oil with them- something I often do with muffin and quick bread recipes. I do NOT recommend this. The coffee and banana do not play well together. It wasn’t terrible- we still ate them all- but I would not do it again. Will try the recipe another time as written.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Really delicious,  minus the filling and halved the chocolate chunks.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My 10 year old made this awsome!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Absolutely divine. Didn't add the ganache filling. Invest in quality chocolate- used two varieties of semi-sweet chips/discs and they were fabulous. Decaf coffee worked well, too.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: You will definitely need more than 5 minutes prep time! Make sure the coffee is hot and your butter is room temp to insure easy smooth mixing.  Also, my chocolate chunks did not sink, so next time I might add half to the batter and place the rest on top pre-baking. Oozing ganache is a nice touch! Gold medal-worthy!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are delicious. Very decadent and delicious. Hits the spot for a sweet tooth without being overwhelming.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Very tasty! I didn’t put them in the paper muffin cups, an absolute must!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are very moist and delicate but definitely a dessert. They were plenty sweet even with 56 grams (2/3 cup) cocoa powder. Even as a dessert, they are rich with either the chocolate chips or filling. I wouldn't add both again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made these for my chocolate and mint loving nephew and he sprinkled chopped andes mints on top of his. I used hot pero in place of the coffee - worked great. My whole family sat at the island and inhaled these…we had chocolate crumbs and ganache all over our faces. It was great fun:) these are killer!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: As a Vietnamese person I want to reassure everyone in the comments that pandan paste is welcome but certainly not mandatory. If you image search bánh bò you'll see that if we don't have pandan paste/extract we'll just use food coloring. We eat bánh bò primarily for the springy, chewy, fun texture; it's not as good on the 2nd or 3rd day, and it changes for the worse when refrigerated, so I'd worry more about finding enough people to eat this cake on the day it's made than finding pandan paste :)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: As a Viet person I just want to reassure others in the comments that for bánh bò the pandan flavoring is welcome but not essential. If you image search bánh bò you'll see that if we don't have pandan paste/extract on hand we simply substitute food coloring. We eat bánh bò primarily for the springy, chewy texture; left 2-3 days it becomes less pleasant, and refrigerating it changes the texture for the worse as well. I'd worry more about finishing it the day it's made than finding pandan paste :)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Followed the recipe.  My wife is Vietnamese and I know this is supposed to be a hard cake to make.  It came out great and all my in laws were shocked at me for trying and succeeding.  Thanks!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I found a video of her making the cake, and she uses the brand Butterfly, available on Amazon. Sadly, the list of ingredients include no pandan. Instead, it includes artificial flavors and food dye. Same for brands like McCormick and Koepoe Koepoe. I found one pure extract - Bionutricia. Reviewers on Amazon are mixed about the flavor, with many saying it's too light to notice. Perhaps what we think is pandan flavor is artificial since the more common brands are probably used in these desserts.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: When I saw that commercial pandan paste was essentially sweet food coloring, I decided to make my own. I got 100% pandan powder online and used organic vanilla paste and organic agave syrup and mixed to a paste like consistency. The color was a darker green but the taste was great. I would agree with another users suggestion to use matcha powder and vanilla. It’s a close enough replacement

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
In step 3 one is instructed to whisk clockwise. Does that hold for both hemispheres?
Do left-handed people have to obey?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: As for others on here, my cake puffed up enormously only to collapse immediately after being removed from the oven into what looked like a puddle of melted Grinch fur. I followed all the instructions, did all the sifting. What am I doing wrong?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@carlota
I had the exact same experience. Grinch fur is a very accurate description. It's cooling now and I haven't tried it yet so am hoping it might taste ok. Made if for my husband for Father's Day! Maybe we could think of this cake as a metaphor for raising kids. Things don't always go as expected even when you follow all the rules!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
This endless sieving back and forth, tip toeing in case we create air bubbles, hushed tones fretting over pandan, the guilt of food coloring ...  like there's not enough to worry about these days. 
Can't we just crank the music up and make brownies?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This did not work at all. The cake fell and the interior, while tasty, was dense and gelatinous. I followed the recipe exactly, taking care not to have too many bubbles and used the pandan paste from Amazon. Bummed.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Kathy SAME ! Does anyone know what could have happened ?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Grey Made a similar cake from NYT today and had the exact same experience. I also would like to know what caused it. It was very cold in my kitchen—maybe I didn’t bake it long enough and the cold caused it to sink when out of the oven

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Kathy A lot of other recipes for this cake have you either let the cake cool in the oven with the door cracked open (for about 10 minutes), or cool the cake on a rack upside down (like an Angel Food cake).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Just made this. Color is not at all the same. More like a pale matcha color. It was a bear getting it out of my buttered bundt pan. I've never worked so hard to get something out in one piece.

It tastes good. It's weird in a good way. I would try it one more time. More pandan -- which I got at the Asian grocery store. If they have pandan in Boise, you can probably get it anywhere.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I found the ingredients at an Asian market but the Pandan I found was liquid in a little vial. I added scrapings of one vanilla bean. I goofed somewhere. The cake ballooned out of the Bundt pan while baking then quickly deflated once cooling, to halfway down the pan. My result had little taste and texture was kind of like a soft gummy bear. Well I tried!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I tried to make this cake to impress my Vietnamese mother in law. First time it was a flop. Second time I made the following adjustments and it was a success! 

1) Eggs at room temp and used a rubber spatula to push them through the sieve instead of whisk
2) Baked at 350 for 45 min
3) More baking powder - I used about 2 3/4 tsp. 
4) Slightly less sugar - 1 1/4 cups
5) Mixed coconut milk, sugar, and salt in a sauce pan over the stove until sugar dissolved, then cooled it and added to sieved eggs

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Maybe I did something wrong in this incredibly complicated recipe.  I ordered all the recommended Asian ingredients and followed all the steps, including the endless sieving, but the results were underwhelming.  The cake is beautiful, but had very little taste.  It wasn’t at all sweet, but very chewy, an interesting texture.  I certainly won’t go to all this trouble again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used coconut creme instead of full fat coconut milk. It turned out wonderfully. And on day two after the crunchy, chewy textures shifted I toasted slices and added butter. Yumm!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Wow! I had put this on my list of things to try to make after having it for the first time at a Vietnamese restaurant, and then grew intimidated by the internet saying it’s notoriously difficult to make.

Well, we followed instructions to a t (with this additional tip: whisk IN the strainer, IN the batter to minimize air bubbles even more), and at *altitude*, and it still turned out perfectly!!Everyone enjoyed this, picky eaters included. Someone who had never had it before said it was like a perfect cookie: crisp on the outside and chewy on the inside. We finished it in a day. Best part is—dare I say it—it was pretty easy (gasp).

I used pandan extract instead of paste!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I followed this recipe to the T and sadly did not work. The whole thing collapsed despite of having veryyyy minimal air bubbles. And 6 eggs wasted!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I halved the recipe using the Butterfly pandam paste on Amazon (all I could find).  Half the recipe fits perfectly into a mini carved Bundt pan.  Baked at 320 for 30 min, then another 30 min uncovered.  As I was taking it out of the oven the pan slipped out of my hand and landed upside down. The cake popped right out and landed right side up! Totally intact.  Problem solved. The pattern is brown with green indentations and very beautiful. Can't wait to cut into it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Collapsed into a gluggy mess on cooling. Was VERY difficult to get out of the pan. Like eating dough. I don’t understand how this could go so wrong!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this cake from the recipe directions and it was a flop literally.  The cake rose in the pan, which was a high sided souffle pan.  As soon as I took it out of the oven it fell and kept on falling until it was 1/3 of its original height.  The consistency of the cake I can only describe as like mochi.  Dense and thick.  Flavor was very eggy.  It was green since I used concentrated pandan flavoring.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: SAME! The sides baked first and puffed up out over the edge, while the center sunk into a globby, eggy mess. I have no idea what caused this!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Genevieve Ko (or any cookbook author) needs to share the source of uncommon ingredients.  This is where a hyper link to the ingredient would be worth while.  The customer (reader) gets the right ingredient and the NYT earns  a commission for providing the convenience. Win, Win.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I finally made this cake, following the directions exactly and using the ingredients suggested. It turned out pretty well - although mine is bright green on the outside without the brown crust shown. It belongs in the pantheon of international cakes, although on the second tier below Chocoflan, Black Forest Cake and Zuppa Inglese. It was also easy to make, but a bit labor-intensive with the all the sieving and getting the lumps out. It's also gluten free, and pareve (with vegan butter for pan)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I made the pandan paste from scratch because I could only find frozen pandan leaves in the Asian market I visited.  The cake didn’t turn out as vivid green as the photo, but not surprising since the ready-made extracts usually contain food dyes.
I made the cake twice 1 week apart with all ingredients halved for my smaller bundt pan.  The fresh pandan paste only last a week, and I didn’t want to waste it.  I made it with different tweaks.

I made the first one per Cece’s comment with more baking powder and baked it at 350 degrees for 45 min.  I lowered the temperature by just a little for the second half of the baking.  The crust was a little more browned than I liked, but the texture of the cake looks similar to the photo, and very springy.

The second time around, same amount of ingredients baked at 325 per the instructions for both halves of the baking.  The crumb is finer with spots that appear unleavened, but springy nonetheless.

I think the baking powder needs to be activated at higher temperature to produce larger crumbs.  More time may be needed for the second half of baking at 325 until the toothpick comes out clean.

I cooled the cake in the pan upside down both times to prevent the cake from collapsing.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I just made this and used matcha instead of pandan because that’s what I had on hand. Like others although I was careful to not make any bubbles but still, it puffed up in the oven Expecially around the sides and then deflated. Not mad about that because texture and flavor is still good, although mine was more like a block of mochi rather than having the air pockets shown in the video. I actually enjoyed the parts that had puffed up as they were crispy and on the inside I still got a  bouncy texture. I like it, but I can get a similar effect with a regular mochi cake so will not be making this again due to how much time this took. I’m sure it was a personal error and nothing about the recipe. I’m happy I tried and pretty pleased with the result even though it didn’t turn out as I expected.

author: Genevieve Ko

commentBody: Grains cook up and store well, so make a big batch, say one cup dry, and use it for several days. Salad can also go 2-3 days, ditto black beans. The point is that the labor can be expended once for multiple days of lunches, making it easier.
