recipeTitle: Grain Bowl With Sardines and Sauce Moyo Recipe
commentBody: Great flavor to effort ratio! I substituted quinoa for fonio and poblano for green bell pepper, but otherwise followed the recipe and it was a great dinner for one. I dumped almost all of the olive oil from the sardines tin in which kept it from getting dry. Generous with salt and pepper. I’ll make this again and again with different substitutions!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Grain Bowl With Sardines and Sauce Moyo Recipe
commentBody:
Spice wimp—used half jalapeño which was delicious
Didn’t have peppers but would make again with red/orange/yellow for color and my preferred flavors
Subbed farro which is a pantry staple for us
After eating 3/4 of my sardines I added cilantro—maybe overpowered the sardines a bit but if you are a sardine newbie like me maybe that’s not a bad thing

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Grain Bowl With Sardines and Sauce Moyo Recipe
commentBody: This was great!!! Makes for a delicious lunch. For the fonio, I cooked it in broth for a little extra flavor, but otherwise made this as written. 10/10!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Grain Bowl With Sardines and Sauce Moyo Recipe
commentBody: I thought this was delicious!  I used brown rice added some croutons, for the crunch.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Grain Bowl With Sardines and Sauce Moyo Recipe
commentBody:
Used Yolelé fonio that I found at my local health food store. Doubled the recipe to have for leftovers.  Served it with marinated seared tempeh instead of fish (vegan family). It was a total winner for dinner!

Definitely season to taste. We love lots of acidity in our grain salads. 

In the future, I’d cook the fonio with a little more olive oil and some ground coriander. I’d also add some green onions and cilantro for more of an herby kick. I probably wouldn’t add more olive oil if eating with canned sardines, though. 

I’d love to see more fonio recipes. The nutty toasty aroma is so lovely.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Grain Bowl With Sardines and Sauce Moyo Recipe
commentBody: Now we know what fonio is, but what is moyo?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Grain Bowl With Sardines and Sauce Moyo Recipe
commentBody: Just look up the recipe for sauce Moyo on this app. It tells you exactly what it is

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Grain Bowl With Sardines and Sauce Moyo Recipe
commentBody: Folio is not widely available, so recommend using available grains.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Grain Bowl With Sardines and Sauce Moyo Recipe
commentBody: I subbed fonio for quinoa and added some chili garlic sauce to taste, as I didn’t have any peppers. Delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Grain Bowl With Sardines and Sauce Moyo Recipe
commentBody: i wonder if forofa (toasted cassava from Brazil) would work here as well.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Grain Bowl With Sardines and Sauce Moyo Recipe
commentBody: Could probably use fine grain bulgur if you can't find folio. GN

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Grain Bowl With Sardines and Sauce Moyo Recipe
commentBody: no disrespect to fonio: i’ve now tried this recipe with a grain mix (couscous, bulgur, quinoa) and again with fonio and i preferred the former.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Grain Bowl With Sardines and Sauce Moyo Recipe
commentBody: This was quite tasty.  I quadrupled the recipe to get 4 servings.  I worried about the spice heat.  I started with 1 habanero and used a half more.  I didn't find it to be unacceptably spicy.  Next time I'd use two.  Whole Foods didn't carry fonio.   I used 1.5 cups of cooked quinoa per serving instead.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Grain Bowl With Sardines and Sauce Moyo Recipe
commentBody: Revelation. Made it exactly as directed and it is amazing!!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Grain Bowl With Sardines and Sauce Moyo Recipe
commentBody: I replaced fonio with quinoa and added extra lime. It was wonderful!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Grain Bowl With Sardines and Sauce Moyo Recipe
commentBody: I made this for lunch and used instant brown rice. It was SO good!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Grain Bowl With Sardines and Sauce Moyo Recipe
commentBody: I had trouble getting the fonio to fluff up and absorb all the water, because I didn't really have a small pan with a lid and just poured boiling water over and let it sit. Used a serrano pepper and tinned smoked trout instead of sardines since that was what I had. Turned out delicious for all that! Very nice meld of flavors and textures.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Grain Bowl With Sardines and Sauce Moyo Recipe
commentBody: I used what I had. Faro - cooked from the freezer - but I will be on the look-out for Fonio. And Jalapeño, celery, and Sriracha in place of the peppers. Delicious! A great lunch.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Grain Bowl With Sardines and Sauce Moyo Recipe
commentBody: Did this with couscous, since we had it. Worked fine and very tasty. Careful with the habaneros!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and definitely worth making. Used a whole spatchcocked chicken placed on top of the vegetables. Increased amount of curry oil by half. It was just enough to cover peppers and onions plus the chicken. I added 5-6 whole garlic cloves to peppers and they were wonderful to come across as we scooped the vegetables. Roasted on 375 in a convection oven for 50 minutes. Served on top of garlic naan bread.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: My only hack was 2 bone in chicken breasts. Other than, I followed it exactly. I diced the veggies and was surprised they didn’t scorch but the veggies made a jammy kind of thing when I took it out of the oven. 28 minutes to 150F. This was easy, quick and wonderfully tasty. A thumbs up for sure!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Just the addition of a little thyme and crushed hot pepper gives this a nice Caribbean vibe.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: As an ardent reader of Melissa Clark’s wonderful writing, I saw yesterday’s  "cooking for one" article where she linked this recipe. I made the recipe as was written,  so I will not be offering any alternatve suggestions like  "I put raisins and chocolate chips in it” or “I didn’t have chicken so I used Spam”  Sarcasm anyone? This recipe is AMAZING- and I will make this all the time now and will share this with guests. Thank you for this site and its contributers,

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: My brother who is a very good cook said "cook this recipe, it's delicious and stupid simple".  Used chicken thighs.  Needed more than 35 minutes.  Put onion/pepper mixture on rice noodles, surprisingly good.  He was right "delicious and stupid simple".

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: I loved this recipe!  I made a couple of small tweaks that I enjoyed—reduced the onion and peppers by half and added a sweet potato, (cubed). Because I don’t live in the land of 3 lb chickens, I increased the cooking time to 40 minutes and put everything under the broiler for 4 minutes at the end, to crisp the chicken skin.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Mae Not -  You made me laugh. If you find the land of 3 lb chickens, let us know where it's located. I haven't been able to find it but I have a collection of recipes from various sources that call for those elusive 3# chickens. Maybe in same place where you find hen's teeth?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Per Jonathan Waxman, the chicken guru, the perfect chicken for cooking is 3.75 lbs. Agreed that supermarket chickens are HUGE!!! I tend to go to specialty butcher for the perfect chicken.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody:
@Jean21
Those 3 lb chickens are usually from recipes in older cookbooks. Now the chickens are bred to grow much larger and I often find chickens in the market that are close to 6 pounds. I’m using a bone in breast that’s about a pound and a half and I expect it to work out well.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Prepared this yesterday and it was awesome and so easy.  A party in your mouth!!! I actually used a whole chicken, split into two pieces.  I did not increase the vegetables, but I did double the curry oil and the quantity of the oil ended up being perfect to season the chicken.  I think an important part of this recipe is releasing the skin (both the breast and thigh & leg) and getting the curry oil onto the meat.  400 on convection roast took about 40 minutes.  Very moist meat.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Curry oil is the oil mixed with the spices.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: I used boneless chicken thighs and cooked the dish at 400 degrees for 25 minutes. Chicken was cooked and peppers still had a bit of crispness. Overall, we thought the dish was good but not outstanding. It might have been our curry powder but it didn’t seem spicy at all.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: We made this with baked tofu and it was delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe.  NYT Cooking should do many more recipes for one!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Agreed!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: I marinated the chicken with the garlic, curry, and oil mix over night, including rubbing the mix underneath the skin, and made more mix for the diced onions and pepper. 425 for 30 minutes. It came out beautifully. Without marinating, I found the chicken can be a bit bland.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: I love how easy this was!  I only had a partial red pepper, so I added brussel sprouts and sliced a parsnip, to the mix, and it worked really well together.  We had three, very large boneless, skinless, breasts, which took about 35 minutes to bake, but still made for a very juicy and flavorful dinner.  I will absolutely make this again.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: This was very good and easy to make. I used bone-in thighs and served the chicken and peppers with brown basmati rice and garlic Naan. Everyone in my family loved it.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: I made this last night and it was totally delicious- great dinner for two with virtually no work.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Just cooked this - it may be my fav from NYT.  Super easy and delicious just as written.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Made with four thighs. A little greasy in the pan -- maybe more fat rendered from the thighs than would have from a half chicken. Otherwise, very good. Very curry-y. I loved the flavor!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Made this tonight and while it tasted fine, there was so much juice from the chicken that I felt like the peppers and onions were swimming in grease.  The picture shows a dry pan.  Not sure why?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: You need to cut off the concentration of fat from the end of the chicken.  Although the vegetables cooked in the chicken fat is usually considered quite tasty.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Doubled the recipe and served on coconut and lime rice. This is now a dinner party staple. It's delicious and easy on the cook.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Easy recipe.  I followed it with the exception that I used thighs with bones.  I removed all of the extra fat before baking the chicken and veggies and used brown rice as the side.  Very tasty and definitely will make again.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, easy recipe. I made this with chicken thighs  because it was what I had on hand and served it over rice with lime zest and butter. The curry smell is not for the faint of heart - 2 days later and our home is still fragrant.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Really wanted to like this, followed the recipe and wasn’t impressed.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Any opinions about trying this in a microwave? I've cooked chicken and veggies in microwaves lots. Having an oven on 400 for 40 min for one-serving meal, even with renewable energy here, seems wasteful.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: You will not get the browning or caramelizing on the peppers. If you really want, you can heat everything in microwave to get it hot, then finish in the oven for 15 minutes or so. But TBH, I don't think it will be as tasty!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: I live alone, and hardly ever use my big over. I havea Brevile countertop oven/air fryer and it does a great job, with a lot of less energy. Those microwaves use alot of power and still will take at least 15-20 minutes. So where is the efficency?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: The instructions (Step 3) say "rub the remaining curry oil all over the bird and under the breast skin."  Does the author mean "rub the remaining olive oil"?  I ask because there's no "curry oil" in the ingredients list and I'm not sure what "curry oil" is.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: That's the mixture of olive oil, garlic and curry powder made in the 1st sentence, 2nd step.  They gave it a fancy name :)

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: The step above it instructs "In a small bowl, mix the oil, garlic and curry powder. Toss the peppers and onion with 2 tablespoons of the curry oil. Spread in an even layer and sprinkle with salt and pepper." They're using "curry oil" to refer to this mixture.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Refer to Step 2 - the oil, garlic and curry powder together = curry oil.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: This may be a dumb question.  If you cut a whole chicken in half and cook both halves, would the cooking time be the same?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: It's not a dumb question, and yes, it should take the same time

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Ye the cooking time should be the same

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: I was wondering the same thing.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: We substituted leg quarters for the half a chicken and really enjoyed it with naan and rice. You could make it with more of the oil/garlic/curry mix and I think it would be even more powerful. Great as is though

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: If you're going to add south Indian coconut milk (for left overs) you should consider using a south Indian curry powder like Bolst's.  (Mahdur Jaffrey prefers it.)

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Would this also work with boneless/skinkess thighs?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: That’s how I’ll be making it tonight. Thighs are pretty forgiving so I don’t see why it wouldn’t work.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: I’m not a big fan of white meat chicken, and am thinking substituing thighs for the half chicken would make roasting go faster (and avoid ovecooking of the vegetables). Anyone tried this yet?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: So, just a random question for knowledge. If I HALF a recipe that asks for a certain amount of oil to brown something, do I half the oil also or do I keep the oil amount the same? Maybe it’s “pan size dependent” and not recipe dependent. Thoughts?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: I am not aure you can half a half a chicken but when youreduce sices cooking rates chang too.  This one is a good basic size with left overs.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody:
“Pan size dependent” :). 
If all you have is a large sheet pan; create a “smaller pan” by using double fold of aluminum foil , crimping sides, and creating size to fit your 1/2 portion. 
Or…if you don’t mind possibility of more oil getting into your food; just use large pan with the full oil portion.?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: I’ve also wondered the same… Read your recipe well. If it seems something needs oil to blossom (spices) or not stick to the pan, I think a halving would be fine- but pan size would matter.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: This is a tasty recipe but I prefer chicken pieces. Far easier for cooking and storage for a single person, which is the entire point of this article.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: looks wonderful. Give him this time of year, I think I might try it outside on my bullet smoker. Does anyone see any disadvantages with that approach?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Can you use two or four chicken leg quarters instead of one half of a chicken

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Why not?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: This is a keeper weeknight recipe for sure, and it will go on rotation at our house. I can see salmon or tofu in lieu of the chicken being a good ideA, or perhaps some Brussels sprouts or aubergine (or both). Delicious! Mmm :)

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Could you use Cornish Game Hens cut in half for the recipe?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Yes. Reduce cooking time some

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody:
I am surprised how flavorful this was despite how simple the ingredients are! I found that cooking half a bird at 400 degrees took closer to 45 minutes -- I used a thermometer, checking for 175 degrees for the dark meat and 155 degrees for the breast (as per Sohla's video!) 

I definitely recommend pairing with white rice. It lasted me two pretty hearty meals when paired with rice.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: My first thought was why not add beans to the veggies.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody:
I loved this easy recipe!!! 

Thank you Melissa!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: I made this last night. I used two whole legs, extra fat trimmed, and only 3 tablespoons of oil. Came out beautifully. A sprinkling of cilantro and lime before serving was very nice. Excellent dish and my husband says it’s going in our regular rotation.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Those peppers sure don't look diced in the photo but rather chopped!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly and this was still pretty meh.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Definitely going in my staple of recipes! A very quick and easy curry recipe that compares to my mom's curry chicken recipe!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: An overwhelming taste of raw curry powder and nothing else.  I cut the amount of oil but didn’t cut the curry and it was a mistake. Perhaps I should have warmed the oil and curry before putting it in the recipe. I guess I prefer more subtle seasoning. I won’t make it again.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody:
Made it tonight with chicken supremes - skin on breasts with the wing attached - superb!  If curry tastes "raw," it may be old.  I mixed the marinade ingredients an
 hour or so ahead of time and the spice was quite mellow.  Also there are many different kinds of "curry powder."  I used some that I brought back from the Caribbean - 
quite different from generic "Madras curry powder" which I 
consider to be a meaningless descriptor.  There are also 
numerous recipes for home made blends.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: This dish is so flavourful and the chicken juicy and tender. Such a simple dish! I did add some thyme and pepper flakes as I like heat, as suggested . This will be rotated in my kitchen a lot!!!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: This is a really easy, delicious meal. I used chicken thighs instead of a whole chicken and added diced sweet potatoes into the mix. Since I used thighs it rendered a little too much fat so next time I'll trim do a better job of removing excess chicken skin as well as use 1 T less olive oil.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: I made a meal for 2 by adding 1lb diced potatoes to the peppers and onions, then prepared/cooked everything as directed.  When roasting was almost complete, I made a creamy sauce by heating 2 tsp of reserved curry oil with two diced tomatoes until saucy, then mixed in one can of coconut milk; simmering to slightly reduce.  When the chicken/veggies came out, I mixed the veggies into the curry sauce.  Served over rice. Rogan Josh seasoning works well here too and not as spicy.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Curry Roasted Half Chicken and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: I used a whole spatchcocked chicken, increased the curry oil by 50%, and as suggested, I added a cubed sweet potato and several whole garlic cloves to the bell pepper and onion mix. Otherwise made as written. Delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The directions for this recipe are WAY overcomplicated, and, well, unfortunate. You know how to make a good grilled cheese sandwich, right? So just take two slices of good bread, make a hole in one slice, and prep the slices as you would for a standard grilled cheese. Just add some good ham along with the cheese. Then, in a pan, grill the ‘hole side’ first, and when that is perfectly grilled, flip it over, break an egg into the hole, and put a lid on the pan. Voilà!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Toast the cutout piece of bread in the butter along with the rest of the bread. Eat with jam for dessert.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wonder if the burnt bottoms could be avoided by subbing mayo for the butter? Game changer for grilled cheese

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Simply excellent! Try it old-school with unapologetic use of Velveeta for melty goodness, or doll it up with fancy bread and every Fromage of your dreams, with or without meat, or add a little spinach or a tomato slice. Don’t stress about the instructions. This is basically Grilled Cheese and Egg-in-a-Hole had a Baby.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: perfection. I have been making this on repeat! if your bread burns, the pan is too hot. don’t skimp on the butter and if you’re using ham definitely put it in the pan to fry with the rest of it. don’t forget to cook the little bread circle in its own smidgeon of butter.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Made this for our weekly brinner! 

I’m not a huge fan of deli meat so I made 2 of our 4 sandwiches with tomato as a substitute and the other 2 as written. Delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’m gluten-free and made this sandwich with Schar sourdough bread Gruyère and it was delicious! Next time, I’ll add a drizzle of honey to the ham for a sweet and savory flavor.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used a Trader Joe’s Breakfast chicken patty, cooked (reheated) in the skillet first, then diced it up and put it on the cheese. I didn’t have sliced cheese so I used some shredded cheese. Came out great.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Maybe just butter one side of each slice of bread before putting it in the pan.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Definitely won’t make this again. Burned bottoms.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It was very good though the bottom was blackened, perhaps because it needed a bit more butter and I forgot to swipe both sides of bread. However, I used a high end burger bun that was almost like a Thomas English muffin, which cooked well with the egg and cheese.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was one of the most delicious grilled cheeses I have ever had and it felt so indulgent to eat I was almost giddy. I didn't use any meat, just a few different cheeses. I did it my way but the idea of this was the catalyst needed to create joy in my mouth. Give it shot. Worth it!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Tasty. But just have your fork to eat it as it is messy

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Yummy and cute. Adding tomatoes and hot flakes are a must.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Really good stuff. Better than a regular fried egg sandwich, AND the cheese stays put instead of sliding off of a fried egg!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: How much is a pat of butter?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I'd say between 1 and two tablespoons worth.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: According to multiple references on Google, a pat can run anywhere from 1 to 1.5 tsp. & is a square 1"x1" on each side & 1/3" thick. (I also found out that a knob is 1–1.5 Tbsp.) But I would say use as much butter as you need to keep the bread from sticking, & as much butter as your taste buds prefer. For me that would probably be a generous pat of butter for each slice of bread.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: When it says "ham" , it means Taylor ham, or pork roll if you must.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It does not mean that at all

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I think it means "ham", however you define it. Never heard of "taylor ham".

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Kind of a nice “everyday” croque Madame hack

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: One pat = 9 grams (10 if you’re feeling crazy).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: A thin tablespoon of butter or about 1/4 inch thick

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: A stick of butter is marked on the wrapper into 8 tablespoons. I was taught that a pat is about a third of a tablespoon, or 1 teaspoon.  But in this recipe they probably consider a pat to be closer to 1/2 tablespoon (or even a tablespoon) because my experience making grilled cheese is that 1 tsp of butter in a pan is a pretty small amount. Given the confusion, a good recipe editor would have clarified what was meant.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Such a simple sandwich with such a confusing recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Use sourdough bread sliced however you like it, thick or thin. Adds a  lot to the flavor!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: You shouldn’t need too much butter. The pan should already be slick. Let the ham cook in the pan too before putting it on the cheese.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wouldn't the butter be for the taste as well?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Eggs-in-prison, eggs-in-a-basket are other terms I’ve hears used for this dish!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Eggs in prison would be appropriate at this time

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Gold mine sandwich!
Denise - Unfortunately you may have a point ;-)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Toad in a hole in Britain

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Does one watch 'V for Vendetta's before or after dining on this sandwich?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Love it!  Our family always called it the Egyptian One-Eyed Sandwich. (When made on one slice only)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Have made a version with one slice for years, so I tried.  The presentation is much nicer seeing the egg but not a huge fan of the extra slice of bread.  Maybe just what I am used to eating

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: At summer camp in the 1970s, we cooked these (sans cheese) on a sheet pan over an open fire and called it "Rocky Mountain Spotted Toast."

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I had some brioche leftover from making the NYTimes French toast recipe so I tried this for lunch. 

Next time I will cut a little bit larger hole for the egg. It took a little longer to cook to my preference. I ended up removing the cheese slice from the pan to avoid the scorching/blackening that other reviewers mentioned. 
Overall, a great quick sandwich with stuff I already had on hand!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Love this idea! So glad I saw it before lunch. I needed something substantial with plenty of protein without too much stove fussing. It's reminiscent of an egg and cheese omelette complete with toast, but 'country style'. Riffed by topping the cheese with some crunchy Pac Choi and a thin slice of red onion. Delish!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Works well open face and without cheese, too!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
AMAZING
I am honestly speechless of how good this was.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Yes, use mayo instead of butter. Perfect.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Perhaps obvious to others, but I discovered that only the middle slices of a challah loaf will allow enough room to cut out a circle. No problem; I used the rest of the loaf to make Melissa Clark's Brown Sugar Sheet-Pan French Toast https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1025409-brown-sugar-sheet-pan-french-toast. Win win!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Easy and delish recipe. Used low sodium turkey bacon and I also flipped the egg bread for a few seconds and then placed on top yolk up because I prefer eggs fried medium. I’ll definitely be making this again!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Tasty sandwich. Crispy bread (I used a round roll which is what I had), soft egg and cheese.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Always cook eggs thoroughly or serve salmonella surprise.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used sliced sourdough bread and added tomatoes and micro-greens on top of the melted cheese. Excellent!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I've been making an egg salad with tzatziki -- holds together nicely in a sandwich. A popular dinner at our home is "scoops" ... and the tzatziki substitutes for mayo particularly well in potato and chicken salads, too.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this with the addition of capers - delicious!!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I've been making variations of this for years. I prefer Bulgarian yogurt for its tangy flavor, as most commercial drained ("Greek") yogurt is pretty bland. Contrary to some commenters, adding all the oil from the can doesn't make it too thin or "an oily mess." It works with the yogurt to make for a good, rich mouth feel and flavor. Using sharp mustard instead of yellow brightens the flavor further. Finally, add some sunflower seeds or pepitas for extra crunch without the bitterness of celery.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was dinner last night, made with poached salmon instead of tuna. Delicious! and will try with tuna next time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I added some red onion to it and I admit I added a little mayo. Used dried dill because i don't love fresh dill and it was really good.  Left most of the oil in and left it chunky.  A nice basic salad and very easy to use.  (Added some to cooked bowties for a little salad too!)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Used tuna in water, did not use the water or add oil and store made Tzatziki. Mixed it really well.  I can see how this would be a dip. Served on fresh baked oatmeal bread. It was perfect. Will try next time for added crunch celery and maybe walnuts.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I just made this two days ago, not because of this recipe but because I had leftover tzatziki( homemade) and added it to tuna. Excellent! My tzatziki is slightly different, garlic, olive oil, white vinegar, a grated cucumber, dill and mint. Take it from a Greek!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I just tried it ... and it's an acquired taste.  Maybe tuna in water would go better, but this one is not for me.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was my reaction too.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I tried to love this, truly! I didn't hate it, but, well...it fell flat. I had some very nice homemade tzatziki (with cucumber) and also some crisp celery. All the elements were right there in place, but...meh. Even after I oiled it up with the finest olive oil in my pantry.

I think what it lacked was a touch of sweetness. I think some pickled red onions, minced, would have given it the zing it lacked for me. Or (yes, I am Southern) maybe finely diced sweet gherkins.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I liked this recipe. To make it work, you need a really thick yogurt. If you're thinking you're just going to buy a store-bought small single serve low-fat greek yogurt for this recipe, be prepared for a soupy mess.

I did end up changing the proportions of the seasonings to taste after I made the original recipe to punch up the flavor. You don't need to add any outside ingredients.

It's delicious. My cat also agrees!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Adapted this as a kind of niçoise, adding (per Lynne) capers to the dressing (of which I made a full cup b/c why go to the trouble of peeling a cucumber for 2 tbsp of dressing?). Added steamed potatoes, fresh tomatoes, kalamata olives, and green beans on a bed of just-picked romaine. Used the water canned tuna I had on hand. Worked just fine w/ a tsp of olive oil.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was great, with one caveat. I used just a bit of the oil from can and it was still too thin for me. Next time I will forgo the oil entirely—I like my tuna salad on the chunky side. Used cucumber and Dijon mustard instead of the yellow.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is a wonderful recipe when you want to dial down the fat. The second time I made it, I drained the oil, my one complaint, added both celery and cucumber plus some finely diced tricolor bell pepper to ramp up the vegetables. Delicious and has more crunch if you like that, which I do. Another reduced fat method is drained and blotted tuna, a couple of heaping tablespoons of muffuletta relish and a splash of red wine vinegar. This holds well for a bagna cauda type sandwich for a picnic lunch.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was a rare NYT miss for me. Soupy consistency and odd flavor combination.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: For years I’ve “improved” everyday tuna salad by using half mayonnaise/half ranch dressing in the recipe. When I have them I gild the lily by mixing in a few soup spoons full of capers, which I like much more than pickle relish. Other enhancements are some dill and a couple of drops of Tabasco. A spritz of lemon juice also perks it up. It’s all up to your tastebuds!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Doubled the recipe and adding the oil from both cans was a little too much. Drain one of them next time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this as directed and was VERY liquidy. Not even a proper salad. Recommend draining the tuna.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This dish may work for some but I really missed the binding creaminess of mayonnaise.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I was skeptical but had made too much tzatziki for a different recipe, so I went for it. Delightful! The yellow mustard is surprising but it works. I didn't have celery so used the white parts of 2 green onions, finely chopped. My only question is regarding the instruction to add "the tuna and its oil"; that seems like it'd end up a greasy mess. I drained almost all the oil -- but less than I usually would -- and was very happy with the results.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Trader Joes has a great Vegan Tzatziki Sauce. I already use it in potato salad, gyros or as a sandwich spread.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I just make the sauce and mix it with the tubs in water. It’s delicious, and you don’t miss oil or mayo

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: As others said, this was easy. I used Dijon mustard and a big jolt of lemon, since it seemed to lack zip. Stuffed into lettuce and cucumber lined pitas.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: All in all I prefer plain tzatziki.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I added dill to store-bought tzatziki and used stone ground mustard.  Excellent easy dish to beat this heat!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was not very good. I followed the recipe exactly, then tried to doctor it up by adding sweet pickle relish, chopped celery and extra lemon juice. Nothing helped. We ate it for lunch but the rest will be pitched.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious. I added 3/4 of a teaspoon of whole grain mustard instead as I like it more and worked really well. Realised I also added a slightly larger clove of garlic than recommended but really loved it. Didn’t know adding salt would take away some of the sharpness from the garlic. The more you know. Super nice on toast. Will be making this on a regular basis.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was tasty, but very wet - I am going to drain most of the oil next time and shred the cucumber and let it sit in a colander for a few minutes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: how would this salad taste if canned sardines are substituted for the tuna?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Considering how healthy this tuna salad is I find the flavor combination to be excellent. Capers great addition!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Easy, delish, lovely, no-mayo tuna salad.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used store bought tzatziki and added a finely chopped stalk of celery.. oh and some celery seeds. Loved it. Did not miss the mayo.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If you’re trying to cut mayonnaise you can also use creamy avocado with a touch of olive. Chopped kalamata olives are also a nice addition. Tumeric or curry powder can really change up tuna salad as well.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: In order to stay true to that tzatziki flavor, opt for cucumber over celery. I thought the yellow mustard over powered the delicate dill and lightness of the yogurt. Will omit next time and may try a splash of red wine vinegar or double the lemon juice instead to replace the acidic punch the mustard was trying to serve.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I substituted Bulgarian yogurt for a different twist and used prepared mustard by half. Tasted great. Popping flavor.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this today and it was delicious! Had a couple ears of corn in my fridge that I’ve been needing to use, so I steamed and cooled the kernels and they were a great addition. Tasty lunch on a bed of greens with slices of tomato and avocado on the side :)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I love tzatziki, but detest tuna fish.  Has anyone tried this with canned chicken?  I would probably have to drain the broth and  add some olive oil?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I can't taste the difference between canned tuna and canned chicken!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I make a very similar recipe with canned chicken. I put in more dill and chopped shallots. I also sometimes add the brine from capers.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Try using fresh tuna it taste so much bet that canned. Cook it, chill it and cut it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Nice and fresh, garlic and salt works well! Thank you.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I was eating my own make-up version of tzatziki tuna salad when I saw yours. I didn't know that I was not the only one to eat tuna salad (or salmon, or bacalao) with tzatziki. Mine is generally store-bought. For my delight you add lemon juice and mustard

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Just perfect- I loved this. I used cucumber, no mustard.
The fresh dill just made it!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’ve been using Greek yogurt in my tuna for several years. I had weight loss surgery, and this was a great way to boost the protein in the tuna salad. It also tasted good when I was in the purée stage. My recipe is similar to this one, but I use finely diced celery once I could have veggies.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Not my favorite NY TImes recipe. I didn't have fresh dill and used canned salmon instead of tuna.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious on lettuce and small unpeeled potatoes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My daughter is allergic to mustard, so instead of using cucumbers or celery, I used a very tangy dill pickle, and in a larger quantity.  This substitution gave the salad the same kind of zing that mustard would.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I used 7 Thai chiles.

On first tasing, there was no heat, only sweet.

Then it hit the back of my mouth and my throat closed up and I couldn’t breathe for about 10 seconds.

Good stuff! :-)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
How do you boil 2T of vinegar & reduce by half?
Does it really need refrigeration?
Why only 2 weeks shelf life?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Yeah, I refuse to believe that honey, one of the best preservatives out there (as well as an anti-bacterial; in Nordic countries it was used like Neosporin on wounds) would only last 2 weeks, especially if you put it in the fridge.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Boil the 2 T of vinegar until there's only 1 T.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is tasty and all, but I found the flavor lacking. I have always made my hot honey with a fermented hot sauce, you get the vinegar zing and more punch, with a lively red color.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Sarah please tell me what kind of fermented hot sauce you used? I’m intrigued. Thanks!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I did this with jalapenos instead of red chiles and it was absolutely delicious!  So good on cornbread or fried chicken.  I'm looking forward to trying it with other chiles.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Super easy and very delicious! I'm confused to see that some readers had problems. It's about as easy as it gets and is very versatile condiment. Thanks.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Immediately went out and bought the chilies (10 BTW), simmered as instructed, and drizzled it on four very thick wagu burger patties on the backyard grille. Extra points if you place a 1/4"  slice of sweet onion on top and scoop the remnants on top of just before you flip the burger.

It's beyond words.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If you didn’t want to cook the honey, could you blend the chiles and vinegar after they cooked and add to the raw honey? Or infuse them whole for a few days?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I was thinking exactly the same thing! Don't understand why the honey should be simmered. If at all, heat it to a gentle 90F and then mix everything.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can someone please tell me why one must "be sure" to wash the chilis before using?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Why the fridge for storage?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Why two weeks? Chiles in vinegar last forever.  And honey of course is found in the pyramids, it lasts forever.  I guess the times is worried about being sued. But be serious.  I keep my honey in the pantry.  During the summer, as I harvest chiles and put them in a bullet blender jar with vinegar, salt, pepper and whatever spices I desire, usually garlic powder.  I keep it in the fridge, adding chiles until fall.  I use if for months. I don't see how it could go bad.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I was wondering the exact same thing! Thanks for posting!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Probably because when you cook the honey it loses it's antibacterial nature.  The real question is why cook the honey at all?  Make an infusion of hot vinegar and add it to the honmey once it's cool.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I followed the directions and wound up with a slab of hard-as-rock flavored honey that had to be dug out and thrown away. Good idea but disappointing at best.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Be aware that this simmering of the honey will take out all of the health benefits from your honey.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I think people are overcautious with chiles. But honey is a preservative and an antibiotic so I wouldn't worry about it. If you are concerned at all, just strain out the chiles, the flavor will be a bit milder but should still be good.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used Mike's hot honey instead of a mild honey as a base and had amazing results. If you have a high spice tolerance give it a go!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This sounds as if it would be a great gift. I wonder if it could be canned safely, or if there's insufficient vinegar left to make it safe. Thoughts anyone?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Honey is very shelf stable and bacteria resistant, adding a bit of chili flavored vinegar shouldn't change that.

author: Genevieve Ko
Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Has anyone tried this with dried birds eye/Thai chilies?  I have a bunch I need to use up!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: i grow lots of chiles and use them indiscriminately no matter what a recipe calls for. it will be just as good.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Kate. some recipes recommend dried chili flakes!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Just made this to drizzle over butternut squash congee. I added 9 Thai chiles, OMG so yummy! Added just the right oomph to congee.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious!  Try microwaving with a Pat of butter to create a great popcorn topping, so yummy!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Microwaving honey completely changes the taste and properties

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I'm going to clarify that the recipe calls for white wine VINEGAR... and not a nice Chardonnay!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Thanks for pointing out. The wording is a bit ambiguous.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Curious - has anyone made this recipe with maple syrup or silan? Vegan here.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I don't understand, how is honey not vegan? It's made from the nectar of blossoms. Where's the killing involved in making honey? With maple syrup you tap into a maple tree and drain its lifeblood, the syrup from it, and that's vegan??

BTW. I'm a beekeeper with over 100 hives.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I’m also vegan but let honey “go.” I know it’s the by-product of animals (well, insects) but I don’t consider honey “non vegan.” (And, I know, there’s a huge community that disagrees with me).

A substitute would be very light maple syrup, but that’s going to “flavor” this condiment more than honey would.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Ace Weems: It's not vegan because it's produced by animals. Milk and eggs are also not vegan, even though they don't require killing. I think the idea is that we shouldn't exploit animals in any way. (I'm not vegan myself, but I don't want to go into a philosophical discourse here.)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Might be helpful to simmer the chiles on a back burner with the fan on "very high" ...

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used shishito peppers from my garden that I had left on the plant to fully redden (10). Maybe they weren't consistently hot; I added cayenne and Tabasco to the end product to spice it up.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Honey is not considered vegan because it is a product created by bees, which are animals. It's why vegans also don't eat non-fertilized eggs or drink milk.

commentBody: Can’t I mix honey with Siracha and that makes hot honey ??

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Has anyone tried this with ancho chiles?

commentBody: Used 6 Thai chilis & ended up with little to no heat.

commentBody: Made by instructions. Result too thick. Add a little distilled water to my first effort And cut way back on heating next time.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody:
Wouldn’t cooking the shrimp for the 4-5 minutes called for in the recipe result in tough, overcooked shrimp? 
Any reason not to cook them for a minute or two, remove them, cook the veggies, and then recombine everything for the last steps?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: If the shrimp were thawed, yes. But as I read the recipe, the shrimp are still frozen.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Start cooking the asparagus before the shrimp; otherwise you'll have pink hockey pucks.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody:
Definitely cook the asparagus first, then shrimp.
Quick, winner dinner

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: @Susan L: not necessary if picked fresh from the garden.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I recently made something similar to this a few weeks ago, I have never joined in on comments, but I want to share a tip.  Using any rice pilaf mix, especially the long grain and wild rice, gives it more flavor and ease.  Used red pepper flakes and a splash of wine, I did not use soy sauce,but I added some fish broth and simmered the asparagus. Added shrimp towards the end and served with a sprinkle of Parmesan and parsley.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. I didn't have a red chili, so I used a serrano with seeds. I sauteed the onion, then garlic with the chili, then added the asparagus, followed by the shrimp, and then the soy/sugar. Before the shrimp were completely cooked, I added a cup of cooked rice and the butter. The best, most sophisticated fried rice on earth. It helps that we live in Savannah, where fresh white shrimp are readily available (in this case, they'd been flash-frozen).

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: These are the kinds of notes I come here for! Thank you, Nina!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: @Nina Altschiller - I just wanted to tell you that I’m extremely jealous that you have access to fresh shrimp.  I can only imagine!  Thanks for your post.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: This works perfectly well made with broccoli or many other vegies if asparagus is not in your fridge. Just remember to re-name the recipe accordingly. I made it with fresh sorrel from the garden, and it was excellent. I like the lemony flavor even better, so I ate the whole thing myself.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Saute the asparagus first.  Cooking 101.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: This recipe won high approval from my family. I followed the recipe as written, with regard to ingredients and procedures. However, I did pull the shrimp out of the frying pan when they were just barely cooked because I could tell that they would have become overcooked had I left them sautéing while cooking the asparagus. I added them back when the asparagus were just reaching completion. It did seem like this approach to frozen shrimp made them tastier.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: @Raro. Brovo-best reply regarding the cooking scenario!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody:
Double the sauces
Cook the asparagus before the shrimp

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Excellent recipe. I added sliced shiitake mushrooms and subbed scallions for the onion, and added minced ginger. Stir-fried in this order: aromatics first (ginger, garlic, red chili, whites of scallions), then added asparagus until bright green, then shiitakes until slightly browned. Removed asparagus and shiitakes from pan, stir-fried shrimp until not-quite cooked (3 min), then added veggies back w finely sliced scallion greens.  Added the soy sauce (3 tbsp) and sugar, then butter.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: @Jill E. Thank you for your excellent note.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I love this recipe and appreciate the thawing suggestions. I have made this several times many as written and some improvised based upon what was available (or needed to used). Always good. I would love more dinner options like this for quick week night dinner.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody:
Salt brine the frozen shrimp for an hour 
Before cooking. It makes a difference.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe

recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: After reading all the comments, I can't help but think that a number of those referring to “fresh” shrimp are overlooking a small but visible “previously frozen” label over the display.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: @Nancy fresh shrimp (never frozen) are actually available all the time in FL

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: @Lindsey Sigh.... (I'm not in FL).

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Wastes a lot of water. Better to defrost overnight in fridge or a few hours ahead on metal pan on counter.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody:
No.  Thawing at room temp is an absolute no-go.  Multiple (i.e., hundreds or more) credible sources say the same thing:

"Under no circumstances should you ever thaw frozen shrimp at room temperature, as this can be extremely dangerous. Thawing frozen shrimp at room temperature will cause the shrimp to enter the temperature danger zone, which is the range between 40°F and 140°F where bacteria grows most rapidly"

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: @Kd-nyt overnight in the fridge is definitely the safe and water conserving way to go

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Dang this is good

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Bunch of asparagus? Please, please, please use amounts that are measurable or that at least approach common understanding.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody:
@NeilB. 
Use as much or as little asparagus, you have.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I suggest using not-too-thick asparagus so that it cooks quickly.  Also, add the garlic after the onion has cooked a bit - don't want to overcook the garlic.  And, most importantly, cook the asparagus BEFORE the shrimp, as the shrimp will cook quickly and you don't want it to turn to rubber.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Step 2: Cooking garlic at high heat burns it. It would be better to cook the shrimp at high heat all alone, remove the shrimp and cook the onions and garlic at lower heat, then the asparagus and shrimp.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Definitely asparagus first. 6-7 minutes. Shrimps need no more than 3-4 minutes.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Wondering why salt the shrimp?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: @Miriam totally not necessary. It made the recipe too salty, even after rinsing.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: @Miriam.  The salting step makes the shrimp firmer, with better texture.  A common step in Japanese cuisine. A Japanese chef would weigh the water and the salt in order to have the desired brine.  We seldom aim for that degree of precision in the US.  Rinse well after brining and consider subbing a low-sodium soy sauce (I use a Tamari that is 50%!less sodium) or cut the amount of soy sauce and added salt to bring the saltiness level down to your liking.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: The shrimp will not be completely thawed after 3-4 minutes under cold running water, therefore the total 4-5 minutes of cooking time will not overcook them

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Delightful meal, restaurant quality. Followed recipe as written, but did use crushed red pepper instead of a fresh hot red.  Perfect blend of flavors, pleasant surprise, (I had my doubts) going into regular rotation.  Thank you!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Cooked this for guests last evening. Very easy and delicious. With the asparagus sliced in thin, diagonal slices, the cooking times for the shrimp and veg were pretty close. The asparagus were crisp tender and the shrimp perfectly done. The pepper I bought was not spicy enough, so I passed chili crisp at the table. Served with brown rice, but I like the pilaf idea someone suggested.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Pretty good! Nice weeknight dinner.  I added a bit of ginger, but still it felt like it was missing something.. maybe lime/lime zest.  Would try it again

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Perfect as-is. The only sub I did was a half tsp red pepper flakes for the chili. Cooking times are perfect for frozen shrimp.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody:
Used mixture of asparagus and broccoli, stir fried them before adding shrimp for last 2 minutes as suggested.
Very good.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I added one anchovy which melted and provided more umami

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Maybe all these recipes should be updated and take out seed oils and replace with ghee, avocado or olive oil?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Why? There is nothing at all wrong with seed oils, unless you believe RFK Jr which no sensible person should do.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: @C this would make a lot of recipes inaccessible to many

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: @Lizzie Thank you!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Would be great, but way too salty. Don’t salt the shrimp, and use some sherry (real, not cooking!) to balance out less soy sauce. I will make it again this way, though.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: It was quick and easy to put together and the three of us polished it off and loved it!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Swapped in my new best friend....chili crisp....for the chili. Very tasty.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody:
I am short on shrimp
How would you add tofu to about 6 oz of shrimp to make it a complete meal?
I want to keep it quick and simple🤞

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Sorry, I may have missed it. I asume you are using UNcooked shrimp or ?.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: The recipe calls for "1pound peeled and deveined frozen shrimp." That is uncooked shrimp; if cooked shrimp were required, it would say so.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: @Jen, Yes the shrimp are uncooked from frozen.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: At least in my country frozen shrimp can be both cooked and uncooked. I would assume the recipe calls for uncooked shrimp, but your are right, it isn't specified.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Ms Ko recommends thawing the frozen shrimp by running  cold water over them. Several brands of frozen shrimp have a warning label on the packaging to NOT use this method for thawing.  I’m assuming the warning was included for food safety issues ( why else ?).  Why should we it be concerned?  I love shrimp, but I am pretty diligent at following food safety guidelines.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Joyce, probably not a food safety issue but a conserving resource issue.  Running tap water for minutes on end is a waste of a non renewable resource, especially here in the West.  Frozen shrimp in a bowl of water for an hour has always worked for me.  Cheers!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: @Michael Agree Michael! But, I was taught that to ensure food safety the water should be running constantly. To achieve the best of both worlds, I run the merest trickle of water over the bowl of  frozen shrimp. They're usually defrosted by the time I've prepped everything else.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I made this with frozen shrimp that was accidentally precooked and it was completely fine! Add a cornstarch slurry at the end for a nicer sauce

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Easy peasy dish. I did add some fresh ginger, a bit of thai sweet chile sauce and a bit of oyster sauce to the sauce. Cooked the shrimp for less time and used very thin asparagus. I had all of the ingredients and wanted to cook something fast for dinner last night. This fit the bill.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: We found this to be rather dry with overcooked shrimp and undercooked asparagus. I added a sauce of 1/2 cup chicken broth, 1 Tbs fish sauce, and 2 add'l tsp of soy sauce. The additional moisture helped but the over- and undercooking can't be fixed without starting over.  Not a keeper for us.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: So, sorry to say, but due to the side effects of asparagus, I keep it off my menus, BUT I made this with thin string beans, and it was perfect.  Feel free to substitute as you see fit :)

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I, too, was afraid the shrimp would be overcooked. However, I decided to follow the directions as written everything, including the shrimp cooked to perfection. Thanks for a great addition to our asparagus rotation.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Loved the recipe - tasty and easy.  Followed the recipe as written and the shrimp were perfect - not at all overcooked.  Next time I’ll substitute brown sugar for the white sugar and add a little sesame oil with the butter.  Overall a keeper.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Made as directed (also added some ramps, because Spring). Really good. I think the key is to not fully thaw the shrimp.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I made some adjustments to this recipe to save it from being basic. (1) added some chinese wine and rice vinegar after carmelization (3) increased soy and sugar (4)added five spice seasoning (5) added a cornstarch slurry and most importantly, cook the shrimp AFTER the asparagus , or cook the shrimp and remove them so they dont get rubbery. I was curious about how this would come out since it was so simple, but in the end it was simple and needed to get elevated-- now I'm one of those commenters

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Add ginger, lots. Double sauce.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I added a couple spoonfuls of chili crisp.  Delicious!!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Used dark sesame oil at the end instead of butter. Asparagus doesn't need to be cooked for long - you can eat it practically raw - we had no problem cooking the shrimp first.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody:
Defrost the shrimp in a bowl of cold water. Takes no more than 30 minutes.

Double the sauce. Add lots of ginger.

Add the asparagus and the shrimp together and stirfry on high until the shrimp or pink on both sides. Everything will be done perfectly.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Made this last night and it was really good! I did add some additional seasonings, gochujang and ginger. I also substituted brown sugar for white and served it over rice. And per the other comments, I cooked the asparagus first and then added in the shrimp. Would definitely make again.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Good method to use with frozen shrimp.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I followed this recipe pretty closely but agree the asparagus should be sautéed before the shrimp is added. To build on the asian influence I added about a tablespoon or two of ginger and added sesame oil which added depth to the dish. At the end I topped the dish off by adding chopped scallions and served over pad thai noodles. It was perfect!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Made this with three smaller frozen shrimp from Costco. Followed the advice to sauté asparagus before the shrimp and this worked well. Also added the garlic a little before the asparagus so as not to burn it. Served with plain ol’ quinoa and this was just so good and easy! Definitely on the radar for future meals.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: How did you get three shrimp to feed four people?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I made this exactly as written with Costco raw shrimp 21-25 per pound. No changes necessary. Fresh spring asparagus and it can’t be beat. Quick and delicious. I used Near East rice pilaf. Perfect!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody:
Very good, plus quick and easy -- I'll definitely repeat while asparagus is in season.
I followed other commentor's leads and cooked the asparagus for a couple of minutes before adding the shrimp.  I also doubled the sauce.  Everything turned out perfectly.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody:
On the edge of being overly salty - next time I will use low sodium soy sauce.  Might try some fish sauce, hmm, what else to make the flavor a bit more complex?

Otherwise, pretty good and will prepare again.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Awesome...I had to sub red pepper flakes for the chilies, but it was knockout

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Remove shrimp from pan to rest while stir-frying asparagus. THEN add shrimp back to asparagus to warm up at the end. That’s how I do a lot my stir-fry’s to get veggies cooked enough without overcooking the protein. It’s also a good way to get the shrimp taste into the asparagus.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Substituted ground pork for shrimp because that's what I had. Cut the asparagus into 1/2 inch chunks and added them after the pork was mostly cooked. Covered pan to make sure pork fully cooked. Accidentally added 2 tbsp sugar. Was delicious over brown rice.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Way too much soy sauce, IMO.  The dish is too salty with 2 T of soy sauce plus additional salt, and it’s drowned in soy sauce overall.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Whenever I have a recipe calling for soy sauce, I use half soy sauce and half water.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I used a dried Thai bird chili with a couple of seeds (for a much reduced single portion) and I'm still buzzing from it. So good!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Note to commenters below. Please re-read the directions. It does NOT say to cook the shrimp 4 minutes. It says to rinse the frozen shrimp 3-4 minutes in cold water until they are no longer rock hard.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Relieved to find a recipe that uses frozen shrimp. Used individually frozen shrimp. Followed other suggestions and cooked asparagus before adding shrimp. Added one extra Tbsp. of Soy sauce. Otherwise, followed directions. Ease and taste will make this a staple.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Good base. I made modifications. More garlic (lots!), a bit of hoisin sauce, some rice wine vinegar,  ginger and honey instead of the sugar. Next time I might experiment more, but this was so good with a whole grain medley and some nice wine :)

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Yum. Made this with marinated extra firm tofu as hubby dislikes shrimp. Easy sub and made the recipe as usual. Would like it a tad more spicy, so will add another chili next time.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: The dry-brine is such an amazing hack for the shrimp! I used wild Argentine red shrimp, 20-25 lb, cooked for 1 min before adding asparagus, and cooked another 2 min 30 seconds in a wok on high heat.... perfect and succulent!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: This dish is so yum yum...I've been doing this dish since it was first published

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: We love this. I threw in the shrimp last, and used chili crisp instead of the pepper. Have made this dish three times now!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Easy and tasty!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I doubled the garlic(by mistake) and used dried Calabrian chili flakes because I didn't have a fresh red chile.  I love this recipe.  I still have shrimp and asparagus left and I'll make this again.  Unlike others, I thought the sequencing of the ingredients was fine.  I don't like my shrimp under-cooked.  I like it nice and firm.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Very yummy and fast, perfect spring meal. Did not have chili pepper so used red pepper flakes. Will make again.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Love this quick easy shrimp dish.  There is a sauce from the vegetables and onions extracting juice while cooking. Bought  amazing fresh asparagus in season from the farmers market. Sweet onions are a must.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I removed the shrimp and then cooked the veggies. Worked out great.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Rubbery shrimp. In so many recipes here & elsewhere we read cook the shrimp for 2 or 3  minutes then add liquid or other ingredients and simmer for another 3 or 4 this will always!!!! end up with rubbery overcooked shrimp. Don't the folks that write these recipes actually cook the recipe!! Clearly as many have noted the rule is with all shrimp dishes like this, you saute the shrimp for 2/3 minutes (they're cooked) take them out, cover, simmer a sauce & the add them back in when you combine.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody:
I used pre-cooked, frozen shrimp (thawed in a bowl of cold water) & added them after the asparagus was mostly cooked, just long enough to heat them through.
I didn't have a red hot chile, so I substituted Thai chili sauce & left out the sugar. Still delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Added black bean paste. Used crushed red pepper instead of a chili.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody:
Yeah, yeah, but it works for me. Prep & cook time: 35 min.

Preheat sous vide to 135F. Thaw shrimp in lukewarm water a few minutes with 1/2 tsp each salt, sugar, & baking soda. Drain & remove shells. Cook onion & pepper. Slice 2 cloves frozen oven-roasted garlic. Sous vide shrimp with onion, pepper, & garlic 30 min. When a few min left in SV, cook asparagus. Add soy & sugar. Drain shrimp & veg, add to pan. Add butter, stir to combine flavours, serve.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I used shrimp and scallops, sautéed the scallops first, and set aside before adding aromatics and hot pepper. I also roasted the asparagus in the oven for about 10 minutes at 400° before adding them and scallops back with the shrimp, etc. In addition to low sodium soy sauce and sugar, I added some ginger paste and sesame oil, and it was an instant hit with the fam.  It's definitely a new go-to recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Yes, cooking the shrimp for the 4-5 minutes called for in the recipe would result in tough, overcooked shrimp. Just as you say, cook them for a minute or two, remove them, cook the vegetables, then recombine everything for the last steps

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I'm sure many will think that this is a dumb question, but does this call for frozen cooked or frozen uncooked shrimp?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Frozen uncooked shrimp

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Uncooked and no questions are dumb!  Happy cooking!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: If cooked shrimp were required, the recipe would specify that. Step 3 should be your clue.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I tend to use olive oil in lieu of any other type when I am not flash frying the ingredients.  It imparts more flavor to the dish.  Otherwise the recipe is fine.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: When do you peel them? If they are frozen solid, they can't be peeled. Peel before adding salt when just sorta thawed? I'll try that and let you know...

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: The recipe calls for already peeled and deveined shrimp that has then been frozen..

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Yes, peel after thawing under running water.  It was very easy to remove the shells at that point.  And to the person who commented that the recipe called for peeled shrimp, you might want to look at the recipe's "Tip."

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Recognizing the amount of butter is modest, anyone try it without butter? How’d it go?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: At the point in the recipe where it's added, the butter doesn't contribute anything to the cooking process. It adds flavor and some unctuousness. You could substitute a good quality "finishing" olive oil.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Delicious as written! I used fresh shrimp

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Made this wonderful dish w/ green beans tonight. Sautéed them w/ sweet onion, garlic; added 3 tbs low sodium tamari sauce; hot dried pepper; 1/4 c. fish broth.
