Albeit bland till I salted dish. Added sautéed shrimps/scallops @ end. Stirred & voila. Will make again w/ varied veges & proteins, including sautéed; browned soft tofu. Over rice. Or a grain of your choice.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Followed recipe thru Step 3 and removed from pan (shrimp cook really fast!). Added more oil to the pan to deal with the asparagus because the pan was pretty dry after par-cooking the shrimp with the onion and garlic. Then added sherry, a tsp of freshly grated ginger, and more soy sauce. Ended up adding some white wine since we like sauce on our rice, and this is a fairly dry treatment with only 2T of soy sauce early on. Overall, a good basic recipe, but I've rewritten it for my files. Thanks!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I classify myself as a reluctant shrimp chef. I haven’t really ventured into making shrimp at home very often, and the results haven’t been good. This recipe has made me a convert! I followed most of the directions as written with the exception of adding the vegetable (broccoli in my case, as I had it at hand) prior to sauteeing the shrimp. I also took the suggestion of adding a bit of white wine and ginger, as I love both. This was delicious and easy. Frozen shrimp will now be a pantry staple!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Delicious!; we had smaller shrimp and followed comments to cook asparagus first.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I made with teriyaki and added pineapple and it was way better. Definitely add shrimp last.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Good weekday dinner.  Quick and easy.  I didn't have any red chilies in the house so I used a tablespoon of momofuku Chile crisp instead.   I needed to use up red pepper so I julienned that in as well.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: If you follow this recipe , you are cooking the shrimp for up to five minutes and the asparagus for up to  three.  Why not cook them together until the shrimp is done?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Genevieve Ko is the author of this recipe, and we can assume this is the way she makes it and advises others to make it. Feel free to proceed with a different method.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody:
Thank you for the recipe. After cooking the asparagus for the directed 1-3 minutes I found them to be undercooked. I should have read the comments first to pick up the tips to saute the asparagus first. This would have made a big difference. I did not have the red chilies so I used two tablespoons of Sriracha which added the desired heat.

Beyond that learning, the recipe was delicious and I will make it again.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I made this according to the recipe—with two changes. One was to add the shrimp late so they weren't overcooked, and the other was to use snow peas as the asparagus in the market was miserable. Sadly, we tossed it in the bin after two bites as it was appallingly salty. I won't rate the recipe until I try it again. Next time, I won't salt the shrimp or perhaps rinse more vigorously. There are so many good reviews that it's worth trying again.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I should have read the reviews first. Yes, the asparagus should go in before the shrimp.  The shrimp were tough and overcooked.  :-(  The sauce was delicious, and I'll try the recipe again, making sure not to overcook the shrimp.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Use fresh shrimp and devein. Patting with paper towels is unnecessary. I use an old linen dishtowel if necessary, but you can just let them sit for a couple of minutes.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: You want the shrimp to be completely dry so that they don't steam

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Brining the cooked shrimp made a huge difference in the tenderness, wow!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Reread the instructions, Kitty! The shrimp are brined BEFORE they are cooked…

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Why not fresh shrimp, shelled and deveined?  Worked for me.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: This is a recipe specifically for frozen shrimp. I'm happy that fresh shrimp worked for you... why wouldn't it?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Relatively few of us have easy access to fresh shrimp. I don't, and I live in Florida.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Also, the whole point of this column/these recipes was to be QUICK.  Shelling and deveining fresh shrimp would add considerably more preparation time. If you have that time and have access to good fresh shrimp, go for it -- but this recipe is geared to people who don't have that extra 30 minutes prep time on a week night.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Loved it.  Agree with others….asparagus first otherwise shrimp will be tough.  Also, double the sauce OR add a few other ingredients so it’s not so soy heavy.   Maybe oyster sauce ?  Great dinner.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Good with tweaks.  After adding onion, garlic and chili, only cook for a minute.  Add asparagus and cook  until the asparagus is mostly tender.  Only then add the shrimp, soy sauce and sugar and cook until no longer translucent and it's firm -- in my wok 2-3 minutes if that.  The size of your asparagus (I used the big fat ones) will determine how long it needs to cook.  The fat ones took quite a while to get tender.  By the time the asparagus was tender the onions were overcooked/too charred.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Took the advice to double the sauce. Would add minced or grated ginger with the garlic and onion next time. Served garnished with peanuts and red pepper flakes. Thought the shrimp was overcooked based on color, but the cooked texture was beautiful, I presume due to the salt brine. One of those so easy, I’m surprised it’s this good recipes!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: This is incredibly tasteless. I know it's a basic recipe but you can definitely do better. Add ginger, rice wine vinegar, sesame oil, more chili and maybe even half a cup of stock.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody:
My own riff
- green beans instead of asparagus- boil for a few minutes first 
- red pepper flakes and ginger
- used dark soy sauce (more flavor less salt) with oyster sauce
- no added salt 
- hmm why butter?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I didn't have a red chili, so I used a serrano with seeds. I sauteed the onion, then garlic with the chili, then added the asparagus, followed by the shrimp, and then the soy/sugar. Before the shrimp were completely cooked, I added a cup of cooked rice and the butter. The best, most sophisticated fried rice on earth.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: My asparagus was a bit thicker and required more cooking, so when the shrimp were cooked through I removed them from the pan before adding in the asparagus. When the asparagus was cooked, I then added shrimp back in, added the sauce ingredients, gave it a good mix and served with hot white rice. Start to finish, from frozen shrimp in the freezer to the table, under 30 minutes!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I try not to use sugar in cooking.  Would it be okay to eliminate the sugar from this recipe?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I used pretty thick asparagus (1/2" dia) so I precooked it for several minutes ahead of everything else, put it aside, and then added it back at the specified step. I used one green serrano pepper (no red available) and a shallot because I was out of onion. Definitely going into the rotation.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Best to remove the shrimp once they begin to turn pink and then return to pan after the asparagus cooks to prevent overcooking.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Too salty and boring.  All I tasted was salt and soy sauce.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: As we are currently enjoying a keto lifestyle, I subbed monkfruit for the sugar and served this over riced cauliflower. I also finished the dish with a teaspoon of toasted sesame oil in addition to the butter. Next time, I will think about adding in some minced ginger to up the spice level, but this was just about perfect as it was.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: This was very good with local asparagus and high-quality frozen NC shrimp, served with brown rice - the method for using frozen shrimp is a great tip! I used tamari (less salty) and upped it by another TB, and did add chopped fresh ginger. Next time I’ll spice it up a little more - 1 seeded serrano pepper (all I could get) wasn’t enough heat.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Added two spoons sugar

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Yum! This was one of the easiest and most delicious recipes I’ve tried from this site, and I’ve tried quite a lot! I used turbinado sugar and added the garlic after the onion, added shrimp after the asparagus (as per other comments). Will make again for sure.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Really good.  Added asparagus first as others suggested, and let cook for about 5 minutes before adding shrimp for final 2 minutes.  Used haiga rice (white rice with wheat germ attached), for a healthy and delicious dinner.  Easily feeds four.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: This was only So-So.  It was bland, not enuf sauce…I could go on, but why?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is a good foundation on which to add additional ingredients.  Had some snap peas in-house that paired nicely with the asparagus.  I also added oyster mushrooms.  Lastly paired with a Kerner from Alto Adige.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: So, in essence, you made another recipe?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Made the recipe exactly as outlined and turned out great. Served over rice. Any suggestions for a side dish. What would be good to serve in addition?

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I've made this 3 times already and its always loved! About over cooking the shrimp, I think the key is just have everything chopped and ready to go. I follow the recipe and the shrimp have been perfect, but you need to be fast!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: We liked this quick and easy dish with some fluffy white rice. Read all the comments but pretty much just followed the recipe. Will do it again.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Tried this tonight without the red pepper, just ground black pepper.  Also added 1 tsp fish source.  Next time I will add some minced ginger.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: We made this as written and won’t do it again. It was fine but it lacked flavors. If I put all my ideas to improve the recipe it would be a completely different recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Onion, garlic, chili, soy in the recipe as is: no need for ideas to increase flavors.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Simple, easy, incredible! Better than any take out!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody:
I keep both raw and cooked shrimp in my freezer. It would be extremely helpful if you were to specify if you’re using raw or cooked shrimp in all your recipes. Or should one simply assume you’re always using raw?
Thank you,
Claire

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Well, you brine the shrimp in Step 1 and Step 3 directs you to cook the shrimp. I'd say you should start with raw shrimp. One should simply assume you're always using raw... unless the recipe specifies cooked.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Very nice! Quick and easy and whole family liked it! Only changes were that I added the asparagus before the shrimp and used a small deseeded jalapeño pepper instead of red pepper because that’s what I had.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Agreed with the other commenters on adding the asparagus first, especially with wider stalks. I added a splash of fish sauce and juice from 1/2 a lime. Was a great, quick, end of a long workday kind of meal.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Shrimp will still be frozen. Cook asparagus before shrimp. Option to add rice to stir fry.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Loved this!  Agreed with the comments and added the shrimp last so as not to over-cook.  Also had a bunch of mushrooms on hand and added before anything else.  Delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Fermented black beans, will take this to the next level. Saute asparagus until almost tender, about 2 min. Set aside. Saute lightly brined shrimp until almost cooked, 2-3 min. Set aside. Add one tbsp lightly mashed fermented black beans and 1-2 tsp chopped garlic to a hot wok with some oil. Stir fry about 10 seconds, then add 1 tbsp soy sauce, 1tbsp oyster sauce, a splash of sesame oil, 1 cup of stock, and 1 tsp corn starch in a slurry. Return asparagus and shrimp to wok to reheat.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I added chopped cherry tomatoes in the last five minutes of cooking. Deleted fresh pepper and allowed guests to to add red pepper flakes to suit their taste. Winner.  Delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: quick, tasty and simple.  one upgrade- a couple of squirts of sirachi sauce for additional flavor and spice

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: This is very easy and quick. My garden asparagus is going crazy, so I needed to use some while it was fresh. We had some peeled small shrimp (wild caught US) in the freezer. I paid attention to the comments and put the asparagus in first, though I think the nearly-frozen shrimp wouldn't get rubbery. Anyway, it turned out great.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Super-easy. Used ingredient quantities to recipe.  Flash-charred asparagus 1st & removed to bowl. Then cooked the rest in order, but much less time for the shrimp, added asparagus back when shrimp done.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: certainly simple to cook; Serrano Chile gave it quite a kick.  But overall kind of plain.  Would rather make a real Asian sauce.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: For two weeks now I've been picking asparagus from my 20-year old bed that produces 1/2" thick spears. Another four weeks of picking, then the bed is left to grow into ferns. Meantime, I'm looking for asparagus recipes. This one is particularly easy to make and tasty to eat. I put the frozen shrimp in a bowl of water to thaw. Saves water!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I’m writing from the land of avocados-consider cubing & freezing any ripe ones you can’t eat fresh for future smoothies. I was sad to learn some pea protein powders may contain heavy metals from the soil & with supplements, it is impossible to know which brands are safe. IMO the taste of whey protein powder is icky & yogurt tastes good, so I love this recipe. Ali is the best!

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: Never defrost shrimp under water......let them naturally defrost or it affects the texture and taste.....

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I followed other suggestions and cooked the asparagus first. I had no red chili pepper, so I used a healthy pinch of red pepper flakes.  It turned out great.As the asparagus season disappears, I will look to broccoli as a good substitute.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: I was unsure about this recipe at first because of how simple it is. I was pleasantly surprised at how delicious it was. Next time, I might add water chestnuts for crunch.

author: Genevieve Ko
recipeTitle: Quick Shrimp and Asparagus Stir-Fry Recipe
commentBody: This was tasty and easy to make. I flipped the shrimp and asparagus to keep the shrimp from bring over done as most readers commented. Definitely would make it again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I make full proof white sauce. Cook butter and flour together until raw taste gone. A couple of minutes. The all at once add all the milk. Cold. Right out of the refrigerator. Turn heat to medium and whisk until thick. It never gets lumps. Easiest method. Unorthodox but dependable

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Nobody ever told me that my white sauce would be lumpy if I didn't heat the milk and add it gradually to the roux, so I never did. I do as mary does and always have a smooth sauce. Reminds me of some of the unnecessary techniques people insist upon for hard-cooked eggs.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @mary substituting Wondra flour will eliminate lumps

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @mary when do you add the cheese?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: this is a perfect base, but the addition of smoked paprika and a generous squirt of yellow mustard elevates it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is basically the recipe my mother taught me about 60 years ago!  My suggestion is to not use pre-shredded cheese.  Pre-shredded cheese is treated with an anti-sticking agent which causes it to be gloppy when you make the cheese sauce. Hand shredded cheese is best.    Another few hints:  take the pan off the heat when you first add the milk and stir to avoid lumps.  And don't skip the nutmeg, also ground white pepper as opposed to black pepper.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Chris B. You can rinse the shredded cheese to remove those. Works pretty well!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: White sauce is one of the most satisfying bits of kitchen alchemy -- and you can flavor it up and use it in place of canned soup in tons of casseroles. You really don't need to heat the milk, though. I've been doing it for years with milk straight out of the fridge.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The best Mac and cheese comes from mixing in lots of cheeses. The last one I made had cheddar, Gruyère, Gouda, havarti dill, a jalapeño jack, and some Brie. My husband said it was the best ever. I said he’d never get it again! The aftermath of parties makes the best Mac and cheese.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I always add a dash of hot sauce and dried mustard to the béchamel before adding the cheese. It adds just enough zip to brighten the dish up.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Why warm up the milk? Credit to Chef John: "hot roux, cold milk, no lumps!"

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Love the mix of cheeses. But instead of American, why not go ALL IN and use that classic of mystery-cheese goodness: VELVEETA! Soooooo creamy-licious! So orange! So perfect! I grew up eating Velveeta grilled cheese sandwiches (with Duke's mayo, because of course). This venerable "processed-cheese food" is a devicive subject, I know--but if you love it, you love it--and in that case you should definitely put it in your mac and cheese!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @jnfalveysc I lived on this in college and you just made me very hungry!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
@jnfalveysc i grew up on it as well. 

The reason it works so well is because Velvetta has sodium citrate in it - which allows any type of cheese to be smooth and silky. I use a little when i mix in pepper iack. It is great.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I make white sauce in the microwave, no other pans required. Put the milk in a microwave-able bowl and whisk in the flour. Cut the butter in chunks and add to the bowl. Microwave for 3-4 minutes, then pull it out and whisk until smooth. Zap,again for 1-2 minutes, whisk again. Repeat with decreasing amounts of time. I don’t usually make as much as this recipe, so I make it in a four cup measure. Just make sure the container is large enough that the milk mixture won’t boil over.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: No need to junk up a second pot.  Cook pasta and drain.  To that same pot make your roux, add milk and cheese, return drained pasta to pot, stir, then thin with reserved pasta water as needed.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Try dumping the cooked pasta into the white sauce, letting that cook and thicken, and then add the cheese. Adding the cheese last seems to keep the sauce from breaking and separating.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I was taught how to make a classic white sauce in 1952 by my 6th grade cooking teacher, and I've never since varied from using it as a base for baked mac and cheese. Grate some of that cheddar and layer it on top before baking. It creates a crispy browned topping that people fight over.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is stove top macaroni and cheese... not baked.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This looks good. I have a faster cheat version that is not gourmet but good enough for a quick meal!  No measurements included, do it to your taste.   Add a cup or 2 of cooked macaroni to pot in which you've melted a few TBs of butter, stir well.  Keep on medium low heat.   Add a TB or 2 of heavy whipping cream, stir.   Add some shredded parmesan or romano to taste, and torn slices of sharp white Vermont or New York cheddar.  Stir till cheese is melted. Voila!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Leftover pimento cheese would be great in the mix.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Been making this for years! Heating the milk in the microwave is an extra and unnecessary step, IMO.  Nutmeg, yes; mustard, yes; a dash of cayenne, yes for me; onion powder or onion salt or grated onion, definitely. And microwaving some seasoned panko with butter and stirred every 30 seconds until toasted to sprinkle on top is clutch. (I make extra and keep in the refrigerator for leftovers.) The sauce does seize up as it sits, so don’t be afraid to use more bechamel than you think you should.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: grated onion sauteed in the butter also adds a very nice flavour.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Take a look at Kenji’s 3-ingredient Mac and cheese. 
Equal parts pasta, evaporated milk, and cheese. One pot glory. 
We made it with 1/2 cheddar 1/2 jalapeño Jack plus a dash of tumeric for that lovely day glow color. It did not disappoint.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I add cream cheese if I have a brick to use up. It adds a certain "creaminess".

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I'm really REALLY disappointed with this! I read all of the reviews - raving - and just SOUP! I wish I could add a photo. Something wrong with the ratio for the bechamel part.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Sue P agree mine is soupy too

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Madeleine I also got soup when I tried this, I am wondering if it’s too much milk for the amount of cheese?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was..... not great. Made way too much sauce, not very much flavor (even with the addition of nutmeg and mustard), think thick, gloppy, macaroni soup. Will try a different recipe next time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I have been making this recipe for about 10 years almost exactly as written, except for nutmeg. I add peas to get some greens in there.  And I bake it in a casserole with seasoned breadcrumbs on top to get the crunchy bits everyone loves.  As for velveeta, well, to each his/her/their own, but I only feed my family and friends actual food, not preprocessed garbage.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I learned how to make stove top macaroni and cheese as a kid.  The comments about using cold milk are right on and the way I learned it.  For your cheese choices, a mixture is good but you are missing a main component: Velveeta.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My mother used Velveeta, too.  Then we used the Velveeta boxes to store little toys.boxes

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I love adding white pepper with the black, along with a wee hint of garlic (even garlic salt is a nice subtle way to go) and some marscapone to the cheese blend for creaminess.  Bam!  Just scrummy.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Maybe non-traditional, but I use corn starch to thicken the milk instead of the flour roux. It's quick and the sauce never lumps.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Incredibly bland and grainy.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
This didnt really get thick for me. Then I went and checked my moms recipe from my childhood and it was the same roux for 2 cups of milk! I think 3c might ultimately be the best ratio. My moms was a bit "dry"/thick, this was too thin.

Also, like mary, I've also typically poured cold milk into the roux and its never broken on me. Although, Julia Child does call for warming the milk...

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This recipe is perfect as is, although if you want to add paprika and mustard, have at it. I make it all the time with whatever cheese I have on hand it’s always delicious.  I used to make the whole béchamel thing too, standing over the stove stirring and dirtying up multiple pots.  No more! This recipe is quicker and fool proof and, trust me, your family and friends won’t know the difference because they’ll have scarfed it down so fast.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Completely bland even with added dry mustard, onion and garlic granules and cayenne.  Added green Tabasco at table

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: In my opinion, macaroni and cheese should be bland: pasta and melty cheese. Good pasta and good cheese. Why mess with simplicity?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I agree it can be bland if the cheeses are bland.  I throw a shake of sriracha in the mix . And then add what I want at the table which is more than my family can handle.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I purchase   several cheese ends at the deli counter. Not only are they a bargain, but add a variety of flavors to my white sauce,  enhancing the taste of my mac and cheese.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is brilliant and just the thing after a tough week and not having seen the sun in a longer time than I can remember. I thought, "That's a LOT of cheese" but it isn't and since I live in Luxembourg (Europe) I had some Gruyere that I added to the cheddar. Yum!  Do add the nutmeg. Essential.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This recipe came out fantastic and easy. My girlfriend rated 10/10. Modifications I recommend: if you add 12 grams of sodium citrate you can skip American cheese and it will still create the smoothest cheese sauce you've ever tasted (sodium citrate is the emulsifying ingredient that keeps American cheese smooth when melted); add some cayenne powder to the sauce for some kick; add some Dijon mustard for more complex flavor; add peas for extra nutrition, flavor, and texture.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Dan Totally agree. Sodium Citrate is the key missing ingredient in the recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Unfortunately, we did not like this. The sauce was too “floury” and not very cheesy. Maybe my measuring spoons aren’t accurate.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The best mac and cheese recipe remains that of Jim Thorpe. It eliminates the white sauce entirely, thus eliminating the gluey, gritty downsides of a bechamel.  Instead, it uses only condensed mild, cheddar and seasoning with the macaroni all slowly combined in a few stages in the oven.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Maybe I missed something here, but who boils elbow macaroni for 3-5 minutes?  7-9 is more like it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this a day ahead and then reheated by baking it in a casserole dish with a cover on. It was better than the first day. The top browned and the whole thing was delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Just made this and it was not great. Perhaps I will try this.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: 1 teaspoon of sodium citrate added to the milk will act as an emulsifier and make the cheese creamy. (I’ve also read that throwing a single slice of American cheese into the mix will do the same but I NEVER buy American cheese so I don’t know if that’s true.)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this car camping--as written--w mixture of leftover cheese from fridge so we wouldn't have to stop at a store on the way out of town.  Took less than 30 min to make and tasted like a luxury as we dined around the campfire.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Didn't work for me. Its grainy and just not good. I was looking for a velvety sauce like in the photo. I made half the recipe and its wayyyyyy too much sauce.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Did your teenage daughter just break up with her boyfriend? Cast list posted? 3rd round of SAT scores arrive? Whether you are celebrating or consoling, this is the dish to make. We use dilatini instead of traditional macaroni because it lowers the pasta to sauce ratio. Everyone is happy!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Useless recipe. Watery sauce. Calls for far too much milk.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Love the additions of hot sauce, white pepper and grated onion to the roux. Cheddar has a tendency to separate, get oily and grainy. I’ve learned to use a bit of Monterey Jack or Velveeta (don’t judge), which are good melters, to keep it together.  Also, use a little less water for your pasta and the water will be extra starchy for velvety (Velveety) smoothness.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: And six pots later, the meal is done in 25 minutes but then there is 30 minutes of dishes to wash.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Two. Two pots.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Actually, it's three pots: Step 1, large pot; Step 2, microwavable pan; Step 3, another large pot.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Frigify it! Got a half an onion? Dice and saute in (2x) the butter before adding the flour. Got any salumi? Dice up some of that and toss it in after stirring in the cheese, to warm it up. Better yet, got any bacon or pancetta? Fry some of that first, reserve a tsp of oil and drop one of the butter before saute of onions or addition of flour. Crumble back into sauce after cheese. Got any pickles? Serve on the side. (I used pickled jalapeños and onions!)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: For a stronger flavor, make this with aged cheddar and aged Parmesan. An alternative is Roquefort.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: way too milky

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I should’ve listened to the comments. The cheese “sauce” really just made cheesy noodle soup. Wouldn’t recommend.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can I use what I have on hand for this which is 2% milk? Should I adjust with more butter?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: am I crazy or does this recipe use 3 pots?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Love this recipe. Added about 2 teaspoons of grainy mustard.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is pretty much my standard Mac and cheese. Velveeta, just do it.  My latest favorite extra cheese is manchego.  It’s perfect.  If you’ve got time, crumble ritz on top and pop it in the oven.  The best part of all, this reheats perfectly.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: A squirt of any kind of mustard makes a huge difference, though my preference is spicy brown mustard.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I grew up on this. My mom taught me to add Beau Monde seasoning (like celery salt) and a splash of worcheshire. I pour in the milk cold and it works fine. If we have extra time and are feeling fancy we pour the results into a casserole dish topped with shredded sharp cheddar and bake it for a cheesy crusty top.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Used vegan butter, soy milk, and vegan cheese. Delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: That’s an analog. It’s not macaroni and cheese. This recipe is for macaroni and cheese. It’s apples and oranges.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Make it this way and have been since the 60s and my mother turned that job over to me.  Can't beat and oldie/goodie

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I learned to make mac and cheese this way.  Bake or broil if you want a crispy top.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I live in remote Sicily and use Emmental and Gruyère when I can’t find Cheddar.  MD stocks both and DECO also stocks both and  occasionally has cheddar. ALDI stocks the first two.  Italian processed cheese, sold in packs of slices is just like American cheese. I’m going to try it. Thank you for sharing the recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Just use heavy whipping cream and you don't need the flour, butter, or milk. Add either monteray jack or American to the cheddar and the sauce won't break. No lumps possible.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My kids prefer their mac & cheese with a creamy sauce, So I skip the roux and just warm 4 cups of heavy cream, then add cheese, a little nutmeg, and/or Tabasco. It’s a bit rich, but it doesn’t set up, as the roux based version tends to do.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My mother made stovetop mac and cheese too.  She would dice onions and green peppers and cook them with the pasta.  She always added a bit of Worcestershire sauce too.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Instant Pot: Macaroni, water (just enough to peek through the noodles), 4 min Manual, Quick Release, toggle vent if it sounds like it's clogging/spitting. Add half Velveeta/American, half cheddar/whatever, splash of milk, white pepper, hot sauce, mustard, stir.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: High pressure or low pressure?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Okay, I give you 5 stars, but only because this is how I originally learned to make Mac and Cheese some 50-60 years ago. I can't even remember how I learned it, my Mom, or was it home economics (where I  learned to make a white sauce) in junior high, or was it from my first copy of Fanny Farmer Cookbook. I don't know, but what I do know is this was what made my husband and kids happy whenever it was on the table. My 3 kids are now adults, and they have continued to make it exactly like this.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Cold milk into hot roux,
Says everyone on here because it is true.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I do that too but I whisk in roughly half a cup at a time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I think I’ll stick with Kenji’s 6-6-6 Mac & Cheese on Serious Eats. Talk about easy and delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Add some mustard and a little Worcestershire sauce to your roux. That's how my Mom did it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Same recipe my mum taught me too but with 1/2-1 tsp both dried mustard (or even plain yellow mustard) and Worcestershire sauce!! The Worcestershire sauce adds even more umami and some zip. Hot sauce is a good suggestion too or maybe some cayenne.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Definitely a classic. My family’s take is to dice about half an onion first and sauté it in the butter until it’s translucent then make the roux and add the milk. Also add some mustard powder and Worcestershire sauce. The Worcestershire sauce I don’t think I see mentioned anywhere else! It really adds to it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Easily the most disappointing recipe I've cooked from the NYT.

Soupy and bland. I'm shocked by the 4 out of 5 stars for this.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The sauce needed more than 2 minutes to cook. I have another recipe with better proportions and cook times.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this with mostly cheddar, some Monterey and 2 oz of Velveeta. I used cold milk, as many of the commenters suggest. The flavor was good, but the sauce was grainy. Any ideas how to prevent that?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: So disappointing. Followed the recipe exactly and at the end the sauce broke, resulting in gooey lumps of cheddar. Inedible.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: PS Added Dijon and paprika before adding pasta

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I followed the directions exactly and it was perfect. Very easy.  No lumps.  My 2 year old granddaughter and 72 year old husband both loved it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This Mac and cheese goes really well with buffalo sauce.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The pasta needed to cook longer. The sauce separated and I had to add milk and butter and whisk it a lot to try to fix it. Not fun. I'll stick with America's Test Kitchen's recipe (and make sure to stock up on evaporated milk).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: i made this recipe exactly as written and came up with way too much sauce…i don’t get it

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Very nice comfort food. I used almond milk because it’s what I had on hand. Although the cheese sauce was a tad thin at first, it thickened up. I felt there was a wee bit more sauce than necessary, so I added a little more pasta. It worked out well. I want to add that it’s easy to make just ¼ of the recipe and share the very generous portion.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This recipe was to serve 4-6. With the soupy sauce I’ve ended up with at least 10 servings. This recipe may not have gone through usual testing.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: So I’ll continue my search for a go-to Mac n cheese recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Ina Garten and Martha have great mac & cheese recipes but they are baked.  Deelish

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: A perfect base recipe. Never thought to thin mac and cheese with pasta water. Used all cheddar, hand shredded. Added a sprinkle of salt pepper mustard powder, a glug of hot sauce and a garlic clove that I forgot to cook with so just added at the end when I was mixing the sauce with the pasta. Complain, go ahead. It sounded tasteless, so I doctored it, but I followed the technique and it was terrific. Will make again for sure. Will doctor it too. The luxury of having older kids who can handle more flavor.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was perfectly delicious and fast! No pasta water needed right away. Used cheddar, manchego, jalapeño havarti and a small remnant of velveeta. Creamy as can be and mouthwateringly yummy! Thanks!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Love NYT Cooking & typically don’t leave recipe comments but felt this one needed some positive feedback in light of all the negative reviews. It was very good. After reading through the comments I used the one pot suggestion and cooked a 12 oz box of Jovial gluten free macaroni till done & drained. (Scaled the recipe to match the 12 oz box and used King Arthur GF AP flour). Added a large grated shallot to the butter. White sauce came together perfectly (used room temp milk) and threw in a good dollop of dijon. Probably the type of cheese one uses makes a difference. Organic Valley makes a Fiesta American version in block form (11 oz) that was perfect. Melted well and end result was delicious for what it was….a quick, easy, stovetop Mac n Cheese. I think using partial pimento cheese would work really well too. Another plus, easy to scale the recipe in half if needed….

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used a nice sharp gouda for most of the cheese. Definitely not pre shredded. Also added a 1/2 cup cream cheese for creaminess.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Fit the bill for fast and easy comfort food. I used sharp cheddar, soft goat cheese, a havarti slice, and a handful of grated parm. And 2% milk. Lovely.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Instead of nutmeg, I add horseradish or horseradish-mustard.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I am a very experienced cook and I love mac and cheese.
All the comments about lumps; lumps happen.
An immersion blender is my favorite go to when lumps happen, even to experienced cooks.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’ve been making this for years, through husband, children, and grandchildren. Never used the roux ( although I do for baked Mac ‘n cheese).  Unnecessary.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
If I’m lazy, I’ve even cooked the macaroni in whole milk with a little water, stirring frequently. It makes its own thickened bechamel-y textured sauce and you’ve only used one pan. Then you can add butter and cheese, ground mustard, whatever your heart desires. I usually add lots of black pepper and garnish with salted chopped tomato. 

But nothing is as good as my mother’s stovetop macaroni and cheese. And I just can’t duplicate it to save my life. Gosh, I miss that.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: How on earth are you supposed to beat the milk IN the microwave? Crawl inside with it?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: You are only heating the milk in the microwave. Then adding it to the roux in the pot on the stove.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Same way you make this ON the stove top.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: For proper creaminess, you need Velveeta.  1/4 lb will do. Add a small can of stewed tomatoes and some Colemans dry mustard and its perfect.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: A staple (!) around here is lobstah mac and cheese…. Add as much cooked knuckle and tail meat as you can afford. For perfection, then top with a thick layer of over-buttered bread crumbs or coarse panko and broil on low until perfect.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I make a version of this with diced onion, dried thyme and a tablespoon of tomato paste, which gives the dish an orange coloring and extra bit of umami. Plus a sprinkle of bread crumbs on top to put under the broiler for a few mins.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Not a descriptive recipe.    How about an actual recipe with actual amounts of cheese and the breakdown of what cheeses and precisely how much.  Otherwise your recipe that is worth CELEBRATING does not have any legs

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: You're correct: the recipe does not give a precise amount of each variety of cheese. The recipe leaves the mix of cheeses up to you. It specifies one pound total. The intro to the recipe says "this is a great way to use up any odds and ends of cheese in your fridge. " I made this recipe and found it quite celebratory!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Why don't you just follow the recipe?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Get yourself a really good aged cheddar. Then some salted butter and that's all you need for an easy mac and cheese. 1 pound of cheese to 1 pound of elbows. Toss the butter and cheese in after you have cooked the elbows. Stir until the cheese has melted. Season with cracked black pepper or a sprinkle of nutmeg and you're done.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I agree with Joan , mustard dry or wet and a bit of hot sauce is the perfect thing to give this a lift -

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Has anyone ever tried cooking the pasta IN the (lower volume than water) milk and then adding the cheese right in?  I want to try this but am curious if anyone has been there done that.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I've followed the recipe from that test kitchen show that's on  American TV for quick stove top mac and cheese.  They cook the pasta in the milk plus some water.  Very easy and very tasty.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Yes! I had found a recipe that said to do this and it does work! You should give it a try. I haven't tried this particular recipe that way. You can read about process on https://www.gimmesomeoven.com/best-stovetop-mac-cheese/

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I make this with skim milk and even sometimes low fat shredded cheese.  Due to the alchemy of white sauce it still tastes rich, creamy  and delicious.  And is slightly better for you

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Excellent recipe. Our family likes this even better with sautéing chopped onion (1 slice of large onion) at the start. To prevent the cheese from curdling at the end:
* When most of liquid is gone, turn off heat.
* Add 1/2 grated cheddar, place on top. Stir after a minute or so. 
* Add remaining grated cheese and stir.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: One other note:  to make this gluten-free, use Ancient Harvest macaroni - tastes the same as regular macaroni.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I would add at least one teaspoon of cayenne!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Craving mac and cheese, but am newly on a gluten-free, dairy-free, low FODMAP diet for a while due to an intestinal infection. I can try to adapt this, but I’m not an expert with the GF/DF ingredient exchanges. Does anyone have a favorite recipe to suggest, or is it an impossible dream for now?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: You might want to check some of the bloggers out there. Minimalist Baker does a lot of vegan and GF recipes. There may be some tips on types and brands of substitutions you can leverage and adapt to this recipe. There are GF flours and plant-based cheeses fairly available that would work. The challenge is the American cheese ingredient, so emulate the reviewer who noted that including a small amount of sodium citrate in the (oat) milk would help with emulsification in lieu.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Check out my simple recipe below, I make it with Jovial cassava flour elbows, which is gluten free (Jovial also makes rice pastas, they are pretty good).   I omit the flour from my quick mac and cheese, as I do a quick and easy "cheat" version.  But King Arthur Measure for Measure gluten free flour is a great substitute for wheat flour for just about any cooking/baking purpose.  I also make individual gluten free pizzas with Siete cassava flour tortillas.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: PS - sorry, I saw "gluten free" and blew right past your other requirements!  So my recipe would not be suitable for you. btw for an intestinal infection you might want to try taking black cumin seed oil (Life Extension makes a good one, capsules) with meals.  It works miracles for some infections such as h pylori.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I live in a rural town in Italy and can’t find any Monterey, cheddar or American… suggestions for substitutions?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Shredded parmesan or romano have a similar bite to cheddar, I use it in my version and it tastes great.  Could pair with a milder cheese.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Ask the cheese shop for their tangiest cheese.  It won't be the same as using a good cheddar but will give you a flavorful sauce.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Buy whatever melty cheese is available in your area.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Add some buttered bread crumbs on top!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Add some smoked paprika for extra flavor & to further color the sauce.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is what my mother made back in the 1950's.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: For those of us who find Mac and cheese a bit bland, trying making it with a high quality habanero cheddar, like Cabot’s or Boars Head. The fat in cheese blunts the heat.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I sometimes add some blanched broccoli and some julienned ham to my Mac and Cheese.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is wonderful! I used mozzarella, Fontina, and Swiss. And, swapped in whole grain macaroni to make it diabetic friendly.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: A splash of Worcestershire sauce in Mac and cheese is lovely.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: More butter, much less milk, and thicken with grated Parmesan instead of flour. Two thirds cheddar or Jack to a third Gouda, Swiss or gruyere is my ideal flavor mix. You can definitely embellish with bacon or sausage or chunks of cooked ground meat, and sauteed mushrooms really cooked down in butter. I melt the butter, stir in the cooked pasta and let it soak up the butter, then just throw in grated cheese and milk and stir, stir, stir, add more milk as needed and finally stir in the Parm.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Yes, milk all at once straight out of the frig! Exactly like my old Betty Crocker Cookbook says to do!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Throwback recipe for the Boomer generation!!  This is my mother's recipe and I have used for the last 60+ years.  I agree that mustard (dry or any favorite) does give it a little zip.  I also never heat the milk and never thin it with pasta water - extra milk will do the job.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this exactly as written. I was initially leery of the huge amount of milk and cheese for one pound of macaroni, but it was perfect. As for flavor additions, I tossed in dry mustard, cayenne pepper, a dash of truffle salt, and smoked paprika (as well as the nutmeg suggested). Half sharp cheddar, half münster. Delicious! But "4-6 servings"? Maybe if you are feeding linebackers! This big pot of noodles could easily serve 12, as a side dish.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this last night and it was easy and fast. Delicious too!  I added a little Cayenne.  It makes a lot.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: great recipe, I also, did not heat the milk up... as... it's fine to add in cold... but I did do it a little at a time... I added other spices, but that's all preference.. Cooking is art! You know what you like! :)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Simmer milk with half an onion, a bay leaf and a few peppercorns. Strain and add hot milk to cold roux or cool milk and add to hot roux. A little Tabasco added before serving is efficacious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My father taught me to add a few drops of Worcestershire to the cheese sauce right before the macaroni.  This little kick of unami really adds something special.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: You can make this even easier: after cooking the pasta, drain lightly, making sure the pasta is still quite wet, and add back to the pan. Add butter, shredded cheese, crème fraiche, and stir like crazy over medium low heat till everything is melted. Perfect and delicious! I do this for single servings as well as for larger portions, eyeballing ingredients.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Classic "top of stove" macaroni & cheese!  As to the step of heating the milk in the microwave - I made this basic recipe for 40 years with cold milk -soooo much stirring and waiting for the milk to heat up & start to reduce.  After a little research, I have now been heating the milk in the microwave for the past 10 years and it is a game changer - especially when you are hungry after a long day and just want some comfort food.  Heating the milk makes it all come together in a jiffy.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My Mac recipe involves no flour or butter: cook pasta of choice, when done, add a good splash of skim milk (my choice, could use whole or 2%), and two slices of Velveeta, torn into pieces. Stir until the cheese is melted, then add about 2/3 cup shredded cheddar. Velveeta adds the creaminess and shredded cheddar adds the cheesy flavor. Be sure to season well with salt and pepper. I sometimes add frozen broccoli and cauliflower towards the end of cooking the pasta.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this with a combination of cheddar and gouda, but it was somewhat bland. I think Parmesan would be better.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Using old, or extra old, cheddar is better IMHO ... plus other cheese(s) you're using. Really adds depth.of flavour.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Use sweetened condensed milk instead of milk-really yummy!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Sweetened condensed?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Sorry but why would you add sugar via sweetened condensed milk or in another form?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Maybe she means evaporated?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: You can mix flour, butter cheese all in one glass container. Then add the milk and Mix thoroughly. Microwave for two minutes, whisk really well and keep microwaving until everything is cooked out

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I cook the pasta first (undercooking it by about a minute) and, while it's draining, make the sauce. That means I use one pot plus a measuring cup (I know by sight how much butter to add).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
This is how I’ve liked my Mac and cheese since I was a kid. Creamy and cheesy. Not baked with breadcrumbs and dried out. 

And only cheddar will do.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I still chuckle at recipe specifics.  I grew up with a mother that told me, "Add some of that until it looks right."

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used half cheddar, a quarter Colby Monterey blend, and a mix of Swiss, American and Parmesan for the rest of the pound of cheese. Added a teaspoon Dijon and a teaspoon yellow mustard. I’ll use this recipe from now on!  Only mistake was putting it in a slow cooker on warm waiting for the guests to arrive. The macaroni did get a bit too soft. Next time i would reheat it quickly. That didn’t stop everyone from having seconds. It was very good !

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Try undercooking the pasta if you're going to keep it cooking for a while (keeping it warm constitutes cooking).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I first made "from scratch" mac 'n' cheese in 1987 from an easy recipe I found in "Today's Parent" (Canadian magazine). It was topped with breadcrumbs and browned. We (I was married with kids by then) later omitted the breadcrumbs and adjusted the components as our family grew. But always more cheese.
It's different every time I make it now depending on how much I want to make and the type of cheeses I have in the house. I at least double the recipe ostensibly to freeze some...though I never do.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Couple of minor suggestions: 1) When cooking the pasta, i add vegetables, e.g., chopped onions & peppers, dried mushrooms. 2) when mixing the cheese sauce with the pasta, i add some anchovy fillets. 3) When thoroughly combined, I grind peppercorns over the top, & sprinkle some fresh scallions.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I added Dijon mustard, bay leaves, garlic and Cajun seasoning!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Great stovetop mac and cheese recipe!  I used full English cheddar grated from the block (no gruyere blend) because I needed to use it.  The sauce was a bit runny for me at first.  I was cooking for a lunch party and was in a hurry, so I sprinkled about a tablespoon of cornstarch over everything and let it boil for just long enough to activate the cornstarch.  It thickened up nicely and made a great mac and cheese!  I figure the thinness was because of the cheese I used.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: "Pre-shredded cheese will also make this thicker..." because it's dusted with flour or cornstarch so it doesn't stick together in the package.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I have made several versions of macaroni and cheese and was excited to make this but disappointed and will take off my favorite list.  I followed the directions closely  and used some American but found the finished dish a bit "grainy" and my husband said not sharp enough even though I used mostly a sharp cheddar.  Will go back to my other recipe.  it Nf

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I followed this recipe to the letter and found it perfect. I love that it made a smooth sauce reminiscent of my childhood eating boxed mac but without needing Velveeta, American cheese, or cream cheese. Curious to see how my 3-year-old niece would think it compares to the boxed kind!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
They're not kidding that this one's for kids - it's really bland.

Too much sauce to begin with (feel free to use the whole 1.1 lb. bag of macaroni), in which I simmered parm reg rinds (as suggested), then added way more cheddar, salt, pepper and finally, nutritional yeast.

Only then did it taste like something worth making from scratch.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Make a 1.5lbs of pasta for the amount of sauce this recipe yields

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Great basic recipe, once mastered you can have so much fun with it!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Great, classic Mac & Cheese recipe! One benefit of this version is it can be modified to be a little healthier without losing all the comforting goodness, e.g., I reduced the butter and cheese slightly and it was still creamy delicious! I know, not as cheesy, but hey, I felt satisfied.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made the roux and melted the cheese in first to get the creaminess of the sauce (and seasonings) just right before adding in the macaroni. I used a combination of whatever cheeses I had on hand that I wanted to use up including smoked cheddar, gouda and havarti. They were already cubed so I just cut them into smaller pieces. Worked pretty well. I'm eating it now for lunch. :)

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Reflecting on Dorothy Raviele's comments below about using several different cheeses...A few years ago I did just that, cleaning out the cheese drawer.  Well-aged Wisconsin cheddar, French Gruyere, Dutch Gouda, Maytag Blue, Parmesan Reggiano and Pecorino.  In all about $75 worth of cheese.  

The result?

Tasted like the Kraft Dinner of my childhood.  Still good, though.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Awesome dish

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made with 2 parts low moisture mozzarella to 1 part truffle pecorino for a really great side dish

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: A couple TBS of chopped pickled jalapeños and a shake of cayenne can’t hurt. And won’t.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Disappointing. Made as written. It is macaroni soup. How can this be?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Sue P that’s how it comes out as written

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’m so confused, are people making this recipe as written? I had to use two times the amount of flour after remaking the béchamel before adding the cheese… It would’ve been watery.  Also, even then there was way too much cheese sauce and not enough pasta :(. If I made this recipe again, I would use 4 tablespoons flour and 3 cups of milk with the same amount of pasta

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is easy and very nice, but I have a couple of caveats for future cooks: don't use fresh mozzarella as one of your cheese, it will make the sauce way too stringy. And don't use the "calamari" pasta shape, it's not a good fit with the sauce. Other than that, I enjoyed it. My cheese mixture included a 4-cheese Mexican blend, Parmesan, fresh mozzarella, and Fontina. Delicious (though stringy).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I will add--yes, there is significantly too much sauce for the amount of pasta. I thought it was just my calamari-shaped pasta that was at fault, but now I see others had a similar issue.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: We used just over 3 cups of milk and that made plenty of sauce.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Just made this for first time as written— only addition was two tablespoons of dried shallots mixed into the pot at the end. Delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I grew up in a family in a single mom situation where I always thought "Mac and cheese" came out of a box.
  I liked it, I still do,
 Once they began selling boxed Mac and cheese with a flavor pack of yellow liquid goo I thought Mac and cheese had found it's apex.
These homemade concoctions brought to pot lucks or family gatherings were dry, bland half measures.
Noodle casserole I guess it was called.
I've come to discover that homemade Mac and cheese is better

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is how my mom made it, she added cubed cheddar cheese folded in at the end to create lovely pockets of cheesy goodness. Give it a short warm up in the oven after the stovetop preparation.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Maybe it was me, but this was a gloopy mess. I'll stick with my slow cooker recipe in the future

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Easy, quick to make, and tasty.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I thought this would be the mac and cheese for me.  It was not. I halved the recipe and followed every direction. However, the cheese sauce was too liquid, I tried adding more cheese, added the pasta but It never turned into a smooth creamy sauce and was disappointing.   Trying to adapt it - I put some in an ovenware dish, baking on high heat - did not help. Added bread crumbs to the top and broiled for 5 minutes... It was okay. Not sure where I went wrong.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This recipe really puts dinner on the table. If all those other elaborate Mac and Cheese recipes are big-budget, multi-production number Broadway shows, this recipe is a group of friends gathering around a piano belting show tunes. I only use one pot, and I have learned to save pasta water before draining. And like your friends around the piano, sometimes someone will be off key.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Cut in half. Quick weeknight version. Added Velveeta as we had it from previous experiment. Served over mound of broccoli. Cured Monday blues.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Followed directions closely, but ended up with a rather bland and disappointing result - despite doctoring it up with plenty of spices and using a good mix of cheeses. It wasn’t terrible…just not a keeper.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I prefer without the flour, and my family prefers extra sharp cheddar. You need relatively fresh, and higher quality cheese, NOT pre shredded to avoid it becoming rubbery. The flour does help with the rubbery stuff, but isn't necessary with good cheese.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Try adding a few Tablespoons of cottage cheese! It increases protein and adds flavor.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Wow. 784 calories per serving. And kids love it. Maybe cut back a bit on the one pound of cheese to make it a bit healthier.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: make the white sauce in the microwave.  See Barbara Kafka.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Kenji Lopez-Alt’s mac & cheese is easier and is perfect. Macaroni, evaporated milk, pasta water & cheese. One pot. Any cheese. Which makes for fab combos!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Love this recipe, but I just add the cold milk all at once and stir. Works fine. If I have time I bake it for 30 minutes at 350. Adds a nice little crunch. Sometimes I add caramelized onions.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I like to include as much minced garlic as my family will tolerate, added into the butter before the flour -- like 5 cloves -- plus a small squirt of brown mustard

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Any French person will tell you that the secret to a successful Béchamel is cold milk! The thermic choc of cold milk poured on the warm "roux" (butter and flour cooked together) is how the sauce thickens.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I scaled the recipe to 0.5, for 4 servings. worked out great! Texture just like a full batch.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I agree with others that this turned out really bland. I followed the directions but it just was kind of grainy and blah. Maybe it was the type of cheese I used. it was so bland my kids wouldn't even eat it.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made 100% according to instructions. The sauce to noodle ratio was WAY off. Overly cheesy… even for the teenager!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My kids love this recipe. What I do is saute broccoli, spinach and frozen peas to the mix to sneak in veggies. I also sometimes add cubed ham steak to the mix for extra flavor and protein.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Too much milk. Reduce to 3 cups

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I make the crumble using equal parts butter, flour, oats and brown sugar.  This can be multiplied ad infinitum, pressed into a slab and frozen.  Then when I need it for the dessert, I just cut off a piece and chop it into crumble size. 

Now, for the fruit - frozen berries are NOT too wet.  Don't thaw them unless you like mush.  Toss any combo of berries with cornstarch and a little grated lemon rind, then pour into oiled baking dish. Top with frozen crumble and bake till bubbling.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @Rose K Very clever! I’m going to do this as fruit crisp is our #1 dessert.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Super easy and helps with a group of people. I used regular rolled oats and they worked fine.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This is similar to my cherry crumble recipe, I simmer a little brandy with the fruit.  Give it a try!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Having made crisps over many years, I have evolved to preparing the fruit and putting it in to the oven before assembling the topping and adding it to the dish. The fruit juices generally thicken by the time the topping has browned lightly.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this on a cold, snowy day. The topping is great, esp with both pecans and walnuts. And the cooked fruit juices a perfect consistency. But the amount of sugar mixed in the fruit was way over the top. Ice cream or sweetened whipped cream would have made us gag. I was using apple and pears, which are naturally sweet, so a quarter or a third of the sugar called for would suffice. Suggest you adjust sugar wisely to fruit used.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can you use regular oats.  I never buy instant.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Rebecca - you ask if regular oats can be subbed in for instant.  Like you I never buy instant oats and while I haven't tried the regular oats in this recipe, haven't made it yet, I often make fruit crumbles during the year and when I use oats I always use Bob's Red Mill regular oats and it works great.  When I make this, I am going to use those regular oats.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: @dimmerswitch I agree: the recipe doesn't state why quick-cooking oats are used, which I never buy, and I intend to use regular oats as well.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: How many cups is 2# approximately?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I have frozen peaches from my peach tree already cut up. What's the best way to make this with frozen fruit? Thanks!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious. Might cut the sugar down a bit in the topping,

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: For the sake of calories I cut down on the refined white sugar and reduced the butter. For the crumble I subbed about a 1/3 cup stevia, 2  tablespoons brown sugar in place of the 3/4 cup of sugar and 1/3 cup of unsweeted applesauce in place of 1/4 cup of the butter. Pulsed all crumble ingredients in a food processor. I diced apples and added golden raisins and 1/3 cup of honey for the fruit mix. Same baking directions and came out great and tastes terrific.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Rhubarb!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I've made this at least a dozen times and serve it with Greek yogurt. From the first time I made this recipe, I revised the amount of sugar. Instead of the 3/4 cup sugar, I use two tablespoons sugar for the crumble. I've only made this with blueberries but only add a tablespoon of sugar rather than the 1/2 cup the recipe calls for the fruit mixture. Tastes perfect without all that sugar in the recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I  made this a few days ago & found that I could put all of the crumble ingredients (except nuts) in the bowl of a food processor for easy mixing.  Also halved the sugar, baked for about an hour in an 8-inch square dish, & used used apples.  Perhaps a little less salt, if you are halving the sugar.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: "Keeping the peel on the apple lends a pleasant chewiness"? More like an unpleasant fibrous stringiness left hanging inelegantly out of one's mouth.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: When recipes call for instant oats I put the recommended amount in the blender and pulse them  few times...works fine.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made the full amount of topping with 1/2 the blueberries. Also just mixed the topping ingredients with melted v softened butter. Prepared this in individual ramekins.  Crunchy, flavorsome, delicious. Topped with homemade whipped cream. This will be my new go-to crisp/crumble recipe!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Does anyone have a recipe for chai spice blend or know where to buy it? Our local grocery stores haven’t got any. Thank you in advance!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this with flour/sugar/butter crumble as we never have oats at home. Three apples and some about to go bad blueberries made a great crumble. But as often with NYT recipes can you really gather, measure, dice fruit, in 5 minutes. Prep to me nearly 1/2 hour though that partly due to totally dried brown sugar I had to reconstitute. Still the end product worth it. Just asking NYT to be a little more accurate with prep time for non professional cooks.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I substituted one cup coarse coconut flakes, toasted, for the nuts (due to allergies). Excellent!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Why does it specify a skillet?  Can't any oven proof baking pan work?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I added more spice to the topping. Ginger and cardamom made it more cozy and interesting for me.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: We found this too sweet. We would cut the sugar down significantly the next time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Regular oats work fine.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: The only theory I can come up with as to why so many NYT recipes need to bake/cook longer than stated is that the NYT chefs must all start with room temperature ingredients.  Home cooks do not.  This fruit crumble took 90 minutes to fully bake and did not burn on the top at all in my perfectly calibrated oven.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Tate's gluten free ginger crackers are My secret ingredient in the topping. 4 cookies pulverized in the food processor. Next pulse in the oats for a few seconds. Recently I pulsed in the butter which makes nice even little blobs. Previously I used melted butter. Not sure there's a big difference. Add some powdered ginger And a little brown sugar. Keeps in the refrigerator forever, and of course can be frozen. This allows me to make individual ramekins of whatever Fruit is lovely any time.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this using a variety of berries that were a bit overripe- blueberries, raspberries, and blackberries- to which I added strawberries and a peeled, cut up Granny Smith apple. Cut the sugar in half. This made for a delicious mixture of flavors and textures in the finished  crumble.  I made it in ramekins and added a small scoop of vanilla bean ice cream to the warm crumble.  Delicious- rave reviews from both the kids and the grandparents .

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Skillet?  What size baking dish?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Any ideas for making this vegan??

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Just use Miyokos "butter" instead.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Skip the sugar in the fruit mixture.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Pretty easy.  Made 1/2 recipe and put in a small white ceramic casserole  - w vanilla bean icecream. Used less sugar than recipe called for and it was fine.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
I made this several times with apples. Excellent!!!
About 4 large Granny Smith.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Why do you put this into a skillet instead of a baking pan? Seems it would be hard to present this at the table or on a buffet with a skillet with a long handle.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I have this same question!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I half-defrosted frozen berries and mixed them with fresh apples. It looked quite watery for a long time and finally solidified at the 45-minute mark. The flavours were excellent but I second the suggest not to defrost frozen fruits, esp. if that's all you use.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: While I haven’t made this particular recipe , I make fruit crumbles frequently.  I always used regular oats ands frozen fruit.  Just add a bit extra cornstarch or thickener of choice depending on the juiciness of fruit chosen.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: No need to add sugar or cornstarch to the fruit. I like to make individual servings in 1  cup ramikins. Just dump the fruit in and put on the topping. Cook at 375 for 35 minutes or till top is browning and fruit is bubbling.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
This turned out really nice! I made the following changes though:
1.  Use Brown Sugar in the topping, not white.
2.  Use Old Fashioned Oatmeal, not instant
3.  Use a pastry cutter to cut the butter in.
4.  Used 1 tsp cinnamon and 1 tsp cardamom
The crumble top turned out very crispy and crumbly and the spices went really well with a combo of blueberries and apples.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’ve made a whoooole lotta crisps and crumbles in my day, and this is the best. I used cinnamon and brown sugar in the topping, decreased white sugar to less than 1/4 c (as someone wise recommended here), and first lightly toasted the pecans. Fresh straws and blues. Fabulous so fabulous.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Mostly great recipe — I cut the sugar like people suggested, and that was prob the right move. But no one is saying anything about how salty it was! I used fine salt as directed and it’s edible and yummy enough with ice cream, but def too salty. I’d do more like 1/4 or 1/2 teaspoon at the most. 

I used regular oats with no issues and a combo of fresh blueberries, raspberries and frozen peaches. No issues with the frozen peaches, and it was a pleasing fruit medley. I’d make again and cut the salt.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If using apples or stone fruit, is it two pounds of fruit after preparing the fruit? Because if one were using only berries, it would be two pounds without any waste.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this recipe with the exact ingredients amounts but placed it in a glass 8x8 dish instead, and it was just the right size without any overflow from juices. I used a 12 oz bag of frozen mixed berries that I allowed to thaw and drain before mixing with freshly peeled and chopped apples and nectarines that I added to reach 2 lbs. The 2 Tbsp of cornstarch was perfect to thicken the juices. I reheat leftovers inside a ramekin in the air fryer to recrisp it, then add ice cream - perfection!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made this with regular oats and it turned out delicious. I was skeptical of the chai spice in the oat crumble with stone fruit ("summer flavors mixed with winter flavors?") but the flavors worked very well together and was a hit among friends who tried it! This will be a dessert staple for me moving forward.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made per recipe choosing fresh blackberries and apple (peeled diced) combo in ratio of about 60% /40%. Used Bob's Red Mill Regular Old Fashioned Oats (they are GF btw). Bake time was about 1 hr to perfect doneness. Next time I will reduce sugar in the fruit by half. Used spice blend referenced in tips, M. Clark recipe. Very good. See link: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1021926-sweet-baking-spice

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I have successfully used Bob's regular, and extra thick oats with this recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delish!I could make and eat this dish daily! What fun! I had no white sugar so I used brown sugar with crumble topping. I subbed in 2 tablespoons real maple syrup with a combo of raspberries, peaches. The sign of an excellent recipe is great results even when you have to sub a few ingredients. Served with plain yogurt.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I used peaches and it was ACES. A tad sweet, but I like sweet, so would reco a little less sugar in the crumble if you’re not a fan. Very easy, def will remake with different fruits.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Delicious and easy as pie?   No, much easier than pie but as delicious

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Cast iron skillet gets too hot and recipe cooks too fast. Be careful. Othe than that its a lovely dessert

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Just a few notes to the gluten intolerant: 
- Some people who are very sensitive to gluten do react to a similar protein in oats. 
- If you are gluten sensitive but able to eat oats, buy those labeled "gluten-free". Oats in the US are frequently contaminated with other grains and not considered "safe" for the gluten sensitive unless they are sourced from uncontaminated supplies and labeled "gluten-free".

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This was very very good. As others have stated though I thought the fruit filling was too sweet. Next time I make it, I will use about 1/3 as much sugar.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Excellent recipe but definitely use half the sugar for the crumble.  The sweetness of the fruit is enough.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Love this recipe - easy to prep ahead and a crowd pleaser. I took the advice of other comments and reduced the sugar. I prefer it that way. But you can always add more if you like it sweeter - maybe even dust it with powdered sugar when cooled and serving.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Subbed with brown sugar and it was wonderful! Just fine with rolled oats.

author: Genevieve Ko
