commentBody: Are oats gluten free?

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Elizabeth Hanson - Extended family members are celiacs and from cooking for them I know that while oats are GF, if they have been processed in facilities to share equipment with gluten grains then the gluten proteins can transfer to the oats. Look for brands that list GF on their labels to note they are not processed along with gluten grains. For example, Bob's Red Mill GF Old Fashioned Rolled Oats. https://www.bobsredmill.com/gluten-free-rolled-oats.html

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Some oats are gluten free. Trader Joe's are gluten-free, for instance, but some oats are processed on the same equipment as other food and are not gf.  Check the packaging.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Can you explain the purpose of the cornstarch? Tx.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Cornstarch is a thickener so the fruit sauce isn't runny.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It is a thickener used because the fruit will release juices.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It thickens the fruit juices

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Actually, the best fruit crumbles are without any oats at all. They are made with flour, butter and brown sugar, on top of whatever fruit you like that is in season.  Add various flavors and spices...or none.  Grated fresh ginger is a good choice with apples, for example.  Always add a couple of tablespoons of water or apple cider into the fruit.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My grandmother made this all the time with apples. I think she called it a "crisp" though.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I now make the topping separate from the fruit so it doesn't get soggy where it touches the fruit and I can control the crumble to fruit ratio.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Thank you for having a gluten free dessert! I often use cornstarch in place of gf flour especially in these kind of desserts where thickening is needed. Not all Oats are gluten free. Bob’s Red Mill has a separate facility for making their gf items.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I never buy butter or stick margarine and generally have no use for it. Would a neutral oil such as canola work in this recipe and if so, what is the ratio of oil to butter as a substitution?

commentBody: Can regular, non-instant oats, but used?

commentBody: This recipe is perfectly delicious with no sugar.  The fruits make it wonderfully sweet.

commentBody: Used juice and zest from two lemons with 2.5 lb of blackberries for the fruit. Delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Easy to throw this dessert together. I used strawberries and substituted monkfruit for some of the sugar. Next time, I will cut back on the amount of sweetener overall because it was a bit on the sweeter side and we enjoy fruit with a little more tartness.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Fruit Crisp or crumble is an old, traditional recipe if ever there was one. Over hundreds of years, North American cooks adapted local ingredients, which is all they had, to produce delightful combinations, that were as sound as the latest drive to local ingredients. When I make this desert, I want regular oats and no cinnamon. Basically, nothing exotic, nothing processed, just seasonal fruit and a basic crumble topping. Nature and heat will do the rest. I agree with Rose K’s comments 100%.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Just ok , pretty good topping

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Used the Baharat spices, wonderful! Used less sugar in both the fruit and the crumble, topped with vanilla ice cream. So good! Will add to my frequent bakes.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this last night; it was delicious!  Used about 1 1/2 lbs. of mixed blueberries, blackberries and raspberries.  Topped with homemade whipped cream.  It was a hit.

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
Best to melt the butter
Add in the flour, oats, light brown sugar and cinnamon. Use a fork to mash the ingredients 
Carefully place the mixture on top of the prepared fruits. Add a touch of ground black pepper for an added flavor.

commentBody: Made last night and family was over the moon. Used cast iron pan which gave outer edges a bit of delicious chew. Used blues, small amount raspberries and chopped nectarine since that's what I had, and it had great texture and flavor. Hubby begged me not to alter the recipe!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I’m always curious about people who leave the skin on fruit, such as apples, in baked goods. I find the texture awful and always peel, but perhaps I’m in the wrong. Any skin lovers out there who care to explain the a-peel??

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Easy and delicious. Didn’t add any sugar or lemon to fresh peaches and a 1/2 lb frozen mixed berries. This was intentional and for me the right way. The crumble and fruit were plenty sweet!  Then vanilla ice cream. Yeah. Didn’t miss the sugar in fact think it was better. More fruit forward, some might say, although I’ve never used those words together so don’t judge if I’ve misused! Forgot the cornstarch, which was not intentional, but wasn’t a crisis, it was a smidge soup, which no one seemed to mind, it just puddled with melted ice cream and was eater with a spoon. One guest definitely looked like they wanted to lick their plate!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Blondies are also good with nuts. The favorite in my childhood home was chopped pecans.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These were delicious and so easy to make!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Did anyone else have a problem with them collapsing after they came out of the oven? Mine were very flat. I wonder if it would have been better to cream the butter and sugar in the mixer.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Baked this according to recipe. Ended up baking 35 minutes and thought I might have over baked them until cutting into them after they cooled and they were bordering on being under baked. Not a recipe I’ll be making again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Just took these out of the oven, at 25 minutes, and they look terrific. These are childishly simple to make. This recipe reminds me of how my mother used to make toll house cookies, by hand mixing. The butter has to be well softened in order to mix; it helps to cut it in pieces, first. I made these according to the recipe and can see how this would be an excellent base for other additions, such as chocolate, butterscotch, peanut butter or toffee chips. Will definitely make this again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made these yesterday, and I was disappointed.  I had the same issue with baking: 15 min longer than the recipe indicated.  In retrospect, I used a newer glass pan.  For some reason, Pyrex glass pans that are supposedly 8 inches are really only 6 inches  on a side at the bottom.  This will cause a longer baking time.  I continue to pick up the old style pans at estate sales for this reason.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Surprisingly good considering it is dead simple to make. I used the darkest brown sugar I could find and added a half cup of Heath chocolate toffee shard thingies to liven it up. Served warm with chocolate ice cream and it was a good enough bday treat.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I loved this recipe but recommend using clear vanilla extract instead!  You get that sort of brighter funfetti flavor.  They were a big hit.

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Chocolate chips, your favorite chopped nuts (especially pecans or walnuts) and flaked coconut are all delicious additions to blondies. Seasonally appropriate m&ms are also a festive touch. For an 8x8 pan you could add a cup total of the above and be very happy.

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Even with the sprinkles, Blondie are a little boring without mix-ins.  My personal favorite is chopped pecans, but if you are looking for something really sweet, add butterscotch chips.  But only 1/3 cup at the most--they are powerful.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: My 7 year old grandson declared, this might be better than chocolate!  My 3 year old grandson immediately said, I want another.

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Vegans often replace egg by mixing ground flax seed with water. Let the flax egg sit for a bit before adding to the recipe.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody:
you can make a quick easy egg substitute with chia seeds:

1 TBSP chia seeds + 2.5 TBSP water = 1 egg

let sit until mixture has the consistency of egg

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are amazing. I added a half cup of white chocolate chips, which probably made them too sweet, but still delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko

commentBody: I did not have the experience you did. I baked them, in a glass Pyrex, for 25 minutes and turned off the oven and left them in for another minute - or two, at most. Cooled them in the pan on a rack. They came out crispy on the edge and softer and chewier in the middle, but still a good consistency.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I made these very quickly last night, using Heath 'Bits O' Brickle' from the freezer instead of sprinkles. Used Pyrex 8x8 which I find burns less than metal dish. Quite sweet and totally delicious.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I needed an extra 10 minutes in the oven, but these were delicious.

To the batter, I added 1/4 cup chopped walnuts and 1/4 cup dried cranberries which had been soaked in rum for an hour or so. Sprinkles on the top only, which worked out great - quite festive.

author: Genevieve Ko

author: Genevieve Ko

I found a jar of pastel matte spring sprinkles & cooked on the center rack for 25 minutes. No problems seen with raw or uncooked centers & used a metal pan.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These were a hit at the party I brought them to (all adults). I used coarse salt because it’s all I had and actually found that the hit of salt was important to balancing these out.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Followed the directions exactly and am very disappointed. The "batter" was quite thick, literally had to use a knife to spread it, and the finished blondies are quite chewy--and very sweet besides. Edible, but I'll stick with the Maple Walnut Blondies.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Added chocolate chips and coconut per comments- definitely would again!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: This recipe was not good. I was excited to make it because of how easy it is. It also doesn't require a mixer. Mixing the softened butter and sugar with a spatula was not possible. I ended up using my hands like pie dough. It looked more like a cookie dough at the end. I could barely spread it into the pan. I kept checking the recipe to make sure I had the right ratio of ingredients because it was so off. The only change I made was using parchment paper on the pan.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: If it was difficult to mix the sugar and butter together with the spatula, most likely the butter was not soft enough. The dough is much like a cookie dough, and it has to be intentionally spread in the pan. After first spreading it with the spatula, I used a butter knife to spread it more evenly and to the edge of the pan.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made exactly as written, and they were perfect! To make my life easier, I creamed the butter and sugar with an electric mixer, then continued using the wooden spoon. They were crunchy on the outside and gooey on the inside. A great blondie.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These are pretty solid. I had leftover candy melts and put a mixure of chopped candy melts, chocolate chips and sprinkles inside the cookie. They're delicious and the texture is also great. 25 min baking time was perfect.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I love these. Most blondies are just too sweet. These aren’t. They taste kind of like caramels with salt. Follow the recipe when it comes to time cooked. I cooked them for the full 25 minutes and when they came out, they seemed really soft and likely uncooked. But I listened to the wise words of another commenter and waited - they firmed up nicely as they cooled.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Good with toffee chips

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made to recipe, except I baked for 30 minutes (at 25 they were definitely underdone) in 8x8 Pyrex dish. They sunk, were too greasy, and the only flavor profile (to me) was sweet.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Easy to make and delicious!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Perfect for my chocolate hating 6 year old. No I am not kidding. Hates chocolate. Loves these blondies.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made this recipe with a group of 4 nine year olds and it turned out great. They were able to execute the recipe on their own to much success. We used a thin 8x8 metal pan and baked for 25m, edges were crispy and center chewy/soft. Would maybe cook longer in a regular (not thin) pan? Sprinkles were a fun addition. I’ll be making this again!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These were ok? I think I had the issues that others had with uneven cooking. I used a glass pyrex dish from the 1950s so it was thinner than today's glass baking dishes. The center was very very soft, and the edges bordered on tough. While they present very beautifully with the sprinkles, I just wasn't a fan of the flavor or texture, and neither was my kid. I don't have plans to ever make these again, but if I did, I'd probably use a smaller baking dish as the 8x8 left them too thin.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Made as written - except used salted butter - using a metal pan as called for in the recipe.   I had nearly used a glass dish, but noticed in the comments that it produced a poor result.  I ordered the sprinkles in the picture from Amazon.   No problems with cooking time or texture.  The blondies came out perfect, sweet and with a nice chewy texture.   Their density allowed me to cut them into small squares for one-bite treats at a party.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Any tips for keeping these from drying out so quickly? Mine were hard as rocks within 2 days because we couldn’t eat them fast enough. Tasted great, though!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Super easy and absolutely perfect as is! Have made this many times.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: I cheated and used my hand mixer for the initial mixing of the butter, brown sugar, egg and vanilla, then switched to a flexible spatula to stir in the flour. The texture was perfect with a 25 minute bake. Will definitely be making these again! The sprinkles didn't really add flavor, but certainly made them look festive!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Another good substitute for sprinkles is toffee bits which taste amazing in the recipe

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: It took a little longer to cook but I think it was because of my pan. The kids loved it and I will make it again.

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: Lovely weeknight treat!

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These were super simple to make. I made them with rainbow sprinkles (topped, not mixed in), but imagine that you could really sub in whatever inclusions you want and the recipe would hold up well. I baked for 20 min and they were soft and slightly gooey in the middle (which is how I wanted them).

author: Genevieve Ko
commentBody: These were good. I questioned if the 130 g of flour was correct or a if it was a typo, so I went with 120 g. No issues.
